author: ap3jedi
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Zimmermann and Prof Anandha
BZA student here. i wont tell you what the mod constitutes as you can look at the other reviews or do your own digging ;). but what i'll offer is my raw and honest review.
As someone who has not ever scored an >A- for the previous CS1010A and CS2030 mods, i went into the mod not having much hope, wishing I could SU it.
Got below mean and median for midterms, failed it, in actual fact. i was crushed... :(
Despite maxxing out the ODAs, THAs and Visualgo quiz, things did not look too good. However, when i realised this might potentially be the last CS coded mod i would ever do, something in me clicked, and i decided to do the best i could.
what i did:
1. grinded about 15 PYPs
2. spam asked chatgpt to help me explain answer keys...
3. attended and listened to all the tutorials attentively (by Prof Roger Zimmermann), asking him questions too.
4. lastly, booked a consult with Prof Anandha during finals season after i did at least 7 papers

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message: How did you manage to grind 15 PYPs. I've just done 2 MCQs sections and my exam is in 8 days. Anyways wanted to say it's a very inspiring review. Will prb grind 2-3 more pyps also b4 finals.

author: ap3jedi
message:
Hi Ilyas! Firstly, all the best for your finals, its gonna be over in a bit so press on.
Sad to say, there's no easy way around it. You'd have to sit yourself down and really go thru paper after paper.
What I liked to do was to mark the paper as soon as I'm done (of course with some questions skipped). In that way, the questions would still be fresh in my memory, instead of re-visiting a paper I've done days ago and trying to und the qn over again. Its my preference though.
Something else that helped was to circle those questions I skipped/made mistakes in, so at the end of it all, I could refer to them if I dont have time to go through all questions.
In my humble opinion, you still have time - 8 days is sufficient for you to do more than 2-3 pyps, though I understand you might have other mods to study for.
Nonetheless, I'd recommend covering at least 5? And perhaps do the 5 newest ones. Maybe save the latest paper for your last, and do under exam conditions - standard tips.
Hope this helps mate!
edit: I forgot to point out that I only had 2 finals to study for that sem...hence this gave me ample time to complete that amount of papers

author: fkLeetcode
message:
AY25/26, Sem 1
Taught by Profs Sanka Rasnayaka, Roger Zimmermann, Low Kok Lim
Jampacked course, many things to learn (and master) but useful for interviews ig. I got spooked by the reviews here and spent about 3 months during the holidays exclusively starting Leetcode and learning Java before taking this course. This helped greatly and I was able to go through the course, especially the first half, without too much struggle. Despite enjoying CS2030 much more, I scored way better in this course because it’s so smurfable I guess. DP and greedy aren't really the focus here though they may inherently be part of other algorithms like APSP. There are a lot of assessments in this course, which on the one hand means the grade is more spread out, on the other hand very busy . I’ll only highlight the parts of the course that I have anything interesting to note, the rest can refer to other reviews.
Lectures
Start and end was taught by prof sanka, middle part taught by prof roger. In my opinion they are both pretty decent lecturers, but prof roger need to 2x speed him. The course is structured based on some CP4 textbook by prof s Halim .They provide crash course prerecorded lectures to learn Java at the start of the course, but it is wholly insufficient. Java is quite different from Python due to the OOP focus and static typing and being more verbose, so best to familiarise beforehand , I think learning it simultaneously thorugh CS2030 will just cause more stress haha.

author: gomuflower
message:
Taken in AY25/26 S1
Lecturers: Prof Sanka Rasnayaka/Prof Roger Zimmerman
Tutor: Prof Low Kok Lim
Lectures
2 + 1 hours of online lectures each week. Lectures are good though they often overrun quite significantly by up to 1 hour which is quite irritating. Prof Roger lectures quite slowly and rushes at the end and that the slides for the topic on BST/AVL trees are really messy.
Tutorials
The tutorials for this course can be quite challenging as you are expected to apply the data structures and algorithms. My tutor was Prof Low Kok Lim who was good with explaining the concepts but I feel he focused too much on class participation which led to classes overrunning. Overall, tutorials are quite useful for prepping for exams and some of the questions are leetcode-style questions as well :).
Labs (Individual Learning Assignment)
Nothing much to say, besides that it can sometimes be stressful to finish the lab by the end of the lab slot (the newly added requirement this sem). It can also be quite disheartening when your code fails to meet a hidden test case. That said, my tutors and were quite goated and helpful in helping us during the debugging process. Would recommend scheduling your lab slot as late as possible so that you have more time to finish the assignment.

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 25/26
Lecturers: Prof Sanka/ Prof Roger Zimmerman
Programming Language: Java (for assignments), DSA people/people who didnt do CS2030 were told to watch a video produced by the CS2040 teaching team on Java basics (about 3-4 hours long).
Exams: All in pseudo code ie you dont have to write out code in python/java (like they did in CS1010S/E) you can just write the idea in English and it will count.
Assignments:
1. 10 x Lab Assignments (Kattis) (10%) (1.25% each capped at 10% so essentially you only need to do 8 to get the full score)
2. 2 x THA (Kattis) (4%) (2% each, 1% per question)
3. Written Assessment in week 6 (8%)
4. Midterms (25%)
5. Visualgo Quiz 1 and Quiz 2 (8% total)
6. Class participation + Tutorial/Lab attendance - 5%
7. Finals (40%)

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY22/23 ST2 with Prof Chong Ket Fah
The Module covers the following topics
1. Intro to Java
2. Abstract Data Type
3. Analysis of Algorithm
- Time Complexity
4. Sorting
- Selection, Bubble, Insertion, Merge, Quick, Radix, Built-In methods
5. Lists
- Array lists, Linked lists (Basic, Tailed, Circular, Doubly)
6. Stacks and Queues
7. Hash tables
8. Binary Heap and Priority Queue
9. Union-Find Disjoint Sets
10. Binary Search Tree
11. AVL Tree and Balancing
12. The Foundations Of Graphs
- Adjacency Matrix, Adjacency List, Edge List
13. Graph Transversal
- BFS, DFS, Reachability Test, Shortest Path Between 2 vertices, Finding Components, Topological Sort (Kahn’s Algorithm), Finding Strongly Connected Components (Kosaraju’s Algorithm)
14. Minimum Spanning Tree
- Prim’s Algorithm, Kruskal’s Algorithm
15. Single-Source Shortest Paths
- BFS Algorithm, Bellman Ford’s Algorithm, Dijkstra’s Algorithm
16. All-Pairs Shortest Paths (Not tested)
- Floyd Warshall’s Dynamic Programming Algorithm

author: anonymous_peanut
message:
AY21/22 Sem 2 Prof. Chong Ket Fah
Tutorial Attendance/ Participation - 3%
Lab attendance - 2%
In-lab Assignments - 15%
Take Home Assignments - 12%
Online Quiz 1 - 4%
Online Quiz 2 - 4%
Midterm - 20%
Final Exam - 40%
The in-lab assignments have a one day deadline. During labs, you are asked to show your pseudo-code and your TAs will help with that. Afterwards, you are asked to figure it out yourself. These assignments took me about 1 - 3 hours to complete.
The take home assignments are much tougher than the one day assignments. It took me about a full day to finish each question. Good to get started as soon as the assignments are out.
The online quizzes are conducted in Visualgo. Definitely possible to get full marks with practice as there's only so many formats that the questions are asked.

author: MA1522 UBottom
message:
AY25/26 Semester 1
Lecturers: Roger Zimmerman & Sanka
Tutor: Nguyen
I was expecting CS2040 to be tougher in terms of workload compared to CS2030 based on the past reviews, but I felt like it was much more chill. I believe they reduced the number of THAs and we only had to do two for this semester. The Kattis assignments are also optional and you can frankly skip it, I stopped doing it after the first few weeks. The only downside of CS2040 is that you have to be constantly on your toes to keep up with the almost weekly assessments (there are THAs, Visualgo quizzes, Written assessments etc.). However, the weekly assessments are really chill and easy to score full marks if you can keep up with the lecture contents.
Lectures:
Lectures are 3 hours weekly, split into a two-hour session and a one-hour session on another day. It is quite draining as the two lecturers will usually overrun every single time, so you have to watch the lectures in your own time if you need to head for another class. Otherwise, I thought the explanations by the lecturers are done well but sometimes they focus too much on the basics they end up rushing towards the end.

author: Naman Agrawal
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof. Tan Sun Teck & Prof. Roger Zimmermann
Assessments:
Labs: 12*1% = 12%
Tutorials: 3% Attendance + 3% Participation = 6%
Mock Practical Exam: 3%
Practical Exam: 12%
Visualgo: 12% (5% + 5% + 2% for 3 visualgo quizzes)
Midterms: 15%
Finals: 40%
One of the most important modules for any technical interview. If you like knowing about new problem solving techniques in the form of computational algorithms, you'll enjoy this module. But you'll have to put in lots of efforts! At the start of the module, Prof introduces everyone to the basics of Java and Vim. While Prof does teach the basics, it's vital that you take time to read up some online references too, especially for the labs and practical examination. If you're regular in your lab work, then you wouldn't face a lot of problem on the coding part. Make sure to familiarize yourself with the important Java API data structures and interfaces: Integer, String, Long, Double, Arrays, ArrayList, LinkedList, ArrayDeque, Stack, Queue, HashMap, HashSet, TreeMap, TreeSet, PriotityQueue, et al. By the end of the module you'll probably be playing with many such data structures entangled in a web of custom classes, interface and references.

12% weekly Labs
12% Visu Algo (best 6 of 9)
12% Practical exam
15% Midterm exam

Overview:
A buildup on cs1010 with a focus on using datastructures and algorithms to solve given tasks as efficiently as possible. In cs1010 variants, most likely you are only concerned with writing programs that solve the given task without much attention to the time/space complexity. However, in this module you would be expected to come up with solutions that run much better than brute force solutions.

author: Damaen Tan
message:
Taken In AY23/24 Semester 2
Lecturer: Prof Chong Ket Fah
Tutor: Ivan Chew
Lab TAs: Tan Si Rui, Issaiah Koh
Assessment Of Module:
Tutorial Attendance (5%)
10 One-day Assignments (15%)
8 Take-Home Assignments (12%)
2 Visualgo Quizzes (8%)
Midterms (20%)
Final Exam (40%)
Content Of Module:
1. Introduction To Java - Java Syntax and Basic Code, Data Types. (Those with CS2030 knowledge can skip this part)
2. Abstract Data Types - Java Interface, Classes in Java
3. Analysis Of Algorithms - Big O Notation, Order of Growth, Analysis of Different Cases.
4. Sorting - Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort, Merge Sort, Selection Sort, Quick Sort, Radix Sort, Bubble Sort with Flag, Time Complexities of these sorting algorithms, stability of sorts, in-place sort.
5. Array Lists and Linked Lists (Including Doubly Linked List, Tailed Linked List, Circular Linked List etc.)
6. Stacks and Queues - FIFO and LIFO algorithms, Palindromes
7. Hash Tables - Hash Functions, Collision Resolution, Separate, Linear and Quadratic Probing, Double Hashing.
8. Binary Heaps - Functions in Binary Heap (Extract Max, Search etc.)
9. UFDS
10. Binary Search Tree (BST) - Ranks, Insertion of Nodes into BST, Deletion of nodes, Successor and Predecessor
11. BST and AVL Tree - BST height and size, balancing BST, Balance Factors, Tree Rotations, Rebalancing
12. Graphs Foundations - Data Structures (Adjacency Matrix, Adjacency List, Edge List)
13. Graph Traversal - BFS and DFS, topological sort (Kahn's Algorithm), Reachability Test, Identifying/Counting Component(s), DAGs, Topological Sort – DFS based algorithm.
14. Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) - Cycle Property, Cut Property, Prim’s Algorithm, Prim’s variant for Dense Graphs, Kruskal’s Algorithm, Sorting Edges in Edge List.
15. Single-Source Shortest Paths (SSSP) - Negative Weight Edges and Cycles, “Relaxation” Operation, BFS for SSSP, BFS on Unweighted Graph, Bellman's Ford Algorithm, Dijkstra's Algorithm, Modified Dijkstra's Algorithm, Lazy Deletion.
16. (NOT TESTED) All-Pairs Shortest Paths (APSP) - Floyd Warshall’s Dynamic Programming algorithm, APSP Solutions with SSSP Algorithms.

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Prof: Chong Ket Fah
ODA and THA:
I found the THA extremely tough. Really dreaded whenever 2 weeks passed and the new THA opens up. Advice to take this module with friends, so that can at least "help" one another in some way or another. ODA were generally easier, with one of two being quite tedious. Do make sure that your pseudocode is okay by checking with you lab TA's before embarking on your code, makes it much easier, at least for me.
VisuAlgo Quizzes:
Do study and just keep practicing on VisuAlgo. And always check your answers, do not be over confident since you should really ensure you get full marks on these 2 quizzes.
Midterms:
Midterms involve MCQs/MRQs, analysis questions and application questions. I found the midterms very tough. During midterms, I feel that since fewer topics are covered, they test a lot more "in depth" questions and really required much thinking. Structure was also different from PYP's, which caught me off-guard.

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
Taken in AY23/24 Semester 1
Under Prof Chong Ket Fah
Assessments:
Take Home Assignments (THAs): 12%
One Day Assignments (ODAs): 15%
Visualgo Quizzes: 8%
Lab + Tutorial Attendance: 5%
Midterm: 20%
Finals: 40%
Overall:
Quite an interesting course that covers various algorithms and data structures. It’s very mind blowing to see that some algorithms just work. I would recommend everyone to take this mod with their friends especially when it is under Prof Chong Ket Fah.
ODA:
As the name suggests, it’s a one day (coding) assignment on Kattis (something like coursemology). For this semester, it started in Friday morning and ended on Saturday morning. There’s a lab on friday for everyone so the lab TAs can help us with the ODA. Out of the 13 weeks of school, there are 10 weeks when you have to do this. However you don’t have to worry because if you actually follow lecture and seek help from your TA, it should be quite doable. This is also where your group of friend might come in because I received so much support from them. It shouldn’t be hard but expect your friday afternoon/evening to be gone because of this. I know people who can solve it during Lab itself also.

author: kok
message:
Taken in AY21/22 ST 2 under Dr Chong Ket Fah
NOTE: I took this under the RNSMen Special Term programme after completing CS1010X, and this review will mostly be tailored to future incoming freshmen who are considering doing the same.
Summary:
Consider carefully if you want to do this under the RNSMen ST programme. It WILL clash with orientations (I spent 8 of the last 10 days before the finals at orientation camps), and it WILL be fairly time-consuming (if nothing else because of the graded attendance/participation components). I juggled a full-time internship + this module + attending all the relevant orientation camps - my performance in all 3 along with my mental and physical health suffered quite badly. For my batch, all the CS1010X -> CS2040 students made a Telegram group together for academic and moral support. I highly recommend that future batches do the same.
Special Note on Module Planning: There will be a note on the Special Term 2 registration saying that “Computer Science Undergraduates cannot take CS2040”. If you are an RNSMan NG student majoring in Computer Science, you can still take CS2040 in Special Term because you aren’t an Undergraduate yet :). For Computer Science majors, taking this lets you do your Data Structures and Algorithms module 2 semesters ahead, since once you matriculate you must do CS2040S which has CS1231/S as a prerequisite, while CS2040 only needs CS1010-equivalent. This lets you do funny things like take CS2220 or CS2105 in Year 1 Semester 1, and CS3233, CS3230, CS2103T, CS3241, CS4236, CS5342 in Year 1 Semester 2 (which other CS1010-exempt students can’t). If you are an EE/CEG major, you may have to wrestle with department admin staff who may get annoyed that you didn’t do Data Structures and Algorithms in C++ (CS2040C), but it should work out in the end.

author: dogshit_cs_student
message:
Taken in AY2021/2022 Special Term(Part 2) under Prof Chong Ket Fah
Disclaimer: I am a student of dogshit-calibre so if you are reading this chances are you will be able to cope better than me. In fact my previous review got flagged for spam so Im guessing the quality of reviews is correlated to my quality as a soc student.
I do not recommend taking this before matriculating if you have cleared 1010X, unless you're a programming god and already know most or at least half of the content. Do note that orientation activities fall on the last few weeks of this module in special term as well. In fact I would not recommend taking this module in special term at all, considering the amount of content that it covers and that there are only 6 weeks.
This is not a complaint about prof's teaching or the syllabus but 6 weeks is just too short and painful. For a module that people emphasise the importance of, 6 weeks is just not enough to absorb all the content taught.
Fun fact the module started with 98 students and at the end only 76 remained.
For course content, you can take a look at kok's review. Each week consists of 2 days of classes. 3 hour lecture + 1 hour tutorial + 1hour++ lab session for the ODA. Do note that there were classes up till the day before finals, the schedule is that packed. Midterms on week 3, finals on week 6.

author: dogshit_cs_student
message: Final grade: A range. I still do not strongly recommend taking this in special term.

author: anon127294529013
message:
Taken in AY19/20 Sem 4 (ST2)
Lecturer: Prof Aquinas Hobor
Assessment: 30% CA, 20% midterms, 50% finals. CA is comprised of 2 one day assignments worth 1% every week and 1 one week take home worth 2%, 2 Visualgo quizzes worth 8% and tutorial/lab participation.
All content was covered within 5 weeks. Don't take this module in special term, it is too hectic and too fast. I learnt more from online youtube videos explaining DS&A than from the lecturer. CS1231 knowledge is needed despite there not being an explicit prerequisite. The TAs are generally good. In order to do well you basically need to eat sleep shit algorithms for 5 weeks. Don't slack off like I did :(.
Expected grade: B/B+
Actual grade: B (grade discarded as a NG student)

author: awake
message:
Taken in AY18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: Asst Prof Harold Soh
Synopsis: CS2040 introduces fundamental data structures and algorithms to students, as well as some basic asymptotic analysis. The module is taught in Java and assumes basic working knowledge of Java.
Assessment: Comprised of weekly/fortnightly take-home problemsets (30%), two quizzes (6%), a coding quiz (4%), a midterm (10%), participation (10%) and a final exam (40%). Weekly/fortnightly problemsets are interesting and are for putting concepts into practice, and are not difficult to do well in, given that collaboration is encouraged and the long deadline. The two quizzes serve as evaluations of learning progress in the module and are not difficult with a good understanding of the module content. The coding quiz is a practical exam and there's a lot of time for you figure out what to do (the problemsets should prepare you for this). The midterm and finals do not focus on how exactly the data structures and algorithms work, but how they can be applied to solve various algorithmic problems, and so exposure to a variety of problems will help. Finally, in-class and forum participation are encouraged and will count towards the participation component of the final grade.
Lecturer: Prof Harold is an excellent lecturer with a great flair for teaching. He engages the audience well and lectures are generally interesting, even if you have prior knowledge.

author: monikernemo
message:
Review for 17/18 Sem 2
Lecturer: AP Tan Sun Teck
Assessment:
18% Sit in Lab
2% Lab Attendance
5% Take Home Lab
15% Practical Exam
5% Tutorial Attendance
15% Midterm
40% Finals
This is an introductory module on Data Structures (linked list, stack, queue, binary trees, heap etc) and Algorithms (primarily focusing on sorting and some graph traversal problems). The content isn't inherently difficult because most, if not all, of the idea are quite intuitive. Despite being a veteran lecturer of CS1020, 2010, Prof Tan's implementation of the module makes the course difficult in some ways (most notably by having sit-in lab assssment once every two weeks is hellish and topped off with us struggling to learning to use Java properly).
The pacing of teaching was also quite off, with him spending too much time in the morning simpler topics at the start and mad Sprint towards the end where more complicated and involved topics were discussed and could have been more beneficial if more time were spent on those.

author: imwaihon
message:
I took this mod in Semester 1, AY13/14.
The delivery of this module is a breath of fresh air. The concept of gamification is applied here through the Coursemology platform. Basically your 'homework' are called 'missions' and you can earn experience and achievements. There is even a leaderboard ranking system which is quite cool to be honest. Having said that, the workload for this module is quite heavy, there are quite a lot of missions to do and they are not easy - if you are new to programming that is. A problem can take either 15 minutes or 5 hours - it varies. Tutors are there to help you out so don't worry. :)
Personally I find this module to be quite manageable and light. Mainly because the homework are really so much fun to complete - it's like problem solving. Or maybe because I have no life. To clarify - I had almost zero experience in programming prior to joining this module. In fact, it might be beneficial to have no experience as you do not have to unlearn bad habits. Just do your work consistently and you will be fine.
Coursework takes up 35% of the final grade, which is achievable by most of the cohort. You just need to achieve Level 35 in Coursemology. I reached Level 38. (But you still only get 35%!)
Another 5% is based on class participation. Try to prepare before your tutorials. Your tutor will appreciate it - and you can have a much more fruitful tutorial session as you can find out what is wrong with your answer that you have prepared. (Or ways to improve it.)
15% each for midterm and practical exam and 30% for the finals. Midterm was kind of easy, practical exam was kind of hard and finals was okay-ish hard. There is no bell curve, so try to score reasonably well for the exams. 80% for everything will likely give you an A.

author: Yangshun
message: I would like to add on that CS1010S tutors are perhaps one of the most dedicated team of teaching staff you can find in NUS. Each CS1010S tutorial group only has a maximum of 8-12 students and therefore you will get a lot of your tutor's attention. This privilege is extended exclusively to only a few modules in NUS; it is highly beneficial for a student's learning.

author: anonymous_tiger
message:
AY21/22 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Ashish
Assessment:
25% Coursemology
5% Participation (Tutorials and Forums)
15% Midterms
15% Practical Exam
40% Final Exam
Not gonna lie and be straight to the point. Prof Ashish is a great lecturer and all other tutors are doing their best effort to help us understand. However, the practical test and final exam are so badly conducted and the difficulty level is nothing compared to previous years. Does the setter of these papers even check or vet their own paper before releasing to students to attempt? The practical test has error at last question which make us debug the paper instead of the question. Moreover, the final exam is more like testing us comprehension skills rather than programming skills. Also, the final paper is 2 hours long but we were stopped by Prof Ashish at least 3 times due to errors in the paper. This is the first time which I took a module which exams were so error-prone. I do not have to explain myself as you can see from the median of the practical test and final exam as compared to previous semesters. Just very disappointed with how the exams are handled in this situation and it could have been a better experience.
Overall: Bad experience (due to how the exams are conducted)
Hopefully future students who took this module will have a better experience.

author: Unknown
message: I agree with Yang Shun, who was our head tutor in semester 1 13/14. There will be many missions to complete on Coursmology. Every mission is tough but very interesting. Moreover, unlike CS1010, it will be taught in a very friendly programming language which can support much about everything - it's called Python. With it, your can focus more on the interesting part of programming thinking rather than syntax. After taking this one module, i felt that it has improve my productivity since most things in our age are going online. I think this will be the most worth-it module to take as your UE for students from other faculties if you ever thought of learning programming. Rest assured that python would become a popular and widely used language in data analysis or web programming and you won't ever need to learn other language again.

author: Unknown
message: Awesome module! Fantastic staff and really teaches us how to code as well as various important lessons about life like managing complexity

author: Russell Saerang
message:
(repost since the old one is uneditable)
AY20/21 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof. Leong Wai Kay and Prof. Ben Leong
Assessment:
25% Coursemology
5% Participation (Tutorials and Forums)
15% Midterms
15% Practical Exam
40% Final Exam
Overview
This module teaches the programming methodology with Python starting from the primitives (data types and simple Python operators), functional abstractions, recursion, higher-order functions, data abstraction (ADT), sequences (tuples and lists), searching, sorting, dictionaries, object-oriented programming (class), memoization, dynamic programming, exception handling, and data visualization (using matplotlib and some other mentioned packages).
Module Difficulty
For those who are new into programming, you might want to spend much more time on getting familiar with the Python environment. But don't worry, the CS1010S tutors are there to help you, also possibly your fellow peers through the Coursemology forum, which is the platform where CS1010S is using for submitting missions. I also suggest to look for more external references which other reviews have provided (I suppose so).

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 1
Lecturer: Leong Wai Kay
Tutor: Jericho
Do NOT walk in to this module with the mindset to S/U. There is no bellcurve and the D/F boundary is pretty fixed around low-mid 50s. It can smell your fear.
Background: Python (up till basic iteration, recursion and function definitions - didnt know lambda functions before this module), Scratch
Assessments are as other commenters have mentioned. If your finals is better than your midterms in terms of percentage then your midterms has no weightage and your finals is 55% instead.
Dreaded this mod quite badly after hearing horror stories of the previous semester. However it seems that Prof Leong has gotten his groove in lecturing + setting the papers for this module (? or whoever sets the papers). Hopefully that will be the same for next semester when Professor Ashish takes this module again.
There are no textbooks, but for those that are entirely new to programming, Codecademy's Python 3 is free (the essential parts). There is more than sufficient practice is given on Coursemology, but HackerRank, Kattis and Leetcode are other suitable platforms that you can try (heard Kattis is used for some other CS mods as well).

author: Justin Tan
message:
AY 22/23 Sem 1 Prof Leong Wai Kay
Assessment:
25% Coursemology (Achieve Level 50 on coursemology through earning xp in missions, lecture exercises, tutorials, extra practices)
5% Participation (1.5% Tutorials and 3.5% Forums?)
15% Midterms
15% Practical Exam
40% Final Exam
Module is fast-paced and has a relatively high workload. Nevertheless, it was an interesting and a very enjoyable module so long as you put in time for consistent practice. Would recommend to do this module with friends, to ask around for help but don't plagiarise lmao. Resources on coursemology are abundant and I was fortunate to have a responsive and helpful TA. If your finals did better than midterms, it can 'overwrite' your midterm results, which was helpful for me.
Tutorials - Once a week (taught by TA)
Recitation - Once a week (revise current week's lecture's content)
My recitation prof, Dr Nitya, was super super helpful and clear in her explanations.
Lectures - Once a week (Hybrid platform by Prof Leong)
Prof Leong is always entertaining and made lectures fun and engaging. However, I would also recommend Prof Ben Leong's CS1010X Lectures released on coursemology as they are quite concised and useful.
Coursemology resources - If you don't procrastinate and actively do your tasks in tandem with the week's lecture content, coursemology will be an enjoyable learning platform. The leaderboard was quite pressurising at the start but it is more of an added gamification feature, not a goal for everyone i guess. It did motivate me to do more practices but I think you shouldn't let it get to your head too much.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Ashish
Tutor: Nigel (Graduating, bye haffun)
Recitation: Karen
Assessment:
25% Coursemology
5% Participation (Tutorials and Forums)
15% Midterms
15% Practical Exam
40% Final Exam
Boy is this mod bonkers. It was also Prof Ashish's first time teaching this module entirely, and I got to say I ain't got much compliments.
The papers set was also tough -- Practical Exam had a median of ~ 7/20 and Finals had a median of ~ 39/90. Not a very nice experience.
Coursemology missions takes up the bulk of your time throughout the semester, just do them before the bonus expires. Side quests are unnecessary unless you really want to hit the leaderboard, just do them if you have way too much time or you are a genius. I always do my lecture and tutorial trainings early to keep myself on track. Overall, doable.
My tutor is graduating so comment is irrelevant.
Recitation -- Karen is open and receptive to questions and appreciates participation, but can work on keeping recitation short and having a better flow in terms of presenting her ideas and teachings.
I was doing ok in the module until PE and finals just decide to kick my ass. Have fun.

author: Unknown
message:
Statistics major here and took this module in Semester 1, AY14/15. This is one of my core mods and the bidpoint for it was quite high (most likely people are thinking to s/u it since it's the gradeless semester and CS has been warned by seniors that it's extremely taxing as it requires consistent effort).
I was very intimidated at first due to the repeated warnings that this mod is crazily hard but as the lectures progresses, I realized that Prof Ben really taught the concepts well. Information are broken down into digestible pieces and as long as your foundations are solid, you can really do higher-level coding which actually is a revisitation of old concepts but with different lenses and applications.
There are 4 main components to this module, namely: Lectures, Tutorials, Recitations and Missions.
[Lectures]
As described above, Prof Ben is committed, dedicated and very passionate. I really enjoyed his lectures a lot as he inject random jokes (sometimes albeit lame but still a pleasant little escape from the content-heavy slides) and shows inspiring/cool videos during breaks. I feel that he is really invested in our learning progress as he keeps motivating us and encourages us. His attitude and passion really made me enjoyed CS lectures immensely (:

author: Nuoyi Jiang
message:
Took the mod in Sem 1, AY20/21
The gamification of this module was generally much better than the other mod that I took which also used the concept of gamification. As a person with little backgrounds in coding, the tutors started from the basic and allowed me to better understand my code. Since this course was purely coding in Python, it was taught much better than R in BT1101 which needed to cover other parts such as data visualisation and statistics etc. However, I would not say I was eased into the process, the learning curve was steep but I guess I just had to deal with it. Generally, the lecturers and tutors are all very nice and engaging. It is a fun mod to take.

author: DrKel
message:
25/26 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Nitya Lakshmanan, Dr Ashish Deepak Dandekar
TA: Ong Keari
Recitation: Dr Ashish
Consistency and hard work are needed to develop the intuition and problem solving skills required to solve questions. Try to dedicate time every week to do lecture training before going for lectures and at least attempt tutorial and recitation questions before going for class so that you are not as lost during lessons (this is an expectation that was not done for many students as it is hard to keep up).
There seems to be an emphasis on recognizing patterns and using a framework of solving subtasks to manage complexity, so practice is much needed (as well for code tracing). Always plan first before writing code! The solution is often quite short and the main issue is mostly the idea and logic. The learning curve is steep, but the most important thing is to not give up! Strongly recommended to attend remedials held before each assessment as they recap the lectures and clarify misconceptions.
TA is goated and the teaching team is dedicated and responsive to questions.

author: blah
message:
AY 22/23 Sem 2
Coursemology - 15%
Tutorial Participation - 5%
Practical Exam - 15%
Mid-terms: 20% (or 0 % progressive scoring)
Finals - 40% (or 60% progressive scoring)
If you're an average student with no prior programming experience, this review is for you. I've never done a mod review before in my 4 years here but I felt compelled to do so here.
I don't regret taking this mod, but there's some things you should know before you take this.
1. Treat this like a 10 MC mod. I spent more time on this than the other 12 MCs combined, and I still did worse for this lol.
2. Coursemology: Make sure you don't fall behind on the deadlines. There's missions, tutorials and trainings. Whatever you do, and as tempting as it may seem, do not plagiarize the missions, they are very strict about it. Complete your trainings and tutorials on time and try to max out the bonuses. Try to do some of the optional side quests early on cos you probs won't have time later in the sem lol (I only did 2 lmao). If you are stuck on a mission, ask in the forum for help, or seek out support from your tutor. They won't be able to give you the answer but they will provide some guidance. If still cannot solve like 1-2 parts, never mind, just submit. Not getting full marks is perfectly fine. It's not efficient use of time to spend hours on one part of a problem. Give it an honest effort and move on. Coursemology is only 15% after all. If you hit level 40ish like me, that's 12%+ already. It's not worth the effort to hit level 50 if you ask me.

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1.
TA: Chang Soo Yen (super dedicated, this sem's head TA!)
You can find reviews of CS1010S everywhere cuz so many people take this mods plus it being so infamous with its heavy coursemology assignment workload. The plus side is that weightage is a guaranteed so long as you hit lvl 50 before finals. The other larger weightage components like midterms and finals are thankfully quite reliable and if you have time to do the revision and work in past practices (provided in loads on coursemo) it's actually quite doable to score in this mod, just counting on the amount of time and dedication you have to this one mod. Definitely takes more than 4MCs of effort especially for those without prior programming experience but it is indeed beginner friendly and the TA support is really really unparalleled with so so so contact time and platform from the forum chat to directly texting your ta. I enjoyed the 1010s tutorials the best thanks to this level of interaction which is for sure hard to find in similar large cohort-sized mods. Having good time management is super impt for this mods as the deadline and bonus cut-offs can quickly snowball very very quickly and actually require you to do extra assignments on coursemo in hitting the lvl 50 requirement, a consideration I didn't think would be true earlier one.
Always look to your TAs for help! The coursemo missions actually by themselves don't require that much time commitment (many can be done within an hour if you understand the problem and know how to troublesome), and the insight is something most TAs are readily available to assist in. Don't plagarise though, besides it being a risky move it just comes with a lot of unnecessary stress, energy than can be better off spent on future missions :D

message:
Context: Year 2 Life Science Major, but relatively okay with math
AY 2021/2022 Semester 1

This module teaches computational thinking, solving problems via abstraction, decomposition, algorithm making, also teaching some aspects of Python and some of its subtleties. author: BeryPhysics
message:
Taken AY19/20 Sem 1
Most of the content, teaching etc. are already covered by the other reviews, so I have some recommendations.
For people with no prior programming experience, and are planning to take this mod:
1. Try out the Python 2 course on codecademy (https://www.codecademy.com/.... There may be some differences in syntax compared to the Python 3 the mod will be taught in, but at least get some practice with the basic operators, functions, how to define variables and functions etc. Those stuff are thrown at you at the start and they kinda expect you to know alr by week 2, and most unprepared people struggle with the mod because of that learning curve at the start, which snowballs from there on.
2. Practice a lot, that's actually the only way to learn the computational thinking. There are quite a bit of optional exercises on coursemology (the online platform we'll be using), as well as past year papers with their solutions on it. Also play around with the stuff introduced in lectures on your own to see how they work.
3.One thing I see tripping a lot of students is the mindset that they get intimidated by a lot of the cheem-sounding concepts just because they haven't learnt programming before. So never think that "i'll never get this, I never learnt programming before". It WILL come with practice eventually - after all, we all had to start somewhere! Quite a portion of our top scorers are people with no prior experience, in fact.

author: Jonathan Chang
message:
AY18/19 SEM 1, Prof Leong Wai Kay
TBH, had no idea what I was doing for the entire time, gotten a C+ for this mod, and I scored the following:
Mids: 32/75
Practical Exam: 14/30
Finals: 39/100
This module takes a lot of effort to digest and understand, lectures go at a super fast pace, seems like they're always covering endless amount of content. If you have a choice whether or not to take this mod, DON'T, the workload is crazy and honestly, there's really better things to do than cracking your head over missions or assignments, especially for people with CCAs/ halls/ other commitments. Well, at least I passed.

author: Owen
message:
AY2018/19 Sem 1 Dr LEONG Wai Kay
I think it's important to preface this review with a disclaimer that I really think you should take this module, but it won't be easy.
Module difficulty/contents: Module is definitely tough for people new to code. New topics are introduced every week, and it is important to be very sure of last week's content to understand the current week's, so it is important to keep your pace. This is where Coursemology (NUS SoC website) comes in as it helps you train and practice the topics introduced in class.
Workload: There's almost a Mission (something like an assignment) every week, together with tutorial and lecture quizzes. Let's just say that some people spent more time on CS1010S than their other modules of the semester combined. If you are new to coding, do not overload; if you aren't, don't be surprised to spend quite a bit more time on this module than what you expect. Tutorials are compulsory and not interchangeable, as points are awarded for attendance and participation during the sessions.
Grade weightage:
25% Coursemology, in which majority will score >23%
15% Practical Exam
15% Midterms
5% Participation
40% Finals

author: Thiru
message:
I took this mod in Semester 1, AY 14/15, taught by Prof Ben.
[TLDR at the bottom]
To be extremely candid, this is a killer mod. I was struggling intensely from the beginning, and spent over 90% of my time on this one mod alone.....
......But it was extremely worth it.
[Overview] 1010S provides a solid foundation for those keen on learning the basics of programming methodology (Important: methodology =/= learning programming languages. It's the underlying concepts that are important), and its taught via a simple and very useful language; Python. Content wise, you will learn a number of concepts like recursion, iteration, common data structures such as lists and tuples, and how to manipulate them, Object Oriented Programming, etc. However, that's not the point of this module. As prof Ben repeatedly emphasizes, the aim of the module is aimed at teaching you how to manage complexity, and how to think, not on how to program. This mod is taught is a I feel that it's one of the most useful module's that you'll ever take, as it provides a solid foundation for any subsequent programming language you wish to pick up. Especially for BA majors, python is an incredibly useful language for data analysis, and plus, this module teaches you about 80% of CS1020, and you'll have a much easier time ahead! This mod is hard, but remember, DONT GIVE UP. Keep struggling and eventually you will find that 'Aha!' moment, so never, never give up.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Cynthia Ho Sin Tian.
I agree with Yang Shun. But because of the hard work that you put in, you will learn the art of programming along the journey. It will also gives you a sense of satisfaction as the module is designed using gamification.
Warning: It won't be easy.

author: small monkey
message:
AY 22/23 Sem 2
TA: Yu Cai
Assessments
Coursemology - 15%
Tutorial Participation - 5%
Practical Exam - 15%
Mid-terms: 20% (or 0 % progressive scoring)
Finals - 40% (or 60% progressive scoring)
Sentiments
Overall, this module was enjoyable as I was incentivised by coursemology - the need to finish missions to get points and level up. The concept sounds corny but it made me quite determined.
Progressive Scoring
TLDR, if you screw up your mid-terms and score better for finals, your finals score will override mid-terms. This (however) did not work out in my favour because finals appeared to be very difficult for me. I've been to about 90% of the tutorials, midterms and practicals scored below median, coursemology level 50 (100%). The only way to get an A- would be to score near full marks for finals which is impossible (those that took this mod in this sem, qn 2 was disgusting). I managed to do ALL the past year papers provided but even that did not prepare me for qn 2 which was worth 24 marks.

author: small monkey
message:
Grade distribution
Coursemology - 15/15 (15%)
Finals - 76/100 // upper quartile 73/100 (60%)
Mid terms - 45.6/60 // upper quartile 45.6/60 (0%)
Participation - 4/5 (5%)
Practical - 11.5/20 // upper quartile 15/20 (20%)
Total percentage - 15 + 45.6 + 4 + 11.5 = 76.1%
Final Grade: B+
Final thoughts
Progressive scoring saved my ass from getting an undesirable grade. I did not practise much for midterms. I did approximately 4 days worth of practice for the practical exam but after the exam session, I realised I did not practise properly by understanding the code and what it achieves. For finals, I did 13 practise papers during reading week and despite my initial fear of producing garbage code for the paper, I did decently well. My advise for future students taking this modules is to spam all the practice papers after understanding the basic syntax, logic and progression of the python code. If my practical and participation scored just slightly higher, I could have possibly gotten that A-. Overall, I still enjoyed the module :)

author: prawira satya darma
message:
Semester 1, AY19/20
Professor Ben Leong and Leong Wai Kay
Grading:
- Participation (Forums and Tutorials) 5%
- Coursemology 25%
- Midterm Exam 15%
- Practical Exam 15%
- Final Exam 40%
This module is a good and interesting introduction to programming module. It's said to be the hardest among other 1010 modules. Profs really did a good job in explaining the materials so attend the lectures if you could. Coursemology provides tons of materials and missions (main problems to be completed) for you to try and if you managed to do all the available materials before the others, the platform will unlock further materials so you could learn faster than other students and on your own pace. The tutorials are really useful and since attendance is marked you should come to the tutorials. The recitations cover materials which are needed to deepen your understanding on materials in the lecture since sometimes the lectures are too short and haven't covered such materials.
If you feel that you haven't done a good job until Midterm, don't lose hope and keep learning because it happened to me and I still could score a good grade.
Expected grade = A
Actual grade = A

author: Bao Dinh
message:
AY18/19 Sem 1, Prof Leong Wai Kay, Dr. Zhou Lifeng
Topics include: basics if/else and loop, functions, higher-order functions, tuples & data abstraction, working with sequences, lists, searching and sorting (mostly tested on application), data structures (important: Dictionary, less important: Set, Stack, Binary Tree), and basic OOP.
Very heavy workload, but the content is absolutely helpful if you want to take higher CS modules. If you are a DSA/BZA student, surely you should spent more time on this module compared to the other Science peers.
I personally feel that Missions are quite badly set in a way that only test our reading comprehension skills (some questions are quite ambiguous which I spent 15-30 mins understanding what they want me to do and then 1 min to write the code out). Tutorial participation is critical; doing tutorial problems well would help a lot for your exams.
Try to plan well for your missions so that you can reach Level 50 ASAP so that you can focus your time on reviewing for finals instead. Doing all missions (not necessarily all correct) + some side quests can get you Level 50 in Week 11/12. Contests are very interesting but I don't have much time to do them. If you already learnt all stuff in advance then CS1010S' Contest is for you to show off your skills.

author: Joyce Yeo
message:
I took this module in AY17/18 Semester 1.
Like what grace mentioned below, the bid points for this module are quite high. It was about 300+ for me, but every point was worth it.
> No programming experience is needed.
To add in my two cents about the previous reviews, I feel that this module is catered to students who have no programming experience. Recognise that there will always be smarter people out there. Instead of complaining about their strong programming, take the chance to learn from them.
> Diligently follow the lectures.
The lecture slides are very useful for me. Aesthetically pleasing, easy on the eyes to read and something that you would actually read. When reading the slides, don't be too fixated on the code. Rather, try to understand the concepts and ideas being taught. The prof explains things very clearly, and lectures are lighthearted - short clips will be shown during the break. Granted, some of the topics can be very frustrating to work with, but the good thing is that the prof gave us an "expected difficulty curve" so we know which weeks are supposed to be tougher and don't beat ourselves up over it.
