author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1A (Week 1 – 6) Course Outline: Week 1: Developing self-awareness and a personal brand Week 2: Knowing what’s out there: Industry Insights Week 3: Your roadmap towards career readiness Week 4: Effective resumes and cover letters Week 5: Ace the interview Week 6: Networking and professional presentation Bonus Video: Maximise your internship Assessments: Elevator Pitch on VMock (Min. score 3/5) Resume on VMock (Min. score 70/100) Quiz (Min. score 16/20, best of 3 attempts) First 3 weeks are F2F and next 3 weeks on your own time. Pitch and resume are graded by AI on VMock. It will recommend changes and you just need to follow and re-upload. My suggestion is to keep the pitch and resume simple and the minimum score should be easily attainable. The quiz is done at your own time and the answers can be found in the slides and e-learning content (or just common sense). I read through the contents quickly and completed everything within an afternoon. Apart from these, they also introduced resources available in school for career support, including career office, advisors, workshops, events, networking etc. It will be beneficial to note them down and leverage on the support provided by the school. Final Grade: CS

author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1A (Week 1 – 6) Tutor: Shan-Rievan Salleh Lesson 1 – Foundations of Effective Communication · Fundamental elements and mechanisms of interpersonal communication · The importance of storytelling · The use of empathy in communication Lesson 2 – Dealing with Others in Communication · Fundamental concepts of the psychology of influence · Explore the strategies for planning a persuasive approach · Acquire essential skills in questioning and articulating explanations Lesson 3 – Building Relationships in Communication · Gain insight into the proficiency of self-disclosure and active listening as a means to cultivate relationships · Comprehend empathetic listening and its mechanisms · Analyze the challenges encountered in intercultural communication and strategies for enhancing cultural intelligence Assessments: Punctuality & Class Participation (70%) 3 x After Class Assignments/Reflections, 10% each (30%) Missing a session without doing a make-up session results in a CU grade Classes are done in seminar style, focused on learning, discussing and practicing communication techniques. Discussions are in groups, sharing personal experiences/stories using communication frameworks and case studies. Be expected to share ideas with the class. Assignments are reflections on personal experience in day-to-day communication in different perspectives (minimum 300 words each). Final Grade: CS

author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1 Lecturer: Professor Lee Soo Teck The lecture slots are used as problem solving sessions, so students are expected to review the lecture content (watched pre-recorded videos or at least read) before coming to class (flipped learning format). Problem sessions will not be recorded, but the slides on the discussion of solutions will be uploaded to Canvas after the class. Coming from poly and was exempted for MA1301, I took this module in Y1S1. The learning curve was steep and it takes time with effort to comprehend the material. Prof Lee explains the concepts well, so it is recommended to attend his lectures (I attended all lectures). I was behind schedule so many of the sessions ended up being a mass copy session for me as I did not review the content before class (I had to catch up afterwards). It was a costly mistake and my work snowballed until before finals, leaving me less time for proper revision. Consistency is required to do well in this module as time and practice are needed to cultivate intuition and formalize the style of writing proofs (assuming no experience). The best way to prepare for midterm and finals is to revise the lecture notes and review the questions discussed in the problem sessions as well as attempt sample / past year papers provided. The test questions were manageable in terms of difficulty (no big brain gotcha questions) but students need to be careful of time management. I thought I clutched for finals but the bell curve was very steep (math students are generally more driven), estimated score 80-85 for finals. Prof said there is no bell curve so it might just be a skewed moderation with a manageable paper. Overall, it was a comprehensive introduction to university math which was way different from the computational math I am used to before. Workload is high for a typical 1k module but it sets the expectations of taking math modules. References: Textbook: Tamara J. Lakins, The Tools of Mathematical Reasoning (Available on NUS Libraries website) Susanna S. Epp, Discrete mathematics with applications. Rbjt Allenby, Numbers & Proofs. Assessments [Score | Mean | LQ / Median / UQ]: Class Participation (15%) [15/15] - Submit your solution for problem solving sessions, graded based on reasonable attempt / effort, only need do it 15 times out of all lectures in the semester (1% each) Midterm (30%) [65/80 | 53.13 | 45 / 54 / 62] Final Exam (55%) Expected Grade: A Final Grade: A-

author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1 Tutor: Dr Clay Eaton The lecture (online, recorded) starts with a quick sharing of recent news and trying to relate to course themes before diving into the lecture content. The structure is something like what do we take for granted, introduction, deep dive to look into an issue, then interconnection to draw common insights from different events / regions (I treat this as scoping, looking at specifics first then generalize). First tutorial was chill, started with self-introduction and ice-breakers. Had to bring an object I see as ‘Asian’, most people brought common household / sentimental items (I brought a teapot which is probably overkill). Afterwards, there was a discussion on the construct of Asia and a short writing exercise. For tutorials 2 onwards, a group will start by giving a short presentation, facilitate discussion, and summary for about 20 minutes before my tutor continues the class. There are 4 groups in a class, so 1 group will take 1 tutorial. Groups are randomly assigned and just hope that you get this out of the way earlier as the earlier readings are easier and you are not as busy early on. My group got the one on ‘When Mr. Pirzada came to dine’ (tutorial 3) which was really difficult for me to understand (and relating to course themes) as I am not comfortable with narratives. In three tutorials, there will be a short in-class writing exercise on your opinion to a question (open-ended), so it is essential to keep up with the content. I found this to be really difficult as I was overthinking about how to jam pack the content with well-rounded explanations, but it is not necessary as it is quite an informal thing. Being concise and clear with structured writing (your point/stand with example and reference to course readings/themes then conclusion) is more important. Less is more. For assignments, part A and B were linked to each other. I think it was different from past iterations. Part A was on research methodology, choosing a region and recent event (within the past 3.5 months) in Asia (beyond Singapore) and giving three supporting sources with explanation why they were the most relevant and reliable sources. Part B was relating to course themes and making an interconnection between the event in part A and another event that happened in another region in Asia (beyond Singapore). It is strongly recommended to read through both parts before starting on part A so that you have an easier time in part B. The neighbourhood video theme is “Challenging Assumptions about (your neighbourhood)”. The primary focus is to inform, engaging the audience Is secondary, less about the aesthetics or quality of equipment. Need to connect each location to the video theme and one of the course themes (History & Language, The Environment, Creating Social Categories, and Social Inequalities). Workload is quite high with somewhat tedious lecture and tutorial readings (mix of narrative and academic papers), but it is already a less fluff module you get from CHS where putting in effort gives a higher chance of doing well. I want to shout out to Dr Eaton for making this module much more bearable with the engaging lectures and tutorials. I struggled a lot for class participation and group work and was going to triple S/U the CHS mods (DTK/HSS/HSA) but I decided to put in some effort and not give up (also there was group work and I did not want to be a complete liability). My english is also dogwater. I guess finding some purpose in learning helps, for example in this module the construct of Asia was discussed; is it just a region we live in or is there more meaning to it (interesting that Turkey is transcontinental)? or when studying about farmers in rural India we could think of social inequalities where the rich get to leverage in technology and the poor get poorer; or during the case study of ‘malayness’ we could also question what we take for granted for our identity and religion? or cases of authority-defined reality where some ‘facts’ and ‘narratives’ can be intentional with other ‘less important’ details hidden? Assessments: Tutorial Participation (15%) Group Tutorial Presentation (15%) 3 x In-Class Writing Exercises (T1/T3/T4, Closed book, 10%) - Grade: Needs work / Good / Good (No score given) Individual Assignment (Part A 15% due week 5, Part B 15% due week 10, 30%) - Part A (78/100), Part B (81/100), stats not shown Group Neighbourhood Video (5 minutes, with script and reference list, due week 13, 30%) * Peer review required for presentation and video Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: A-

author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1 Tutor: Ian Tan This module teaches design ‘thinking’, try to place your mindset towards learning the principles / thoughts behind design and less about ‘actual’ design. It will be less daunting. ILA = Individual Learning Activity, TBW = Team Based Workshop (Tutorials) ILA/TBW 1: Intro to Design Thinking ILA/TBW 2: Empathize ILA/TBW 3: Systematic Creativity ILA/TBW 4: Do, Undo, Redo ILA/TBW 5 (Wrap-up): Thinking about Design Thinking The schedule runs in blocks of 2 weeks. First ILA in weeks 1 and 2 and attend TBW 1 in week 3 or 4 while working on the next ILA at the same time (and so on). There are some short videos to watch for the ILAs and I recommend watching them to get an idea of what is required for the ILAs. They allowed resubmission for ILA 1 to 4 in around week 10 so can submit something not so good early on then improve later (to earn the completion marks). For the wrap-up ILA, I reflected on a memorable moment in DTK and in the second part I did a case study in the real world (or you can teach a design thinking mindset). Required to relate this to your field of study / discipline. No grades were revealed throughout the semester. My tutor gave general feedback during the TBWs and was open to individual feedback if asked (I did not). His tutorials were chill and I appreciate that he does not force people to speak up. He used a Miro board to track our progress where I uploaded photos after every TBW. I did not expect a good grade as I kept thinking that I am doomed in a design course and was on the verge of giving up as the ILAs required huge effort. Assessments: Submit ILA on time (ILA 1-4, 5% x 4 = 20%) ILA Quality (ILA 1-4, 5% x 4 = 20%) Wrap-up ILA (25%) Team Based Workshops (Tutorial) (7% x 5 = 35%) Expected Grade: B Final Grade: A-

author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1 Assessment: Completion of online lesson package (100%) From this sem onwards, it is no longer a pre/co-requisite with CFG1004, so you can skip this if you do not need an introduction. I spent some time reading through the package and additional materials provided such as considerations when evaluating financial advisors, budgeting spreadsheet, and money narratives. They are optional but I found it beneficial for perspective taking and knowledge. Contents 1. Financial wellbeing 2. Disruption and the flourishing life 3. Financial emotional resilience 4. What is your money behaviour like today? 5. The negative and positive money narratives 6. The 8 forms of capital 7. Money management principles 8. Money management: 5 pillars of money 9. Why budget? 10. My current budget 11. Three life hacks for a great budget 12. Putting it all together 13. Budgeting for the future 14. Final thoughts and future considerations 15. What is CPF (Supplementary) 16. What is Insurance (Supplementary) Final Grade: CS

author: DrKel
message: 25/26 Sem 1 Tutor: Yu Depeng This module is about looking at crisis (social issues) using different disciplines such as geography, sociology, psychology, economics etc. Some methodological frameworks (systems approach, outcome, differential impact and power) were introduced to help examine the crisis and to write in the essay. The idea is there, but the grading is nonsense. Forum posting was just submitting a question based on the content/webinar for the week and graded by the tutor, there was no discussion involved and no feedback given on your question (there was class feedback for the first few but did not help). The grading was strict at the start but relaxed after midterms. The stress was really unnecessary as I only clutched at the last 4 attempts. I have no idea what constitutes a good question in their books other than something like ‘To what extent can we…… without marginalizing/exacerbating… something’ and ‘How can we reimagine/reshape/reform/rethink …whatever, especially when …whatever’. My essay outline was promising, just to get screwed at the final essay. My tutor gave the same word-for-word comment for students with the same score, so I do not know what was particularly bad about the essay (questionable if he graded my work seriously). Group presentation was meh, feedback was the content seems kind of disjoint across the members and interdisciplinary can be elaborated further for the proposed solution (future crisis). Tutor asked a question about navigating power dynamics with stakeholders involved. Overall a discouraging module, I skipped all assigned readings and did not participate much in tutorials, sometimes watched the budget podcast (weekly online webinar), thinking of how I can come up with a big brain gigachad question (or you can just ask chat). The assessments seriously need to be restructured to improve learning and reward mastery of content instead of facing uncertainty and ambiguity. I could not convince myself to put in effort for this module, hence the worst grade I got for this sem. Take my words with a grain of salt and ymmv. Assessments [Score | Mean | LQ / Median / UQ]: Forum Posting (best 4 out of 7 weeks, 6% each, 24%) [24/24] Class Participation (Tutorials 1-4, 16%) Essay Outline (10%) [8/10 | 6.76 | 6/7/7.5] Final Essay (15%) [10.5/15 | 11.02 | 10.5/10.5/12.5] Group Abstract (500 words, 10%) 10-minute Group Presentation (20% group, 5% individual, 25%) Expected Grade: B Final Grade: B+ (S/U)

author: Owen
message: AY2020/2021 S2 (Partial Online Sem) ZHANG Chun Module difficulty/contents: The introduction of the module is a wild ride, taking you from quantum physics, numerical analysis and complex analysis. For each chapter you can practically refer to the relevant chapters in their associated NUS modules (PC2130, MA2213, MA3111). Thus the introduction is rough, but it is not expected for students to have a deep understanding of the module; questions regarding these topics were not too technical and were approachable. The rest of the module deals with Hartree-Fock methods and then Density Functional Theory, essential models used for molecular modelling and simulations in the current age. Fundamental examples were covered but nothing too recent was looked at. Workload: Light. The lectures were pre-recorded and weekly tutorials could be skipped. Assessments did not require too much time to prepare for. Grade Breakdown: 50% Midterms 50% Review of 2 papers Skip Lecture?: NIL, all were recorded. The live consultation sessions were helpful if you had questions regarding lecture content. Additional comments: Prof Zhang knows his stuff and is rather approachable when seeking his help. I suppose the mode of teaching makes me feel less for the module. Additionally, I didn't need to study after midterms as the final assessment of paper review did not require much lecture material, although your mileage will definitely vary if the module went back to having final exams. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2020/2021 S1 (Full Online Sem) Cristina CARBUNARU Module difficulty/contents: This module is difficult, no doubt. The contents consist of Processes, Memory, and Storage, which can be pre-studied through the OSTEP online textbook. The difficulty of this module arises not really from the content, but that the design decisions made for the implementation of each component also need to be understood to achieve a decent grade for the module. As a result, some of the questions ask if a certain implementation taught in the lecture was done differently, or if various methods are combined together, etc. Another difficulty arises during lab sessions, since they involve using POSIX library calls written in C, which are generally daunting for students. Knowing how to read and understand UNIX man pages for the system calls is important, but because UNIX is quite advanced the system calls also contain advanced options, which make things more confusing. However, even if you know how to use the man pages, finding the correct function to use is also another skill needed for the module. I am incredibly grateful for both my lab and tutorial TAs (Bernard and Hao Wei) who were approachable and answered my questions about some quirks of the system calls. Additionally, taking this mod with friends will also make things easier, since you can learn from each other's mistakes in the lab assignment. The midterms and exams were a mix of MCQ and SAQ. Workload: Very heavy. It is incredibly important to keep up with the module every week, as some weeks build up on previous weeks. Additionally, the lab assignments never stop (i.e. the due date for an assignment is followed by the opening of another in the same week), and there is a need to present your solution (to the first question of the lab, usually a simple problem) to the lab TA, to ensure that you are on the right track. I rarely finished a lab more than a week before the due date of the lab assignment. Tutorials are also graded, as there are lecture quizzes after the tutorial recapping the concepts introduced in the lecture and addressed in the tutorial. Grade Breakdown: 10% Tutorial Quizzes (11 choose 10) 35% Lab Assignments (x5) 15% Midterms 40% Exam Skip Lecture?: Nope, see module difficulty. Additional comments: Prof Cristina is really approachable and teaches quite well, and tries to make the online lectures interactive. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A+

author: Owen
message: AY2020/2021 S1 (Full Online Sem) ZHOU Lifeng and Roger ZIMMERMANN Module difficulty/contents: Module introduces the five layers of networking, as well as some applications of networks, including multimedia networking as well as security. Overall, content was not difficult, but there was plenty to take in. Hence, as commented by many, the bell curve for the module is steep, due to the overall simplicity of the module. The most difficult questions were the calculations of packet delays as well as the routing algorithms. Additionally, it will be good to notice if the questions ask for bits or bytes, which is a trap I fell for again and again. Workload: Light. There are four assignments (three programming related, while one requires you to play around with multimedia and answer the prof's questions). They are generally manageable, not requiring more than a day to work through them. It will be helpful to have a good grasp of the lecture content and concepts as the programming assignments were mainly implementing the protocols introduced in the lecture. I skipped the weekly tutorials, hence unable to comment on how the tutorials affected the workload. It did not seem essential to catch up to the tutorials every week. Grade Breakdown: 25% Assignments (3 + 1) 25% Midterms (MCQ + a few SAQ) 50% Exam (MCQ + a few SAQ) Skip Lecture?: Yes, all were recorded. There were some live consultation sessions but I did not go for them. However, the notes given are not complete, hence it is essential to at least listen to the recordings. Additional comments: The lecturers like phrasing questions with slightly weird phrasing, leading to students asking loads of questions during the online exam. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: B+

author: Owen
message: AY2020/2021 S1 (Full Online Sem) Edith CHAN & ZHANG Sheng Module difficulty/contents:Module builds upon some knowledge of CM2111 and CM2121 but not necessary to understand the experiment and write the report. Additionally, there were only one real-life experiment conducted for both the organic and inorganic component, although both had two sessions each. Hence, not much laboratory techniques were introduced. Workload: Light, fortunately, because of the online semester. Lab sessions were restricted and thus only one report per experiment for a total of two reports. Some online assignments were introduced as virtual experiments were conducted. The lecturers would film themselves conducting the experiment and ask some related questions (on both scientific concepts and lab techniques) on an untimed LumiNUS quiz. The online exam was conducted with a similar scope of questions, but timed. There was also a need to prepare risk assessments for the lab sessions which should not take too much time. Grade Breakdown: Inorganic 5% Risk Assessment 20% Short Report 10% Online Assignment 15% Online Exam Organic 5% Risk Assessment 20% Long Report 5% Library Workshop (compulsory lecture attendance) 20% Online Exam Skip Lecture?: There were hardly any lectures due to the online semester. Even so, I skipped most lectures since they were held at 8am. Additionally, it did not seem like more content was covered other than what was already given in the notes/lab manual. Additional comments: Dr Edith is an extremely meticulous person and will give detailed feedback. However, she is also very strict in terms of submission standards, so do follow her guidelines accordingly. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A
thread_title: CM3291 Advanced Experiments In Organic & Inorganic Chemistry

author: Owen
message: AY2020/2021 S1 (Full Online Sem) CHI Chunyan & TAN Zhi Kuang Module difficulty/contents: The first half of the module details the various reactions and synthesis schemes for polymers, as taught by Prof Chi. Knowledge of organic chemistry mechanisms will be needed for this portion, although the mechanisms tested do not require much advanced knowledge, and in fact maybe only requires CM1121 content. The second half introduces the various analytical techniques used to characterise polymers, and here a lot of physical chemistry is used. Some CM1131 knowledge on thermodynamics will be useful, but a large portion involves more advanced thermodynamics and hence is generally new content. If you find equations scary, then this portion of the module will be daunting. Workload: Light. In this semester, there were only a few tutorial sessions held as content from multiple lectures were consolidated together. Thus there were multiple weeks where there were no tutorials. Preparation was necessary for the quizzes, but could be done over a few nights instead of constant attention throughout the semester. Additionally, once the first half of the module was done, there was no need to revisit the content again in the later half. Contrary to the review below, for this semester, it seemed that Prof Chi set a much tougher paper (with a tight duration) of mechanism drawing and some calculations, while Prof Tan's assessments were MCQ based and thus had a steep curve. Grade Breakdown: 20% Quiz 1 (Prof Chi) 30% Quiz 2 (Prof Chi) 20% Quiz 3 (Prof Tan) 30% Quiz 4 (Prof Tan) Skip Lecture?: Depends, I found the notes provided by both lecturers to be essentially complete and comprehensive. Prof Chi had live lectures while Prof Tan pre-recorded his. Additional comments: Both Prof Chi and Prof Tan are very nice and caring professors, and are easy to approach. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2020/2021 S1 (Full Online Sem) ZHAO Jin & Martin HENZ Important: Form a team of three, and hope that many other friends are taking the mod too, as your group of three will be randomly matched to another group of three (it was not important to pick the same timeslot in this semester). Module content did not change much from the previous review. Module Difficulty: To be accurate, this is a weird amalgamation of CS2103 for the SE principles as well as CS2040 for the efficiency component. Hence the module focuses a lot on design of the product as well as testing to ensure that you have covered all necessary bases. Workload: This module is extremely notorious for having a heavy workload, comparable to 16MCs. You will need to pick up C++ as well as its quirks for software engineering (SE), the definitions and requirements of the project as well as writing an over 100 pages report. Not to mention actually coming up with the product itself. For the product, there is a need to ensure that the right person fits the responsibility given to them for the project, as it is extremely unlikely that there will be time near the submission date to help patch up other components. However, it might be good discuss design decisions at the start, so that components are built efficiently and effectively. Additionally, this allows everyone to have an idea of the whole product as the knowledge is needed for Test 2. The implementation of the product is split into three iterations, where the first is relatively trivial (but lays the foundations for additions to the product, as required for the final implementation). All cohesion problems within the group should ideally be ironed out by this date. The second iteration does not have much workload, as you will only need to demo your product and address responses to your report. The final iteration carries the heaviest weightage, in which you will need to implement all advanced requirements, optimise your product and work on extensions. Lectures and tutorials are all geared towards helping you work out the details for your product and hence do not bear additional workload. Tests require additional preparation for, because you (instead of your product!) have to evaluate queries. A take home test is done for Test 1, and hence is relatively straightforward (thus the low weightage). However, Test 2 is a nightmare, with three separate sections done consecutively under stiflingly tight time limits. This is painful also because the queries are more complicated in nature (part 1), an essay-based question is included for your component of choice (part 2), and that you need to work together with your groupmates to answer a final design and API discovery question (part 3). Grade Breakdown: 7% Lecture Attendance + Participation + Quizzes (on LumiNUS) 10% Consultation Attendance + Participation 3% Test 1 (on project definitions and evaluation of queries) 15% Test 2 (timed) 20% Iteration 1 (Code + Report) 5% Iteration 2 (Report + Demo) 40% Iteration 3 (Code + Report + Presentation) Skip Lecture?: No, as attendance marks are given. Additionally, some helpful advice is given for product implementation details. Additional comments: This module has an unhealthy focus on grading on a curve; do not believe the lecturers when they say that the module is not bell-curved, as individual components of the Iteration are bell-curved, even your mistakes in the product as well as your report. Effectively, this makes the whole module bell-curved as well, even if the grade is not done so. It will also be good to match expectations with your TA for consults, as both CS2103 and CS3203 have an over-reliance on TAs who may not know the specifics of grading or what is needed to meet the lecturer's expectations. Finally, contrary to expectations, although Dr Zhao is quick at responding to queries, he does so with elusive and evasive answers, avoiding questions that can help illuminate or clarify concepts that students experience while coding the product. Prof Henz was also not really involved in the module and only showed up for lectures and sometimes consults. Hence, unfortunately, you may have to expect help from the poor head TAs.

author: Owen
message: AY2019/2020 S2 (COVID Sem) Goki EDA & CHUAH Gaik Khuan Module difficulty/contents: Lots of things to memorise. My summary notes for this module spanned 17 pages; you'll get exposed to a new topic every week, so do be prepared. Some calculation is needed as there is some physical/quantum chemistry in terms of crystal orbitals; ensure that you understand what each equation does, as the form presented in the equations list for assessments contain no explanations for symbols nor helpful abbreviations/markings. The first part of the module had more focus on the physical, quantum side of things, as taught by Prof Eda. He is a very knowledgeable person and it shows through his lectures; apparently the previous year his slides were too technical so there was some dumbing down this iteration. However he manages to showcase the technicality and complexity of material properties without going too deep into the equations. The second part was more focused on the inorganic side of things, so you'll need to memorise crystal structures and material compositions etc (other than a whole chapter on phase diagrams that might be a bit scary, but can be overcome with rote learning and memorisation). This was taught by Prof Chuah. She is nice in the sense that she goes around the LT during breaks to check in on students, and ensures that everyone understands her during tutorials. Workload: Light, as you will only need to complete tutorials every week which are mostly manageable - not much thinking is needed to solve them. The main workload comes in when studying for midterms/finals, since a large amount of content needs to be memorised. Grade Breakdown: 40% Midterms (x2) 60% Finals Skip Lecture?: Depends; live lectures cover content well, and include media other than lecture slides to deepen your understanding. Additionally, questions are worked live in lectures which does help. However, when I missed a lecture, the webcast adequately covers the content. Additional comments: This was the first chemistry module I took that I actually enjoyed, as it shows how materials manifest in real life and is a very practical application of our knowledge (compared to the mainly theoretical concepts introduced in compulsory foundational modules). I do recommend this if you are majoring in chemistry, as the materials industry is ultimately a growing industry. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2019/2020 S2 (COVID Sem) Akshay NARAYAN Important: Form a team and hope that you all get the same timeslot. Even one other friend together in the same team is good enough. I was blessed that though the majority of my team was randomised, we all worked very well together and the journey was smooth and enjoyable. Module Difficulty/Contents: This module is notorious for the creation of your own java software, together with the difficulty of handling a team. Ensure good delegation of tasks and project management - someone needs to be the visionary and help brainstorm ideas for the project, while another role is to debug and ensure that the application is free from trivial bugs. In terms of contents taught, not much is very applicable actually. It is generally okay to skip the lecture contents and focus more on what features you need to build for your project. Workload: Heavy. Every week you have something to code, even if it's recess week or week 1. Ensure that you adhere to the standards specified in the website and check in with the bot if there's something you missed - there's a heavy penalty for this. For the individual project, it can be thought of as a CS2030 lab assignment every week. Somewhat heavy, but the end goal is there and you have a straightforward path to it. Not too difficult. Starting from week 5 or 6 you'll start getting into the team project. Here there's more to be done, as you'll need to think of the application's features and development as well as UI and storage. The workload is dependent on the goal you assign yourself to, as well as the technical complexity of your feature. However, it wasn't as heavy as I expected, and with great foresight you can finish your feature in recess week with another burst a few weeks after that. Tutorials generally require minimal effort, as the bulk of it is usually spent discussing with your group on your team project. Grade Breakdown: This changes every year. Check the module website for more details. 5% Lecture Quiz 10% Individual Project 65% Team Project (was 45%) 20% Final (was 40%) Skip Lecture?: I don't recommend it, as lecture time is also another avenue where you can hang out with your teammates and sort out any issues with the project. However, there is nothing to be gleamed from the lectures. Additional comments: Find reliable teammates, hope that you get someone with good UI skills, and keep up with work so you don't drown in it during weeks 11 ish when you have to submit your team project! Expected Grade: A-/A Actual Grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2019/2020 S2 (COVID Sem) WONG Yean Ling & CHAN Yiu Man Module Difficulty/Contents: Easy. Most of it is already covered before we came to NUS, so some attention is only needed in the later half regarding statistical tests and estimations. Note that the students taking this class are mostly from SoC, not actual math/stats majors. Workload: Almost none. There was supposed to be a midterm, but that got converted to LumiNUS Quizzes held online. With a few reads of your lecture notes, they are definitely doable. Grade Breakdown: 30% Midterms Online Quizzes (x3) 70% Finals Skip lecture?: Yes! The notes are comprehensive and easy to understand. I did not attend them since the start of the semester, even before lectures got pushed online. I was taught by Ms Wong who seems nice for the only 30min I spent in physical lectures. Additional comments: Administration of this module is terrible, especially for finals, since the instructions for the next day's MORNING exam was given the PREVIOUS NIGHT, with only around twelve hours to prepare. The online final was too lengthy and hence most did not complete it; do budget your time wisely if this happens again. Expected Grade: A-/A Actual Grade: A+

author: Owen
message:
AY2019/2020 Sem 1 WANG Fei & Victor TAN

Module difficulty/contents: Easy if you have the background. More attention may be needed sporadically throughout the module in terms of technicalities of definitions as well as deeper concepts. That said, if you have no background, you'll probably need to struggle as there is new content every week that builds upon the previous. 

Workload: Almost none - you can afford to cram everything during midterms and finals. However, there are three homework assignments for you to submit, which are generally doable and require several hours (especially for the workings). 

Some preparation is needed for the MATLAB test, which is probably the most useless portion of the module as it is not needed anywhere else. The Midterm test and finals had multiple practice papers to work on, hence some time should be devoted to these. 

Grade Weightage:
12% Homework Assignments (x3)
8% MATLAB quiz
20% Midterm
60% Finals

Skip lecture?: Yes - notes are extremely comprehensive and detailed, but lecturers are good if you want to drop in and listen. They sometimes go through past year papers as well. 

Additional comments: Dr Wang Fei is a really good lecturer; I previously had him for Calculus and I was glad to take another mod taught by him again. Also, use Wolfram Alpha for your homework, it will work wonders!

Expected Grade: A-
Actual Grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2019/2020 S1 SOO Yuen Jien (Uncle Soo) Module Difficulty/Contents: There are a lot of things to grasp here. Be prepared to learn some C by yourself, but there's not much need to use it. MIPS (using QtSpim) is featured more, especially at the labs, but you will need to understand how to convert MIPS to C and vice-versa for other written assessments. Concepts wise, you can imagine learning a somewhat unrelated and new topic every week, which is difficult and not easy to process. My cheat sheet for this module is the longest out of all other CS mods that I have taken (as of Y2S2). Workload: Moderately heavy. The tutorials are 1h every week. The questions are doable but still take some time to finish; even then, the concepts taught still require a bit of time to digest. The weekly lab sessions do not last for one hour - usually, you can complete these at home, then show them to your tutor in session for the mark, and you can leave within ten minutes. On the later half of the semester, you'll be playing with wires and chips (which is really fun!) but will take slightly longer to complete (and if you don't prepare, will probably take longer than one hour). Additionally, if you do not keep up with content, it is very easy to get lost (even missing one lecture is a great cost - note that it's split to a 2+1h lecture). Prioritise this in terms of weekly upkeep to stay afloat. Grade weightage: 5% Class attendance for tutorials 12% Assignments (x3) 13% Labs (x10) 20% Midterms 50% Finals Skip Lecture?: Hell no, attending Uncle Soo's lecture helps you internalise the content a lot faster than reading it by yourself, at least from my experience. It doesn't hurt that he's a great lecturer and very approachable should you have doubts or other admin problems. Additional comments: Looking at the past year papers, when this module is under Prof Aaron, expect a tough time - his papers wear you down and are unlikely to be completable within 2h. However, Prof Soo's questions are more gentle, except that there are times when it is not easy to get the answer - more thinking is required rather than rote learning. Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A+

author: Owen
message: AY2019/2020 S1 Henry CHIA & OOI Wei Tsang Module difficulty/contents: Manageable. Most of it is just follow the lecturer's code style, that's it. Content wise, it's a really stupid module. Think of it as a java fanboy wanting everyone to learn his favourite module (who else has spearheaded this module for godforsaken years), and he met a person who is too attached to things (no changing objects! everything must be immortalised!!) A lot of paradigms and concepts taught in this module is subjective, and not necessarily standard practice in the working world. At least, teach some students how to comment their code properly! Workload: Definitely heavy. Only the first lab can be done within the two hour lab period, while the rest usually take a few more hours. And of course, lab occurs every week. Then there's the individual "project", an excuse to pile another lab assignment on you (there's two parts to this, so expect two weeks with double the workload). The lab is good practice for the practical exams though, which occur twice in the whole semester and forces you to use a barebones text editor. Thankfully recitations can be skipped without much repercussions - they have really weird implementations that try to make you be a Java compiler. Nothing else. The problem literally gives you code for classes and asks you "what happens when they are compiled/run?" Class participation exists for this module to build up hype about using Java. This was assessed through Piazza, and points are given for either answering other students' questions (which are mainly about Java) or creating your own problem on CodeCrunch (in Java!). The 5% is not worth it at all, and is used to fund Prof Henry's research. So you can't opt out. How ethical. Finals was shit since it was Examplify. Good luck if you're on it, because your screen allows you to see maybe only 3 lines throughout your entire answer due to the numerous newlines you need to adhere to their coding style. Grade Weightage: 5% Class participation 10% Labs (x10) 10% Project (2 parts) 35% Practical exam (x2) 40% Finals Skip Lecture?: Hell yes, although Prof Henry burps live in front of all the students. However, if you want to better understand Prof Henry's intuition regarding Java, and his live implementations, do attend the lectures. If you're fine reading off the slides, then you won't miss much. Additional comments: Shit mod, too bad it's compulsory. For labs, using IntelliJ because their linter is really good; for the practical exam, use 'gvim' instead which is already installed in the computer labs there and allows you to use your mouse (which greatly aided my friends). However, ensure that you don't over-rely on the autocorrect features in IntelliJ. Expected Grade: A-/A Actual Grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2019/2020 S1 Aaron TAN Tuck Choy (SoC) & Tan Kai Meng (FoS, Math) Module difficulty/contents: Moderately difficult. If you are coming from a math olympiad background, it definitely helps with the proof writing as well as number theory aspect. However, functions and groups are in general rather difficult to grasp. Workload: Decent. There's compulsory tutorials (2h) every week, and you are expected to present your solution in class! There are quite a number of questions in each tutorial as well. The take home assignments generally are around the length of a tutorial, but do not require that much effort. Revising for finals and midterms can be daunting as there are infinitely many different problems that they can throw at you for you to prove, so it is good to read through what was done previously to build up a general understanding. Grade weightage: 5% Tutorial participation 20% Take home assignments (x2) 25% Midterms 50% Finals Skip Lecture?: Yes, if you have the background. Slides are also easily to follow and comprehensive. Additional comments: CS1231 and CS1231S had the same content (and midterm, and final) for my semester, so not very sure what the difference was. Expected Grade: A-/A Actual Grade: A+

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 2 Assoc Prof Adrian Michael LEE Note that Prof Adrian is probably not going to teach this module again, so the module format is most likely going to change. Module difficulty/contents: There is a reason why this module is called Physical Chemistry, although I personally feel it veers a lot more towards chemical physics. The main bulk of this module focuses on spectroscopy, the various techniques in this discipline and how to calculate energy levels. Without a solid physics background, this module is daunting. Unfortunately CM2101 has quite a bit of physics concepts, including moments of inertia and quantum mechanics, so when such equations are used lots of people freak out over them. Workload: Decent. Lectures and tutorials are not strictly compulsory. There are participation marks for lectures, as well as weekly IVLE quizzes to ensure understanding of content. Prof Adrian also focuses on the usage and handling of data, so there are 3 homework assignments focused on finding out certain variables when give spectroscopic data, as well as a practical modelling test on your own laptop. Grade weightage: 5% Lecture Quizzes 5% Lecture Participation 15% Midterms 15% Homework Assignments (x3) 20% Modelling Test 40% Finals Skip Lecture?: No. Regardless of the lecture participation marks, you should attend lectures as Prof Adrian is good at teaching; there is a reason why he is deputy director at the Centre for Development of Teaching and Learning. This is helpful in a module like CM2101 when content is usually not as straightforward as presented in the notes, and I took down quite a number of tips from him when he is working out problems live. This is important as there are many equations that need to be understood before applying, since the instructions given in the notes are too detailed/complex to be followed. Additional comments: Be prepared to slog through the equations without understanding if the next lecturer is not as approachable. Historically this module is not done well by Chemistry students, so do seek help when needed. Expected grade: A Actual grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 2 Dr. Steven HALIM Module website: https://www.comp.nus.edu.sg/~stevenha/cs2040c.html Module difficulty/contents: This module is challenging, which is to say that most people think it's difficult but it isn't too bad. Steven does ensure that there are ample opportunities for consultations, both with him and his dedicated team of TAs. There are separate Lab and Tutorial TAs who are in charge of different parts of the module but can generally be consulted for either component. In terms of content, however, the module does have a lot of content, especially if you have no background on C++. Steven essentially starts this module on Week -2, so look out for that and practice accordingly. There is a new topic to revise every week, so it is essential to keep up with the module website and practice problems on Kattis. Note that Steven will not spend much time teaching C++, especially if you're transitioning over from another language. Take the assignments from Week -2 as practice. He does go through some tips & tricks, especially before the practical test, but this is not enough of an intro to C++. Be prepared to learn it yourself. If you're shopping around for a CS2040X module, note that CS2040 (AY18/19 did not have CS2040S) is taught in Java and has slightly more content than CS2040C, but the delivery of CS2040C is undeniably better. Workload: Heavy-ish. If you're struggling with coding, be prepared to code every week for practice and for the Kattis problem sets, in which a new one is introduced every 2-3 weeks. You also have to keep up with module content every week on Steven's VisuAlgo. That being said, it is possible to survive Steven's lectures without going through content in VisuAlgo, because this module isn't strictly flipped classroom, but more like Steven speeds through VisuAlgo lectures to focus on live-solving Kattis problems. Tutorial and Lab attendance is compulsory. Every session is tracked because there are participation points for both sessions (3% each), with the last point out of three being awarded only to active students in the session. Grade weightage: 15% Problem Sets (x5) 12% VisuAlgo Quiz 3% Tutorial Participation 3% Lab Participation 12% Practical Exam 15% Midterms 40% Finals Skip Lecture?: No. Steven is a good lecturer, and even though it's not strictly flipped classroom which might bore some of you when he goes through the content again, his teaching style is the reason why the appeal list is long (and he has to turn people down). Additionally, I learnt a lot when watching Steven live-solve problems. However, some lectures are skippable, especially when they are unfortunately scheduled on a 8-9am on Wednesday. Additional comments: Steven is a dedicated and hardworking lecturer, and he selects his TA team with care. If you do have problems, please do not hesitate to approach your TA, or another one during any consultation slot. Expected grade: A-/A Actual grade: A+

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 2 Tutor: Maurice Note that every student has to take this module, regardless if you are in USP/Utown/RVRC or not! Module difficulty/contents: Should be a breeze for students who have a technical major. Concepts introduced include uncertainty, probability, design of experiments, bias etc. Workload: Light! Attendance is only required for fortnightly tutorials. The GER project might cause a bit of a headache, but I am thankful for a good team as we only spent 2 days on the project together, which is one day for each of the assessments. Grade weightage: (not very sure about this) 20% IVLE Quizzes (weekly) 20% Project (5 + 15) 60% Finals Skip Lecture?: There are no lectures to attend. Tutorial participation is compulsory, and in general I think the tutors are a lot better equipped to handle questions. Additional comments: Lots of people don't try in this module. If you want to, aiming for an A-/A should be manageable with appropriate revision. However, do watch out for the finals which was killer this year. Possibly steep bell curve. I'll also like to say the Maurice is a good tutor who puts in effort to get to know students, and creates avenues for discussion of GER topics. Also rather responsive on Discord which is his choice of medium to disseminate information. Expected grade: A Actual grade: A-

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 2 Dr. LIN Qingsong Module difficulty/contents: For a module that is a major requirement for Chemistry students, and for the "Biochemistry" name of the module, this has close to no chemistry involved. Be prepared to S/U the module. Lectures consist of slides with content as well as e-practicals, which are just slides of practicals with voiceovers. Thankfully, the lecturer provides notes in Word document format which makes things a lot easier to study for. Workload: Light! Attendance is only required for 3 sessions - midterms, quiz, and "finals". Studying for these assessments isn't very hard either, but it depends on how much you want to score for the module. Grade weightage: 10% Quiz 1 (Game show format, 10% guaranteed if you show up) 30% CA1 ("midterms") 10% Quiz 2 (Essay about one biochemistry topic) 10% Quiz 3 (IVLE quiz, get your Life Science friends to help out) 40% CA2 ("finals") Skip Lecture?: Yes, less than a quarter of the cohort shows up for lecture anyway. Word document notes are a lot easier to digest than the lecture slides, although the slides have slightly more details. Additional comments: Lots of people don't try in this module. If you want to, aiming for an A-/A should be manageable with appropriate revision. Expected grade: Anything S/U-able lol Actual grade: B-

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 2 Dr. CHEN Litai Jeremiah (first half) and Saradha THYAGARAJAN (second half) Module difficulty/contents: A general introduction to lab work in Chemistry, with emphasis on several laboratory techniques like column chromatography and TLC. Lab report writing skills and safety is briefly touched upon. Workload: Lectures every week but lab slots every other week. Be warned - lab was from 10am-4pm on my batch; although you are free to leave for lunch, most of us power through and finish our experiments first at around 2-3pm before departing. Only one short lab report is required throughout the module, with the rest of the other experiments only requiring completion of a worksheet. There is also a short oral presentation on one of the experiments near the end of the semester. Grade weightage: 32% Pro Forma (worksheets) (x4) 13% Short Report 5% Pre-lab Assignments (x5) 20% Viva (Oral Presentation) 30% Written Test Skip Lecture?: Yes, because lectures are webcasted, and notes are not very detailed anyway. Referring to just the lecture notes is enough to pass the pre-lab safety quiz. Additionally, it is difficult to study for the written test too as the scope of the module is too broad. Additional comments: My reports have an average of A-, but I still managed to score decently at the end. However, we did not receive any feedback for our reports/worksheets as they were all marked at the end of the semester, after the written test. The written test is very hit-or-miss, and although I didn't study the right topics, I think I wrote enough to score enough points on the written test to do well for the module. Hence, I believe the bell curve is steep for the lab reports, and your final grade does depend heavily on the written test. Expected grade: A-/A Actual grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 1 Asst. Prof Rowan Drury YOUNG (first half) and Assoc. Prof HUYNH Han Vinh (second half) Module difficulty/contents: Practically the same as A-level Chemistry, with a bit of Quantum Physics (brief enough to explain atomic orbitals) and Molecular Orbital Theory, which are the only new topics in the first half of the module. After recess week, Prof Huynh takes over with a recap in acid-base reactions and electrochemistry. Workload: Tutorials are held almost every week but are optional to attend and interchangeable depending on class size and tutor. In fact attendance starts to drop to 3s or 2s as the semester progresses. Answers to tutorial questions are posted online anyway. Grade weightage: 40% for 2 CA tests (each half of the module), and 60% for the exam. Skip Lecture?: A third of the cohort says yes, seeing the class size as the semester progresses. Benefits of attending lectures are that the profs sometimes go through interesting questions that are emailed by the students. If not, lecture slides are good enough to gauge what kind of questions will come up during the midterms and exams. Additional comments: Bell curve might be slightly steep as most content is already covered in A levels. Be prepared for fluctuations in percentiles, especially if tests have more MCQ; finals was mainly open-ended so there is still some differentiating factor. Expected grade: A-/A Actual grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 1 Dr WANG Fei Module difficulty/contents: This is an extension of A-level mathematics, going through epsilon-delta proofs of limits to the various derivative tests to solving ordinary differential equations. Many people are taken aback by the first chapter (limits, especially epsilon-delta proofs) so it is recommended to keep pace with the module and consistently work on the tutorial questions, like most math modules. There is a plateau (which is a local minima) in the middle where the derivative tests are introduced again, but it spikes back up with solving ordinary differential equations. Workload: Tutorials are held every week but are optional to attend and are interchangeable. Most are graduate students, so it'll do you good to find a good tutor and stick to that slot. There are also take-home assignments which are made to be tricky and lengthy, so do allocate enough time to work on it and discuss with friends. There are also lab sessions every other week but these are a joke, especially if you have coding experience, and are ultimately useless throughout the module except for the Lab Quiz. Grade weightage: 5% Lab Quiz 15% Homework Assignments (x3) 20% Midterms 60% Finals Skip Lecture?: Lectures are webcasted. It still is possible to skip lectures for those with university-level mathematics background. Otherwise, it is highly important to go to lectures as Dr Wang is really good at explaining concepts and showing how to think in order to solve the equation, without referring to his notes. Dr Wang also summarises the previous lecture at the start of every session which is good for recall. Additional comments: Honestly, the lab sessions are really useless, so not much time and worry should be spent on them. Module coordinators are unable to come up with a good reason as to why the lab sessions exist in the first place. However, Dr Wang and Prof Goh (other lecturer) are very approachable if students have problems regarding module content. Expected grade: A-/A Actual grade: A

author: Owen
message: AY2018/19 Sem 1 Prof YAO Shao Qin Module difficulty/contents: Enolate chemistry from CM1121 is built upon, and is the main bulk of the module. However, I feel that the fundamental teaching objectives of this module are chemoselectivity, regioselectivity and stereoselectivity, which is definitely emphasised on in the second half of the module as the reactions in the first half of the module are reviewed to take into account different factors (steric hindrance, different electrophilic sites, reactivity of functional groups etc.). Spectroscopy was briefly touched upon but still included in one of the exam questions. Workload: Tutorials are held almost every week but are optional to attend. CAs are held during midterms and a few weeks before the end of the semester, and quite a bit of effort is needed to review the relevant content. Grade weightage: 40% for 3 CA tests (of which the first was not counted), and 60% for the exam. Skip Lecture?: Possible, as content covered in lectures are highly similar to what is in Clayden's. Lectures are rather fast paced with very minimal breaks given; however Prof Yao definitely knows his stuff and is able to answer any question given without reference. I'll recommend attending his lectures if you are able to keep up with his speed. Additional comments: Exam was open book, so do prepare to buy Clayden's for this module. If you're thinking of taking organic modules in the future, Clayden's is definitely worth buying. Expected grade: A Actual grade: A+

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 2
Lecturer: Asst Prof Han Shaoning
30% 4 Assignments
30% Midterm
40% Finals

This module was kind of boring by nature of the content - forgot I was taking this module at times as I did not go for a single lecture or tutorial. The prof's handwriting is quite hard to read so try to ask around for the old pdf notes, they basically say the same thing. No calculators were allowed for both midterms and finals so make sure your mental math is brushed up.

Assuming that this mod still fulfils a few programmes' requirements in the future, there is a high chance that most people taking this module will come from DSA/other math-adjacent majors, and that the paper will likely be highly computational as a result (since proofs will just kill everyone anyway). As such I decided to abandon this mod in the second half of the semester and do a funny little challenge called "Not studying anything from duality theory onwards until the night before the exam". Entered the 9 am final with no sleep and rode the bell curve to a grade I definitely did not deserve for the effort I put in.

Total (excludes finals): 59.4/60
Final grade: A

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 2
Lecturers: Prof Muhammad Rizki Aulia Rahman Maulana, Prof Frank Patrick Rebentrost, Prof Hu Conghui
30% Coursemology
10% Mini Project
30% Midterm
30% Finals

You can get full for Coursemology by skipping one problem set so use that to your advantage. I really did not appreciate that they release the slides after the lecture as I study better by just reading. The hardest component for me was the mini project - I spent quite a lot of time not making any progress at all. So no, I do not agree with zai (see below) when he says that 40% is free. For future batches, I'd say that cooking up random heuristics can get you a pass in the project and not to waste too much time crying over neural networks/the 80k dataset. I got below median for the project which was a little disheartening for the amount of effort I put into it. For the people out there who are better at theory like me, the exams were easy. My experience seems quite unusual though, the statistics seems to indicate that people do worse for the exams. The content was enjoyable (ML/Neural networks is cool to me) but the amount of math required is less than I would like.

Total: 91/100
Final Grade: A+
thread_title: CS2109S Introduction to AI and Machine Learning

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 2 (Cross listed as MA4251)
Lecturer: Prof Wang Wanjie
10% Tutorial submission
20% Group Project
20% Midterm
50% Finals

If you held a gun to my head and asked me to teach you martingales, I'd ask you to pull the trigger.

Topics:
1. Poisson Process
- Non-homogenous, Conditional, Merging, Splitting, Compound
2. Continuous time Markov Chains
- Chapman-Kolmogorov Backward/Forward Equations
- Infinitesimal generators
- Birth and Death processes
- Basic Queueing theory
- Solving basic ODE's to get the transition probability matrix in terms of t
- First step analysis, limiting/stationary distributions
- Link between limiting distribution of embedded chain and continuous process
- Time reversibility
3. Martingales (Nerfed version)
- Optional Stopping Theorem (Nerfed version)
- Basic transformations of non-martingale processes into martingales
4. Renewal Theory
- Renewal Equation
- Renewal reward, Delayed renewal and Stationary renewal process
5. Brownian Motion
- Distribution of multiple time points, conditional brownian motion
- Brownian motion with drift, Geometric Brownian motion
6. Gaussian Process
- Brownian Bridge
- Integrated Brownian motion

Took this as a Math major. Your mileage will vary if you come from stats/DSA expecting a stats-adjacent mod because this really is just an applied math module under the statistics department.

Same flavour as her Stochastic Processes 1. It was enjoyable but slightly heavier in terms of content. Despite being a 4k mod, it still feels like an introductory module in terms of difficulty, much like its prerequisite MA3238/ST3236. Group project and tutorial submissions were giveaways.

Being taught under the stats department meant that much of the rigour was sacrificed, but it still didn't deter Prof Wang from trying to teach us martingales. If MA3238 gave me war flashbacks of how Prof used the unfortunately unenlightening "It only hurts when X = 2" to intuit indicator functions for first step analysis, then MA4251 has given me nightmares of whatever the hell was the martingales chapter. Bless her heart for trying though. It is truly an impossible feat to teach a class of 50% stats majors and 50% applied math majors who last touched pure maths in MA2108 about sigma-algebras, filtrations and measurability - concepts from level 4k pure math mods that I wouldn't have dreamt of touching, ever. Learning this portion felt like doing a lot of guesswork/high level symbol manipulation that was not really enlightening, but it produced the right answers so I'm not too upset about it.

As usual for me, I screwed up midterms - blanking out, misreading questions, computational errors, pure dumb mistakes - and got near median (high 70s/low 80s) for midterms. Knew I had to haul major ass and get it together for finals so I spent most of my time studying this mod by grinding martingales. The question on martingales in the finals turned out to be almost directly ripped from tutorials so you could imagine my pain. I think not many people could do it though so it ended up being one of the major discriminators for your performance. Apart from that, the other discriminator was probably a whole question on first step analysis - which most of us were not expecting to show up as it was a concept that was only emphasised upon in MA3238. But the paper was doable, conditioning on the event that one had done all the tutorials and extra practices.

Content-wise, I would have preferred for more depth in terms of the rigourous logical foundations of martingales and probability theory in general. But this is just wishful thinking - a rigourously taught stochastics course would realistically kill 90% of the cohort after all, myself included. Or perhaps I should take up some measure/probability theory classes to satisfy my curiousity for how painful and deep the rabbit hole goes.

I still thoroughly enjoyed this mod overall and I'm thankful for Prof Wang for these two semesters... Prof, if you're reading this, I don't blame you but I still don't really know what a martingale is 🫶🫶🫶🫶
Total (excludes finals): 46.2/50
Final Grade: A

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 2
Lecturer and Tutor: Prof Zhang Louxin
40% 4 Assignments
60% Finals
Bonus 3% (1% for each tutorial question presented/each tutorial typesetted)

1. Introduction
- Handshake lemma, Prüfer sequences (and the associated algorithm)
2. Bipartite Graphs and Trees
- Minimum spanning trees
- Prim's and Kruskal's algorithms for minimum spanning tree
- Phylogenetic trees
3. Matching and Independence
 - Hall's theorem
- Augmenting path algorithm
- Tutte's theorem
- Vertex covers, Independent sets
- Konig's theorem
4. Eulerian multigraphs and Hamiltonian graphs
- Chinese postman problem
- Guan's and Edmond-Karp's algorithms for Chinese postman problem
- Travelling salesman problem
- Approximation algorithm (Christofides' algorithm) for Travelling salesman problem
5. Connectivity 
- Whitney's inequalities on connectivity
- Menger's theorem (max-flow/min-cut duality)
- Block decomposition
- Ear decomposition
- Dirac's theorem
6. Colouring
- Brook's theorem
- Perfect graphs
- Lovasz's theorem
- Chromatic polynomials
- Vizing's theorem
7. Planarity
- Characterisation (Kuratowski's theorem)
- Euler's formula
- Wagner's theorem
- Planarity algorithm for 2-connnected graphs and multi-graphs
- 5-colour theorem
8. Introductory algebraic graph theory
- Matrix-tree theorem
- Study of eigenvalues of Laplacian and adjacency matrices
- Cheeger's inequality
- Bisection problem
- Spectral bisection algorithm
9. Digraphs
- Landau's theorem on tournaments
- Kings and bases
- Pagerank algorithm
- Counting Eulerian circuits

I get the impression that many students took this course thinking that it was still taught by Wang Fei/Huang Hao - check the teaching allocations and temper your expectations accordingly. MA2214 is not really required, but MA2001 is needed for algebraic graph theory.

On top of last year's content, Prof Zhang has also added algebraic graph theory into the syllabus. This results in a content-heavy course and it shows in the speed of the lectures. I found myself regularly playing catch-up and not really following his explanations at times. This is alright though, as his slides are relatively rigourous so it is quite self-studiable, and each topic is relatively self-contained so you could study each topic somewhat independently of each other (bar some useful results that show up everywhere). The proofs get quite involved at times but they are somewhat more approachable to me compared to other fields. Assignments and tutorials are reasonable. As a whole, I enjoyed the content as someone whose intuition relies on drawings and visual arguments.

We were only allowed a double sided A4 cheatsheet for finals - it is currently the densest cheatsheet I have by far in my university life. The paper was relatively difficult and I only completed ~60% of it confidently. The cohort seems to have flunked it even worse.

Total (excludes finals): 39.5/40, no clue how Prof Zhang computes the bonus but I presented twice
Final Grade: A+

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Liu Mei Hui
20% Group Project
5% Peer Feedback
5% Peer Grading
20% Canvas Quiz
20% Individual Essay
30% Finals

Full disclosure, I signed up for this module only because I heard they gave out free food.

They fed us saltine crackers, gluten free bread, candy and deep fried crickets. 9/10 experience, -1 because I want more food :D. I ate a lot of the gluten free bread even though it tasted awful. Granted, there were takeaways that were intended to come with each food but I kind of forgot them after exams. Nonetheless I appreciate the effort put in to feed a group of 180+ extremely food motivated university students. Prof Liu and my TA were both extremely friendly and approachable. Lectures and tutorials were not compulsory but they were a good wrap-up of a long day for me - plus they gave food.

The group project was due before midterms which was great as that meant that we could focus on the work of other modules closer to the end of the semester. Group members are allocated by the course administration with the intention to spread the Life Science/Chem/FST-adjacent majors evenly across all groups. The project required us to design a food-related case study and to design and answer some questions to promote inquiry - which I thought was really weird having to both ask and answer our own questions. I'm not a fan of the freedom given by the group project and would much rather have to answer to a more specific prompt instead. The group project and the individual essay were the same as 23/24 Sem 1.

I think the content difficulty is overstated in the reviews below (See Damaen Tan's and kk's review). Having not touched biology since Sec 2, dropped chemistry since Sec 4, and not looked at the lecture slides for the whole semester, I thought the content was understandable and mostly memorisable in a night's worth of frantic mugging. Finals was closed book, 1 hour, on Examplify with a cheatsheet provided by Prof Liu, with around 20+ MCQs and a short essay question on the NOVA classification of two different drinking water products. I did not find the cheatsheet useful at all. No prior knowledge needed, just find the humanities side of you to smoke out arguments. HSI1000 being a prerequisite for this class and the HSI2K series is a huge sham in my opinion.

Group Project (the only released component, no numerical score): A-
Final Grade: A-

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 1 (Cross listed as MA3238)
Lecturer: Prof Wang Wanjie
10% Tutorial Attendance
10% 1 x Assignment
30% Midterms
50% Finals

Topics:
1. Discrete Time Markov Chains (Finite and countable state)
- First Step Analysis
- (Ir)reducibility, Recurrence (Null and Positive), Transience, Periodicity, Regularity and Ergodicity
- Limiting and Stationary Distributions, Local and Global Balance Equations
2. Basic applications of Discrete Time Markov Chains
- Inventory model
- Queueing system
- Gambler's ruin/random walks
- PageRank algorithm
- Markov Chain Monte-Carlo (Metropolis-Hastings algorithm) 
- Branching process
3. Introduction to Continuous Time Markov Chains (Poisson process)

Took this as a math major. Overall it was one of the easier math modules. Your mileage will definitely vary depending on the major you come from and whether you like more mathematical modules.

Prof Wang is really nice and would stay back to clarify doubts and speak to students after the lecture. There were no official tutorials - tutorials were held during the 2nd hour of the 2nd lecture of each week, and she would grade attendance during that time. We also had to submit our tutorials on the Saturday after the tutorial which were graded only for completion.

I think the first topic went by too slow - setting up the tools to analyse Markov chains took up two-thirds of the lectures, which could probably be condensed... In contrast, MCMC felt rushed. I liked that she adapted to the speed of the cohort and introduced us to the basics of Poisson processes at the end of the sem, which were supposed to be in ST4238/MA4251.

There was only one homework, which was given out really late into the semester. Only assesses you on the basics of Markov Chains.

Midterms was quite computationally heavy and so I goofed up some calculations. Finals was too straightforward - there were multiple people leaving an hour into the exam. There were 4 questions, 1 of which was on the Poisson process. The answers to each part of the Poisson process question was a one liner and did not need any additional knowledge from this module - knowledge on the Poisson and Exponential random variables from ST2131/MA2116 was sufficient if you see the way to use the memoryless property of the exponential distribution and the linearity of expectation.

I assume that this resulted in quite a steep curve for the finals which meant our results were largely dependent on our midterm performance. But I would like to say that it's possible to go against the trend - your midterm performance does not lock you in any grade, don't be complacent and don't give up either :)

Midterm stats (out of 90):                 https://uploads.disquscdn.com/images/07e6e3a5d26d2e08d46fe856ac6848ae74fc2bac1b6832a6dec5690abbef7db5.png                 
Overall, I enjoyed the content quite a lot, and Prof Wang is really nice - might follow her to Stochastic Processes II.

Total (excludes finals): 42.5/50
Final Grade: A

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 2
Lecturer: Assistant Professor Michael Choi
20% 6 Homeworks
30% Midterm
50% Finals

Topics:
1. Maximum likelihood estimator, Method of moments, Biasedness of an estimator
2. Confidence intervals, Hypothesis testing, Critical regions and the connections between them
The tests include but are not limited to:
 - 1-sample Z/T-test
 - Paired Z/T-test
 - 2-sample Z/T-test (include Welch's t-test)
 - F-test for variance
 - Chi-square test for Goodness of fit and Independence
 - Sign test, Wilcoxon's signed rank test
 - More tests could be used, either by definition on the spot or through the Neyman-Pearson lemma
3. Basic linear regression models
4. Order statistics, QQ plots, Distribution free confidence intervals
5. Power of a test, Neyman-Pearson lemma, Likelihood ratio test

Fun mod. The content might seem like a lot at first (due to the amount of hypothesis tests and vairants that you have to learn) but the process is the same across all the tests - calculate test statistic, see if it is in the critical region or just calculate the p-value. Prof Choi also sets assignments that require coding in whatever language that suits you, which I think are cool to do :3

Tutorials were skippable.

I think the main pain points for the cohort were 
1. no precise feedback/solutions for all of the assignments, you were just told briefly which question part was wrong (I THINK you could email him?)
2. no midterm solutions were released, the question paper and the scripts were not returned (but you could go down for a review session)
3. scientific calculator only, and HANDWRITTEN A4 cheatsheet for both midterms and finals

These made the module slightly different than what we were used to. But overall Prof Choi's notes are great (they're self-contained!) and his assessments and lectures are very targeted -  he clearly specifies what he wishes for us to take away from each topic. It seems to me that he managed to make the content in the module fit together very well - which cannot be said for the previous sem as my friends told me.

All in all would highly recommend people to do this mod under this professor, but just be aware of his assessment policies being slighly different from the usual.

Total (excludes finals): 49.97/50, lost a mark in an assignment 
Final Grade: A+

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 1 (Cross listed as MA2116)
Lecturer: Senior Lecturer Wang Fei and Senior Lecturer Timothy Wertz
Tutor: Wang Ming Jie
20% 4 Canvas Quizzes
20% 4 Homeworks
60% Finals

Bell curve for ST2131 and MA2116 are identical, the only difference is the ModReg quota at the start of the semester and the final seating plan at the end of the semester.

Why does the department always allocate Wang Fei to the 8 am lectures :") I'd sometimes forget I was taking this module because I did not attend the 8 am lectures or the tutorials. Wang Fei's notes are supposedly ideal when consumed in his lectures but I can't really confirm that for myself - when I try to study by reading his notes I find them quite excessive and hard to distill/summarise. Word of advice under Wang Fei would be to do this mod with your math major friends - and if you don't have any go and make some - compare and discuss quizzes and assignments since they can be really hard (the quizzes in particular).

Finals was a massacre but as usual the curve will help you alot in Wang Fei's mods. Super duper computation heavy as usual and scientific calculators only - if I recall correctly there was a question with a 4-set principle of inclusion and exclusion which was just an absolute pain to compute. Did 5/6 of the questions in the finals - the last question on a random walk was plucked directly from tutorials (not the numbers but the concept of the random walk and how to solve it) and I might've known how to do it but .,.,.,. I did not go for tutorials ,.,.,.,.,,., 👉👈👉👈👉👈👉👈,.,.,.,,.,.,.,.,,.,.,,.,. anyway I wrote garbage and sad faces for that question and spent the rest of the time checking the other questions and getting different numerical answers each time. 

Total (for known components, excluding finals): 40/40
Final Grade: A+
