author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 1
Lecturer and Tutor: Prof Zhang De Qi

Assessments: 
10% 5 Homeworks
30% Midterm
60% Finals

1. Vector spaces over a field
2. Vector subspaces
3. Linear spans and Direct sums of subspaces
4. Linear Independence, Basis and Dimension
5. Row space and Column space
6. Quotient spaces and Linear transformations
7. Representation matrices of linear transformations
8. Eigenvalue and Cayley-Hamilton theorem
9. Minimal polynomial and Jordan canonical form
10. Quadratic forms, Inner product spaces and Conics 

Seems quite tame? Compared to past year reviews of MA2101 and MA2101S. Prof also likes making 10 Canvas announcements in a row with all the text in the header of the Canvas announcement and an exclamation mark or a "Pls!" in the body. So we'd get push notifications with nothing but "!", "Pls!", "!!", Really entertaining LOL  

Huge overlap with MA2001, and I think Prof does spend too much time in the first half of the topics (1 - 6). I didn't really attend live lectures because he has a tendency to mumble, his handwriting on the digital screen is quite bad and the seats at LT26 are really uncomfortable. His tutorial questions are great though, and his tutorials are way more clear (I don't know why but I find them way easier to follow). He also has great memory and just starts doing the tutorial questions without reference on the whiteboard. Also quite a caring prof and would message you through Canvas if your homework/exams fell below expectations/if you skipped tutorials.  

Midterms and finals were mostly computational, with a bit of proving. Due to the huge overlap with MA2001 there wasn't a need to study for midterms. Nothing much to say for the computational question but some of the proving questions gave a feeling that you couldn't write anything more than "This statement is true because it is true" - so I guess you really need to be rigorous and detailed down to a T - handwaving not really tolerated.

Total (for known components, excluding finals): 36.8/40
Final Grade: A

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 1
Lecturers: Prof Aaron Tan Tuck Choy, Prof Colin Tan and Prof Wong Weng Fai
Tutor: Enzio (Tutorials)
3% Lecture Quizzes
5% Tutorial Attendance
12% Assignments
10% Labs
20% Midterm
50% Finals

Insanely content heavy. Bless my TA Enzio for keeping me awake in an afternoon tutorial slot (very loud voice). If you intend to bid for labs and tutorials back-to-back, put the tutorial in the earlier slot since labs tend to finish very early. Enjoyed learning (last minute mugging?) the content, especially datapath, sequential logic and state machines.

Absolutely hated the assessment format. 50% of the marks are allocated to the most annoying type of questions, like
    1. How many times is this line MIPS code executed
    2. Tracing out the MIPS code (with loops lord help me lol) for a full pipeline state with/without forwarding and with/without early branch assumption (which all result in different rules to trace out the pipeline) and the state of cache with/without associativity

Like god lmao is it that hard to set a less mind-numbing/brain-rotting paper? There's so much other content in both cache and pipelining that could be tested which doesnt necessitate this kind of bull.

It really isn't the case that the profs are unable to create well-designed questions as well. Midterms tested on the data/control path implementation for the j instruction - which we did not learn but if you understood control signals well you could refer to their diagram and answer the question. As for finals, the datapath + pipeline question which tested on the registers and control signals used for forwarding (which we were not taught) relied on students to synthesise and infer patterns in the pipeline registers based on the diagram they provided - this is 1. not a question that is pure tedium and 2. required us to combine knowledge learnt from different topics. Why couldn't have this been the case for the questions on pipelining and cache? I do not know.

Either ways it might've been my fault as well, went in the finals at 5 pm with zero sleep after having rawdogged a 9 am exam on the same day. Did study for pipeline and cache but didn't bother to actually learn how to trace out the state of the cache/pipeline (I gave up on that lol). Turns out it was near 50% of the finals and I just wrote garbage for them while trying not to piss or defecate myself or breakdown from the pressure (pipeline: guessed random numbers, cache: wrote the starting address for every blank that required an address).

Just glad this mod is over.

Total (for known components, excluding finals): 47/50
Final Grade: A-

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 1
Lecturers: Prof Arnab Bhattacharyya and Prof Rahul Jain

20% Weekly Problem Sets
10% Tutorial Participation
10% Programming Assignments
20% Midterm
40% Finals

BARK BARK BARK BARK BARK BARK ARF ARF ARF ARF ARF ARF ARF BARK BARK terrible terrible TERRIBLE TERRIBLE TERRIBLE mod. I had one regret throughout this semester and it was taking this module this semester. 

Will not repeat what others have said. Lectures were like watching the systematic breakdown of the psyche of 2 research track profs waffling their way through slides that were plucked from online. Painful for them and painful for us. The tutorial participation component was a bad fix for this iteration with poor pedagogy. I feel for the head TA and the other undergrad tutors holding this module together. They are underpaid (probably).

I'd also like to point out that 20% of your grade comes from the weekly problem sets, but no standardisation of marking has happened. Compare with the midterms with the exact same weightage, where apparently the marking was very standardised. On top of that, the TAs have vastly different marking speeds (there was a significant bunch of students which had more than half of their problem sets unmarked AFTER FINALS WERE DONE AND RETURNED). Mine were returned timely and I thank my TA for the effort they put in. I highly suspect the rushed marking towards the end of the semester meant that many 10s were given away for free just like that. Watching the median mark on Canvas for each problem set slowly climb up above my mark post-finals took a toll on my bell-curve-phobic heart and I wouldn't wish it on anyone. Both my mids and finals were above 75th percentile but my overall was knocked down a huge portion by the unstandardised PSet marking. I really should've just copied the answers :/

Total (for known components, excluding tutorial participation): 73/90
Final Grade: A-
thread_title: CS3230 Design and Analysis of Algorithms

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Zhang Teng Ren
Tutor: Jiang Yi Peng

Assessments:
15% 3 Assignments
10% 9 Quizzes
25% Midterm
50% Finals

Topics:
1. Theory of Interest
    - Accumulation function, simple and compound interest, present/time value, annuities and loans
2. Bonds
    - Pricing of a bond, price-yield curve, Macaulay/Modified duration and sensitivity, Macaulay duration of a portfolio
3. Mean Variance Analysis
    - Random variables, expectation, variance, covariance, correlation coefficient, rate of return/log return, portfolio weights, Global Minimum Variance Portfolio and its risk-return graph
4. Forwards and Futures
    - Arbitrage principle and how to construct them, pricing of forwards and futures, margin accounts and margin calls, the perfect hedge, minimum variance hedge
5. Options
    - Calls and puts, trading strategies (Covered calls, protective puts, bull/bear/butterfly spreads using calls/puts, long/short straddles), bounds on option prices, put-call parity, 1/2-step binomial option pricing model

I don't know why I took this module. Lots of CS students (maybe 90%? I could not find more than 3 QF majors.) It was also overbidded by Round 0 so plan your bidding accordingly. 

Prof Zhang is an amazing lecturer - his notes are good and are full of exercises that he tasks the students with during lecture period, while he walks around the lectue hall answering questions and making sure that people are following his speed. My tutor Yipeng is great too and is one of the student tutors who I like - energetic and clear in his tutorials. I look forward to seeing either of them in other modules.

Homeworks and exams were incredibly tedious, and many of the questions boiled down to calculator bashing. Not a lot of proving was involved. 1 A4 cheat sheet was allowed for both midterms and finals.

Content of the module was a little boring in retrospect - most of the time (especially the first half of the semester) was spent fiddling with geometric series which..... wasn't the most mind-stimulating thing to do. Many of the concepts - present/time value, bonds, loans - in the first half of the semester boil down to drawing a cashflow and chugging out a massive geometric series. Difficulty ramped up with the binomial option pricing, but Prof Zhang didn't seem to require an understanding of the derivation of the pricing formula, so another plug and chug as usual. The finance concepts are interesting, but they do not add much to the learning experience when distilled down to its core of pure computation at the 1K level.

All in all I'm just taking this module to unlock higher level QF mods like QF4103. But they all need QF2104, which seem to be a rehash of this mod which content I did not really enjoy. I need a break before touching QF again :(

Total (for known components, excluding finals): 48/50
Final Grade: A

message:

Tutors: Chee Meng and Giorgio

Assessments:
40% Final exam
15% Individual project (Labs 1, 2, 3, 5), last Lab (5.2) due Week 12
15% Practical assessment #1 in week 7
20% Practical assessment #2 in week 13
4% Labs (might be the non project labs but it's not explicitly mentioned. Labs 4, 6, 7, 8)
6% Class participation and peer learning activities (5%) + self-practice exercises (3%)

My favourite (and highest workload) module this semester!! Bidding for this module as a Y1 non SOC student was nerve-wracking, get a CS minor for the bidding priority.

I think it takes a bit of masochism to enjoy this module to be very honest. For comparison, CS1010S is known to be heavy and I think I take around 20% more time on CS2030 work compared to CS1010S.

The most fun part of the module (maybe even of all the modules I took) was the project on the discrete event simulator. Was expecting that I had to make some user interface (given that many people say this is a SWE mod), turns out you just have to work out the logic behind the DES.

I also really liked the practical assessment format, where you have 90 minutes to code in the lab (compilation errors is fine) and around 1 week to wrap up and pass all test cases. The final score is based on a similarity comparison between the tokenised forms of your first and second submission. This assessment format better determines our ability to design code dependencies, and helped with my stress of being unable to compile/debug code within a short period of time (grumble grumble CS2040C grumble grumble). A word of advice? Keep the original code in if the program can function with it.
 
Kind of dislike the disparity between how code is written in practice versus in this module (with all the restrictions like having all objects be immutable), but I guess this is the necessary evil to prevent people from producing bad code in the future??? I have no clue tbvh

PA2 was bonkers (as it has always been in the past), but finals felt like a whole new thing compared to past year papers. This sem, Prof Henry decided to make each question depend on all the previous questions - like a PA3. So people who were banking on jumping around trying to earn as much marks as possible - a strategy that worked in other semesters where questions were isolated from one another - were at a disadvantage. I think my code started to get a little too gibberish towards the end, decided to write in English (not even pseudocode) for the last few questions because my brain was too fried. Don't think that helped my grades.

Had tons of fun taking this module this sem - the large cohort of BZA/Info Sys students and the "Let's just suffer together" mentality helps with your morale when struggling in this module :)

PA1: 12.75/15
PA2: 13.87/20
Project: Completed Week 8
Final Grade: A-

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2

Lecturer: Hui Yu
Assessments:
10% Participation
10% Homework
30% Midterms
50% Finals

If you're an absolute dumbass like me then don't go into this module without studying beforehand. Baby Rudin Chapters 1 - 6 were covered.

Prof Huiyu's assessments are very generous in that if the question is True/False and you write a "True", a significant portion of the marks would be given to you. Many of his questions are in the style of Rudin's exercises as well.

He doesn't give notes/handouts, not sure if this is a common practice for higher level/small cohort modules. His handwriting has the tendency to devolve into chicken scratch (I don't really blame him, some of the proofs have really convoluted notations/constructions) as the lecture progresses and the proofs get more and more tedious - so paying attention to what he says is way more important as he talks about the intuition and the motivation behind the construction of the proofs before writing them down. He likes making many self deprecating jokes which the class eats up.

There aren't any tutorials (like Prof Chin, he converts the 2 hour tutorial into a third lecture session), so a lot of the learning is quite independent.

Midterms (before recess) was hell for me, topics tested were chapters 1 and 2 (mostly 2). Bombed it and <s>got the lowest in the class</s> was the infimum of the class - panicked and struggled with the abstract aspect of metric spaces as I was dealing with it the first time.

Finals was significantly better, testing on chapters 3 - 6, likely because I had the time to revise, and that the topics covered results that are more concrete (e.g. IVT, EVT, MVT) and had appeared in MA2002, where they were taken for granted.

Midterm: 28/100
Final Grade: B+ (I expected to fail so I was very happy already 🥳)

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2
Lecturer: ???
Tutor: Mr Samuel Yuen

Assessments:
11% 6 Quizzes
4% Pre + Post course exercises
15% Tutorial participation, in-class quizzes
30% Group project
40% Finals

Very light workload module compared to the other CHS common curriculum modules. Mr Samuel talks like a tour guide at the Singapore Zoo during tutorials which I think is very funny. Also he likes making a lot of stupid dad jokes which helps us survive the 2.5 hour lessons.

The project has two sections for the report - one on extracting basic statistical info from a research paper (like reading confidence intervals and being able to identify what are the independent/dependent variables), and the other on doing basic data exploration on a data set (CS1010S helps with this). I hate cleaning and organising data so I was lucky to have other teammates who were more than happy doing so. Pray for someone who still remembers H2 math in your group as that can take you quite far.

The presentation was extremely relaxed with Mr Samuel - we were allowed to just sit down and watch (while the person speaking stands up and presents). There is a QnA portion on basic statistical knowledge (again, H2 math or a math major will help). Throughout the presentation he was very friendly and when we got stuck he would slowly guide us to the right answer.

Finals was pretty doable but some people felt cheated as the mock paper was significantly easier.

No clue why math majors need to take this module to be very honest. I didn't learn many new things. Mr Samuel is quite good though, wouldn't mind seeing him in a stats module.

Final Grade: A+

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2
Tutor: Ms Zaneta

Assessments:
5% Book Chapter Reflection
20% Science News Article 1
5% Peer Review of Science News Article
30% Science News Article 2
10% Reflective Commentary
25% Individual Oral Presentation
5% Participation

A pretty okay module for what it aims to teach. A downside is that the module tutorials can tend to get repetitive in the middle of the semester, where every lesson consists of going through one text and analysing how certain techniques are used in the text for certain purposes (e.g. to provide motivation for the study, to pique the interest of the reader, to convert scientific terms into words that are understandable to the general educated population), all done on a class-shared document.

Before the semester starts there is a pre-course news article assignment (ungraded), where you choose 1 out of 5 research papers from different fields and (naively) convert them into a science news article that is accessible to non-specialised audience. I do not recommend doing the math research paper (on population dynamics and how it is applied to predicting burglaries). 

The science news article has to be done with reference to a peer reviewed research paper related to your major. For example, math majors are allowed to do math, physics, statistics, data science, and quantitative finance research papers only. As a math major this gave me leeway to sneak in a computer science/data science paper which I felt more comfortable dealing with.

The workload tends to be higher towards the end as the presentation, 2nd draft of the news article, and reflective commentary are all due in the same week. Procrastinated on the presentation, stayed up all night doing the slides and had to improvise my 10 minute speech :(. I really don't like writing, but Ms Zaneta is quite chill and approachable so this module was more bearable than I expected. Have to say that I'm happy with the final article that I've submitted - gave it to humanities friends and they said they could understand it so :0 maybe the techniques actually work? It's one of those modules that needs group discussions so I think its a good way to make friends with other majors! :D

Book Chapter Reflection: 50/100
Science News Article 1: 58.1/100
Individual Oral Presentation: 70/100
Science News Article 2: 84.4/100 (massive jump - this was after a 20 minute compulsory consultation for every student which apparently worked :OOOO)
Reflective Commentary: 94/100
Total (for known components): 66/90
Final Grade: A-

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Alan

Assessments:
8% Visualgo Quizzes
15% Practical Exam
20% 2 Quizzes
20% 5 Assignments
40% Finals
No clue how it is rebased to 100% but whatever

In contrast to CS2030 which I really liked (and took in the same semester), I think this module slightly disappointed me. Prof Alan is a sweet guy and all but his assessments tend to be very weird compared to CS2040/CS2040S. Nevertheless, his lecturing style is pretty good, and I actually quite enjoy the fact that he brings in topics beyond the normal scope for the CS2040 variants, from his own field of study and higher level modules (like binary space partitioning in video games).

For the written components (the 2 quizzes and the finals),  there are many T/F questions which boil down to "UHHHHH ACKTUALLY THITH IS FALTHE 🤓☝️" and being aware of C++ specific behaviour. There were only 2 question parts in the finals dedicated to the application/modification of the algorithms (for a total of 16 marks out of 100), which made me a little sad as that was what I studied for. 90% of the remaining questions were all dedicated to you acting as the computer and tracing out the inner state of the data structure/algorithm as it executes code - very tedious.

The 15% practical is the most difficult component (said by Prof Alan himself). Not because the application is difficult, but due to the fact that the standard library is prohibited - everything must be done without it. I spent most of the time crying over segmentation faults. Bombed the paper and proceeded to bomb the toilet straight after. Mean was 22/100.

The only continual assessement that needs some effort is the 5 assignments, of which 2 are dealing with basic linked list manipulations, and the rest are just recoding the same data structures/algorithms (AVL tree, heap and Dijkstra's algorithm), with very little application/extension to the algorithms.

This iteration under Prof Alan may be good for people who are already familiar with all the basic data structs/algos needed in computing, familiar with handling raw pointers/arrays in C, and want to have time to do other modules. Otherwise I'm not too sure if this module under Prof Alan will be the best match. It probably was my fault taking this module without C/C++ background since I came from CS1010S which is done in Python. The assessments might be less jarring for the people that this module is dedicated to (like CEG/Info Sec people). 

Again, I have no hard feelings against Prof Alan - my review is quite likely to be biased due to me not taking CS1010.

Quizzes x 2: 94%
Assignments x 5: 99%
Practical: 0% 🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡🤡
Final Grade: A (?????????????????? bless the bell curve god LOL)

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 1

Lecturer/Tutor: Goh Jun Le

TLDR: SoC students ARE allowed to take this module. Contrary to what SoC says, they have no administrative power over the math dept (I'm aware of some CS single major students who took this module). Give this module a shot - email the math dept about it (the only way to get this module) and they'll gladly allocate it to you! As to graduation requirements......... I guess you can take CS1231S in your 3rd or 4th year if SoC is really adamant about it but thats a problem for future them to deal with. This review is not responsible for softlocking you from graduating.

Assessments are as other commenters have mentioned.

Props to Prof Goh for being receptive to feedback and increasing the difficulty of the tutorials in the second half of the semester to better prep us for finals.

I enjoyed this module but if you're like me and not the type to self study maths in your free time then it's going to be a new experience interacting with people who live and breathe maths.

Midterms: 14/24 (exact median) - And I don't remember screwing up significantly for finals.
Final Grade: A-

author: M90jC6M7b!Yy
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 1

Lecturer: Whoever compiles the notes and sends them in one large PDF file in the Telegram chat

Tutor: Chia Hao Kai (No complains, great tutor, makes this module more tolerable with his entertaining workshops and lets us off quite early)

TLDR: Strongly disliked this module. Feels like it's having an identity crisis. Don't take this module if you prefer one with depth.

Assessments are as below.

This module started off pretty promising with an introduction to the scientific method, some logical fallacies and a skillset to breakdown the reliability of a source.

The rest of the module proceeds to disappoint. The overall depth of the 3 blocks (the scientific method, climate change and biodiversity in Singapore) was massively underwhelming. Mastery of content is not enforced by building upon knowledge from previous topics - I got whiplash from the transition from one block to the other. I also really disliked the treatment of some of the statistics - especially how confidence intervals was introduced and barely touched on after.

The lack of depth in this module was compensated with breadth, which made for a module that covered a disappointing amount of everything - and notes with a lot of fluff. Props to the first lecturer for his comprehensive notes.

The assignments are so-so. It may be my bias, but the second assignment is probably the weirdest and most pointless one to me. Creating a proposal to solve climate change from the En-roads simulator feels simultaneously too broad (with too many variables and limiting factors) and narrow (the weirdly specific requirements we have to meet in terms of CO2 emissions and temperature increase). This doesn't make for an interesting task to write. Pray for a good group for the second assignment and not one with more than half slackers. Ended up working with just 1-2 other people for most of the task.

Not a good module. The fact that HSI2k mods have this mod as a prerequisite is just a sham - many of them are repackaged GE% mods which had no prerequisites. And the purpose of adding this additional requirement is for... adding artificial depth/progression in the CHS curriculum??? I recommend that CS students (found out they can take this as part of the interdisciplinary mod requirement) who echo my opinions of preferring depth and having a more fulfilling semester look somewhere else to fulfil their interdisciplinary module requirements. But if you want to slack and SU yeah go ahead this module is perfect for you.

I did not study at all - just downloaded a ton of compiled notes from the telegram and CTRL-F'ed my way through the exams (it's still very doable this sem but you need general knowledge as backup. Many people did not know what Ad Hominem was during midterms). My only takeaway is probably what culverts are.

Midterms: 73%
3 Workshop Assignments: 84%
Final Grade: A- (forgot to submit a quiz, might've cost me the grade. Both the commenter below and I got the same results but wildly different approaches to studying, take that however you want)

Edit: I forgot what culverts are.
thread_title: HSI1000 How Science Works, Why Science Works

author: Thiru
message:
I took this mod in Semester 1, AY 14/15 by prof Geng (Visiting Prof)

Might I just start with saying that Prof Geng is one of the best profs ever. He is humorous and lessons are always fun. BT1101 is your first and core foundational module in bza. Prof Geng gave an incredibly solid base for all future mods, and the stuff he taught is what is currently being used in the industry today. You will learn:

Association Rule
Classification
Clustering
Recommender Systems
Sentiment Analysis

You will also learn key paradigms such as 'Less is More'. 

We learnt how to use rapidminer, a tool that strips out the need for programming while reinforcing more of the thinking process. The project was also fun, and provides a hands on experience to what you'll be doing in the future.

Be warned tho, the bell curve is INSANE, with the median for midterms being 94/100.

Final Grade: A-

author: Alistair Chia
message:
AY2021/2022 Semester 2
Taught by Prof Lee Wang Yen

2% Trial computerized test
14% Homework assignment (LogiCola)
13% Test 1 (MCQ)
13% Test 2 (MCQ)
18% Computerized test
40% Finals (MCQ)

Workload: 2 hour lecture (weekly), 2 hour tutorial with assignment (0.5-1 hour) (biweeky), 30 mins homework (LogiCola)
I think that the workload is manageable and light enough.

Test 1, Test 2 and Finals are all MCQs, but each question can have more than 1 correct option. I felt these were much harder than the examples and homework I did. These will test concepts covered in the lecture, but you will have to apply them in a much different way. Because of this, scoring well is quite difficult for these tests (as can be seen in other reviews). I highly recommend doing the past year paper prof Lee will provide, it helps you understand how the basic concepts from the lectures are applied in the tests in ways that I would struggle to understand from the lectures and tutorials alone. The homework and trial test are free marks so I recommend completing those. Overall, I don't think this module will be an easy A if that is what you are looking for.

I found the content, which covers formal logic, to be quite interesting, since it taught me to understand how to evaluate the quality of arguments. It also overlapped a bit with some concepts in learnt in Cognitive psychology. If you are not into any of these however, I think the content might be quite dry. Overall, I would recommend this module if you are fond of math, since I think are similar in terms of its more formal nature.

author: anon127294529013
message:
Taken in AY20/21 Sem 1 (Covid sem)
Lecturer: Dr. Henry Chia
Recitation Instructor: Assoc Prof. Ooi Wei Tsang
Grading Scheme: Weekly lab (10%, 1% each), Individual project (10%), 2 Practical Exam (15% and 20%), class participation (5%), Final (40%)

It took me until the end of the semester to really understand WHY i was learning what is being taught in this module. Honestly I didn't study this module up until when they started introducing Functional Programming in Java as the first 7-10 weeks of this module is simply OOP concepts in Java. I literally spent 0 time other than time spend completing the labs, which are not super difficult if you have java experience. At least for the first 7 weeks worth of labs.

Only take this module if Prof Henry or Prof Ooi is taking. They are super dedicated and love their students a lot. Project(s) can be heavy and hard if you don't know how to code in java. You will be forced to learn vim. Especially if you are a freshman and this is your first CS module. Vim was a serious pain in the ass and made me panic during the PE as even after 7 weeks of using vim i still can't get used to it. Especially during PE when you have to use Vim on SoC servers which can sometimes lag and hang.

Practical Exams are manageable, depending on how good you are under exam pressure. They don't test algorithmic thinking but more of design. But you are allowed to take home the PE to solve if you can't solve it in time.

Lectures are really long, module coordinators can consider reducing the length of the lecture. I feel like a lot of the content can be condensed down a lot more and delivered faster.

Finals was unexpected.

Expected Grade: B+/A-
Actual Grade: TBC

Mod rating: 2.5 because of covid sem delivery, if not probably a 3ish/5
Instructors rating: 4.5/5

author: anonymous_peanut
message: AY21/22 Sem 2 Prof. Henry Chia Weekly labs - 10% Individual project - 10% Practical assessment #1 in week 7 - 15% Practical assessment #2 in week 12 - 20% Class participation and peer learning activities - 5% Final exam - 40% The weekly labs can take a few hours or up to the whole weekend to complete depending on your proficiency. There are people who complete the weekly lab assignments within the 2h lab session itself too. The individual project is really tough. I left it till the end and really regretted it. Ultimately ended up finishing it but had to stay up for a few nights. Would be good to get started as soon as the levels start coming out. The practical assignments have a pretty interesting format. You are asked to attempt the practical within the exam timing itself, and then given a week to take home and complete it after. The grading would be based on how much changes are made. Honestly, the curve for the practicals are pretty forgiving so don't worry too much about it. The finals were really tough. To put into perspective, I was able to finish most of both practicals with only 1 or 2 levels left but the finals stumped me too. For the finals, I think a good way to prepare would really just be to go through all the content again and really understand it well. Expected Grade: A Actual Grade: A- Enjoyability: 4/5 (Pretty satisfying to complete the labs but the project was really killing me) Usefulness: 3/5 (Don't see the significance of it as of now) Workload: 4/5

message:
AY21/22 Sem 1 under Prof. Lifeng

There's a 2 hour tutorial and a 2 hour online video to watch every week. The lecture slot is meant as a consultation session whereby you approach with any doubts or questions.

Tutorial Attendance - 5%
4 Problem Sets (homework) - 8%
Mock Midterm Assessment - 2%
Midterm Assessment - 25%
Practical Assessment - 20%
Final Assessment - 40%

The mod is taught fully in Java, covering the basics of programming. We covered loops, conditional statements, simple OOP and basic recursion. 

Each problem set contains about 30 questions, but only required to solve 11 to get the full 2%. The online videos aren't necessary either, you can get by just reading the lecture notes. In total, I spend about an hour reading through the lecture notes and doing the tutorial questions per week. For the problem set, it takes me about 1 hour to complete it.

Personally, as someone with prior programming knowledge, it wasn't too difficult but it was harder to score than expected. The tracing questions during tests were quite tricky and full of "traps". It became much more of how careful you are rather than how well you understood the content.

The practical was much harder than the mock exam. It may prove to be useful to spend more time thinking about how to solve rather than starting to code immediately.

Expected grade: B+
Actual grade: B+

author: anonymous_peanut
message:
AY21/22 Prof. Chan Heng Huat

2 2-hour lectures + 1 hour tutorial per week
Grading was initially meant to be:
Online quizzes (33%)
Tests (66%)

but after some cheating fiasco, it was changed but I'm not sure of the updated grading.

The weekly quizzes were a mix of MCQ, True/False and fill in the blanks question. Not particularly hard but you do have to pay attention to lectures.

Prof. Chan is really caring, you can feel his passion for mathematics when he teaches. He gets back to you with speedy replies on email. I've emailed him 11pm on a Saturday night, expecting to get a response on Monday but he replied almost immediately.

However, I think the lectures sometimes get a bit confusing with international students asking him concepts that aren't in the curriculum. Attending the lectures don't prove to be much good as well, since he seems to go quite fast. It may be better to watch it on webcast and rewind as needed.

TBH after getting my results, I find that it may be better to pay attention to other mods. I've gotten full marks for my online quizzes, and was 87/110 for my first 2 tests.  Just to get to let you get a better understanding, I've spent most of my time on this mod and I still ended up with a B.

Expected grade: B+
Actual grade: B

author: ap3jedi
message: AY25/25 S1 Prof Nicholas Mac Gregor Garcia Took this module due to my interest in breaking into fintech. Was a little taken aback at how much finance (time value of money, valuation of bonds/stocks etc.) content there was at the start ~ the entire midterm was on the finance topics covered. Then it made more sense why afterwards, because the group project itself required submission of your fintech venture's projected financials. This module introduces you to a few of the hot topics of fintech today - mainly on payments - existing and new rails including blockchain (layer 1 and 2) - which is covered only in the latter half of the semester. If you'd like to go deep and technical into the technologies itself, I don't think this is the point of the module. However, if you'd like to be exposed to how the above topics are integrated into the financial sector and perhaps a sandbox environment to test your novel fintech ideas, this may be the module for you. Personal preference, but it would have been nice to have a little less finance, and more tech. Either... Breadth: touching upon more technologies used in L/M/HFT trading or market making (quant), AI/ML , or alternative payment rails (the likes of Revolut, Wise, YouTrip & Airwallex) OR Depth: deeper dive into blockchain, like fundamentals on dApps and smart contracts development As a computing student, this module is useful if you'd like to: - break into global payments solutions arms of traditional banks (otherwise known as transaction banking) -- (to compete with biz and econ kids haha) - be exposed to Web3 & blockchain (module only touches upon the payments and crypto aspect) - explore fintech entrepreneurship/startups (grp project is akin to a VC pitch, with financials as mentioned) - simply gain a little business acumen as a tech student - find an evening lecture that aligns with your ATAP lol Final Grade: A+ (@Prof Garcia hire me as a TA, please!)

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Prof: Gayatri Thanu Pillai

Grading:
Midterms: 30%
Group Presentation: 15%
Attendance & Class Participation: 10%
Final Submission: 45%

Prof Gayatri is very passionate during lectures and tutorials. Lectures are not recorded, only lecture slides is uploaded. Tutorials are alternate weeks, but actually the last week is used for group presentations so honestly quite few tutorials throughout the sem.

Midterms was straightforward if you read the lecture slides diligently. It covered most concepts introduced in the lecture slides. It was 30 MCQs. Can definitely "mug" for it and score well.

Group Presentation is mainly on the field trip which you will attend in place of one of your tutorial slot. The field trip is quite fun, mine was planting trees. Although it was on a Saturday morning, I found it quite fun and enriching. The group presentation requirements are clearly stated and each group have about 10 people, and you present for 10 minutes, so you basically speak for a minute or so LOL. But do work diligently on your group presentation as it covers the main portions you have to include for your Final Submission. Final Submission involves writing about your experience at the field trip and the connections to the literary ecological readings covered throughout the Sem. If you are lazy like me, do at least use ChatGPT to summarise the readings and be familiar with the readings to make it easier for your Final Submission.

Overall, this module is very literature based, and has quite a few readings over the course of the module so it can be quite dry. However, I think it's a pretty good module to take to clear your communities pillar, since it can be done in 1 sem and is definitely scorable if you dont want to SU.

Midterms: UQ
rest of components are unknown.

Expected Grade: A-/A
Actual Grade: Unknown

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Prof: Michael Choi

Grading:
6 Assignments - 20%
Midterms - 30%
Finals - 50%

Overall Thoughts:
Lectures were generally shorter than the usual 1.5hours, but Prof Michael still manages to cover the content coherently and  provide some intuitions to concepts, especially in the tougher later topics.

Assignments were generally ok, only the 1st assignment was tedious. Prof Michael includes 1-2 coding questions each assignment, which we can answer in whichever language (R, Python, Excel) we are comfortable with. Assignments do help to consolidate learning so it was generally very useful for me. Tutorials have few questions, and that will be one of the complain I have, since coming from ST2131 where there were tons of tutorial questions to practice, ST2132 offered much fewer. Nonetheless, the tutorials do help to consolidate learning for each chapter more or less.

Midterms was tough for me, maybe due to lack of practice. Question on linear regression made me super confused and I guess I almost got no marks from it. Midterms involve some MCQ and some Structured Questions. 

Finals was pretty straight forward, and a lot easier than Midterms for me. Similar to midterms, it had 6 MCQ worth 5 marks and 70 marks worth of Structured Questions. Be consistent and try to get the intuition behind the concepts covered and it will definitely be easier for you in exams.

Both midterms and finals allowed 1 HANDWRITTEN A4 cheat sheet.

Overall, this module covers quite a bit of concepts that are fairly abstract and it really isn't that easy to get a grasp of it. If not for it being a core module, I wont take it. I think it is important to strengthen your ST2131 basics because continuous r.v is quite widely used throughout this module. 

Assignments: Full marks (Most people get full marks for all assignments too)
Midterms: /65 (Not sharing exact marks but I got somewhere between median and UQ)
Finals: /100 (Unknown but quite confident it's near full marks assuming no carelessness)

Expected Grade: A-/A
Actual Grade: tbc

author: bellcurvefeeder
message: AY23/24 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Tim Wertz Structure: 4x HW - 20% 1x Midterms - 30% 1x Finals - 50% Workload: 4/10 HW were spaced out, and a lot of time is given to complete each HW. Generally, this module touches many algorithms on the surface level. If you want to understand deeper beyond the module, then the workload may increase as you explore the enrichment section in Prof Wertz slides. Otherwise, the workload is a lot less than expected. Difficulty: 3/10 I struggled the most with counting operations throughout the module, but otherwise, most algorithms are quite straight forward and easily applied in exams. Tutorials were also straight-forward, but covers whatever essentially concepts we have to know. Enjoyment: 9/10 I love calculations so this module was fun to me. Much intense calculations were required throughout the modules. Love how Prof Wertz uses Piazza to clarify our queries, which I actively used and his response is also super quick! HW: HW were mostly straightforward, but each HW consists of a question which requires you to write a code. Most HW median were near full marks, except for HW2, whereby there was a question on counting operations which took me very long to solve. Otherwise, HW are very useful imo, as it touches on most of the concepts covered in lectures and serves as a platform for me to check my understanding. Midterms: Consists of 4 questions. It was quite straight-forward but I made many careless mistakes and was overconfident. Ended up paying the price for it as I obtained a result way below my expectations, especially with how well the entire cohort did for mid-terms. Finals: Consists of 4 questions. Q1 caught me off-guard as I expected either T/F questions or explain statements as in mid-terms. Especially how counting operations were my weakest link in this module, I suffered doing Q1. Otherwise, questions are very standard and pretty much expected, though I couldn't do certain parts of Q2. Overall Rating: 9/10 Super fun calculation module, with quite a light workload. Bellcurve is quite left-skewed though, nonetheless, it is a module that one can easily score well. Do note exams are closed book (No cheat sheet), but with consistent work, all the algorithms etc should be second nature to you by exams! Results: HW: Near full marks Mid-terms: 42/50 (UQ 43) Finals: ?? Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: TBC

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY2324 Sem 1 
Lecturer: Prof Bernard Tan
Assistant: Lydia Lam

Structure:
Essay: 20%
MIDI: 20%
Mid-Terms: 30%
End-Terms: 30%

Workload: 2/10
Lectures were all pre-recorded and 2 were uploaded on the website weekly. Slides were also uploaded alongside the lectures. Honestly, reading the slides is mostly sufficient. For me, I read the slides and went to the lectures for parts I couldn't understand. Essay and MIDI were both due quite late into the semester, Week 10 and Week 13 respectively iirc. So for the first half of the semester, there is only the Mid term test to study for.

Difficulty: 3/10
The 3 is purely for the MIDI project. As someone with no musical background, it was difficult. I wasn't sure how or where to start, but after watching some basic composition videos, I got some inspiration and worked my way from there. While it was difficult for me, it was quite fun playing around with the MIDI platform. MIDI is probably the only components whereby people with music background have an edge over others, so if you don't have musical background, fret not, the rest of the components have little to no emphasis on musical background.

Enjoyment: 5/10
It was a module I took because I know it has a relatively low workload. However, I did learn more than what I expected from this module and did have fun creating the 20-60s MIDI music piece.

Overall Rating: 8/10
Mid-terms and End-terms are very math and physics orientated and involves quite a bit of calculation. However, it is very easy to score as the format is identical to PYPs. Therefore, you just have to actively do the PYPs and should be able to do well for both tests. However, this means the tests are very left skewed and any careless mistakes could be detrimental to your final grade. Essay required no more than 800 words on your experience at a concert. It was a relieve for me as I don't think they expect you to have a lot of musical rigor within your essay but you still have to demonstrate how science and technology impacted your experience. Otherwise, the essay was generally quite vague and allows you to express your experience quite freely. 

Results:
Mid-Terms: 24/25 (Mean: 20)
End-Term Test: 25/25 (Mean: 20)
MIDI & Essay: Unknown

Expected Grade: A- (Don't have much confidence about my MIDI piece)
Actual Grade: TBC

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Wang Fei and Prof Tim Wertz

Structure:
4 Quizzes and 4 Homework 5% each 
Finals 60%

Workload: 7/10
There are a total of 8 assignments (4 Homework and 4 Quizzes). Quizzes were quite tedious to me, but in general the Quiz and Homework are all generally doable, best is if you take with friends so that you can discuss. Weekly tutorials attendance are not taken, and answers are uploaded. Lectures are also uploaded on Canvas.

Difficulty: 9/10
Tutorials were very difficult to me and I struggled to do quite a lot of questions in each tutorial. I spent much time understanding tutorial questions and its concept, eventually somewhat getting the gist of it. I advice whoever taking this course to properly understand the concepts of the questions in tutorial and not brush them aside. For this semester, there was no midterms, which means that there was no platform to properly determine our proficiency for the first half of the semester, so we had to actively review our understanding of the topics covered.

Enjoyment: 5/10
A huge part of the lectures were on proofs and it got overwhelming after awhile. The last 2 chapters especially were very theoretical and I couldn't picture what was going on. However, I do seek some enjoyment from the other chapters as I enjoy certain portions like solving double integrals etc. 

Final Rating: 6/10
To me, it is a hard module that took up pretty much most of my time this semester. Finals was generally doable to me, but I couldn't do the last question which was 20 marks. But actively doing tutorials and understanding the answers did help in the finals as some concepts tested were similar to certain questions in tutorials. While I dreaded this module in the first 6 weeks, I slowly understood the topics covered and it got a lot less overwhelming as I approached finals. Do try to be consistent in this module (watch videos etc to try to understand certain concepts), as I have seen others that struggled quite a bit after failing to keep up with the pace of lectures and tutorials etc. Do try to attempt the tutorial questions before tutorial no matter how tough it is.

Results:
Quiz + Homework: 39.5/40 (Very left skewed as how all math/stats mods are)
Finals: ?/100 (Didn't know how to do 20 marks)

Expected Grade: B+/A-
Actual Grade: TBC

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1

Assessments:
5% Book Chapter Reflection
20% Science News Article 1 (600-800 words)
5% Peer Review of Science News Article
30% Science News Article 2 (800-1000 words)
10% Reflective Commentary (300 words)
25% Individual Oral Presentation
5% Participation

General Module Structure:
Book Chapter Reflection was done in groups of 3 and for me, we could choose our groupmates. Article 1 and 2 are using writing strategies we learnt in tutorials and applying it to our writings to educate and engage general public about certain scientific findings. Reflective Commentary is done together with Article 2 and is submitted at the end of the semester. Oral presentation is 8-10 minutes with a 3 minute Q&A session. Peer Review is done on your peers Article 1 submission to help them improve their Article 2 submission.

Workload: 5/10
In general, work load is generally low until end of semester period since you have to submit your second submission, prepare for your 8-10 minutes oral presentation etc. 

Fun: 3/10
The most fun aspect is probably the fact that you can choose any article within the scope of your study, so please do strategically choose an article you are passionate about. It will make everything onwards easier and much less of a chore since your essay submissions and oral presentation are just developed from the article you choose. Do start early in seeking for your chosen article because there are certain requirements to satisfy, alongside wanting it to be an article you can write eloquently on etc.

Overall Thoughts: 
Tutorials are now 1x3h instead of 2x2h which is good because less travelling time for me. Always planned to use a SU on this module from the start but was forced to take this in Y2S1 because I did my QET after Y1S1 so couldn't take this module in Y1 to maximize my SU. I gave an adequate amount of effort, but what I couldn't conquer is the oral presentation due to my intense stage fright. Held my phone throughout the presentation (Do memorize your script for the Oral Presentation, everyone else in my tutorial evidently prepared very well), even though in the end I didn't refer to my phone but was evidently stuttering and very nervous due to lack of preparation. Looking back, it is quite possible to score well for this module with consistent effort.

Results:
Book Chapter Reflection:  72/100
Science News Article 1: 75.4/100
Individual Oral Presentation: 71.3/100
Science News Article 2: 71.9/100
Reflective Commentary: 100/100
Peer Review of Science News Article & Participation: Unknown

Expected Grade: B-/B
Actual Grade: TBC

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/25 Sem 2
Prof Lim Tiong Wee

40% Tutorial worksheets + homework assignments

60% Finals

No midterms.

I think the prof is one of the better, if not one of the best stats lecturer because he makes an effort to be clear and gives a lot of examples. He is also eloquent.

Downside is that content can be quite dry which is a common problem amongst all stats courses.

Tutorial worksheets are done in class in a group of 3-4. They are usually doable.

Homework assignments are extensions typically of the tutorial worksheets and are usually doable as well. However, he marks a bit stricter for homework so do make sure you be a bit more detailed otherwise you’ll get little points deducted here and there like me.

Finals … terrible. Terrible doesn’t even begin to describe my experience. 60 marks 2 hours 4 questions and I wasn’t able to solve a single one, with two of them as good as blank. It was terrible for me. I’m not sure if it was just my performance but equations were really tedious and ugly and my numbers were all so weird. 

I even studied, and typically during tutorials, I was able to solve the questions eventually. 

I might fail the course.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/25 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Xia Yingcun 

Midterms + Tutorials:  30 - 40%
Finals: 60 - 70%

Physical lectures, no attendance. There were recordings.

Uhhh it's average + a bit in difficulty. You start with some introduction to simple finance knowledge + a bit of portfolio theory, then go into more difficult statistical models and things like copula, time series and what not.

I think this course was fine until copula and time series then I think my life fell apart from thereafter. 

It's also a stats course so my interest were low (yes I'm a stats major) and I was y4 so motivation to do anything was also low.

The prof is not very eloquent but I think he tries to annotate and stuff to make things clearer. He is approachable. He also tends to be a bit awkward and soft-spoken.

Midterms was alright but it was graded really leniently by him.

Finals was... I felt it should have been manageable but I couldn't do a couple of questions and many of my answers were pretty uncertain so god knows man. Praying for a B+.

It's all open book, physical notes only. No coding expected, but interpretation of code results is expected.

The cohort size was 23 so I doubt there's bellcurve so I really have no clue how it's going to pan out :/ welp.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY2024/2025 Semester 2 
TA: Kana Sensei
TB: Utsumi Sensei
Lecture: Hamasaki Sensei 

Workload: Manageable. There's a exchange project with the Japanese Primary School kids which was pretty fun. If you can't make it, there was one with Niigata University which I did not go.

Content: You learn to better express yourself such as "I think", "I've been thinking", "if" etc. It may not be as useful for people who just want to learn simple Japanese for travelling, but it would be useful for people who wants to be able to do simple conversation with Japanese.

Difficulty: Honestly okay. 

Weekly vocab quiz is simple. Sometimes I made careless mistakes because I did not notice the tense. 

Listening quiz is as usual, hard. Some questions were really a coin toss. 

Test 1 (Midterm) was easy imo. I only lost a couple of marks to listening. 

Test 2 (Final) was still manageable but I had a brain fog so I made some really silly mistakes, and wasn't sure of my answers for some. 

Oral was... disappointing for me because I panicked and made some errors that I think shouldn't have been expected of me. You would be given a role play to act out for 2 minutes between you and your partner, and also then talk about 2 * 2 topics for 2 minutes each between you and your partner. It's definitely difficult to gauge how much to talk. My partner and I spoke fast so we finished fast... but I'm not sure if they'll grade the content based on how much we've said or how much time we've pad.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Prof Sam Jindani

Tutorial 20%
Midterms 30%
Finals 50%

Most interesting economics course I've ever taken. Toughest economics course I've ever taken. Every other 3k econs I've seen is a borderline walk in the park but this one was w h a c k for an econs course.

Prof Sam is pretty alright. He is British (I think) so he's naturally eloquent but I think it'll help if he spoke louder. I'll appreciate if the concepts are illustrated more clearly, and if there are less typos in the solutions and lecture notes :P. Overall, nice prof. One thing to note is that I think game theory under Prof Sam apparently covers more content and can be more mathematical.

Concepts, especially after midterms, can be quite abstract and sometimes needs quite a bit of thinking.

Tutorial you present a question. Attendance is also graded.

Midterms was kinda tough, but he marked really leniently. Had he not, I doubt I would've passed.

I felt that the finals question were supposed to be more manageable but perhaps I clowned too hard and couldn't really solve a few parts.

Considering my finals performance, I'm not expecting an insanely good grade. Also, like what the ancient comments mentioned, somehow this course attracts really smart people and it's rather evident. I'm not one of them sadly.

However it had been, it was enjoyable.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Prof Alex Thiery

Quizz: 10%
Project: 30% 2*15% each
Midterm: 30%
Final: 30%

I took this to fulfil part of my specialisation in Data Science.

I think Prof Alex is pretty good and he's pretty chill. He's very approachable and contactable. Nice prof, even better considering it's stats department. 

We had pre--lecture videos to go through content. Then lecture slots are used for live-coding exercises. Only 1 of the 2 lecture slot is used, the other one is freed up. 

Midterms was okay difficulty, well-set so the distribution wasn't too right or left skewed. A bit left skewed but not too bad. 

Finals was harder, but I feel it's still reasonably well set. 

For projects, he gives a list and you pick 2 to do. It can be group or individual (bless no forced group work). He's open to give feedback on preliminary drafts submitted 2 weeks before the submission deadline, and you can always seek the prof out on teams to have clarifications and discussions. 

I'm bad at linear algebra and anything multi-dimension so I don't think I'll perform very well in this course. However, I did gain a better understanding on the different optimisation tools and techniques out there, and their implementation.

Would recommend.
thread_title: DSA4212 Optimisation for Large-Scale Data-Driven Inference

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Prof Zhang Jin-Ting

Final exam (2 hours)                 60 % 1*1 page cheat sheet
Midterm test (1  hour and 30 minutes)    25% 1*1 page cheat sheet
Class/Tutorial Performance   15%

Bad. 
It's those courses where in the first lecture, you hear the lecturer speak and you go 'I guess this is one of those courses'. Regret not dropping it and taking another ST4K instead.

Content wise, it's a repeat of ST3247 with a bit more depth as well as introduction of some concepts like metropolis hastings, gibbs sampling and bootstrapping.

The lecturer has a really thick chinese accent, and his slides are filled with words, symbols and equations and a ton of pages because he included the slide animations in the pdf so there was a lot of duplicated pages. I really respect him for his research output (he's stacked af in research) but I genuinely don't think he can teach well, though I guess I shouldn't expect much from the stats department. And of course, one has to include my poor probability and mathematical statistics background into consideration.

Tutorials are graded, and you are expected to submit it every week. He grades really leniently. However, I did not really care so I did not get full marks for all my tutorials because I really can't be bothered with this course.

Lectures were 6pm-8pm and tutorials take place at around 8pm. They are recorded but god blast this damn course because tutorial attendance are graded what the hell.

Midterms was ok difficulty. I performed slightly worse than median because I can't be bothered.
Finals was much harder I feel, even compared to his 2004 past year paper. Yes, the past year paper he gave us was from 2004.

This course may become a stain on my transcript but at this point I'm just so glad I'm done with this. High key regret taking this under him.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/24 Sem 1
Dr. Samriddha Lahiry

2*assignments*5% each 
Midterms 30%. 1page cheatsheet.
Finals 60%. 1 page cheatsheet.

Midterms and assignments are both baby difficulty.

Finals is still easy in general, but a few small questions that I'm not too sure about. Plenty of the questions were lifted from the tutorial.

This course's bellcurve is s t e e p. It's so steep it's probably a vertical line and all the grade distributions are left skewed as hell. 

I think the lecturer is okay, not too shabby. I have my expectations set super low for stats department's teaching. I didn't go for lectures because 8am. Recordings were available. 

I think it's cool to have a chill course but it's not cool when your grade depends on whether you made any careless mistake :(. Stats courses are literally coin toss between this and hell.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
LAJ2201 AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecture: Hamasaki Sensei
Tutorial A: Hamasaki Sensei
Tutorial B: Morikawa Sensei

Build up from Jap 1, now with more confusing usage of particles and introduced various forms to conjugate different verbs etc. Also introduced plain style conversation and writing.

Bellcurve is still steep which is a bit annoying for me since I'm quite a careless person, especially when it comes to vocab quiz. I may have made enough silly mistakes to perhaps even drop myself to an A- but it is what it is.

Both sensei are nice and teaches pretty well.

I don't really spend too much time on this course to be honest but ymmv. Pretty enjoyable and manageable in terms of workload for me.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY24/24 Sem 1
Frau Sentosa

Frau Sentosa is pretty energetic and interesting. She encourages participation and gets everyone involved. In general, I think I've not had bad experience with language courses thus far (I took LAJ1201 and LAJ2201 as well). 

I think the tough parts of this course are
1. There's a shit ton of vocabulary and you also have to remember the gender associated with each noun. 
2. The sentence structure composition of German is quite different from English so it can take quite a while to get used to. 
3. Conjugation is truly whack. 
4. Pronunciation can take awhile to get used to . 
In general, tough course. However, bell curve is still steep because welcome to NUS.

I took this course because I had a spare SU in my fourth year and wanted to use it. Nevertheless, I did still put in a fair bit of effort (though not a lot) to ensure I learn something.

Would recommend.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Dr Mun Lai Yoke
TA: Cai ChunBing

Class Participation: 15%
Quizzes: 10%
Midterms: 30%
Final Exam: 45%

Lecture: I think Prof Mun is pretty good for most people. I'm just not one of them. She is patient, and rather eloquent and energetic but I just can't find lectures interesting so I'm disassociated most of the time. Maybe it's the content but I think most people would like her. 

Tutorial: ChunBing is fine but she tend to ramble on even when the question/concept involved is trivial so tutorials can feel draggy at times. Tutorial questions are not too difficult. You are expected to do group discussion and have one person present, which I feel is quite redundant as the questions' difficulty does not demand group effort. 

Quizzes: Easy. 

Midterms: Closed book. Super easy. It was all MCQ on Examplify and most of the questions was so trivial. LQ/M/UQ = 26/29/31 out of 33.

Finals: Open book, access to computer stored notes but no internet. Mix of MCQ and analytical questions on Examplify. The calculation questions are easy again but the analytical questions are actually quite a challenge to me. There are several questions that would require you to write a response/analysis, which is 3 marks each. Total is 65 marks. But I'm satisfied that it's not a super trivial paper again like midterms. I also wondered if she could set adequate papers like finals, why didn't she do it for midterms.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Prof Prabhu Natrajan
TA: Naman Agrawal 

One of the more useful CHS courses, especially for Stats/DSA people. 

Tutorial Attendance	5%
Class Participation	5%
Assignments	      20%
Midterm	                      20%
Final Exam	              25%
Project	                      25%

Coursemology instead of canvas.
No live lectures, but recordings.

Everything is more or less OTOT, so as long as you finish them before the deadline, it's fine. I finished nearly everything in week 3, and completed the remaining 1 or 2 assignment/quiz in week 4 so I only had to care about the tests and project.

Recordings are fine but it can get a little dry.

Tutorial by Naman is pretty well conducted. You can tell that his passion for AI and DS is well above the common folk.

Assignments are quite manageable.

Quizzes are also pretty manageable and you are allowed to make some mistakes to get the entire credit.

Midterm is adequately set, though the question phrasing can sometimes be a bit off/ambiguous. Always clarify if you are in doubt. LQ/M/UQ = 32.67/35/37.13 out of 46.

Project can be a bit tough if you are new to programming and DS in general. You are supposed to pick amongst datasets of varying difficulty to do a DS project (regression, classification, computer vision etc.) and prepare a jupyter notebook, a report and presentation. I think I learnt the most from this project, but given that it's a group project, ymmv if you end up with bad group mates. I got pretty good ones.
Only advice is to not bite off more than what you can chew, if you are not that confident, just stick to easy datasets. LQ/M/UQ = 75.13/82.5/89 out of 100.

Finals is doable, but quite tricky I'll say. Adequately set, but again, some questions' phrasing can be unnatural/ambiguous so clarify if you need to. There is adequate time to solve all questions.

Arguably the CHS course I enjoyed the most.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Prof He BingSheng
Prof Ai Xin

I took this as a UE. 

Chill course.
Two assignments that you can complete in a day with really long due dates. 25% each.

Finals is 50%, it was harder than the past sem paper given to us as practice. I would say most of the questions are doable, but some I wasn't so sure about my answers.

Tutorials were typically conducted after lecture, and there were only 4 tutorials in total.

Overall I expect a steep bell curve. Nevertheless, it's a fine course to get an introduction in to HDFS and Spark without adding on too much to your workload.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Lecturers:
Dr Robin Loon
Mr Jeffrey Tan
Miss Peggy Ferroa

Tutorial: Miss Junesse

You learn various theatre techniques with the goal of engaging a community. You are not expected to procure a theatre performance nor write a script. You also do not actually interact with the said community, nor carry out any community service.

Tutorial Participation - 30%
Individual Reflections x 2 - 20%
Final Pitch - 30%
Final Exegesis/Reflection  - 20%

The pitch involves you, as a group, visiting an area of choice to observe, and not interact at all, a community of choice. Then, you are supposed to, as a group, come up with a hypothetical feasible plan to engage the community with the various theatre techniques you have learnt. The pitch is 3000 - 4000 words in total spread across 3 sections.

Lectures are fine. It's not recorded.
Tutorials are also pretty alright and engaging. No prior preparation is really necessary.

Reflections are reflections, write. 

I took the course to fulfil the Community and Engagement pillar, and because it's only one semester. I took the course with the full intention to SU. It's a pretty low workload course.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Lecturer: Morikawa sensei
Tutorial A: Morikawa sensei
Tutorial B: Lai Siew Hoon sensei

Took it for interest, and the only regret I have is not taking this earlier. 

During lecture, you learn new vocabularies and sentence structures. You also get some opportunities to practise speaking, albeit not much. Morikawa sensei is nice and clear, and she makes an effort in making the lecture engaging. However, sometimes the lecture hall can get a bit noisy and she can struggle in getting the students attention back because she's quite a soft spoken person. She sometimes would also share Japanese music or events, which is well appreciated.

Tutorials are where you mainly practise speaking. To benefit the most out of it, I would advise to be more proactive and be not afraid of making mistakes. Both Morikawa sensei and Lai sensei conducted the tutorials well. I enjoyed the sessions every week.

Frankly, the workload can be quite substantial for procrastinators because this course demands consistent effort, and doesn't reward last minute efforts. Do your homework and practices consistently, and you should find the workload rather alright honestly. Homework come in the form of course packet practices, Hyoojun Mondaishuu practices and Kaiwa Shadowing. I enjoyed the course so I didn't find the homework a chore at all.

There are weekly vocab quizzes to test on newly learnt vocabularies. These quizzes aren't difficult, but careless mistakes can occur here and there. 

There are 2 listening tests. The first one is rather alright but the second one was quite tricky and tough. 

There are also 2 term tests, one in the middle of the semester and one at the end. Difficulty is alright, but the listening section, again, can be difficult to get full marks in. 

Course material wise, Hyoojun Mondaishuu physical copy is essential. The other materials can be pdf.

Ultimately, I think the best approach to this course (or language courses in general) is to look past the grade aspect, be consistent and be proactive. The bell curve can be steep, but if you let the fear of getting a undesirable grade stop you from learning a new language, I'd wager the one who's at a loss is you.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Dr Nick Huang
Dr Vik Gopal

Tutorial TA: Chai Jing Wen

10 % - Weekly tutorial participation	
25 % - Problem sets 
20 % - Mid-term test 
20 % - Project
25 % - Final essay

Course is about learning some linguistics like semantics, word syntax and how to perform simple NLP using python.

I heavily, immensely regret picking this course. 
Even though I picked this course with the full intention to SU from the start, the workload is just too much and the CA can be quite troublesome. 

Lecture was recorded. For the linguistics part, it's taught by prof Nick. He is fine, but his attempts to get lecture response can feel quite flimsy. I think he can explore better methods like pollev if he really do intend to get better lecture responses. 
Data science part is taught by prof Vik. I took this course partly because of the good reputation prof Vik has in teaching ST/DSA courses, and it's my first time learning under him. I think he's eloquent and I appreciate the 5 minute break in the middle of the lecture, but the data science part is actually not that easy and as expected, can feel quite dry. One lecture had additional required supplementary video viewings that I did not watch, but I do wonder how many actually bothered to.

There are 3 problem sets, which are supposed to be done in groups assigned in tutorial. The problem sets are typically jupyter notebooks with some codes at the top and some discussion questions at the bottom for us to answer. The questions can be quite cumbersome and tedious to answer. 

Midterms allowed 1 double sided cheat sheet, it was on examplify. It was a mix of MCQ and expository questions that required you to write. No coding was needed. I flipped through the materials the day before the test so I can't give a good judgement on the midterms difficulty. It doesn't help that it's week 13 and we still don't know our midterms score. 

Project required you to pick 1 out of 5 topics as a group to make a 12 minute video on. It would require you to scrape data from the web, but fortunately, web scrapping is NOT taught so you have to figure it out on your own :D, or ask the one who is more familiar with coding to figure it out on his own. The video needs to have captions, like wtf, it's the first course I've seen to have explicit instructions on caption requirements. Then, you are assigned another group's video to view and give comments. It's honestly like a massive self-study and ChatGPT segment. 

Final essay is an indivdual TWO THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED WORDS 2500 WORDS 两千五百字 にせんごひゃくじ yo wtf man I lose my shit bruh. This CA is probably dedicated to all the arts major because as a Science major, this segment is a mega mood. You pick 1 out of 4 topics and is expected to write an essay on the topic and in their words: aimed at a non-specialist, layperson audience: think of someone who is interested in language but not familiar with the technical details, e.g. a fellow student who has never taken HS2914 or a linguistics course.

You also get assigned 4 datacamp tasks: 3 courses and 1 chapter to complete. 

I feel like this course had a good intention and I think the professors are passionate in this field. However, I think they are over ambitious in their approach. Content aside, I think the essay writing just irks science students and the coding and data science part can be quite difficult for arts students. I really think you have to be super invested and interested in linguistics AND NLP to really like this course.
