author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 2
Prof Lim Chinghway 

Tutorial Attendance 10%
Assignment x4 30%
Finals 60%

You learn how to generate from various densities and the respective R codes using Random Uniforms. You also learn stuff like Monte Carlo Simulation, estimation, importance sampling, variance reduction, validation etc.

Prof Lim is actually pretty eloquent. He's one of the better stats prof I have encountered thus far. He's really kind and approachable as well.

Content to me was kinda tough, my weak background in ST2131 and 2132 kind of amplifies it. Shit starts to hit the fence when rejection method surfaced and then I start to get confused. 

Tutorial is mass conducted, and the questions are actually not easy. I started off diligently attempting then, but as I get lost in the sauce, I slowly started losing motivation and begun slacking off. 

The assignments are much more manageable. However, I did make quite a few mistakes which landed me at below the lower quartile for scoring 8 and 8.5 for 2 assignments. The upper quartile is 10/10 :') 

I wasn't feeling too bumped until the finals. The finals was actually pretty manageable? I had little motivation to study so I didn't put in much effort but I end up being able to solve all the questions and submit early. Though, I may have been unable to spot my mistakes or misunderstandings if any, given my weak foundation and understanding of content. After the finals, I felt like I should've cared about the assignments a bit more as I think the bellcurve would be quite steep, and thus each little % may make quite a bit of difference. 

However, I'm already year 2 going to year 3, so I'm becoming more nonchalant about grades. Do take cautions when doing your assignments if you are still conscientious about scoring a high grade. 

Overall, the content was kinda rough and tough for me so I started losing steam and motivation. However, I actually had decent lecturer and materials this time so I'm attributing any low score to my lack of effort and IQ.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 2
Prof Wong Wei Kang

Quiz 10 - 20%
Tutorial Attendance & Participation 10%
Midterms 30%
Finals 40 - 50%

My TA was Shane but he's not going to TA this course anymore. 

My friend from NTU who took Prof Wong's financial economics course during SUSEP had good reviews of him, so I  had relatively high expectations. Unfortunately, Prof Wong's style of teaching doesn't click well for me. I think you either find him good or he's not for you, not much in between. There's no physical lecture, that was a first L for me because I hate zoom and recorded lectures. Though the lecture slot is 8am, I'll give it to him that I hate 8am physical lectures even more. Lectures before week 7 was still on zoom, after week 7 was just pure recording. His eloquence is okay, but I just can't find myself concentrating during zoom lectures or watching the recordings. You'll probably enjoy it a lot more if zoom/recordings are to your liking. 

The math used in this course is quite laughable. Pretty trivial to be frank. The toughest you get is simple partial differentiation of trivial functions. I feel like the math level is ~secondary school. 

There's quite a lot of content though, so if you are a person who can't do math for shit and just loves qualitative macro content, you'll love it. Sadly, I don't. I was largely uninterested and unmotivated for the course because I found it so boring. Slides are wordy, and the content just refuse to be absorbed in my brain. 

The tutorial questions are just largely repetition of lecture content. Not much to be said, I found it lame. I think I stopped attempting tutorials after midterms, and stopped listening during tutorials after that as well. The lack of motivation and interest hit its peak. 

Midterms was slightly interesting, but apparently too tough for the general cohort. MCQ component /28, written /7. MCQ median was a 12, upper quartile at 16. Written median was a 3, upper quartile at 4.5. I was slightly above upper quartile. 

Finals was mega lame. I thought I was doing JC econs. There's 0% math, purely graph and analysis. Just draw a ton of graphs and write a ton, nothing much to be said. You would score well if you bother to memorise. I didn't, wasn't motivated enough to care. People below mentioned doing textbook questions, but looking at the finals question, think you would be fine skipping out textbook and just memorise lecture notes.

If you don't really care about the overall take away, I think for finals, memorisation can easily get you a pass or even higher. 

That being said, I have to give credits to Prof Wong for making himself largely available for consultation. So if you find the content interesting, do use it to your advantage. TAs are approachable too.

Overall, I didn't really enjoy it but I'm aware of people who find his teaching style pleasant, so ymmv. Normally I would strive for an A in econs courses but I'll settle for an A- for this course.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 2
Prof Vu Thanh Hai

Midterms 30%
Finals 40%
Project 20%
Attendance & Participation 10%

R stuffs.
What is vector, matrix etc, how to write loop, functions, working with date and time and some common package in the tidyverse. Content is pretty easy and workload is non-existent, but I had quite a long R background. Nevertheless, R is known to be beginner friendly so I doubt it'll be much of a steep learning curve even for beginners.
It's an ok module to help solidify foundations, especially if you have only one introductory module in R or self taught using online resources. Though if you are an advanced R user, as much as you can 'smurf' (nearly everyone here is a smurf), you would just be wasting your time. I did learn quite a bit of stuff though.

Midterms was easy but I was mega clowning and decide to be careless and mess up big time. Don't be like me and you should score really well. Finals was harder, but most questions are still manageable. Though, 7 coding questions to solve in 2 hours wasn't easy for me either, then again, I wasn't feeling that smart that day so it might be just me.

Prof Vu is a nice and kind prof, but his effectiveness is impeded by his  command of English, both speaking and question instructions wise. Nevertheless, it's bearable and more so given the simple content. He does make really lame dad jokes, hardly anyone laughs. He's approachable but highly busy, takes quite a long time to reply to my emails. Given lecture notes and tutorials can be quite messy and disorganised, with varied naming structure that could make an OCD person irk. 

Project is group work. I had a functional group so not much to say about that. Given task isn't too complicated, and can be solo'ed if your teammates are absolute junk deadweights. Easier and take less time than my CS2040 lab assignments.

Overall, pretty ok pretty chill. Class size is small so he might not use bellcurve, which should work in most people's favour since the difficulty of the course isn't high.
thread_title: EC3305R Economic Data Analysis with R Programming

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 2
Prof Sng Tuan Hwee

Class participation: 10%
Quiz: 2 x 5% (total 10%)
Midterm: 30%
Final: 50%

Content is pretty easy and workload is nearly non-existent. 
However, questions tested in quiz and test can be rather tricky and require careful attention to subtle details.

Prof Sng is nice, a bit way too nice. He speaks eloquently and inserts some jokes here and there so I guess he's pretty good.

My TA was Wang Mengru who is okay. The tutorials are generally easy so I never really pay attention in class apart from extensions on concepts or novel questions that require more thinking.

The midterms was easy, but I did have a few careless mistakes so I only landed 2% higher than the upper quartile, pity. Upper quartile was 22/30, median 19. 

Finals was a lot trickier with more game theory stuff, the rubinstein bargaining question was kinda whack. But I wished for a tougher finals to help smoothen the steep bellcurve and I guess he delivered. It's not tough until you can't pass though.

Math used was pretty elementary. Simple differentiation, simple simul equations, secondary school stuff. Concepts though, esp game theory and public goods/externalities can take some time to get comfortable and grasp. The prof also showed through finals that he can make difficult questions if he wants to.

Overall, rather easy, I wish he had made quizzes/midterms harder. Expect a non gentle bellcurve.

author: ChlorineCl2
message: The project score came out and even for that, it's pretty left skewed so I guess this mod has a rather steep bell curve.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 2
Dr Stephane Bressan

30% Group Project
25% Test x 2 
10% Individual Project x 2

Allowed to miss two tutorials with no penalty, -1% overall for each tutorial missed afterwards. 

I quite like the lecture. The Prof's accent is pretty endearing and he is really engaging. He's so engaging that he literally walks up near you and maintains eye contact, which can be pretty daunting sometimes. However, his ability to not read from the slides makes him a pretty good lecturer. 

We had 3 TAs Sri, Jia Hui and Mehdi. 
Sri was in charge of tutorials relating to writing SQL queries. She's alright. 
Jia Hui was in charge of tutorials relating to Functional Dependencies and Relational Calculus & Algebra. She's alright too.
Mehdi had the short end of the cake and was tasked with the Project. I've consulted him individually before, great guy to be honest. 

You learn how to write simple, algebraic, aggregate and nested queries mainly in PostgreSQL using pgAdmin4. SQLite was used but only for one lecture as demonstration. After midterms, you learn about functional dependencies and close off the syllabus with relational algebra and calculus. 

Learning the SQL queries isn't really difficult. Test 1's median was around 17/25, and the upper quartile was around 19. It does take some conscientious practice though as some questions aren't that straightforward. For the latter half, I had background in discrete math (bless you Prof Lee Soo Teck) so it was rather easy for me. My peers, who did not have such background, did find it tougher to grasp. However, I would say the content isn't that tough even for beginners, unless you really suck at logic. 

The toughest part is the project and oh boy it was such a major L. We had to build a website/simple app using Python Flask with html/css/javascript as our frontend and communicate with our backend database with is done using PostgreSQL, preferably using pgadmin4. It had such a rough learning curve because most people who take this course have no background in front-end programming or python flask, let alone the fact that the skills required are not pre-requisites to taking this course. The group project is done in groups of 4 and no one in my group was adept in it. We had to learn as we do the project. Also, the documentation to setting up the virtual machine, connecting to database, setting up docker etc. weren't the most clear and it took a lot of time because of the many errors created, where we had to debug it ourselves most of the time. Like I said how Mehdi got the short end of the stick, because this project is rough for many so he was bombarded with loads of emails and questions, so replies and aid from him may not be fast as well. I did not really had a fun time doing this project as I took the course mainly to learn SQL, not experience being a full stack developer. I grouped up with my friends but them pulling their weight only near (I put in rather consistent effort throughout )the end really gave me a scare. The bright side is that you can put this project on your resume and assuming you put in lots of effort, you would have a lot to talk about it when brought up by interviewers. 

Overall, I would want to say I really like the module BUT the project gave me such a terrible time I can only say the module is eh. Lectures are not recorded, but the prof is a good lecturer so physical is worth it I feel.

Bellcurve is rather left-skewed I would say as most assignments and tests aren't tough. The project would be the differentiating factor.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 2
Dr. Zeehan Jaafar/Dr. Donna Brunero

ASSESSMENT [100% CONTINUAL ASSESSMENT]:

CA1: 15% Fieldtrip I Assignment
CA2: 20% Documentary Critique 
CA3: 30% Marine Protected Areas 
CA4: 15% Reflections
CA5: 20% Class Participation 

It started off feeling pretty annoying but towards the end, it started feeling alright, but I'm not a huge fan of the 4 hour lecture block. There is no tutorial.
Workload is quite low but quite  fair bit of group work involved. 
Apart from class exercise where you choose your groups, groups are randomly allocated so just luck based. 

Contents are largely water/marine/sea related. Stuffs like sea creatures, piracy and fishing and many more. There's also assignments related to visiting Lee Kong Chian Natural History Museum and Changi Beach. If you are into those, this is the mod for you. I'm okay with the museum but I dislike sunny outdoor places so the beach was a dread. 

The professors are pretty eloquent and passionate but faces the same problem with nearly all CHS mods. Most of the students do not share the enthusiasm so many of times, their prompts and attempts to promote discussion and participation are met with little support. However, I would say this is a problem with CHS mods rather than the profs. They are very approachable with all due respect. 

I did this module with the full intention to S/U because I doubt I can score anything >= A-. Hence, I hardly participated in the class exercise/gave low quality work for the individual assignments. I did  put in a fair share of effort for the group works though, because that's what responsible people do.

To score well in this module, I guess you need to be creative and rather okay with writing, and definitely be comfortable with working in groups, and throw in a good RNG grouping on top of that. You can take it with friends so you don't have to face the same awkwardness I did when the profs insisted on having group work for lecture class exercises.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Yap Von Bing

This module makes you want to switch major.
Content is hard to follow and concepts are tricky to link together. My weak foundation from ST2131 amplifies this. 

The prof tries to make lecture engaging, but the content is so ugh.

Tutorial was tough for me to do, and the TA is absolute garbage. 

This mod doesn't offer much avenue for you to seek help from. 

Finals was bonkers difficulty for me, even though Siong Hwee thinks it is easy. Then again, he was able to solve CS2040 mock PE in 5minutes,and I certainly ain't that calibur. 

Only thing easy was the assignment. 

In lecture quizzes are more like Permutation and Combination with your friends. You can also just wait for other people to blurt out the answer. Kinda dumb not gonna lie.

Goddamn I hate this module.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Tan Ser Peow

Strangely, people in the comments find the mod easy but I found it really tough for the later parts. To thrive in this mod, you would need to be quite good at visualisation and have a good spatial sense, sadly both of these talents I lack. 

Prof Tan is ok, notes are generally taken from the textbook so I wouldn't credit him much for that. NTU's multivariable calculus' notes are similar and so are the given tutorial questions. 

The lecture had this person, clearly mental, who kept interrupting the prof non-stop and burping loudly. His presence, as much as I sympathise with his condition, really killed my interest and motivation for this mod greatly. However, I doubt the prof could've done much because this person was present in my previous math mods and was a catastrophical disturbance as well. 

Content wise I found it dry (really it's hard for me to take interest in math lmao) as usual. 

Midterms was trivial, really trivial. I had careless mistakes for 2 qns and ended up only with 44/50, else it would've been 48/50. No grade statistics were released but my hunch is that the median is around 40 given how trivial the paper was. 

Finals is standard, with some hard questions and some normal questions. I'm too average to solve every question but I did my best, really couldn't care as this is the last math mod I would be doing in NUS.

Tutorial attendance is graded, which feels like a sin for math mods. I go there but I don't listen. Tutor is eh, forgettable. I didn't get the prof for my tutorial, maybe it would've been better idk.

If you want to score high, practise. I didn't do much because really, I have near 0 interest in math. NGL kinda regret taking this mod instead of MA2311 because I heard 2311 is easier. My run had so many smart people from math that I recognise from other mods and china scholars so I doubt even a tough paper could deter a steep curve for getting >= A-.

On the bright side, if you are good, you can do well and you do learn more useful things from this mod compared to MA2311. Your choice, really, but be warned that this mod is not as easy as the seniors below mention.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1

Dr Ong Ee Cheng
Don't really remember the exact percentages but
Midterms 25% 
Finals 50%

If you've taken EC1101E under Prof Ong, you wouldn't find her teaching style foreign. But for those who didn't, you basically watch lecture videos -> do quiz -> attend(or not, up to you) live lecture for concept checks.

Content is not difficult but can be largely tricky. Math wise, it's really trivial but I come from a more robust mathematical background compared to an average econs major because I major in Statistics. If you major in math/DSA/CS or anything similar, you wouldn't find the math difficult. 

Application of the theory is the tough part because you really have to understand the questions to know which equations and what values to use and sub in. Some questions can be quite subtle in its hints and may be difficult to solve. 

Problem set submissions are done in group, and as with all group work, depends on how lucky you are in finding decent group mates. I took this mod with a friend and fortunately had a competent stranger (who turned friend later) join our group so our collaborations were pretty smooth. If you find yourself in a bad luck situation, you don't really have to panic as the submissions is based on effort and not accuracy so you can just exclude the useless deadweights' names. I think some people might dislike the submission requirement, but I'm personally fine with it as it helps me be on task with the module, which saves quite a bit of catch-up work later on. I did attempt each problem set on my own before my group meetings to discuss, they don't take very long. People who are used to bs CS mods or ridiculously tough math proving questions should find problem sets manageable.

Professor wise, I'm really biased because her teaching style matches my liking a lot. But at the fundamental core, at least she speaks eloquently and with adequate pacing. She can come across as rather direct to some people but I'm ok with that, adds a bit of character even. 

Midterms and finals are both physical exams using Examplify and some structured questions done on paper.
Midterms was rather tricky, especially for people who has not been exposed to her style of paper before, and had a fair amount of graph drawing. The bell curve for midterms was rather gentle.

Finals was more alright, but MCQ and MRQ can be really tricky and test you deeply on the concepts itself. It can be quite punishing as each question is worth like 6points at least out of the total 180 points. I find the structured questions rather acceptable. There was no explicit graph drawing question tested in my finals, though you could draw if you wish to use it as a form of working. 

Overall, I find it an enjoyable module under her, though the papers can be a bit stressful. Workload is eh, I took it when I was overloading with 24mc and still manage to cope pretty alright. I mean, after you are abused by CS2040, hardly any non-CS modules come close in workload.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
CHS common curriculum is bad, let me get this out of the way first.

AY22/23 Sem 1
Prof Cindy Ng

I recommend you take this mod with a group of 2 other friends. I didn't.

Quiz - 10%
Two term tests - 45%, 22.5% each, closed book.
Group Presentation - 25%
Peer feedback - 10%
Forum - 10%

Solar System
Spaceflight
Satellite
Space exploration
Formation of the Solar System
Moon landing
Life on Mars
Extraterrestrial liquid water
Extraterrestrial atmosphere
Near-Earth object
Commercial use of space
Space colonization

Table of contents looks somewhat interesting but really, the module is dry to hell. There is no tutorial, but just 2 lectures each week where the prof reads off the slide in not-so-eloquent English, and have some Q&A and announcement making. There is no lecture recording at all. 

Quizzes are really simple and just a CTRL-F of lecture notes. Most people score full points. She is nice in allowing up to 10 mistakes out of 97 questions. Meaning you only need to get 87 questions correct out of 97 over the entire semester to score full points. 

Forum is just posting and replying to people based on the given criteria. I suggest one to post super early and reply as soon as possible so you have a wider choice of topics and content to talk about, otherwise you run the risk of overlapping with someone else's choice later in the module. 

Group presentation is a 10 minute recorded presentation. Groups of 3 or 4, whether you make or break depends on the quality of your group mates. I was randomly assigned a group and you could tell that the demographic of people who makes up these portion of students try less than the ones who bid with friends. 

Term test is god tier garbage, it makes you feel like you are in a secondary school again. It just tests pure regurgitation of details in lecture notes. People who score full marks are probably nerds who locked themselves in their room and memorised every single detail on the lecture notes, or they got lucky with guessing.

0 Calculation required, hardly any thinking required, pure content mod. You would enjoy it if you have a thing for braindead memorisation and vomitting. 

I didn't enjoy it because I feel pure regurgitation modules should be a secondary school level crap, this being part of a university education is really pathetic.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Brenda Yuen 

Book Chapter Reflection 5%
Science News Article 1 20%
Peer Review of Article 1 5%
Science News Article 2 30%
Reflective Commentary 10%
Individual Oral Presentation 25%
Interaction and Engagement 5% 

Brenda is pretty energetic and engaging so lessons are pretty interesting and fun. Kinda depended on how your group is too, a quiet group would probably end up rather awkward and embarrassing to be in. 

Difficulty wise -- I hate essay writing so it was quite a pain. The fact that we should try to use more conversational words really made my life difficult because my strength lied in vocabulary, whereas my weakness is grammar and sentence structure. I feel that the bellcurve is still rather steep though if one is striving to achieve >= A-.

Workload wise it's like slightly above average, need to write quite a bit and prepare quite a bit. It's quite a fair amount such that I felt it would be quite a waste if I end up SU-ing it so fingers crossed. 

Oral Presentation is 10 minutes. Tough part is memorising 10 minutes worth of script (3/4 of my class did, those who didn't, you can tell they really want to SU). 

Overall, module is okay but assignments were still a chore for people like me who hates writing.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 2:
Lecturer : Prof Lee Soo Teck

Took this as a stats major for my cs minor(but blundered on cs1010s so not even sure what's the point anymore)
Disclaimer: I actually hate university math.
Overall: I actually enjoyed it! Which was hella surprising given how much I disliked proving in math. Prof Lee is eloquent and clear, which I didn't really get for my Calculus/ Linear Algebra module. Being fluent really helped with making me wanting to go to his lectures and tutorials. He is also approachable for consultation and is receptive to questions, both via email and forum.

HWA: 20% 
Easy, aim to get full marks, though if you struggle with HWA, something is wrong. I did make a careless and scored 8.5 for one but most should be rather easy to score 10/10.

Midterms:30%
Manageable, median and mean is rather high so you don't really want to be careless over here. I kinda made a few critical errors so I ended up only between the median and 75 percentile.

Finals: 50%
Surprisingly more doable than midterms for my semester, or it might've just been me who has been enlightened on the day of finals. However, it really test your understanding of fundamental concepts. I couldn't prove a part of a question, and there were some where I'm not sure but I could estimate a good ~80/100, unless everyone scores 100/100 which would really suck, though I doubt so.

Verdict: Surprisingly enjoyable under Prof Lee. However, people who take this mod are usually the more driven crowd, so do expect intelligent people and a non-gentle curve despite the difficulties, especially in semester 1 where the module is likely to consist of a higher proportion of math majors.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 2
Tutor : Rachel

Chill module. 
I can't be bothered with the readings, who even reads things beyond 5 pages.
Just do the quizzes and should be freelo to SU.

Rachel, though, makes tutorials kinda painful. She always gives like tutorial prepdoc which requires us to read readings/clips before hand and the thing is , those things aren't necessarily short. You can tell she's trying her best but definitely need to adjust her expectations since the class is probably made up of plenty of people who just wants to S/U the mod and likely just wants to have an easier, enjoyable time, not more readings that we ain't gonna read anyway.

Nothing much gained tbh, going down in history as part of my S/U likely.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:

Chill, steep bellcurve, quite fun. Pretty freelo to pass but can be quite competitive to achieve an A. Content taught is also rather relevant and useful generally.
I just don't like how assignment 2 is a group work essay and the thing about group work is that it's purely luck based -- who you get as your groupmate. Mine were pretty eh so I pretty much took things into my own hand and carried 2/3 of the assignment. If you don't want to SU the module, be prepared to do something similar.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 1; Dr Ong Ee Cheng(Micro), Dr Chan Kok Hoe(Macro)
Tutor : Dhanvin

Tutor was decent, does his job well. He does occasionally push us to think further.

I liked Dr Ong a lot because she was quite down to earth and relatable. Her lectures are interesting, clear and engaging.
Dr Chan is also good because he is also eloquent, clear and interesting.
Both are receptive to questions.

I found the difficulty to be just right. It doesn't feel like a demoralising module and you do see your efforts bear fruit on your grades. The bellcurve also doesn't appear akin to mount Everest so some mistakes here and there are allowed. Exam questions are a good mix of easy and tricky/tough questions. I very much appreciate the more math focused nature of university economics because I, frankly, am very tired of the essay writing from JC econs.

Overall, a very enjoyable module for me.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 1; Lecturer: Assorted Tutor: Nicholas Cai

Workload: Moderate but for Y1S1 where overload is not possible, it was quite light.
Texts: (How I feel about the texts are mine and mine alone and does not represent everyone's feeling)
The Ones who Walked away from Omelas - Interesting, short, nice.
Hind Swaraj - Super lengthy and boring
The Epic of Gilgamesh - I found it interesting partly because the FATE series has given me some interest in the lore of Gilgamesh and Enkidu but some of my classmates found it confusing and boring. Lengthy.
Xunzi - Absolute pain, lengthy
We were supposed to do No Exit but there were some issues so we changed to Aristotle and the Gun, for future semesters it should be No Exit.
Aristotle and the Gun - Interesting, especially if you liked Steins Gate or things related to time travel. Short.
Humanimals - Fun, interesting, short, supplementary, i.e. not tested.

Lecture : Very eloquent and engaging lecturers across the board. 

Tutorial : Nicholas is eloquent, nice and dope.

Quiz: Tough for me, but people on average at least pass, I don't. 

Group Discussion summary and Meme Competitions: As with any other group components in the world, great if you have a good team, bad if your team is dysfunctional. I had a decent team.

Test: Quiz(MCQ) + 2 very short essays. 

General: There is so much time given for deadlines for this module. Heck, extensions were given like free as long as you email the professor. Interesting module and great teaching team overall though and I enjoyed it a lot, though my grades wouldn't certainly reflect so.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 1; Lecture: Daisy Pham Tutorial: Wong Yean Ling

Workload: Light
Tutorial questions: Mostly trivial application of formulas 
LumiNUS quiz: Infinite attempts, take the highest score. I would argue the quiz are poorly set and there are no solutions pdf provided, rather the lecture would go through the quiz. I find it really redundant as many of the questions are trivial and providing the pdf solutions would be more effective.
Programming: R. Only simple R is required, learning dplyr would help a lot but not necessary. 
Assignments: Toughest part of the module, but people score well nonetheless.
Final Quiz: A very slightly bit tricky but very much doable.
Lecture: Most people don't turn up, the prof would go through programming in R/ quiz answers and have Q&A. Prof Daisy is nice and patient but I think the lecture has a poor flow as she stops everytime a question is raised. Albeit some are interesting/useful, questions asked are often quite stupid/repetitive.

Tutorial: Miss Wong is very patient and nice but very lengthy, even with trivial questions. We have tutorial groups to discuss and present answers for each tutorial but I found it quite redundant as the simple nature of the questions hardly required group discussion and only made tutorials more "draggy". 

Forum: I hated it. Anonymity drastically reduced the quality of questions asked so be prepared to see really stupid/ignorant ones multiple times.

Datacamp access was provided which was a huge boon for beginners like me in programming to start/catch up. Do utilise it if you are an amateur. 

Overall: Given the simple nature of this module, it has a steep, very steep, extremely steep bellcurve. Any mistake, careless or not, is magnified.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY21/22 Sem 1; Prof. Wang Fei

Homework assignments : 25% 
Mid term : 25%
Finals : 50%

In terms of managing the module and attending to students, Prof Wang Fei is someone I came to appreciate much later in the course. However, the papers he set are not things I look forward to. He is very responsive on the forum and emails. He provides plenty of resources so if you are very much an independent learner, you can benefit quite a lot. That said, lectures and tutorials are dry and there is hardly any participation required.

Workload wise it is light in terms of submission deadlines, but me being stupid increases the workload because I spend quite a lot of time on attempting the questions and grasping the concepts.
Difficulty wise, it was quite tough for me as this is my intro mod to university math which is a far-cry from the simple computations in JC and Secondary school. Homework assignments started off on manageable difficulties but grew difficult, especially when more proving questions are involved. Result statistics weren't released for homework assignments so I have no idea how people do but I figure it should average around at least 80% for this component.

Midterms - Rather standard questions for most, cept the last with some unconventional questions here and there. Time was very tight so the pressure could affect your performance.

Finals - Absolute nightmare. It was kinda sad to see a module where I put in a lot of effort and practised much but only to be crushed by the tedious and tough finals, but heard it might just be prof Wang Fei's papers as the past year papers weren't that devastating.

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof Yap Von Bing Tutor: Shen Tao Assessments: In-Lecture Quizzes - 15% Assignment - 20% Tutorial Attendance - 5% Final Exam - 60% Course Content: 1) Simple Regression - Description 2) Simple Regression - Inference 3) Multiple Regression - Description 4) Multiple Regression - Inference 5) Other Topics - Model Selection, Residual Analysis and Transformations (NOT EXAMINABLE) 6) The One-Way Layout Module Ratings: Workload: 3/10 Difficulty: 8.5/10 Enjoyability: 7/10 ST3131 under Prof Yap generally has a much lower workload compared to the semesters taught by GFF. There are only six chapters being taught, one of which is not tested in the final exam. Similar to ST2132, there are no midterms. However, I felt that ST3131 is the hardest among the three statistics courses that I have taken as a DSA major (ST2131, ST2132 and ST3131) in terms of grasping of understanding of various nuances used. Be prepared for many symbols to be used, such as straight epsilon, curly epsilon, e, Beta0 till Betan, y.., yi. etc. Sometimes, I had a headache trying to grasp important concepts such as straight epsilon being a realisation of curly epsilon which has a normal distribution of (0, sigma^2). You need to have the patience to look through each definition carefully before proceeding. Similar to ST2132, prof Yap's notes are very reader friendly unlike GFF who simply places snapshots of the textbook content in his slides which are not user-friendly at all. Yes, there are some slides which are blank but he wants you to write in order to learn, which is a good thing. He mentioned that writing is thinking. Lectures and Quizzes: Lectures are held twice a week at LT32 and Prof Yap's lecture pace is great, neither too fast nor too slow although the pace went a little fast towards the end when he realized that he has short of time. In-lecture quizzes are held almost every lecture (except for the first lecture in week 1) with 2 MCQ questions. You are usually given 4 minutes to complete the quizzes, quite time tight if the questions are complex and tricky. You are allowed to discuss the quizzes with your friends around you. Median grade for the quizzes is generally 2/2 except for a couple where the grade is 1/2. Prof Yap will extract the best 80% of quizzes in the computation of your final quiz grade, so you have some room for mess up. Even so, he will give credit to those who have tried. His mark allocation is this: 2/2 (10 marks), 1/2 (8 marks), 0/2 (7 marks), never try (0 marks). Tutorials: Similar to ST2132, there are a few questions for you to try for every tutorial, most of which are either proving questions or questions in R code. Very few computation questions are involved. Unfortunately, one thing not so good is that Prof tutorial solutions have lack of working and sometimes left me puzzled as to what he is doing. Fortunately, my tutor also sends his working and notes for most tutorials to us through webmail, hence some doubts are cleared along the way. Assignment: 8 MCQ questions testing on Chapters 1, 2 and part of Chapter 3. Given out in around Week 10 and we are given around 1 week+ to complete them. Similar format to the in-lecture quizzes, albeit a little tricker as we are given plenty of time to complete. You can also discuss the assignment questions with your peers. Median grade is 8/8. Final Exam: This semester's final exam is challenging. There are 4 questions, comprising 60 marks. The first question is 18 MCQ questions of various types - True/False, Calculation, I and II type. Many MCQ questions are very tricky and require some thought, even till the finest detail. For example, 1 question seems to be True but in reality, the answer is False just because the question stated y but should be yhat. The second question is all on proving (8 mini parts, 24 marks). Most are testing on the proving of formulas and theorems taught in lectures, which most of us have taken for granted. I struggled badly on this question. The third question is a single 6 marks question, asking whether the given statement is True/False and provide a justification. It is testing on the one-way layout regarding the uniqueness of solutions of alphai - alphaj. There are mixed answers in the exam hall, some state True while others state False, giving all different explanations. The last question is the ultimate worst - calculation of F-values from a given ANOVA table with so many degrees of freedom. I had some confusion with the degrees of freedom regarding SSE and SSR and hence, lots of mistakes along the way. Worst thing is that all questions are linked and if the first part is wrong, your subsequent parts will be wrong. Hoping for ECF marks. Overall, ST3131 is challenging in terms of the nuances and the symbols used in Prof Yap's notes although content is considered on the low side. Taken under Prof Yap if you prefer low workload or proving questions. In-Lecture Quizzes: Full Assignment: 8/8 Tutorial Attendance: Full Final Exam - Die Expected Grade: B- Final Grade: TBA

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof Winston Low Tutor: Mr. Chong Lock Kuah Assessments: Tutorial Attendance + Presentation - 10% In-Lecture Quizzes (Best 2 of 3) - 15% Midterms - 30% Final Exam - 45% Course Content: 1) Introduction To Financial Management 2) Financial Statement Analysis 3) Time Value Of Money (I) - Annuity, Perpetuity, Cash Flows 4) Time Value Of Money (II) - APR and EAR, Loan Types 5) Risk And Return (I) - Portfolios, Arithmetic and Geometric Return 6) Risk And Return (II) - CAPM Model, Beta Calculation, Market Portfolio 7) Introduction To Bonds 8) Stock Valuation 9) Capital Budgeting (I) - Various Metrics (AAR, PI, Payback Period, Discounted Payback Period, NPV, IRR, MIRR etc.) 10) Capital Budgeting (II) - WACC, NSV, OCF Terminology 11) Long-Term Financial Planning 12) Short-Term Financial Planning 13) Introduction To Options (NOT TESTED IN FINAL EXAM) Overall Ratings: Difficulty: 7/10 Workload: 7.5/10 Enjoyability: 9.5/10 Overall, FIN2704X is my most favorite module for this semester. FIN2704X gives us an introduction to the world of finance with many apt concepts. This module is extremely useful to me being a QF second major. As QF modules usually test on the mathematical aspects of finance, this module tests more on the theoretical side (although there are also some calculations involved). I intend to take this module to satisfy my last QF 2nd major requirements (FIN3702). Held at 8am at LT16. I think I have never come across FIN2704 having 8am lectures before though, this is probably the first time. Lectures are not recorded but still many do not turn up for lecture due to the fact that the notes given out by Prof Winston are quite self-explanatory. The first few lectures simply re-enforced content taught in QF1100 and QF2104 (a little add on in the theory side). However, things get tougher after recess week, where new content not taught in the QF curriculum is introduced. Hardest part is Week 10 and Week 11 on capital budgeting ii and long-term financial planning. Tutorials: Once a week from Weeks 3 to 13. Around 4 to 7 questions for each tutorial. Try the questions for extra practice for the midterms and final exam. The tutor will tell you which week and question you will be presenting to the class so do get prepared. An excel file will be shared on canvas regarding the week that you need to present. You are required to create PowerPoint slides/Canva slides with your group members (members that will be presenting the same week as you) for all the tutorial questions. Which question and how many questions to present is up to your luck. Median grade based on my tutor is around 7/10. Quizzes: There will be 3 in-tutorial quizzes held in Weeks 6, 10 and 13 respectively. For these weeks, there will still be tutorial questions but probably less. After finishing the tutorial questions, the quiz will start immediately. For this semester, the quizzes are of this format: Quiz 1 - 20 minutes (Lectures 1 to 4) Quiz 2 - 15 minutes (Lectures 7 and 8) Quiz 3 - 20 minutes (Lectures 9 to 11) All 3 quizzes are 5 MCQ questions. The quizzes are set by your tutor, not the prof. For instance, if your tutor is Prof Winston, expect more calculation questions but if your tutor is Mr Chong, expect more theory questions instead. I am not so sure about the last tutor. Quizzes are the hardest for me as Mr Chong typically sets tricky questions. Median grade for all 3 quizzes are 3/5. Midterms: 1 hour 15 minutes, 20 MCQs. Tests on lectures 1 to 6. For the midterms, most questions are computational and quite standard. You are allowed to bring in 2 calculators - 1 normal calculator and 1 financial calculator. They both need to be used for the midterms and final exam. Also bring along some blank pieces of paper to be used for workings when solving calculation questions. Final Exam: Similar format as midterms, 1 hour 30 minutes, 25 MCQs. Tests on lectures 7 to 12, but concepts from previous chapters might also be necessary. For instance, EAR and APR concepts are tested on Short-Term financial planning. Final exam was moderately harder than midterms, where the theory questions are trickier and the calculation questions demand more working. Time is tighter as well. Barely had enough time to finish the questions but some left the exam hall earlier probably because they gave up solving the questions. Tutorial Participation: 7-8/10 Quiz: 12/15 (Median 9, UQ 12) Midterms: 19/20 (Median 13, UQ 16) Finals: ? (Around 19-21/25 I guess) Expected Grade: A- Final Grade: TBA

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturers: Nidhi Sharma, Sudatta Tutorial TA: He Yuan Assessments: In-Lecture Quizzes - 15% Final Report - 30% Tutorial Group Projects - 25% In-Class Quiz (Week 11) - 30% Module Content: 1) Energy Consumption And Carbon Emissions 2) Electrification 3) Clean Energy Transition 4) Energy Generation, Operation and Markets 5) Fundamentals Of Energy Systems 6) Large Scale Renewables 7) Solar/Wind Energy - Transmission And Distribution 8) Electricity Pricing 9) Energy Justice Overall Rating: Difficulty - 4/10 Workload - 4/10 Enjoyability - 8/10 The difficulty of HS2920 might vary from person to person. Although this module is from the Geography department, most of the content is related to Physics, Chemistry and Economics, which I had learnt all three in A-levels. Weeks 4 to 6 are more Physics and Chemistry heavy while Weeks 8 to 11 are more Economics heavy. Be prepared for some calculations questions in the test as well. Lectures: Held from 8am to 10am on Fridays at LT9. Lectures, although recorded, have in-lecture quizzes and hence sort of mandatory attendance. In-lecture quizzes contribute to 15% of the overall grade which is quite significant. I am not sure why the lecturers made them compulsory despite the bad timing. However, due to low turnup rates for the lectures, as well as some complaints in the midterm survey, the lecturers decided to be more relaxed by giving everyone the full marks if they try to answer the open-ended questions even though the answers might not be fully accurate. If the answers are accurate, a score of 1.5/1 will be given as an added bonus. Tutorials: For each tutorial conducted once in two weeks, you are required to complete a group project (group members are allocated to you by the teachers/TAs). The project is usually quite fun such as calculating the average power consumption of a typical household in a certain country of your groups' choice. There are some questions for your group to complete but no stress, the questions are rather straightforward. Some research however needs to be done as some questions need knowledge on policies/suggestions to address certain problems of the chosen country. Quiz: Quiz held in the lecture of Week 11. Course content tested include Week 1 to Week 10. It is a 1 hour 15 minutes quiz with 30 MCQs (no MRQs, only 1 correct answer per question). The quiz contains a mixture of theoretical and calculation questions. Most questions are of acceptable standard although there are a couple of questions which are vague. Some questions are tricky and you need to read them carefully. Quite time tight as some questions involve complex calculations such as determining power generated from a turbine based on area, blade diameter and wind speed. Final Report: Question for AY24/25 Semester 2 You have been hired by the Ministry of Finance of a country of your choice. As a seasoned energy analyst, you have been asked to recommend 3 key actions that your country should undertake in its transition to creating a just and clean electricity infrastructure. The report needs an introduction, three actionable recommendations and a conclusion. Introduction requires a brief overview of the chosen country's energy situation, key plans and major energy challenges it faces. For each of the three recommendations, you need to describe how big of a challenge is the action addressing in terms of emissions or population affected, what might happen if left unaddressed and the action that you are proposing to tackle the issue. An added bonus will be to explain energy justice in your recommendations. There is a maximum word limit of 2000 words (+- 10% leeway) and this report can be done from Week 8/9 when the question is released till the end of Week 13. Overall HS2920 is quite enjoyable for me despite the fact that this module is being held for the first time and the lecturers need time to consolidate feedback from the students to further improve subsequently in future batches. Quizzes: Full Marks Group Project: 3.7/5 + 3.5/5 + ? + ? + ? Quiz: 21/30 (Distribution: 10/17.5/20/22/27) Final Report: Not Revealed Expected Grade: B Actual Grade: TBA
thread_title: HS2920 Transition to Clean and Just Energy

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof Danny Poo Tutorial Teacher: Prof Danny Poo Assessments: Class Participation - 6% In-Lecture Test - 30% Assignment 1A - 15% Assignment 1B - 15% Assignment 2 - 34% Course Syllabus: 1) Introduction To Database Applications 2) Data Management Systems 3) Conceptual Data Modelling 4) Entity-Relationship Models 5) Logical Data Modelling 6) Normalization 7) Database Management With SQL And DBMS 8) Data Visualization 9) Tableau And Dashboard Design Concepts 10) Visualizing Complex Data 11) Data Storytelling In Tableau 12) NoSQL * MongoDB has been removed from the syllabus this semester. Overall Rating: Difficulty: 5/10 Workload: 4/10 Enjoyability: 7.5/10 BT2102 teaches the core concepts of database management, delving into certain concepts in-depth such as conceptual and logical data modelling. BT2102 is a substitute for CS2102, and compared to CS2102, it is lighter in terms of workload (no midterm and final exam). As a DSA major, SQL is very important in the working world being a data analyst/data scientist. The DSA curriculum unfortunately does not cover anything related to SQL hence I decided to take BT2102 as an unrestricted elective to fulfill the "database SQL pillar". Lectures: Lectures can get quite theoretical in nature especially the first few weeks on conceptual and logical database models. Be prepared to stay attentive during the lectures as the test covers a lot on these concepts. Concepts such as primary keys, candidate keys, superkeys and foreign keys must be properly grasped and you need to understand the differences among them. Lectures are initially not recorded as mentioned by the prof, but were subsequently recorded due to the low turnup rates from Week 3 onwards. However, the recordings will only be made available around a week after the actual lesson. Tutorials: Only 6 tutorials from Week 4 to Week 9. No tutorials from Week 10 onwards. First tutorial is on building conceptual models and the second tutorial is on creating logical data models while will be very useful for Assignment 1A. Third tutorial is on writing SQL queries which is important for Assignment 1B. SQL queries are done in MySQL flavour. The subsequent three tutorials are focused more on data visualizations and Tableau dashboard building, which are super important for Assignment 2. The tutorial questions are of moderate standard (not so difficult fortunately). Prof Danny will ask students questions on the tutorial questions in order to be awarded the participation points (attendance is not enough), so do be prepared before the tutorial starts. Assignments: Assignment 1A: Developing conceptual and logical data models from a complex dataset. This assignment is the most difficult one actually. You are required to first identify entities, attributes and relationships from a given complex story. Afterwards, you are required to define relationship types (1:1, 1:n, n:n) as well as participation types (mandatory and optional participation for the various relationships). The Chen notation will be employed, UML not allowed. Next, perform normalization and create the overall logical data model from the entities, attributes and relationships identified. Assignment 1B: Querying in SQL. You are required to complete 10 SQL questions using the same dataset from Assignment 1A. All the 10 SQL queries must be placed in a file and written in MySQL. Do follow the instructions in the rubrics carefully as one mistake might make you panic (unable to import the files correctly). The SQL questions are rather straightforward and for several questions, there are solutions for you to cross-check to ensure that you are on the right track. Assignment 2: This is a group project on Tableau Dashboard and data visualizations. In a group of 5/6, you are required to construct several business questions on the dataset that is being assigned yourselves and afterwards, construct interesting visualizations in Tableau or any other platforms like PowerBI, Excel etc. to answer the business questions. You are required to showcase your visualizations, the insights/analysis and business strategies/recommendations on Google Slides/Powerpoint for a 10 minute presentation to the TA/prof in Weeks 12 or 13. There is a +- 2 minute leeway (maximum 12 minutes), followed by a 5-minute QnA. Overall, BT2102 is quite an interesting module and expanded my horizons on database concepts not taught in the DSA courses. If you would like to go further, consider CS2102/IT2002 although the difficulty for these modules is much higher. The downside of this module is that the bell-curve is very high, especially on the Assignments. Despite trying very hard for the assignments, especially 1A, I am still below the median. Assignment 1A: 11.5/15 (Median 13.5) Assignment 1B: 15/15 (Median 13) Assignment 2: 29.5/34 (Median 30) Participation: 6/6 (Median 6) Quiz: Not Revealed (Probably around 22 to 24 / 30) Expected Grade: B Actual Grade: TBA

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof Sergio Hernandez Marin Teaching Assistant: Chirag (For my group) Assessments: Class Participation - 5% Quiz (During Week 9) - 25% Datacamp Assignments - 5% Group Project Success - 30% Group Video Presentation - 10% Group Project Wiki - 10% Peer Reviews (Individual + Team) - 15% Course Content: Week 1 - The Data Process Lifecycle Week 2 - Data Preparation, Cleaning And Synthetic Data Generation Week 3 - CNY Public Holiday (No Lesson) Week 4 - Data Exploration And Analysis Week 5 - Work On Video Presentation (No Lesson) Week 6 - Modelling Week 7 - Introduction To LLMs Week 8 - Work On Quiz Preparation (No Lesson) Week 9 - Quiz (Mandatory Attendance) Week 10 - Work On Wiki Deliverables (No Lesson) Week 11 - Work On Presentation Roadshow (No Lesson) Week 12 - Presentation Roadshow (Mandatory Attendance) Week 13 - Guest Fireside Chat (Mandatory Attendance) Overall Rating: Difficulty: 6/10 Workload: 4/10 Enjoyability: 3/10 The format of DSA3101 for AY24/25 Semester 2 has changed significantly compared to previous semesters. Firstly, instead of compulsory attendance for all weeks, there are only 3 weeks where attendance is compulsory this semester (Weeks 9, 12, 13). Secondly, many concepts taught in DSA3101 last time have been removed, including Docker, Javascript, HTML and CSS. Other concepts such as Git and GitHub are also not taught in lecture and we are required to learn from the Datacamp videos ourselves (ie. No specific slides from the prof related to Git, Docker and SQL that are available in previous semesters). All code is to be written in Python without any emphasis on Flask and Javascript. However, personally, I felt that there is a big deterioration in the quality of DSA3101 this semester despite the lighter workload. These are the reasons why: 1) Lack of proper guidance from the prof/the TAs - Concepts from the lectures are only gone through very briefly during the optional lectures without sufficient real-life concrete examples. This leads to us having weak foundations and had to rely on a lot of external help from other websites/stack overflow etc. 2) Course content is lacking - Only very few concepts are actually covered this semester, where there is a significant overlap of content in CS3244. It is basically a recap of CS3244 content. Many weeks that are supposed to be lecture time are being converted to preparation for the quiz or group project, which wastes time. 3) Very disorganized and lack of proper control - This is pertaining to the roadshow in Week 12. Groups are busy trying to snatch preferred venues from one another, had to run from room to room during peer evaluation, had to peer review a group even during our presentation period, as well as completing 6 reviews by the end of the roadshow. The roadshow was really a big mess and some groups had to even resort to writing fake peer reviews just to get the 15% due to insufficient time and no groups actually wanted to present more than once. 4) Lack of structure and clarity in many areas - Announcements and instructions are made randomly on canvas (not always at the announcements page, some are at the home page on canvas while others are even in the playbook). We need to spend a lot of time trying to find the proper guidelines, necessities and rubrics for the group project, at times not knowing where they are placed. For this module, the ace to scoring well is surprisingly NOT the group project although it attributes 65% of the total weightage. It is actually the quiz in week 9, which is the differentiating factor. Quiz (In-person): Do revise on the course lecture notes, as well as the Datacamp slides to score well for the quiz. For this semester, the prof mentioned that there will be around 40 MCQ or fill-in-the-blanks questions on both the content taught in Datacamp, as well as common sensical business questions. Most of the question tested actually involve the latter. In my opinion, the quiz this semester is not set well. There are no questions pertaining to Docker at all and only 1 question on SQL where there are multiple answers. For the questions on Git, the answers can already be found through the screenshots without testing our knowledge on Git commands at all. The quiz is conducted on Examplify, 1 hour 30 minutes. Group Project: We need to complete a project in a group of 10 (instead of 8 the previous semester) We are given 5 projects to choose from this semester: Project 1: Enhancing Guest Experience through Data-Driven Journey Mapping and Analysis Project 2: Automating ESG Data Extraction and Performance Analysis 11 Project 3: Machine Learning for Personalized Marketing Campaigns in Banking Project 4: AI-Powered Knowledge Graph Generation from Enterprise Content Management Project 5: AI-Driven Merchandise Customization Platform for E-commerce First, we are supposed to come up with a video presentation on what we are going to work on from our chosen project, such as the analysis of the industry, the problem space, the proposed approach and the project organization. The video is maximum 10 minutes where all group members are required to be included in the video. (Basically 1 minute per person, ridiculous time limit imposed) Next, we are supposed to tackle 10 business questions (given, cannot choose). Recommendation is to assign each member with 1 business question so that the work distribution is evenly spaced. Most of these questions involve the use of data analysis, collection of raw data sources such as surveys and reviews, exploratory data analysis, data visualizations and Machine Learning. Machine Learning questions can test on anything including sentiment analysis, Natural Language Processing and Text Classification. All the code, visualizations made and the colab files/jupyter notebooks are to be submitted in GitHub for the prof/TAs to see. One user of the group is required to create his/her own GitHub repo for all the data files, sources, code and requirements. Best is that all group members have a equal number of commits for the prof to gauge the level of work each member is doing in the project. This is to ensure that the prof mark down on slackers in the group. Each group is also required to set-up a wiki page on GitHub containing the overall summary of the project, the solutions to the 10 business questions, as well as key insights and future improvements. The wiki also has a word limit of 2000 to 2500 words in total. Adhere to the requirements of the project stated in the playbook and your group should be okay with the project! Even naming conventions are important before submission. If in doubt, simply ask your TA on Microsoft Teams and they should reply your queries promptly. Roadshow: Conducted in Week 12. Each group is supposed to present to some external speakers, as well as other groups during the allocated time periods. In this semester, there are 4 available venues (Level 3 of S16) and there will be 3 to 4 groups presenting at the same time. The roadshow starts at 6.30pm and ends at 7.50pm approximately. There is no interval between group presentations. There will be 10 minutes allocated for presentation tine and 5 minutes of QnA by the external guests. During times when your group is not presenting, your members are supposed to head over to the venue of your assigned groups for peer evaluations. There is also some catered food for everyone to have their dinner due to the bad timing of the roadshow. Nothing much for this semester though. Fireside Chat: This semester's guest speakers involve one from OpenAI, the second from UOB and the third from Wise. It is just a panel discussion where one of our TAs ask them some questions regarding their day-to-day job at their companies, what they do during their working hours, technical skills required and some tips on how to ace interviews to secure internships. Nothing much to be honest and some questions from students are answered by the speakers during the remaining time. Disappointed when there are no job opportunities available in these companies as they attributed to the fact that they are not actively hiring or their companies reeling over the bad economy. Overall, DSA3101 was not really an enjoyable experience for me due to the reasons stated above. Additionally, the quiz made it worse as I was not feeling well on that day and lost concentration often in the midst of the test. Group project was a so-so for me but some slackers in my group made things worse and I had to do some of their parts as well. This semester is probably the last semester taught by Prof Sergio as he most likely have left NUS (his name disappeared from the NUS Statistics and Data Science website). Participation - Full Datacamp - Full Group Project - Not Released Quiz - 14/43 (5th percentile around, LQ 28, Median 32, UQ 38) Expected Grade: D+ Actual Grade: TBA

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: NIL Tutor: NIL Assessments: Weekly Quizzes - 30% Individual Formative Assignments - 30% Group Project - 40% Course Content: Week 1 - Introduction to GAI Tools Week 2 - Solve General Problems with GAI Tools Week 3 - Use GAI Tools as Effective Study Aid Week 4 - Apply GAI Tools to Advanced Learning Use Cases (I): Coding And Data Analysis Week 5 - Apply GAI Tools to Advanced Learning Use Cases (II): Research And Media Creation Week 6 - Adverse Use and Societal Implications of GAI Tools Module Ratings: Workload: 3/10 Difficulty: 3/10 Enjoyability: 9/10 CP1008 is a 2MC CS/CU module, purely online-based with no in-person lectures. This is by far one of my favorite modules in NUS as it is very practical. There are neither midterms nor final exams for the module. The module covers only 6 weeks, so by recess week, this module will be completed already! Workload wise, it is rather low, probably around 2 hours (maximum 3 hours) per week. For every week, you are first required to watch around 2 to 4 online lectures (pre-recorded videos) about the content. Each video is only around 10 to 15 minutes long which is quite condensed. You need to listen to the videos carefully as you are required to take a short quiz afterwards, where the questions test on the video content. The quiz questions are either MCQ or MRQ format. The questions are not tricky and are rather straightforward. Also, for every week, you need to complete an assignment regarding the content taught for the specific week. Some of the assignments are quite fun as you are allowed to use AI tools (not only ChatGPT but many others as well like Google Bard, Perplexity etc.) to complete certain portions. These include generating AI-created images, leveraging ChatGPT as your virtual tutor and learning a new progamming language. From Week 3 onwards till Week 6, there will be a group project where you need to form groups of 4 to 5. The group project requires you to think about a learning or assessment task that your group has experienced in a current or past academic course taken in NUS, reflect about the learning or assessment process without the use of GAI tools, as well as improve the learning or assessment process with the use of GAI tools. Best is to perform a cost-benefit analysis on your suggestions. Each group needs to create some Powerpoint slides, as well as a 15-minute video on your content. However, do take note that for the allocation of groups, it is not pre-assigned. You need to form your own groups yourself. Be proactive and try your best to find one. Although the teaching team mentioned in the slides that students with no groups will be randomly assigned to a group by the end of Week 4, it is not done by the coordinators. As a result, some students have no groups and we tried emailing the professors but no response. Overall, this module is quite enjoyable and interesting - I consider this module to actually be part of a stress-reliver away from my core modules where I get a chance to play around using generative AI tools. I encourage you to try out this module as it might be useful for your work/future career where AI might be needed to perform some tasks. Expected Grade: CS Actual Grade: CS
thread_title: CP1008 Learning with Generative AI Tools

author: Damaen Tan
message: Latest Update: QF4102 has changed its prerequisites. Initially, only QF3101 is the prerequisite of QF4102. From AY25/26 onwards, the prerequisites have changed to both QF3101 and QF3103. This review might not be the most accurate as I have taken this course in AY24/25 before the prerequisite change. I did not take QF3103, but am still allowed to take this module. Neither did I take QF4103. I jumped straight from QF3101 to QF4102. Due to the lack of pre-existing knowledge, it explains my slightly lower than average grade for the module. Many students taking this module are Year 4 or Master Students like what ph has mentioned who did QF3103 and QF4103 (or taking concurrently). I took this module in Year 3 as I wanted to quickly finish my last QF coded module for my 2nd major before switching to FIN2704X and FIN3702 for my last module requirement for QF. If you have taken QF3103 before this module or will be in batch AY25/26 or later, the module will definitely be slightly easier for you, although it is still very content heavy. With hard work, a B+/A- is considered attainable. Recommended QF Path For QF 2nd Majors/Primary Majors: QF1100 -> QF2104 -> QF2103 + QF3101 -> QF3103 -> QF4103 -> QF4102 -> QF4205/QF4211 (For Primary Majors). QF4212 is another possible option but for now, I will advise you against taking this module unless the lecturer changes. Do take note that there are pre-requisites of QF4211 and QF4212 as well.
