author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY24/25 Semester 1 Course Coordinator 1: May Hua Wong Course Coordinator 2: Dr Lakshmi * This is a full online course and there are no in-person lectures and tutorials for this module. Assessment: 20% VIPS Worksheet 20% Practice Assessment Tests on Graduates First 20% VMock Interview Report 10% Information Interview Information 30% Reflection Journal Difficulty: 2/10 Enjoyability: 8/10 Workload: 2/10 This is a 2MC module with a CS/CU grading system. CFG3001 is suitable for Year 3 and 4 students who are interested to hone their skills for upcoming internships during their penultimate/final year in university. The module will teach you how to: 1) Define your brand 2) Build and communicate your personal brand 3) Networking for success 4) Essential interview skills 5) What to do for the first 90 days of your internship/job 6) Etiquette for the Office and Remote Working You are required to watch all lecture videos, read several slides in canvas and complete all the assessments and several small random quizzes to get a CS for the module. Best is to complete them as fast as possible (of course internalize what you have learnt and not blindly scheme through) before the semester gets heavy with the introduction of exams and assignments. A good module to take, especially in Year 3 where many will start to source for internships after learning enough content/hard skills for the job roles and positions. Year 1 and 2 students are not allowed to take this module based on requirements on NUSMods. The best is to complement this with the Roots and Wings courses (PLS8001, PLS8002, PLS8003, PLS8004, PLS8005) to improve your soft skills as well to stand out from the rest in order to increase your chances of landing a job successfully. You can refer to my reviews for several Roots and Wings courses: PLS8001, PLS8003 and PLS8005. Expected Grade: CS Actual Grade: CS

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer 1: Prof Joel Chow Lecturer 2: Prof Martin Mattsson Assessments: 20% (Lecture and tutorial participation) 25% (5 in-lecture quizzes) 25% (Group Project slides + presentation) 30% (Individual Essay) Topics Covered: Week 1: Introduction To Welfare and Effective Altruism Week 2: Why Welfare Matters Week 3: Diving deeper into Welfare - What exactly is Welfare? Week 4: How Can We Measure Welfare? Week 5: Cause Prioritization - Why and How Can We Promote the Welfare of the Global Poor? Week 6: Global Health and Development - Why is it important? Week 7: Global Health and Development Initiatives - What Has Worked and What Might We Do in the Future? Week 8: If Welfare Matters, What about Animal Welfare? Should We be Concerned about Animal Welfare? Week 9: The Economics of Social Movements Week 10: What (if anything), Do We Owe to our Distant Descendants? Week 11: Existential Risks and Discounting Week 12: Criticisms of Effective Altruism Week 13: Review of course content + Wrap up Difficulty: 4/10 Enjoyability: 6/10 Workload: 3/10 HS2921 teaches a lot about Effective Altruism (EA), why EA is important and matters and the use of EA under various contexts and issues that the world is currently facing, such as animal welfare, global health and development and social movements. Lectures: Once a week, 2 hours. This module is rather chill and you will attend a 2-hour lecture weekly. Best is to come for lectures as there will be several in-lecture quizzes which must be completed within s short period of time. Quizzes are in weeks 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 respectively during lecture timing. Content can get quite heavy and dry at times due to many words and slides in the lecture notes. Some information in the lecture slides are highly philosophical and economics heavy - so a strong foundation in these areas will give you an advantage in understanding. Quizzes: 5 quizzes in total, score will be based on best 4 scores out of 5, hence there is some room for one mess up for the 5 quizzes. Quizzes are related to the readings for the week and the prior week - for example fir the quiz in week 9, weeks 8 and 9 readings will be tested in the quiz questions. You are not allowed to discuss the quiz questions with your friends/group members and must be done individually. All quiz questions are either MCQ or MRQ (1 exception for the first quiz) and most are rather okay if you read the readings prior to the quiz. Tutorials: Once in 2 weeks, 2 hours. Tutorials serve as an extension to the lectures in terms if content and critical thinking. There will be several group discussions for us to ponder about how EA can come to play under various contexts as well as content that we have learnt in lectures to apply to these real-world case scenarios. Participation is highly encouraged - via both discussions and pollev questions as the lecturers will be able to track your participation rates and that 20% grade weightage for participation is rather high. Group Project: We need to come up with a comprehensive analysis on a particular issue society is currently facing. These are the basic requirements: 1) Identify the main issue 2) Explain using the INT framework (important, neglectedness, tractability) 3) Discuss possible interventions to circumvent the issue 4) Explain the benefits and limitations of the interventions We are subsequently required to give a 20-minute recorded presentation in our analysis. All 4 to 5 team members are required to be speaking in the presentation. The project is rather heavy to be honest. Individual Essay: Similar format to that of the Group Project, but to be done individually and you need to write a maximum 2500 word essay. You are allowed to use the same issues in your group project and individual essay, but not recommended. You can start the individual essay anytime and it is due at the end of Week 13, hence there will be plenty of time for you to brainstorm for ideas and perform a thorough analysis. No recordings of yourself are required for the essay. There are no midterms and final exam for this module and this module can be SU-ed due to it being an interdisciplinary module. Overall, HS2921 has vastly changed my thinking about EA, helping me clear several misconceptions along the way. A good course to increase knowledge about EA from multiple viewpoints, as well as having a bonus guest lecture (Peter Singer) in Week 12 :) Quizzes: 4/4 + 4/4 + 2/4 + 4/4 + 4/4 (Full marks, best 4) Participation: Full Group Project: ? Individual Essay: ? Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: B (S/U option exercised) I was so disappointed by the grade, don't know what happened, I thought worse case is B+.
thread_title: HS2921 Effective Altruism in Theory and Practice

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Lam Xin Yee Tutor: Lam Xin Yee (only for my tutorial group) Assessments: 5%(Weekly after lecture quizzes) 15% (3 Homework, 5% each) 25% Midterms 55% Final Exam Topics Covered: 1. Introduction to convex optimization 2. Optimization models 3. Unconstrained and constrained linear programs 4. Convex sets and functions, Weierstrass Theorem + properties 5. Local and global minimizers, saddle points 6. Hessian (2nd derivative) 7. Coercive functions 8. Bisection, Newton and Golden Search selection methods 9. Steepest descent and coordinate descent methods (Jacobi + Gauss Seidel) 10. Stochastic gradient descent method 11. KKT points and 1st, 2nd theorems 12. Lagrange multipliers + Support Vector Machines 13. Lagrangian Dual problems, Primal problems 14. Weak and Strong Duality Theorems, Duality gap 15. Subgradient and Projected Gradient Descent method 16. Quadratic Penalty method 17. Augmented Lagrangian method 18. Barrier Function method Difficulty: 7/10 Enjoyability: 7.5/10 Workload: 7.5/10 DSA3102 is a module that focuses heavily on the mathematical concepts of optimization processes. It is an extension of DSA2102, where certain concepts are repeated but taught more in-depth. There are other additional new concepts not taught in DSA2102. The lecture content of this module is quite heavy and best is to not overload when taking this module. Lectures: Only once a week, 3 hours. As the lecture time per week is less than usual (2 lessons per week, 2 hours each), Prof Lam had to speed up in her teaching sometimes, especially the last few lectures. The lectures are conducted from 7pm to 10pm, which is a very unsuitable timing for many, especially non-hostel students like me. The lectures are not recorded, but there are several pre-recorded lectures that Prof Lam uploaded on canvas which is basically the same content as taught in live lectures. Tutorials: Weekly tutorials for us to try several questions first before lessons, some computation and some proving questions. For several weeks, there will be one programming exercise in Python for method implementation and usage. Prof Lam explains the tutorial questions thoroughly and well, which is really commendable as 1 hour to explain everything is not an easy task at all. Assignments: 2 normal mathematics assignments and 1 fully Python-related assignment. They are given out in weeks 4, 9 and 12 respectively. The three assignments are generally on the difficult side, especially the last one. Please allocate some time to do the assignments and don't rush them, careless mistakes are very common in this module. Quizzes: Similar to DSA2102 under the same lecturer, the quizzes are meant to basically aid in your understanding of the concepts taught in the lecture/recorded videos. Again, there are no trick questions and you will get them correct if you understand the content sufficiently well. On average there are 3 to 4 questions per quiz, most of which are MCQ and short-answer questions without the need to do lots of working. Midterms: 1 hour 15 minutes, closed-book, 1 double-sided helpsheet allowed. Midterms basically tests on the first four lectures till hessian functions. The midterms was quite easy and most questions are rather standard, similar to that of tutorials. Most are computation questions without much proving. Surprisingly, the cohort did not perform as well as expected probably due to time constraints. Final Exam: 2 hours, closed-book, 1 double-sided helpsheet allowed. Finals tested everything taught in lectures with the exception of proving of KKT theorems and Barrier functions. Finals was way harder than midterms although most are still computation questions. This is because almost all the questions in the final exam tested on high-dimensional matrices, which slowed me down tremendously when solving the computation questions. There are 5 questions and 2/3 questions are really tough. One reason is probably due to lack of examples of computing such matrices in the lecture notes, leading to lots of confusion in the exam hall. There are mixed reactions to this paper, some mentioned it was really easy (probably attributed to the fact of their strong foundation in MA2001 Linear Algebra) while some like me found it really difficult. Overall DSA3102 was quite enjoyable for me until the final exam struck me. I was really sure that I screwed up the final exam. The lecturer teaches well and I recommend taking such modules under Prof Lam. Quizzes: Full Assignments: 50/50 + 50/50 + ?/50 Midterms: 41/50 (Median 31, UQ 37) Final Exam: ? (Probably 50 to 60%) Expected Grade: B+ Actual Grade: A
thread_title: DSA3102 Essential Data Analytics Tools: Convex Optimisation

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer 1: Prof Eric Han Lecturer 2: Prof Brain Lim Tutorial TA: Tong Yao Assessments: 5% Lecture and Tutorial Participation 10% Python Assignment 5% Group Project Statement 25% Group Project Machine Learning Video Submission 20% Midterms 35% Final Exam Topic Covered: 1. Machine Learning Pipeline 2. Paradigms Of Machine Leaning 3. K-Nearest Neighbours 4. Decision Trees and 5. Ensemble Learning 6. Linear Models 7. Support Vector Machines 8. Bias and Variance 9. Regularization and Validation 10. Model Evaluations (Precision, Recall F1-Score etc.) 11. Data Processing 12. Feature Extraction and Engineering 13. Perceptrons 14. Neural Networks (CNN, RNN) 15. Deep Learning 16. Explainable AI 17. Unsupervised Learning 18. AI Ethics Difficulty: 8/10 Enjoyability: 5/10 Workload: 7/10 Personally, I feel that CS3244 is way too theoretical and mathematical with too little emphasis on programming in Python and Machine Learning which is much more useful for the working world. CS3244 gives a solid foundation in the concepts of Machine Learning models that are available but I feel that there are too few hands on sessions with the implementation of Machine Learning models in Python throughout the course. Lectures: Twice a week, 2 hours each. Prof Eric taught from Week 1 to 6 while Prof Brian taught from Week 7 to 12. Prof Eric came back to teach AI Ethics in Week 13. The issue is that these two lecturers have completely different teaching styles which make learning a mess. Prof Eric focuses heavily on the theoretical and mathematical portions of the models, even delving into Lagrange multipliers and complex differentiation. Many students were either disinterested or even confused about what he was teaching due to the mathematical rigor in his slides. Meanwhile, Prof Brian explains to the class using visuals and images to explain concepts. Although his notes are much more user-friendly, the lack of words make it sometimes hard for me to understand what was going on, especially in the CNN and RNN lectures. I felt that the lectures were a failure due to the stark differences in the profs teaching styles. Additionally, both Profs, especially Prof Brian, were way too fast in the delivery of concepts during lectures due to content overload. Perhaps this module should be revamped to include less content topics but more on programming skills. Tutorials: Similar to many other modules, once a week 1 hour. There will be tutorial questions for us to try before the sessions, most of the questions being theoretical. No coding is required for the tutorial questions. For the first few weeks, the tutorials were given too late for us to try beforehand, especially Monday tutorials but after some reviews on Slack, the tutorials questions were given earlier. Assignment: I felt that the assignment was meaningless to be honest. Although the assignment is done in Python code which we wanted, the focus is wrong. The assignment wants us to derive the Machine Learning algorithms from scratch (like how to construct a Decision Tree Classifier Model). This is unnecessary as there are already pre-built packages in scikit-learn that serve this purpose. We need to crack our brains to tackle these redundant problems that have already been solved. Group Project: Personally, I feel that the group project is the saving grace of this module. We are supposed to construct at least two models that we have learnt in lectures to solve a dataset. There are many datasets available that we can choose from - our group chose the Guns Incident dataset. There are various difficulty levels and we chose an easy one due to the fact that we are tackling Machine Learning for the first time. Fortunately, my group members are co-operative and we successfully implemented Decision Trees and Logistic Regression although with only satisfactory accuracies. Best is to include all knowledge learnt from lectures. A 15-minute video is needed for submission, explaining the overview of what we did for the project. Well, I felt that 15 minutes is way too short for a group of 6 to present but there is a strict time limit imposed. Midterms and Final Exam: Midterms an Final exams are both conducted on Examplify. Again, many of the questions are too theoretical in nature. Both exams are open-book, no internet. The exams consists a mixture of MCQ and MRQ questions. There are a couple of True/False questions as well. There are optional explanation questions that we can justify to get partial marks. If your answer is wrong but you are able to justify correctly in some way, partial marks will be given. However, if your answer is entirely correct but you justified wrongly, you will be penalized. Final exam was moderately harder than midterms due to the fact that certain complex concepts such as GradCAM and LIME are heavily tested. Overall, CS3244 is a module that is not to my liking and hope that there will be a change in focus from more theoretical-based to coding-based. With theory but without coding experience, one cannot be a successful Machine Learning Engineer/Data Scientist. We really need more coding practice. Hence, I did other courses on Datacamp to boost my coding knowledge. The Profs mentioned that coding in Python should be self-practiced and not really covered in this module. Assignment 1: 9.5/10 Participation: Full Group Project Draft: 14/15 Midterms: 26.1/34 (Median 25.5, UQ 27.7) Group Project Submission: ? Final Exam: ? Expected Grade: B Actual Grade: A- (Pleasantly surprised)

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Bertrand Sim Tutorial Teacher: Bertrand Sim Assessments: 4% - Tutorial Participation and Presentation 36% - 3 Assignments (In MATLAB) 60% - Final Exam Topics Covered: 1. Binomial Tree Method For Vanilla Options 2. Path-Dependent Options (Barrier Options) 3. Similarity Reduction (Lookback and Asian Options) 4. Forward Shooting Grid Method 5. Stochastic Calculus 6. PDE Models For Financial Derivatives 7. Consistency Between BTM and Black-Scholes PDE 8. Introduction To Finite Differences 9. Explicit And Implicit Schemes 10. Upwind Treatment And Iterative Methods 11. Lattice Methods For Lookback And American Options 12. Options With Two Underlying Assets 13. Monte-Carlo Simulation Methods 14. Variance Reduction 15. Flaws In Black-Scholes Model 16. Local Volatility Model 17. Stochastic Volatility Model 18. Jump Diffusion Model Difficulty: 10/10 Enjoyability: 2/10 Workload: 8.5/10 In my opinion, QF4102 is the second worst module that I have taken in NUS, just slightly better compared to DSE4212/QF4212 Data Science In FinTech which I dropped in Week 4. The content difficulty of this module is way beyond me - a lot of complex formulas that I cannot understand. This is exacerbated by the poor teaching of the lecturer, who is teaching in NUS for the first time. QF4102 is definitely not well-run with a lot of hiccups along the way. Initially, this module has been taught by Tan Hwee Huat, who just retired from NUS a few months ago. Lectures: Only once a week, 3 hour sessions. The lecturer has the tendency to come late for lectures and tutorial. The way the lecturer teaches is not suitable for my learning as he is not even confident when delivering lectures. He makes many errors in his lecture notes, as well as his teachings. He will sometimes state “Oh what is wrong with this, ok I will check on this later or next time.” which shows that he is unprepared for lessons. In the end, he either did not get back to us about his mistakes or he will say it very briefly during the next lecture. Lecture notes are full of errors, resulting in lots of confusion among the students in the class. Tutorials: Similarly, once a week 1 hour sessions. For me, the tutorials are of not much use as more than half the time, we will hear other students present their answers on the whiteboard or screen while the lecturer simply sits at a table and evaluates us. Usually, there are about 2 to 4 students presenting per tutorial for the 2% presentation points. Prof will just explain the hardest questions in the tutorial or questions that no students want to volunteer to present. His tutorial solutions also missed out on lots of workings, leading to confusion about the steps to the final answer. Assignments: This is the straw that broke the camel’s back. 3 MATLAB assignments, 12% each. I never once see a lecturer giving such high percentages to assignments. The MATLAB assignments are insanely difficult. The assignment can be done either as pairs or individually. Without any close friends who are taking this module together with me, I have to complete them on my own. I spent many days and days writing MATLAB code, debugging for errors, constructing diagrams and writing full reports but my hard work did not bear fruit at all. In the end, I end up being below lower quartile for two assignments, one of which is a failing grade (19/40). Final Exam: 2.5 hours, closed-book, 1 helpsheet double-sided allowed. This is the first ever module where no calculators, including non-graphical ones, are allowed. The worst thing is when the lecturer told us that there will only be simple computations like 0.5 times 8 or something of this sort, there are actually some complex calculations which requires the use of a calculator for the last question of the final exam. I spent a huge chunk of time computing the calculations by hand and as a result, cannot finish the paper. Most questions are hard and involve the use of MATLAB pseudocode and advanced transformation techniques. Overall, I am disappointed with the module, especially with the organization, content difficulty and extreme incomprehensible formulas that just keep springing out of the lecturers’ notes. For QF primary majors, you should take QF4103 first before taking QF4102 as the lecturer mentioned briefly that QF4102 content involves heavy use of QF4103 content although QF4103 is not a prerequisite. For QF second majors, stay clear of this module unless you enjoy formula proving questions or MATLAB. Assignment 1 - 27/40 (Lower Quartile) Assignment 2 - 19/40 (Below Lower Quartile) Assignment 3 - 36/40 Final Exam - ? Expected Grade: B- Actual Grade: TBC

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY 24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Xu Huansang Tutorial TA: ? I will not be posting the contents of this module as I have withdrawn from this module since Week 4. I will be sharing my experience with this module for the first four weeks when I was attending lectures and tutorials. Comments Of The Module: This QF/DSE module was held for the first time. Intrigued by the name "Data Science In Fintech", I was really interested to learn some Data Science skills and concepts in the finance-tech industry since I am a primary major in Data Science and a second major in Quantitative Finance. Hence, I opted for this module with initial high hopes to expand my learning. However, when I realized who was going to teach this module (I only knew who was teaching after I chose the module), I was deeply disappointed. She is neither a professor nor a lecturer. She is a research staff who has no prior teaching experience at all. My confidence began to fall as I am not sure how she will teach in class. This marks the beginning of a rough patch.... But perhaps she might teach well as I did not know her and is teaching me for the first time. I do not want to judge her - I just know that she is teaching in NUS for the first time. When I attended lessons for the first week, the content was just simply overwhelming with lots of formulas and symbols placed in her notes. I could only understand about 30% of the notes in week 1. The prerequisite of DSA2102 and ST2131 is obviously inaccurate based on the difficulty of the course. You are required to have knowledge on CS3244, MA3238 and QF4102/QF4103 to take this module more confidently. The "teacher" did not slow down and just breezed through the formulas and symbols without giving us time to understand what they mean. Even if explained, we do not know what was going on as abstract concepts just came out from nowhere. She thought that we have already a lot of knowledge about Fintech content, Machine Learning and Financial Algorithms even though we barely scratched the surface of Machine Learning. There are already about 15+ students who withdrew from this module after Week 1 as they cannot stand her explanations. I decided to continue with the lectures and tutorials for a while more, perhaps Week 1 was just a hiccup. Unfortunately, when subsequent weeks' lecture notes are uploaded, I saw them and realized that the number of symbols and formulas just keep on increasing and when I attended lectures, she was just reading from the lecture notes in her monotonous voice, I felt that I cannot learn well under her. Many of the students in the class share the same sentiment as me. The concepts became too technical and I did not know what to expect for the final exams. By week 3, over 30 students out of the initial 100+ students pulled out of the module. Tutorials: Weekly tutorials, given a few questions for us to try before attending. I was surprised that most questions are actually proving based (not computational ones) and the mathematical symbols simply killed my interest. Some tutorial questions are not directly related to the lecture content as well. After Week 3, I did not know what was going on. I could only understand perhaps 5% of the content in the lecture notes. The teaching did not improve and remained as bad since the start. I knew that with this rate of understanding, I would flunk the final exam. Simultaneously, the group project was about to begin and I feel that it is best to drop the module at this juncture to prevent myself from being a freeloader for my assigned group. Hence, I dropped the module on Modreg and this ends my painful journey with QF4212 Data Science In Fintech. Please do not ever take this module under Xu Huansang. You will just be very confused with all the equations, formulas and symbols that come out from nowhere from her notes without sufficient teaching. For QF minors/second-majors, stay clear of this module. For QF primary majors, probably take QF4211 or QF4205 instead for your major requirements. I am not sure if there will be a change in lecturer for upcoming years but probably not since it is her first year teaching the module. Actual Grade: W

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY24/25 Semester 1 Tutor: Dr. Lakshmi Assessment: 70% Tutorial Attendance + Participation 10% Reflection Piece * 3 Ratings Of Module: Workload: 1/10 Difficulty: 1/10 Enjoyability: 10/10 Overview: PLS8005 is covered among one of the five modules under the Roots And Wings Workshop, led by both the CFG and Psychology teams. This is a 1MC module and is graded on a CS/CU basis. In PLS8005, Elevating Interpersonal Communication, there are three sessions, each lasting 3 hours, fortnightly. The weeks where you attend the lessons depend on the class that you have chosen on Modreg. In PLS8005 (or any other Roots and Wings) Module, you first need to attend all three sessions. Not attending any of the three lessons without a valid reason will give you an immediate CU grade. You need to inform the teacher in-charge if you cannot make it and you need to do a makeup lesson. Furthermore, active participation is a must. Attending the lessons without participating will affect your participation marks greatly, so do put in some effort and try to say something relevant to the concepts learnt in the module. Content: Lesson 1 - Introduction to communication and networking, skill model of interpersonal communication, essential factors in communication, (verbal, non-verbal and para-verbal techniques), storytelling, the dramatic arc, the hero's journey Lesson 2 - The science of influence (6 principles), persuading others, the art of questioning, 5P model of explaining, effective questioning, the questioning funnel, having productive conversations Lesson 3 - Social exchanges, self-disclosures, deciding when to self-disclose, active listening, empathic listening, 5-part formula for Empathic Communication, intercultural communication, culture and miscommunication, cultural intelligence Group Activities: Several group activities for all three lessons, where you need to communicate with your buddy or group of 3 about some of your stories that you have during your childhood/academic journey etc. Group activities are fun where you get to hear your buddy's childhood journey. Sharing of group discussions is required and highly encouraged afterwards to showcase what you have learnt during lessons. Opinion Of Module: Highly interesting for all three lessons, especially when there are several students in the class (including me) who like to actively participate and share their thoughts and opinions. This makes the atmosphere of the class more lively and engaging. Reflection Journals: At the end of each session, you are required to write/type a reflection journal of about 500 words or more (equivalent to about half a page) about insights that you have learnt from the session and how you can apply them in your daily lives. Giving lots of examples will be great! Simply copying the information from the slides posted is a no no, you need to think out of the box. Of course, no plagiarism allowed! If caught, you will get a zero for the reflection and you cannot redo the assignment again. Demographics: Most are Year 2 and 3 students in the class and similar to me, they are probably taking this module to improve on their soft skills as most of us tend to focus on our academics too much without even considering soft skills mastery. Some are Year 4 and take this module for graduation to meet their MC requirements. I enjoyed PLS8005 tremendously and I recommend that you take this module under Dr. Lakshmi as she is really passionate and enthusiastic in her teaching! Predicted Grade: CS Actual Grade: CS

author: Damaen Tan
message: Final Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof Tan Hwee Huat Tutor: Prof Tan Hwee Huat Assessment: 2% Tutorial Attendance + Participation 2% In-Lecture Quizzes 15% Midterms 21% Computing Assignment 60% Final Exam Content Of Lecture: Ratings Of Module: Difficulty: 9/10 Enjoyability: 8/10 Workload: 6/10 (Due to computing assignment) Qf3101 is an extension of QF2104, but the difficulty of this module has increased many folds compared to QF2104 (About 10 times to be exact). This module is a very calculation intensive module, with a lot of formulas being thrown in. Do not blindly memorize the formulas like I did. You need to understand the proof behind these formulas and derive them if necessary for computation subsequently. This module holds the record of the largest number of complex mathematical formulas for all the modules I have taken in NUS. The focus of the modules is on Chapters 1 and 2, with it being tested in the midterms and the final exam. Chapter 3 onwards are only tested in the final exam. There are a lot of technical terms and concepts that I am ignorant of as these concepts are not taught in previous QF modules. Concepts like REPOS, SOFRS, Interest Rate Swaps, Currency Swaps, Forward Rate Agreements etc. Fortunately, the examples given in Prof Tan's slides are simplified and easy to understand, unlike other lecturers who copy and paste from textbooks. This will help me to improve my learning greatly as too wordy notes will more likely make me give up. The online quizzes are held in lectures, usually at the 1-hour mark. The quizzes comprise some MCQ and some structured questions. Majority of them are computation questions. There is a time limit (5 to 10 minutes for 2 to 3 questions) imposed. Some questions are tricky, but most can be solved if you revised your notes properly! For the tutorials, it is conducted weekly. Prof Tan will upload the tutorial worksheet one week before the class for you to try. You need to try them as you might be called to present your answer on the whiteboard. You need to present a minimum of 3 to 4 times to get the 2% Tutorial Participation points. Attending without participation is insufficient. Most tutorial questions are also computation questions. Some involve rigorous proving which are out of scope for the exams. The scariest component is the computing assignment. The computing assignment is done in VBA code. There will be one lecture dedicated to Prof Tan teaching how to solve the VBA assignment - listen carefully, it is really important. Not only do you need to write VBA code, you also need to write a report on your analysis and the results of the graphs plotted and how you obtained the resulting figures from the calculations of your VBA code. The report must be detailed, highlighting the possible reasons for the graph results (like why the curve of the interest rate SOFR is decreasing at a decreasing rate). My report was generally well done, but most of my VBA code was wrong. 30% of the total points for the computing assignment is dedicated to your style and formatting of the excel spreadsheet, with the relevant figures obtained from the websites that Prof Tan has uploaded. Do not procrastinate on the assignment, finish it as fast as possible and start early, else you will find yourself having to rush through and having no time to solve the challenging problems. The midterm was manageable with topics 1 and 2 tested. All are computation questions except for one where we need to construct an arbitrage argument. Important concepts include computing value of interest rates, REPOS and accrued interest. Prof Tan will give partial marks if there are intermediate workings correct although your final answer is wrong. Closed book, 1 hour exam, with one piece of cheatsheet , double-sided. The final exam was an extreme disaster. Closed book, 2-hour exam, with one piece of cheatsheet , double-sided. We are given 6 questions, but each question consists of many parts (I can remember one of them is from part (a) to (g)). The final exam questions are very, very long! One question makes up one whole sheet - with so many figures and numbers till I had a big headache. Just one wrong interpretation or careless mistake will make you get everything wrong, all parts are linked. Time is really tight; I doubt I can even finish the paper even if I had 3 hours. I did not even stop lifting my pen once as I knew I cannot finish the paper. When time was up, I only managed to solve just over 4/6 of the questions, with many mistakes along the way. QF3101 albeit interesting, is a difficult module and I regretted taking this module in Year 2. I should have taken this module next year when I can handle these concepts better with improved maturity. However, I cannot turn back the clock. Prof Tan has been teaching this module for many years (perhaps more than 10-15 years), but he said that he has retired and is not a regular staff at NUS already. Online Quizzes: 2% (Best 80%) Tutorial Participation: 2% Midterms: 26/36 (Median 26) Computing Assignment: 73/100 (Median 84) Finals: Not Revealed (Around 40% I guess) Expected Grade: C+ Actual Grade: B+ (Pleasantly Surprised)
thread_title: QF3101 Investment Instruments: Theory and Computation

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken In AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Lam Xin Yee Tutorial TA: Wang Chusan (Default, there are 3 TAs for this module) Assessments: 5% Weekly Quizzes (10 of them) 15% 3 Homework Assignments (one is a coding assignment in python) 25% Midterms 55% Final Exam Topics: 1. Numerical Systems - Decimal numeral system, Binary numeral system, Conversion from binary to decimal numbers and vice versa. 2. Floating-point formats - IEEE standard, Double-precision floating-point format, Machine epsilon, Rounding rule, Subnormal floating-point number, Computer arithmetic, basic operations in computer arithmetic. 3. Computer arithmetic - Some dangerous operations, Computational error, Relative rounding error, Algorithms, Implementing pseudocode, Computational cost, Big O notation, Time complexity of algorithms. 4. Matrix multiplication - Artificial neural network, Sparse matrices. 5. Bisection Method - Intermediate Value Theorem, illustrating bisection method, Implementing bisection method in Python. 6. Fixed-Point Iteration - Illustrating fixed-point iteration, Backward error, implementing fixed-point iteration in Python, Convergence of fixed-point iteration, Root- finding problem, Evaluation of polynomials and functions, Horner's Method. 7. Gaussian elimination - Basically a recap from MA2001, Row operation using augmented matrix, Backward substitution using augmented matrix, Division by zero - Swapping the rows. 8. Gaussian elimination with pivoting strategies - Dealing with zero pivot elements, Partial Pivoting, Scaled Partial Pivoting. 9. LU factorization - Multiple linear systems with identical coefficient matrix, solving linear system via LU factorization, Existence of LU factorization, Permutation matrices, PA = LU factorization, Incorporating pivoting strategies. 10. Cholesky factorization - Symmetric positive-definite matrices, Eigenvalues of symmetric positive-definite matrices, Principal submatrix, constructing Cholesky factorization, Solving linear system via Cholesky factorization. 11.Iterative Methods - Jacobi Method, strictly diagonally dominant matrices, Gauss-Seidel Method, Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR) Method. 12. Interpolation problems - Properties of interpolating function, Simplicity and Approximability, Linear interpolation, Linear spline interpolation, Cubic spline interpolation. 13. Lagrange interpolating polynomial - Basis functions, Lagrange basis polynomials, Adding more interpolating nodes, Two forms of interpolating polynomials. 14. Newton's divided differences - Computation of divided differences, Table of divided differences, adding extra interpolating nodes, Newtons interpolating polynomial. 15. Chebyshev Interpolation - Error of interpolation, equally spaced interpolation nodes, Choosing appropriate interpolation nodes, Chebyshev nodes, Chebyshev polynomial, properties of Chebyshev nodes. 16. Linear least squares problem - Least squares and the normal equations, Length of vector, Orthogonality, solving normal equations via Cholesky factorization, Fitting models to data. 17. QR factorization - QR factorization via Gram Schmidt orthogonalization, least squares problem via reduced QR factorization. 18. Numerical Differentiation - Definition of derivative, Taylors Theorem, Finite difference formulas, Two-point forward-difference formula, Generalized Intermediate Value Theorem, Three-point centered-difference formula, Approximation formula for higher derivatives, Rounding error, Extrapolation. 19. Numerical integration - Definite integral, Newton-Cotes approach, Trapezoid Rule, Simpsons Rule, Degree of precision, Composite Trapezoid and Simpsons Rule, Composite Midpoint Rule, Romberg Integration, Gaussian quadrature, Legendre polynomials. Ratings of Module: Difficulty: 6.5/10 Enjoyability: 7/10 Workload: 9/10 DSA2102 is one of the most content heavy modules so far for the 2 years in NUS. I don't know about other lecturers, but Prof Lam gave a lot of content for this module. The pace is 1 chapter a lesson (hence 2 chapters per week as lectures are held twice a week). DSA2102 is another version of MA2213 (Numerical Analysis), but teaches almost the same thing, this module is meant for DSA/DSE students instead of math students. I find the lecture pace this semester towards the fast side due to high content, although it was really surprising that more than 50% of students found the pace slow or just right. I attended lectures online as there is webcasting since the lectures are at 6pm. You will learn from Prof Lam well if you slow down the lecture speed to 0.75x the original pace. She states really important concepts from the lecture notes that are highly useful for the exams. Tutorials are also conducted weekly, most of the questions in tutorials are proving questions. Some are computational which are generally simpler. My TA taught the proving questions first due to the demands of the class. The online quizzes are held weekly and they consist of simple MCQ or fill in the blanks questions. Many of the questions are directly related to the lecture notes and you do not need to overthink to solve them. There are three homework assignments. One of it is a computing assignment, where you need to type out python code on the algorithm that is being tested and state its effectiveness. For my semester, we are tested on Jacobi, Gauss Seidel and SOR methods. I had a hard time coding and I still had errors and inaccuracies in my output even during submission. You have about 2 weeks to complete the computing assignment. The other two assignments are the typical written assignments, most are difficult questions. The midterms was challenging and to my dismay, out of 6 questions, 4 questions tested on my weakest topic, pseudocode writing and evaluation, together with time complexity. The remaining two questions are computation ones which accounts for the least marks in the exam. I made a careless mistake on one of the computation questions for the fixed point iteration. Advice: Always read the questions carefully and do not solve hurrily for computation questions. Closed book and 1 piece of cheatsheet allowed, double-sided. Topics tested ranges from Chapter 1 to 9. Time given is 1.5 hours. The finals was manageable, a couple of difficult ones here and there. Closed book and 1 piece of cheatsheet allowed, double-sided. Topics tested include everything except for the last part of the last chapter on Gaussian Quadrature onwards. Some of the questions are computationally heavy and involves multiple parts. It is still quite time tight to complete the paper in 2 hours. Online Quizzes: Full Homework: 136/150 (Median 142) Midterms: 32/50 (Median 31) Finals: Not revealed (Around 75%) Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: B+
thread_title: DSA2102 Essential Data Analytics Tools: Numerical Computation

author: Damaen Tan
message:
Taken in AY23/24 Semester 2

Tutor: Ms Monica Owyong

Assessment: 
Group-Based Chapter Reflection (5%)
Science News Article Submission 1 (20%)
Science News Article Submission 2 (30%)
Oral Presentation (25%)
Reflective Commentary (10%)
Peer Reviews (5%) 
Tutorial Participation (5%)

Ratings: 
Difficulty: 5/10 
Enjoyability: 7.5/10
Workload: 7/10

Content Covered: 
How to write science articles in an understandable way so that readers who are not in the field can understand these articles. This can be done so by reducing the use of technical jargons and mathematical theorems in the articles as these users cannot understand the concepts behind them.

Concepts covered include the use of analogies, evaluative language, functional recontextualization, definitions, descriptions, deontological and teleological appeals. These language use tips can help you to explain science news articles throughout the science news articles that you need to submit.

For weeks 2 to 7, you will be exposed to a few articles (some are technical and long science research articles, usually from PubMed or other credible sources while others are science articles that are easy to read). There will be instructions that will tell you which readings are compulsory for group discussion during the tutorials. At times, after reading the articles, there will be pre-exercises on Miro Board for you to pen down your ideas (for instance the use of any appeals in the science article) with a short video on the lesson agenda and content. Do make sure to complete them as this will affect your class participation score.

Tutorials are 3 hours long without any break, hence do be alert for the tutorials or eat some sweets in the middle to keep yourself energized throughout the lesson. For the lessons I have completed, they are quite interesting and Ms Owyong was really a nice teacher! Although more than half the class was not really participative and there was an awkward silence in the class for seconds, even minutes, she tried her best to make the class enthusiastic. Some might not like the format of this module and thinking of SU-ing since the first lesson.

For the Group-Based Chapter Reflection, we are supposed to choose one of the 2 articles provided and find any 2 relevant language use method used in the article and explain why they are effective in explaining to non-specialized readers. Best is to identify the strategies correctly; else your marks will be capped. You also need to provide examples of how these strategies used can be applied to the article chosen. Word limit is 200 words.

For the Science Article, you can select any article that you have found online, recommended that you choose PubMed ones as the assignment requires the articles chosen to be peer-reviewed and need to be published 2019 or later. Read the science article and write a science news article that is simplified yet understandable to readers. You are allowed to add things not in the article chosen, but do use referencing if you source from other websites. Word limit is 800 words.

Submit the first science article and your teacher will give you some areas for improvements for your resubmission of your news article. My tutor uses color coding (like blue for language errors, green for content insufficiency) to point out the possible improvements. Your second submission is the same as the first, after you improve and you need to add two sources that helps to explain the significance behind the analysis and results of the experiment that is included in your article chosen. Word count is 1000 words.

Next comes the reflective commentary. It is exactly the same as the group based chapter reflection, just that you need to identify the two language use strategies from your science news article. Requirements are the same.

Oral Presentation - You need to choose a science article (can be the one chosen for your science article) and deliver an 8 to 10 minute presentation on the reason why the experiment is to be conducted, the method and analysis, the results, the significance of the experiment and the benefits and limitations of the experiment. DO NOT include too much wording in your slides as this results in a loss of marks. Best is to use animations and pictures which are simplified as well, ensuring that the slide content follows smoothly without any logical gaps. Also, try to memorise the presentation and you will be at a disadvantage if you read from your script. Eye contact and gestures are important as well. You will record yourself using your phone as you place your phone on a tripod. Tip: Just stay calm.

Grading for this semester is especially strict based on what my tutor said in class. I am not sure why.

Overall, SP1541 is a useful module, especially if you are explaining to the audience that do not specialise in your area of expertise. This is especially useful in the working world where you present ideas and content to your boss or colleagues at work.

Group Reflection: 72/100 
Science News Article Submission 1: 65.3/100
Science News Article Submission 2: 72.5/100
Oral Presentation: 72.5/100 
Reflective Commentary: 70/100 
Peer Review: 5/5 
Tutorial Participation: 5/5 
Overall Grade: 72%

Expected Grade: B+
Final Grade: B+
thread_title: SP1541 Exploring Science Communication through Popular Science

author: Damaen Tan
message:
Taken in AY23/24 Semester 2

Lecturer: Prof Deon Chan 
Tutor: Elaine Tan

Content Covered: 
1. Financial Accounting Uses & Users (Financial Statements Overview) - Purposes of Financial Accounting, Users of Financial Accounting, Standards of Financial Accounting, Ethics in Accounting, Technology in Accounting, Overview of Financial Statements, Balance Sheet, Revenues and Expenses, Net Income, Statement of Comprehensive Income, Retained Earnings, Statement of Changes in Equity, Notes to Financial Statements.
2. The Accounting Cycle - The Accounting Equation, Using Accounts to Categorize Transactions, Journal Entry Format, T-accounts, Posting Journal Entries.
3. Accrual Accounting - Adjusting Entries Under Accrual Accounting, Unrecorded receivables, Unrecorded liabilities, Prepaid expenses and Supplies, Unearned revenues, Closing the books. 
4. Revenue and Receivables - Revenue Recognition, Sales Discounts, Sales Returns and Allowances, Treatment of Bad Debts, Treatment of Uncollectible Accounts Receivable, Estimating Loss Allowance, Notes Receivable, Impact of foreign currency on value of accounts receivable.
5. Cash - Major Business Activities, Accounting of Cash, Performing Bank Reconciliations, Accounting of Petty Cash, Internal Controls, Control Activities, Fraud, Unintentional Errors & Earning Management. 
6. Inventory and Cost of Sales - Costing of Inventory, COGS, Perpetual vs. Periodic System, Shrinkage, Formulas of inventory costing under Periodic System, Inventory Management - Efficiency Ratios. 
7. Operating Activities and Liabilities - Payroll and payroll-related, Sales tax payable, GST payable and VAT payable, Property tax payable, Income tax payable, Provisions and Contingent Liabilities, Product Warranty, Earnings per Share. 
8. Property, Plant and Equipment (I) - Acquisition, Depreciation methods, Straight-line Depreciation, Units-of-Production Depreciation, Declining-Balance Method, Partial-Year Depreciation Calculations, Units-of-Production Method with Natural Resources. 
9. Intangible Assets - Impairments of Long-Term Assets, Disposal of Property, Plant and Equipment, Selling PPE, Types of Intangible Assets, Goodwill, Amortization of Intangible Assets, Capitalize versus Expense. 
10. Financing Activities and Equity - Issuance of share, Ordinary vs Preference shares, Share Repurchases, Reissuing Treasury Share above and below Cost, Retained Earnings and Dividends (Cash and Share)., Share Split. 
11. Statement of Cash Flows - Operating, Investing and Financing Activities and how to classify them accordingly. 
12. Financial Statement Analysis (FSA) - Vertical vs Horizontal Analysis, Liquidity & Efficiency Ratios, Operating Cycle, Solvency Ratios, Profitability Ratios, Cash flow Ratios, DuPont Framework.

Ratings of Module:

Difficulty: 6/10 
Enjoyability: 9/10 
Workload: 4/10

Lectures are conducted once a week, no webcast and recordings available (due to rules from the business faculty). One should attend all lectures and tutorials for this module, as you will lose out information if you do not attend them. Some key points which are stated by Prof Chan are not in the lecture notes. Content might be somewhat on the heavy side for several lectures, and you need to be consistent in your learnings, if not it will be difficult to catch up.

Tutorials are conducted once a week as well, there will be certain questions (a mixture of Tutorial Notes and textbook questions) for you to try at home before the tutorial. You need to try them as there is tutorial participation for this module worth 20%. Ms. Elaine will gauge the participation of the students by giving out colored slips where you will write your name if you either explain an answer you have gotten for the tutorial questions or if you ask questions. Most students will score between 15 to 17 out of 20 for this portion as stated in the rubrics.

Midterms was a closed-book, 1 hour 15 minutes exam, all of them are MCQ questions of varying marks. Topics include chapters 1 to 6. 1 piece of cheatsheet allowed. There are a few MCQ questions worth 2 to 3 marks as they involve heavy calculations such as calculating financial ratios. The difficulty was manageable, several theory questions are asked as well (NOT just calculations and Journal Entries).

Finals was a closed-book, 2-hour exam, 80% of which is structured questions and the remaining 20% was MCQs. (I think this is the first semester where structured questions are introduced in ACC1701X module). 1 piece of cheatsheet allowed. Topics tested include everything, with a focus on chapters 7 to 12 not covered in the midterms. Surprisingly, the finals turned out to be easier than expected where there are many giveaway points for the calculation structured questions.

There is no more group project for this module unlike previous years where there are no midterms.

Personally, ACC1701X is one of the most enjoyable modules I had this semester although I started from 0 knowledge on accounting. There are technical terms and jargons along the way during the lectures, some of which are long and hard to remember. But don't worry, getting exposed to these concepts from tutorial questions again will make you get used to these terms.

Tutorial Participation: 17/20 approx. (Getting 2 to 3 slips every tutorial lesson without any absence) 
Midterms: 37/45 (Median 33)
Finals: Not revealed (About 90%)

Expected Grade: A
Actual Grade: B+ (Disappointing) 
Bell Curve must be really really steep.

author: Damaen Tan
message: ** Late Post ** Taken in AY22/23 Semester 1 Lecturer 1: Prof Tran Chieu Minh Lecturer 2: Prof Jonathon Teo Tutorial TA: I cannot remember :( Grade Weightage: 30% 3 Sets of Homework 15% Weekly Quizzes 15% Midterms 40% Final Exam Contents Of Module: 1. Real Numbers and Sets - Operations on sets, Real Numbers and Intervals, Special Subsets, Properties of Operations on Real Numbers 2. Functions - Domain and Range, Graph of a Function, Vertical Line Test for a Function, Operations on Functions, Compositions, Linear and Polynomial functions, Transcendental Functions, Algebraic Functions, Rational Functions. 3. Limits - Limits by Observing the Graph of a Function, Limits by Evaluating a Function, Limit Laws, Sandwich Theorem, Definition of Limits, Limit Inequality Theorem, One-sided Limits, Limits involving infinity, Limits at Infinity of Rational Functions, Horizontal and Oblique asymptotes, Vertical Asymptotes. 4. Continuity - Continuity Test, Removable Discontinuity, Jump Discontinuity, Infinite discontinuity, Oscillating Discontinuity, Continuous Functions, Properties of Continuous Functions, Limits of Continuous Function, Intermediate Value Theorem 5. Differentiation - Tangent lines to curves, Differentiability, Differentiable ⇒ Continuous, Differentiation Rules (Sum, product, quotient, reciprocal, power etc.), Derivative of Trigonometric Functions, Exponential and Chain rule, Derivative Rule for Inverse, Higher-order Derivatives, Implicit Differentiation. 6. Application of Differentiation - Local Min and Max, Extreme Value Theorem, First Derivative Theorem for Local Extreme Values, Critical Points, Finding Absolute Extrema, Mean Value Theorem (Rolle's Theorem), L’ Hopital’s Rule 7. Integration - Summation, Algebraic Rules for Finite Sum, Riemann Sum, Definite Integral, Integrability of Continuous Functions, Not Integrable Functions, Properties of Definite Integral, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Antiderivative, Logarithmic Differentiation, Indefinite Integrals, Area Under and Between Graphs, Improper Integrals. 8. Techniques of Integration - Integration by Substitution, Integration by Parts, Trigonometric Integrals, Trigonometric Substitution, Integration of Rational Functions by Partial Fractions, Universal Trigonometric Substitution (NOT TESTED) 9. Applications of Integration - Slicing Method, Solid of Revolution: The Disk Method, The Washer Method, Volume using Cylindrical Shells, Arc Length, Surface Area of Revolution, Area of Surface of Revolution. 10. Differential Equations - Solutions to Differential Equations, Initial Value Problem, First-Order Linear DE, Bernoulli’s Equation, Applications including compound interest and population growth, Newton’s Law of Cooling, Draining Tank Problem. Ratings Of Module: Enjoyability: 8/10 Difficulty: 5.5/10 Workload: 5/10 MA2002 serves as a continuation to 'A'-level calculus knowledge. The lectures are webcasted as the two lectures available are at extreme timings (8am and 6pm). There are many in-lecture activities such as using GeoGebra to plot complex functions before testing for continuity and differentiability, TRUE/FALSE quizzes for my lecture slot and several Kahoot games. However, be prepared to learn many more new concepts that are not taught in 'A'-level which are really important for this module. Most important concepts include knowing how to prove the existence of limit using the delta-epsilon method (I sucked at this the most), applying L Hopital's Rule, and proving the continuity and differentiability of a certain function. Tutorials are held weekly and there will be tutorial worksheets for you to try out. My TA was really patient, and he explained the concepts well, trying to make sure that he covers all the questions by making full use of the 1-hour time, which is really commendable. The bulk of the tutorial questions are proving questions where most of us are weaker in for extra practice. For the lectures, both Prof Minh and Prof Jonathon teach decently well, although Prof Minh was behind schedule for the first half of the content (since there are 2 different lectures, the pacing of the two profs are different). Prof Minh allocated more time to the proving of the limits, and this was great as this proving was heavily tested in the midterms. For the Homework sets, the difficulty is moderate with the first set testing on functions and limits, the second set testing on differentiation and applications of differentiation, the last set testing on integration, applications of integration and techniques of integration. Differential equations are not covered extensively in the homework sets nor are they heavily tested in the finals. The median score for the Homework sets is around 39/40. Midterms was manageable as it focuses only on functions, limits and differentiation (covered in the first 6 weeks of the content). Lots of L'Hopital rule was tested and this brought me great relief as I liked that concept. However, the marking of the midterms this semester was strict - they will only look at the final answer and if your final answer is wrong, you will not get any marks for the question although you might have correct steps and workings along the way. I had certain questions that I could solve halfway, but cannot get the final answer, hence 4 marks are deducted for each question. The last two questions are proving questions, worth 10 marks each - one is about continuity while the other is about differentiability. Finals was difficult - 6 questions with multiple parts, most testing on Differentiation, Integration and their applications. I could hear lots of frantic erasing of answers in the MPSH as many made careless mistakes along the way while computing the volume and surface area of the solids. Time was quite tight, and I could only solve certain parts of the questions, leaving other parts blank. Proving made up about 30% of the exam. Overall, MA2002 was quite an enjoyable module for me, especially due to the interactivity and the engagement of the class as Prof Minh tried to make the class lively through activities for the 8am class. Quizzes: Full Homeworks: 114/120 (Median is 118/120) Midterms: 74/100 (Upper Quartile 65) Finals: Not revealed (Around 65%) Expected Grade: A- Final Grade: A-

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY23/24 Semester 2 Tutor: Mohana Tan Assessment: 70% Tutorial Attendance + Participation 30% Reflection Piece * 3 Ratings: Workload: 1/10 Difficulty: 1/10 Enjoyability: 10/10 Overview: PLS8003 is covered among one of the five modules under the Roots And Wings Workshop, led by both the CFG and Psychology teams. This is a 1MC module and is graded on a CS/CU basis. In PLS8003, Cultivating Resilience, there are three sessions, each lasting 3 hours, fortnightly. The weeks where you attend the lessons depend on the class that you have chosen on Modreg. In PLS8003 (or any other Roots and Wings) Module, you first need to attend all three sessions. Not attending any of the three lessons without a valid reason will give you an immediate CU grade. You need to inform the teacher in-charge if you cannot make it and you need to do a makeup lesson. Furthermore, active participation is a must. Attending the lessons without participating will affect your participation marks greatly, so do put in some effort and try to say something relevant to the concepts learnt in the module. Content: Concepts taught in PLS8003 include Brain’s Negativity Bias, Thinking Styles, Beliefs and Biases, Thought Experiments, Stress Patterns, Defeating Negative Thinking, Cognitive Restructuring, Changing Unhelpful Thoughts, Zoom Out 10:10:10 rule, Attention Regulation, Mindfulness, Leading a Meaningful Life – PERMA+, Nourishing & Depleting Activities, Personal Strengths & Self-Narratives, Internal Locus of Control Group Activities: Less group activities compared to PLS8001 but has more class discussions and reflective thoughts during the seminars. Really enjoyed the class discussions as a whole while teacher Mohana gave several really interesting stories along the way to improve the liveliness and eagerness of the class. She talked about her personal life stories and how these events that she had experienced are related to the idea of resilience, the core topic of this module. At the end of every lesson, you need to share with the class 1 takeaway from the lesson. Opinion: I enjoyed all the three sessions, especially the second lesson where I am exposed to mindfulness and got to learn hands-on certain mindfulness exercises in class. These exercises can really help to reduce your current thoughts so that you will not feel stressed during your exams. Through this module, you will learn Body Scan, Deep Walking and others that you can practice anytime anywhere. Certain misconceptions of mindfulness are cleared. Reflection Journals: At the end of each session, you are required to write/type a reflection journal of about 500 words or more (equivalent to about half a page) about two main insights that you have learnt from the session and how you can apply them in your daily lives. Giving lots of examples will be great! Simply copying the information from the slides posted is a no no, you need to think out of the box. Of course, no plagiarism allowed! If caught, you will get a zero for the reflection and you cannot redo the assignment again. Demographics: Most are Year 4 students that are about to graduate, and they are taking Roots and Wings modules to meet the MCs upon graduation. Some are Year 2 and Year 3 students. I enjoyed PLS8003 tremendously and I recommend that you take this module under teacher Mohana as she is really passionate and enthusiastic in her teaching! Expected Grade: CS Actual Grade: CS

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Lam Xin Yee Tutorial Tutor: Lam Xin Yee Contents Of Module: 1) Introduction to python - Basically Recap of CS1010S Lecture 1 to 4. Includes Lists, Dictionaries, Tuples, Sets and String Concatenations. Includes lambda expressions and map/filter functions. 2) Introduction to NumPy - ndarray, statistical methods, mathematical operations, multi-dimensions, reshaping and resizing, stacking, flattening, Boolean indexing. 3) Introduction to pandas - Data frames, Time Series Data, Basic Visualizations using Pandas, Grouping Operations, Concatenation, Joining, Merging, Performance aspects of code. 4) Introduction to matplotlib - Plotting styles, types of plots, plotting multiple graphs, static 3D plotting, interactive 2D plotting using plotly, Quant figures, technical financial analysis using matplotlib and plotly. 5) Financial Time Series - summary statistics, log returns, resampling, rolling statistics (SMA and EMA), correlation analysis, high-frequency data. 6) Math Tools - Regression, Interpolation, Optimization, Approximation Techniques 7) Stochastics - Generating Random Numbers, Discrete and Continuous Distributions, Monte Carlo Simulations, Stochastic Processes (Geometric Brownian Motion and Square Root Diffusion), Black-Scholes-Merton option pricing model, Moment Matching, Antithetic Variates, Credit Valuation Adjustments. 8) Statistics - Normality tests, QQ Plots, portfolio optimization, Markowitz Portfolio Theory (MPT), Sharpe ratio, Risk-free asset, Capital market line (CML), Efficient frontier, Bayes Formula, Linear Regression, Machine Learning using scikit-learn (K-means clustering, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Deep neural networks (DNNs), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Transforming Features) 9) Trading Strategies - Vectorized back testing of SMAs, Optimization on the choice of parameters, Linear Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression, Frequency approach, Clustering approach, digitized features, Sequential/Randomized train-test split 10) Automated Trading - Kelly Criterion in a Binomial Setting, Kelly Criterion for Stocks and Indices, Verifying Kelly Criterion through simulation 11) Simulation of Financial Models - Object-oriented programming (OOP), Modeling time, Wrapper modules 12) Derivative Valuation - Delta of the option, Estimation of Delta, Vega of the option, Estimation of Vega, European options Ratings of Module: Workload of Module: 9/10 Difficulty of Module: 9/10 (might seem a little biased as I am not a coding type of person) Usefulness of Module: 7.5/10 Grade Weightage of Module: Online Quizzes - 5% Group Project - 15% Computing Assignment - 10% Midterm - 20% Final Test - 50% Review: QF2103 is considered a high-level module to me as it involves a lot of machine learning algorithms and programming although it is a level 2000 module. Do not underestimate the difficulty of this module. As this semester is the first time QF2103 is ever held, all of us do not know the contents of the module beforehand. This module is all in Python Code. If you have a weak foundation in the CS variants (CS1010S/CS1010E particularly), do revise them as this course needs CS1010S/CS1010E background. Warning: Without adequate Python background and content, you will most likely not do well in the module as Python code is heavily tested. Pace of the lectures and tutorials are okay, but a little on the fast side due to lots of content needed to cover. About 2 to 3 chapters every 2 weeks. Prof Lam is clear in her explanations, especially for the machine learning and stochastics portion of the lecture content. She is highly approachable, and you can ask her any questions after class/after tutorial/through email or canvas. She is prompt in her replies. There are online quizzes at times to gauge your understanding of the topics. The lecture quizzes are rather simple and straightforward, not tricky. (Consists of 3 to 4 MCQ and fill in the blanks). Do revise the lecture notes again if you get the quizzes wrong as this illustrates a weak foundation. Tutorials are held weekly and nearly all questions involve coding in Python. There is a platform called Jupyter Notebook that will be introduced in the course and do get a hands-on experience with it. Jupyter Notebook will be used for all assignments and tutorials, coupled with lectures that Prof will be carrying out. Group Project is to compare the performance of two trading strategies applied on different stocks. Strategies chosen must be from Chapter 4 of the book: Machine trading: deploying computer algorithms to conquer the markets by Chan, Ernest P, published by Wiley trading (2017). and cannot be directly used from the Lecture Notes. Implement the trading strategies in Python and you need to submit the codes, documentation featuring the processes and concepts behind the algorithms used, potential challenges and risks associated with the strategies and the future improvements made. Your group of 4 to 5 also needs to deliver a 8–10-minute presentation on the trading strategies used to the class for assessment. Computing Assignment: All questions are testing on Python code, was really a difficult one and I have to spend days doing it. The assignment is due 2 weeks from the release date, so don't worry as you will have ample time to complete it. Involves using Black-Scholes-Merton option pricing model but modified to carry out exotic price options using Jump Diffusion Models for one of the questions. Total of 5 questions. Midterms: Midterms was manageable and involves concepts from chapters 1 to 6 only. Comprises 20 marks MCQ, 15 marks short answer questions and 15 marks coding questions. Most of the midterm questions are very close to the lecture notes and tutorial questions content, so do prepare them well before the midterms to ace it. Finals: Finals was much harder than the midterms and involves chapter 7 till the end of the lecture content. Comprises 25 marks MCQ and 25 marks coding questions. Most questions unfortunately are not easy as they are novel type of questions and involve critical thinking, especially the coding questions. Best is to read up on trading strategies used other than that taught in the lectures! Both the midterms and the finals are open book. Once again, do revise on your python skills to ace this module. Without sufficient python knowledge, this module will be a challenge and you will need to spend hours getting the correct code for the assignments and tutorials like me. Quizzes: 5/5 (Almost everybody gets full marks here) Group Project: 85/100 Computing Assignment: 48/50 (Median is 47/50) Midterm: 40/50 (Median is 41/50) Finals: 30/50 (Stats not disclosed) Overall Grade: 73.35/100 Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: TBA

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY 2023/2024 Semester 2 Teacher: Prof Au Kin Chung, Al Assessment: 70% Tutorial Attandance + Participation 30% Reflection Piece * 3 Ratings: Workload: 2/10 Difficulty: 2/10 Enjoyability: 9/10 Overview: PLS8001 is covered among one of the five modules under the Roots And Wings Workshop, led by both the CFG and Psychology teams. This is a 1MC module and is graded on a CS/CU basis. In PLS8001, Cultivating Collaboration, there are three sessions, each lasting 3 hours, fortnightly. The weeks where you attend the lessons depend on the class that you have chosen on Modreg. In PLS8001 (or any other Roots And Wings) Module, you first need to attend all three sessions. Not attending any of the three lessons without a valid reason will give you an immediate CU grade. You need to inform the teacher in-charge if you cannot make it and you need to do a makeup lesson. Furthermore, active participation is a must. Attending the lessons without participating will affect your participation marks greatly, so do put in some effort and try to say something relevant to the concepts learnt in the module. Content: Concepts taught in PLS8001 include competition vs collaboration, givers, takers and matchers, reciprocity styles, strategies to become a successful giver, generating win-win situations, techniques of negotiation, biases in negotiations, rights vs interests, BATNA, keys for a successful team, psychological safety, social loafing and conflict resolution models. Group Discussions: In PLS8001, there are plenty of role-play activities and group discussions for interaction and communication with like minded peers. This is sort of graded and will be recorded in the teacher's attendance sheet. These activities are highly involved in what you will be learning for the particular session. I particularly like the Kukui Nut exercise as that really taught me that collaboration between two parties is key instead of each party focusing on their self interests. There will be reflections and sharing of thoughts at the end of the activities. Reflection Journals: At the end of each session, you are required to write/type a reflection journal of about 500 words or more (equivalent to about half a page) about two main insights that you have learnt from the session and how you can apply them in your daily lives. Giving lots of examples will be great! Simply copying the information from the slides posted is a no no, you need to think out of the box. Of course, no plagarism allowed! If caught, you will get a zero for the reflection and you cannot redo the assignment again. Overall, PLS8001 is an enjoyable module, especially the activities and role play exercises introduced in the sessions. It will be a bonus if you like to share your ideas across the table and like to interact with others and make new friends. Expected Grade: CS Actual Grade: CS
