author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY22/23 Semester 2 Lecturer: Dr P. Tutorial TA: Li Nianze Module Weightage: Tutorial Attendance: 20% 2 Reflections: 40% (20% each) Group Project (Video) : 40% Ratings and Reviews: Enjoyability: 2/10 Difficulty: 10/10 Workload: 1/10 Advice: Reserve an SU for this module. This might not apply for future semesters as the topics have changed recently based on the recent reviews. I have nothing much positive things to say about this module. The only thing I learnt from HSS1000 is "The Good, The Bad, The Uncertain" and "Freedom". To be frank, I sacrificed this module since week 4 to concentrate on other more difficult core modules like ST2131 and MA2104. The content taught in lectures are uninteresting and not to my liking. It teaches various contexts where social norms and decision making comes to play. Half of the time, I do not even know what Dr P is trying to say as she speaks like a bullet train, seriously. I left lectures and attended 3/5 of the tutorials. My TA will not give any attendance points unless I participate actively. My TA will ask the students who participated to write their names on a piece of paper to track but unfortunately, I didn't have any chance to participate at all. There are two reflections that you need to complete (each one is 200 words) about the good, the bad and the uncertain. The weightage of the reflections are insane as it is out of 2 points and if you score 1/2, you lose 10% of the final grade. That's insane. Group work and video comprises 40% of the final grade. We are supposed to choose any of the lesson content from weeks 2 to 12 and elaborate more about the good, bad and uncertain. My group didn't do the video as there was a fallout between two of the members. There was no group discussions afterwards. Overall, the lecture is boring and I do not like the style of the module for this semester. Other modules like DTK1234 are more interesting (varies according to individual). First Reflection: 1/2 Second Reflection: Not Revealed Attendance: 12/20 Group Project: 0 😵 Expected Grade: F Actual Grade: ** Inhales **

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in Semester 1 AY23/24 Lecturer: Prof Liu Mei Hui Tutorial Tutor: Neo Teng Whay Assessment Weightage: Online Quizzes (12%) Mini Tasks (8%) Tutorial Attendance (5%) Group Project (20%) Peer Evaluation (5%) Individual Essay (20%) Final Exam (30%) Module Ratings and Review: Enjoyability: 7.5/10 Difficulty: 7.5/10 (varies according to your interest/faculty) Workload: 8/10 Warning: This module will likely not be easy for you if you do not take biology in secondary school/JC. There are many technical terms that you need to learn which involves biology. If you are in FST/Life-sciences, this module will likely suit you well! However, I am not from these faculties but wanted to know more about food :) You will learn many interesting content about food such as the Maillard reaction, caramelisation, types of food additives/colourings/emulsifiers/additives/anti-foaming agents just to name a few. Other concepts such as vertical and urban farming, GMO food, CRISPR process and labelling of food items will be taught in the lectures as well. Lectures are conducted weekly. However, you only need to attend the lecture onsite once every fortnight as there is alternating online and onsite lectures. Similarly, tutorials are also held once every fortnight and are conducted in the same weeks as the onsite lectures. In the tutorials, the quiz and mini-task questions will be discussed and afterwards, there will be a case study for discussion. In Week 1, Prof Liu will release 4 different case studies. Each case study consists a few questions. You will be assigned to a group of 4 or 5 (no choice as Prof Liu will allocate randomly, most likely other members from various majors) and for one of the case studies, your group needs to discuss it and answer the questions before posting the solutions on canvas for the tutors to look through. One of the group members need to voice out the answers in tutorial (depends on what your tutor asks you to do). For the group work, your task is to find an interesting article/advertisement/magazine page/website article about something related to food. You need to construct three questions as a group and answer the questions as well. The best is to apply the relevant concepts learnt in the lectures and tutorials. For example, my group selected an online article about insect protein bars/cricket spaghetti. Referencing is still mandatory for your group project although the format of the references is flexible. Finals comprises 30MCQ questions and one essay worth 20 marks. Unfortunately, I didn't find the final exam easy and didn't know how to do a lot of questions. No cheatsheet is allowed as there is already one printed cheatsheet ready for the finals. However, the cheatsheet is of not much help. Many of the MCQ questions tested a lot on the different names of ingredients used and due to my poor memory, I couldn't remember some of them. The essay tested on GMO and I actually misinterpreted the question totally according to the remarks made by Prof Liu on canvas a few days after I sat for the exam. I felt that the question is not well set as based on the question, the approach is indeed testing on whether the particular food (tomatoes) is considered GMO or not. I enjoyed the content of the module as a whole, but the finals sort of messed everything up. The online quizzes are a practice for you as points will already be awarded if you complete them (whether your answer is right or wrong). The mini-tasks are ones that you really need to think of the concepts taught to solve application problems. You need to get the answers right for the mini-tasks. This is a module worth considering if you are into food or want to know more about food but be prepared to learn some jargon or technical terms along the way. Quizzes: Full Mini-tasks: Full Attendance: Full Group Project: 11.1/15 (B+) (Lower Quartile) Peer Evaluation: Full Individual Essay: Not Revealed Final Exam: Around 50% Expected Grade: B+ Actual Grade: B (Pending Results Review)

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in Semester 1 AY23/24 Lecturer: Prof Yap Von Bing Tutorial TA: Wang Zhong Grading Scheme: In-lecture Quizzes (23 of them) - 20% (count the best 80% of them) The Assignment (In R programming) - 20% Tutorial Attendance (attend at least 8 tutorials) - 10% Final Exam - 50% Topics Covered: 1. Probability Review I (Finding expectation and variance, discrete and continuous functions, variance and standard deviation, covariance, law of large numbers, Introduction to Monte Carlo approximation, fairness and independence, multinomial distributions) 2. Probability Review II (Conditional Expectations, Random Variables, Draws without replacement, CLT, chi-square, t and F distributions, multivariate normal distributions, gamma distributions) 3. Point Estimation (Binary Variables, SRS, Standard Error, Draws with replacement, Unbiased estimation, Gauss Model) 4. Bias, Standard Error and Mean Square Error relationships 5. Interval Estimation (three different types, some of which involve t-distributions and standard normal distributions) 6. Method of Moments (Parameter estimation, parameter space, estimating moments, Monte Carlo in MOM, Bootstrap method) 7. Maximum Likelihood Estimation (Likelihood functions, KL divergence, Monte Carlo and Bootstrap in MLE, Genetics) 8. Fisher Information (Asymptotic normality, Advanced Gamma distribution, partial derivatives, HWE trinomial vs general trinomial distributions, Cramer Rao inequality) 9. Hypothesis Testing (Size and power of test, alpha and beta values, simple vs composite hypothesis, confidence intervals, p-values, one-tailed vs two-tailed test) 10. Goodness-of-fit (Likelihood ratio test, calculation of L0 and L1, Nested Models, Multinomial LR test, test of independence) Ratings of Module: Difficulty: 9.5/10 Enjoyability: 8/10 Workload: 7.5/10 Review: Overall, ST2132 is an interesting yet challenging module. Do have a good revision on ST2131/ST2334 first before attending the ST2132 lectures as the first three weeks or so involves intensive ST2131/ST2334 revision or else you might get lost. Compared to other Professor's notes from previous batches, Prof Yap's notes are my favorite as they are concise yet targeted at the most important concepts in Mathematical Statistics. Furthermore, Prof Yap has certain slides which are incomplete that allows us to think about the process to get the final answer, improving our critical thinking skills. Prof Yap is patient, yet quite engaging. He even gave some interesting examples such as using a roulette from the DemoLab department. There are in-lecture quizzes (2 questions) every lecture (excluding the first and last lessons) to test your understanding of the concepts taught. All are MCQs with three/four/five options. If you try out the in-lecture quiz, you will get 8/10 already. The last two marks determines if your answer is correct/wrong. Tutorials are somehow a mad rush. There is really insufficient time to complete all the questions in every tutorial class as my TA will usually spend the first fifteen minutes to recap what was taught in the lectures. That is good as I tend to have a bad memory and refreshing what I have learnt a week ago will help in my understanding. Certain tutorial questions are extremely challenging as they involve rigorous proving. The assignment is done in R programming language. For this particular module, we are tested on Monte Carlo approximation for the Method of Moments on some interesting context that involves our matriculation numbers and certain values that are randomly assigned to. The assignment might be different for other semesters. We are supposed to simply paste our R code in a pdf file and submit it directly on canvas. Submitting the R code via R Markdown is also accepted. There is no midterms for this semester. The final exam comprises 12 MCQ questions and 3 structured questions. I was hoping that the last topic, Goodness-of-fit, won't come out for the exams as the formulas are just simply way too hard to remember. No Cheatsheet is allowed, and I am not able to remember some of the formulas. Unfortunately, I am just so unlucky as 16 marks out of 50 tested on Goodness-of-fit. Fortunately, certain components such as Fisher information and calculation of probabilities are manageable. The MCQ questions are really tricky and despite looking out for any traps in the questions, I still might have missed some. They are generally True/False questions.To my dismay, the three structured questions are all proving questions. Overall, ST2132 is an extremely challenging module that needs quite a lot of practice. Most of the topics are related to one another and if you are shaky in the first few topics, the subsequent chapters will get even more challenging. For instance, Fisher information is related to both MOM and MLE. Similarly, Bootstrap method and Monte Carlo approximation is used in many instances. Lots of proving are involved on this module with less emphasis on calculations. Try not to overload a semester if you take this module as this module is content heavy. Quizzes: 19.2/20 (after conversion) Attendance: Full The assignment: Not released Final Exam: Not released Expected Grade: B+ Actual Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message:
Taken in Semester 1 AY 23/24

Lecturers: Prof Yu Chien Siang
Course Coordinator: Prof Wong Tin Lok

Assessment Breakdowns: 

11 Quizzes (5 questions each) - 10% (count the best 10 )
Extra Quiz (Optional) 
Midterms - 20%
Team Paper - 20% 
Team Video - 10% 
Individual Brochure - 10% 
Final Exam - 30%

Ratings: 
Enjoyability: 7/10
Workload: 6/10 due to group project 
Difficulty: 4/10 

This is a pure online module, where all lectures will be held on Zoom (2 hour sessions from 7pm to 9pm approximately) by Prof Yu. There are six such sessions, once every two weeks. Prof Yu will talk about some AI theory with certain applications involved such as using various AI platforms including ChatGPT, Google Bard and Microsoft Bing Chat to gauge the responses from the AI. 

Topics: 
Week 1 - Introduction to AI and why care?
Week 2 - AI Capabilities (Language)
Week 3 - AI Capabilities (Vision) 
Week 4 - AI Sectors 
Week 5 - Applications of AI 
Week 6 - Technology used in AI 
Week 7 - Midterm Test 
Week 8 - AI in economics 
Week 9 - AI Ethics (Very important chapter!) 
Week 10 - AI in governance 
Week 11 - Conclusion and seeing the future 

Several weeks have no lectures and you only need to read the content posted on canvas and do the quiz afterwards. There is no tutorial for this module. Certain content can get quite abstract and information heavy, especially Week 6 (Technology) and you might need to read the content a few times to better understand what they mean. 

Most quizzes can be solved by looking through the content notes and the answers can be found. Some of them, unfortunately, are not related to the content and require some thinking to get the right answer. Average score for the quizzes is 3-4/5. 

Midterms: 40 MCQ questions, open-book, no backward navigation. You need to be careful when selecting the options as you cannot go back to change your answer. Topics include Weeks 1 to 6. Most questions are of reasonable standard and some are tricky. Majority of questions focused on the application of AI in various sectors such as Law, Zoos, Marketing, Finance etc. Some are easy as they test on definitions which are easily found in the lecture notes. Median grade is 29/40. 

Group Project: 
Team Paper (No limit on number of words, maximum 20+ pages excluding references, various font sizes and formatting you need to adhere to from the instructions provided. Many topics can be chosen from the instructions. ) My group focused on the ethics of AI in various industries such as military and policing, healthcare, education and the environment. 

Team Video (Related to the team Paper, but in a video format, maximum of 8 minutes, no limitation in speed of reading/talking) Similar to the content of the Team Paper. 

Individual Essay (Focus on the work that you contributed in the Team Paper, not your other group members work unless there is an obvious relation or link, maximum 20 slides in Microsoft PowerPoint excluding referencing. Design is important for this component as well such as readability and font size.) 

Final Exam: 60 MCQs, open-book, allowed backward navigation. Backward navigation is allowed as someone from the cohort insists that backward navigation be allowed which is a great move. Due to the backward navigation, I managed to get some answers right from wrong. Topics include all the weeks content. Median grade is 51/60. The questions are easier than the midterms, probably because the lecturers want more students from the cohort to do better. 

Overall, HS1501 is a doable module with some effort needed in reading the lecture notes. Great start for learning AI without coding background. If you want to have more coding in the module, you can opt for HS1502 or IT1244 instead which require basic programming knowledge, Python. 

Quizzes: 9/10 (after conversion) 
Midterms: 29/40 
Team Paper: 48/80 (way lower than expected, idk why) 
Team Video: 32/40 
Individual Brochure: 29/40 
Finals: Not revealed (approximately 48/60) 

Expected Grade: B 
Actual Grade: B

author: Damaen Tan
message: Taken in AY 23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: Prof Yip Hon Kay, John Lab Instructor: Dr Chui Sin Yin, Stephen Assessments: Test 1 (Theory) - 20% Test 2 (Theory) - 20% Two Lab Reports - 20% Final Exam - 40% Content Taught in this module: Part 1: 1. Introduction to the course (2 lessons :0) 2. Atomic Structure 3. The Periodic Table (trends and explanations of anomalies) 4. MO diagrams, extension from CM1102, including both homonuclear and heteronuclear molecules. 5. Redox Reactions and concepts (Latimer diagrams) 6. Chemical Bonding (Covalent, Ionic, Metallic, Van der Waals etc.) 7. Acid-Base Theory (Including Bronsted Lewry and Lewis) 8. A little bit of electrolysis Part 2: 9. Groups 1 to 18 elements more in-depth and their associated properties Review of Module: Enjoyability of Lecture: 3/10 Enjoyability of Lab: 9/10 Workload: 4/10 Difficulty: 8/10 Overall, I am quite disappointed with the module (except for the lab sessions). The content of the module is okay, but the way the lecturer teaches the module this semester is way below my expectations. Prof John is not enthusiastic in his lectures and does not answer student's questions after lectures. He left the lecture theatre immediately after lesson most of the time. His only mode of answering student's questions is through email/canvas, which is slow. Moreover, he teaches content that does not come out in the exams and tests quite a lot, resulting in inadequate time for more important concepts that are actually tested. Time management is poor too and he has only less than two weeks left to teach the whole of part 2 (usually need 4 to 5 weeks for this section). Test 1 focuses on pointers 1 to 4, excluding MO diagrams for heteronuclear molecules. Many explanatory questions are set and few calculation questions came out, resulting in a substantial loss of marks for students weak in explanatory (including me). Test 2 focuses on pointers 4 to 8. Similarly, many explanatory questions are set. Both tests are quite difficult and are really skewed towards explanations rather than calculations. There are two specimen papers for us to try and they are way easier than the actual tests. Prof John said in lecture that he was disappointed with the results of the tests and that he would set the finals easier. To my dismay, the finals is the absolute hardest of them all! No cheatsheat is allowed and all we have is a periodic table containing electronegativity values. All questions are explanatory except for a couple (1 on Latimer Diagrams and 1 on calculation of pkb). Many of the questions in the finals are not even related to the lecture content and we need to rely solely on pure intelligence to answer them correctly. I obviously do not have and just attempted all the questions as best as I can, but surely most are wrong answers. I really wish to su this module but it is not allowed. John's way of marking is extremely strict and harsh. There was one question which I explained correctly and calculated correctly too. My final answer for both explanation and calculation are correct. I just missed out on writing a half equation and I only got 2/6 for the entire question. Well, I didn't enjoy the theory part of the lecture to be truthful. For the practical, I enjoyed both sessions! However, one word of caution. Accuracy and yield of your final product is really important for the labs and there will be a substantial loss of marks if your accuracy is poor. First practical involves the production of Tin(IV) Iodide from tin powder and iodine crystals and the second practical involves the production of Copper(I) Chloride from Copper metal and Copper(II) Chloride solution. You will be exposed to many apparatus that you have not seen before in JC/Secondary school hence try to find them out before the lab sessions to improve your familiarity. You will be exposed to interesting apparatus such as a spectrophometer, magnetic wand etc. Each practical contains many steps (more than 20) hence you need to be really careful when following the instructions, else your yield will be greatly affected. Ask your lab assistant or Prof Chui if you need assistance and they are glad to help you out. There are two assignments that you need to take home to complete such as calculating your yield of the product obtained and providing some referencing for the observations of your product. Both assignments have a due date of one week. Caution: You need to buy your own lab coat for the lab sessions or you will be the laughing stock of the class. Since I have no friends who are taking chemistry, I faced this problem and I had no choice but to borrow one lab coat form the office which is too small but I have no other option. Goggles and gloves are provided. The bell-curve of the module is still quite high, especially the lab sessions. I would say as steep as CM1102, if not worse. I have a distinct disadvantage compared to the rest of the cohort as I am a chemistry minor and most of them are majors. Test 1: 68/100 (Median is 60/100) Test 2: 66/100 (Median is 67/100) Lab session 1: 74/100 (B+) (Worst in class 🤡) - Probably bad accuracy Lab session 2: 74/100 (B+) (Worst in class 🤡) - Probably bad accuracy Finals: Around 30 - 40% 🤡 Expected Grade: C+ 🤡 Actual Grade: B+ (Pleasantly Surprised)

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: Prof Yuting Huang Tutorial: Agrawal Naman Assessment Components: Tutorial Attendance - 5% Data Camp assignments (around 10) - 10% Group project (new) - 15% Midterm exam in Week 8 - 30% Final exam - 40% Content Taught In The Module: 1. Basics In R Programming (Revision of weeks 1 to 3 of DSA1101 content) 2. Importing Files and Data To R (csv, json, Rmd, data from the web, tidytuesday) 3. Tidyverse concepts - data manipulation (using R keywords such as filter, arrange, select, mutate, group by and summarize) - tidying data (using R keywords such as gather, spread, pivot_longer and pivot_wider, separate and unite) - joining data (using R keywords such as inner_join, full_join, left_join, right_join, semi_join and anti_join) 4. Principals of Visualization (how to create great graphs and plots with as little chart junk as possible) 5. ggplot concepts (R commands used to make plots, 4 layers of ggplot including faceting, layering, geoms and aesthetics. You will also learn which type of plot is the most suitable in different scenerios.) 6. Cleaning data in R (how to handle missing values, duplicates, inconsistencies in formatting etc.) Personal Opinion: Enjoyability: 10/10 Difficulty: 5/10 Usefulness: 9.5/10 Workload: 4/10 Personally, I find DSA2101 to be a really enjoyable module :) Though lectures this semester start at 8am, there are recorded lectures for you to watch. Prof Huang teaches this module well. Unlike some other lecturers, Prof Huang gets straight to the point and is really clear in delivering the lecture content. She ensures that everybody is following the lecture content as she frequently asks the class if they have any questions for her. She encourages the class to revisit the lecture content by asking us to follow her by running the codes from her slides into R script/R markdown in R studio which promotes active learning. Pace of the Lecture: Split equally into 3 sections, including Tutorials Weeks 1 to 4 - Learn Base R Weeks 5 to 8 - Learn Tidyverse Weeks 9 to 12 - Learn ggplot For Tutorials, I enjoyed them as well. I am really lucky to have Naman as my TA as he is really professional in the field of Data Analytics. He delivers the tutorial content well (although might be a little fast at times due to time constraint) and encourages active participation in the students by asking many questions to ensure everybody has a chance to practice what they have learnt in the lectures to tutorial questions. The mood is really lively in his tutorial lessons. Group Project: (New from this semester onwards) There are four options given to us and we are supposed to choose one of the four options. Our group chose the world cup dataset unanimously and we are supposed to perform certain tasks such as cleaning the data, creating three visualizations on any topic we like from the datasets we have and providing certain explanations why the trends occur. Total mark is 15. The group work will be due in about a month after the start day, which is plenty of time. Our group created a world map, a scatter plot and a line plot in different contexts. We enjoyed ourselves! Midterms: Midterms was on the difficult side due to the time constraint imposed. Midterms comprises two pure coding questions with multiple parts. Time limit is 1.5 hours. Since it is an 8am exam, I was still half asleep and had a mental block when doing the exam. I knew I most likely scored a single digit mark for the paper. However, I re-attempted the questions at home after the midterm exam and I managed to complete most parts correctly, which gives me extra practice for the final exam. Finals: Finals comprised around 20 MCQ questions and three coding questions (only 1 part for each question). Total mark is 40. Finals was surprisingly easier than midterms. The coding questions were manageable, and all three questions focused on ggplot and tidyverse concepts. The MCQs were okay as well, some questions were tricky though. Most focused on the theory of R programming with less coding. Overall, DSA2101 is one of my most favorite modules I have taken for the past three semesters, and I will highly recommend you take this module if you are allowed to bid! Midterms: 22.5 / 30 (Graded really leniently, median is 21/30) Group Project: 13/15 Attendance: Full Data camp: Full Finals: Approximately 34/40 Expected Grade: A- Actual Grade: A-

author: Damaen Tan
message:
Hi! From Prof Li's notes, these are the books that she uses: 

1. Investment Science by David G. Luenberger. Oxford University Press (Chapters 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 12)
2. Options, Futures and Other Derivatives by John C. Hull. Pearson (Chapters 11, 12, 13)
3. Bond markets, analysis, and strategies by Frank J. Fabozzi. Pearson (Chapters 2, 3, 4, 5)

Prof Li will compile the chapters from these books and she will upload the notes about a week before lecture starts (6 chapters in this course in total). She will use some content from these three books in her notes but it is not compulsory to buy/read through the books. Hope this helps!

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY23/24 Semester 1 
Lecturer: Prof Li Wei 
Tutor: Prof Li Wei (teaches all the tutorial classes as well) 

Weightage of Grades: 

25% Midterm Exam
10% In-Lecture Quiz 
65% Final Exam 
5% Bonus Attendance Marks: Can be added to the Midterm Exam and Quiz Grades, but NOT the Final Exam. 

Lecture Topics: 

(Recap from QF1100 module) 
1. Simple and Compound Interest 
2. Continuous Compounding 
3. Equivalent Interest Rates 
4. Present and Time Value 
5. Deferred Cash Flows 
6. Equation Of Value and Internal Rate Of Return 
7. Bonds Calculations and Terminology, including Perpetual bonds and Zero-coupon bonds.
8. Mean-Variance Analysis (Return and Risk of Asset, Correlation of Returns, Short Selling, Portfolios of Two Assets)
9. Options (Call Options and Put Options, Option Payoffs and Profits, Moneyness of Options, Options Trading Strategy, Arbitrage-Based Restrictions on Option Prices, Early Exercise and Put-Call Parity, Binomial Option Pricing)

(New topics not taught in QF1100) 
1. Rate Of Discount 
2. Inflation 
3. Commercial Paper and T-bills 
4. Proof of Macaulay and Modified Duration
5. Proof of Bond Duration
6. Convexity Of Bonds
7. Duration and Convexity of Bond Portfolio
8. Yield Curves and Term Structure of Interest Rates
9. Spot and Forward Rates
10. Expected Utility Theory (Expected Utility and Risk Attitude, Concave and Convex Utility Functions, Jensen's Inequality, Characterization of Risk Attitude, Positive A ne Transformations, Certainty Equivalent, Risk Premium, Arrow-Pratt Measures of Risk Aversion, Portfolio Selection).
11. Mean-Variance Analysis (Portfolio Mean and Variance, Portfolios of Three or More Assets, Feasible Sets, Minimum-Variance Set and Efficient Frontier).
12. Portfolio Theory & Capital Asset Pricing Model (Markowitz's Portfolio Theory, Method of Lagrange Multiplier, Two Fund Theorem, One-Fund Theorem, Sharpe Ratio, Portfolio Beta of Assets, Proof of CAPM, Security Market Line, Systematic and Non-systematic risk)

Overview Of Module: 
Difficulty of Module: 8/10 
Enjoyability of Module: 9/10 
Workload of Module: 9/10 

QF2104 this semester is purely taught by Prof Li, with no TA's teaching the tutorial classes. The first three to four weeks of the lecture are a revision of the content taught in QF1100, but at a faster pace since there are a lot of other content to cover. Do revise the QF1100 notes before taking this module to make yourself better prepared. 

Prof Li Wei is really clear in her explanations and teaching, highly recommended that you take the module under her! Similar to QF1100, she compiles her notes with lots of details and annotations to prevent any unclear concepts. She even gave some extra lessons in Weeks 12 and 13 to finish the content, all of which are recorded. Tutorials are also run by her, and she will usually explain the discussion problems. There are also several more basic problems in front to check your understanding of the QF concepts that were taught in lectures. 

Most difficult topics include the proving of portfolio theories, one-fund and two-fund theorem and using the method of Lagrange multipliers in Chapter 5 of the module. The rest of the content should be manageable. There will be a couple of lessons which involve rigorous proving of certain theorems which can get quite abstract. 

Midterms: Rather manageable but quite time tight. Tested on chapters 1 and 2 of the module (concepts taught in QF1100). Median grade is 35/50. 4 questions with multiple parts to be completed in 1 hour and 15 minutes. 

Quiz: 12 questions, 12 marks (1 mark per question). This quiz is marked based on whether your answer is right or not. No workings are needed. Focused on chapters 3 and 4 of the module (Expected Utility Theory and Portfolio Mean Variance Analysis). Median grade is 9/12. Time limit is also 1 hour 15 minutes.

Finals: Finals is on the difficult side, 100 marks and 4 questions with multiple parts. Chapters 3 to 6 are tested, including options. Q1 tested on options and binomial models and Q3 tested on expected utility in an application setting. These two questions are okay. Q2 tested on the calculation of portfolios of three assets and capital market line, portfolio beta. Certain parts are okay but some of them are challenging. Q4 is a pure proving question worth 20 marks, which I am completely stumped. I did not have enough time to complete Q4 of the finals. Luckily, no Lagrange multiplier concepts were tested at all. 

I did enjoy the module quite a lot under Prof Li as she explains finance concepts really well. All lectures are recorded but you need to attend tutorials onsite. Prof Li encourages participation and hence, the best is to attend both lectures and tutorials onsite as active participation will improve your confidence in this module. 

Midterms: 46/50
Quiz: 11/12 
Attendance: Full 
Finals: Approximately 65 to 70% 

Expected Grade: B+ 
Actual Grade: TBC

author: Damaen Tan
message: AY 22/23 Semester 1 Lecturer: Prof Dilip Raghavan Tutorial Teacher: Ivan Tan MATLAB teacher: ?? Assessments: 15% (12 quizzes, majority 10 MCQ questions) 20% (4 Homeworks, 5% each) 5% (MATLAB Assignment) 60% (Finals) Topics Covered In The Module: 1. Basic Operations Of Matrices 2. Row Echelon Form (REF) and Reduced Row Echelon Form (RREF) 3. Linear Independence and Invertibility of Matrices 4. Linear Spans and Subspaces 5. Adjoints (not so important) 6. Linear Combinations 7. Redundant Vectors and Applications 8. Bases and Dimensions 9. Coordinate Vectors 10. Transition Matrices 11. Row and Column Spaces Of Vectors 12. Ranks And Nullity 13. Orthogonal And Orthonormal Bases 14. Gram-Schmidt Process 15. Best Approximations (not so important) 16. Least Square Solutions 17. Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors 18. Cofactor Expansions (*) Difficulty Of Module: 8/10 Enjoyability Of Module: 8/10 This module is one of the most content-heavy modules I have taken in Year 1. Do be prepared if you are taking this module and I suggest to read through the lecture notes before coming for lectures, otherwise you might get lost as the lecture pace is rather fast. For some strange reason, this semester has only one 2-hour lecture a week unlike two 2-hour lectures in other years and semesters. This made the pace of the lecture twice as fast. Lectures are recorded and it is advisable to watch the lecture recordings instead to slow down the pace of the lecturer to understand concepts more clearly. There are also certain open web lectures on MA2001 on canvas where the pace of the lecture is slower. Tutorials: Conducted once a week, 1 hour. Ivan is really knowledgeable in linear algebra and encourages the class to participate actively. Unfortunately, he needs to conduct the tutorial classes a little fast due to time constraint. Ivan has certain consultation sessions for the students to ask some questions on the tutorials and assignments. MATLAB: Applying some coding/computing commands to perform certain functions in Linear Algebra such as addition of matrices and check for linear independence. There are four sessions of MATLAB for this semester. I was really disappointed with the quality of the teacher in charge of the MATLAB as she seems not to know anything. She left us to our own devices, and we just walk out of the venue when we finish the assignments. In the end, out of a class of 35 students, only 2 attended the last two MATLAB sessions (including myself). Homework Assignments: 4 homework assignments given out every 3 weeks, most questions are on the challenging side, some of which involve rigorous proving. Proving questions are the killer for the module as sometimes you do not know how to start. Bell curve is very high for the homework sets. Final Exam: 100 marks paper, 2 hours, closed book. Only double sided A4 sized cheat sheet available. 70% of the questions are proving questions, many of which I am not able to prove accurately. I tried my best to prove some of them but cannot finish a couple due to time constraint. The other 30% of the questions are computation questions, which are easier. Pro-Tip: Proving correctly is really important for the module. At least 50% of the finals and homework sets will involve proving for most semesters. My semester has more proving questions than expected. The lecturer told the class that if you focus only on computation questions but ignore proving questions, you will only barely pass the module (like B- or C+ grade). Overall, this module is quite interesting and fun to learn albeit challenging. Expected Grade: B (due to steep bell-curve) Actual Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY22/23 Semester 2

Lecturer: Professor Zhang Tengren 
TA: Li Zhi Xuan

Assessment Components: 

Midterm: 25%
Finals: 60%
3 Homeworks: 15% (5% each) 

Topics Covered: 

1) Vectors - cross and dot product, unit vectors
2) Volume of Parallelepiped (3D figures) 
3) Vectors - Line and Planes 
4) Limits - Proving if limit of multivariable functions exists and does not exist 
5) Differentiation - Implicit, chain rule, partial derivatives, Lagrange multipliers, directional derivatives
6) Applications of Differentiation - Area and Volume of regions, Types 1, 2, and 3 (for volume), optimization problems 
7) Integration - Integration over 2D and 3D domains, double and triple integrals (find area using double integrals and volume using triple integrals) 
8) Polar Form, Cylindrical Form, Spherical Form (in addition to cartesian form) 
9) Change Of Coordinates (for double and triple integrals) using Jacobian. 
10) Line Integrals - vector and scalar fields 
11) Surface Integrals - finding area of curved surfaces over a region or domain. 

Difficulty of Module: 9.5/10 
Enjoyability: 5/10

Personally, I regretted taking this module as a DSA major. This module involves a lot of visualization skills and spatial experience. As I am pretty weak in spatial, I find several chapters of this module very challenging and at times, I do not know what I am doing. 

The first 2 chapters on vectors are pretty much similar to 'A'-level vectors and hence, you need to be strong in 'A'-level vectors to master the first 2 chapters. Otherwise, you will struggle in the following chapters afterwards. The remaining chapters are an extension to MA2002 (Calculus) module, but the difficulty has increased many folds. 

Lectures: 

Professor Zhang gave several practices in his lecture notes for all the nine chapters while giving us some time to try them before he explained his solutions to the class. I find that the pace of the lectures is a little on the fast side, at the pace of 2 to 3 lessons per topic. Several topics require more time. Prof Zhang will upload the lecture notes a week before he starts the chapter so that we have some time to try out the exercises at home. 

Tutorials: 

Really fast and TA did not really go through the questions well. He sometimes makes mistakes in his tutorial solutions and some of us need to point out his mistake. 1 hour a week is definitely insufficient to go through all tutorial questions and as a result, some questions are left unanswered. Fortunately, the TA allows us to ask questions through email to get our questions verified. 

Important concepts needed to master the module: 

1) Proving if limit exists/not exist and justify using the correct method. 
2) Applying chain rule (multivariable) 
3) Using Lagrange multipliers to solve optimization problems
4) Applying polar, spherical and cylindrical coordinates (many questions in finals will test on that) 
5) Changing of coordinates (aka limit of the integrals) - usually for triple integrals
6) Calculation of vector fields 

Midterms: Difficulty 7/10
1.5 hours, closed book, allowed 1 double-sided help sheet. Tested on the first 4 chapters of the module till Lagrange multipliers. Difficulty is to the hard side, but not unreasonable. Time is very tight. First question is True/False which focuses on the conceptual understanding. 

Finals: Difficulty 9/10
2 hours, closed book, allowed 1 double-sided help sheet. Tested on the remaining chapters not tested in midterms. This is the killer as many questions involving triple integrals, changing of coordinates and surface integrals are tested. 

Homeworks: 
3 homework sets, 1 given out in week 4, 1 in week 8 and 1 in week 12. About 2 weeks to complete them. Questions are again on the hard side and some questions require heavy thinking. The homework questions are quite closely related to the tutorial questions, hence if you grasp the tutorial questions, the homework questions should be manageable. 

Overall, this a module that is very challenging and not to my liking. However, I still enjoyed the lectures and discussions on the whole and learnt some important concepts in calculus. I should have taken MA2311 instead which is an easier module based on the comments on Reddit. If you can take MA2311 for your graduation/core requirements, take that one instead. All the best for the rest who are taking this module and require this module for your graduation! 

Midterms: 33/50 (median grade)
Assessments: 22/25 + 21/25 + 30/30  
Finals: Not Reviewed 

Expected Grade: B- 
Final Grade: B

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY 22/23 Semester 1
Lecturer: Prof Loy
Teacher for tutorial: Edwina Jeremiah 

Grading Scheme:

Reflective Essays (choose 2 out of 4 questions) - 15% 
Class Participation - 15% 
Quizzes - 25% (best 5 of 6 quizzes) 
Surveys & Peer Review - 5% 
Final Quiz - 10% 
Meme Competition - 5%
Group Discussion (GDS) - 25% (5 of them) 

Readings for HSH1000: In order

1) The one who walked away from the Omelas
2) Gilgamesh 
3) Hind Swaraj 
4) Xunzi 
5) No Exit 
6) A Number 
7) Sexual Politics Of Meat 

Enjoyability of module: 6 / 10 

Lectures are carried out weekly online through zoom. Slides will be uploaded a few days before the lecture starts. You are expected to read the lecture readings above before the lectures and tutorials. This is because in the tutorials, you are expected to know the reading content in order to participate actively. Participation points are obtained when you discuss with your groups and tutorial teacher. Lectures will go in depth about the reading and teach you certain life lessons and morals for each reading. 

Opinion of the readings:  

Personally, I find the Omelas, A Number and the Sexual Politics of Meat decent as the readings/poems are of acceptable length and we are given sufficient time to read through. However, some readings such as Hind Swawaj (117 pages :0) and No Exit are long and dry and I at times doze off while reading through the story halfway. Oops! Xunzi can be interesting to some people, but it serves no use for me since I have no Chinese background (exempted from Chinese) since primary school. 

Quizzes: 

The quizzes are generally of reasonable standard with several tricky questions for certain readings. However, we need to understand the text well in order to answer the questions. Group discussion of the quizzes is allowed. Only the final quiz is to be done individually and no group discussion is allowed. Final quiz is much more difficult and certain questions require in-depth thinking. All the questions are multiple choice questions. 

Group Discussions:

5 Group discussions, each GDS focusing on 1 topic. No GDS for A Number and Sexual Politics Of Meat due to a lack of time, coupled with the fact that there are only 6 tutorial sessions, 1 of which is an introduction. Our group is expected to come up with a short essay (450 to 550 words) on the opinion of the question that Ms Edwina gives. Every group has different questions. Fortunately, my group came up with lots of interesting ideas and our teamwork is strong. We dedicate quite a bit of time to effectively come up with essays that demonstrate the concepts well. 

Surveys: 

Given out throughout the semester, just do it and you can earn 1% of your total grade for free. However, almost everybody did it as well but free points is great. Peer-review is also provided to evaluate the performance of your peers during tutorial lessons and GDS group discussions. 

Overall, this module is decent except for several readings that are too dry and long. Last but not least, the bell-curve. The bell curve is not so steep fortunately as some decide to put in less effort to SU this module. There are 2 or 3 students in my tutorial class (not my group) that are slack and attend the tutorial lessons at times. ( maybe because the tutorial is at 
8am) . 

Expected Grade: B
Actual Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY 22/23 Special Term I

Teacher: Ms. Deborah Choo Ann (Ms. Debbie) 

Grading Components of the module: 

CA1 (synthesis partial essay): 20%
CA2 (introduction + situation + problem before peer review): 10%
CA3 (introduction + situation + problem after peer review): 20% 
CA4 (solution + evaluation + conclusion): 20%
CA5 (final essay - combination of CA3 and CA4): 10%
CA6 (text analysis PowerPoint slides with audio recording): 10%
Participation (in class): 10%

This module is taken if you obtain a grade of 'D' or lower in the 'A'-level General Paper and if you get 'Band 2' for QET. If you obtain 'Band 1' for QET, you are required to take ES1000 first before taking this module. 

This module focuses on the academic rigor of writing essays. 
For the first week of the module, after the introduction, there will be four linguistic toolkits that are really important for the module. 

1) Textual Cohesion
2) Logical Links
3) Noun Groups
4) Evaluation

From week 2 onwards, you will learn more in depth about the four linguistic toolkits and what are the components involve. You will learn conjunctions, lexical chains, noun groups, pre and post modifiers, nominalization, hedging and modality etc. and you are supposed to use all these concepts taught in class to write your essays. As I did this module in the special term, pace of the lecture was really fast and in the blink of an eye, the module lessons are over. (3 hours of lecture, twice a week for special term). 

Deadlines for the CA assignments are rather tight (especially for special term) so be prepared to complete them as fast as you can (of course put in your effort!) and not procrastinate. Heard from my teacher that not completing an assignment on time or not turning up for lecture (even once) could affect your final grade greatly due to the steep bell-curve. 

In this module, language is more important than content in terms of grading. You are required to use the toolkits actively throughout your essay. Organization is also key for this module as the teachers will check the flow of your essay in-depth, checking for any logical gaps or errors in your essay. For my teacher, she gives one-to-one consultation on my CA3 and CA4 and she will go through my areas of improvement in my essays for half-an-hour. I am not sure if other teachers will provide one-to-one consultations with you. 

Knowing the APA format is important for the module. You are required to cite your sources properly using the APA format. You can either do it manually (taught in lecture) OR use Zotero. Things to look out for include the font size and spacing of the references, the indentations, which part of the reference to use italics and the presentation of the references. You are not required to use APA referencing in CA1 to CA4. 

For the text analysis presentation, you are required to create PowerPoint slides that showcase what you have learnt in class and in the module using a source (preferably a journal or an article) obtained from the internet. This includes whether the source has done APA format referencing. If not, explain why APA referencing is not used. Also, you need to identify the four linguistic toolkits in the source that you have chosen and showcase them in your slides. There will be sample text analysis presentations that you can reference to for better understanding of the text analysis presentation organization. 

Overall, this is a doable module. Effort really counts so put in effort to write your essays using what you have learnt in class and you can score decently for the module. 

Expected Grade: B+ 
Actual Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY22/23 Semester 2
Teacher: Prof Pham Kim Cuc
You can call her Daisy for short and simplicity. 

Assessment:

5% Tutorial Participation
5% Assignments on Data camp
30% Midterms
60% Finals

Enjoyability of Module: 7 / 10

This module teaches you the basics of R programming language. Not only basics, but also several machine learning methods that involve R code.

Topics covered include:

1. Basic R syntax
2. Functions and Conditional Statements (for, while loops)
3. Linear Regression and Multiple Linear Regression
4. Decision Trees
5. k nearest Neighbours
6. Naive Bayes
7. K means
8. Logistic regression
9. Association Rules

Topics 3 to 8 are all machine learning concepts that will be further taught in CS3244.

In previous semesters. the exams in DSA1101 are all theory-based and involves the computation of certain machine learning concepts, such as getting the RSE for linear regression.

However, for this semester, Professor Daisy is teaching this module for the first time (guinea pig semester) and she decided to change the format of the exam to pure R code instead. She feels that the bell-curve is too high for the module where almost everybody receives very high grades, thus, unable to differentiate the cohort properly. I was cringing as my programming skills are really bad :(

Fortunately, Professor Daisy was quite clear in her explanations and was rather approachable. She taught at a pace of 2 lectures per topic, fortunately not very fast. There are 3 hours of lecture and 1 hour of tutorial per week for this module.

Categorizing data is really important for this module as many of the machine learning topics require that and if you cannot categorize them correctly or forget to change them to factors, your code will be incorrect throughout the question. Data camp gives an extra boost in learning of concepts for this module. "Introduction to R" will be a great start!

The exams are open book for both the midterms and finals. Maybe this is because this type of exams is held for the first time and Prof Daisy gives us chances. Furthermore, it is challenging to remember all the Machine learning algorithms altogether and many of us need constant referencing from the notes to attempt the questions.

Midterms: 30/80 (messed up, median was 41/80)
Data camp: Full
Attendance: Full
Finals: Approximately 75/100 but not released.

My midterms messed up because I did not change one of the variables to factors and end up losing more than 10 marks. I was not so comfortable with the algorithms at that time yet since midterms was tested so quickly and we had very little time to revise. My other components pulled me up in some way. Finals was easier than midterms surprisingly as they keep repeating on the same concepts such as AUC and ROC curves.

Expected Grade: B- due to midterms.
Final Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY22/23 Semester 2 

Tutorial Teacher: Benjamin Goh 

Assessment: 

15% Participation 
20% Group Poster On Fieldwork 
30% + 20% Two individual essays, 1 in week 6, 1 in week 13. 
15% Group Presentation on 1 of the 4 Topics. 

4 Topics Covered: 
1. Migration and Diaspora 
2. Race and Ethnicity
3. Religion and social change
4. Social Inequalities. 

( 2 weeks of lecture and 1 tutorial for each topic) 

Enjoyability of lecture: 4 / 10 
Lectures are rather dry and a little boring at times. Lectures are conducted online on zoom and hence lack the interaction. Questions from students are answered at the end of the lesson. 

Enjoyability of tutorial: 9 / 10
Tutorials are fun and interesting. Teacher Benjamin spoke clearly and concepts are taught in an engaging way.  One group will present each tutorial session. Topic chosen for the group is based on chance. 
Presentation lasts for 30 minutes and question and answer which the groups must craft themelves to ask the class lasts for 15 minutes.  

Field Trip: Visit anywhere in Singapore,  except Nicoll Highway,  where you can observe sites and landmarks associated to any of the 4 topics Covered. Most popular is the city area close to NUS where many buildings are associated with religion. Several ambitious groups chose locations far away as far as Pulau Ubin, Punggol and Sembawang for the project. There will be a vote for the best posters. 2 rounds, 1 within the class and 1 more for the whole cohort. Winners will get an additional 2% bonus points. 

Posters are related to the field trip and are judged not only on content, but the layout of the poster. Design expertise is important too! Poster is of A2 size and citations are necessary in the APA format. 

For the individual essays, you are supposed to write 750 words (+- 10%) on Migration and Diaspora (for the first essay where only topic 1 is taught). The second individual assignment is about the field trip and how your field trip is related to any of the 4 topics covered in lectures. In-depth explanation and links are required. 

Bell Curve for the module? Extremely High. Participation is the key factor. Individual assignments are graded where many students score around the same unless you show no effort. Same thing for posters, except for the winners. Answer questions from the teacher/TA and you will get extra points for Participation. Attendance is insufficient to get points for this module. 

Individual Essay:  73/100 (Median is 73 as well) 
Field-Based Project Essay: 72/100 (Median is not revealed, but most scored between 70-75 based on the Telegram poll) 

Expected Grade: A-
Actual Grade: A-

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY22/23 Semester 2
Tutorial Teacher: Joanne Lim 

Assessment: 

5 ILAs (each one is 6%, total 30%) 
5 TBWs (each one is 6%, total 30%) 
Interim DTJ - 15%
Final DTJ - 25% 

4 Topics Covered in this module: 
1. Creativity 
2. Empathize
3. Prototype
4. Evaluating with people

Creativity -> Generating multiple ideas (including problems with the design, which is known as a Norman Door, reasons for the problems and think of solutions to tackle the problem). 

Empathize -> Consider the needs of other users, especially those who are in the minority groups, consider in terms of age, ethnicity, background of the user, height etc. Place them into consideration when developing the ideas. 

Prototype -> Building Low-fidelity Prototypes such as cardboard prototyping, paper prototyping etc. and you will get a chance to create a low-fidelity prototype with your pair mate in one of the TBW's in class. 

Evaluating with people -> Conducting interviews, how to ask the right questions when conducting interviews, types of evaluation, learn about usability test, desirability tests etc. 

Enjoyability: 10 / 10
All four chapters have pre-recorded videos that you should watch beforehand, otherwise you might get lost in the ILA'S. The videos are interesting and comprehensive, and you will learn a lot from these videos. The TBW's are fun and engaging! Although the ILA's require quite a bit of work, they reinforce the flow of the design thinking process (4 steps) clearly. This module is basically a hands-on experience on the design thinking process, both the practical and theory forms. 

Effort really counts in this module and you will not score well for the ILA's if you leave certain questions blank or unanswered. Do your best and I am sure you will do well for this module! This module serves as a strong foundation for those who wish to pursue more advanced design courses in the future like UX design. 

Expected Grade: A+/A
Final Grade: A

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY22/23 Semester 2

Lecturers: Dr Chong Yuan Yi (Environment), Prof Chng Shu Sin (Life), Prof Chin Wee Shong (Materials) 

This module is split into 3 categories, Physical, Organic and Inorganic Chemistry. Each category takes 4-5 weeks. 

Overall enjoyment of module: 8/10 
(My personal favorite was the first section on environment) 

Assessment of the 3 categories: 

Environment: 
1. Types of bonding (Ionic, covalent etc. )
2. Hybridization 
3. MO diagram (this is the killer) 
4. Orbital Theory and Orbital Identification 
5. Orbital Shapes
6. Physics 'A'-level concepts - Wave-particle duality, electromagnetic spectrum etc. 

Life: 
1. Mechanisms (many) 
2. Resonance Effects
3. Acidity and Basicity 
4. Newman Projections
5. Keto-enol tautomerization

Materials: 
1. Energetics (Hess Law) 
2. Kinetics (rate equations, half-life) 
3. Activity (formula and applications) 
4. Electrochemistry
5. Crystal Structures (FCC especially) 
6. Micelle Formation
7. Consumer Products 

Personally, I find the environment section my favorite as the lecturers are really helpful and engaging. There was a lot of participation which helps me widen my knowledge about the bonding section of the chemistry module. Life section was also great although the difficulty of this section is very high. There are certain concepts such as enol-keto tautomerization that are still really hard to grasp, especially when you are required to draw the mechanism of the reaction. Materials section of the course was not really well-run. The lecturer tends to go a little on the fast side and keeps track of time. On the whole, the concepts are still taught decently but I need to revise the life and materials section once more after lecture to comprehend the concepts better. 

Tests: 

Environment - Open Book On Examplify (20%) 
Life - Closed Book, written (basically mechanism drawing and explanation on acidity/basicity) (20%)
Materials - Closed Book On Examplify (20%) 
There are certain questions on materials exam that requires one to get the question totally correct. Otherwise, no partial marks are given and one gets an automatic zero for the question. The lecturer said that those questions are the differentiating questions :( 
Participation - 16%
Assignments - 22%
Peer wise Group Activity - 2%

Peer wise activity was interesting as we are tasked to set our own questions (like a teacher) and this teaches us to set really well-crafted questions. We can try each other's questions and get points on the peer wise platform. This contributes to the 2% of the module grades. I get to learn from other's questions and when I make mistakes, I can learn from my peers on the explanations of the solutions. This is a great learning platform and I recommend that Peer Wise should stay. This will also be a great learning opportunity for you if you take this module. 

This is on the whole a great module for learning and if you are into chemistry. The only bad thing about this module is the bell-curve. The bell-curve is really high for the module, especially the assignments. 

Expected Grade: A- 
Actual Grade: B+

author: Damaen Tan
message:
AY22/23 Semester 2

Lecturer: Professor Chan Yiu Man

Assessment: 

20% Midterms
20% Online Quizzes (4 quizzes, 5% each) 
60% Final Exam

Topics Covered: 
1. Permutations and Combinations
2. Probability 
3. Bayes Theorem
4. Expectation and Variance
5. Discrete Random Variables
6. Continuos Random Variables
7. Joint Distributions
8. Other types of Distributions (Beta, Gamma, Weibull, Cauchy)

Difficulty of the module: 6/10 

First 3 chapters on permutations and combinations and probability till Bayes theorem were manageable. The difficulty increases progressively increases with the continuation of each chapter. Hardest was joint distributions and properties of expectation. A lot of effort is needed to master these two topics. 

Lecture notes are decent with many examples of the various types of Distributions. Application of the Distributions are on the difficult side. Fortunately, the level of difficulty for the tutorials and exams is not as high. 

Enjoyment : 7/10

Prof Chan explains concepts really clearly and to the point. I liked his teaching style and even gave subtle hints as to which questions will come out for the exam. However, you need to be really observant. If you attend his lectures regularly and internalise his lectures,  you will do decently well for the module. 

Friendly and approachable, answer students questions after lectures. 
Midterms was easy but bell curve is really high as well. Median mark is 28/30. 
Finals was the killer. There are 6 questions for the finals. The first 3 questions,  which test on the first 4 chapters, are manageable and can be answered with practice. Last 3 questions were extremely challenging and I couldn't answer most of the questions. All chapters were tested in the finals except for Limit Theorems. 

Midterms: 29/30
Quizzes : 18/20
