author: Naman Agrawal
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecturers: Prof. Lorenz Goette & Prof. Xiaoyue Shan

Assessments 
Final Exam - 50%
Online Quizzes (5 in total) - 15%
Problem Sets (3 group-based sets) - 20%
Attendance and Presentation of Problem Sets - 15%

Content Overview:
This course basically teaches how behavioral insights challenge standard economic assumptions along mostly empirical and some theoretical dimensions. It’s structured around four five areas where behavioral economics has made major contributions:
1. Present-biased preferences: Examines how individuals often display short-term impatience but long-run patience, leading to time-inconsistent decisions. This ties into self-control problems where people might plan one thing for the future but change their decision as the moment arrives (e.g., planning to save but ending up spending).
2. Beliefs and discrimination: Covers how beliefs—especially biased, stereotypical, or miscalibrated ones—influence decision-making. There are some really fascinating papers on reversals of discrimination and spillover effects of discriminatory behavior, and how these beliefs affect both individual outcomes and broader societal dynamics.
3. Reference-dependent preferences: Explores how people’s utility depends not just on outcomes but also on comparisons to reference points like past experiences, peer outcomes, or expectations. Includes theories and empirical findings related to endowment effects, loss aversion, and goal setting.
4. Limited attention and salience bias: Looks at how people sometimes focus only on the most noticeable aspects of a decision while neglecting less visible details.
5. Social preferences: Introduces non-selfish motives such as inequality aversion, reciprocity, altruism, and fairness. The course discusses both the positive aspects of these preferences and some more complex or darker motivations, such as superiority-seeking behaviors, drawing on recent empirical research to highlight these dynamics in real-world data.

Personal Reflection:
Very fun course with lots of cool and unconventional ideas. It challenges the traditional economic models we learn in other modules and shows that the real world is much messier. I really enjoyed the discussion heavy format, exposure to behavioral experiments, and seeing how some of the theories translates into policy. Some of my favorite parts were on reference-dependent preferences and the calibration theorem—super interesting and intuitive. Both professors are very nice, approachable, and true experts in the field. It was especially cool to study papers they’ve written themselves!

Enjoyability: 10/10
Usefulness: 9/10
Workload: 2/10

If you’ve any questions, feel free to message me on Telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message:
AY24/25 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof. Feng Ying

Assessments:
Prior Knowledge Quiz – 5%
Homework 1 - 5% (effort-based)
Homework 2 - 5% (effort-based)
In-class Midterm Exam - 30%
Final Presentation in pairs (Week 12, based on a research paper) - 25%
Face-to-face Final Exam - 30%

Content Overview:
The course introduces the major theories in modern macro, starting with foundational models like the Solow Growth Model, Ramsey model, and overlapping generations (OLG) models. It covers development accounting and long-run growth facts, with some fascinating papers and real-world ideas on how different countries’ growth patterns can be explained, along with  some simple empirical exercises. The course then moves on to more advanced concepts like endogenous growth theories, Romer’s model, and the Real Business Cycle (RBC) theory. The last part of the course also explores some basic search models for unemployment. The main reference is Romer’s Advanced Macroeconomics, though lecture notes are the core material.

Personal Reflection:
Very fun course with lots of ideas and engaging discussions. Prof is excellent at explaining concepts and building intuition for models that may seem complex . I especially enjoyed the parts on endogenous growth theory and the final project gave a great chance to explore academic papers in macro. Reading the textbook helped deepen my understanding, especially on technical details. While there’s some math involved, it’s quite manageable if you understand the economic reasoning. Recommended for anyone keen on macro theory or research.

Enjoyability: 10/10
Usefulness: 9/10
Workload: 4/10

If you’ve any questions, feel free to message me on Telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY24/25 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Sam Jindani Assessments: Tutorials: 20% Midterm Exam: 30% Final Exam: 50% Content Overview: 1. Introduction to Game Theory: Strategic interactions and mathematical prerequisites; Prisoner’s Dilemma, normal-form games, and dominant strategies. 2. Nash Equilibrium and Mixing: Best responses and expectations; Formal definition of Nash equilibrium; Mixed strategies and expected payoffs; Equilibria as good predictions (e.g., Hawk–Dove game, matching pennies, congestion games). 3. Dominance and Duopoly: Iterated deletion of strictly dominated strategies; Cournot model, collusive outcomes, and the differentiated Bertrand model. 4. Dynamic Games of Complete Information: Entry games and credibility; Backward induction, centipede game, modelling dynamic games; Extensive-form games, subgame-perfect equilibrium, Stackelberg competition. 5. Information Sets: Extensive-form games with information sets (e.g., entry game with inventory). 6. Repeated Games: T-period Cournot games and prisoner’s dilemma; Infinitely-repeated games, one-shot deviation principle; Strategies like tit-for-tat, forgiving trigger, and grim trigger; Conditions for cooperation. 7. Bayesian Games: Bayesian Nash equilibrium with examples (e.g., Bayesian Hawk–Dove, first-price sealed-bid auction, heterogeneous Cournot, double auction). 8. Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE): Strategies in extensive-form games with information sets; Beliefs and PBE (e.g., market for lemons, Selten’s horse); Challenges with PBE. 9. Signalling Games: Pooling and separating equilibrium (e.g., challenging an incumbent, job-market signalling by Spence 1973). 10. Screening Games and Principal-Agent Problems 11. The Pivot Mechanism and Cheap Talk: VCG Mechanism and Clarke Tax; Cheap Talk Game (e.g., full-disclosure equilibrium, babbling equilibrium, partial-disclosure equilibrium). This was one of the most enjoyable courses I’ve ever taken. Prof Sam Jindani is an exceptional lecturer, always willing to help and answer questions after class. While the module doesn’t require advanced mathematics, it emphasizes logical thinking and a solid foundation in basic math and probability. This often requires significant effort as there are many different settings and games. Identifying the key models and assumptions is critical and takes practice. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 10/10 Workload: 9/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).
thread_title: EC3312 Game Theory & Applications to Economics

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY24/25 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Zhang Jin-Ting Assessments: Class & Tutorials: 15% Midterm Exam: 25% Final Exam: 60% Content Overview: 1. Review of Basic Probability and Statistics Terminology: Recap of key discrete and continuous distributions and their properties. 2. Random Numbers: Pseudo-random number generation using congruential methods, Monte Carlo integration, and transformations. 3. Generating Random Variates with Non-Uniform Distributions: Techniques like the inversion method, rejection method, composition method, polar method for generating normal random variables, and sampling from multivariate distributions. 4. Variance Reduction Techniques: Methods such as simple sampling, stratified sampling, importance sampling, control variates, and antithetic variates, including variance estimation. 5. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC): Basics of discrete Markov chains, including types and properties (recurrent states, periodicity, ergodicity, stationary states); Algorithms like Metropolis-Hastings, Gibbs sampler, and simulated annealing; Applications in Bayesian statistics. 6. Statistical Analysis of Simulation Outputs: Techniques for confidence intervals via batch means, covariance estimation (window and regenerative methods), and handling asymptotically stationary output. 7. Introduction to Bootstrap Methods: Fundamental theory, bootstrap probability space, and its application for estimating mean squared error (MSE). Tutorials: Weekly tutorials were straightforward, required submission before lectures, and were easy to score. Exams: Both midterm and final exams focused more on theoretical understanding than calculations. It included things like presenting algorithms to generate samples from specific distributions, Proposing target Markov chains to solve optimization problems (e.g., knapsack problem), Estimating variances for specific sampling techniques. TL;DR I found the ideas incredibly useful for many of my projects. There is also a lot of overlap with other courses like stochastic processes and simulations, but overall a good amount of content was covered. The use of R for demonstrations added a nice practical depth to the theoretical content. Enjoyability: 8/10 Usefulness: 8/10 Workload: 2/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY24/25 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Ta-Cheng Huang Assessments: Participation: 10% Homework Assignments: 10% (HM had extra 25% weightage here) Midterm Exam: 25% Final Exam: 35% Group Project: 20% Textbooks: There were no required textbooks, but the following were recommended: 1. FI Martellini et al., “Fixed-Income Securities,” John Wiley & Sons, 2003. 2. FE Fabozzi et al., “Financial Economics,” John Wiley & Sons, 2012. 3. OPT Hull, “Options, Futures, And Other Derivatives,” 8th edition, Pearson, 2012. Content Overview: 1. Introduction to Financial Economics: Basics, largely a recap from EC3333. 2. Fixed-Income Markets, Bond Prices, and Yields: Topics included continuous compounding, coupon rates, spot rates, forward rates, YTM, current yield, and par yield. 3. Term Structure and Interest Rates: Analysis of yield curves and their dynamics using PCA, factors influencing yield curves, and classical term structure theories like Pure Expectation and Risk Premium. 4. Deriving the Zero-Coupon Yield Curve: Explored theoretical methods and practical approaches like bootstrapping (Fama-Bliss) and fitting models like Nelson-Siegel and Nelson-Siegel-Svensson. 5. Hedging Interest-Rate Risk: Covered reinvestment and capital gain risks, bond duration (dollar, modified, and Macaulay), convexity, and general hedging approaches using 2nd-order Taylor expansions and Nelson-Siegel. 6. Decision-Making under Risk: Topics included Expected Utility Theory (also in EC3101), Risk Aversion (Arrow-Pratt coefficient), Certainty Equivalent Wealth, and Stochastic Dominance (1st and 2nd order). 7. Mean-Variance Portfolio Choice: Covered portfolio risk, diversification, and optimal portfolio derivation, revisiting EC3333 concepts more formally. 8. Introduction to Continuous-Time Finance: Introduced Wiener processes, Ito’s Lemma, Markov stochastic processes, and the Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation with its derivation under risk-neutral valuation. The content was well-structured and organized weekly, making it easy to follow. The use of R/Stata and Excel for demonstrations—like PCA for term structure dynamics or fitting Nelson-Siegel models—enhanced the practical understanding of theoretical concepts. Project: The group project required selecting a topic of choice (either data-based or a literature review). Groups delivered a 20-minute presentation and submitted a written report. Many projects focused on yield curve dynamics, such as fitting Nelson-Siegel models under varying conditions or replicating results from relevant research papers. Exams: Both the midterm and final exams leaned more toward theoretical and applied questions rather than extensive calculations. The exams required applying knowledge to discuss recent economic events, such as analyzing yield curve shapes, deriving simple proofs, or expressing key relationships. While some calculations were present, they were minimal and focused more on conceptual understanding than computation. TL;DR This module is highly engaging! While the lectures are easy to follow, they require some mathematical maturity. Prof was very patient and addressed numerous questions after lectures. Unlike other modules that skip proofs of more technical results, Prof delved into details—like Ito’s Lemma and stochastic dominance proofs—making the content more intuitive and comprehensible. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 9/10 Workload: 4/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Prof Sun Baoluo Assessments: Project: 10% Midterms: 30% Final Exam: 60% Textbooks: An Introduction to Statistical Learning with Applications in R by James et al. 2nd Edition 2021, (Ch 01-06, 12) The Elements of Statistical Learning by Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman. 2nd Edition. (Reference) (Lots of addition material, for ref only) Content Overview: In the initial weeks, we revisited linear regression from ST3131 and ST3248, exploring various extensions such as polynomial regression, piecewise regression with basis functions, splines, local regression, smoothing splines, and generalized linear regression models. Subsequent weeks delved into tree-based methods like decision trees, bagging (e.g., random forests), and boosting classifiers. Discussions focused on theoretical aspects such as different hyperparameters and algorithms like greedy search and cost complexity pruning. The module then shifted its focus to Support Vector Machines, covering linear separators, maximal margin classifiers, support vector classifiers, the kernel trick, and multi-class extensions. After the recess week, attention turned to kernel regression (Nadaraya–Watson kernel) and density estimation, including Rosenblatt Parzen Window and extensions, as well as classification algorithms like Naive Bayes. Subsequentlu, the majority of the module content centered on Deep Learning, discussing concepts such as perceptrons, ANNs, RNNs, LSTMs, and CNNs, with some mathematical formulation. This part should be straightforward for those with basic ML background from courses like CS3244/IT1244. Additional topics included auto-regressive models for time series forecasting, pre-trained DL models, and various DL applications. The module concluded with a look into common ensembling techniques, revisiting bootstrapping (parametric, non-parametric, semi-parametric), stacking, and boosting (including discussion on AdaBoost). Overall, the content was well-organized throughout the weeks. The use of R to demonstrate different algorithms/techniques enhanced the practical understanding compared to just abstract high-level discussions. Examinations: The midterm exam was conducted in a take-home manner, making it almost similar to a graded assignment. Most of the questions were R-coding based. In contrast, the final exam was theoretical (a mix of MRQ and open ended questions), with many questions requiring you to derive specific results. Project (Individual): The project required us to apply our knowledge from ST3248/4248 to tackle a statistical learning problem. While a list of topics was provided, it was up to us to select our datasets, define problem statements, determine approaches, and choose models, making it highly open-ended. TLDR This module is enjoyable! Lectures are generally easy to follow, and the workload is lighter compared to many other stats modules. Prof. Sun Baoluo is patient and is open to further discussion after lectures. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 9/10 Workload: 2/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Prof Ng Teck Khim Assessments: Tutorial: 20% Project: 30% Final Exam: 50% The module provides an excellent introduction to media computing, standing out as one of the few courses that go into the mechanics of how to handle/process audio and image data. Content Overview: 1. Concept of basis functions: Review of Basic Linear Algebra, Sine and Cosine Basis for Audio Signals 2. Analysis of audio signals in the frequency domain: Understanding fundamental frequencies, Fourier Series, and Transformation 3. Types of Fourier Transforms: Continuous-time continuous frequency, discrete-time continuous frequency, and discrete-time discrete frequency, along with their inverse counterparts 4. Properties of Fourier Transform and common results (impulse functions, rectangular functions, etc) 5. 1D Convolution: Linear and Circular convolution on 1D data using FFT 6. Sampling: Frequency Resolution and Nyquist Rate for Sampling 7. Analysis of images in the frequency domain and 2D convolutions 8. Image coding formats (JPEG, GIF, etc.): Discrete Cosine Transform, lossless techniques (Huffman Coding, Run Length Coding, LZW compression), and lossy compression techniques (Quantization, downsampling, filtering) 9. Optional Lectures (non-examinable): Generative AI: LLMs and Stable Diffusion Models Overall, the content was well organized across different weeks, which made it easy to follow and digestible. The use of MATLAB to demonstrate the functioning of different techniques made the content much more useful and intuitive than just an abstract high-level discussion. Tutorials: They were weekly tutorial assignments, that needed to be submitted before the actual tutorials, a total of 7 of them. Most of them were straightforward Matlab exercises (like performing dot products with sine basis functions or implementing circular convolution). Some of them (especially the one on implementing approx JPEG compression) were very useful to better understand the materials discussed in the class. Project: The project was individual, consisting of a direct question (requiring us to analyze a human whistle) and an open-ended one which required us to come up with a simple MATLAB application that utilizes the extracted audio to do something useful. It gave us a chance to try out many interesting techniques outside the course. TL;DR It's a fun module! Most of the lectures are quite easy to follow and the workload is much less compared to many other CS courses. The Prof was also very patient and took time to address several questions after the lecture. Note: Prof Ng Teck Khim's lectures were not recorded, and as mentioned in the other comments, it's crucial to attend his lectures to understand most of the content covered. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 9/10 Workload: 4/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Dr. Vishal Sharma Assessments: Group Project: 40% Final Exam (MCQ): 60% Content Overview: 1. Introduction to Natural Language Processing: General Information about the rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the evolution of NLP as a field. 2. Artificial Neural Networks: Feedforward and backpropagation, variants of gradient descent (momentum, RMSProp, Adam), regularization, and deep neural networks. 3. Text Preprocessing and Vectorization: Tokenization (Byte Pair Encoding, word piece, and unigram), stemming & lemmatization, Bag of Words, TFIDF representations, Skip Grams, and Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW). 4 NLP Applications: Topic Modeling (Latent Dirichlet Allocation; Non-negative Matrix Factorization), Doc2Vec, Sentence Embeddings via BERT & Evaluation of topic models. 5. Modern Neural Network Architectures: RNN, LSTMs, GRUs, Attention mechanism, GPTs & LLMs, Introduction to RAG, Encoder-Decoder Architectures. 6. Transformer Architecture: Attention Mechanism (Self and Cross, Masked Attention, Multi-head), Positional Embeddings, Layer and Batch Normalization, Inference (Greedy, Exhaustive, and Beam Search), Evaluation (Clipped Precision, BLEU Score), Greedy Decoding, BERT & T5 architecture and training. 7. LLM Alignment: In-context Learning, Neural scaling laws, Supervised Fine-tuning, Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (PPO, DPO). 8. LLaMA Transformer Architecture: Rotary Positional Embeddings, RMS Normalization, KV-Cache, Multi-Query Attention, Grouped Multi-Query Attention, SwiGLU Activation. 9. Mistral Transformer Architecture: Sliding Window Attention, Rolling Buffer Cache, Sparse Mixture of Experts, Model Sharding. 10. Evaluating LLMs & Prompt Engineering: Common Benchmarks, ELO scores, Principles and Techniques for Prompt Engineering (like CoVe, Many-Shot, etc). 11. Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG): Chunking Strategies (character, recursive, and context-aware), embedding models (Siamese BERT, BGE-M3), Vector Databases (Hierarchical Navigable Small Worlds), Re-ranking, Hyde, and evaluating RAG. 12. Optimize LLM Inference: GPU arithmetic (calculating model parameters), Quantization methods, Families of Weight Quantization (Post-Training Quantization and Quantization-Aware Training), Fine-Tuning Strategies (Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA). 13. LLM Augmentation: Multi-Agent Systems. Project: The project involved building an app using LLMs in the backend using h2o Wave as the app development framework in Python. The overall workflow was quite straightforward; we used the h2o GPT RAG pipeline to upload data via the UI and simply make API calls to the LLM. TL;DR It's a good module! The lecture content is extremely useful, especially with the growing popularity of LLMs. For some topics, the depth may not be extensive, but there is certainly a lot of content to go through. Also, while the topics covered are useful, the course as such leans more towards theoretical concepts than practical coding applications. The professor has very deep knowledge about the field and his teaching style was also nice, made it very easy to understand some of the more challenging concepts and ideas. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 10/10 Workload: 5/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).
thread_title: DSA4213 Natural Language Processing for Data Science

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Dr. Mun Lai Yoke Assessments: Class Participation: 15% Quizzes: 10% Midterms: 30% Final Exam: 45% Textbooks: 1. Berk and DeMarzo (2019), Corporate finance, 5th edition, Pearson. [Main Recommended Text] 2. Bodie, Kane, and Marcus (2017), Investments, 11th Edition, McGraw-Hill. [Second Recommended Text] Content Overview: 1. Portfolio Theory: This segment covers fundamental aspects such as risk and return metrics, historical performance analysis across various investment instruments, the advantages of diversification, Portfolio Frontiers, Capital Allocation Line, Markowitz's Separation property, and the application of the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) to find the tangency portfolio and compute required returns. 2. Valuation Models: This one is all about valuation methodologies for fixed income securities (bonds) and derivative products (options). Topics include arbitrage, yield analysis, term structure of interest rates, duration and convexity for managing interest rate risks, as well as models like the Black-Scholes model and binomial option pricing model, alongside risk-neutral valuation techniques. Topics: 1. Historical Risk and Return (basic probability review) 2. Diversification & the Efficient Frontier of Risky Assets 3. Investing with Risk-Free Asset 4. CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model) 5. Time Value of Money and Applications 6. Bond Prices and Yields 7. Managing Interest Rate Risk (duration, convexity and immunization) 8. Options Markets (types, combinations and put-call parity) 9. Option Valuation (binomial tree, black scholes and risk free probabilities) Note: The course does not go heavy on the math. You won't be doing any stochastic calculus or simulation models in this course. TLDR; It's a great module! The lecture content is extremely useful, you'll learn a lot about analyzing portfolios and comprehending asset pricing models with many important concepts such as risk neutral world and replicating portfolios. The professor's teaching style was also very nice, made it very easy to understand some of the more challenging concepts and ideas. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 10/10 Workload: 3/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Dr. Kong Yu-Chien Assessments: Tutorial Participation: 10% Quizes (Weekly): 10% Midterm: 30% Final Exam: 50% Textbooks: 1. Mishkin, Frederic S. (2021), The Economics of Money, Banking and Financial Markets (13 th Edition), Pearson. 2. Champ, B., Freeman, S. and Haslag, J., (2016) Modeling Monetary Economies (4 th Edition), Cambridge University Press. The module offers a solid introduction to money and banking. The content is well-structured a great mix of theoretical foundations and practical applications: you'll learn about fundamental concepts in Money, Banking, and Financial Markets, including interest rates, bond and stock markets, financial institution management, and central banking policies. Content Overview: Part 1: Why Study Money, Banking, and Financial Markets, Overview of the Financial System and Money (Intro, important terminologies, and general overview) Part 2: Financial Markets - The Meaning of Interest Rates (Present Value calculations, coupon bonds, annuities, fixed interest payments, effect of interest rate changes, basic financial economics) - Bond Market and the Behavior of Interest Rates (Bond demand and supply, money market, liquidity preference theory + discussions on income, price, and expected inflation effects) - The Risk and Term Structure of Interest Rates (risk and liquidity premium, yield curves: rational expectations, segmented markets, liquidity premium theories) - The Stock Market (computing stock prices: generalized dividends and Gordon growth), the Theory of Rational Expectations, and the Efficient Market Hypothesis Part 3: Financial Institutions - Banking and the Management of Financial Institutions (Bank Balance Sheets: Liquidity Management, Capital Adequacy Management, Credit Risks, and Interest Rate Risks) - A Model of Demand Deposit Banking and Bank Runs: Intro to overlapping generation models, bank runs and failures, deposit insurance, etc Part 4: Central Banking and the Conduct of Monetary Policy - The Money Supply Process (Fed Balance Sheets and instruments for Monetary base Control, relation between Monetary Base and Money Supply, etc) - Tools of Monetary Policy (Fed Funds Rates, Market for Reserves, policies: OMO, discount rate, interest on reserves, reserve requirements, quantitative easing, etc) - An overlapping-generations model of Fiat Money [Samuelson (1958), Wallace (1978)] TLDR It's a great module! The lecture content is extremely useful, especially for Econs majors to get a proper basic understanding of financial system's mechanisms and operations. The professor's teaching style was also very nice, made it very easy to understand some of the more challenging concepts and ideas. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 10/10 Workload: 3/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Prof Lim Tiong Wee Assessments: CA: 50% Final Exam: 50% Textbooks: 1. Albert, Jim (2009). Bayesian Computation with R (2nd ed). Springer. 2. Hoff, Peter D. (2009). A First Course in Bayesian Statistical Methods. Springer. The module offers a solid introduction to Bayesian statistics and inference techniques. The content is well-structured, transitioning from theoretical foundations to practical applications with lots of cool demonstrations using R. Content Overview: 1. Introduction to Bayesian statistics: (The so-called Bayesian machinery and way of doing things) 2. One-parameter models (Beta and Gamma models, Posterior predictive, Credible Sets and Sensitivity Analysis) 3. The normal inverse gamma model 4. Monte Carlo approximations 5. Normal approximation and Laplace approximations 6. Rejection sampling and importance sampling (SIR) 7. Markov chain Monte Carlo I: Metropolis-Hastings algorithm (random walk and independent chain) 8. Markov chain Monte Carlo II: Gibbs sampler 9. Hierarchical models Overall, the content was well organized across different weeks, which made it easy to follow and digestible. The use of R to demonstrate the functioning of different algorithms/techniques made the content much more useful and intuitive than just an abstract high-level discussion. The first few weeks were quite theoretical with the content being much more computational-based in the later weeks (as was reflected in the tutorial and homework problems). CA: There were 6 homework sets over the semester. The homework sets were short (1-2 questions) and generally easy to do with the knowledge from the lectures. Besides the homework sets, we were required to present 3 questions (from the tutorial problem sets) over the semester as a group. Final Exam: The final exams had a bit more challenging questions, but they were also quite fun and served as a great wrap-up to the course. TLDR It's a great module! The lecture content is extremely useful, especially with the rising popularity of Bayesian techniques. The course gives a solid intro to many interesting techniques in the field. Although we didn't cover more advanced applications like Bayesian regression and LDA, the techniques we did learn laid a strong foundation. The Prof was also very patient and took time to address several questions after the lecture. His teaching style made it very easy to understand some of the more complex concepts discussed in the course. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 10/10 Workload: 7/10 If you've any questions, feel free to message me on telegram (@namanagr).

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY23/24 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Sun Baoluo Assessments: Midterms: 30% Final Exam: 70% Textbooks: An Introduction to Statistical Learning with Applications in R by James et al. 2nd Edition 2021, (Mostly Ch 01-06, 12) The Elements of Statistical Learning by Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman. 2nd Edition. (Reference) (Lots of addition material, for further reference only) The module provides an excellent introduction to the statistical aspects of machine learning, standing out as one of the few that takes a statistical approach to grasp the fundamentals of ML. Content Overview: The module started with a clear overview of statistical learning and the various problems encountered in machine learning. The initial weeks covered foundational topics such as supervised/unsupervised learning, error types and loss functions, matrix notations, and some basic R programming. The subsequent weeks delved into linear regression, addressing formulation, common issues (leverage, multi-collinearity), and residual plot analysis. While the discussions remained mostly at the surface level, the professor was readily available for more in-depth conversations. The focus then shifted to classification problems, exploring standard algorithms like KNN, logistic regression, LDA, and QDA. The midterm syllabus concluded with a discussion on resampling methods, such as Cross Validation techniques, Monte Carlo simulations, and Bootstrapping. Post-midterms, linear regression returned to focus, emphasising variable selection methods. This included best, forward, backward, and mixed stepwise regressions; shrinkage methods like Ridge and Lasso regularisation were covered in detail, along with dimension reduction techniques such as Principal Component Regression and Partial Least Squares. The module concluded with an exploration of common unsupervised techniques: dimension reduction (PCA) and clustering (K Means and Hierarchical Clustering). Overall, the content was well organised across different weeks, which made it easy to follow and digestible. The use of R to demonstrate the functioning of different algorithms/techniques made the content much more useful and intuitive than just an abstract high level discussion. For some of the weeks, the content also overlapped largely with ST3131, so if you've taken it before or are planning to take it with this course, it could be quite useful! Examinations: The midterm exam was relatively straightforward, closely aligning with class discussions and involving simple calculations. In contrast, the final exam was more difficult with many questions requiring you to derive or proof certain expressions or results. TLDR It's a great module! Most of the lectures were quite easy to follow and the workload is much less compared to many other stats modules. The Prof is also very patient, and took time to address several questions after the lecture. Enjoyability: 10/10 Usefulness: 9/10 Workload: 2/10

author: Naman Agrawal
message: Workload was decent (5/10). Some of the lectures required you to read a bit more about the topics. If you're not from a science background, might need to spend a little more time to understand the concepts. But mostly, attending/watching the lectures was sufficient for the quizzes and the exam.

message: Not really, the time was more than sufficient for writing the essay. Most importantly, you had to plan the essay carefully to best respond to the question. Yes, lectures were recorded.

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY22/23 Sem 2 Lecturer: Prof Valerio Scarani Assessments: Weekly Quizzes: 8*5 = 40% Video Project: 30% Final Exam (Essay Based): 30% The module serves as a good introduction to Quantum Physics. If you're genuinely interesting in not just knowing about what the world of quantum is, but also it's applications in various field including cryptography and computing, this module is perfect for you! Content covered: The module began with an introduction to quantum physics, including a discussion on how it differs from classical physics and the associated terminologies. In the subsequent weeks, the focus shifted to interferometry, both with one and two particles, and the discussion covered popular notions in quantum physics, including superposition, entanglement, uncertainty principle, indistinguishability, wave-particle duality, and decoherence. This eventually paved the way to a discussion on Bell nonlocality, one of the most important notions of the quantum revolution. The course then covered various interpretations in quantum physics, such as Bohmium mechanics, operationalism, many worlds interpretation, super-determinism, contextuality, and more. For the first few weeks in the second half of the module, Prof talked about the history of quantum physics and how different ideas have evolved and are being applied to classical theories across various sub-disciplines of physics. The course also included lectures on basic quantum cryptography, such as key distribution protocols like BB84 and E91, and quantum computing (e.g., Shor's algorithm for factoring), which according to me was the most useful aspect of the module. Prof also spent time talking about the scope of quantum (its application to macroscopic objects, gravity, Heisenberg's cut, Wigner's friend), and prevailing ideas in biological processes, time-travel, and teleportation. Overall, the content was well organized across different weeks, which made it easy to follow and digestible. The depth of the content covered was perfect for an introductory course. During the semester, Prof also organized a visit to the quantum labs to showcase some of the existing experimental work. Quizzes: There were 10 quizzes across the semester, and the best 8 scores were chosen. In general, the quizzes questions were easy to solve as long as the lecture materials had been review and understood carefully. Video Project: This was probably the most interesting (and difficult) component of the entire module: creating a 5 min video to explain any concept covered in the module. Final Exam: Contrary what you'd expect, the final exam was essay-based, requiring you to write a one page essay (two-sided) on the question asked. The question well encapsulated the essence of the module and was a good metric to evaluate your overall understanding of the contents covered. TLDR It's a great module! If you're afraid that you will find it difficult because of no mathematics/science background, don't need to worry about it. The math wasn't complicated, just high-school level. There was some use of concepts such as probability amplitudes, tensor products and subspaces, but none of it was tested for finals/quiz. As long as you take time to understand the theories and the implications, you don't have to worry about the math. The Prof is also very patient, and took time to address several questions after the lecture. Hope you found the feedback useful!

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY22/23 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Prabhu Natrajan Assessments: Tutorial Attendance: 5% Class Participation (weekly quizzes, lecture videos, discussions): 5% Assignments: 30% Midterms: 15% Finals: 20% Group Project: 25% The module serves as a good introduction to AI. If you're genuinely interesting in not just knowing about AI, but also having hands on experience in implementing and developing algorithms, this module is perfect for you! Content covered: In the first 2 weeks, Prof spent time talking about AI in general, its achievements, opportunities and branches. After week 3, the content became very interesting as Prof discussed more about various Search techniques (Informed/Uniformed). The specifics of various search problem formulation strategies, and searching algorithms (UFS, DFS, BFS, A*, etc.) were discussed over the next 2 weeks. Eventually, various ML algorithms were discussed such as KNN, Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, K means Clustering, and Artificial Neural networks (including deep learning frameworks such as RNN, LSTM, CNN). Prof spent about one week on every ML algorithm. The module concluded with a discussion on fairness, model interpretability and privacy in AI. The content was split into small videos, which made it very easy and digestible. The depth of the content covered was perfect for an introductory AI course. The use of Python to demonstrate the functioning of different algorithms made the content much more useful and intuitive than just an abstract high level discussion. Tutorials: The tutorial questions were very fun. They had a mix of direct questions (which could be easily solved using the lecture content) as well as more challenging problems which required you to self research on various topics. Several extensions of existing algorithms such as KMeans++ and Generative Adversarial Networks (briefly) were presented as tutorial problems. Assignments: There were around 5-6 programming assignments, most of which required us to implement the machine learning models from scratch. The good thing about doing so (instead of directly using inbuilt functions from various packages) was that it allowed one to understand and gauge the mechanics of each algorithm and how they are actually implemented in practice. The assignment problems were supplemented with sufficient comments to guide your implementation. Project: This was probably the most interesting component of the entire module: using an existing dataset to build various prediction models. There were a wide variety of project options from simple supervised learning task to more sophisticated review analyzers, and image recognizers. Working on the projects gave you a chance to implement many more models and truly appreciate the power of AI and the complexity it heralds. TLDR One of the best modules I've taken so far. It may be useful for quant interviews if you have little/no background in AI. Basic programming knowledge (CS1010S or equivalent is much more than sufficient) in Python is quite important for this course. If you're ever stuck in any assignment or project, you can always use the Piazza forums. The Prof is very friendly and approachable. The module itself is well organized and the content covered is very relevant. Expected Grade: A
thread_title: IT1244 Artificial Intelligence: Technology and Impact

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY22/23 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Timothy Wertz Assessments: Midterms: 30% Homework (4% * 4 = 16%) Tutorials: 9% Finals: 45% The content covered in the module was very fascinating, systematically developing and understanding algorithms to perform various numerical tasks. Prof spend the first few weeks talking about the basics of machine representation of numbers, floating point operations and conditioning, with some background on analyzing time complexities. In the next few weeks, Prof focused on a variety of algorithms to solve linear system problems: Gaussian Elimination, LU factorization, Partial Pivoting, Cholesky Factorization, Jacobi Method, Gauss Siedel Method, Successive Relaxation, etc. The focus of the module gradually shifted to Least Square Problems, which relied on various orthogonalization algorithms (Gram Schmidt, Givens Rotation, Householder reflections) and Singular Value Decomposition. After the mids, various Eigenvalue problems were discussed bringing some more algorithms (power iteration, Rayleigh Quotient Iteration, QR algorithm, PCA) into the picture. In the next few weeks, approaches to solving non linear problems were explained (the usual bisection, fixed point iteration, newton, secant, inverse quadratic interpolation, et al). Subsequently, various interpolation strategies were developed (monomial basis, Lagrange basis, Newton basis, Chebyshev nodes, cubic splines, et al). Finally (and perhaps the most useful), methods to performing differentiation and integration on computer were taught (centered difference approaches, Richardson extrapolation, Newton Coates Rules, Gaussian Quadrature, Adaptive Quadrature, et al). Yup, that's a lot of algorithms. Personally, I found the module quite useful. Even though, one may not really use a lot of these algorithms for practical purposes, it's quite important to understand their limitations, the conditioning of the problems they attempt to solve, their convergence and time complexities. All in all, the module uses a lot of concepts from linear algebra (and ofc some basic calculus for the later half of the module). The pace of the module was perfectly right, focusing on one problem at a time. Prof patiently cleared any doubts/questions after the lectures. He also often used R to demonstrate the working and limitations of various algorithms. Homework sets: Most of the homework questions were quite easy. But if you hate doing calculations, you may find some of the problems annoying. But, it's good practice as you apply the algorithms taught. Tutorials: The tutorial problems were generally quite easy. Most of them had 1-2 programming questions (however note that no programming questions were asked in mids/finals/homework). Some of them also had theoretical questions. Exams: The midterms were the hardest, especially because some of the problems required you do to an intense amount of calculations. The finals were much easier, and most of the questions were pretty straightforward. TLDR Whether you like/dislike the module is very subjective, depending upon how much you enjoy designing and analyzing algorithms. Make sure to revise your linear algebra and calculus before taking this module. Also, ensure that you're clear on the concepts of conditioning and error analysis form the first few weeks as the module builds upon these concepts throughout the semester. Finally, although programming questions were not tested, trying to implement the algorithms yourself on a computer might help a lot in understanding their functioning and pre-conditions. Expected Grade: A
thread_title: DSA2102 Essential Data Analytics Tools: Numerical Computation

author: Naman Agrawal
message: AY22/23 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Wang Fei Assessments: Midterms: 25% Quizzes (5% * 5 = 25%) Finals: 50% The module is a prerequisite for several mathematics and statistics module. The content covered is also quite useful for quant interviews. For the first 4 weeks, Prof spend a lot of time covering basic combinatorics and discrete probability distributions (principles of counting, combinatorial problems, axioms of probability, conditional probability, independent events, properties of various discrete probability distributions: Bernoulli, binomial, Poisson, geometric, hypergeometric, negative binomial, et al). The content was digestible, and the midterms tested it thoroughly. In the later half of the module, concepts related to continuous probability distributions were introduced, which relied on extensive usage of basic calculus and some multivariable calculus (multiple integrals, partial derivatives, Jacobian). Taking a module in multivariable calculus (MA2311/MA2104) alongside/before could be useful! Topics covered included continuous probability distributions (uniform, normal, exponential, gamma and beta distributions), hazard rate functions, cumulative distribution functions, joint and conditional probabilities (continuous analogues), convolutions, properties of Expectation, Moment Generating Functions, and limit theorems (Markov Inequality, Chebyshev inequality, Law of Large Numbers, Central Limit Theorem, et al). Some questions from tutorials/quizzes also explored basic properties of Chi Squared distributions, Rayleigh distribution, t-distribution etc. Quizzes: (5 LumiNUS Quizzes) Some of the quiz questions were quite tricky and required a lot of thinking. But, the quizzes were quite helpful in practicing the concepts. Tutorials: Many tutorial problems were from Sheldon M Ross book (A first course in Probability). The problems were quite interesting and a good reflection of the kind of questions asked in the exams. TLDR Since the mod was taught by Math's department, some concepts were quite theoretical (especially towards the end of the module). But Prof did include a fair amount of application based problems for the stats students. Overall, a good experience. Recommend practicing tutorial problems form the first week of tutorials by yourself, very important for understanding problem solving techniques. Especially since probability questions can often be solved by multiple strategies, it's important to pay attention to which strategy might be useful for a particular question and what could be the potential pitfalls of a given method. Expected Grade: A

author: Joyce Yeo
message:
Took in AY17/18 Semester 1.

The content went like this for us:
- Basic Logic
- ZFC Axioms (These are set theory axioms)
- Functions (injective, surjective, bijective)
- Countability and Infinite sets
(Most of what is mentioned below is also covered, but they are 2-3 slides each..)

This module basically redefined how I thought of sets. You don't need to be a "math genius" to take this module. I loved the rigour and detail in this mod. It relies a lot on careful thinking more than prior knowledge, which helped me since I don't have a strong math olympiad background or the like.

<LECTURES>
The first part of the module involved the prof handwriting proofs to establish new things, based on what we already knew. I didn't have to take much notes at this point because the proofs are detailed enough to follow. The second part of the module involved slides, and the slides may not explain everything so you have to pay attention during lectures to fill in the gaps. The /bad/ thing is that the slides are uploaded about 1 hour before lecture commences so printing is not a very feasible option.

We have a textbook but I didn't have much time to read it except at the start of the semester. Very interesting book to keep. Recommended investment.

<TUTORIALS>
The tutorials are out of the world. The prof will usually go through new whacky proofs but if you pay attention they are very interesting. His main purpose is not to teach you how to write 1001 proofs but to put you through the process of being able to formulate a proof. Later on in the semester, when you have homework and tests, he will go through these instead.

<HOMEWORK>
There will be homework given, and it is best 4 out of 5 to be taken. (Or something like that -- the point is that you can afford to make mistakes.) I usually try on my own, then get hints for questions I can't do. Some online resources can also be used (NUS Box).

<MIDTERM>
Entirely TRUE/ FALSE questions. They will ask things like is 0 = {0}? (Null set vs. a set of the null set).  Learn the definitions well and this will be manageable. The prof even went through some of the questions during tutorial, cut-and-paste to the midterms. It was pretty sick/ challenging/ fun at the same time because there was negative marking for answering incorrectly. Reason? Mathematics is about building knowledge and statements from known axioms. If you are not sure about something, then you are better off admitting you are not sure and leaving the question blank. 

For those interested,
80 T/F = 80 marks
-10 marks for wrong name/ matric number (just follow instructions!)
Min marks = 10 - 80 = -60, Max marks = 80

I was initially very afraid because my experience with the homework and lecture content made me feel that I was inferior. Nevertheless, I studied the correct things and tried to be extra careful with each T/F I put on the paper. A past-year paper I looked at happened to be almost copy-and-paste into this midterm. I didn't memorise all the T/F but looking through those questions made me more aware of the logic that was being tested in the questions. Anyway, revising T/F questions is a good way to quickly test your logic and concepts :)

<FINALS>
Some simple proof writing. The proof format modeled what we covered in lecture. The bulk of the paper was still T/F, and most of the questions were "imported" from the Midterms. I guess the prof was really rewarding effort more than being a Math genius.

author: Bao Dinh
message:
GES1017 AY18/19 Sem 2:
40% Project, 40% Finals, 20% Class Participation + Homework
Most of the things mentioned in LRock Kimberly's review are still True, these are the only differences:

Prof Chai Kah Hin is the only Prof (from NUS); there are also quite many guest/industry talks which are very helpful for Engineering students.

There is class attendance taken. There is class every week, but the number of full lectures is about ~8 (2-3 industry talks (usually take half of the 3-hour span), 2 weeks for project presentation, 1 week for field trip to Singapore Maritime Gallery, and 2 weeks off (one for Eng day, one for CNY)). You are allowed to miss 2 lectures without penalty. However, class participation is not important. I got 9/10 without saying anything for the whole sem. There seems to be a lot of discussions in the first few weeks, then it just becomes a solo presentation from the Prof. in the later weeks. There's no "a name stand in front of you, and there is an assistant recording down who is asking questions." Content is still almost the same from his iteration of the module.

I hoped for a pass grade only, ended up with a B+/S. A huge thanks to my teammates who should already be graduated by now :).

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message: Happened to see this but they take up 10% of the final grade. Prof Yap will take the best 80% of the total no of quizzes) though as mentioned in my review even if you get everything wrong for the quizzes you'll secure 7/10 for that component just by attempting them.

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY25/26 Sem 1:

Lecturer: Dr Yap Von Bing (the GOAT)
TA: Wang Youjia

Assignments breakdown:
22 in lecture quizzes with the best 80% of quizzes taken. - 10% (0/2 correct will get 70% of the individual quiz grade, 1/2 correct will get 80% of the individual quiz grade and 2/2 100% of the individual quiz grade)

10% tutorial attendance (Attend 8/10 tutorials for full percentage)

25% midterms on Examplify (first time Prof Yap having midterms) with 1 page cheatsheet allowed.

55% finals pen and paper with no cheatsheet allowed (closed book).

Overview of Module: 
ST2132 builds off of ST2131, going deeper into estimating parameters of statistical models and also learning about these statistical models through mathematical rigour. As much as it is similar to ST2131 it is also completely different so your experience with ST2131 will not fully determine your experience with ST2132, coming from me a person who hated ST2131 but actually enjoyed ST2132 much better and found it more intuitive. Granted you will need some knowledge from ST2131 though in the words of many maths profs, the knowledge you need is trivial as you'll just need to know about the different distributions covered in ST2131 (ie Gamma, Poisson, Normal, Bernoulli, Binomial, Exponential, Geometric etc). You won't need combinatorics and Bayesian statistics as the course is more relevant for the second half of ST2131 (the calculus part though there is minimal multivariable calculus also come to think of it). Would recommend watching zed statistics in YouTube and Khan academy as they explain concepts in ST2132 quite well.

Lectures
There are 2 lectures a week each about 1.5-2h long. Prof Yap will go through the content whilst also dropping knowledge and wisdom on you also during the lecture.  As mentioned in other reviews, prof yap is very passionate about ST2132 and also loves to talk during lecture. So much so that there is a lot of talking but I don't mind cause he yaps good sh*t about Statistics unlike Gan Fah Fatt who talks absolute nonsense. Prof Yap is a certified G especially with regards to statistics and aims to share his knowledge with us during every lecture. He is also a good Samaritan and extends his generosity to the people who don't attend lecture, where almost every lecture he breaks the fourth wall and talks directly to the projector/people watching the recording about the importance of attending lecture and encouraging them to attend lectures in person. Very humourous. It's because of this that I attend his live lectures because it's like watching Deadpool but with statistics given how often he breaks the fourth wall during lecture. Absolute cinema.

He also drops words of wisdom during the lecture such as telling us not to be addicted to watching ig reels at the urinals because we may accidentally send a selfie to the internet/Reddit. And how he doesn't like to allow cheatsheets at exams because he says if we have cheatsheets we will just be pouring water (he actually goes on to demonstrate this by pouring water from one bottle to another. Bruh.) saying that pouring water effectively means copying over things blindly but he wants us to think and test us on that, a pedagogy I agree with greatly.

The lectures end with a 4 minute quiz at around the last 30 minutes of lectures so if you're not attending lectures (which you should be attending lectures) just camp at the last 30 mins and keep refreshing the page until the quiz comes out. The quizzes are relatively straightforward but just put into chatgpt to check the answers or discuss with your friends. Had many 90+25' min assists because of that in order to get the max score. As mentioned there are 22 such quizzes but prof only takes the best 80% and the gradings are mentioned above so even if you get every qn wrong for all the quizzes you'll still get 7% out of the max 10%.

I found Prof yap very engaging and one of the better profs I have met and is genuinely a very nice person and educator.  However I felt that especially for later topics like MOM, MLE and Hypothesis testing, he makes things overly complicated by linking them to random stuff which ended up in us becoming more confused cause we couldn't see the bigger picture/were just mechanically trying to implement the estimation methods without knowing what it's full purpose was. No diss to prof yap cause he really does try his best to teach us, but his teaching may not suit everyone especially those who do not have strong foundations in probability. I usually ended up watching zed statistics on YouTube especially for the Method of Moments, Maximum Likelihood Estimation and Hypothesis Testing topics as I found I could understand Prof Yap better once I did that as they built up assuming you having 0 knowledge in statistics. You can also speak to prof yap for consult because he is very caring and is willing to go to the basics to teach the concepts. I went for a consult with him and he was very patient in explaining questions I initially thought were stupid. He is also free after lectures though you'll prb only get to talk to him for 5-10 mins. He is open to email also.

Topics covered: 
1. ST2131 review (4 weeks)
2. Standard error 
3. Confidence intervals 
4. Method of Moments 
5.Maximum Likelihood Estimation
6. Hypothesis testing

Tutorials (10% for attendance)
There were weekly tutorials where unlike ST2131 prof yap only sets a maximum of 6 qns per tutorial. I found it helped to just do the qns first even if you get the answer wrong cause like Prof Yap says, you cannot just be pouring water. You need to work out the soln even if it's wrong to have chemistry in your brain. My tutorial TA was really good in explaining concepts and the questions and although he seemed angry at first I realised it was just his voice cause he was legit one of the nicest TAs I met and was willing to explain simple concepts in the tutorial during and after class. What I didn't like about the tutorials was how prof yap arranged the questions like a recursive stack. One such example would be for Tutorial 8 qn 3: Please refer to Tutorial 4 qns 5 about Confidence intervals. And then at Tutorial 4 qns 5 : Please refer to slide 10.2 about the level alpha confidence interval.  Seriously this mad pissed me off cause I had to keep recursing back in order to just understand what the question was asking. Moreover, the solns he released were also equally as nebulous so imo either copy down the TA's solutions or just put the solns and slides into chatgpt plus and get it to generate solns step by step so you can check your working cause if you don't, like ex Manchester united goalkeeper Andre Onana, you will catch no ball.

Midterms (25% with cheatsheet)

Unlike previous semesters', this iteration of ST2132 has Midterms which were conducted on Examplify and was 1 hour long. There were 25 mcq questions and most of the questions were from the lecture quizzes with some tutorial questions modified. Time is tight though so you wouldn't want to be deriving stuff during the exam. Instead in this instance you should be pouring water and putting all the quizzes into your cheatsheet along with important formulas. I didn't do well for midterms but it was better than my st2131 midterms. Prof yap allowed a cheatsheet for midterms cause it was done on Examplify and realised it'd be hard to give everyone a formula sheet.  Prof Yap once again broke the fourth wall during midterms asking everyone to start coming for lecture. Really admired his dedication there. Like Liverpool he wants to assure everyone that they will never walk alone.

Grades - 16/25

Finals (55%)

Finals was worth 55% and was closed book with no cheatsheet but a formula sheet was provided. Any calculator could be brought in also. The finals had 10 MCQ qns worth a total of 10 marks and the remaining 3 Qns were 15 marks each and overall it was out of 55 marks. Finals were quite difficult where it was essentially extensions of tutorial questions. Doing tutorials would help though I think you'd also need to know your sh*t very well in order to do well in the finals. There was a Method of Moments and Max Likelihood question and also a hypothesis testing question ie the one about calculating the likelihood ratio and also some stuff about standard error. I didn't do tutorial 11 but I'd like to think that if you get a rough idea of the aforementioned topics you should be able to attempt the finals questions. Hoping prof would be lenient with the marking cause there was no cheatsheet so people wont be pouring water or doing voodoo like the Nigeria coach claimed the DR Congo football team were doing when they beat Nigeria on penalties to advance to the world cup playoffs. I managed to attempt all the questions because I drank red bull before the exam and red bull gives you wings so I'd like to think that I was speeding through the exam.

Overall, this module was surprisingly enjoyable and really fixed the damage Gan Fah Fatt did when he taught us ST2131. Prof Yap truly is a GOATED prof like the GOAT Cristiano Ronaldo and never gives up on people like how Liverpool never gives up in the premier league. I learnt a lot from prof yap and also on YouTube and admired how he was willing to clarify our doubts and making it clear that there were no stupid questions. As long as we were willing to learn he would be willing to teach and help us in the module. That is the type of teaching other profs should replicate. It's a shame prof yap is not taking st3131 next semester but have to say, he really did make me enjoy stats much more and in spite of some inadequacies in his teaching I decided to nominate him for a teaching award because why not? He deserves it. I also gained a lot of wisdom like how we cannot be pouring water when it comes to learning. As my idol the great Bruce Lee said, be water but that doesn't mean pour water. I also learnt wisdom in the form about how it is important bit to doomscroll on ig reels or TikTok in the toilet and also the important of breaking lectures. And also how red bull gives you wings which is why I drink red bull before my exams so I can finish them on time. Looking forward to the future stats mods and hope I pass this mod. But till then, don't pour water and red bull gives you wings. Trust me on this. I'm a doctor.

overall rating - 10/10

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message: Taken in AY 2025/2026 Lecturers: Dr Tan and Prof Woon Overview of the module: The module essentialy serves as an introduction to political and economic geography, touching on geopolitics through investingating these concepts through the lenses of various actors. Each week covers a different actor and how it links to the political and economic geographies. I found this module very interesting and enjoyable because the lecturers made a very difficult concept very easy to understand through their lectures and tutorials. They were also very passionate about teaching and answering questions (be it through consults or email). I usually just emailed them and they would answer within a week/two. Moreover, I felt this module was a massive improvement over GE1101E (the geography intro mod) which I found to be less structured (at least when I took it in 2023/24 before I changed my major to data science and geography became my second major, yeah I only did GE2102 2 years later but better late than never). This module was an improvement over GE1101E due to there being a greater level of academic rigour with regards to the writing and was also more structured and allowed for greater colaboration for projects and between the teaching team and students. Moving on, I also admired how dedicated the profs were in guiding us and also listening to our ideas in order to shape our perspectives and opinions on the matter. they beleived it was more important to develop our own thoughts and opinions on the matter rather than just regurgitating information which is a pedagogy I agree with alot and is a major improvement from H2 Geographyat A levels and also HL Geography at the IB (I did HL Geog at IB so yeah). Lectures There is one 2 hour lecture every week and each week will cover a different actor. One of the weeks (I think in week 10), there was no lectures because it was meant for consults for our group projects. Lectures were very interesting and enjoyable cause the profs would break down the complicated readings so that it was more understandable and also try to engage the class through showing videos (we watched a part of the Jamie foxx movie called the Kingdom during the week we covered the Media). I really looked forward to the elctures and i used it as an opportunity to clarify my doubts with the lecturers also if I had qns about the topic. The following are the topics covered during each lecture. Topics covered/Syllabus (from canvas) 1. Thinking geographically: Introducing the importance of thinking about politics and the economy in spatial terms and the closely intertwined nature of politics, the economy and the environment in geographical debates. 2. Thinking through political-economy: Explicating the value of an actor-centered perspective on political-economy and how it is underpinned by the concepts of power, resistance, agency and performance. 3. States Focusing on states’ governing frameworks and regimes and how these impact upon economic and diplomatic activities and interactions. 4. Media: Highlighting media outlets and journalists in re-presenting politics and the economy for popular audience consumption. 5: Supranational organizations: The efforts of global and macro-regional institutional structures in shaping economic and political integration at different spatial scales. 6. Planners: The role of urban planners and property developers in shaping the urban landscape and their associated geopolitical and spatial consequences. 7. Firms: Explores how firms, and transnational corporations in particular, are powerful economic and political actors in the contemporary global economy 8. Workers/migrants: Foregrounding both formal and informal modes of worker and migrant organisation, agency and resistance 9. Consumers: Considering how the purchasing power and everyday habits of consumers are both the targets and shaper of political-economic processes. 10. Citizens: Exploring the democratic potential of citizens and their participation in political and economic life. 11. Nature/non-humans: The political economy of 'nature' and the environment and its relationship to non-human agency. 12. Conclusion: Intellectual horizons and pathways. Readings There are weekly readings especially from the textbook but tbh I felt the textbook readings were too long and boring and I often fell asleep when reading the textbook. There were other readings from academic papers and they were better than the textbook which says alot. I would say just get the main ideas and then follow up by Googling case studies that are more modern/relevant (like past 5 to 10 years). Tutorials Tutorials took place fortnightly and you would have to do some pre tutorial work like readings or watching videos so that you could contribute to class discussions or presentations during the tutorials. once again, like the lectures tutorials were very enjoyable as we got to engage in fruitful discussions regarding the topic and the profs were very chill about the points we brought up as long as it was related. It was very refreshing to hear different perspectives and challenge our thinkings. Moreover, some tutorials involved us working in groups to reate a short 10 minute presentation regarding the week's topics. Tutorials were graded and accounted for 10% of the class part component. So use the tutorials to speak and share ideas to get the class part. Assignment 1 Essay (5% outline and 20% essay) The first assignment was a 2500 word essay regarding a political/economic event that took place in 2025. It was preceded by a 5% one page outline where we would have to write down what topics we intended to discuss in our essay. we would also receive feedback for the outline before we wrote the essay, which was due at the end of recess week. The outline really helped me to structure my essay and which topics to focus on and for the essay I chose to write about the Iran-Israel 12 day war (in 2025) because I was following it closely when it was happening. I really enjoyed writing the essay probably because I used it to criticise Israel and the evil the state was doing in the Middle east. So was really invested in deracinating that evil regime to help the world. Anyways had to rush through the essay because I had a lot of things on during recess week but I think I clutched it. Grade - B+ Assignment 2 - Group project (25%) The next assessment was the Group project. After recess week you'll form groups of 5-6 and work on a 15 minute presentation on an actor, comparing and contrasting two dufferent types of that actor. My group got the actor 'states' and like Real Madrid winning against Manchester City in the champions I have to say we prayed for miracles and miracles have arrived. Was really exciting working with my friends and coming up with a presentation everyone was proud of. Only 2 people would need to present on the final tutorial and there was also a 5 minute Q and A from the profs and the students. Really enjoyed working with my groupmates and how everyone came together even during the busy period in order to pull through the project. Grades - A- Finals - 40% (Open book but only printed materials) Finals involved a 2 hour examination, where we had to answer 2 essay questions each worth 20% of the grade. The finals was open book but we could only bring printed materials and was pen and paper. I literally printed out all my notes and also looked for case studies in the readings and on google in order to write the essay. The profs also want you to display critical thinking and analyse the topics and underlying concepts but when in a rush I usually write what I can and blame the government. The questions were related to the different actors and the profs actually gave hints of the finals qns during the final lecture of the semester. So please attend the lectures or at least make friends with people who do. I went for most of the lectures so great success there. Drawings were also allowed and tbh if i ran out of time I wouldve considered pulling a picasso the way i kept drawing. Also I decided to add some humour in my essays by talking about the GOAT Cristiano Ronaldo and the world cup. Great success there. Overall the module was very well strucutured and was intellectually very stimulating. It made very difficult but important topics of geopolitics understandable to people with minimal knowledge about geopolitics and focused on bulding us up as political/economic geographers. Although geography is not my primary major, I thoroughly enjoyed the module especially as a break away from my data science/stats/maths modules and also learnt alot about the world around us, which is very important in this day and age when people get their news from places like tiktok or instagram reels (the bane of humanity at this point). Also sparked my interest in taking some 3k/4k mods about the middle east for obvious reasons as mentioned in my Assignment 1 essay above. Module rating - 9.5/10

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Module taken in AY 24/25 Sem 1

Workload to pass - 2/5, Workload to do well ie get A - 4/5 (I think)
Assessments: 
4 x 10% Tutorial assignments due the friday the week after the tutorial (includes 4% class part across all tutorials)

15% Group project report (15 pages)

15% Group Project Presentation 

30% Final Essay (3000 words)

Overview of the module

The module is aimed at teaching students about renewable energy resources and the importance of adopting and integrating them into the current global energy systems that aim to move away from traditional fossil fuel based ones. I found that the module was very well designed by Dr Nawaz who makes it very engaging and informative especially during lectures where he would crack jokes and show videos about the topic. He also shares about his personal life and energy resources. The best one was during lecture when he showed us a clip from the 3 idiots movies, the one about 'How does an induction motor start?". Very hilarious, he is a major troller also. 

Tutorials

Tutorials involved us getting into groups to research on a topic (for about 40 minutes) before we ended off with presenting our findings to the class. Afterwards we would be given until next Friday to submit the assignment which I though was really good because it gave us time to review and digest the content covered in tutorials. Other activities included debates or group submissions for the tutorials. Dr Nawaz will given compulsory pre readings though he just says read the abstract and conclusion and also videos to watch which are very informative, like the ones about geopolitics of oil etc. 

Group project (Presentation)

In the module, one of the key assignments is the group presentation, rfocusing on a renewable energy resource of your choice (the options given inclued, solar, hydropower, nuclear, wind etc). My group got wind energy. Here, you could group with anyone in the cohort. I didnt know anyone so I just asked a girl i randomly met if I could join and she said yes. Our group had 3 exchangers but contrary to popular belief, everyone pitched in  and pulled their weight thereby leading to our project being well executed (I think). Bad news was that 2 of my group members had their flight from Laos delayed on the day of the presentation so the remaining 3 of us had to present on behalf of everyone. However, Dr Nawaz was very chill about this and the presentation went well eventhough we only practiced 1-2 times before the actual presentation. Presentations take place during the last 3 lecture slots of the semester and are 15 minutes long so do take note of that if you skip lectures. I skipped 2-3 of them cause of midterms and tbh I kind of regret not going for all of them. 

Group project report (15%)
The follow up to the presentation is a 15 page report regarding the renewable enery resource. Although it seems daunting, it is essentially the presentation content with citations so just copy paste your script over and add the citations. Because of this, we could complete the whole thing in about 2-4 days. 

Final essay (30%)

Unlike previous semesters, Dr Nawaz replaced the finals with a final essay because people were too stressed from finals. This surprised Dr nawaz because he told us that he didnt mean for finals to be stressful but just wants to see what people think about the topic, which is a good philosophy to have imo. But not complaining because I have one less final to study for. The final essay was due at the end of reading week and for this semester it was "What was the topic you enjoyed most in this course?". Essentially it was meant to be a very reflective and content driven essay and I really enjoyed the process reviewing all the materials and tutorials in creating the final essay (in around 3-5 days). 

Essentially, I really enjoyed this module and learnt alot from it, irregardless of what my final grade would be. Prof Nawaz and the TAs are very nice and engaging people and you can tell they want to develop you into a better academic and person also. Also because Prof Nawaz is hilarious and a troller. Really recommend this mod to people who are interested in energy futures, sustainability or just want to have a relaxed time learning something new. Trust me on this. I'm a doctor. 

Module rating - 10/10
thread_title: GE3254 Energy Futures: Environment and Sustainability

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message: ST2131/MA2116 (Director's cut): I did one originally on the st2131 page but realised i didnt do it here. And since it's the end of semester here it is, though 1 sem late. I've added more information regarding how to study for the module if you're interested in self study (esp after taking ST2132, at least I hope I know what I'm doing). This new part is labelled extension. Hear Afiq Daniel is taking over from Gan Fah Fatt and I have to say as per Real Madrid coming backing against Manchester City, "We prayed for miracles and miracles have arrived". Was speculating that Prof Yap von Bing would take over from Gan Fah Fatt but I think anyone would be better than Dr Gan if they actually bothered to teach properly. Plus heard good reviews about Afiq Daniel from ST1131 so going to trust he will teach ST2131 well also. Extension (content not originally in my previous review): As mentioned by me 6 months ago, the prof really emphasises on self studying, prb cause he doesnt want to teach probability and instead talk about his life. In order to self study I would recommend watching videos on Youtube regarding probability, I think Zed statistics is a good resource and also covers ST2132 stuff. Statsquest is very good also though only as a starting point, for more info you should watch videos by zed statistics/Harvard because they really explain concepts well to beginners without good maths background so almost anyone can develop a decent foundation in probability. the textbook is a good resource also but there are too many questions so just do like 3 questions per topic to gauge and learn the rest from lectures. No point doing so much yet fall into the law of diminishing returns paradox. You have other mods to focus on. The Harvard one is here: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL2SOU6wwxB0uwwH80KTQ6ht66KWxbzTIo&si=zwAovpUBnbD_0GrC Also I think for probability its not solely practice but also developing a way of thinking (esp about combinatorics). Drawing things out make the mod much more intuitive and also dont blindly memorise formulas unless you have no choice. Try and find the simplest way to understand overarching concepts before diving into the mathematical rigiour. Pro tip also. Do consider doing this mod alongside MA2104/MA2311 cause the second half requires multivariable calculus (and integration) to find area under the curve. Or just watch youtube for it. Dr Gan was saying: "I assume everyone has taken MA2104". We told him: "No" Dr Gan: "Oh ok (looks stunned). Can you drop the mod still?" (to the lecture theatre). All of us: 'No". Dr Gan: "Oh well. Multivariable calculus is easy, you just integrate over the x and the y and done". (Prb one of his better explanations though it is few and far between). But yeah, really focus on internalising the concepts cause the knowledge carries over to ST2132 and because my foundations in ST2131 were weak, I struggled alot in ST2132. So really focus on building a strong foundation for ST2131/MA2116. Overall, this module was a nightmare. Many times I thought I would get an F but thankfully I did not and now I have greater interest in probability I gues. Overall rating: 2/10 (after review)
