author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in Sem 2 AY 24/25
Taken under Prof Ng Kah Loon

Difficulty: 4.5/5
Workload: 3.5/5

Textbook: Prof Ng's textbook. It is a good book but he doesn't give you the PDF as he wants you to purchase it. It is probably for him to get money from the sales and I don't blame him, imagine having to write a textbook. Fantastic textbook imo. Instead you can message in the MA2001 wide telegram group, they will willingly give you the PDF for free. It is piracy but that's how things are I guess. Prb why they didn't want him joining the telegram channel lmao.

Overview of the module: 
There are many different levels of pain. Linear Algebra ranks amongst the top few. The course starts out ok in my opinion. With basic Gauss Jordan elimination, finding determinant, showing if a matrix is invertible or not etc but goes on to become a complete hell after recess week where spans, linear independence, orthogonality and rotations are taught and at a rapid pace. It is easy to lose track of the module here and if your foundations are poor prior to recess week, you're going to enter a nightmare, kind of like when Manchester United lost 7-0 to Liverpool. I will discuss more on this in the following.

Lectures: There are weekly recorded lectures, 2 slots are available however you only need to attend one of them. They are essentially repeats of one another though Prof Ng will prb use his first lecture as a guideline to improve for his second lecture. Prof Ng will spend the lecture doing problem solving from simple computation questions to proving questions. Be sure to take note of the proving questions because they are very difficult and requires high level thinking. He will give you time to attempt the questions first so make sure to bring pen and paper or your iPad to attempt the questions. If you are caught slacking he will tell you not to do so and you shouldn't because Linear Algebra is important. Once my friend did not bring stationery and Prof Ng asked him why. Luckily it was the first lecture and my friend said that he didn't know writing was needed. Prof Ng forgave him and followed up with an announcement on canvas saying "Please bring stationery for lectures.", very amusing. Moreover unlike the last editions under Prof Ng, he actually recorded his lectures this time, prb because people couldn't keep up with the content and kept missing stuff from lectures when unrecorded. Great improvement by him. Anyways Prof Ng is a fantastic lecturer and he gives very insightful points pertaining to Linear Algebra and Problem Solving. Do remember to try the problems yourself. In his own words it's ok to get them wrong. Just attempt it and learn from it. Moreover your TAs will create a telegram group. If you really need help just ask there or the MA2001 wide telegram chat. Someone will help you.  Just throw your pride away and have no shame when asking. Better than not learning at all and risk failing the module (it is really possible to do so).

Tutorials (4%, graded): There are weekly attendance graded tutorials where the TA will go through the problems, probably about 4-5 questions of which 1-2 questions are proving questions. Some have a ridiculous number of parts some up to 6 parts I believe. Was going insane after I did question 1 which had 6 parts to it. Looking back, I should've attempted the tutorial problems before hand, even if I was going to get them wrong, rather than just waiting for the TA to give the answers because such is linear algebra,  a very abstract subject. But being the lazy person I was I did not and paid the price for it later on in the module when my foundations were poor. I would suggest at least writing out the approach to the question at the very least then you can at least clarify stuff with your TA if you go wrong and learn from it instead of just going to the tutorial and wasting time. If you intend on wasting time and not listening you should probably turn up to the tutorial at the last 20 minutes and mark your attendance. Be warned though Prof Ng takes one of the tutorials so if you pull such underhanded tactics he will destroy you and with good reason, like how PSG destroyed Inter Milan in the Champions League final. You also need to do prep work before his tutorials according to my friends as he will ask people to solve the problem. Once again it's ok if it's wrong just attempt and learn from it. If you attend all the tutorials you will get 4%. It really is free marks so don't be ret**ed and actually go for them. Do note, not all tutorial questions are relevant for finals or midterms and some just test the strangest shit that won't even come out. I'd suggest looking through the past year papers are seeing which questions regularly come out and focus on those. The ones that are irrelevant just read through them and understand, not worth spending 2 hours on a question that doesn't come out.

Quizzes (16% total Graded based on attempts): There are 8 quizzes throughout the semester, each 2% graded based on attempts. If you attempt it at least twice no matter your score, you will get the full 2%. Again it is free marks, when it opens quickly do them and secure the 2%. I was an idiot and deadass forgot to do one of them and threw 2% down the drain. Essentially don't be like me and do the quizzes. Some of the questions are whack though so if you want to increase your knowledge I'd suggest doing it with friends (esp those good at maths). Can prb ask your TA for help also if needed.

Midterms (20%): Midterms were held in Week 7 after recess week. GCs are allowed and you should use them especially for Gauss Jordan Elimination questions cause although you must do it by hand, having the GC allows you to check your answer so if you made a mistake you'll know how to rectify it easier. I'd recommend getting the tinspire like the ones they use in the IB because I find calculations with it for matrices are much easier and beginner friendly. If not the ones they use for A levels also works. Do remember to watch YouTube tutorials for how to use it because they don't teach you this during lecture. Essentially you should be using your GC to do computations because it really is very easy to make careless mistakes. Like how Jurgen Kloop said If your name is not Lionel Messi you have to press. Same here, if your name is not Prof Ng Kah Loon, you need to use your GC for midterms. I didn't use my GC for midterms and got 6/20 for it, could've gotten 12/20 if I had done so. Essentially don't learn from me and actually use the GC.

Finals (60%): Tbh at this point things were looking bleak and like Gan Fah Fatt said in ST2131, my fate was in God's hands already. Was thinking like how everton could get relegated in almost any season, I could also fail this mod. But like Everton, I was looking to save my ass once again and did whatever I could to do so. Prof Ng will upload some past year papers (he gave 5 I think) but they didn't have the answers. If your prof does not give you past year papers, go find them yourself or ask in the MA2001 wide group. Can ask your TA also. Mine was actually in Math society. Like I said, you can get them from the Maths society as they regularly create solutions to the midterms and finals. However, ensure you don't just copy the answers but get ideas and implement them yourself in your attempts before doing the corrections when referring to the full answers. Like prof Ng says just attempt, it's ok if it's  wrong, just learn from it. Would recommend you start from the most recent finals and work your way back. Would recommend you start doing past year papers minimally 3 weeks before finals if you're strong and as early as before midterms if you are weak. Doing 1-2 questions daily would help you greatly. I left it till 2 weeks before finals to do the past year papers and with my weak foundations it was hell. I printed 7 finals but I only did 3 so was quite disappointed in myself for it. Finals had about 10 questions where 70% were computation and 30% were proving. Struggled during the finals due to time crunch but knew I did my best with the time I had. Regardless, I felt better prepared than for midterms and now that I was using my GC, I think I performed much better for finals. Prb around 27/60 which i think is a great improvement from midterms. Could've been better if I started post midterms but that was my fault. :(

There is also a saying that you can do only one question, get part of it wrong and still get a D. But I think that is only if you passed midterms. If you failed midterms you can't use such tactics. You'll actually fail.

Essentially, MA2001 was probably the worst module academics wise for me and it has left me wondering if I should even continue with data science after my trash performance in it. I would recommend people who want to get decent grades like B to keep practicing and clarifying your doubts with people who know their shit because if not you will be in deep shit. Just be shameless and ask questions no matter how stupid. Better to be stupid before the exam than during the actual exam. Also do past year papers often, I find they are more relevant than the tutorials. Prof Ng may say otherwise but imo he will also design the finals so that people can get 40% and above and those stuff is standard like the things you can find across the past year papers. Difficult module but what can you expect for something that is only taught in H2 Further maths whereas I didn't even have H2 maths background (I took MA1301 so you can see my review on it also, shameless plug. :) ).

Expected grade: F 
Final grade : D (Better than expected but still dogshit, don't listen to people who say can do 1 qn in finals and get C, I actually know people who have failed this mod).

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 2 
Lecturer: Prof Godfrey Yeung 
Expected Grade: B 
Final Grade: B+ 

Workload to pass (D grade): 1/5 
Workload to do well (B+ and above): 3/5 

Overview of Module: 
This is a revamped GE2101 where the previous editions were considered to be more fluff and less technical than this edition of GE2101. To elaborate, the module utilises the Jamovi software which is regarded by Prof Godfrey as a simplified version of R. Hence if you have take a variant of CS1010 or DSA1101/ST1131, which uses R programming, this module would be much easier. The module's objective is to teach the statistical methods and practices in data collection in Geography and I would like to think that Prof Godfrey does it excellently, albeit there is still room for improvement given it is the guinea pig batch. Still, he did an excellent job in designing a beginner friendly statistics module for Geography and limited knowledge is required to do well in the module. Just a good attitude and an open mind. 

Lectures: Lectures are held weekly and are non compulsory however, I would encourage students to attend these lectures as prof shares important information during them not covered in the slides. They are recorded also but I'd recommend you go for the live session because his accent is very thick to the point of being incomprehensible in certain scenarios. Do stop him if you can't understand things. He is more than willing to clarify doubts and repeat himself. Prof Godfrey will go through important concepts during the lectures and also have some mini quizzes to test your understanding. These are not graded but are good learning checkpoints. I initially did not attend them because they were boring but after 2 skipped lectures I felt bad and attended the rest. Realised Prof Godfrey is actually good and knows his shit. Shame that most of the cohort did not echo this sentiment and the attendance for lectures dropped to 20% after a while. If you don't want to attend the lectures just look through his slides and ChatGPT stuff you don't understand to make notes, similar to HSI1000. It will suffice for this course and the quizzes discussed later. 

Tutorials: Held once every 2 weeks. Each tutorial Prof Godfrey will give a crash course on the lecture content. Afterwards, he will go through the assignment handout and the remaining time is spent discussing and doing the assignment which is due a week from your tutorial slot. I initially picked the Friday tutorial slot but decided to attend the Tuesday one and had 10 days as opposed to 7 to complete the assignment. Jose Mourinho tactics right there. The tutorials are not graded so you can do this but I'd recommend attending them because you'll get hands on experience using Jamovi software and it is best to play around with it and discuss with friends to get the answers. 

Assignments  (55%) : As mentioned above there are 4 assignments, Assignment 1 weighted 10% and the remaining 3 weighted 15% each. Although you are given a week to finish it you can actually finish it in 2 hours if you collaborate with your friends to get the answers. Afterwards complile yours answers in a Word document and copy them down into Examplify as it will be used for submissions. The Assignments are quite straightforward, most of the questions are like in GE2215 (Intro to GIS) where you just need to follow the instructions and you'll get the answers. Some questions may throw you off but it's usually to differentiate the B+ from A- and above. There are also some long answer questions also to do the same but focus more on statistical theory ie when to reject null hypothesis etc for example. 

Quiz 1 (20%): Quiz 1 covers the content from Weeks 1-6 and will be held on Week 7 lecture slot. If you pon lectures, please remember to come for this and check the announcements of when the lecture quizzes are. They largely comprise of MCQs and some long answer questions and are open book, similar to HSI1000. Submission is also on Examplify.  The quiz is 30 minutes and afterwards prof will continue with the lecture. You'll see the attnedance go from 100% to 20% in a matter on seconds. Quite entertaining. 

Quiz 2 (25%): The final Quiz is held on Week 12 but check the announcements and covers all the content. It is similar to Quiz 1 but may have some curveball questions ie for mine it was "What is the least improtant statistical concept you learnt in GE2101? Explain why.". The final quiz is 45 minutes and the rest of the time is lecture and once again the lecture attendance goes from 100% to 20% in a matter of seconds. 

Overall I think that in spite of it being a guinea pig batch, this module was very well designed by Prof Godfrey who intends to make a beginner friendly statistics module for Geography students. Don't be worried if you think your statistics is bad, if you go in with an open mind and the willingness to learn, Prof Godfrey will help you and you can do well in the module. He also makes very hilarious comments like "People use the important concept question to criticise the module" or "I tried interviewing people in Hong Kong but the first hour they just talk bout how they hate China.". Very amusing. 

Overall rating - 8.5/10
thread_title: GE2101 Methods and Practices in Geography

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Sem 2
Workload to pass (Get D): 2/5 (This is really the bare minimum cause language mods have relatively high workload, even to just pass I would say it is akin to the workload to get a B- for an average mod). 
Workload to do well (A- and above): 4.5/5
Taken Under: ?aacaan (this is thai for teacher) Rungnapa 
Expected Grade: C 
Final Grade: B 

Overview of the Module: I took this mod as I was doing my RC mod in ST1 and needed to find another module to do. Plus I was always interested in learning Thai and I heard language mods are very fun from my friends. Granted they are fun but the learning curve is quite steep especially for Thai where the alphabet is complicated (we only learn 27/44 so go figure for the future Thai mods how hard they can be) and the tones are also difficult (mainly cause the only other language other than English I speak is Malay so it was a steep learning curve but for people who learn tonal languages maybe not so much). The workload is quite intense mainly as there is a lot of things to submit ie Homework Assignments, Oral Assignments, Listening Comprehension, Writing Quizzes, Midterms and Finals (Finals is in Week 13 before recess week). If you don't keep up with the week's content it is very difficult to do well and you may even fail if you don't study at all. However the Thai department is very dedicated. They treat us like their own children and provide ample guidance face to face or over Whatsapp/Telegram. If you have any questions or difficulty feel free to reach out to them over these platforms and they are more than happy to help. Really am inspired by them. I even recommended my teacher for a teaching award. Also, I find it is one of the better language mods as there are fewer smurfs in it compared to Korean/Japanese language mods. So if you're interested in learning Thai just go for it. 

Textbook: The one major negative of the course is that you need to get the textbook and it is quite expensive (40 for 2 books). If you are tight on cash don't purchase it. Feel free to reach out to me, I can send you the PDF. My teacher was ok with it but I think if your teacher is Titima best to buy the book cause she is the author of the book and I don't think she would appreciate piracy as she gets royalties/commission for every book sold. 

Assessement Breakdown: 

ASSESSMENT

 

Your grade for LAT1201 will be determined based on your scores of 100% CA (Continuous Assessment) as follows:
 
1.  Tutorial (E - participation)   = 10%
2.  Homework Assignment      = 20%
3. Written Tests                       = 50% 
    a) Quizzes (Listening and Writing Quizzes)  = 20%
    b) Mid-term Test                                             = 15%
    c) Final Test                                                     = 15%
4. Oral Tests                          = 20%
                                                              
Total for CA: 100%

The Assessments are listed above but I'll go through them below. 

Tutorial: 2 times a week 3 hours each. The first 2 hours is conversational Thai ie speaking in Thai, forming sentences etc. The last hour is spent doing Thai writing. The textbook is used heavily here so it will be your "Bible" for the course. Take time to prep before class so you won't be lost. This can be done by reading the textbook and doing the exercises (fairly simple if you follow along). The first few lessons is basic stuff like introductions, directions, comparisons etc. Don't be afraid/shy to speak in class. Like my teacher said you only get better through practicing/speaking Thai. The teaching team is also very nice (as per my friends from other classes) so don't be embarrassed. My classmates are also very friendly and helpful and you all get through the course through working together. Also the learning curve becomes more steep after recess week so build a good foundation. For Thai writing they start with basic stuff ie mid consonants and progresses to low and high consonants. Thai writing is very difficult so practice it regularly. I think once a week outside of class is the bare minimum. Do the practices in the writing book and send to your teacher via whatsapp, you can do this for the conversational thai exercises also. They encourage it. This will help for you Oral and Writing Quizzes. The conversational segments were very amusing and they allowed you to explore your creativity by creating funny/whacky sentences. ie Brad pitt is very handsome etc. I look forward to the classes and they really are the most memorable parts of the week. Also remember to speak for class part (easy 10%). The final part of the class part is a Thai cultural presentation. You and your team of 4 will do a 10 minute presentation on a topic about Thailand chosen by your teacher (ie Thai games, places in Thailand, Thai food etc). Very fun and make sure to talk alot for it to get higher marks. 

Homework Assignment: 5 Homework Assignments. 4 of them are exercises using Romanised Thai released on Canvas comprising of fill in the blanks, sentence forming, Creating questions and Answers etc (can msg me if you want an example). If you follow the lessons and od the exercises in the textbook you will be able to do 70% of the homework. The remaining 30% is more difficult stuff not covered much/only briefly in class. For this collaborate with your classmates to find the correct answer/direction, especially for Fill in the Blanks as there is only 1 write answer. Don't need to worry about Thai script as it is in Romanised Thai (ie writing Thai but using Latin alphabets kind of like Hanyu Pinyin for Chinese) so make sure you write it properly especially the tonal marks. You are given 3-4 days to complete it but since they release it on the weekend it may be a rush during the work week. You'll submit it via pdf to your teacher's email. The last Homework assignment  (Homework 5) comprises of 7 writing practices to be done OTOT throughout the semester. This is literally a free 4%, just spend a day a week doing it and you'll get the free 4%. It's also good practice for writing quizzes. Don't need to be fully correct also. Just need to attempt it, it is enough for 4%. 

Grades for Homework - Above 30/40 for Homework 1-4 and Homework 5   full marks.  

Writing Quizzes - There are 3 Writing Quizzes throughout the semester, one before recess week and one after. If you do the 7 writing practices and exercises in the book it is quite easy. I only did it religiously after recess week so my first one was bad. The last quiz was combined with the Finals. 

Grades for Writing Quizzes: 23/40, 35/40, 12.5/20. 

Listening Comprehension/Quiz - Same like in Secondary school, there will be a passage in Thai read and you must answer questions (MCQ mainly) based on the passage. There is also a part where you must correctly identify tones read. The passage section is read quite quickly so you must pay attention to what is dictated and the tone identification (counts for 50% of the total marks) is a giveaway. Make sure to listen carefully. 

Grades for Listening Quizzes - 16/20, 13/20

Oral Tests - There are 2 Oral tests and it involves working with your partner to come up with a 10 minute conversation. The first one is after recess week and you'll get to choose 1 of 5 topics. You'll be assessed during class time by your teacher for the first one. The second one is much harder and of higher percentage and will be done in week 11/12. For this you'll have to prepare for 5 topics and they will randomly draw the topic on the day itself and you will have to have the conversation in fornt of 2 other teachers. To prepare well for this make sure you work well with your partner, if they are slacking make sure they do the work and practice regularly. Send voice recordings to your teacher to make sure you are on the right track. Me and my partner did this and as a result I think we did decently for it. 

Midterms and Finals: Midterms and Finals are similar to the homework assignments 1-4 in terms of format (ie Fill in the Blanks and Sentence construction). However, instead of out of 40 it is out of 50 and instead of 3-4 days to do it you'll have to do it in 1.5 hours. However, I think due to the time constraints they are more lenient in terms of whether or not you write the tonal marks (ie miss a few you'll still get the full marks) but don't miss too much such that the entire meaning is changed. To prepare for Midterms and Finals, I'd suggest redoing your homework exercises as the stuff tested is very similar to what is covered in the homework. No point going through the entire textbook (7 chapters) other than to refresh quickly. The midterm is held during class time (the class right after recess week) and the finals is held in week 13 (they choose a date to attend) and is combined with the final writing quiz (ie 20 mins writing quiz, 1.5 hours for the final paper afterwards). All are in Romanised Thai, other than the Thai writing section for finals. If you redo your homework should get at least 30/50. 

Grades for Midterm: 36/50
Grades for Finals: 35/50

Additional stuff: Thai Welcome Tea - The teaching team and sneior students will plan a welcome tea near the end of the semester featuring Thai Games, Trivia (Kahoot games), Thai food and Music also (I can send a video if you want just reach out to me). Very nice. Great success. 

Overall, although this module had a high workload (almost comparable to CS1010), it was one of the most enjoyable modules so far. Being able to work with a dedicated teaching team was very enjoyable and I looked forward to classes every week. The fact that most of the teaching team is nominated for teaching awards is testament to that. I am also considering doing Thai 2 because of this. You can also get a language cert if you do Thai 2 and it is worth because it just builds on Thai 1. 

Module rating - 9/10 (Having to purchase the book and the workload prevented it for being 10/10).

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
AY24/25 Semester 2
Lecturer: Prof Gan Fah Fatt

Expected grade: F 
Final Grade: C 

Workload to pass (ie D minimally): 3/5 (Prof says he will try to shift the curve to pass everyone ie D and above so it is very hard to fail). 
Worload to get A: 5/5 

Overview of the Module: 
Having watched a lot of movies about gambling such as the Hangover Part 1, the Kevin Spacey movie, People betting on the Champions League Winners (PSG won this time), the 2022 World Cup (scripted), I was very excited to take this module. However, all these burst into flames following the very first lecture with Prof Gan Fah Fatt. He is retiring this year (finally I think) so he was in ORD mode the entire semester. This did not sit well with the students however because it was a lot of self studying. He encourages self studing also in his own words. So the module may actually be taken over entirely by the Maths department for Sem 1 and 2 instead of split between Maths and Stats department. Or maybe they'll ask prof Daisy to take the module, she's actually a good prof imo. The lectures were superficial, the tutorials difficult and the exams more ridiculous than the Fast and Furious Movie franchise nowadays (they went to space. Complete nonsense). There were moments I thought that I would fail this mod but like how goals are like ketchup and how red bull gives you wings, I either win or learn. But I never lose and hence I will never fail. The following are more details about the module. 

Lectures: There were weekly lectures, each 2 hours long. They are mandatory to attend because there are lecture quizzes (each contributing either 2% or 4% based on the progression of the module). The prof will open it on canvas during the lecture. Although he says no discussion, we just went ahead and discussed because we didn't care. Essentially don't be dumb, discuss the questions. Chat gpt and Deepseek AI helps alot also. The prof recommended using them during his lectures also. The remaining part of the lecture was spent on him going through the "hard" problems in the lecture notes. However, his explanations were quite nebulous/superficial and me and my friends along with 90% of the lecture theatre just walked out when he goes through the problems as we found it is better to learn the content outside. I do give him credit for his humour. For example when drawing a tree diagram he says that "It does not look like a tree so I will add a trunk.And it has to be a strong trunk." He then goes on to draw a trunk and the whole lecture theatre sits there in confusion. Another instance was when he talks about how he used to climb Bukit TImah hill daily and see monkeys talking about how they need to be tagged to estimate the population of them and how common gambling knoweldge is required in the course saying it is common sense to know gambling terms (ie the 52 cards and how many in each suit etc). I don't gamble because I'm Muslim but yeah go research about it if you don't. He also talks alot about God and how he is fair so because of that he keeps gambling and buying 4D in hopes that he wins it. Saying he practices what he preaches. Very humourous. On top of that the Quizzes became 4% towards the end because he got Covid halfway through and had to make the final quizzes 4 qns instead of 2. This is in his own words on Canvas. 

1. There will be 4 quiz questions on 24 March, to make up for the lost quiz on 17 March. 

2. The quiz will begin at 6.00 pm and stop at 6.15 pm. 

3. You do not need to be in the lecture theater in person to take the quiz because the questions will be released on Canvas.

4. Lecture will start at 6.15 pm. 

5. The 4 quiz questions on 24 March will be based on the lectures on 17 and 24 March.

P/S: For students who are concerned with my well-being. I found out that I got Covid (second time for me). My fever has subsided and I am resting well at home. (He also sends a screenshot of his Covid test results). Very interesting. 

Quizzes (20%): I described a lot of it above but essentally overall it is 20% and almost everyone gets the entire 20%. According to the Prof you can skip the quiz if you have a valid excuse ie sickness, competition or the most outrageous one "getting married". A friend of mine told him his entire family was in the hospital and the prof excused him. Turns out my friend was at KFC. Shocking. But yeah if you miss the quiz just email him with a valid excuse. Good if you have proof also. 

Tutorials: Weekly non graded tutorials where the TA will go through the questions and answers. There are a ridiculous number of questions and most of them are very difficult. I go to the tutorials to see how they do the questions before going back to attempt them myself. The prof sends the solutions but they are quite vague or unintuitive. I usually end up asking my TA or my friends. Also pro tip is to go around different tutorial slots as your TA may not be the best for you. I found this out in week 8 when I missed my tutorial slot and went for another one and the other TA was much better. Great success there. 

Midterms (20%): 20 MCQs and a lot of them are based on the Past Year Paper he gives not much so the tutorials at least for me. Barely passed it though but I attribute it to my weak Maths foundation. Still a pass is a pass. I think if you want to do well ensure you finish all your tutorials before recess week and start grinding PYPs. He only gives one though but I think if you ask seniors they'll be able to give you more. 

Finals (60%): There were 5 questions each 12%. Prof gave 3-4 PYPs and I did 2 of them. In the finals I ended up writing symbols half the paper and the remaining half basic probability knowledge. Still I gave it my all so no regrets there. Similarly best to complete all your tutorials by week 12 and start grinding PYPs for weeks 13 and recess week because the PYPs really are very hard and time consuming. 

Overall, this module was a nightmare. Many times I thought I would get an F but thankfully I did not. Still, I did learn alot from self study and my friends and not really the prof. But he is retiring and I wish him all the best. We gave him a standing ovation in the final lecture (I think I still have the video can pm me if you want it). He is also legendary for trying to pass everyone. :)

Overall rating: 4/10

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Semester 2
Lecturer: Dr Robert Lieu Zi Zhao 
Expected Grade: B 
Actual Grade: B+ 

Workload to pass the mod (ie min D): 1/5 
Workload to get A: 4.5/5 (though there's luck also) 

An Overview:
Having taken HL Biology during the IB, I selected this module because it sounded as though it covered interesting and integral concepts such as cell based proteins and how to integrate them into our lives for a sustainable future. Although this may have been the idea, I was in for a rude awakening. This module was an abject failure at teaching students about the aforementioned topic. Instead it dived a lot into trivial things such as how to get influencers to promote cell based proteins and how such proteins taste. Granted some of it was useful but it was only 10% of the module and the remaining 90% was utter rubbish. Moreover, there were many assessments and all of them were more unclear than the ending to the movie Inception (Go watch the movie though it is very good unlike this course). More will be covered in the following segments. 

Lectures: There were weekly 2 hour non recorded lectures. They were mandatory as there will be poll everywhere quizzes that counted towards participation. I'm not even sure how participation was graded but according to my TA as long as you participated in 60% of the poll everywhere quizzes you'd get the max points for that part of class participation (it is even more nebulous after this so bear with me). I'll give credit where it is due, Prof Robert really does his best to engage the class and make the content understandable to people who don't have HL/H2 Biology background but when it comes to the execution of lectures if I speak, there will be fire and it is here now. His lectures involved screening videos about people trying the meat (ok I guess) but moves on to talking about trivial things such as how influencers can promote the meat and how he got students from Hong Kong to try the Impossible Burger at Burger King. He skimmed through the actual details of the production process but I guess it is difficult to explain to people without prior knowledge. Anyways, it was the going off topic that really made me more confused and resort to watching youtube or doing my other assignments during the lecture. I felt bad and tried to pay attention but realised it was useless so I just went back to doing my assignments. Jose Mourinho tactics from me I guess. Great success. Essentially only go for 70% of the lectures to participate afterwards you're better off just skipping them. In the end of the sem only 20% came of the lectures, so little that even I did not show up for the last lecture. Like Arsenal in the Champions League/Erling Haaland in big games. Man really needs to stay humble. 

Quizzes: These were post lecture quizzes but they covered stuff not even in the lecture. Shocking. The pre recorded videos helped a bit and maybe some of the slides but essentially they were very unclear and you'd need to spend alot of time looking through it to get the answer. Instead, I'd suggest discussing with your friends, quite sure they'll be happy to help, to get the answers or if you have no friends then upload the slides to Chatgpt and ask the questions there. I missed 2 quizzes cause of how nebulous this module is but like Kanye West to his opponents, I don't really care. 

Tutorials: If you thought the quizzes were off tangent to the lectures covered, this was out of the universe. Tutorials were a continuation to HSI1000 ie Using BDTK to detect whether or not a source is nonsensical and the use of Bias in the Media. Honestly because if was completely different from the lecture content, and related to HSI1000 (a well executed module), I actually learnt relevant things here, more so than the lectures. You'll be split into groups of 4 here and you get class part by answering questions and filling up in class slides collaboratively. Quite fun.  

Assignment 1: Involves writing an essay analysing the different media framing in the framing of cell based proteins. It was 2-3 pages long. For this just use your tutorial materials and chatgpt to write the essay. Can be finished in 2 days if you're on task. Tbh just get it out of the way as it is not worht investing too much time into it. 

Assignment 2: This is the group project where you'll work with your groupmates to create an infographic about cell based proteins and integrating them into popular local dishes. I liked the idea of the assignment as it would teach us to apply the knowledge we learnt from the lectures but here's the catch. We learnt nothing from lectures. So it was a lot of research, chat gpt'ing and collaboration to get through it. Like the Rock Dwayne Johnson says: I"t is about drive, it is about power.".  My group was solid so it was enjoyable to do this. 

Assignment 3: The final assignment was about stakeholder analysis in promoting the adoption of Cell Based proteins into a certain group/demographic in Singapore. Similar to Assignment 2, it was to apply "knowledge" but once again there was no "knowledge" from lectures so once again a lot of self learning and research. There were three parts and one of it involved an essay segment and the remaining 2 parts were in powerpoint. However you could only submit one document type, creating even more problems. But instead of giving up, I thought of what my idol the GOAT Cristiano Ronaldo would do and I got the answer, which is more than I can say for the lectures. I copy pasted my essay into a powerpoint presentation along with the other parts and submitted it. Great success and I hit the SIUUUU after submitting it once I got back from JB. 

Peer review: Just do it on teams I think it is 5%. I gave my group members all the full score and I'd like to think they did the same also. 

Overall, this module was absolute garbage. I give credit where it is due and said the prof tried his best but there really needs to be a revamp here because I found this module more nebulous, confusing and frustrating than watching trash clubs like Manchester United or Tottenham Hotspurs play. It invoked anger in me I only experienced in National Service when we get knocked down during SBU. It made me infuriated, like how the social media community feels whenever Kanye West posts something on Twitter (it is X now) or when Donald Trump won the elections. It made me question how this could even be considered a properly run module similar to how I question how Barcelona operates when they are 2 billion dollars in debt. Essentially, this is one of the worst modules I have taken. Good prof, I guess but garbage module. Please do consider taking another HSI2k if you don't want to be conned. Only good part is the link to HSI1000 and that there are no exams. Everything else absolute trash. But what can I say? This is HSI2004 heritage. 

Rating: 1/10.
thread_title: HSI2004 Cell Based Proteins for a Sustainable Future

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 1:

Workload (to SU) - 0.5/5 
Workload (to get B+) - 2.5/5 
Workload (to get above B+) - 3.5-5/5
Graded components 
ILAs (5 in total) - 5 in total (5% × 4 (Segments1,3,4,5 and 25% for Segment 2)
Team Based Workshops (TBW)/Tutorial - 25% (5% per tutorial)
Design Thinking Journal- 30%

Review:

ILAs: 
The course is structured around identifying problems with the design of things and how to better improve them where you'll be tasked with creating a prototype to solve the problem you originally identified by the end of the course. The ILAs will build up to it, each of them graded. Segment 2 ILA is 25% whereas the other 4 are 5% each. The ILAs are due every alternate Friday at 1200 so do take notes of that. Some of them are quite time consuming like the first one where you need to brainstorm alot of solns to problems but I think if you focus can prb finish in 1-2 days max of the given 14 days. Tbh not advised to spend too much time on it. Also the tutorials will point you in the right direction to answer the ILAs so rmb to pay attention during them so you can do it easily. Also be explicit and straightforward to the point when answering the questions. Don't bid around the bush too much.  Grades are EE (Exceeding expectations), ME (Meeting expectations) and BE (Below Expectations). I got ME for most.

TBW/Tutorial:

Every alternate week. Felt like a good time to just go and enjoy ourselves. May differ from person to person but we usually played games/discussed about the week's topic and how to structure our ILAs or prototype. It was very insightful and my TA was very good. Do participate actively to get class part. And some of the TBWs will literally help you find the solns to your problem/what prototype you need to build.

Design Thinking Journal:

The final part of DTK where you reflect on how the design thinking process and course went and create a video summarising all the reflections. I just reused all the reflection segments from the Past ILAs and made the video in half a day as I had other stuff to handle at that time. The previous ILAs will build up to it so don't need to think too hard to come up with something if you're pressed for time.

Overall it was a good course contrary to what most people say and I really did enjoy it and the principles it taught. Also learnt how to work with cardboard.  8.5/10.

Expected grade: B+ (I just cruised through the course so wasn't expecting much).
Actual grade: A- (Pleasantly surprised, don't need to used another SU)

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message: Taken in AY 24/25 Sem 1 Workload: 5-6/10 (Do take this mod concurrently or after doing CS1010 as it makes learning much easier especially for loops) Assessment/Grading Criteria: 5% Tutorial Attendance - Attend all tutorials to get 5%, come to class to clarify concepts and go through tutorial Qns (Onsite and Offsite),. My TA was really good so it was fun to go for tutorials. (Got the full 5%) Grades: 5% (Full) 5% Canvas Quizzes (New for this Semester) - Weekly Quizzes regarding Lecture Content on R. Serves as a good way to revise content and ensures you watch the pre recorded and recorded lectures. Discuss with friends to get the full socre. Grades: 5% (Full) 5% Datacamp Assignment - 2 Assignments on Datacamp which can be done in half a week. Its intro to R and some intermediate R also if I remember correctly. Grades: 5% (Full) Assignment 2 (10% Statistical Report) - Using R and the Machine Learning Methods to analyse a given dataset and writing a report on it. I think it is a 6 page report if I remember correctly. This is probably the most tedious part of the course and you're given 2 weeks to do it so best to start early. Discuss with your friends so you can do well in it. Grades: 85/100 ie 8.5% This semester for the exams Prof Daisy said she made it much harder so that the bell curve would be flatter. This was verified by my TA who took a look at our midterms and finals and was stunned it was much more difficult than when he took it (the year before) 25% Midterms - 4 Questions of varying marks and difficulty. Didn't manage my time properly so I flunked it (ie I got 29/60) cause I didn't do 26 marks worth of questions. And I spent a long time trying to find code for R. I think if you don't know how to answer the question, just write down code related to the topic and modify it to suit the question. You'll still get method marks. Just don't leave anything blank or submit blank R file once the exam ends. Grades: 29/60 (RIP) ie 12.3% 50% Finals - Like the Midterms, finals were hard. Most of the questions were related to the assignment 2 code so had to modify them to suit the question. Alot of the questions required deeper thinking and problem solving. There were also MCQs similar to the weekly quizzes. This time, I made sure to attempt every question so felt much better than my Midterms. Grades: Unreleased but prb around (32/50) ie 32% Overall: 67/100 (I think) Overall, the module although challenging at times, was really interesting as it exposed me to different machine learning methods and reinforced my knowledge learnt from CS1010E, this time applied to the R language. Was a really enjoyable module and Prof Daisy is very approachable and an excellent lecturer. Shame that the final grade was not very good but I guess this is one of those crawl so that I can fly moments. 9.5/10. Expected grade: C (After I flunked midterms) Actual grade: B- (Major W considering I flunked Midterms. Felt happier than when Everton avoids relegation and the fans storm the pitch :) ).

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecturers: Wang Fei (I went for his slot) and Bao Huanchen

Assessment criteria: 
21 (18 of the best are taken) x 1% Quizzes (2 times in a week)
2% Attendance (Scanning QR code)
6 x 5% Homework 
50% Finals (No cheatsheet allowed)

Workload
Moderately high to just pass cause of the weekly quizzes and homework (almost back to back). Lecture qns also can be tough.

Very high if you want to get A.

4.5/10 to pass ie get D
6/10 to get B 
9.5/10 to get A.

*Wang fei says that for all the other components (other than 50% finals) he wants to try and give everyone full marks sot thats a good thing. Think he wants to try and pass as many ppl without removing the rigour from the module in terms of exams. Agree fully with him on this as it promotes learning rather than route memorisation.

Grades breakdown: 
18% Quizzes (Full) - Have to thank the TAs and friends for the 90+8 assists. 
2% Attendance (Full) 
28% (out of 30) Homework 
Finals (not released) but I did about 70% of the paper so I'm guessing 50-60/100.

Overall, although I didnt do very well in the module, I really did learn a lot. Prof Wang Fei is one of the best Maths profs and teachers I have studied under. He explains difficult concepts in ways that are easily understandable to people with a decent understanding of Maths. The blended learning style also allowed me to learn more about the topics in depth especially when he goes through the lecture problems,. I find myself rewatching and reattempting the questions to solidify my foundations. He is also very willing to help and makes jokes during the lectures to keep the class entertained. He gives sound life advice also like saying its ok to come late for lecture if you cannot make it on time. Just come and watch the recording afterwards. I feel that contrary to popular belief, if prof Wang is taking the module i'd highly recommend you taking it because you'll learn alot. Plus he tries to give as much marks as possible for everything other than the finals so that's really nice of him.  Only problems I had with the mod was that there was no cheatsheet for finals and some of the pre recorded videos were too long. But overall, a fantastic mod. 8/10.

Expected grade: C 
Actual grade: B

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message: Hi sflr. By alone I would assume you mean not taking w other friends? In that case you can study alone and still do well cause the nature of course is such that science is taught in a very basic way that even ppl w limited science background can do well in it even by studying alone. High chance you'll get a B+ if you're consistent in doing the bare minimum each week. You'll never rly be alone per say cause you'll be doing a lot of group projects during the workshops and exploratoriums and also the field trips. So it's a great way to make new friends and learn more about science in an interesting and relaxed way. The mod was a good way to de-stress from all the other hectic mods I had that sem.

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 2:

Professors: Zhou Li Feng (Weeks 1-6), Alan Cheng (Weeks 7-12) 
TA: Aman

Pre review:
Do this mod in y1 if you want to SU it cause high chance you'll get a B+ unless you have experience in python or programming. Cause you have 5 SUs in Y1.

Professors: 
Prof Zhou is p solid he is abit boring at times but he does his job decently and delivers lectures well. For Prof Alan I think he's a decent person esp after he helped me find my laptop (though some would disagree if you look on Reddit about his policies regarding PE and exams) but when it comes to teaching I won't rly say much cause if I do there will be fire. Feel he is enthu about what he teaches but he links it to many concepts that ppl end up more confused :( so eventually ppl started skipping lectures.

Assessments :
- 7% attendance (Just attend tutorials)
- 4 × 2% (8%) Coursemology Assignments
- 23% Midterms 
- 1% PE 0 (Mock PE)
- 1% Mock Midterm
- 20 % PE 1
- 40 % Finals

I took this mod cause I wanted to switch my major to Data Science and I couldn't bid for 1010S cause of timetable clash. And imo it is better than 1010S cause of how the assessments are structured (more MCQ and fill in the blanks focusing on code tracing rather than hard coding which is only in PE and Coursemology Assignments and a small part of the finals) so the learning curve is not as steep. That being said it is still v steep esp for ppl w little to no programming exp (like me).

Attendance: 
Just attend 7 tutorials and get the free 7%. If you can't go for your pre assigned one just go for another one and get the TA there to mark it for you. It's free marks. The tutorials will also go through the weekly tutorials given on coursemology and you can clarify your doubts with the TA during the 2h slot.

Grades: 7/7 (just attend tutorials)

Coursemology Assignments: 
There are 4 Coursemology Assignments each worth 2% where you will need to code in python to solve problems (2 of them so each is 1%). They will be released on coursemology once every 2-3 weeks and are HARD (more so than the standard tutorial qns). My advise would be to ask your TA or friends for help and solve the qn together. I find the best way is to get a friend who is able to sit down with you and guide you to the soln step by step  Imposter syndrome rly sets in here cause I see ppl being able to solve the problem in 1 h whereas I will take up to a week to solve it (even w help). But at the end of the day it's ok. It's more impt we learn and the more problems we solve the better we become at coding. Same applies w anything in life. Can try and find solns on github though I wouldn't recommend that unless no choice.

grades: 7/8

Midterms: 
Midterms cover the content covered from wk 1 - 6 (before recess wk) and based on past yr trends would be held on a Saturday. It has 23 qns, each one worth 1% and all MCQ. It was 70 mins and rly is a rush so make sure to do the Past Year Papers they give on coursemology/canvas during recess week and once you have done them all accurately try to do time trials to practise w time. The midterms and finals are done on examplify so make sure your laptop works. Try to use python tutor and idle for code tracing if you cant figure out the solns.

grades: 15/23

Practical Exam (PE1): 
Other than the final 2 coursemology assignments this is prb the hardest part of the Mod. For sem 1 there shld be 2 PEs cause Prof Alan is course coordinator then but it is a bloodbath. They will test 3 qns which are the level of the hard assignment questions each one worth 30-40 marks. To prepare try and spam at least 3 yrs of PYP which answers can be found on GitHub or check with your TA if you have doubts. Also make sure you can screen record using the command prompt (prof Alan mandated it and tbh I disagree w his logic cause examplify alr blocks internet) and make sure you correctly copy paste the code or they'll minus 50% of your marks for your qn. Cause of this I flunked the PE and heard about 200 ppl getting 0 marks for it. Just do your best to prep for it cause it rly is hit or miss.

grades:  16.6/100 (major L) -> 3/20

Finals: 
After PE prep for finals was much easier. Like midterms try and spam PYP. Only difference is that finals test stuff post recess week like Recursions, OOP, Higher order Functions (lambda), 2D arrays (hate this w a passion). There is a small coding segment where you have to type out your code though it is much easier compared to coursemology assignments. Just manage your time well and practise and you shld do well.

grades: idk but shld be between 25-28/40

Expected grades - C
Actual grades - B (there's no bell curve but I believe some moderation is still used or alot of ppl will get F).

Final comments:
All in all this mod felt like a literal hell at times making me question my capabilities and decision to switch from Geography to Data Science. Yet as prof Alan says nothing worth achieving was easy and likewise the rigour and difficulty of the mod helps you become a better problem solver and increased your mental resilience and I guess mathematical thinking in some ways. It is also fascinating to learn about programming and the amount of effort programmers put in to develop things arnd us. If you are stuck on a qn no pt sitting there for 5 h just to get a blank screen and no progress (speaking from exp). Ask your friends for help or your TAs or someone good in computing. If you're a returning NSF try to do 1010X instead cause you'll have 6 months to learn the content properly whereas the other variants of 1010 and 1101 have 3 months w more mods so it's more rushed. The mod is meant for engineers so would help in engineering in some ways I think I don't know cause I'm not an engineer :) just do your best and you'll gain a lot from the mod.

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 23/24: Semester 1 
Lecturers: Prof Gretchen/Prof Menusha (another Prof may take it along Prof Gretchen in AY24/25) 
Lectures: Every week 
Tutorials : Once every 2 weeks (5 in total w 2 for human geography and 3 for physical geography though the last one is the group presentation)

Workload - 2-2.5/5
Difficulty - 2-2.5/5 
Enjoyability - 4.5/5 (It is a fun mod)

Assignments:
1. Class Participation - 10% (iirc) 
2. 1.5 K word Human Geography Essay - 25% 
3. 2 × Physical Geography Worksheets (10% each, 20% total)
4. Infographic on Water/Carbon Footprint - 15% 
5. Final Human + Physical Geography Group Presentation - 30%

Overview:

The mod serves as a good introduction to Geography revisiting concepts from H2 and HL Geography. I feel the teaching team did a great job breaking down A level concepts such that ppl who have not taken geography before can still follow along quite easily which is a testament to their abilities as educators. The staff also cares a lot about your personal development and that's a good thing also cause it gives you an all rounded education in a sense. The first half of the course covers Human Geography and is conducted by Dr Menusha. Here there is are 2 graded tutorials where you will learn about geographical imaginations and sense of place and also concepts about orientalism and culture and place making amongst other human geog related concepts. You'll also be taught how to analyse research papers (IE not just reading them but how to properly read them efficiently w/o wasting time (prof Menusha has a v good technique for doing this and her tutorials are v enriching). You'll end off the human geography segment with a 25% 1.5 K essay on placemaking in a community of your choice, similar to Geog IA for the IB students. (this is prb the hardest part of the human geog segment but you shld be able to do it within 1 week). During the human geog tutorials you'll also form groups for your final human + physical geography group project where you'll have to use concepts taught in class to help a group of ppl so choose your group mates wisely. The tutorials are a free 10% if you attend I think.

The second half of the course revolves around physical geography. Here prof Gretchen will take over and you'll learn about landforms, natural hazards etc. I feel prof Gretchen is v enthusiastic and I enjoyed her lectures and the lessons i took away from it. However, not all would agree and though I don't fully agree w the majority I can see some of their points. IE sometimes lectures may go off tangent and may get confusing if you didn't do the pre readings for the course. Ppl started skipping lectures at this pt which I dont agree with since you're alr paying for the course and Prof Gretchen is more than willing to help answer your queries if you have any (she legit helped me w my group project so big thanks to her). Other than that I feel you can Google most of the stuff taught just that the profs can kind of explain it better if you need more guidance so do what you think is best for your learning. This part has 2 graded worksheets to do (out of 100) worth 10% each iirc. You'll also have an individual poster worth 10% I think. Finally you'll have to create an infographic on your carbon/water footprint and how to reduce it worth around 15%.

Finally the last assessment for this part is the human + physical geography group presentation. You and you're grp will have to give a 30 mins presentation where you'll use concepts learned from Physical and Human Geography Lectures to resolve a problem experiences by a group of people. It was pretty tiring at times esp cause it was rushed but I spoke with prof Gretchen and she said she's going to allocate more time for the grp project the following sem (this is the first sem they had it). Iirc it's around 30% of your total grade.

Overall I feel this cause was a good way to bridge the gap for ppl who hadn't taken geography before or last did it in secondary school. However feel that in some aspects it was rather simple IE to do decently in the mod IE B grade, you'll just need to use Google and work w friends. Of course if you want to broaden your knowledge you can read the readings provided (which I did) though I know of friends who didn't even do the readings and got B+ and above (A also). Feel that this trivialises the mod which in reality if one puts in the effort they can reap a lot of benefits regarding impt physical and human geography concepts. That's prb why alot of ppl think it's a fluff mod though if you apply yourself you can prb see that it's not as fluffy and has some insightful takeaways.

Anyways: 
Expected grade: B+ (thought the bell curve would mess my grade up)
Actual Grade:  A (guess doing the readings was worth it :) )
thread_title: GE1101E Geographical Journeys: Exploring World Environments

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Semester Taken: AY 23/24 Sem 1 (Yeah I took the mod with Regan who did the review on this mod also so check his review out also)
Lecturer : Dr Yan Yingwei 
*V impt* Laptop: ARCGIS software used in this course is only supported on Windows and not on Mac so unless you are ok w going to the GIS lab for 3-4 h a week to do the assignment get/loan a Windows laptop.

Expected Grade: B+ 
Actual Grade: A- (Thought I messed up the final but pleasant surprise; only bad thing that isn't rly a bad thing was that I was planning on S/U ing this mod and thankfully we are not in NTU where you have to Pre S/U) (:

Before I begin I feel Regan did a great job explaining the mods assessment criteria and content (shout-out to Regan) so I wont go into it too much (you can read his review for that). I'll discuss about the more miscellaneous matters.

Workload - 3.5/5:  The mod is likened to a v simplified CS1010 variant IE compared to a CS mod the workload is v little but to other mods it'll be slightly above average in terms of workload. There are assignments once every 2 weeks (same week as the labs) and you're given 1 week to complete them iirc (refer to Regan's review for confirmation).

Lectures: Other than that there are weekly lectures that are not recorded so pls come cause prof yingwei will explain the content in depth and answer qns. Plus prof yingwei is unintentionally funny and v well versed w GIS and the software. Ppl may think he's boring but tbh I feel he's one of the better NUS professors. He is willing to go the extra mile to ensure you clarify your doubts if you ask him and actl cares about students' understanding and development, even stopping to have short chats outside of class. Truly a king and a goated prof.

Assessment criteria:

Class Participation (10%) - This involves answering a qn posed by the prof on canvas regarding the GIS software (based on wk 5 and 6 content I think). Also based on how much you participate and attendance during labs. Ask the TAs questions if you need help cause they are the ones grading you. So please go for the labs.

Assignments (60%) (Refer to Regan's post for more in depth coverage): There are 5 lab reports to be done once every two weeks using the Arc GIS software and each has a 12% weightage. Some of the qns are tough but if you follow the step by step instructions prof ying wei gives you shld be ok. He'll also do a speedrun walkthrough at the start of the lab. Lab 4 is the hardest cause of how tedious it is but once you're done w Lab 4, Lab 5 is significantly easier. Don't need to worry if you're not v tech savvy this is the best place to learn and would recommend you do this b4 doing CS1010 variant if you want to pursue a minor in GIS cause it'll help bridge the learning curve slightly when you eventually do a CS1010 variant (for me at least). My advise is that you discuss w your friends and compare answers to ensure that you all scored as high marks as possible. Also if you have any doubts please consult the TAs they are there to help you.

Score for Assignments (55/60)

2h Written Finals (30%). Iirc it was 10 m MCQ and 20 m Structured Essay qns based on the Applications of GIS (what you've learnt in the past 12 weeks plus the concepts behind using GIS). The MCQs were abit tricky so for me I did all the Structured Essay Qns first writing everything I thought was relevant for the qn cause they use positive marking (IE as long as you write the points they are asking for they will give you the marks. They won't rly penalise you if you write something wrong). Just make sure you manage you time well and leave 30 mins - 40 mins for the MCQs (IE 1h 20 mins minimally for structured essay qns). I rushed through it cause saw ppl leaving early but realised they did less than half the paper so I had more time to check.

There aren't alot of PYP cause prof yingwei only introduced finals in the semester post COVID but if you ask him for sample qns he'll be happy to give you one or two and go through them also if there is enough demand.

Score for finals : Think I got 21-23/30 for the Finals

Overall this mod is prb one of if not the hardest 2k Geog mod and would advise you to take this mod as early as possible if you want to S/U it as it is only available in Sem 1 and you have 5 S/Us in Y1. Go in with an open mind and enjoy the process of doing GIS Assignments cause it is really a good skill to learn.

PS Wanted to Thank Regan for also reviewing the mod. Gives the best of both worlds when evaluating a module.
thread_title: GE2215 Introduction to GIS & Remote Sensing

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 2 (Realised that there's little to no review for sem 2)

Prof/TA: Leung Ka Hin

Workload - 3-4/5 (Prb felt that way cause I took CS1010E in the same sem also)
Difficulty - 3.5/5 (my maths is not v good and some content is rushed) 
Enjoyability - 3-4/5 (Prb cause I had to juggle it w CS1010E so it was less enjoyable)

Lectures - Prof Leung has pre recorded lectures (4h long) going into detail about the topic and is a good resource though he does make some mistakes in calculations cause he speeds run it. He also has a live lecture slot every week which he will use 1-1.5 h to go through the highlights of his pre recorded lectures. You don't have to physically attend them as you can also attend them on zoom and are recorded but I like live lectures and can always ask the prof and afterwards cause as he says I'm happy to help. Most of the time only 10 ppl will attend so p conducive envt.

This module serves as a bridging module for those who did not do H2/HL Maths. Unlike Sem 1 which is 40% assessment based and 60% finals, Sem 2's one is different in terms of the assessment criteria (cause different coordinator).  Would say the prof is p good and does a decent job but he does seem to rush through the content (can't blame him though when you have about 1 yr of JC Maths to teach in 3 months). He does make mistakes in workings cause he rushes but he does care and will be willing to answer any qns you have (rly solid guy cause of this tbh).

Assessment criteria: 
Class Participation (10%) - There are Tutorials every week from wk 3-13 and it will be spent going through the tutorial qns. You will have to submit the tutorial to canvas on Sunday and Prof will ask you to present your soln to the class during the tutorial slot (each person takes 1 qn). He will correct any mistakes on the spot. According to him present minimally 3 times to secure 10% and doesn't even have to be correct. Though I'd recommend checking your answers w your friends who are good in maths to make sure you are on the right track cause one tutorial a week is not enough (shoutout to the Engineers, Computer Science, DSA, Stats and Maths Majors who helped me out). I presented 5 times cause ppl started ponning tutorials towards the end (imo not v ideal cause he goes through the solns during the slot).

Quizzes (15%) - Prof Leung will give you 4 open book Quizzes throughout the sem taken during the lecture slot (usually once every 2 weeks apart from week 7 and 13 but check with him when it will be). Each quiz has arnd 4 questions covering that modules specific topic and is out of 20. He will take the best 3 (IE each is 5%). The quizzes has standard and hard qns but if you do the tutorials you shld be able to guarantee at least 12/20. Also if you don't know how to do a qn just write smth that makes sense. He is v generous w the marks and may give you 2 marks if you just attempt it and write something that makes sense. (yeah he's v nice). He doesn't allow GC though but he says can use any other resource. These quizzes will be v useful for finals cause prof Leung will modify qns from the quizzes for the finals.

Score from quizzes - 45/60.

Midterms (20%) - Unlike in sem 1, Prof Leung's version has midterms. There's no PYP so make sure you have a good foundation from tutorials and redo your quizzes cause prof tests similar qns from them. Mid term exam is arnd 1.5 h long. You're allowed a double page A4 cheatsheet.

Score from mid terms - 21.5/40 (Rip)

Finals (55%) - Prb the worst part of the mod is the high weightage final. It's 2h long and has about 9 qns. Prof gave 1 PYP but did not give solns (prb the only thing I disagree about his teaching methods, however he does have zoom consultation for some of the qns). So to prepare do the PYP and Double check w friends who are good in maths. Also make sure you practice w time once you are sure of your basics cause time management is v impt (only did 80% of the paper). Once you do 1 PYP if you have time try to do others found on Holy grail. Would have practised more but had CS1010E to prep for also so took the L for that. Note to self don't take a maths mod w a CS mod if you want to do well in the maths mod. Finals has some standard qns that you can do if you do the tutorials but also some hard ones that one or 2 will be in the quizzes (the rest are differentiating ie to weed out the A+). Once again you're allowed a double page A4 cheat sheet.

Guessing I got about 62/100 for finals (Bombed it imo)

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 2:

Enjoyability - 4.5/5
Workload - 1/5 (to barely pass IE D+ - C+), 2.5/5 if you want to get B+ and above. (Worse case scenario just SU)
Difficulty - 2-2.5(at most)/5

Tbh was initially worried about the mod cause of what my friends told me about it IE can't rly study for it and it's largely common sense and in part they are correct. Then again being a science student in JC it was nice to revisit science and it played a big role in wanting to switch my major to DSA.

Lectures: 
They are pre recorded but tbh don't watch it unless you're rly interested. At most watch at 2 times speed. They are divided into 3 blocks. The first is the nature of science iirc, the second is climate change (worst part and most fluff), the third is on the environment (most goated one). Tbh I actl watched the environment lectures b4 the exam cause I was rly interested in it. Prof Siva (the lecturer for it) is the Goat and Id actl consider taking his LSM mods in the future.

Assessments:

10 Graded Quizzes (10%) - Unlimited attempts so can secure easily.

3 x Workshops (30%) - Honestly these were v fun cause we just went into class for 2-3 hrs and discussed about the scientific method and nature of science. The first one was about BDTK, the second one was about climate change and the last one was about the Environment (6/10 is an individual report that you will do following your group fieldtrip to the nature reserve). You get to work in groups also so choose wisely who you sit with. I sat with my secondary sch and JC friends (from SJI) and my NS friend and another guy I met so our group had a very strong brotherhood and was always a joy to come for class. We helped each other w the report and quizzes also so ez game. grades for workshops - 7/10, 8/10, 10/10 (yeah I was surprised).

2 x Exploratoriums (5% each) - You just do simple science experiments. I think the first one was density and the other one was about heat transfer (can't rly remember). Don't worry if you're not a science person, the TA will simplify it so that even sec sch ppl can understand and enrich themselves learning about science. Grades for explo - 9/10.

Mid terms (15%) - I flunked midterms cause I tried to control F every option and ran out of time. Hurts esp cause the results came out right before my CS1010E mid term (the next day). The mid term made me lose motivation but after seeing my grp mates and watching WWE and Real Madrid perform well we encouraged each other to persevere and 2-3 weeks till finals I decided to study for HSI instead of not caring. My advice is use common sense to answer first then Ctrl F to double check. Fr when reviewing the paper using common sense could have helped me get a 14/20.

Grades for mid terms - 9.17/20 :(

Finals (35%)  - Fr the academic comeback happened here. Decided to study for the mod 2-3 weeks b4 finals and it was only like 2 hrs per week. At this pt just compile all lecture materials and copy paste the transcript from the recorded videos. Then during the exams just use common sense to answer b4 double checking w ctrl F. I think I prb got 26/35 for the finals.

Tldr. HSI 1000 is one of the better CHS mods. Learnt a lot from it (surprisingly) even though I half assed it until the final 4 weeks. The teaching team is one of the best and they actl care about your learning and development rather than solely grades. Plus made v close friends here and it proved that it's not always about the destination but the journey and the company and I'm blessed to have had both. Plus the academic comeback happened. Prb would have gotten a C if I didn't decide to study for it last min.

Mod rating - 9/10.
Expected grade - C+ (right after midterms)
Actual grade - B+ (thought I could go for A- after finals but close enough)

Comments:
Lectures are recorded.
First half of the module is learning about the databases, pairwise alignment and BLAST.
Second half is about multiple pairwise alignment, their respective databases and phylogenetics and genome-wide experiments.

Came in thinking there would be coding haha but there wasn't any coding needed at all in this module.

Honestly sometimes i got abit confused with the content but prof greg is really nice and will take your questions :). It would also help for you to pre-read and look up videos to understand some of the concepts before lecture like substitution matrices (BLOSUM62 vs PAM250 etc) and how to interpret the numbers and like how PSI-Blast works as its honestly difficult to explain these concepts with just words.

Theres some algorithms that are learnt like Needleman and Smith-waterman which everyone should learn how to do manually, and one should also try to know how to interpret dot plot graphs between two sequences.

It'd also be great for non Life-science students to maybe take a refresher on H2 biology before taking this as I had to spend quite some time googling what some terms mean.

Problem Sets: 1 was a pair and 1 was individual. Would really help if you know how to code so u can cross-check ur values to the ones in MEGA. The problem sets are very doable although if you want higher marks, you need to add in extra analysis or do extra searches (like in PSI-Blast) and say things like why some results might be the same/diff in different phylum.

Grades:
Quiz 1-4: 45 / 60
Problem Sets: 28 / 30
Participation: Unknown but probably a 7-8/10?

Final Grade: A+
