author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Dr Joel Chow
TA: Wee Shin Yang Shaun

Participation 10%
Weekly Quizzes 5%
Midterm Essay (1.4k to 1.5k) 27.5% (Recess Week)
Group Assignment 22.5% (Reading Week)
Final Essay (2k to 2.5k) 35% (Reading Week)

The breadth of content in this module is phenomenal. I really mean that, because every week Prof Chow flies through a hundred-over slide deck and it sort of feels like x2 recording speed even though I come for the live lectures (also on Zoom and recorded). Not to worry though, because all 3 major assignments do not need you to know all the cumulative knowledge from each lecture, and you can pick and choose which of the topics you want to expand on in your writing/group project.

Broadly speaking, the focus of the mod lies in the nature of nationality, identifying and questioning its nature under various contexts from knowledge markets to political systems. I seems like the readings are optional because I didn't read any of them and managed to follow the lecture content just fine, given how comprehensive they are (though some of the texts seem quite interesting). Prof times the lectures so well and jams each week's load of content into the 1h35m timeframe so accurately I'm convinced he rehearses it like a performance. I feel the content could do with some curation, but at the same time, it gives a sort of relaxed "seminar" vibe to the module, that you don't need to know and regurgitate everything, but just have a broad sampler of contemporary social science. Seems like a matter of taste. Either way, the tutorials do help to streamline the material a bit with handouts and group discussions (subjective).

What I truly appreciate about his assessment is the flexibility he provides. On one hand, the papers are quite open-ended and he's super open to giving you both suggestions on how to write, as well as detailed feedback (for the midterm paper). The group assignment is also quite out of the box in terms of deliverables (my group did a screenplay text, and others have done podcasts, videos, etc.) On the other hand, deadlines are lenient and you can get a 2-day extension without needing a valid reason. But of course, remember Parkinson's law.

Welcome to PPE! (actually a sizeable number of the students in this mod are from SOC as it clears their breadth requirements so it's kind of a diverse crowd. Not sure how that influences tutorial dynamics)
thread_title: PE2101P Introduction to Philosophy, Politics, and Economics

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Dr Song Moonyoung 
TA: Liu Jiachen

Participation 10%
Quizzes 25%
Midterm essay (1.4k to >2k) 30%
Final essay (1.4k to >2k) 35%

After taking Intro (GEX1015) under Prof Song, I decided to continue to take this mod (besides out of interest) as I appreciate her style of flipped classroom learning. Though it might not be for everyone (slows down the lecture content pace), if you want to explore having proper discursive learning, she provides a lot of space during the lecture for us to sit and exchange views and counterarguments on the thesis of each writer. This made the quality of my ideas a lot more coherent and tangibly held when I can present them, informally every week, and develop my lines of reasoning. Really a soft skills W.

The workload is super manageable. Readings are mostly around 20 pg a week. The deadlines for the papers are for some reason quite early (on Wed W6/13). The good thing is wordcount is only for the lower limit and you can write as long as you want (assuming analysis remains relevant). Take note however that Prof Song’s grading for the essays seems to be not very differentiating so the quizzes are pretty essential if you want to do well. Review both the lecture slides and assigned readings, as trick questions appear often.

If you are an artist of any form or just someone who enjoys consuming art, one unintended benefit I took away from the mod was a new, contingent/grounded view towards how I approach or produce art. By taking away the assumptions and my subjective unquestioned "mental picture" of what art is, somehow my experience of enjoying art is richer now that I don't have to vaguely imagine what art is or is formed through, but I can also take autonomy of what art entails. It's a bit of a vapid idea, but that's my take- feeling like I "own" art better now.

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Dr Timothy Wong
TA: Hoon Yong Zhi, Joseph

Tutorial Participation, Submissions and Quizzes 25%
In-class quiz (W6) 10%
Midterm exam (W8) 25%
Final exam 40%

If you can manage the workload of all the previous core EC-mods, this one should be a breeze for you as the content is not very challenging and requires more logic and clear deduction, instead of math precision. The 3 tests also make assessment a bit more spread apart and Prof Tim lets you take back the exam scripts (excl. finals) for revision, which is unusual from the prior core mods. The workload is quite low with the usual lectures and tutorial assignments (graded twice, group work, accuracy marking). Since the lecture materials are quite general, actually this leaves the exams to be quite open-ended (at least IMO), so the difficulty can be quite subjective because it feels similar to those math and logic questions where if you get what the Q's saying, then you can do the question quite easily but if you can't, you can't.

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Supervisor: Prof Cindy

% breakdown was pretty vague during the assessment but generally the deliverables are:

- Report (5K words excl. models, tables, figures, appendix) (there is an interim report due Recess week but ungraded)
- Presentation (15 mins main presentation + 15-35 mins Q&A)
- Poster (visual summary of the report)
- Reflection (of the individual research process so far, no word count, somewhere in W10 I think?)

I thought the concept of this mod was pretty interesting-  basically independent study for an RC4 module, where you can pick any topic you like, use a systems thinking method for analysis and then just write a paper on it! 

So the process is quite unique, where you independently approach one of the Fellows/RFs during the break on a desired topic. You should be free to pick any topic you like but keep in mind the range of ability your prof can assist you on a more novel topic/domain may vary likewise. (for eg. my paper was on young local gay males even though my supervisor was in the econs-domain)

The recommended workload is quite unclear, loosely between 3-10 hours/wk (idk what mine was but I had an insane catch-up to do during W13 woops).

Don’t worry about prior knowledge/ability -this mod is quite a good platform for you to practice your own research skills, motivate independent studying and of course lots of writing. Having the opportunity to my prof about once every week (inconsistent) to work on my paper is a p rare opportunity, especially at the 2k level.

The workload is definitely greater than the standard SS mod so do take that in mind, though it is quite a novel experience, especially for someone interested in academic research.

Some grievances though- it’s really independent (if you pick your own topic). Very few people take this mod (~3 a sem, barely know what they do and rarely meet). Gotta be p good with the time management from day one.

I know of many (possibly the majority) of Proj SS students taking this mod as there are no official classroom hours (easier for workload/timetable) and not necessarily for personal intellectual interest, so that is any alternate viable consideration you make take in deciding to this mod. (though it explains why there is minimal academic culture within this mod as compared to the 3k mods from the other RCs).
thread_title: UTC2704 Projects in Systems Thinking and System Dynamics

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Prof Isaac Wilheim (Lecturer and TA)

Covered topics: Determinism and Free Will, Ontology and Grounding, Survival of Personal Identity, Groups

The full grade is split equally across class participation, a short midterm test and a final paper
1/3 Class Participation
1/3 W8 Midterm Test (5 MCQs and 2 short typed responses)
1/3 Term Paper (1000-1250 words review of one of the assigned readings)

Papers covered in this module are considerably quite contemporary articles, published within the past century. 

Despite it being listed as a 2k mod, personally, I felt my experience was quite limited as someone with little logic background. The papers explored were quite dense and it can be difficult to engage meaningfully with the material during lectures/tutorials.

Prof Isaac sends out his handouts i.e. summaries of broad ideas in the assigned texts right before lectures, which are quite apt and helpful, though I wonder if they are too oversimplifying.

My main worry with the module is the assessment (interestingly), or the lack thereof. Class participation seems to be merely about (lecture?) attendance which Prof irregularly checks on, the midterm test was too short to be an accurate test of understanding and the final paper was a bit too short to be meaningful.

As a whole, the workload is very manageable but makes it quite difficult to determine your mileage. Discussions were often informally limited within a small group of more engaged peers, whilst the lack of continuous assessment made it challenging for me to ascertain my understanding of the material. I feel I would appreciate this mod a lot more if I had more prior knowledge on the field (the environment was casual but didn’t feel inclusive) and discussions were more guided (loose conversations were freeing but often felt quite unproductive.)

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Dr Loon (Lecturer and TA) (Don’t call him Prof! o.o)

Quite a number of submissions!! In order or appearance...

10% Midterms
10% W7 Performance (small group 2/3)
20% Play Critique (~W8)
10% W11 Final Performance Draft (group of 5/6)
20% W12 Final Performance 
30% Finals

I think many people here have left many detailed reviews already, I’ll keep mine short:

The lectures were so enjoyable in the very small and engaging 20 ppl class size, Dr Loon is really good at engaging with us and the material. I’ve made many many friends within this mod- even frequently meeting up for lunch before tutorial/labs! - something that seldom happens in other mods.

Though the workload seemed high and the performances required some out-of-classroom hours, the process of rehearsals was so cordial and enjoyable that it didn’t feel like an extra workload. The first performance is a re-enactment of a scene from Undercover (by Tan Tarn How) whereas the 2nd is a (pretty open-ended!) 20 min group-devised performance based on godeatgod (by Haresh Sharma). Dr Loon gives good feedback and support, at least whatever was feasible within a 4MC module setting. The environment felt energetic, experimental and understanding even for my peers with no performing experience.

*Such* a fun mod! A great sampler of both (hands-on) performance-making and (more academically-focused) theatre studies. Highly recommended even for those considering just UEs.
thread_title: TS1101E Introduction to Theatre and Performance

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
40% Quizzes (9 in total, so rebased accordingly)
40% Final (25 MCQs in 2 hrs, v. manageable)
10% Class Attendance (just attendance; everyone is entitled to freely miss 1 tutorial in the 10 wks)
10% Class Participation (talking ig)
Lecturer: Prof Moonhyung
TA: Emma

Covered Topics: 
Goodness (Hedonism and alternatives), Consequentialism (and alternatives), Cultural Relativism, Morality and Evolution, All-Perfect God, Free Will, Computers and Computer Simulations

The material is super super super accessible. The workload is very manageable, with just one (usually short) reading along with the weekly lecture, tutorial and quiz. Prof is good at simplifying the paper into its main argument and she conscientiously devotes a lot of time to answering questions and nuances about the arguments, something  many many other profs do not avail themselves to. The best! 

The course workflow is quite consistent- having regular attendance and taking the time to cover the readings are sufficient. The quizzes and the finals (basically one big MCQ quiz) are undoubtedly tricky and generally differentiate the cohort, despite its length and the ample time provided.

I am aware that this mod varies greatly when taught by different profs (along with the topics covered), but at least my experience was very balanced, manageable and enjoyable! This is much to do with Prof Song’s very welcoming and inclusive attitude, something I cannot say is always the case across other philo modules.

Prof recommended not doing any of the past year practices as her questions were quite different and we regarding different topics and texts, and true enough it wasn't necessary- all the content is quite self-contained in the lectures, slides and quiz feedback. (may not apply in future sems should the lecturer change).

author: charlie
message:

20% Class Participation

Felt like the workload for this module was super manageable! Course materials were very apt in this module with the selected readings being not too long and super accessible. The lecture slides are quite concise and clear- I'm able to understand the bulk of the material from the slides before I even start on the readings. Prof Elaine was my TA as well and I can tell she tried to keep the discussions balanced and inclusive (even as it gets a bit scattered at times). Class part matters here however as she does take down the names of those who ask questions in class.

The main focus of the workload lies in the term paper, a case study of a state/regime concerning one of the prior readings. Very open-ended but quite manageable with proper academic research, and preparation, quite beginner-friendly imo (I also used the free PS Peers peer-review platform which is quite useful).

Finals were a bunch of (typed, closed book) essay questions (1 long Q 20%, 2 short Qs 5%) over Examplify. Not sure how critical finals are to the overall grade but I've heard that the term paper is comparatively more differentiating than the exam itself.

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Lecturer: Wong Kwong-Yu
TA: Bian Xiaochen

10% class participation (iirc just once a sem)
20% PS submissions (the usual groupwork)
30% midterms
40% finals

The workload is really light, at least compared to EC2101. Of course, this is very subjective as it varies greatly depending on your mathematical background, but I feel the standard process of understanding the lecture slides and doing all the PS questions (at least attempting them independently) is quite sufficient to prepare for the finals. Time management seems to be a little (not much more) easier as compared to EC2101 (which I took under Prof Wong as well). What I didn’t  like however was the particularly anal “presentation”-marking style where I had to show all the intermediate steps, especially for calculus before obtaining the final answers- unnecessarily tedious, but it apparently makes the overall content tested easier? Cannot confirm that.

Did not attend lectures as I had prior experience on the content so will not comment on that.

A little STATA is covered but not tested at all.

If you are taking this as a minor/2nd major from a quant background this mod is a breeze.

author: charlie
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
30% midterms (mostly MCQ, 1 written Q)
50% finals (essay-ish? style, kinda like H2 econs essays)
10% class participation 
10% quizzes

Prof. Wong Wei Kang (Lecturer and TA)

I think this mod is quite dry. The first half is very similar to EC2101 for all the micro-foundations of the macro model - but that doesn’t make the midterms (which had sufficient time) easy- the average was a fail score. The 2nd half of the course content was more interesting, however, combining all the models and producing a meaningful model that accounts for how fiscal and monetary policy (can) affect macroeconomic variables. ec2101 is effectively a prerequisite as taking this mod without it is just a lot of unnecessary stress for the first half of the term. Lectures were online, which is convenient, but made the experience even more mid than it already was. The PS are sorta helpful in learning but fyi were not submitted nor done in group work (than they usually were). Having the prof as the TA- he's a bit intense at probing questions during tutorials which I didn't find to be particularly effective, though I know he's pretty responsive at addressing queries and doubt.

The midterms were curiously a lot more difficult than the finals, which is basically like essay writing without the lined paper o.o Not sure if that’s good or bad but it’s such a curveball I have no comments about it (though I’ve heard finals had the same format the  previous sem this prof conducted the mod).

But overall, the workload is similar to EC2101. A good sense of logic and clear distinction of the many many macro variables covered are the main skills needed. Whilst objectively the level of math is higher, the actual math used in the course is much lower than in EC2101; Prof generally focused on qualitative understanding instead.

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2.
Lecturer: Tan Ser Peow

Unlike MA2001, the H2 further math advantage is not as obvious although consideraly still prevalent. Topics wise, the only new content covered in MA2002 that most H2 math-only students have not taken in applications of integration (cylindrical shell volume, curve length, surface area) and ODEs (1st order integrating factor), which is the minority of 2002 topics. Skills wise, FM helps alot with general practice on solving integrals so that is helpful in the exam setting.

I'd say 2002 is noticeable different from calculus in A levels through an increased focus on the fundamentals and proof-techniques (delta-epsilon, using differentiation theorems to prove basic derivative properties- imo that hardest aspect of 2002). It's only a portion of the questions however and most Qs still focus on standard application/computational skills (at least under my lecturer) so I found it largely manageable. Spent very little time on the mod due to the considerable overlap with prior experience (1-2hr/wk) but the finals ended up being quite easy so i expect the bellcurve to be a lil insane :0

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2
Taught by: Dr. Wang Fei & Dr. Goh Jun Le

My review is not representative of most other students taking this mod as there is an EXCEPTIONAL advantage from taking H2 FMaths in A levels. I'd say a good 60-70% of the computational and application content has already been covered in the linear algebra section in A levels. That being said MA2001 has some key variations:
- No GC allowed (learn how to do all the ref computations by hand :(. very mundane/tedious actually with multiple 4-row matrices to ref during the exams)  
- Proof-based components (relatively small weightage, about 10-20% for either exam), meaning it's important to actually understand how some concepts link across topics (eg: full rank to diagonalisation)
- Some more topics (mainly transition matrices and orthogonalisation)

This might be subjective to the prof covering the mod but I felt my sem's papers were quite easy and very standard computation focused (which means bell-curve might be quite tight). I don't think that's always the standard across cohorts, however.

Not sure if I am allowed to share this but: math mods (at least the degree requirement ma2k variants) all have a very big module class size and no tutorial grading. Which means: 
- Strategic to save all the maths mods to round 3 in ModReg (you will definitely get it)
- Strategic to put math tut slots at the bottom during tut bidding (doesn't matter which tut grouping you get, you can go to your preferred tut class regardless; usually there is space)

I didn't go for lectures or most of the tutorials as I felt that fundamentally most of the effort really boils down to self-practice and revision. If you did well in linear algebra in JC with a similar background, I estimate the weekly workload can be whittled down to 1-2hrs a wk (excl. the near finals crunch time ofc)

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2
Lecturer: Dr Wong Kwong-Yu
TA: Teh Ren Jie

1hr Midterm 30% (up to Exchange economy competitive equilibrium)
2hr Finals 40%
(all exams were closed-book majority open-ended Qs with some MCQs)
rest 30%- class part, weekly quizzes, PS submissions (seems to include a 5-10% mandatory lecture participation which I believe is via pollev although I'm not sure whether it is enforced)

There's already a lot of reviews on EC2101 over the past few years so I won't comment on what has already been covered since content material remains roughly the same across profs/years. There indeed is a significant focus on math rigour as compared to EC1101E, but personally, I felt that the most noticeable difference is in terms of the scoping of contents. 1101E is like a kacang-puteh assortment of different discrete economics topics across the entire discipline whereas 2101 is wayyyy more focused on the fundamental bottom-up model building of consumer/producer theory, from baseline rationality assumptions to demand/supply, and market equilibrium behaviour. This means following the lecture content becomes a lot more important as the previous lecture almost always builds on the previous week's topics. 

PS questions are now more math-like with fewer word responses and I think for most of us coming fresh out of the characteristic horrors and anxieties of econs exams, it pays to just do all the PS questions regardless of what your group assigns as exam questions are quite challenging (given the time constraint) and practice questions are few. Econs tutorials have felt a lot more crucial for clarifying doubts (moving forth from a largely intuitive 1101E to technically-demanding 2101), so do all the questionsssssss

Besides that, meh, I believe most (core) econs mods are quite similar with the whole lecture-quiz-PS-tut-exams pipeline. Not sure how the class part is computed (no presentations) but imo doing all the questions and keeping up to lecture contents naturally made me already have too many questions that I feel needed clarification in the tutorials.

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2
Prof Marissa (should be the only prof heading/teaching the module)
2x1.5hr tutorials/wk

15% reflective essay (~wk 5)
25% 8 min individual presentation (pitch for research paper) (~wk 9)
40% short research paper (1.5k words) (~ wk 13)
10% 2x peer reviews
10% class part

I've heard this is one of the higher workload IEMs out there and there is quite a strong focus on academic writing skills and paper analysis instead of other domains that other IEMs might explore like argumentative writing. 

Thematically it's about neoliberalism and how it has shaped the self-perception of individuals (of the post-90s financial crisis) globally into marketising themselves as well as traditionally non-market-oriented domains of their lives. They are briefly covered through sociological and political lenses as well through weekly research paper readings 

The first half (before recess) of the mod is quite intensive with many readings which vary quite widely in content and scope whereas the second half is a bit more lax but self-directed where all the major submissions start to pile. That being said, even if the workload looks a bit daunting prof is very accommodating in providing out-of-class support in assignments with weekly office hours and mandatory pre-assignment 1-1 conferencing. 
Class environment is very subjective of course to the makeup. Mine was an interesting near-half split between Tembu and RC4 (where I'm from). The wide range of major backgrounds didn't seem to lead to very divisive discussions, and the environment wasn't too dry nor hostile for most of us even from different disciplinary backgrounds.

Even though I had no background on these topics, it became so meaningful through how the papers reveal the human condition in the modern age. If you're interested in the individualised implications of the political economy, competition mentality and all the sg gover****t social programming, I think it's worth the while :) Do take note that this is ultimately a writing mod so the main focus is not on the contemporary social science discourse but rather the "pen to paper" writing and research skills themselves.
thread_title: UTW1001A Identities and Ideas in Modern Market-Driven Societies

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2

TA: Ricky (seems to be very into migration studies uwu)

Assessment:
20% Group Poster on group fieldwork
20% Individual essay on group fieldwork
30% Individual essay (about personal history)
15% Group presentation (about a tutorial reading/video)
15% Class part
(i.e. half of the assessment are take home essay based)

The earlier topics were quite similar to social studies, though I felt the latter topics on religion were quite new ground in the challenging status quo perceptions of self and community identity via common social markers (eg: race and religion).
Workload is very manageable, and the distribution between group work and individual assignments are reasonable. I didn't spend a lot of time of the essay (sometimes I shows) but I believe it's quite doable even for those without extensive humanities background as the lecture content moves at a very leisurely pace, though research skills can give some advantage in the 20% paper.

Tutorials can be fun but lie the rest of the CHS mods it's mostly a lot of radio s i l e n c e. My TA was quite committed it trying to get everyone to contribute and I believe many of the TAs have a similar approach. Prof mentioned that the grading system for class part is not very stringent (i.e. only assessing talking to the class) but more of a holistic assessment of general engagement.
One of the more fun CHS mods imo (mostly cuz of tut and the group work), even though some of my assignments flunked -_-. Definitely more bearable if your willing to engage and interact with the material (give or take)

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2

TA: Pan Ling

40% group recorded presentation

40% 2 short reflections (graded on a 0/1/2 basis; non-differentiating)

20% class part

Of the 4 1k disciplinary CHS mods (HSI/HSS/HSA/HSH) this was for sure the most lax one, especially since they got rid of the 50% quizzes component. So really the expected study plan is >1 hr recorded lecture, 1 hr live lecture commentary about lecture contents (podcast style), reading short articles to cover in tut, (biweekly) 2 hr tuts. My TA used a flipped classroom format of flashing a long list of open-ended questions on the article's contents. Not sure if that's the standard in other tut groups, but I imagine for many students it must be a bit intimidating/unengaging even if I thought some of the topics were quite interesting. 

It seems to me now that the weekly quizzes are good keeping up on the weekly content no longer seems to be a major requirement besides generating ideas for your reflection/group video. That being said occasionally some of the content can be quite "wow never thought of that before" but not really very academic lol. 

I think it's quite accessible to everyone regardless of arts/science background you just have to be willing to participate. I don't think this is a good mod to want to SU from the start cuz the playing field is already so levelled you might as well save your SUs for other more competitive mods.

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2
TA: Xin Ying

I believe the (100% CA) assessment breakdown has remained the same.

This CHS core mod is the epitome of Murphy's Law, i.e. you'll take as long as the time available to finish any assignment. The biweekly ILA tasks were quite easy to do (especially if you not diligently conducting legit surveys/evaluating tests) that I just rush each ILA task to the last 1-3 days before the deadline. Workload is extremely light with only biweekly tut (in utown, don't be late! they mark absent after 15m) and no lectures.
Personally I think the whole design thinking methodology is quite legitimate and useful even regardless of your major BUT I think execution wise it's quite difficult to really take the insights and the assignments of the module especially when the themes are on topics that most of us have little interest on (domestic chores ??) In the end I felt like I'm just wayang-ing the assignments and fluffing my engagement/participation and not actually engaging with the content.
And not sure what is the "strategy" for excelling in the mod (haven't received grades yet) but so far it seems like the generic class part + writing a lot in the ILAs seem to fare well. This is possibly the only chs mod without exams / major group project/essays submissions.
3 post it notes 📃 out of 5 design thinking helmets ⛑

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1
TA: Kannan

Seems like this a hit-or-miss mod for most people. I thought it was so boring especially nearing the latter chapters. Content is split roughly into 3 sections: the Scientific Method, Climate Change and Biodiversity (in sg). Whilst the workshops might be interesting for those interested in these earth science topics, imo they don't exactly do convincing job making them engaging, esp in the lectures.

This is the only CHS "disciplinary" mod with midterms/finals (HSA/H/S all don't have). It's just an mcq quiz but was completely sidelined by my other major mods. Even though it was open book, there is barely any time to look up for answers esp when there is so much content provided (not that latter lecture no long provide notes). Unlike the previous batches my exam they made the questions a bit more application-style and IRL which made the ctrl-F ing not very feasible and the cheating issue gone. Bellcurve is likely to be less steep now but lol god knows :p Also the weekly quizzes can be very easy to forget so always take a lookout for that, even though they are very easy (with basically guaranteed max score)
thread_title: HSI1000 How Science Works, Why Science Works

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1
Conducted by Prof Elizabeth

This is not to flame anyone or anything BUT we really should just call it Introduction to Vensim cuz that what my experience felt like, 2 hrs in an aircon room watching technical videos on how to use the Vensim software. It's not exactly like very ups ah esp when I look around at other RCs JS mods :" but okay la just CS/CU, you take what you can take from it. If you like the whole systems dynamic thinking and analysis I guess this might be intellectually fun but of those ppl I knew close to none.
Practically, workload is great. Barely any work besides the mini group project which ended up taking more time than the main project lol, but can be done within a day esp when you know where everyone in the group lives lmao. 
That being said I heard the other RC4 JS were even more stonks (eg: everything can be done in class)
thread_title: UTC1702D Thinking in Systems: Population Dynamics

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1
This mods really said ~exposure~ as it crammed so much breadth content into 1 1k mod. I guess...you can say it's a decent bang for your school fees buck, but I know everyone who took this mod as a UE dissolved their hopes of considering a minor lol (see result la huh)

I know EC1101E is not representative on the mathematical rigour of higher lvl EC mods, but if anything it is def representative of the EC dept exam style of intensely time-tight exams and minimal revision practices (know what you know and deal with it- sorta vibes). I guess that helps us curb our obsessively uncontrolled revision plans but ngl it rubs me the wrong way too yikes.

For those with H2 Econs background, part 1 (micro) is muchhh easier content wise than part 2 (macro). That being said, content difficulty != exam difficulty, and I imagine after years of trial and error the dept knows how to balance the variation in examinable difficulties. I thought midterms (fully micro) was so difficult but finals (mostly macro) was much much easier and I even had more time. Shouldn't matter tho cuz bell curve evens everything out in the end lol.

I noticed TA support was very very underutilised as for a cohort of 600+ ppl when I went for zoom consult during reading week office hours, there was 0 queue at all, and barely anyone asks qs to my TA in his teams channel either. If you have any doubts dun care just ask cuz I forsee higher lvl mods having less of this accessibility :0

Lectures were quite skippable imo it started out full house but over the weeks dropped q alot. This mod uses a flipped classroom model so tbh most of the content can be well absorbed if you pay attention to the weekly pre-lect videos (more impt than u think!) and tut (goes thru bonus concepts that may not be covered in the videos).

Was quite meaningful! I learnt a lot. Not always fun but coming from JC econs background I v much appreciate the focus on precision of economic concepts and models, and away from the repetitive wayang that is a lvls essays.

author: charlie
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1.
TA: Nicolas (philo nerd)

Seems like everyone explains the finer details of HSH quite comprehensively. Imma share my personal experience. 

HSH markets itself to be very inclusive to both the science and arts students and that's largely quite accurate esp after every quiz the teaching team will specifically post stats about the statistical insignificance between the 2 cohorts. SO I think it's worth the while as a science kids to at least watch the lectures read the texts maybe go for a few HSH with me sesh if can actually makes the experience quite manageable and interactive for such a god-insane cohort class.
Tbh when I started the mod I was quite on the fence no sure how to benefit from the material but actually there are some texts and views that were genuinely engaging and lowkey like life-transformational hahaha eg: the No Exit play and how we should claim full responsibility for our choices- it got more fun over the weeks.
To each their own la but for sure the workload is amazing. Quizzes were hard but they don't take much time to discuss and do. All the texts can be speed read (if you care for it, not necessary) within an hour, tutorial GDS can be quite fast cuz the actual writing took less time than that the time my group took to agree on a common timeslot lol. Final assignments were also quite doable and could be done in a day if you already have your ideas in mind.
My group got into the meme finals with none of us having experience in memes bfore so either the dept has a really lame sense of humour or everyone was just really bopes about it.

tdlr:.work def less than 4 MCs, go with a open mind it's really not that bad those who complained perhaps didn't read the Gandhi text lol :/

author: charlie
message:
AY 22/23 Sem 1 (only offered in sem 1)
This mod is conducted by Prof Huang (1st half) and Prof Denis (2nd half). 

1st half recaps basic stats and linear, logistic regression, whilst the 2nd half covers a range of ML supervised and unsupervised models. imo the 2nd half is more difficult and easy to snowball if you don't review after lecture. However when it comes to finals the paper was evenly set across the syllabus.

There are no tutorials, basically 2 lectures a week with the 2nd half of the 2nd lecture focusing on R codes instead of the stats of the various ML methods.

I was in the 2nd cohort of this module, which i heard expanded slightly from 50ish to 80ish. Not sure if it's exclusive to DSE students only but everyone I knew was taking that major.

imo the workload was super manageable as there was 0 weekly assignments except in the 2nd half when Prof Denis had weekly R assignments which you can rush within an hour if you understand the R codes provided.

Midterms and finals were much easier than expected especially when I heard Prof Denis had the rep for setting difficult finals so uh oh! bellcurve.

I'll say the main difficulty of this mod is the individual project which distinguishes DSE1101 from DSA1101, which does regular quizzes instead (the econs application actually quite a superficial difference between the mods). Seems like the project grading was non differentiating, but definitely helpful in coming face-to-face with handling actual data, cleaning and basic analysis. Lectures don't cover data cleaning in R, so half the time I spent googling how to clean lmao (but not too difficult! I think most ppl like also last minute the project cuz it was due middle of reading wk).

Super worthwhile, workload is amazing, though class vibes is ded af I thought it'll be better cuz like freshies and smol cohort but guess not :<

The profs tho omg the best esp Prof Huang after midterms she only let us see out results via 1-to-1 consult and she would go through each error individually, which honestly I didn't even think profs (or even TAs in fact) would even be bothered to do wow!

Expected: A
Actual: we do it for the learning ya 0.o
thread_title: DSE1101 Introductory Data Science for Economics

author: awake
message:
Taken in AY18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Henry Chia

Synopsis: CS2030 introduces various programming paradigms to students, beginning with object oriented programming and design principles, followed by some functional programming and ending with a bit on threading and parallelism. The module is taught in Java and assumes no prior knowledge of Java, but a solid understanding of basic programming constructs is required.

Assessment: Comprised of weekly take-home labs (20%), two practical exams (20% each) and a written final exam (40%), all of which are open book. Doing well for the weekly take-home labs and practical exams is crucial in securing a good grade. Note that the labs and two practical exams are not graded by the TAs, but by an automated script developed by the lecturer, which is rather ruthless, and hence having a clear understanding of the requirements for every lab and following them as closely as possible is important. The written final exam is a practical exam in disguise, thus familiarity with Java and having documentation on hand is crucial to doing well.

Lecturer: Prof Henry tends to do lots of live coding demos during his lectures, and his lectures are generally not hard to follow if you know what's going on. Other than that, his administration of the module is a mess this sem, with random cancellations of lectures and tutorials, uncertain TAs due to lack of information from him, and almost no feedback (in terms of marks) for work submitted, so hopefully that improves in future iterations of this module conducted by him.

author: kok
message: Taken in AY23/24 Semester 2 under Prof Olivier Danvy TL;DR: This is my favourite course I've taken in NUS so far, and it was eye-opening in many ways. It'll be offered again in AY24/25 S2 -- you should take it. Admin stuff: - 3h of class per week, usually ends at ~2.5h mark. Usually includes some live proving for the last ~1h (with the Coq Proof Assistant) - 10% Participation and non-mandatory exercises - 30% Weekly hand-ins (there were 7 -- one every week before recess week then 1 in Week 9) - 15% Midterm project - 15% Term project - 12% Oral Exam 1 (around recess week) - 18% Oral Exam 2 (around exam weeks) Weekly hand-ins + projects were done in groups of ~4-8 this semester, though I think group sizes vary based on the class size and simply how students end up arranging themselves. I was in a group of 8. Review: IMO there are 3 things very unique about this course: the way it's delivered, the commitment the Prof has to education, and the takeaways you get from it the way it's delivered The course is very different from a "standard" CS mod in NUS which has assignments + giant exams, and the workload will feel higher if you were previously a lazy student like me who liked to rush things before deadline + never bothered with anything that didn't contribute much to a significant portion of grades. For one, the weekly hand-ins force you to keep engaged with the course, for which you're not just solving problems, but also writing a full proper report in LaTeX where you document your learning, reflect on the exercises, and try to make observations here and there. The report felt like a huge chore at the start, but I eventually realised that the reports are probably the most important bits of work I did for the course in terms of contribution to my learning (which I'll explain in the last section). It's a really nice feeling to be able to track your growth throughout the course and really feel like you're getting better each week. The work isn't really "divisible by X" (where X is your group size), and you should really not attempt to split it up -- they are curated well to equip you with the knowledge and also attitude to thinking that will be important for future exercises. I think it would be very difficult to coast through the course and just "cram" what's necessary once you reach the projects or oral exams. If there is someone who finished the specific exercises + report faster than others (which is natural), I think it's important for the other group members to still do the exercises and go through the process of introspection that they'd have done for the report. That said, the trade-off is that as long as you consistently do what's asked of you, I am certain you will end the course having had a great education about proofs, programs, and proof assistants, and also very likely a great new approach to thinking and learning. the Prof's commitment to education Working in groups may be clunky, but a trade-off is that Prof Danvy will be able to give detailed feedback about everything you submit. He will go through every proof you wrote and every section of your report, including any non-mandatory exercises, and give you comprehensive comments. This is a level of feedback I've not seen before. He's also extremely receptive to questions over email (and probably over Zoom, but I don't think many people in this batch arranged consultations). I suppose you have some motivation to ask questions because there's a participation grade, but he really does provide extremely comprehensive and complete answers to questions even if they are just tangentially related to the course. You can learn a crazy amount solely by emailing the prof. These are just symptoms of the larger fact that Prof Danvy really cares about your learning. It feels like everything from the way he structures the topics in the course, to the style of his notes, to the exercises he curates, are all deliberately made the way they are so that we can maximize our learning and get a great education in both proofs/programs and in life/thinking. It's really impressive to see (especially compared to some other courses where they just throw a textbook to regurgitate in a final exam), and I think his deliberate didacticism definitely worked to give me a better education. He even made custom questions for each person for our 1-on-1 oral exams which were based on previous interactions we had (e.g. through email or submissions), i.e., on our individual participation. o7. the takeaways As long as you do what's required, you will leave this course with good foundations in using theorem provers and reasoning about proofs and programs. This has appeared to be surprisingly helpful and applicable in the real world / in industry even if you never write a line of Coq again -- for example, when I write tests in my current internship, it's helpful to think about the properties of programs I want to test (where previously I would kinda just randomly pick functions to throw random inputs into), and we can model tests off things like commutative diagrams or properties like idempotency or involution. When I begin to write code, it's also been helpful to outline a specification of the program I'm writing, then later ideate over different implementations or abstractions as long as they fit the specification. Lastly, I've gained a lot of useful humility when writing programs even if they have tests -- after all, as Dijkstra said: Testing only proves the presence of errors, not their absence :) In addition to the content, I also learned a lot about how I should learn and generally approach computer science / mathematics. Something Prof Danvy will drill into your head is that you should not be a "problem-solver", but instead a "mathematician" or a "thinker". I did not understand what this meant until a few weeks into the course -- when I was starting to be broadly comfortable with writing Gallina code and Coq proofs, and starting to realise that the more interesting things were how certain exercises linked to each other, how certain proofs looked like other proofs, how certain techniques were same same but different. The course will train you to be a far more more open-minded, vigilant, and observant thinker. Which is pretty unique, and in my opinion pretty awesome. Take it if you can. Notes on Math and pre-requisite knowledge: I took this course because I used to be interested in program analysis for security, and I'd heard of things like "formal methods" as an alternative to techniques like fuzzing. I never knew what it was about except that it was another way to think about program analysis that was more popular in Europe and a popular tool was Coq, so I was excited to see a course "about Coq". I am not someone who is very good at mathematics, and I felt like I was below-average in terms of ability to come up with proofs in general. For what it's worth, I have below-average grades (Cs and Bs) in the compulsory math mods for the CS degree. I did better for CS1231S (A-) but didn't actually answer most of the proof questions in those exams. I was pretty scared about whether I'd struggle considering my relative weakness in math, so hopefully it's helpful for you to know that this isn't a course designed to test you on your math skill, but it's more focused on relating proofs with programs. Frequently, as we went on to prove properties of more complex mathematical constructs, we would be provided with informal math proofs that we just needed to read and formalize. The oral exams also don't test your ability to prove things, moreso your ability to use a proof assistant to formalize proofs and reason about programs.

author: kok
message:
Taken in AY 23/24 Sem 1

It is what it is. You will find it fine if you're comfortable with presentations.

Graded components:
- 20% CA1, Week 5 presentation with random group about a communications-related topic (e.g. Negotiation Skills, Elevator Pitch Skills)
- 20% CA2, Week 11 presentation with CS2103T project group giving a demo of your CS2103T project
- 20% CA3, your CS2103T project User Guide
- 20% CA4, submit a reflection on the course in any format, with suggested possible formats including a infographic, podcast, or video (essentially must pick video if you want to do well) (videos in my class came in the form of skits, mockumentaries, visual novels, mock interviews) 
- 20% Attendance + Class Participation

Grading is not very transparent, I think in an effort to encourage students to care less about grades. We received "feedback written in prose that uses vocabulary strongly mapping to the rubrics" for all CAs, and then raw scores for CA1 and CA2.

Based on a rough reverse-engineering of the feedback to rubric bands, getting the second-highest band (out of 4 bands) for your components with the very occasional highest band here and there is enough to get A/A+.
thread_title: CS2101 Effective Communication for Computing Professionals

author: kok
message: TL;DR More content-heavy than other RC4 mods, but Prof & coursework is super flexible so you can choose to do things you enjoy. Taken in AY23/24 Sem 1 under Prof Maharaj Pandit (Note: This course is cross-listed as UTC2730 or UTS2715, so it can fulfil both SS and SSU reqs for RC4) I've now completed the UTCP, and I can definitively say that this was my favourite RC4 UTCP course. It is a shame that it will probably no longer be offered after this year since Prof Pandit is a Visiting Prof, but I would recommend this for those who need an SS/SSU in AY23/24 Sem 2. You can think of this course as "Introduction to Invasion Biology". No Vensim / CLDs / Stock and Flow Diagrams. Workload: - 3h of class per week, usually ends at ~2.5h mark - 20% Midterm Quiz - 20% Essay (choose from 12 topics or propose your own, 800-1000 words) - 40% Project (1-4 per group. Propose your own or fallback to a proposal centred around a learning from a course field trip) - 10% Attendance - 10% "Forum" (a solo presentation during one of the seminars) Topics (in somewhat chronological order): - History & Etymology of Invasion Science - Ludsin & Wolfe's 10 Conceptual Framework Points + Similarities to Darwin (https://academic.oup.com/bi... - Continental Drift - Southeast Asia + Singapore Biotic History - Global Change / Colonialism & Biological Invasions - Modes, Causes and Models of Biological Invasion - Invasion Science & Xenophobia - Plant Invasions: Soil‐Microbe‐Plant Relationship, Water Loss - Challenges to Biodiversity Conservation - Economic Impacts of Invasives - Synthetic Biology & Designer Microbe Invasions - Singapore's Strategy for Invasions Content: (Disclosure: I took the equivalent of H2 Biology in my pre-tertiary institution, but I am now a CS major through and through.) The topic list make look scary, and truthfully there IS a lot of content that you are bombarded with through the seminars, but only the first 6 topics above are tested in the midterm quiz, and I believe that they are manageable. An equivalent of H2 Biology will definitely help (I didn't take it but based on https://www.seab.gov.sg/doc... the stuff under Section 4 Biological Evolution looks legit), but it's definitely not necessary and the concepts are explained from scratch. If you've heard of the terms r-selected vs K-selected, abiotic and biotic stressors, and genetic diversity, the bio parts of the course examinable in the midterm quiz will be a breeze. If not, in my opinion ecology and evolutionary biology are the most easy-to-understand topics in biology, so you will be fine too. The beauty of this course is the amount of flexibility you get in all the coursework, which keeps the lower bound of learning manageable but has an upper bound of however much you want to try. Outside of the 20% Midterm Quiz, the course felt like a DYOM course where you have legit full agency on whatever you want to talk about as long as it is related to Invasion Science (not that hard, since the field is so broad). The Forum generally has people giving an introduction of a particular invasive species, the Essay gives you 12 topics across a very wide range to choose from (but you can propose your own!), and the Project can be anything from a research study to interviews with your friends on invasive species to a proposed publicity campaign for NEA. To give one data point, for the forum I talked about identifying invasive species in practice with mosquitoes as a case study, for the essay I talked about why language used in invasion science papers is xenophobic using computational and functional linguistics methods, and for the project I attempted to replicate and slightly improve on existing research work analysing species checklists to show invasion trends. At each step of the way, Prof Pandit was very encouraging and supportive of students trying to do their "own things" with invasion science. My advice is to be creative in applying your strengths (in my case, the ability to code) to work in invasion science for the coursework -- this gives you motivation to actually do stuff since it ends up being related to your "real major" and probably leads to a more interesting final result for both you and the class. Teaching & Assessment: I must first caveat this section by saying that Prof Pandit tries to structure his classes based on what his students want. A good chunk of the first lesson was just him trying to ask us how we want the class to go, and us sitting silently twiddling our thumbs as Singaporeans do. If you and your class expresses a preference on how the class should be taught, it will probably be incorporated. The Prof is a caring person and was willing to postpone deadlines and cut down on some deliverables when we look (or say we are) stressed. This course is a seminar in the way that some graduate mods in NUS are seminars. It is not like other JS or SS where seminar means there's 20 mins of content per class following which everyone goes to a whiteboard and does some random exercise and fails at drawing a CLD or something. Instead, it's a seminar in that the Prof acts as a (and is...) a scholar who is presenting to you an overview of a field of study, in which he has his own contributions and opinions. Concepts taught are appropriately presented as ideas / work by rather than facts you should just accept. He makes it clear when something he says is his opinion, and welcomes questions or discussions that challenges ideas presented. This does come in the form of almost 2 hours of essentially lecture per seminar where the prof covers content and occasionally poses questions. This is then followed by the Forum where 1 or 2 people give their presentation. The length of the lecture part meant I occasionally lost track of things / got distracted, but the lecture slides (which are always uploaded before the class starts) are enough for you to read and catch up after class. The midterm quiz was closed-book, and almost pure regurgitation (and basic application) of concepts. I am thankful that it only covered ~4 weeks of content, but I would have preferred if it was open book and the questions were more application-based or required more thought. No grades for any components are released. Overall, I think the course is fantastic, and I would recommend that you come in with a good attitude and take advantage of the flexibility of the assessments to do stuff you enjoy.
