author: kok
message:
Taken in AY23/24 Sem 1 under Asst. Prof. Bryan Hooi and Dr. Ai Xin

Topics are the same as both of the AY22/23 reviews below.

Assessment:
- 25% Assignment 1 (write a basic MapReduce program on Colab, and suggest some optimisations)
- 25% Assignment 2 (Spark ML pipeline on Databricks, need to hit a certain Mean Absolute Error for full credit)
- 50% Finals

I liked the course in general. 

The course essentially goes through the different ways data is:

1. Processed -- MapReduce, Spark, Stream Processing (Spark Streaming and Flink), Graph Processing
2. Stored -- NoSQL Data Models, Distributed Database, Delta Lake

I think it gives a good overview of these techniques and acts as a good primer for further learning. I like that the course is fairly unopinionated and presents the pros and cons of each technique for different contexts. 

I also think the course is especially useful for system design interviews one may encounter when applying for software engineering jobs.

The course is pretty self-contained, and IMO you don't really need CS2102 knowledge for it as long as you are familiar with what a relational database is. There was a very brief mention of how relational algebra operators (selection, projections etc) map to MapReduce steps, but it was never assessed.

I did not attend live lectures, but they were recorded and I watched most recordings. Lectures by both lecturers are excellent, and both lecturers are responsive in Canvas Discussions. Tutorial worksheets are useful and slide decks with answers are prepared and uploaded by TAs.

It's definitely more chill than other CS mods -- up until finals, you can technically AFK from the whole course except for the 2 Assignments, which most people score full marks for. This means that the finals are effectively 100% of the weightage of the mod, and with the finals themselves not being very difficult, the bell curve is probably going to be very steep. 

I think the assignments could've afforded to be harder and require greater application of the lecture concepts (for both assignments, the assignment briefs themselves were enough to get full marks for the assignments, even if you never watched or read a single lecture). I also think assignments on other concepts like Stream Processing would have been cool. I'm not really sure how grading can scale for this course though -- grading ~300 Colab / Databricks Notebooks manually must be hell.

Overall, it's a chill but useful course that I would recommend. While it's a DB Focus Area Primary I think it's pretty decoupled from other DB mods -- and IMO it's useful to take even if you don't plan to specialize in DB.
thread_title: CS4225 Massive Data Processing Techniques in Data Science

author: kok
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2 under Dr Vivek

This review is for people looking to S/U an ID mod. IS2218 is fantastic S/U fodder, one can literally spend less time on this than CFG1002 Career Catalyst, hover below 25th percentile for midterms/finals and finish with a B. Use some combination of 1) asking friends for their cheatsheets 2) common sense 3) looking at examples in the notes 1h before the test.

The module does teach some useful things though, and learning bits and pieces about the different topics has been useful for me when reading business news about tech companies.

author: kok
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2 under Ms Angie Chew

Works great as S/U fodder since it's one of the few (only?) Senior Seminars with literally 0 graded groupwork components, so you can AFK with social impunity and be free of guilt.

3h of yoga and mindfulness and stuff -- I think mindfulness is actually useful but if you've learnt about it before these 13 weeks become a pretty huge chore.

High workload (weekly reflections + a bunch of essays + a whole-day retreat the Sunday before midterm season begins) but you can get away with missing some weekly reflections if you just want to S/U.

No phones / devices during class, which is a good break but something to keep in mind if you're doing internships or whatever during the semester.

Hopefully someone who tried leaves a review but my sensing is that it's probably not easy to get >=A
thread_title: UTC2700 An Undefeated Mind: An Experiential Inner Reengineering Approach

author: kok
message: Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2 under Prof. Teo Yong Meng and Prof. Dumitrel Loghin https://www.comp.nus.edu.sg/~teoym/cs5224-23/index.html Summary: Ok module where you'll learn some stuff without heavy impact to your schedule / workload. Feels more like an IS module than a CS module aside from the random 2-week detour into MapReduce. Very much just learning (memorising?) content / concepts. Meeting and working with Postgrads is cool. Note: Review from the perspective of a CS undergrad. Workload: - 2h Lecture per week - 6 Canvas Quizzes across the semester - 4 Labs across the semester, mapped to 2 Lab Assignments to submit - 1 Group Project - 1 Optional Research Assignment - 1.5h Final Test (in Week 13) In the first Lecture the Prof mentioned that Lectures would frequently overrun (maximum 3h). Lectures are not recorded but slides with annotations are uploaded promptly. Everything other than lectures are completely asynchronous and OTOT. Assessment: - 20% Canvas Quizzes - 2 x 10% Lab Assignments - 10% Group Project Preliminary Report (5 pages) - 20% Group Project Final Submission (Report, Poster, Codebase) - 30% Final Test (1 page open book) - 5% Bonus from Optional Research Assignment Topics: - Introduction - Concepts & Models - Cloud Architecture - Resource Hosting and Datacenters - Virtualization & Multitenancy - Applications & Paradigms - Case Studies: K‐means Clustering & SaaS Video‐Sharing - Cloud Software Development - Pricing Models and Modelling TCO - Summary and Open Issues - Beyond Cloud Computing Bidding / Registration: You can take this module as an undergrad, just appeal in CourseReg Round 3 Appeals, the option is something like "Unable to Select Module". NUSMods / CourseReg is not lying to you -- there are NO pre-requisites for the module, so you can take it as early as Y1S2. That said, it would be helpful for the Group Project to be familiar with full-stack dev and/or have used AWS services before. For this semester, only the L2 slot had spaces to appeal into, and the L2 slot is for Masters of Science students while the L1 slot is for Masters of Computing students. Therefore, it is likely you will meet and work with Masters students with varying backgrounds and levels of technical ability, but IMO it's a positive experience to learn to work in such a diverse team. Review: The module is very much geared towards industry, and is pitched like an introductory module. The amount of technical content in a topic ranges from something like Pricing which is basically introductory business / economics, and caps out at a Lab on MapReduce where you write max 10 lines of parallelizable code (think the end of CS2030s). It's possible that these 10 lines are the only lines of code you write / see for the whole course if you don't code for the Project (and there is plenty of "business case writeup" type work to do for the Project). Quiz questions are mostly questions from AWS Solutions Architect cert exams. They can be tricky especially since there are maximum 2 attempts, but most people will get full marks or close to full marks. Labs have you follow instructions and shouldn't cause any issue. The Lab Assignments that you submit basically have you take screenshots to show that you did the stuff in the Labs. The Project has you design and implement a Software-As-A-Service or Platform-As-A-Service solution to a business problem of your choice. You can think of this as a hackathon-as-a-module. There is a decent amount of both dev work and writing work to do, so if you're a CS one-trick having Postgrad teammates from undergrad backgrounds like business or other writing-heavy courses can be helpful. You submit a Report, Poster, and a Zip file of your codebase with documentation on how to set it up. The Optional Assignment has a restriction that <10% of each Lecture Slot can sign up for it. However, the actual intake for my batch was tiny -- of the >200 students in the module, only 7 students signed up in the first place and probably less submitted it in the end. This is for good reason -- the workload is high and payoff is tiny. The Optional Assignment has you submit a 10-page literature review research paper, IEEE format, on the state-of-the-art of any Cloud Computing related topic you want, where you must also compare 3 current cloud providers in whichever subdomain you pick (See past submissions: https://www.comp.nus.edu.sg/~teoym/cs5224-21/2021-May-CS5224-Cloud-Computing-Review-final.pdf). While you can score up to maximum 5 bonus % for the optional assignment, the Prof mentioned that students will either score close to full marks or close to zero -- so, you should only spend the time on the optional assignment if you are confident and and have a nice topic in mind. I happened to be doing a part-time internship while taking this mod where I was working on a very specific type of security problem related to Cloud Dev Environments, so I submitted my paper on that and luckily managed to get a score in the "close to full marks" bucket. Otherwise, I wouldn't really recommend spending time on this when you can just study for finals. The finals are ok, but the killer is that it only allowed for a cheat sheet of 1 page (not even 1 full piece of paper). There is far too much content to cram on 1 page, so you will definitely have to memorise stuff. It's mostly open-ended questions like "Name 2 ABCs and tell me why they are better than XYZ", but there was a tiny section where you finish the implementation of a MapReduce program. I did not attend lectures and the slides alone aren't really enough because they're slides used as presentation tools instead of notes, but slides + YouTube + asking ChatGPT to explain things to you was enough. There is another review here that may be helpful, I think it's pretty accurate https://medium.com/@royce963/my-year-3-semester-2-review-for-nus-computer-science-d9ce65c2181b

author: kok
message: yes — the main advantage experienced people get is that they can clear the CTF faster. you can score as well or better as long as you are diligent

author: kok
message: a+. i was fortunate. took it with friends who i don’t believe have much of a difference in security knowledge compared me but got a couple of grades lower. keep an open mind and try to enjoy learning about security and you will have a less negative time than what most of the reviews here describe, and just remember to prepare for finals

author: kok
message: Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1 under Dr. Sufatrio Summary: Steep bell curve, mostly focused on cryptography. I think it’s not bad, but it’s not amazing either, but it’s hard to introduce information security with just 1 module I guess. Workload: - 2h Lecture per week (not recorded, but on Zoom) - 1h Tutorial per week - 3 x 15min Quizzes - 2 CTF Assignments Assessment: - 10% Quizzes (best 2 attempts, Open Internet) - 30% CTF Assignments (15% each) - 5% Group Presentation - 5% Tutorial Attendance - 50% Finals (Open Book) Most people will get close to full marks for the non-finals components (quizzes are open-internet), so pretty much a 100% finals module. The bell curve is really steep, be warned – based on discussions with friends taking the module there doesn’t appear to be many marks between an A+ and even a B grade. Content: For context, I have experience with security and played in a bunch of CTFs in my pre-u days. I am more familiar with web and software security (re, pwn) compared to cryptography. This can be a somewhat “absent” module that me and my friends barely touched except the morning before each quiz, the day before finals, and for a few hours when each CTF assignment or the group presentation was released. Except for the tutorial where I presented the presentation, many students spent every tutorial class just doing other work. Good module to take if you plan to take other higher-workload modules. The module content is around 15% generic security terms (e.g. confidentiality vs integrity vs availability), 70% cryptography and the remaining 15% is split across Network, Web, and Pwn (Software Security). It is fairly dry and lectures have poor attendance, though as someone who avoided cryptography in the past, I did learn some things in the cryptography section that I ended up using for security interviews (eg. key exchange and analysing cryptographic protocols). However, the network/web/pwn content was extremely shallow and touch-and-go, though they are useful as a list of things to Google by yourself if you actually want to learn. Some of the attacks presented (that we would have to recall during exams) are a bit outdated and laughable. The CTF challenges are set by Greyhats members and are pretty well-designed. They will be a breeze if you have prior CTF experience. You need not solve all the challenges set, but instead they will state that you need to solve X easys, Y mediums, and Z hards to get full credit for the CTF. That said, there is often a fairly large difficulty disparity between challenges in the same “difficulty category” – it would be useful to look through all of them to then find the “path of least resistance” if you are just concerned with getting full credit ASAP. You can get bonus credit contributing to your presentation and quiz based on some criteria they will set out. Quizzes are open-internet but it may be worth looking at past tutorial answers to prepare you. The group presentation is pretty chill – you will be randomly-assigned groupmates and will get a choice out of 5 topics to present on. The finals are formulaic and have many questions based on recalling definitions and properties. Doing at least 1 PYP and printing out the entire set of lecture slides for the module is enough for you to get A+ if you are extremely careful. Tons of students left early. Teaching: COM1 printer psc011 was adept at printing out all the lecture slides. Additionally, 2018_Semester_1_Finals_Answers.pdf clearly and accurately demonstrated to me how to answer basically half of my finals questions. On a more serious note, I think Dr Sufatrio has a good heart, and this semester I don’t remember there being any admin hiccups, but it’s just that the content itself isn’t super exciting or difficult enough to warrant most students attending lectures. Tutorial questions are set decently. My TA (Noel Matthew Isaac) was also pretty clear and made an effort to be helpful by sending us extra resources and past cheat sheets. Assignment TAs (Greyhats members) were pretty responsive over email.

author: kok
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1 under Dr. Navarun Varma

Workload:
- 2h Seminar per week, usually ends by 1h 40mins
- 2 Mini Projects which will take your group max 1h after class time
- 1 Advanced Project which will take you group max 1.5h after class time
- 3h Final Exam which is finishable in ~30mins if you learned Vensim properly and can type/move your mouse fast

Junior Seminar for RC4 students, so it’s CS/CU. The Prof is a really nice guy regardless of your exhibited skill/interest level, and is enthusiastic when delivering lessons. You learn some concepts related to modelling disaster resilience like risk vs hazard vs vulnerability, but the more important part of the module is learning about how to model systems with Vensim and learning about different system archetypes. The latter is probably useful for the Senior Seminars that will actually be graded. There is also one lesson dedicated to students playing some strategy game (https://games4sustainability.org/gamepedia/forest-at-risk) which is somewhat interesting.

I was told that is generally accepted as the 2nd-chillest RC4 Junior Seminar and having now taken the mod I can see why.
thread_title: UTC1702F Thinking in Systems: Disaster Resilience

author: kok
message: Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1 under Dr Colin Tan, Dr Prabhu Natarajan, Mr Ravi Suppiah Topics: See https://www.comp.nus.edu.sg/~cs2100/2_resources/lectures.html Workload: - Lectures all recorded (blended learning) - 2h of Recitation per week - 1h of Tutorial per week - 1h of Lab per week - ~2 Canvas quizzes per week - 2 Assignments across the semester - 2h Midterms - 2h Finals The workload is unrelenting. Please take CS2100 together with friends. I would caution other future freshmen who are considering taking this in Y1S1 because of exemptions against doing so. This semester, there were very few spots for the module on ModReg after Round 0, so if you manage to snag the module in Y1S1 (where you can only ModReg from Round 1) it’s likely that you’re doing this alone. Assessment: - 5% Canvas Quizzes - 1% Tutorial Attendance - 2 x 5% Assignments - 14% Labs - 20% Midterms - 50% Finals Midterms and Finals are both pretty standard, there won’t really be any surprises. The difficulty level for the assignments, labs, and exams were all about similar. The bell curve is quite steep though, at least for Midterms the IQR was 18-20-22 out of 25. Content: There is a ton of content (24 Lecture Chapters!!) in this module, but I concur with other reviewers that the module is “intellectually easy but tedious”. IMO the topics are ordered in a weird way, going from higher-level (C, Assembly) then gradually down (Datapath, Control Path) to Boolean Algebra / Logic Gates / Simplification, then back up again to building larger Circuits, Pipelining, and Caching. The result of this is that at least for me, around the middle of the course the topics felt very disjoint and I couldn’t see how each additional topic contributed to my understanding of Computer Organization. It was only when we reached the end (especially Pipelining) when I could finally see the big picture. The labs are really fun and my favourite part of the module. You really get to apply the concepts you just learned to solve interesting-enough problems. The lab sessions themselves generally only last 5 minutes – the labs are released a week in advance, so you just bring your completed lab sheet, show some demo on your laptop to the TA, and you can leave. You must print and staple the lab sheets before bringing them to class, which I found bizarre for a university module in 2022. All in all, I think the content is important and useful for both future CS mods and in industry. Teaching: The pre-recorded lectures are delivered by both Dr Colin and Dr Prabhu, with slides by Prof Aaron Tan who teaches this module in Semester 2. The slides and the lectures are pretty good, albeit a bit overwhelming in number. I didn’t get to watch all of them due to time constraints, but they’re interesting enough that I’ve downloaded them to watch slowly over the December holidays. The lecturers are also fairly active on a module-wide Telegram channel, which is impressive. I did not attend tutorials and recitations, but it seems that a lot of effort is put into those by the lecturers. There was a bit of a kerfuffle in the first half of the semester where the lecturers said they would stop uploading recorded recitations, but I think it was resolved amicably. Lab TAs mostly exist to just physically receive your lab reports and grade them. That said, they did do some teaching when we were introduced to the Logic Trainer (breadboard thingy), and were very helpful when I was lost at the start (I didn’t take Physics).

author: kok
message: Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1 under Prof Aaron Tan Tuck Choy Summary: Hard, fun, don’t give up Topics: See http://web.archive.org/web/20221201082133/https://www.comp.nus.edu.sg/~cs1231s/2_resources/lectures.html Workload: - 3h of Lectures per week - 2h of Tutorial per week (attendance graded) - 2 Assignments across the semester - 2h Midterm - 2h Finals Assessment: - 5% Tutorial Attendance (strictly attendance, no need to participate) - 2 x 10% Assignments - 25% Midterm - 50% Finals Of the 100 mark finals only 18 marks were based on topics that were covered in midterms. Assignments are meant to be completed individually, but almost everyone gets close to full marks for assignments, so that rule is obviously ignored if you consider that the median for assessments hovers around 50%. I was around the lower quartile for my assignments and just below the median for midterms. I was fully intent on S/U-ing, but sage advice from a senior told me to not completely give up because the second half of the module is fairly distinct from the first half, and contains topics that probably favours those with programming experience. I ended up far exceeding my expectations for finals (upper decile). Content: This module is known for being tough because most students will have 0 familiarity with any of the content prior to NUS. This however means that there’s a relatively even playing field, since most of the students who have prior background and would have ez no kick destroyed the bell curve are probably in MA1100 or MA1100T. That said, complete knowledge of pre-university level Mathematics is assumed, and as a former bad student I personally struggled with doing basic algebraic manipulation which you had to show steps for during the early topics. By the end, you could get away with just writing (by basic algebra) in your proofs. Of all the modules I took in Sem 1, this had the most Δknowledge by far for me, and thus was my most satisfying (albeit difficult) module. Even though this was my only S/U-able module, the content, especially the later topics on cardinality/graphs/induction, was interesting enough for me to not have the discipline to stop reading the slides/tutorials and focus on saving my other modules even though I was fully intent on S/U-ing this module. Most of the content is really helpful for other CS mods, which I wouldn’t have predicted going into the module. Something a bit weird that I would recommend is to find someone who takes MA1100T (the discrete math module for prospective Special Programme in Math students) and ask for their Cardinality slides/notes (I discovered this by accident because I was lucky enough to have a friend who was smart enough to be in MA1100T and helpful enough to teach me stuff the day before my finals). Cardinality will be the most difficult topic for most students because it feels so abstract and somewhat defies common sense. Their module goes far deeper in-depth than CS1231S, and a lot of “hard questions” in CS1231S that we would through are just trivial results and corollaries for those students. For example, the hardest MCQ question in this semester’s finals paper (Q17, 27.6% correct) asks you to verify statements that are trivial results in MA1100T lecture slides but incredibly difficult if you only have CS1231S resources and a normal human brain. Teaching: Lectures were taught by Prof Aaron with some taught by Prof Leong Hon Wai, and they were recorded. I did not attend or watch lectures. The slides are good and comprehensive enough to use to study, and my friends say that the lectures are good. Tutorials are taught by 1 TA, who can be an undergraduate (usually assigned to 1 class) or a graduate student (usually take multiple classes). You are expected to have attempted the tutorial questions and thus have some familiarity with the content before the tutorials. My tutor was a grad student Sherman Yuen who was fantastic – I wasn’t the best student and often didn’t pay attention or attempt the worksheets before the tutorials, but when the stars aligned and I did both, his explanations were always very clear, his delivery was engaging, and he makes a huge effort to follow-up on doubts in class via email. If I had to choose between paying attention to the lectures XOR paying attention to (+ doing the worksheets for) the tutorials, I would choose the latter. If you have a grad student TA, it is worth being friends with students who have undergraduate TAs, because they usually make Telegram groups where they dispense Past Year Papers and their Answer Schemes to their students. Otherwise, PYPs are uploaded without model answers.

author: kok
message: Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 + ST 1 (iBLOC) under Prof Tan Tiow Seng NOTE: Prof Tan hinted that he probably won't be teaching this module in AY22/23 anymore, so take any review here with a grain of salt Summary: I highly recommend that you take this module regardless of your experience. Teaching and materials are good and should set you up for success. It seems that most people taking this mod ORD-ed in November/February or were in non-combat vocations, though exceptions exist (who I have a lot of respect for!). Decent way to get into the groove of uni and meet new friends. Good way to get a taste of the bell curve (though it’s not shaped like a bell in CS1010X) and how godly hardworking your batchmates can be (multiple people finishing everything on coursemology and doing >5 PYPs per exam). Manageable with an internship/part-time work if you’re concerned about that. Topics: The topics are the same since the comment by Uxinnn 2 years ago. The content covered is almost identical to that of CS1010S, but with one added chapter on Dynamic Programming (non-trivial, must be comfortable with solving DP problems) and two added chapters on Java (trivial, just need to know basic syntax). Workload: - ~1h of pre-recorded lectures per week - 2h of Recitation per week (you must pre-do the recitation worksheet) - 1h of Review (optional) per week for the first half of the course - 2h of Tutorial per week (you must pre-do the tutorial assignment) - ~1 Mission and ~1 Lecture assignment on Coursemology every 2 weeks, with optional Sidequests and Contests - 2h Midterm - 2h Practical Exam - 2h Finals Something interesting about the Coursemology homework is that if you finish them early, you unlock future homework earlier. This means that it's possible to secure full marks for the Coursemology component by late-March and you no longer have to do anything for the module except study when exams are near. Assessment: - 33% Homework (Reach Level 35 on Coursemology) - 2% Free (Finish End-of-Module Survey) - 16% Midterm - 16% Practical Exam - 33% Finals Many people really liked Prof Tan’s problem-setting compared to those you saw in PYPs before 2019, since there is a more genuine focus on legit computational thinking (which favours those who understand the taught material well) rather than the idiosyncrasies of Python or classical graph algorithms (which just favours those with prior experience). Content: Similar to CS1101S and CS1010S, a bulk of the initial content of this module is based on the famous Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP). Most of the difficulty for this module, especially for self-taught programmers (or even experienced programmers whose “mother tongue” is a procedural language), will therefore be at the first half of the module, where you are expected to reason about problems in a purely functional style. You will be introduced to lambdas, function composition, recursion, and map/filter/reduce before getting to do anything with loops or anything mutable like lists. I came across a few people who underestimated the module and thought it’d be simple but were swiftly humbled. IMO, here are the prerequisites for one to genuinely find it “simple”: - Python has been your main language at some point - You have self-studied an SICP variant or have experience solving basic problems in a functional language (for this module, I think SML derivatives will prepare you marginally better than Lisp derivatives, but if you can do anything in any of those languages you will find the first half trivial) - You have basic experience in data structures and algorithms/cp, specifically in 1) time/space complexity analysis, 2) reasoning about data structure tradeoffs, and 3) solving DP problems (no need to know classical algorithms / data structures) That is to say that even if you have a lot of programming experience, you probably won't find the module "simple". Therefore, the best way for you to benefit from and do well in the module is ultimately just to finish the Coursemology assignments and pay attention during recitations and tutorials. If you want to prepare before the module begins, work towards these 3 goals. Teaching: Lectures are pre-recorded, 90% by Prof Tan and the rest from Prof Ben Leong. I did not watch the lectures. Recitations and Reviews are taught by Prof Tan. Everyone I know greatly enjoyed and appreciated Prof Tan’s teaching. He explains things clearly and interestingly and is prompt in answering questions. He expects the class to have attempted the recitation before the lesson, and if he poses a question to the class will wait until someone answers (you can’t just sit there and wait for him to answer himself). Tutorials are taught by Zheng Jiaqi, Prof Tan’s PhD student. A fair initial reaction is to think that his voice is boring and to not pay attention (especially since tutorial questions are on coursemology so you know if you pass test cases before the tutorial starts) but once you start paying attention you realise he spits some hot fire in the form of his extension questions. These are much closer in difficulty to the exams and are a good test of your understanding. Undergraduate TAs (Y1s and 2x Y2s) (mine was Samuel Yow) grade and provide comments on Coursemology, and email you moral support / other tips prior to exams. My TA was friendly and gave insightful comments on how to improve even if code passed test cases. It is useful to have a group of friends taking the module with different TAs so that you can all share the pre-exam emails and benefit from the union of exam tips. In particular, the email sent by one of the Y2 TAs Benjamin Tan before the midterms carried me for all time/space complexity questions in both the midterms and finals, which was a big W since that was my worst topic by far coming into the module. tl;dr 10/10 take it if you can dedicate the time and don’t expect to come in and destroy with no effort regardless of your programming background.

author: Jonathan Chang
message:
Taken in AY18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Jessie Teng
Expected grade: B/B+ 
Grade obtained: A- (heng got bellcurve)

Tutorials & E-lectures:
this mod generally quite chill, 2 hrs tutorial every week. but it's a flipped classroom approach, so need to review the e-lectures weekly before tutorials. e-lectures pretty chill, like at most 1.5-2 hrs can settle the content. tutorials just go there and lepak. just pay attention to the tutorials where prof jessie will go through the approach to tackle assignments. 

Assignments:
pretty chill write ups, with average word count of 500~700 words, which is quite little already. i spend about a week on each assignment because my english not very good. if you're good at analysing content and have a good command of english, assignments should be a breeze for you in my opinion. i'd say don't look down on these small assignments, because my group project was absolute trash and the only reason why i got an A- is probably because of individual components.

Marks obtained: average over the few assignments was 75~80

Group Project:
pretty chill report write up as well, 4 page report with a maximum of 6 pages of appendix. tbh we rushed through the project in 3 weeks- 1 month without any review from the prof (because we redid the project, bless), and i felt that the report was a mess.

Presentation:
also very chill, 10 mins group presentation on your report in front of the class. just spend some effort making pretty slides, and just speak confidently, that should do. took about 1 week to prepare everything with the group, went well in the end. if you're closer to the other groups and want to wayang abit, can get their help to ask questions that you prepared before hand, maybe that'll boost your marks? 

overall, a mod i'd recommend, it's pretty chill. this mod is not just offered to those who stay in hall fyi.

PS: dont be affected by prof jessie's comments, she gives ALOT of comments, just to help guide you in your thinking, doesn't mean you wouldn't score well.

author: gomuflower
message: Taken in: AY25/26 S1 Lecturer: Dr Wong Tin Lok (Lawrence) TA: Wu Xingyu Assessment Components: Quiz 1: 25% Quiz 2: 25% Finals: 50% Content Propositional Logic, Predicate Logic, Proofs, Sets, Relations, Equivalence Classes and Partial Orders, Functions, Cardinality, Countability, Counting, Graphs, Trees Topics are similar to the past semesters. Note that CS1231S and MA1100 cover the topics in greater depth (though not necessarily a good or bad thing). Lectures There are 2 + 1 hours of lectures each week, where Prof Tin Lok uses to covers 1 topic. Prof Tin Lok has quite an interesting style of using 2 screens during the lectures, one for the slides and the other for referencing past topics or drawing diagrams. The lectures themselves can be quite dry due to the nature of the topics. By the time we get to Cardinality/Countability and Graphs, the topics can get quite abstract that it is hard to get the intuition on how to approach the proof. To his credit, Prof Tin Lok does try his best to use diagrams and examples to explain the intuition. Tutorials Weekly 2 hour tutorials which go through questions relating to the topic taught in the previous week. The tutorial questions can sometimes be quite hard so it is not expected to be able to do the questions. My tutor Wu Xingyu was quite poor as he was mumbling quite a bit and he was reading off the solutions and copying them onto a whiteboard. As tutorial attendance is not compulsory, it is probably better to read the solutions in your spare time or attend Prof Tin Lok's tutorial class slot (if he teaches one). Quiz 2 open-book hardcopy quizzes, one in week 5 and the other in week 8. Quiz 1 covers chapters 1-3 while quiz 2 covers chapters 4-6, excluding induction proofs. No sample papers are released but the past year quizzes can be found on Studocu. Grind (and possibly print) these as they follow the same format as the actual quiz. The quizzes themselves are generally more straightforward than tutorials. They are mostly computational questions and the proof questions are generally quite straight forward. Due to the question phrasing, it is quite easy to make careless mistakes. The bellcurve for the quizzes are quite high so be careful and check your answers thoroughly. https://uploads.disquscdn.com/images/a061ec7e3627276f7baff99a767a94c3af8d9728c8cf49cf03c572e71e0938fd.png https://uploads.disquscdn.com/images/464f25ed7ee6911431bfae0e3a3740a9ab7b0dc0c7cfb64e160b433d9c8ce1ad.png Finals Finals cover chapters 7-12 with induction based proving tested. The finals was generally tougher than the quizzes, though this semester's final was not as hard as past semesters. Prof Tin Lok provides one sample paper but you can likewise find other papers on Studocu. Print all the notes and some past-year papers so you can have the definitions and different approaches on hand. https://uploads.disquscdn.com/images/3c2c65a4378104a64dbf5212469179f75553e8d2b8e014fc859a0bab54bba5a3.png Overall Thoughts Even though this course is a more gentle introduction to discrete math compared to CS1231S/MA1100, it is significantly more challenging compared to other CS math mods I have taken so far which are mostly just following formulas. CS1231 in contrast requires you to use definitions and proved propositions to prove new things. The course itself isn't exactly high workload but rather the workload comes from trying to understand the content. Prof Tin Lok is quite a quirky lecturer which some may not like but overall he is quite caring and I liked that he spreads out the assessments. My only pet peeve is that I found that the course canvas page was quite messy and that you had to download one file at a time. While the content can be admittedly quite dry, it does have a lot of relevance to many other modules such as ST2334 (Counting) and CS2040 (Proving Techniques + Graphs/Trees). I would recommend taking CS1231 along with a CS2040 variant for those who wish to better understand the intuition/ideas behind the Graph/Tree algorithms. Grades: Quiz 1: 22/25 Quiz 2: 21/25 Finals: Not released Actual Grade: A :)

author: Yang Victoria
message:
Took this module in Sem 1 AY16/17.
This module is well taught and interesting. It's a great chance to learn a foreign language on campus. The senseis are really kind and knowledgable. They always answer my questions patiently and explain grammar structures clearly. In tutorials, I get to know people who have similar interests in Japanese food and anime. 
It's suitable for people who are interested in Japanese culture and score B-~B+ on average for their major modules. Since every tutorial counts towards the final grade, A~A+ people are quite stressed all the way along.

author: Yang Victoria
message: Useful and interesting module. Taught by teaching-award-winning lecturers Prof.Tan and Prof.Wang. Very suitable for chemistry students who took MA1102R in place of MA1421. I also saw life sciences students who took this module to fulfil Faculty Requirement.

author: Yang Victoria
message:
Taken in AY16/17 Sem 1. Taught by Prof Greg Tucker-Kellogg.
This module is well taught and very interesting. The lectures are well organised and the practicals give us a chance to put what we learnt into use. 
Very suitable for life sciences students. Students from Computing who take this as an elective may find the biology terms hard to memorise. Good English gives you an advantage.

Contents:
Search methods(AND, OR...   frequently used databases)
Sequence Alignment (Dynamic programming: global, semi-global, local)
Blast ( psi-blast being extremely important and frequently tested. Need to know the basic programming methodology behind. The standardised bit-score can be a source for calculation questions when it's related to the amount of information provided.)
Sequence profiles
Molecular Phylogenetics(transition and transversion: a source for calculation questions. Character-based and distance-based methods: UPGMA is the most important one. Need to understand everyone of them.)
protein(Newman projection, Pymol(can write some simple Python programs for it, quite interesting))
basic homology modelling(RMSD calculation, Swiss Modeling)
pathways(notations on the pathway map)
Personal Genetics(maybe I can have my genome sequenced someday as well)

Assessment:
CA: easy to score
Practical Participation mark: be sure to come to the practicals every week. Plan your summer school, volunteer work and intern well to ensure that you can come back to Singapore before the first practical.
Personal essay: extremely hard. I spent days and nights on it for nearly a week. 
Group essay: extremely hard. I spent days and nights on it for nearly a week. 
Final: Open book:) Need to write very fast. Questions aren't difficult but it's hard to explain things in English. Can find good notes on Paperbaton.

Workload: 15/10
Difficulty: 10/10
Bell curve: not sure (I am quite stressed during the exams since the class size is smaller than LSM cores)

author: anonymous_tiger
message:
Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2
Grading: 10 LumiNUS quiz (100 Marks Total) 
Lecturer: Prof Lu Weiqian

Content/Workload/Experience: 1/10

This module basically is fully e-learning which taught us about ethics in computing. Personally, I do not find it useful or taught us anything related to course of study. Feel like the quiz is ambiguous and all about luck. I still remember in one of the quizzes which I do actually read and studied but scored 5/10. Then, for the next quiz which I just basically guessed the answer for each question and actually got 7/10. Then, for the subsequent quizzes, I just decided to play by luck and I actually did way better than when I studied for it. So in conclusion, there is no point studying this module and I really advise that you do not spent so much time on this module because effort != result. Remember to spare an SU for this beforehand.

Final Grade: B+

author: anonymous_tiger
message:
Taken in AY21/22 Semester 2

Lecturer: Prof Tan Ban Pin

Test 1: 25%
Test 2: 25%
Final Exam: 50%

Overall, if you are a JC student, good for you, this is just mainly pure recap of your maths syllabus studied previously. If you are a poly student, it will probably take more time for you to get used to the concepts and pace. The module is fully e-learning style, all videos and tutorials solutions are posted weekly on luminus. The lecture slides provided by the prof is comprehensive but lots of slides and content. My advise for future students is to study and understand the key topics rather than learning the entire slides. Moreover, the prof hold consultation weekly, so you have any doubts, feel free to attend the consultations. Since tutorial solutions are provided, many students tend to skip all the consultations which I feel is a big mistake because the Prof usually give out additional questions for students to try which may be small hints to the tests/exams.

Test 1 mainly test understanding of graphs,limits and continuity, first principles of differentiation and intermediate value theorem. (Should really score in this)

Test 2 mainly test understanding of differentiation, implicit differentiation, applications of derivative (abs max/min, chain rule, optimisation problems), Rolle's Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, Basic Integrals, simpson's rule, Integration by substition/parts. (Try to score in this)

Final Exam test all content of test 1 and test 2, and also includes application of integrals (area, volume of revolution, linear ODE)
(Hardest out of all, try to score many method marks as possible)

Expected Grade: B+/A-
Final Grade: A

author: anonymous_tiger
message:
Taken in AY21/22 Semester 2

Lecturer: Prof Sharon and Prog Yingda

Online Quiz & Datacamp Assignments 15%
Tutorials (best 6 of 9 to be counted) 30%
Online Assessment 15%
Online Final Exam 40%

First half on descriptive analytics were taught by Prof Sharon and Second half on predictive/prescriptive analytics were taught by Prof Yingda. For someone who as zero experience in data analytics, the content taught by prof sharon (descriptive analytics) is easy to grasp BUT prone to many careless mistakes (in tutorials) and exams. The content taught by Prof Yinda (predictive/prescriptive analytics) were way less intuitive (harder to grasp), especially the part on regression and time analysis. It took me to reread/clarify concepts multiple times before I can say that i 80% grasp it. 

In Overall, I did way better than expected as compared to my subpar midterm results. My midterm experience was not very good, leave few questions blank due to lack of time. The statistics is not shown to us but I believe I am below the median range. For online quizzess/assignment, its basically free marks as long as you attempted them. For the tutorials, I am mainly getting 8/10 and 9/10 due to many careless mistakes. However, for my finals, I think I did decently, though not enough time to complete (even though 2.5 hours).

Tip for future students: try to clarify any doubts with profs immediately and understand the content. For exams, time management is especially important and do not focus too much on 1 question and try to get out as many method marks as possible.

Expected Grade: B/B+
Final Grade: A

author: កម្ពុជា
message:
Prof: Patricia 
Taken 23/24 sem 1

Fun mod, have the opportunity to go on many fieldtrips, and less workload.

The CA is divided into six assessments, totaling 100%:

CA1 (20%): Group presentation on wildlife topics, evaluating research, issue analysis, and presentation skills.
CA2 (15%): Peer teaching based on CA1 topic, assessing case study selection, session structure, and discussion facilitation.
CA3 (15%): Contributions to RVRC and NSS iNaturalist databases, focusing on contribution quantity and identification skills.
CA4 (35%): Final proposal for a mini-jungle/urban rewilding project, judged on site and strategy selection, socio-economic analysis, and presentation quality.
CA5 (15%): Participation in fieldtrips, graded on interaction, survey activities, initiative, group work, and online engagement.

Workload 2/10
Difficulty 4/10
Fun 9/10
Grade A-

author: កម្ពុជា
message: Taken in 2022/2023 sem 2 Prof: Pham Kim Cuc, Daisy The course was revamped entirely this semester by Prof Daisy from just calculations on paper to applying the theories learned using the R language (So no past papers for us). We learned basic machine learning algorithms and applied it in an IDE called R Studio. If you have taken CS1010S or other variations of CS1010, then the programming in this course would be a breeze to you. (Suggest taking it concurrently with CS1010S or after having taken CS1010S) If you want to prepare ahead for this course, I suggest you learn R language syntax and some coding fundamentals like functions, conditional statements, loops and etc. To do well in this course, pay attention to the R codes in lectures and tutorials. Midterm and final exam questions were similar to the one in the tutorials and lectures. So if you understand what the code means and include the codes in your cheatsheet, you will be good to go. Enjoyment: 9/10 (The prof was very knowledgeable and funny + I like how she tested us on R instead of just calculations on papers) Workload: 4/10 (In comparison to CS1010S which was 9/10) Usefulness: 9/10 Final Grade: A

author: កម្ពុជា
message:
Taken in 2022/2023 sem 2
Prof: Dr. Goh Jun Le

While Linear Algebra might be new to many students, those who have taken further math in JC will find some familiarity in the concepts covered.

The course served as an introduction to fundamental concepts in linear algebra, which are widely applied in various fields such as science, engineering, statistics, economics, and operations research. The focus was on both the algebraic and geometric viewpoints of linear algebra, aiming to develop skills in computing with vectors and matrices.

The textbook, "Linear Algebra - Concepts and Techniques on Euclidean Spaces," served as the primary resource, with the lectures closely following its contents. The course covered all seven chapters, and students were encouraged to work through the exercise sets in the book. Selected problems were discussed during tutorial sessions, providing an opportunity for further understanding and practice.

The course covered a range of topics, including linear systems and Gaussian elimination, matrices and their operations, vector spaces and their associated matrices, orthogonality, diagonalization, and linear transformations. These concepts formed the foundation for understanding more advanced linear algebra concepts.

Assessment in the course was divided into a final examination worth 50% and a mid-term test worth 25%. Additionally, four homework assignments accounted for 25% of the final grade. The assignments allowed students to apply their knowledge and practice problem-solving skills.

The tutorials and laboratory sessions were valuable in enhancing the learning experience. Some homework problems required the use of MATLAB, providing practical application of the concepts learned.

For further reference, several recommended books were suggested, including "Elementary Linear Algebra" by H. Anton, "Linear Algebra with Applications" by S. Leon, "Schaum's Outline of Linear Algebra" by S. Lipschutz and M. Lipson, and "Linear Algebra" by S.H. Friedberg, A.J. Insel, and L.E. Spence.

Overall, the linear algebra module was a positive learning experience, providing a solid foundation in the subject. I would recommend this module to anyone looking to gain a strong understanding of linear algebra concepts.

Rating: 4.7/5
Grade: A+ (Unexpected because I skipped all the proving questions)

author: កម្ពុជា
message:
Taken in 2022/2023 sem 2
Professor: Patricia

The course was overall a fun and enriching experience. While there is room for improvement in terms of the course structure and grading criteria, the best aspect of the course was the learning journeys it offered.

The course delved into the impact of diverse cultural values and norms on meeting the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and understanding socio-cultural issues in regional and global contexts. Through team-led seminars, stakeholder dialogues, field trips, and critical discussions, the course aimed to develop students into sensitive global citizens with a holistic understanding of culture and its relation to the social pillar of sustainability.

One of the highlights of the course was the emphasis on practical application and collaboration. The various components of continuous assessment (CA) allowed us to actively engage and showcase our learning. The CA breakdown included a group presentation on a chosen cultural topic, peer teaching sessions, field trip reflections, poster presentations, and active participation in class activities.

The group presentation component (CA1) enabled us to conduct independent research, analyze current socio-cultural issues, and present our findings. It assessed both team and individual performance, emphasizing presentation skills and the ability to relate the chosen topic to social sustainability.

The peer teaching component (CA2) encouraged interactive classroom activities and critical evaluation of case studies. We applied peer teaching techniques and were assessed individually and as a team on the suitability of the chosen case study, the structure of the teaching session, and our ability to facilitate critical discussions.

The field trip reflections (CA3 and CA4) provided opportunities for experiential learning. We captured our observations and experiences in Little India and AWARE through narrative photo essays and reflective Instagram posts. These reflections allowed us to deepen our understanding of different cultures and their connection to SDGs.

The poster presentation component (CA5) required in-depth research and contextualization of socio-cultural issues in Singapore. We had to showcase a solution and present it in a clear and concise manner, similar to a conference poster-presentation. The extended Q&A session tested our ability to explain and defend our poster.

Active participation (CA6) was encouraged throughout the course, involving engagement during field trips, talks, classroom discussions, teamwork, and exercises. This component assessed our overall involvement and contribution to the learning process.

While the course could benefit from improvements in its structure and grading criteria, the learning journeys provided valuable insights into the complex relationships between culture, sustainability, and the UN SDGs. 

Overall, I would recommend this course to those interested in understanding the influence of culture on social sustainability.

Rating: 4/5
Grade: SU
thread_title: RVC1001 The Rocky Road to SDGs

author: កម្ពុជា
message:
Taken AY 22/23 Sem 2
TA: Joanne

The course is divided into six segments, each consisting of individual learning activities (ILA) and team-based workshops (TBW), except for the final wrap-up segment.

The ILAs are asynchronous activities that involve watching learning videos and completing worksheets. I appreciated the flexibility of working on these activities at my own pace within a two-week period for each segment. The guided videos and worksheets provided clear guidance for understanding the concepts.

The TBWs were biweekly in-person tutorial sessions conducted by DTK instructors. These sessions allowed for deeper learning and collaboration within teams. I found the TBWs to be engaging and hands-on, and working in teams enhanced the learning experience. It's important to plan the timetable carefully, considering the traveling time between classes when choosing tutorial slots.

Assessment in the course is based on continuous assessment, with no final exam. There are two stages of assessment: the interim assessment in Week 6 and the final assessment in Week 13. The ILAs and TBWs contribute to the assessment criteria. In ILAs, demonstrating effort, insightfulness, and coherence in the work is essential. For TBWs, active participation, thoughtful contributions to discussions, and collaborative teamwork are valued.

Throughout the semester, instructors provide feedback on ILAs and TBWs but not on an individual basis. Instead, they share consolidated feedback summarizing patterns and observations across all classes. However, students can arrange one-to-one or small-group consultations with instructors for personalized feedback. After each assessment stage, students receive an indicative grade (Below Expectations, Meeting Expectations, or Exceeding Expectations) to give them an idea of their performance. However, the final assignment results are not shared due to moderation and university policy.

Overall, the DTK1234 course provided a well-structured learning experience combining individual and team-based activities. The ILAs and TBWs allowed for practical application and deeper understanding of the concepts. The assessment approach, with feedback provided at different stages, was helpful in tracking progress.

Rating: 4/5
Grade: SU

author: meower
message: MA4207 Mathematical Logic AY25/26 Semester 1 under Dilip Raghavan The reference textbook is Enderton Ch 1-2. Some subsections are omitted. Topics and assessment components are identical to the previous batch. It seems to be Prof Dilip's second time teaching MA4207. Lectures are not recorded. Tutorials are not recorded but are uploaded. [+1] on riceyrice's review, everything is accurate 1) I like Prof Dilip. He's extremely clear in what he expects from you with regards to exam content, which made revision easier for me. 2) He didn't seem to cover Post's lattices. Or maybe it's my fault for not attending tutorials and skipping the first half of the lectures. 3) Exams are brutal, especially Q6 of the midterms and finals, involving an application of compactness and finite axiomatisation respectively. That last question feels quite hopeless, because Prof will usually give out 1 mark unless you get the construction right. In fact, the whole finals felt so hopeless, because you have to do a ton of wizardry and observe some sentence and language that happens to exactly satisfy the question's requirements, and then you have to prove how it exactly satisfies them. I think I managed to do 30% of the paper with utmost confidence, handwaved 20% of the paper, and fought for pity marks by scribbling on the other questions. Not a fun experience. Thankfully, Prof Dilip uploads his past year papers, and specifies exactly what he's testing. Not that it'll help, because you will need to identify some random observation that happens to resolve the problem at hand. Exam Questions --- I am including them here because Prof Dilip seems very transparent about the key concepts that he's testing and looking out for. Will be deleted on request from Prof Midterms (ZOL only): T/F, truth table and taut implication, prove completeness, prove incompleteness, apply compactness Finals (FOL only): T/F, prove definability, prove undefinability, observe a sentence for a spectrum, formally prove a statement, prove EC(Delta), disprove EC(Delta), apply Los-Vaught for finite/recursive axiomatisability

author: meower
message: HSI2010 New Worlds Beyond Earth (Read: Pop Astronomy) AY25/26 Semester 1 under Prof Cindy Ng There seems to be no reference textbook. Other reviews have covered topics and assessment components. Lectures are recorded and livestreamed on Zoom. There are no tutorials. [+1] on WhaleShark's review, especially with respect to effort required for this non-SUable module [+1] on ChlorineCl2's review, with facts playing a crucial role in term tests Based on the general sentiment of the other reviews, you'd think that Prof Cindy would ask us to state the exact distance between Earth and Jupiter. But that's not the case. She asks simple questions from lecture. I'll share a couple for reference. 1) MCQ: Historical observations wrt canals in Mars... --> Just pay attention to class 2) MCQ: All seasons have the same duration --> You can draw a diagram using the heliocentric model and intuit (or even use irl experience) to argue that they have different durations Well, to be fair, "just pay attention to class" can get difficult when there's so much going on. While I do think that some amount of facts are inescapable, especially for these random planets with random properties that we can't yet explain with our current knowledge, it's still possible to test the content in a more interesting manner. For example, it would be interesting to insert an image of some unidentified planet, then use its features to guess which planet (covered in class) it is. Or Prof Cindy could focus more on testing general processes. What are the immediate consequences if the atmosphere is stripped away? (rip gravity, radiation, hydrosphere, temperature, pressure) How could it still support life even without an atmosphere? (some life can survive under extreme conditions) Perhaps an open-ended section for people like me to yap. Overall, no regrets. I'm quite interested in the subject matter, and none of the other HSI2ks scratch that itch.

author: meower
message: MA4262 Measure and Integration (Read: Measure Theory) AY25/26 Semester 1 under Akshay Hegde Textbook seems to be a mixture of Yang Yue's notes, Stein and Shakarchi, Panchenko Probability (Ch 1-2). Topics include outer measure, measurable sets and functions, lebesgue integrals, fubini, tonelli, fourier, radon-nikodym, jordan + hahn + lebesgue decompositions, basic measure-theoretic probability, Lp and inequalities. Assessment Components include 4 x 12.5% assignments and a 50% closed-book finals. It's Prof Akshay's first time teaching MA4262. In fact, it's his first time teaching any course here. I thought it was okay. Lectures are recorded and livestreamed on Zoom. Tutorials are not recorded and not uploaded. 1) I would have liked more support in terms of practice questions. Throughout the semester, there were only four problem sets of like 5 questions each (20 questions total) with 1 or 2 in each set being submitted for grading as homework (this seems like something Oxford would do). There were no past-year papers provided, or sample questions, or outline of the finals format. I felt like I was being thrown into the fireplace that is the finals. I wasn't sure if he wanted us to reproduce lecture proofs such as MCT or DCT (which seems like something Oxford would do) or whether he'd give us the most insane trickiest questions out there. Thankfully, the final exam was probably one of the easiest I've seen. And the class size was 32, so pretty much no curve, I think. I'll put the rough questions for reference below. 2) I would have liked more visual intuition and diagrams. I attended the first 6 7-ish lectures and it was fine, but certain proofs regarding outer measure or the idea of outer measure could be conveyed better with more diagramslop. 3) I would have liked a different set of topics. It feels pointless to cover basic measure-theoretic probability when I'm planning on taking MA5260 later anyway. I know it's good scaffolding or whatever, but I would've probably liked to see abstract measures, lusin, egorov, or whatever. Just my preference. Fubini, tonelli, fourier, Lp, decompositions are fine though. 4) Prof Akshay is quite a receptive lecturer with regards to feedback. Since I was overseas for quite a bit, I requested a Zoom recording for lecture, to which he kindly acquiesced. I also liked his little touch of covering Hausdorff dimensions and Wang Hong's conjecture on the same day that she'd present her work here. Very good. Overall, I rate Prof Akshay 6 or 7/10. First 3 bullet points sound negative but that's what I zoom in on when I ramble on about my thoughts like this. Honestly I'd take it under Prof Akshay again. But maybe I'd be more assertive in terms of asking for visual intuition. I didn't really speak up and request for intuition during the lecture, since I was feeling unsure whether it'd be okay to disrupt the lecture pace. And also because I wasn't in lecture half the time. My bad. Exam Questions - I wanted to write the exact question, but upon reflecting for a bit, it might be disrespectful to state the questions word-for-word. Section 1: 5 x 6 1) Prove a relatively simple common lecture result. 2) Prove that a finite-measure subset intersecting with something has measure approaching something. Prove that this fails for slightly different conditions. 3) Prove that a function that is positive and negative over non-null sets satisfies some certain inequality. 4) A slight modification of a tutorial question. 5) Three T/F questions with no justification required for Lp spaces. Section 2: 3 choose 2 x 10 6) Calculate the integral of a limit of functions. 7) Prove that some event has 0 probability of occurring infinitely often. 8) Expectation of something idk it's one of those r/1+r It was really easy, since I managed to complete it within 1.5 hours, and apparently half of the lecture hall did so as well. But I'm not complaining. I appreciate finals that don't try to destroy you, and are just testing for solid knowledge and foundations. I say the final exam served its purpose well. Maybe just add more questions so that it's more tightly completed within 2.5 hours.

author: meower
message:
For the debate, each team sends a member out for 2 to 3 minutes to either make new points, or counter opposing points. Every debate flows like this:

Affirmative team makes 3 points
Negative team makes 3 points
Affirmative Speaker 1 then Negative Speaker 1
Affirmative Speaker 2 then Negative Speaker 2
Affirmative Speaker 3 then Negative Speaker 3
Affirmative team concludes
Negative team concludes

There are five members in each group, and about six groups per tutorial session. So that's three unique debates to be held during the final tutorial. Probably the most enjoyable and memorable part of the course IMHO. And something that should never be taken away.

author: meower
message: HS2915 Beyond the Good and Evil of Drugs ~im on drugs rn while writing this AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Dr Linda Hong (my oshi) and Dr George Radics (slay king) Textbook: Not any official references that I know of Topics 1. Drugs 2. Some Pharmacology 3. Religious and Cultural Drugs 4. Punishing and Prohibiting Drugs 5. Preventing and Reducing Harm from Drugs 6. Medicinal Drugs 7. Recreational Drugs 8. Drugs, Mental Health, and the Internet 9. Greedy Drugs 10. Violent Drugs Assessment Components and Timeline 10% Class Participation 10% Post-Lecture Quizzes (~Week 3-12) 25% Picture-Caption and Concept Map (~Midterm Recess Week) 25% Midterm (~Week 7) 10% Strategy Report (~Week 10/11) 20% Debate (~Week 12/13) There is no peer review. Brownie Review Queen Linda cooks excellent brownies, if a bit on the dry side. But still excellent nonetheless, since I ate seven of them --- free food tastes exceptionally delicious. They're surprisingly filling as well. As I was stuffing brownies into my mouth, Prof. George walked up to me for small talk. But I was too busy stuffing my mouth to respond properly. This moment keeps me up at night. CS Students Invaded My Course I need to make a disclaimer --- this module was an impulse bid. I'd initially planned on reading 3 math modules and 1 HS29xx module, but all of a sudden, there were 3 available slots and 160 bidders for EL1101E. I blame this on the CS students. Therefore, I had to read another module, and so I chose this one. There are two main pull factors for me in this decision. 1) This module doesn't have finals, so my workload at the end of the semester will be slightly eased. 2) The disciplines aren't compartmentalised. There's no discrete "Economic Approach" or "Psychological Approach" or "Computer Science Approach" to drugs that runs counter to the spirit of interdisciplinarity. As expected, it delivered on these aspects. And I chose my other HS29xx course based on this, as well. In any case, because this module wasn't my initial first choice, this will surely skew my review. monkaS The Lectures Lecture attendance is not compulsory, and recordings will be uploaded. Overall, I thought that the content was quite lackluster. I think Linda could've gone harder on the pharmaceutical side, since it felt like most of the course (Week 4 onwards) was dedicated more to the societal implications of drugs. But then again --- there are pharmaceutical science students who would be bored to death by this. Okay, fine. On the other hand, George's societal side also felt a bit aimless. I'll just illustrate by using the lecture on Religious and Cultural Drugs. Here's the lecture outline. Opium: history, production, depressant effects, examples of religiocultural use Psilocybin: history, production, hallucinogenic effects, religiocultural use Cannabis: history, production, psychoactive effects, religiocultural use Cocaine: history, production, stimulant effects, religiocultural use First of all --- that's very densely packed with information! Second of all, it felt like an aimless wander. It was like --- here's a drug. Here's the history. Here's how you can add acetyl groups and become even more addicted. It makes you depressed. It's an anaesthetic and cough suppressant and anti-diarrheal substance. Oh BTW Sumerians used it. And also Crete and Egypt. I would much rather prefer an indepth, interdisciplinary, case-based format. Here's an example. Cocaine: history, production, stimulant effects Case Study: Urban US (1980s) -Reaganomics, deindustrialisation, urban decay -Invention of crack, then dump the pharmaceutical side and explain how it helps with economics to weave the disciplines better -War against Drugs, black communities, hiphop as protest -Legacy and impacts today Something like this for one lecture. Instead of hopping from OxyContin to Tobacco to PEDs. Again --- it's personal preference. But I feel like I would gain way more out of this, and it'd make lectures more memorable. As of right now, I cannot recall anything significant from the lectures. It was all a prolonged blur of commonsensical facts that I would've known. Okay, I'm being harsh, because there were interesting things such as pharmacovigilance and a Normalisation Thesis or whatever. In fact, I think Lectures were the weakest part, by far, of this course. I just feel that there needs to be more weightlifting in terms of the societal aspect. For example, my other HS29xx class involved discussions on the morality of awareness, parallels between abuse and pornography, ethics and neocolonialism, etc. From this class, insights such as "every policy creates winners and losers" are clear, and of course ultimately extremely important for every person to know, but I'd love some higher-order insights that'd really hammer this course into my memories. Like how pathologising drug users legitimises state violence and how psychiatrists become in control of the populace, the social construction of drugs and medication, the body under capitalism is expected to be productive and sober, etc. Well, of course I can write about this in my assessments and whatnot. But more interesting stuff please! Or maybe I wasn't paying attention and that these concepts were actually mentioned. But that'd reinforce my point --- I don't want these things to be one-liners. I want them to become full-fledged discussions. The Tutorials I'm going to write about this here, because Linda attends every tutorial session (that I know of) like the hardworking queen she is, so tutorial experience will be fairly standardised. In these tutorials, there seem to be a bunch of graduate students, presumably from the pharmaceutical side due to their lack of humanities weightlifting, guiding discussions. If you could call it that --- because in the very first tutorial session, I was in shock of how it was conducted. It was just, here's a question. Someone answer. Okay thank you. Next question is this. Someone answer. Etc. Of course, it got better as the sessions went on, no doubt thanks to Queen Linda's efforts. Okay, I suspect that Prof Linda wanted to give these grad students(?) some experience in teaching, which is cool and all. But it really was an affliction on my experience --- I would've much rather they shadowed Linda before actually conducting lessons. But even then, these improvements were not enough, as tutorials were far eclipsed by the other HS29xx module that I was reading in the same semester, administered by some frazzled History grad student. That course brought about higher-order insights such as the morality of awareness, which I wrote about earlier. Linda was like a diamond in the rough, really. She was like the oshi that stood out amongst her team. She's like that one backup dancer that outperforms the center. She commanded all the attention In tutorials as she should. Okay, I will stop glazing. Anyway, she decided to slay the tutorials by actually promoting back-and-forth debate-style discussions, which should've happened from the first tutorial anyway! And she even suggested the class to form an opinion and stand on both sides of the room and debate. I was really all for it. Anything to make the tutorials more exciting. And also because I can't sit still. Let me continue singing praises. I can really tell that Prof. Linda cares about the class. She posts very frequent feedback sessions and polls, and really checks in with the students. She posts very good summaries of exam statistics, reassuring students that the arts-science gap isn't as big as perceived. The Assessments Okay, this review is getting too long. But I'm committed to giving future batches a lucid picture of the HS2915 experience (TM). A majority of the students disliked the class participation component, as indicated by a lecture poll. And for good reason. It just isn't clear what I should do to participate, and how much is actually required. Prof. Linda is nice by providing numerous outlets such as Canvas boards, talking in lectures, talking in tutorials, solving some crossword puzzles or something, and so on. But I'm sure it makes people confused --- what exactly do we do? How much? And maybe the point is to encourage people to do everything and prioritise contributions to discussions rather than ticking a checkbox for grades. But realistically, I've got so many other courses to juggle. This unclear rubric significantly disrupts my module experience. And I'm speaking as someone who yaps non-stop in tutorials. Again, no easy solution here. A transparent class participation rubric would result in people doing the bare minimum, unless they love yapping. I acknowledge that it's not easy. But you got this Linda. Other than class participation, there were 1000 other components towards the final grade that really just made me annoyed. There's just so many of them --- it feels like a never-ending checkbox, like a pest that won't stop bugging you. But, to be honest, they felt way more rewarding compared to lectures. Okay that's not a tall order. For the Post-Lecture Quizzes, you're supposed to spend 5 minutes answering 5 easy MCQ questions after every lecture. It closes like 18 hours after the end of every lecture. Don't spend too much time on this. GPT if you're lazy. For the PCCM, you're supposed to select a drug and draw a mindmap with interdisciplinary connections. This was great, because I keep drawing maps in my mind, trying to connect the disciplines --- how the drug economies are facilitated by yet impact political decisions, how economies and societies are inseparable, how literature reflects and in turn shapes societal thought, how literature is often adapted into films with the added dimension of visual techniques. But then I realised that my mindmap was super vague. I didn't know how exactly Linda wanted my mindmap to look like. But it's okay, I just winged it. And you're supposed to design a poster or something, I don't know. I didn't get feedback on it until Week 12, and even then, it was just a grade. I wish the lecturers would tell me how much they enjoyed or hated my poster, the parts they found intriguing and the parts they found unclear. But that could lead to some lengthy debates for marks and arguments about subjectivities, and that it takes a lot of effort to write comments for each student amidst grading the 1000 other components, so I understand the decision just to leave a final mark there. For the midterm, a double-sided A4 cheatsheet is allowed. There were Multiple Choice Questions, Multiple Response Questions, and some cloze passage reading comprehension thing. I found some questions to be flawed. [May be remembering wrongly] Question: What's the maximum penalty for a first-time drug user in Singapore? 1) Fine up to $10000 2) Fine up to $20000 3) Up to 10 years in prison 4) Up to 20 years in prison 5) Mandatory death penalty I ruled out options (1) and (2), since I figured that wealth shouldn't be a major factor --- punishments for both the poor and rich should be fair in theory. I ruled out (5), too, since MDP is only for drug importation. So I was left with a coinflip, and lost it. The answer was (4), and not (3). I get the same mark as someone who thinks that drug consumption leads to the MDP. :( I also found some questions to surprisingly test really niche facts presented in the lectures. Like about the Hmong, the Chinh, or whatever. Apparently, you're supposed to be able to identify from the clues in the question, but that felt way too obscure. It felt less about knowing the concepts, and more about remembering what the lecture covered. Which is valid, I guess, but I wouldn't have set that Hmong question. I'm glad I remembered hearing the word "Hmong" somewhere before. The strategy report was just a 1000-word essay outlining two strategies for a specific case study. Since it was due one week after the fourth tutorial (I believe), Prof didn't expect crazy levels of effort. There's not much to say here. I feel like one person could just complete this entire essay, so you'll be fine even if your teammates aren't pulling their weight. But my teammates were cool. <3 For the debate, you're supposed to argue back and forth. I'm tired of typing. Message to Prospective Students Hands down one of the nicest teaching teams I've seen. Linda in particular is a highlight. Hands down one of the least meaningful modules for me. This could in part be due to the fact that this module wasn't my first choice. If you feel like putting in effort, just put in the effort. If you don't feel like putting in effort, just don't put in the effort then S/U.
