author: meower
message: MA2101 Linear Algebra II ~a very polarising module AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Brett McInnes Textbook: The lecture notes with font size 48 Topics 1. Preliminaries: recap of taken-for-granted ideas such as matrices and change-of-basis 2. Vector Spaces: axioms, subspaces, direct sums, dimension (and its welldefinedness), orthogonal complement, basis as an isomorphism 3. Linear Mappings: linear operators, dual vectors, rank-nullity theorem, basis of linear maps, the matrix 4. Classifying Linear Mappings: triangularisability, JCF 5. Geometries: inner product, bilinear forms and its basis, gram-schmidt, riesz representation, sesquilinear forms and its basis, schur triangularisation, spectral theorem 6. Determinant: multilinear form, n-form and its basis, transpose, determinant, volume form Assessment Components and Timeline 25% Quiz 1 (Read: Midterm 1) (~Week 6) 25% Quiz 2 (Read: Midterm 2) (~Week 9?) 50% Finals Examplify is required for the finals. 🥀🥀 Uhhh... Prof Brett is really cool, delivering his lectures and interacting with students with unapologetic style. By now, there have been plenty of reviews outlining the styles of MA2101 Linear Algebra II in Semester 1 and Semester 2. They're basically worlds apart. What I want to do is go against the grain. There's Not Enough Rigour...? The whole relationship between intuition and rigour is one of the most widely debated things amongst mathematicians and even philosophers. In this case, Prof's treatment is sufficiently rigorous. While it's true that the lecture notes tend to adopt a more verbal conversational style, there is an absolutely clear demarcation of Definitions, Theorems, and Proofs. This is not open for debate. Prof has spent 4 pages proving the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra. He has proven the Spectral Theorem, he has proven the Rank-Nullity Theorem, he has proven the Cayley-Hamilton Theorem as well. This is not open for debate. The whole idea of "rigour" is subjective, anyway. But do not conflate rigour with mathematical correctness or depth. Prof Brett's course is very rich in details. Here's how Cayley-Hamilton was proven, which was issued as a tutorial exercise. "Prove the Cayley-Hamilton theorem for diagonalisable matrices. Now, let delta be any non-zero real, and define a matrix M = [1+delta, tan phi; 0, 1]. We know that the Cayley-Hamilton theorem holds for this matrix, since it is diagonalisable. By taking the limit delta -> 0, prove that this shearing matrix satisfies the Cayley-Hamilton theorem. It's easy to use this kind of argument, combined with triangularisability, to give a proof of the Cayley-Hamilton theorem for all complex matrices." In the solution sheet, he further elaborates. "In general, the CH theorem for non-diagonalisable matrices can be proven by taking a limit in the CH theorem for diagonalisable matrices". Surely this is a sufficient explanation. I get that some parts may be slightly unclear. For example, despite talking about a limit, all topological discussion was avoided --- we don't know whether CH is preserved by limits. But I think that Prof's proof sketches provide great insight into the result. It is the job of a mathematician to convert this "intuitive" or "verbal" argument into something more "rigorous". Now, let's use https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cayley%E2%80%93Hamilton_theorem to compare. The proofs are more thoroughly written, but less accessible than Prof's explanation. In conclusion, I'd gladly take Prof's intuitive explanation. Of course, he should probably explain more about how CH is preserved by taking a limit, and refine some of the rougher edges --- rigour serves as a tool to sharpen our intuition. But the big picture is there. But I Do Understand the Concerns... Concern 1: Font size I do think that the ridiculously huge font size was kinda annoying to read through. Concern 2: Tutorials I highly dislike the fact that important results are hidden in the tutorials. This is just personal preference, but I like having all the important results in the lecture notes. This is also why I hate Dummit and Foote. I just disliked the tutorials, overall. On a conceptual level, they were amazing. We'd do even more cool stuff like getting a coordinate-free definition of a trace, or finding an isomorphism from finite-dimensional V to dual(dual(V)). Good stuff. However, they just felt like an extension of the lecture notes that should've just been in the lecture notes in the first place. As a result, I felt that I was completely unprepared for the relatively computational style of the Midterms and Finals --- they're MCQ on Examplify with Prof reading your working and bad handwriting. An exam question would ask you to compute some dual vector and tensor product thing, which is totally doable without practice, but I would've really liked to have like... more computational practice, I guess. Tutorials were basically a second Lecture. Once, I even resorted to getting GPT to generate some questions involving computing the trace of a 2x2 matrix using its coordinate-free definitions. I think the tutorials didn't have anything like that at all, since it was so busy trying to motivate the coordinate-free definition of transpose or trace or whatever. I respect that --- just give me computational exercises! Also, no PYPs. Concern 3: Exam format examplify thing I won't go over how exams are administered. Just know that it involves Canvas and a phone camera. And for the finals, just add Examplify to the mix. So you're answering Examplify questions, taking pictures, then answering the questions again on Canvas for some record-keeping purposes or whatever. First of all --- why not just have a booklet of questions and answers? I think it's more convenient than taking pictures. Second of all, I ended up going over how exams are administered. Prof Knows What He's Doing I 100% trust Prof in what he's doing. He's aware of some complaints, including the ones that I have. MCQ Exams --- An intentional choice to allow students to start from somewhere with an idea in mind "Easy" Exams --- To make it more accessible(?) Verbal Lecture Notes --- To make it more engaging You get the point. I disagree with some of his choices, but I respect that they were an intentional part of his teaching style. Personal Thoughts I decided to read this module under Brett to get a taste of his teaching style that a lot of people were talking about. And I got exactly that. On one hand, I really hate the tutorial format. On the other hand, I really respect his teaching style/ideas. Some people may say that the exams are easy --- but this was the hardest exam I took this semester! ST4238 and MA3211 were way easier. Let me just drop the finals questions (numbers may be wrong) here, without the useless MCQ options since most of the correct answers were "none of the above", to help future batches study. You're welcome guys! Q1: A 3x3 matrix has eigenvalues 2 and 2 + sqrt 2. Prove that it defines a complex inner product. Then, find the angle between this 3x1 vector and that 3x1 vector in a complex inner product space. (Required a tutorial result) Q2: Find the transition matrix for turning this 3x3 matrix into its JCF. (Standard question but I lacked some practice and was winging it, skill issue) Q3: Find the JCF of 2_{8x8} (Similar to a tutorial question) Q4: What is the range of values that an angle between two PSD matrices (using Frobenius inner product) can take on? Q5: Given an inner product involving integral of pq (?), find the dual basis of some polynomial basis. Then find the determinant (Left it blank, tutorial practice only had a diagonal thing, skill issue) Message to Prospective Students Prof Brett has a very distinct style, which may be quite polarising. Make sure to compare the Sem 1 and Sem 2 reviews carefully.

author: meower
message: ST4238/MA4251 Stochastic Processes II ~intuiting measure theory AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Wang Wanjie Textbook: Introduction to Probability Models by Sheldon Ross Topics 1. Poisson Processes - specifications, generalisations 2. Continuous-Time Markov Chains - specifications, important examples, properties 3. Martingales - discrete-time, filtration, continuous-time 4. Renewal Processes - definition, generalisations 5. Brownian Motions - definition, generalisations, Gaussian processes Assessment Components and Timeline 10% Tutorial Submissions (~Weeks 3-13) 20% Group Work (~You choose the week) 20% Midterm (~Week 7) 50% Finals There is no peer review. Not that it matters here. Quick Disclaimers For some reason, NUSMods doesn't sync the ST4238 and MA4251 pages, so make sure to check both of them. They're literally the same mod with the same content and same exam venue. Also, this module is offered right after Wang Wanjie's Stochastic Processes I in AY24/25 Sem 1, so many things apply from the previous courses. Namely, the tutorial submissions work the same way as before. Make sure to check both the ST3236 and MA3238 pages. The Experience According to an old Facebook review written in like, 2018, or something, this module is easier than Stochastic Processes I. Well, that's not true. I mean, it isn't crazy hard, but you should be prepared to work a bit. Weeks 1 to 2 were boring, since she was recapping the specifications of Poisson processes taught in Stochastic Processes I (I still maintain that she shouldn't have introduced Poisson processes at the end of that course). The course started to pick up by Week 3. It was quite a change of pace for me, since there is slightly more content here compared to Stochastic Processes I. I recall just sleeping and relaxing through the first course, but here, it felt like there was way more things to do. Lectures are thankfully recorded. The 10am time slot, combined with one of the venues being some obscure UTown Auditorium 3 hidden somewhere inside one of the NUSC buildings, highly discouraged me from attending lectures. However, I still did pop by every now and then on the Thursday time slots, since it was more convenient, located at LT28 at the Faculty of Science (as it should). For the group project, registrations for lecture typesetting, tutorial presentation, and problem set completion opened somewhere around Week 3. You could do it solo or with a group. If done in a group, everyone gets the same mark. Once the groups are formed, each group has to select one of the three tasks, and one of the weeks. So I check Canvas 10 minutes after the task registration opened, and ALL the lecture typesetting tasks from Week 3 to 13 were taken. Wow, talk about cutthroat competition. Meanwhile, the tutorial presentation slots were largely untouched. I completely agreed with the mass decision to do lecture typesetting, since tutorial presentations were based on correctness, and were apparently graded based on clarity and time control, putting huge pressure on speakers. Meanwhile, lecture typesetting, while still based on correctness and format adherence, can be done at a more relaxed pace. Prof saw this and decided to introduce the third task, problem set completion, so I immediately grabbed that as opposed to a live presentation with heavy burden of execution. The extra problem sets are basically harder versions of the tutorials, also marked for correctness. Prudent as I am, I chose to get it done ASAP at the start of the semester. In any case, things were fine after that. Even though I somehow got 0/10 for the True/False questions during the midterms. It was like five questions with no negative marking, and I got it exactly wrong. Random guessing would literally have been better. It was something like "every M/M/s queue is a birth-death process" and "every birth-death process is a M/M/s queue" and I got it the wrong way around 🥀🥀🥀 And then I just kinda zoned out for the rest of the semester, putting in an average amount of effort. I don't know, the second half was just a blur to me. For finals, a double-sided A4 cheatsheet is allowed. No T/F questions. I think there was like one PYP available without solutions. Other than PYPs, I was quite grateful to Prof for uploading 10 Extra Exercises with solutions (done as part of the Group Work), which provided ample practice. Most of the questions were adapted from tutorials and lecture examples --- Prof's exams are quite friendly and accessible. A general rule that I followed was not to be too caught up in the details. For instance, when conditioning on N arrivals to obtain some ordered statistics or whatever, I just argued that "since N people have arrived by the 20 min mark, we expect each of the N people to have waited for 10min". Something like that. Of course I didn't write it out in words --- I just flubbed the notation here and there. I think Prof just saw the final numerical answer and was satisfied. As for the martingales and filtration topic, I was deeply confused and trying to figure out the definition of a sigma-field. I only found a convenient intuition for it right before the exam. Let's say that F_n = sigma(X_1, X_2, ..., X_n). This means that when you're given F_3 (e.g. E[X_4 | F_3]), you have information of what X_1, X_2, X_3 are. Is information of X_1, X_2, X_3 weaker than X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4? Yes. So F_3 is in F_4. Because F_1 is in F_2 is in F_3 is in F_4... we say that it's a filtration. Say that X_{n+1} = X_n + \xi_{n+1} where \xi is 1 or -1 with probability 0.5, basically a symmetric random walk on the integers. Given F_3, we already know what X_1, X_2, X_3 are. So we know what \xi_1, \xi_2, \xi_3 are. So E(\xi_3 | F_3) = \xi_3. But we don't know what \xi_4 is yet, so E(\xi_4 | F_3) = 0. Cause it's 1 or -1 with equal probability, and we don't know what it is yet. Or something like that, whatever. I'm going to learn measure theory and probability theory, so hopefully my intuition gets further refined. By the way, Prof uses \subset in the lecture slides to mean \subseteq. 🥀🥀🥀 Message to Prospective Students Take this course. I don't think it's a high-priority module, but it's quite chill, I think. Why does the website say I've already made this comment smh (it somehow detects my review under ST4238 even though it isn't shown here?)

author: meower
message: MA3211 Complex Analysis I ~no, complex analysis 2 does not and will not exist AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Dinh Tien Cuong Textbook: Lecture handouts, with the main references being Brown/Ahlfors/Conway (GTM 11)/Lang (GTM 103) Topics 1. The usual Complex Analysis I contents as covered in previous years 2. No Complex Analysis II speedrun contents (whew) Assessment Components and Timeline 30% Midterm (~Week 7) 70% Finals I Was Searching for the Course Information While writing down the assessment components, I realised that I didn't even know the finals weightage. I just assumed it was, like, half-half. Maybe I should've put in more effort into my finals (skull). Yeah, the course information wasn't covered during the lectures, and is hidden in some obscure "Slides" folder. The Lectures This is the second math class that I attended every lecture for in 2 years. Surprisingly, I really like the lectures, because there's no room to slack off. The pace is pretty much perfect for me, somehow. It's not fast enough to be incomprehensible, but not slow enough to make me sleep. That said, self-studying the lecture handouts is probably sufficient to understand the content to be honest --- you won't miss out on too much, since Prof doesn't stray too far away from the notes, except for when he mentions the Lakes of Wada (skull). One thing to note is that Prof doesn't pause at all. He's basically Chatting for an hour and a half straight. Very consistently, might I add, since he started and stopped at the exact same timings throughout the semester. It took me some time to get used to, and I usually just mental boom at the 50-minute mark, working with like a quarter of my brain power afterwards. But after every lecture, I always feel like I've learnt a lot! Other than that, some lectures feel useless, to put it bluntly. The first two lectures (Week 1) were basically recaps of the fundamentals of complex numbers learnt in high school previously, as well as point-set topology, which made me quite bored. Okay, maybe he's required to do that. Preliminaries are important, I guess. The last two lectures were devoted to looking at some key theorems in Complex Analysis without proof (Week 12 Lecture 1) and catching up on some tutorial (Week 12 Lecture 2), which felt quite aimless. I feel like Prof should have just stopped after wrapping up the last chapter (Week 11 Lecture 2). Week 12 Lecture 1 involved Prof stating a bunch of results such as rigidity and Koebe Quarter without proof, which felt a bit meaningless to me. I mean, I get that it's good to have a rough overview of some important theorems, but I would've liked to prove them too! I'm starting to think that he should've just done his Complex Analysis 2 speedrun for this module (/s)(unless?). At least I now know that a Mobius strip can be constructed using equivalence classes or something. Okay, I might be sounding too negative --- so I'll just have to balance it out by singing words of praise. Prof is really an excellent lecturer, and I'll try to read more of his classes (subject to modrekt circumstances). The Tutorials Most of the module is computational in nature, and therefore, not too difficult, contrary to my expectations. It's mostly just applying Cauchy-Riemann here and there, finding some harmonic conjugates, computing residues, doing some contour integration, etc. Actually, now I've got a great idea for lecture content. Prof. Dinh should just become a contour integral farmer and do contour integrals for the entirety of Week 12. This is because the tutorials only cover integrals of polynomial/polynomial and trig/polynomial. While revising for finals, I wasn't sure whether log/polynomial would be tested --- so I googled a bunch of them. Some computations get weird, like choosing different non-principal branch cuts for Log. This is the perfect filler content really. Message to Prospective Students Since Prof Dinh will likely not teach this course next year, this review may not be too relevant. But thanks for reading~ And I hope this provides some insight into Dinh's lecture style and general MA3211 details. Again, classes are highly prof-dependent.

author: meower
message: HS2923 Human-Animal Relations: History, Biodiversity & Ethics ~cat pfp reads an animal module, shocker AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Dr. Gregory Clancey Textbook: Animals and Society: An Introduction to Human-Animal Studies (by Margo DeMello) Topics 1. Animals 2. Philosophy of Animals 3. Domesticating Animals 4. Eating Animals 5. Not Eating Animals 6. Worshipping Animals 7. Cherishing Animals 8. Entertainment Animals 9. Protecting Animals 10. Medicalising Animals 11. Eliminating Animals 12. Conserving Animals Assessment Components and Timeline 25% Class Participation 35% Individual Essay (~Week 7) -- 1200 words 40% Group Essay (~Finals Recess Week) -- 3000 words There is a "soft" peer review in the form of a work allocation statement at the end of the group essay. Module Structure The two biggest pull factors for me reading this module, other than HS29xx being compulsory for graduation and my general love for animals, were that 1) this module doesn't have finals, so it helps ease the workload at the end of the semester 2) the disciplines aren't compartmentalised --- there isn't a discrete "Psychology Approach" or "Economic Approach" or "Political Approach" to animals that runs counter to the spirit of interdisciplinarity and it really delivered in those aspects. Unfortunately, Prof. Clancey sounds really bored in the compulsory lectures (complete with an attendance sheet). I mean, I get the animal-loving vibe from him, but it feels like he'd rather be going to Clementi and rescuing cats rather than stoning in front of 150 bored students. And I don't blame him for that, to be honest. What I do blame him for is opening the semester with a detail-dense lecture on... philosophers. It's great to know about Peter Singer and Jeremy Bentham and all, but I really wanted to fall asleep. The rest of the lectures were by and large fine, but nothing too outstanding for me. Wait, actually, there was something outstanding. Not in a good way. This guy keeps playing so many videos in class, but I really have to question the slant or intention of these videos. On the one hand, you have the professor showing Dodo (seriously) videos and feel-good stories about animals being rehabilitated and whatnot. I was just rolling my eyes. That whole channel is so ethically sus, it warrants a whole tutorial discussion tbh. And on the other hand, you have the professor showing an entire movie during the last lecture. It's apparently award-winning or whatever, but I wasn't too interested. Also, I got clocked because I've watched quite a lot of the in-class videos already from my daily utube brainrot, like that one cat saving some kid from a dog or that one border knowing 1000 words or whatever. Okay, let me say something positive about the whole in-class video shtick --- that one timelapse of a bird constructing its nest was honestly really beautiful. Tutorials, as usual, are a dice roll. Have fun. Personally, my tutor sounded very anxious and frazzled, but hidden beneath that rapping were actually insightful ideas. I made sure to let him know too, but I feel like the whole class was annoyed, or hopefully slightly humored, by my onz-ness. I swear I'm not sucking up for participation marks, and I legit really love yapping and talking, especially if it's about animals. Okay half the time I'm speaking rubbish and freestyling sorry. In any case, I thought the tutorial quality was really good, if possibly a bit aimless (maybe idk) due to the nature and generality of the module. Essays One thing to note is that you're actually allowed to choose your groups, kind of. In the first/second tutorial class, students propose several topics. Then, you bid for your preferred topic, or something like that. I'm pretty sure most students are topic-agnostic, so you can easily squeeze into a group with your friends somewhere. My class had a cat welfare group and a dog welfare group and some zoo group and some exotic parrot group or something I don't really know. Whatever I picked based on my favorite animal hehe I really liked the flexibility of topics. You can write about anything you want, subject to approval, for both the individual and group essays. Prof is probably tired of reading the same thing over and over again, and I don't blame him at all. Closing Comments Last thing I'd have to mention is that for a CHS module, this course has relatively little course components. It's just participation (TM), a mid-semester individual essay, and an end-semester group essay. I took another HS29xx module at the same time as this, and I wasn't having fun with the 1000 components involved. Yeah I debated in my mind whether I should write about the other HS29xx module I was reading, but it's probably irrelevant anyway. And even though it sounds like I'm being really negative regarding the lecturer (I'm only slightly negative), I still have to say that I learnt quite a bit (writing vaguely here because I don't wanna start yapping about specifics), and do not regret reading this module at all. Much love, teaching team~! Message to Prospective Students If you feel like putting in effort, just put in the effort. If you don't feel like putting in effort, just don't put in the effort then S/U. I have a positive opinion overall because it's a tall order to make an animal-related class boring, cmon.
thread_title: HS2923 Human-Animal Relations: History, Biodiversity & Ethics

author: meower
message:
Hi! I don't think it would be good for me to circulate her materials since it is her intellectual property. Not that I have them saved anyway.

If you're looking to do well in class, make sure to contribute to discussions, and if in doubt, just ask Piya herself regarding what you should do to improve. She will be infinitely more helpful than me.

author: meower
message:
Yeah he provides like the past 10 semesters' worth of exam papers and you can find even more on the library database

Probably not very applicable for 24/25 Sem 2 because CCW is teaching it and he'll probably use Rudin and start from metric spaces first

author: meower
message: EC1101E Introduction to Economic Analysis ~huge time sink, but huge reward AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturers: Ong Ee Cheng (Micro), Chan Kok Hoe (Macro) Content Advisory Well, content advisory in the sense that this module is really heavy in content. Like, I spent ~80 hours (I think) on this module throughout the entire semester. The bulk of my efforts were for the macroeconomics taught in the second half of the semester, where each week's lecture slides easily number at the 80-to-100-page-mark. And the information is quite new and dense too, as opposed to the slightly-more-intuitive microeconomics (for me at least). It's as content-heavy as technical Math/CS modules (for me at least). Before anyone mentions that the microeconomics slides also have similar numbers (~80 slides?), I must say that the content density per slide for macroeconomics is much, much harsher compared to spending 10 slides on one supply-demand diagram, for like, bananas or something. Interesting Content, Though Despite the heavy workload, I'd say that this module is worth reading. It teaches a lot of applicable stuff --- the difference between fiscal and monetary policy, money as a construct, free trade, etc. Many readings of real-world events are provided, which enables a good blend of both quantitative and qualitative economic analysis. It's just a good learning experience overall, with economic literacy being important for any individual. Especially since overall prices have been increasing lately RIP. Message to Prospective Students For the Economics minors and second majors and degree-holders, you literally have no choice but to take this module. For non-Economics students, do consider this as an unrestricted elective, since it's a good learning opportunity --- especially the section on macroeconomics. Just be aware of the amount of effort required, and plan your semester accordingly so you don't end up biting off more than you can chew.

author: meower
message: ST3236/MA3238 Stochastic Processes I ~chill mod AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Wang Wanjie The Proverbial Elephant in the Room There were more students in this year's iteration of ST3236/MA3238 Stochastic Processes I, so the professor ended up converting the second half of the second weekly lecture into a mass tutorial session, and removed the need to bid for individual tutorial slots. This ended up making the course content lighter than usual, for better or worse. Well, that's my opinion at least, since it seems that the professor ended up covering the same content as last year's iteration (I think) without having to cut any content due to time constraints. Whatever its effect, it made me feel very relaxed... very chill... I was just in a casual mood, overall. Oh, and tutorial attendance is compulsory. Arrive by the 45-minute-mark, though the professor shows the QR code at random timings. It's like a stochastic process, really. Message to Prospective Students Highly recommend this module to both ST and MA majors. It has a relatively computational flavour (homework has the limit of a 10 x 10 matrix LOL send help), with small proofs limited to things like Regular Implies Irreducible and Aperiodic. Which can be practised. I'm also lowkey biased towards stochastics, hence my recommendation. Oh, and no past-year papers are released BTW.

author: meower
message: ST3236/MA3238 Stochastic Processes I ~chill mod AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Wang Wanjie The Proverbial Elephant in the Room There were more students in this year's iteration of ST3236/MA3238 Stochastic Processes I, so the professor ended up converting the second half of the second weekly lecture into a mass tutorial session, and removed the need to bid for individual tutorial slots. This ended up making the course content lighter than usual, for better or worse. Well, that's my opinion at least, since it seems that the professor ended up covering the same content as last year's iteration (I think) without having to cut any content due to time constraints. Whatever its effect, it made me feel very relaxed... very chill... I was just in a casual mood, overall. Oh, and tutorial attendance is compulsory. Arrive by the 45-minute-mark, though the professor shows the QR code at random timings. It's like a stochastic process, really. Message to Prospective Students Highly recommend this module to both ST and MA majors. It has a relatively computational flavour (homework has the limit of a 10 x 10 matrix LOL send help), with small proofs limited to things like Regular Implies Irreducible and Aperiodic. Which can be practised. I'm also lowkey biased towards stochastics, hence my recommendation. Oh, and no past-year papers are released BTW.

author: meower
message: MA3236 Non-Linear Programming ~2^p inequality cases with n+m+p (possibly non-linear) equations and unknowns. each. AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Lam Xin Yee Mathematical Programming From Wikipedia: "The term mathematical program can refer to a problem formulation of an optimization problem in terms of an objective function and constraint (in this sense, a mathematical program is a specialized and now possibly misleading term that predates the invention of computer programming)". This class essentially involves optimising (potentially non-linear) functions, given some constraints. A brief taster is something like the Lagrange Multipliers from MA2104 Multivariable Calculus, except you're repeating it over and over at some point with bigger systems of equations. Topics and assessment components have already been covered by other reviews. My Thoughts While this module is initially fairly straightforward, especially if you've had some previous experience with Linear Algebra and Topology which allows one to skip the discussion on inner products and norms and compactness and whatnot, the difficulty soon ramps up towards the second half of the course, with the introduction of the dual problem and subdifferentials which have, certainly, very detailed computations. The shift from unconstrained optimisation to constrained optimisation, which itself can be seen as a general case of unconstrained optimisation, is quite challenging and rewarding. Though difficult, I found the concepts introduced in this course quite interesting --- in particular subgradients which serve as an extension of gradients for non-differentiable functions. Furthermore, the course is really helpful in introducing a systematic way to perform optimisation. For instance, previous multivariable optimisation problems would involve optimising f(x) over some closed boundary. The recommended solution would have been to perform optimisation over the interior, then analyse each boundary separately through Lagrange Multipliers (or observation). It turns out that this corresponds to performing the usual constrained optimisation over 2^p inequality cases (Case 1: not lying on any boundary, Case 2: lying on the boundary on inequality constraint A but not on any of the other boundaries, etc). With some caveats. This optimisation class has certainly optimised the way I think about optimisation, forcing me to think about the existence and uniqueness of global optima and whatnot. Message to Prospective Students One possible improvement, or better framed as a pointer for prospective students, is that the lecturer has extremely detailed slides and theorems that outline the correctness of some algorithms. Under time constraints, it is advisable to skip these correctness theorems (which make up, like, half the content). Given that these theorems are not really required, and I've only seen like one question that required it (for the Steepest Descent Method? forgot), I think it would be fair to at least outline that these correctness theorems aren't really required/not the focus. Or maybe it's a me issue, because I might've missed anything the lecturer said in live lectures. Okay, but still read everything anyway. It's a good read. And also because I want to absolve myself of any responsibility when Q-linearity is one day tested for some reason. BTW these theorems and Q-linearity stuff aren't marked as optional. The optional content is also a good read though. Go read about m-strong convexity or whatever.

author: meower

author: meower
message: ST1131 Introduction to Statistics and Statistical Computing ~you can do nothing until week 12 AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: Daisy Topics and assessment outline have already been covered by other reviewers. I'll outline my personal experience instead. My Thoughts Coming into this module having already learnt R and the relevant knowledge from H2 Further Mathematics, I was convinced that it would be a low-workload module. And I was right (~25h?). That said, there were still many learning points for me, and this module serves as good revision for the things I've learnt years ago. In particular, the chapter on linear regression and validating its assumptions was new and interesting to me. It makes me interested in ST3131 Regression Analysis. Somewhat. The course was fairly relaxing throughout the semester, with no real pressure except for weekly tutorial attendance, weekly quizzes, and that one random DataCamp assignment that is just a repeat of the course content. And so I remained relaxed, until the time crunch with a statistical report due on Week 12 and a midterm on Week 13. For those with prior knowledge/experience, it's possible to finish them off in one or two days each. Not that it's a good idea to rush like that. The statistical report was quite fun. Again, I will recommend every student to pick up LaTeX so that the report can be formatted nicely and easily instead of using something like Word. I think markup languages like LaTeX are easier to learn than ever before, with LLMs like GPT being able to assist with the burden of learning the syntax. Exam Questions Since there's no past-year papers, I'll try to provide a brief idea of the style of exam questions. It's okay to say this, right? Midterms: Running R commands, assigning stuff, running more R commands, explaining and commenting stuff, running R commands, a random probability question (chance of 3 independent 95% tests failing I think), running R commands, explaining and commenting stuff Finals: Plotting bar graphs based on a 2 x 2 contingency table, R-assisted 2-sample t-test, proving that the best-fit intercept model is the mean of the points, sensitivity and specificity stuff, and interpreting a linear regression model with pictures of the R code given Message to Prospective Students Have a good grasp of interpreting statistical results, be able to prove stuff, be able to run some calculations, be able to draw stuff, be able to code... uhh that's a lot actually. But the content itself isn't that hard. You have to talk your way through statistical results, or something like that. And make sure to use Jason's cheatsheet. My GOAT.

author: meower
message: HSI1000 How Science Works, Why Science Works https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lv7_luRx7Rw AY24/25 Semester 1 Lecturer: HSI Team /startnarratorvoice Welcome to another review of HSI1000. Today, we will outline my subjective experience of the module, since other reviewers have kindly outlined the topics and assessment format. Are you ready? Let's get started. /endnarratorvoice My Thoughts Initially, I came into this module thinking that it wouldn't be too helpful, with it repeating things that I already know. While true to some extent, there's certainly a few things to be learnt here and there; the module's heavy emphasis on breadth inevitably introduces something new, even to someone who has already read about Science quite extensively. I can see the vision of this module --- introduce Science as a whole, then dive into relevant topics whilst detailing the histories behind them and outlining fact-checking skills. It certainly does a good job of being interdisciplinary by blending the history of Science, the psychology of fearmongers, the politics of lobbyists... you get the point. Certainly better than delivering a week-by-week format. To all CHS module planners, do not deliver a week-by-week discipline format. Don't do it. But there are problems --- subtle and not-subtle-at-all. The most obviously problematic part of this course is Workshop 2, which requires the entire class to jointly partake in an online climate simulator... except that you spend the entire time period shifting levers around to make the number drop below 1.5'C. Deforestation levels are too high? Let's decrease it to zero deforestation. Wow, the projected temperature increase decreased from 3.0'C to 2.5'C... let's shift more levers. Wow, the number has decreased. I feel like it was sorely lacking in capturing the essence of climate negotiations. We did have different groups acting as different stakeholders and communicating, but what would've been really cool would be to introduce some kind of lobbyist that would actively try to sabotage class efforts, showing how difficult it is to reduce the projected temperature increase. After all, our politicians can't really just set deforestation levels to zero. And of course, make the 1.5'C target not part of the grading rubric, because our global leaders and elites would fail miserably. The high point of this module, for me at least, is Workshop 3. Each person is required to write a scientific report about tree statistics in some nature park. It was fairly cool to go out and touch grass, then write a report with the whole Abstract-Introduction-Methods-Results-Conclusion format. I'd strongly recommend picking up LaTeX so that you can format things neatly instead of... Word. With up to 350 pages of course notes (it's just a transcript), there will definitely exist some points that I disagree with. For example, the treatment of confidence levels is misleading, and could confuse people. A confidence level is the proportion of interval constructions that contain the true value, not the probability of finding the true value within one interval. It's a subtle difference, but it's something like that. Statisticians, please chime in. Furthermore, I think that parapsychology was dismissed too quickly as a pseudoscience. One can get into a huge discussion about the replication crisis, the file-drawer effect, subconscious bias, and the controversies that surround the field. To be fair, there's a shortage of time. Maybe the biodiversity block's content can be cut or something, I don't know. I would've liked for the replication crisis to have been mentioned at least once throughout the entire course, though. And speaking of the biodiversity block, I hope the HSI team can transcribe that block's content into a prose format, because it really sticks out like a sore thumb. And convert Block 2's content from .docx to .pdf, and send a final merged file for my convenience. I could use pdftk, but I'd rather not. For midterms and finals each, one past-year paper will be provided. Do it under timed conditions, and re-read the notes to familiarise yourself with the content, then just whack the questions with common sense on the day itself. The most troublesome ones will be the questions involving biases, fallacies, and the like. Not too much work is required here --- this module has half the workload of a regular content-heavy module. (~40h?) That's it for my unstructured thoughts. Message to Prospective Students You can SU this. It may be a good option to try for the midterms, and approach the module thereafter based on the midterm results. P.S. I'm using the word "projection" in an everyday casual context, so don't come after me for failing to distinguish between a prediction and a projection as outlined in the first block hehe
thread_title: HSI1000 How Science Works, Why Science Works

author: meower
message: Sounds like there is an underlying current of elitism and sexism among these students and the professor... big yikes

author: meower
message: IT1244 Artificial Intelligence: Technology and Impact ~okay. okay. okay. AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prabhu Natarajan Topics 1. Artificial Intelligence - introduction, opportunities, disruptions, Python, NumPy 2. Searches - breadth-first search, uniform-cost search, depth-first search, greedy best-first search, A* search, local search 3. Supervised Learning - k-nearest neighbours, (multivariate) linear regression, gradient descent, logistic regression 4. Unsupervised Learning - K-means clustering 5. Deep Learning - single and multi-layer neural networks, convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks 6. AI Implementation - hyperparameters, train/validate/test split, confusion matrix, overfitting, accuracy, *F1, *recall, *precision 7. Possible Societal Issues - data security, discrimination, interpretability, misuse, future jobs, AI inequality ^Midterm tests Searches. ^Finals tests Supervised Learning to AI Implementation. *F1, Recall, Precision were tested as part of the confusion matrix but are not explicitly covered during lectures. Assessment Components and Timeline 5% Tutorial Attendance (~ALL weeks) 5% Class Participation (~includes weekly Coursemology quizzes) 20% Assignments (~6 choose 5) -- 5 x 4% 20% Midterm (~Week 7) 25% Group Project (~Week 12/13) 25% Finals There IS a peer review. The group collectively decides each member's contribution in the form of a percentage (%). The Objective Stuff This course is conducted almost exclusively on an own-time-own-target (OTOT) basis; all content and quizzes are available by the beginning of the semester. Lecture lengths vary. They can be as short as 40 minutes in total (introduction) to as long as 1h 30min in total (neural networks part 2). Tutorial is conducted once a week, and last up to 1 hour each. Attendance is compulsory. Everything is done on Coursemology; Canvas is a barren wasteland. The Subjective Stuff Somehow an actually useful CHS module with valuable technical knowledge. Though treated at a surficial level, as expected from an introductory level 1000 module as opposed to its level 2000 and 3000 counterparts, it wonderfully serves its purpose of informing science students about the rough workings and details of AI and Machine Learning algorithms. You don't need crazy theory; you're just taught what Gradient Descent, for example, is helpful for and how to compute it. The project further reinforces this, providing students an opportunity to have a go at implementing AI. Groups aren't assigned, so find some reliable people. There are about ~20 possible projects to choose from, and they involve a wide variety of media such as CSV files, audio files, video files, etc --- this caters to both the uninitiated and AI-lovers. While this module is GOOD on the technical side, the delivery can be improved on. Notably, Prabhu needs to run everything he types through an English checker or GPT; I see numerous grammatical and spelling mistakes littered throughout the assignments and whatnot. Not that major of an issue, but not that minor either. Furthermore, there seems to be a 1-week offset between lectures and quizzes. For example, you'd have weekly quizzes testing concepts from the next week's lecture --- this is incredibly confusing. I just ended up putting off the quizzes until the next week. For example, on Week 9, I would complete the Week 9 Lecture and Week 8 Quiz. Multiple-Choice Questions Exams are almost exclusively MCQ-based, with one or two computation questions here and there which require no intermediate working --- I suppose a measure to save the time and effort required to mark papers. While I'm fine with this, I'll need to let every fellow Linux user out there know that Examplify is required for the exams. I hope Jia Tan helps work on Examplify. Message to Prospective Students Good module for the AI pillar. Or take CS2109S if you're a Math-CS subscriber. Same difference.
thread_title: IT1244 Artificial Intelligence: Technology and Impact

author: meower
message: MA2202 Algebra I ~category-theoretic dummit and foote AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Chee Chin Whye Textbook: Abstract Algebra by Dummit and Foote (hereafter abbreviated DF) Topics 1. DF Chapter 1 - axioms, symmetric groups, homomorphisms and isomorphisms, examples 2. DF Chapter 2 - subgroups, centralisers and normalisers, cyclic subgroups, lattice diagrams 3. DF Chapter 3 - quotient groups, cosets, Lagrange's Theorem, Isomorphism Theorems, alternating group 4. DF Chapter 4 - group actions, Cayley's Theorem, automorphisms, Sylow Theorems *Additional content includes the Universal Properties of Z, Z/nZ, Subgroups, Quotient Groups. *Cosets are introduced before quotient groups unlike in DF. The Objective Stuff Two lectures per week lasting up to 1h 40min each, and three tutorials per week lasting up to 1h each. While students technically bid for one tutorial slot, the tutorial system is abused, with the professor going through different questions for different classes, and expecting/encouraging students to view all tutorial sessions. The Subjective Stuff The course follows DF very closely, with some Category Theory sprinkled in through Universal Properties. For an even more category-theoretic treatment, I've heard that Algebra: Chapter 0 by Paolo Aluffi is a good read, though I've never checked it out myself. With respect to the amount of Category Theory involved in treating the Algebra, DF has the least, and Aluffi has the most; CCW's Algebra (still following DF) would be somewhere in between. Other reviews are fairly accurate; CCW is a joy to listen to, and his working is clear and accessible. Message to Prospective Students Read this module under CCW.

author: meower
message: MA2104 Multivariable Calculus ~this is one of the modules of all time AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Tan Ser Peow Textbook: Multivariable Calculus by James Stewart A Textbook I Like: Vector Calculus by Susan J. Colley Topics 1. Basic Vectors - dot product, cross product, triple product, lines and planes, derivative of products, arc length 2. Surfaces - level curve, contour plot, level surface, quadric surfaces 3. Limits - e-d definition, limit theorems, squeeze theorem, continuity theorems 4. Differentiation - Clairaut's theorem, tangent plane equation, partial differentiation, multivariable differentiability, multivariable chain rule, implicit differentiation, directional derivatives and level curve, local and absolute extrema, saddle______ point, critical point, boundary point, EVT, Lagrange multipliers 5. Integration - Fubini's theorem, double integration, triple integration, change of variables and Jacobian, polar coordinates, cylindrical coordinates, spherical coordinates 6. Line Integrals - line parametrisation, scalar and vector line integrals, conservative fields and criterion, potential function, fundamental theorem for line integrals, Green's Theorem (reverse, extended) 7. Surface Integrals - surface parametrisation, scalar and vector surface integrals, smooth, orientation, divergence and curl, Gauss' theorem, Stokes' theorem Assessment Components and Timeline 8% Quizzes (~Week 4, 6, 10, 13) -- 4 x 2% 8% Homework (~Week 3, 6, 9, 11) -- 4 x 2% 4% Tutorial Attendance -- 8 x 0.5% 20% Midterm (~Week 7) 60% Finals The Objective Stuff This course, Multivariable Calculus (read: the calculus of at most three variables with no mention of linear transformations from Rn to Rm), places great importance in computation of lines (1 variable) and surface (2 variables). While this isn't inherently bad, it'd be good material to cover, considering the fact that all contents were taught by the start of Week 12 --- three lectures' worth of content! Lectures last 1h 30min every week (twice), and tutorials 1h each. Out of the 11 or so tutorials, students must attend at least 8 of them to secure full marks for tutorial attendance --- 0.5% out of 4% every time you go to tutorial. Tip: Pick a convenient time slot and clock your attendance right before tutorial ends. Especially if your tutor just makes students sign on a piece of paper. You can alternatively present your solutions to three of the tutorial questions to clock 2 tutorials' worth of attendance. The Subjective Stuff I don't know. I skipped lectures and went to tutorials five minutes before they ended. Other reviewers are mostly accurate; you need good geometric intuition and the bell curve is cursed. For reference, the upper quartile of the midterms was 45/50 (one mistake). Tips for triple integrals: I just defenestrated geometric intuition and bashed the inequalities; it's easier to replace the bounds that way. Or you can replace the bounds 2 variables at a time if you like to draw. Message to Prospective Students This is a module. I think the Mathematics department should also have this offered as part of Advanced Placement Tests. Actually, can we just have AP Tests for every module? Based.

author: meower
message: MA2116/ST2131 Probability ~a joint race towards mediocrity between GFF and Ross AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Gan Fah Fatt Textbook: A First Course in Probability by Sheldon Ross Topics 1. Combinatorics - Permutations, Combinations, Binomial Theorem; Algebraic and Combinatoric Proofs 2. Probability - Three Axioms of Probability, Principle of Inclusion and Exclusion, Union Bound 3. Conditional Probability - Bayes' Rule, Independence 4. Discrete Random Variables - Binomial, Geometric, Poisson, Hypergeometric RVs; Expectation, Variance 5. Continuous Random Variables - Uniform, Exponential, Normal RVs 6. Joint Random Variables - Sum of RVs, iterated integrals 7. Conditional Distributions and Conditional Expectation - Formula Whacking 8. Limit Theorems - Weak/Strong Law of Large Numbers, Central Limit Theorem, Markov/Chebyshev/Chernoff Inequalities, Moment Generating Functions Basically what you'd find in the Ross textbook, since GFF likes Ross. I was losing my mind while taking this module and am losing my mind writing this review given that the WHOLE Canvas page has been wiped blank (wHy) .;s?1oszxk>? i managed to recover it only because someone asked me for the ma2116 canvas materials previously pog Assessment Components and Timeline 20% In-Lecture Quizzes -- 10 x 2% 30% Midterms (~Week 8) 50% Finals The Objective Stuff Lectures: 1h 30min a week, compulsory, at a hellish 6pm-8pm time slot. Have fun. Strategy: Show up at 7.25pm to clock your attendance/quiz. Tutorials: 1h a week. Optional. I skipped them. No assignments. The Subjective Stuff GFF needs to take some graphics design lessons --- for his lecture notes are in Times New Roman (or some other font to that effect) and his sections and subsections literally have the same size as regular text. He also needs to take some linguistics lessons --- for I tried clarifying the phrasing of a question during the midterms, and he just dismissed me with a quick "never do your tutorial". Hello sir, I do the tutorials. Multiple times. Maybe I forgot about the definition under the intense pressure of the examination? Be more tactful? I'm digging up the probability midterm right now to find the exact phrasing. The question is as follows: "In a freshly shuffled deck of 52 playing cards, what is the probability (correct to 2 decimal places of accuracy) that you are dealt a blackjack (an ace and 10, Jack, Queen or King)?" There are two (or perhaps more) possible interpretations of "an ace and 10, Jack, Queen or King". 1) an ((ace and 10), (Jack), (Queen) or (King)) --- my initial interpretation; my immediate instinct having read widely 2) an ((ace) and ((10), (Jack), (Queen) or (King))) --- the intended interpretation; my guess based on how a game of Blackjack should flow (the ace and 10 from the first interpretation is an incredibly obscure combination that makes no sense, especially when it is somehow equivalent to just a Jack/Queen/King) Anyway, I ended up getting that question right so I'm less salty now. I think I heard my exam neighbour also ask that same question after I asked it. Methinks the professor should try to avoid syntactic and semantic ambiguities. I'm definitely not alone in this. Ross Quite a boring textbook. Just way too many examples for my liking --- one section on continuous random variables had up to like 10 examples... as part of the main text. I personally think there should only be up to 2 or 3 salient examples per concept, with the rest of them relegated to the exercises section (which in Ross' case is actually pretty good). Don't like 30 pages being flooded by examples. Message to Prospective Students Quite a useful and relevant module, possibly on par with CS2040 (for techbros), for finance-related interviews. Professor is annoying to deal with. To mitigate this, turn up to lectures at the exact one-hour-and-twenty-five-minutes mark (7:25pm) to do the in-lecture quiz/attendance (all of them can be solved in your head/ask around for answers). Optional tutorials and no homework is an upside. Lowkey wish the Mathematics department offered this module, alongside MA2001 and MA2002, for the Advanced Placement Tests.

author: meower
message: PS2204 Modern Western Political Thought ~cool dead people AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Dr Matthew Lepori Topics 1. The English Civil War 2. Arguments for Democracy 1: Locke and Rousseau 3. Arguments for Democracy 2: Putney Debates and Sieyes 4. Arguments for Democracy 3: Mill 5. Arguments against Democracy 1: Guizot 6. Arguments against Democracy 2: Montesquieu and Madison 7. Arguments against Democracy 3: Mill 8. The Excluded Women: De Gouges and Mill 9. The Excluded Slaves and Colonies: Douglass and Du Bois 10. The Excluded Proletariats: Marx and Engels Assessment Components and Timeline 35% Midterm (~Week 6) -- 1200 words 45% Final (~Week 13) -- 1200 words 20% Participation -- Attendance and pre-tutorial question submission The Objective Stuff Unrecorded lectures last 1h 30min every week with laptops banned, with the author(s) of the week having their background, ideas, and arguments covered. The slides only have guiding questions; any salient point will come out of the professor's mouth. Because of this, it is advised for students to bring pen and paper for notetaking (or just use some digital tablet thing). Tutorials last 1h 30min as well, but are conducted by the man himself every fortnight instead. Out of all the pre-tutorial question submissions, the professor picks three of them to go through in great detail during the tutorial. Students typically spend 10 minutes discussing a question amongst themselves, before expressing their ideas and debating with the professor for... a long time. Rinse and repeat for the rest of the questions. The reading list is highly curated, all of it required reading, averaging about 20 to 30 pages each week. On Weeks 6 and 13, the midterm and final papers (respectively) are released to students in place of a lecture, with exactly one week for students to complete it. The Subjective Stuff You can tell that Dr Lepori cares a lot about teaching. Each one of his decisions --- the laptop restriction, the pruned reading list, the atypical exam format --- have been consciously made (check Twitter). And yet, I think it would be fair to label this paternalistic. Excessively paternalistic? Maybe. Personally, I wasn't affected by the restriction on laptops at all, since I don't use a laptop to jot down notes during lecture in my other classes. In fact, I don't even watch lectures. Based. To echo the previous review, Dr Lepori neither expects nor wants students to use materials outside of the reading list. Combined with an extremely curated texts with carefully selected snippets, the reading load is extremely light. While 1200 word doesn't seem like a lot for essays, the midterms and finals each requires extreme concise and precise phrasing. As such, it may be quite different from the essays required in other courses. You're basically writing down a bunch of facts with an extremely tight word limit --- a measure taken against chatbots that tend to output flowery and longwinded language. Message to Prospective Students A decent module to clear your Political Theory pillar with. Plus you can S/U it. Or take it as an unrestricted elective like me. Based. Or audit it. Even more based.

author: meower
message: Textbook is Introduction to Real Analysis by Bartle and Sherbert.

author: meower
message: SP1541 Exploring Science Communication Through Popular Science ~I'm going to become the Oshi no Ko. AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: SP Team Topics 1. Scientific Papers - types, formats 2. Reading Papers - reading skills 3. Communicating Papers - explanatory strategies, reader engagement, communicative moves, evaluative language, cohesive devices, sources 4. Topics - genes and evolution, CRISPR, Riemann Hypothesis, prime cicadas, hunger hormones, 17 molecules, Courtroom Statistics, gravitational waves 5. Presenting Papers - assertion-evidence, flow, content, accessibility, visual and vocal delivery Assessment Components and Timeline 5% Group-Based Book Chapter Reflection (~Week 5) -- 300 words 20% Science News Article (~Week 7) -- 800 words 5% Peer Review (~Week 12) -- 2 x some words 30% Science News Article Second Submission (~Week 13) -- 800+200 words 10% Reflective Commentary (~Week 13) -- 350 words 25% Individual Oral Presentation (~Week 12/13) -- 10 minutes 5% Tutorial Participation The Objective Stuff This course, typically taken by CHS students to fulfil their Writing pillar, requires one to become both a TED Talk speaker and a Guardian writer. Crudely speaking, you'll have to talk to the normies about sophisticated stuff by dumbing things down. There are no lectures --- you'll bid directly for the tutorial slot during ModReg, under the guise of "sectional teaching". Pretty similar to DTK1234. Tutorials are conducted once a week and officially allocated 3 hours, but to be honest, they'll probably be wrapped up within 2 to 2.5 hours. Before each tutorial, students are required to complete activities on an online Miro board and finish their pre-tutorial readings. In theory, that is. These requirements (especially the Miro board) eventually became more lax over time, making me question their necessity and effectiveness. The Subjective Stuff Unfortunately, I didn't find this course too helpful, given that I already know how to speak and write to the normies. FFS, I know how to use similes and analogies. I know how to use evaluative language, too. I weasel my way out of arguments all the time like a sneaky politician. Furthermore, this course employs quite the rigid writing structure. You are, quite literally, expected to a follow a strict format in outlining a research paper. I mean, this rigidity is probably a decent guideline for novice writers --- as with the PEEL paragraph structure in high school --- but it just makes me annoyed. My Suggestions Cover different styles of writing in a more nuanced manner. I would love to see something other than Hook-Summary-Background-Rationale-Methodology-Results-Society. And no, this wouldn't bloat the module's content. Not if you cut down on the weird stretch of five weeks in the middle of the term where every class literally just involved the same old repetitive dissection of science articles (albeit with different topics). There doesn't need to be one week dedicated to each of Biology, Mathematics, Chemistry, Statistics, Physics. We get the point, and we appreciate the practice in analysing science articles --- but five weeks is probably too much. Perhaps merge Mathematics and Statistics, or Chemistry and Biology? Or just remove Physics, it's just discount Math anyway (this is a joke, much like Computer Science). Or something. Cut it down to three weeks --- that'll be a good improvement. Tutor is King/Queen As usual. Just permalisten to your tutor, and pray that they are good. I personally found my tutor quite based and dank. The Saving Grace ...is the lack of lectures. I'm so glad that we get to interact and talk in tutorials for like two hours a week. I love talking. Bless. Like literally even if you don't learn anything you get to at least practise speaking and talking and defending your opinions and whatnot. The Assessments Don't be intimidated by what seems to be a severe crunchtime during Weeks 12 and 13. Tutorial sessions end after ~Week 10 in order for students to work on their simultaneously-due Oral Presentation, Science Article, Reflective Commentary, and Peer Reviews. All you have to do is to work on your stuff early. Start on your presentation --- slides and all --- three weeks early. Make sure to finish up your Science News Article (and Reflective Commentary) quickly --- you'll probably be too exhausted after the oral presentation to do so. No script. TBH it was quite fun to become the Oshi no Ko and present my stuff. Message to Prospective Students Too easy of a module if you're good at speaking and writing. Don't get me wrong, it DOES serve its purpose of introducing students to popular science writing --- I just think it needs... more.
thread_title: SP1541 Exploring Science Communication through Popular Science

author: meower
message: MA2108 Mathematical Analysis I ~Uh, so, uh, so anyway, uh, so, uh, we will, uh, use our, uh, discrete mind, okay, to, uh, uh, study, uh, continuous functions, okay. AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: Wing Keung Topics 1. Real Numbers - ordered field axioms, absolute value, triangle inequality 2. Boundedness - bounded above, maximum, supremum, Supremum Property, Archimedean Property, floor, kth-root, Density Theorem 3. Sequences - convergence (epsilon-K), operations on limits of sequences, Squeeze Theorem, Monotone Convergence Theorem, exponential limit, subsequence, Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem, limit superior (using a subsequential limit definition), Cauchy sequence, Cauchy Criterion, contractive sequence, proper divergence 4. Series - partial sums, convergence, geometric and p-series, limit tests (nth term, Cauchy, comparison, limit comparison, ratio, root, alternating series), absolute and conditional convergence, Rearrangement Theorem 5. Limits of Functions - cluster points, epsilon-delta, Sequential Criterions, Squeeze Theorems, one-sided limits 6. Continuous Functions - epsilon-delta continuity and sequential criterion, EVT, IVT, uniform continuity and sequential criterion, Lipschitz continuity, Dirichlet functions 7. Metric Spaces - Euclidean metrics, discrete metric, metric subspaces, neighbourhoods, convergence, open and closed sets, open set continuity, sequential criterion, boundedness, sequential compactness, Heine-Borel Theorem (for sequential compactness) Assessment Components and Timeline 25% Homeworks (~Week 6, 10, 12) -- 3 x 8.33% 25% Midterm (~Week 8) 50% Finals The Objective Stuff This course, Mathematical Analysis I (read: Real Analysis I), focuses almost exclusively on the reals as compared to the more metric-focused approach features in MA2108S, with metrics being relegated to a surficial treatment as part of the last section of the course. It starts off with the field axioms and completeness of the reals, using the Supremum Property (read: Axiom), that is, every nonempty subset of reals with an upper bound has a least upper bound in the set of reals. From there, it does some sequence acrobatics to obtain Cauchy Completeness (Cauchy implies convergent). And then some infinite series and continuity stuff, I guess. Lectures last 1h 40min every week (and are recorded), being 10 minutes longer than usual to make up for the absence of lectures during public holidays. Tutorials are conducted every week, lasting up to 1h each. Students are technically required to present their solutions as assigned. The Subjective Stuff Wing Keung stutters a lot, and his lectures get very boring and sleepy. I skipped them and self-studied as per usual, checking each lecture recording for the appropriate section to stop off at. MA2108 starts off from a more concrete setting compared to its S-counterpart, both a boon and a bane. Personally, I liked the focus on the real line, since I've already worked in the more general setting of metric spaces before --- you really get to see the typical analysis tricks shine through. However, this concreteness comes at a price, namely, lower generality and weaker mathematical power. This abstraction-intuition tradeoff is pretty prevalent throughout all of mathematics. My stance: do both the abstract and the concrete. Since I've already worked in a more abstract setting ("top-down": topological spaces \rightarrow metric spaces \rightarrow real line), this concreteness ("bottom-up": real line \rightarrow metric spaces) value-added my understanding of analysis. The Exams Wing Keung sets the same questions. Every. Time. This disproportionately rewards the grinders. Like me. Here's a typical final exam. 1) Limits. Two of them. Squeeze, factor, exponential limit, do your thing. 2) Some random rational-irrational piecewise function. Regurgitate the solution. 3) Convergence Tests. Two of them. Absolute/conditional convergence. One of them. 4) Some IVT question that involves defining a new function. 5) Some metric definition question, and using open set continuity. 6) Some epsilon-delta. 7) Some convergent recurrent sequence that requires contractive or MCT with induction. 8) Some limit of a floor function. 9) Question 6b. I mean --- I don't think the exam gets as (mentally) relaxing --- or rather, routine --- as this, even with a time crunch (my hands died scribbling down all the solutions). During exam preparation, I pretty much ONLY did Wing Keung's papers. I guess AXL and TWK have very different renditions of Analysis I --- this review is more aptly one for "Wing Keung's Mathematical Analysis I" rather than "Mathematical Analysis I". Message to Prospective Students If you're taking the module under Wing Keung, grind his papers. Work hard, I guess. If you're taking the module under another professor, you can ignore this review.

author: meower
message: HSH1000 The Human Condition ~it's time to doublethink. AY23/24 Semester 2 Lecturer: HSH Team/Dr Loy Hui Chieh Topics 1. The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas - Morality, Utilitarianism 2. The Epic of Gilgamesh - Civilisation, Mortality, Liminality, Anachronism 3. Xunzi - Language, Social Context, Human Nature, Abductive Reasoning 4. No Exit - Historical and Personal Narratives, Sources 5. A Number - Philosophical Theories, Genre and Medium, The Uncanny 6. The Sexual Politics of Meat - Hegemony, Absent Referents Assessment Components and Timeline 3% Surveys 2% Peer Reviews 10% Tutorial Preparation 15% Tutorial Participation 20% Group Discussion Summaries (~1 week after each tutorial) -- 4 x 400 words 30% Quizzes (~Week 3/6/10/13) -- 1 x 24 questions, 3 x 32 questions 20% Final Project (~Week 13) -- 900 words The Objective Stuff This course, compulsory for Year 1 CHS students (despair), is usually preallocated to students alongside HSI1000 How Science Works, Why Science Works and GEA1000 Quantitative Reasoning with Data, neither of which I read (based). It's a tour around the various disciplines categorised under the "Humanities" branch of CHS, including, but not limited to, Philosophy, History, Linguistics, and Theatre Studies. Lectures last 1h to 1h 30min every week, and are recorded, each covering the plot, themes, and ideas of a selected book (or tablets, whatever). I have no idea whether the selected text has to be read before, between, or after the lectures, because I speedread through everything (as I should). Tutorials are conducted every fortnight, and last up to 1.5 hours each. The reading list comprises of the relevant books and some other articles that I don't know about. The Subjective Stuff It's finally time to say my opinions without sounding like a robot. This course is pretty decent. At the very least, I'm glad it didn't reach HSS1000 levels; the disciplines covered in this course are quite integrated without incredibly artificial discipline-of-the-week delivery. The lecturers did a decent job integrating historical texts with philosophical ideas about humans, featuring Chinese philosophers talking about language and linguistics... you get the idea. That said, this module is largely dominated by philosophy --- perhaps due to the very nature of philosophy itself. As usual, the price of trying to cater to a thousand students (rounded up) and conducting a tour guide through the humanities section of CHS is the inevitable focus on breadth over depth and watering down of content. Not that this is an easily fixable trade-off, or one that shouldn't be taken, anyway. I just needed to give the usual disclaimer for CHS mods. The biggest flaw of this course is probably its emphasis on delivering concepts and ideas rather than actually engaging with them in detail. Don't just accept ideas. Fight them! The worst offender of this, and the lecture that alerted me to this problem, was The Sexual Politics of Meat. Dr Cathay Liu is an incredibly fluent and engaging lecturer (the only lecturer I remember other than the theatre kid's weird lecture/play/skit), and probably couldn't do much because this text was shafted and allocated just one lecture, but it really rubbed me the wrong way to have Carol Adams' ideas --- controversial ones --- be outlined and not discussed in sufficient detail. It felt like a mere report of facts and a proselytising session. "Meat bad! Meat bad!". Where's the balanced viewpoint? At least just give me a one-liner PLEASE. Can we also talk about how most idealism comes from a place of privilege? This brings me to my next point --- I would like a FULL DISCLOSURE of positionality for each lecture, especially for more controversial ones like The Sexual Politics of Meat. Don't get me wrong, I LOVE talking about language, erasure, psycholinguistics, the whole package. 1984 is awesome. I know meat has its destructive aspects. But I feel like a vegan in the teaching team just sneaked in their ideals in one lecture. This feels really wrong. REALLY wrong. I'm going to join the teaching team and add one lecture on why we should all be furries soon. Watch me. You know what? I just realised that writing these reviews really forces me to challenge module structures. I'm going to write more reviews. Fight ideas, right? Tutor is King/Queen As usual. If you're going to read this course, just listen to the tutor --- their instructions supersede all others'. Hell, they have full control over your experience --- tailoring tutorial sessions, and by extension, the final project, to their specialty. I'm not going to write much about my tutor, because the tutorial experience is rather variable, and also in part because I've already written the important stuff in the official module feedback. The Assessments Just fill in surveys (on Canvas) and peer reviews (on TEAMMATES) for free marks. Just complete your online reading tasks (on Perusall), usually asking or responding to questions, for free marks. Go to tutorials and just talk for free marks. Group Discussion Summaries, on the other hand, is a mixed bag. Since groups are pre-allocated, just pray for a good team, I guess. And be a good teammate. After every tutorial session, each group has to get together for a short 30-minute group meeting to expound and elaborate ideas and concepts outlined in lectures and tutorials, and summarise the meeting details within 400 words. These were... okay, I guess. The Online Quizzes are also a mixed bag, well, for me at least. They're pretty difficult, as evidenced by the fact that no one got full marks --- without negative marking and each question being T/F --- for all the quizzes (except for the first one, which is relatively short). These quiz questions are REALLY subtle. You get 3 attempts, so use each of them wisely. A good strategy is to have two attempts be identical, except one with TRUE and one with FALSE on the question that seems the hardest. You can try to --- and are in fact encouraged to --- challenge the answer key and argue with the teaching team, though I was too unbothered to do so. As for the final project, the topic and theme varies based on instructor. Expect yourself to reflect on your experiences and what you've learnt throughout the semester; record important things throughout the semester to make your life easier. Personally, I just used the most recent learning outcomes that I could remember. Message to Prospective Students This module exists. S/U if you hate it or just need to burn the credits in Year 1 Semester 2. Some cool stuff here and there. Positionality needs to be disclosed.

author: meower
message: CFG1002 Career Catalyst you can literally finish this in one day AY23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: CFG Team Topics 1. Resumes and Cover Letters 2. Personal Branding 3. Global Mindset 4. Interviews 5. Internships and Networking Assessment Components and Timeline Attend two lectures 20% Canvas Quizzes (At least 12/20) 10% NUS ConnectUS Account 10% NUS IAAS Account 20% VMock Elevator Pitch 40% VMock Resume (At least 60/100) All of them due by Week 6/13. Objectivities This module runs from Week 1 to Week 6, or Week 7 to Week 13. You can choose either schedule, though I chose to complete it from Week 1 to Week 6. Materials are categorised into 6 sections, each of them to be completed every week. However, since they're pretty much all open-access on Canvas, this module can technically be completed on the first day of the semester (save the two in-person lectures, 1.5 hours each on Weeks 1/7 and 4/10). Each section has about 10-20 pages worth of material, and can be completed in under an hour. The ConnectUS and IAAS accounts are very easy to set up, and though filling up details is mildly inconvenient, you can use these accounts later on to apply for internships. Same goes for the resume -- create it properly rather than haphazardly trying to fulfil the CFG completion requirements. The Elevator Pitch is a meme -- just spend 1 minute talking. The score doesn't matter. Subjectivities I found this module useful in understanding the professional working world -- though there is only so much one can learn about in theory. Message to Prospective Students Free 2MCs. Spend your time creating a proper resume.
