author: meower
message: MA1100T Basic Discrete Mathematics (T) omg wtf is going on AY23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: KING Jun Le Topics 1. Logic - proposition, truth table, arity n operators, logical equivalence, predicate, existential and universal quantifiers 2. Proofs - direct, contrapositive, contradiction, cases, counterexample, (strong) induction, well-ordering principle, infinite descent 3. Sets - Russell's Paradox, set operations and identities, power set, Cartesian product, arbitrary unions and intersections, ZF axioms, relations, well-orders 4. Functions - (co)domain, (pre)image, well-definedness, range, inclusion, surjection, injection, bijection, (left/right) inverse and composition, restriction, set (pre)image, category of sets 5. Number Theory - divide operator, infinitude of primes, division theorem, principal Z-ideal, Bezout's identity, gcd, Euclid's lemma, FTA, lcm, modular addition and multiplication, congruence class, universal property, square-free factorisation 6. Equivalence Relations - equivalence class, quotient, projection, set of functions (Maps), partition, construction of Z and Q 7. Cardinality - equinumerous, (in)finite, Cantor's theorem, Pigeonhole, cardinality and mappings, boundedness and finiteness, countably infinite, uncountable, Choice, Cantor-Schroder-Bernstein theorem, comparability of injections, Dedekind infinite, chain, Zorn's lemma, continuum hypothesis 8. Real Numbers - Archimedean property, existence of infinitesimals, density, Dedekind Completeness, supremum, Dedekind cut, construction of R Assessment Components and Timeline 24% Homeworks (~Week 3, 5, 9, 13) -- 4 x 6% 13% Midterm 1 (~Week 6) 13% Midterm 2 (~Week 9) 50% Finals Objectivities Two live unrecorded lectures per week lasting 1.5 hours each, one tutorial per week lasting 1 hour. Try to self-study outside of class. The other reviews (by mathlover and riceyrice) are by and large very accurate, so I'll try to avoid repeating what they've said. Subjectivities WTF IS GOING ON HELP ME?!?!?!?! Also wish there were more practice/past-year papers, though this problem shall be alleviated over time since this module is relatively new (at least that's what I've heard). Message to Prospective Students It's literally an S mod that you can S/U so just give it a try and see what happens.

author: meower
message: DTK1234 Design Thinking every idea is a good idea (except for yours) AY23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: DTK Team Topics 1. Introduction 2. Systematic Creativity 3. Empathise 4. Do, Undo, Redo 5. Evaluate with People Assessment Components and Timeline 35% Individual Learning Activities (ILAs) -- 5 x 7 35% Team-Based Workshops (TBWs) -- 5 x 7 30% Design Thinking Journal (DTJ) Objectivities This course, compulsory for Year 1 CHS students, is the college's attempt in instilling an appreciation of design principles among its students. It is usually preallocated to students alongside HSS1000 Understanding Social Complexity, and HSA1000 Asian Interconnections. Operating on a fortnightly schedule, pre-recorded videos outline a topic last up to 1 hour total every two weeks. Then, students are expected to complete the ILAs, which may be completed within varying times. One hour if you're lazy, and maybe three (idk) if you work hard. As usual, tutorials (TBWs) are conducted every fortnight, and last up to 1.5 hours each. Subjectivities Honestly, this course was somehow actually pretty well-designed. Each fortnight's ILA videos and worksheet were actually pretty aesthetic, and TBWs are actually just really fun arts-and-crafts workshops with a flavour of design principles. Personally, I just went into the course with the intention to S/U it (you can literally tell from this review). Without the pressure of grades, it's actually pretty fun. Imagine if this course was CS/CU ~ Message to Prospective Students If you don't feel like putting in effort, just S/U it, I guess. If you feel like putting in effort, just put in effort, I guess.

author: meower
message: HSA1000 Asian Interconnections fun but tiring fieldwork AY23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: Dr Yang Yang Tutor: Piya Topics 1. Interrogating the Concept of Asia 2. Narratives and Heritage in Asia 3. Creating Categories: Race, Ethnicity, Caste, Nationality 4. Religion and Society 5. Social Inequalities 6. Society and the Environment 7. Contemporary Issues in Asia Assessment Components and Timeline 15% Tutorial and Group Participation 20% Essay 1 (~Week 5) -- 600 words 15% Group Presentation (Tutorials 2/3/4/5) -- 15 minutes 20% Group Poster (~Week 10) 30% Essay 2 (~Week 13) -- 1000 words THERE ARE PEER REVIEWS (yay) Objectivities This course, compulsory for Year 1 CHS students, is the college's attempt in instilling an appreciation of Asian Studies as a discipline among its students. It is usually preallocated to students alongside HSS1000 Understanding Social Complexity, and DTK1234 Design Thinking. Live lectures (that are also recorded) last 1.5 hours every week, with each week's lecturer explaining various case studies around Asia. As usual, tutorials are conducted every fortnight, and last up to 1.5 hours each. There is a reading list which, in theory, is required before every tutorial session. Subjectivities Unlike its Social Sciences counterpart, HSS1000, this module is very example-driven. Throughout the module, various local contexts are described and analysed, which are then synthesised into key points. It's basically a field trip around the different countries, provinces, and towns of Asia. One point that initially bothered me about lectures was Dr Yang Yang's insistence on delivering the perfect sentence, complete with a flawless voice rasp. This resulted in choppy speech, with her repeating various words repeatedly, with an example, and I quote verbatim: "In Lu-- Luan-- Lua-- Luang Prabang...". Thankfully she was no longer stumbling by the sixth week. No hate intended, I just found it to be a funny quirk. On the topic of lectures, I found that the lecture quality wildly varied between, and even within, lectures. Here's an exemplar: The second lecture of the "Religion and Society" unit comprised of possibly the best and worst lectures in the entire course; of a guest speaker concisely outlining the transmission of Teochew Buddhist music with great clarity and precise statements, and Dr Yang Yang's religious veiling full of research questions but no answers. It could've been as good as her Luang Prabang lecture. And don't get me started on the second lecture of the "Social Inequalities" unit involving... dissecting the technical aspects of film? Okay, I get how lighting and cinematography can convey themes of social inequality, but the links weren't mentioned or even touched upon at all during the lecture. It randomly came out of nowhere. At the beginning of the semester, the tutorial class was divided into four discussion groups A/B/C/D. One group will then present in each of the next few tutorial sessions. With my tutor Piya's cogence and charisma, I quickly got into the groove of tutorial sessions and managed to contribute productively in healthy and respectful debate (no matter how controversial my arguments were). As Week 10 rolls around with the poster due, things may get hectic. The entire group is quite literally required to visit a neighbourhood and take a photograph of at least five of its major landmarks, with group selfie(s) as a proof of independent work. Our group literally spent an entire afternoon going around a big neighbourhood and snapping photographs, then had to still complete the write-up afterwards. Thankfully, the poster was not as tough of an assignment as I had initially made it out to be, with a greater emphasis on the write-up as opposed to premium graphic design (at least, not among students). Though I found this module engaging, and enjoyed the limited yet grounded scope of Asia, I personally am still uninterested in an Asian Studies minor. I want to finish up a humanities second major, and these CHS modules are bogging my progress. Message to Prospective Students Was I forced to read this module? Yes. Would I choose not to read this module if given the choice? Yes. Was it fun and did I meet cool people? Yes!!! If you don't feel like putting in effort, just S/U it, I guess. If you feel like putting in effort, just put in effort, I guess.

author: meower
message: HSS1000 Understanding Social Complexity ~weekly anxiety AY23/24 Semester 1 Lecturer: Dr Veronica L. Gregorio Topics 1. Introduction to the Social Sciences 2. Conceptual Connections Across Disciplines 3. Critical Questioning 4. Framing Crises 5. Individual Responses to Crisis - Psychology 6. The Crises that Race Produces - Sociology 7. The Food Sustainability Crisis - Geography 8. Humanitarian Crises and Sovereignty - Political Science 9. Financial Crises - Economics 10. Work and the Crisis of Digitalisation - Communications & New Media 11. Crisis Faced by the Least, Lost, and Last - Social Work Assessment Components and Timeline 8% Weekly Forum Post 12% Tutorial Participation 5% Essay 1 Outline 15% Essay 1 (~Week 6) -- 800 words 5% Essay 2 Outline 20% Essay 2 (~Week 13) -- 1000 words 10% Group Abstract (~Week 10) -- 500 words 25% Group Presentation (~Week 11/12) -- 15 minutes THERE IS NO PEER REVIEW (??? send help) Objectivities This course, compulsory for Year 1 CHS students, is the college's latest attempt in instilling interdisciplinarity in students. It is usually preallocated to students alongside HSA1000 Asian Interconnections, and DTK1234 Design Thinking. Pre-recorded lectures last 30 to 45 minutes every week, and can be viewed at any time, explaining simple concepts related to the discipline of the week. Live webinars which act as QnA sessions, conducted on Monday afternoons, last for 1 hour each, with students expected to spend up to 5 minutes afterwards to post a question on the online forum. Tutorials are conducted every fortnight, and last up to 1.5 hours. There is a reading list. Subjectivities This module is basically a field trip to explore the various social science disciplines -- except any content coverage is highly surficial and watered down due to time constraints. I feel that this approach isn't particularly effective in making students genuinely appreciate the various disciplines CHS has to offer, though this may perhaps have been a task doomed to fail due to the module being compulsory in nature. If there was something to genuinely learn from this module, it would probably be general knowledge, ranging from conditioning methods to the mechanisms behind the failures of banks. Apart from the emphasis on breadth rather than depth, another aspect I didn't like was the weekly forum post. Though I do recognise the importance of open-ended and critical research questions, it felt very stifling and unrewarding to try to come up with an impressive and well-formulated question that would never be answered anyway. In total, there were around 750 students in the module, with 8 weekly forum posts. That amounts to 6000 questions, and 0 answers. Furthermore, I felt that the framing of critical questioning exercise wasn't presented too clearly. Throughout the module, the lecturers and tutors would consistently remind the students to "post the question online", without emphasising the difference between research and "casual" questions. After all -- not every question needs to be open-ended. At least, I hope "Is the homework due next Monday?" isn't too terrible of a question. In any case, the framing of the exercise is neglectful at best, and sinister at worst, potentially discouraging trivial binary questioning. Unlike its sibling module HSA1000 Asian Interconnections, this module is explanation-driven, generally introducing concepts before fitting in the various case studies, especially the obligatory link to Singapore's context. This isn't inherently bad or worse, but is something to take note of -- you won't be learning about the instruments of Teochew culture transmission in 20th century Southeast Asia. Message to Prospective Students This module certainly exists. If you already have a decent foundation in essay-writing, just put in the effort to ask decent open-ended research questions and write two relatively short analytical essays. If you feel that you are weak at essay-writing, just give it a try and see what happens, I guess. Having to hone your writing skills is an inevitability by hook or by crook, anyway. Or just S/U it if you're overloading in Semester 2.

author: DarkAxel
message:
AY25/26 Sem 1
Lecturer: Zhao Yiliang, Zhao Rui

Syllabus:
1. Foundations of Detecting Anomaly (Data Visualisation and Analysis; Benford's Law)
2. Data Preparation
3. Predictive Analysis (Linear Models, Performance Measure, and Regularization)
4. Investigation using Deep Learning (Basics)
5. Text Analytics
6. Investigation using Deep Learning (CNN and Adversarial Attack)
7. Investigation using Graph Analysis (Laplacian, Clique, Community, Page Rank, Spectral Clustering, Hierarchical Clustering etc.)
8. Foundations of Language Models
9. Training and Deploying LLMs
10. Detecting LLM-generated content
11. LLM Workflow and Agent Automation for Fraud Analytics
12. Human-in-the-loop

Weightage:
Quiz = 20% (10 weekly quizzes)
Kaggle Competition 1 (Detecting Bots Classification Problem) = 20% (10% Report, 5% above baseline, 5% ranking)
Kaggle Competition 2 (AI-Generated Text Classification Problem) = 20% (10% Report, 5% above baseline, 5% ranking) 
Final Project = 20% Report 20% Presentation Slides + Video

The workload is quite high, especially if you chasing rank and trying to get as high as possible to get 5% (as last place = 0%) with 3 submission per day and choosing 2 for final submission. Code and Report need to be submitted and the code must be ran offline without any problem which is a hassle if you use pre-trained LLM model. (No AutoML, pre-trained model (read the rules in Kaggle) etc.)

Take each of the competition as an individual ml project with the dataset given to you. and Final Project is just a group ml project where you find a dataset to do fraud analysis. 

The weekly quiz is 30-45 second per question flashed on the screen in a powerpoint slides and we use google forms to do the question with A B C D as options. (basically once the questions jump, you won't rmb the options/question, basically "no backward navigation" unless your memory so good to rmb the question and which option is which. Most quiz median is 8 to 10 depending on how tough the topic is. 

Content-wise, definitely one of the better content ML spec module. Basically covers the whole of BT3017 (minus Audio) + 3x more (Take note that I did not take BT3012 and there is nothing much carried forward from BT3012 as I followed just fine without it and I asked my friends who took it and say BT3012 is not really useful for the content though is pre-req - Appealed Waiver)

My Quiz (Slightly above median), Competition 1 (Slightly above median), Competition 2 (probably below LQ)

Expected Grade: B+/A- (My 2nd competition my rank was like bottom 3 and everyone grades are so close to each other - Up to final project)

author: DarkAxel
message:
Taken in AY25/26 Sem 1

Assessment Components:
• Initial Objectives (Job, Technical Skills, Office Skills what you want to achieve)
• Monthly Report
• Interim Report
• Final Report + Presentation

Pretty Chill in terms of the Workload. Your academic supervisor will be a student and he/she will be approving your report (alongside your company supervisor) and listen to your presentation. The workload for the most part is what you learn, what you do. Do note that you need VPN / School Wifi to submit your work as it is in project admin website. I used FortiClient VPN to connect to NUS wifi. Just put in a small effort for the report and showcase some effort as some acad supervisor may ask you to redo and submit if no effort. The presentation is based on your acad supervisor time but for mine she created slots at night or weekends via calendify for you to book (fcfs). Just submit and do your presentation = Pass liao

Monthly Report (Answer 4 questions):
1. Give a summary of your accomplishments for the last month.
2. What knowledge/skill (both technical and non-technical) did you gain from the internship last month?
3. What are the difficulties/challenges you faced during the past month, if any? How did you overcome (or plan to overcome) them?
4. What do you plan to accomplish during the next month?

Interim Report and Final Report can just follow the reports in Dspace where all students report are public (and you need to upload your report too). Max 20 pages.

Expected Grade: CS

author: DarkAxel
message:
Taken in AY25/26 Semester 1
Lecturer: Prof Shashank Shekar Tripathi
Class: 3hr lecture

Content:
• Introduction to Financial Markets (Primary, Secondary Market etc.)
• Investment Methodologies (Long Short, Open High Low Close)
• Financial Metrics (Sharpe, Sortino, Calmer, Beta, Alpha etc.)
• Strategies and Working with Python
• Technical Analysis (Moving Average, Relative Strength Index etc.)
• Backtesting System (Vectorised Backtesting)
• Event Based Backtesting
• Portfolio Development and Behavioral Finance (CAPM, Efficient Frontier, VAR)

Assessment Components (Typical of Prof Shashank Assessment like in BT3103):
• 15% Class Participation & Class Activities (5 Class Activities and QR attendance) - Assume 15%
• 30% Quiz (3 Quizzes)
Quiz 1: 26.17 / 30 (LQ:22.17, Median: 25.08, UQ: 26.5) - First 2 Topics
Quiz 2: 24.33 / 30 (LQ:22, Median: 24.5, UQ: 26) - Next 2 Topics
Quiz 3: 27 / 30 (LQ:22.5, Median: 24, UQ: 26) - Next 2 Topics
• 15% Group Mid Sem Project + Presentation (2 Strategies | Vectorised Backtest)
• 10% Portfolio Management Assignment - Assume 10% (Prof say most should get 10% or minus a bit if wrong - Can do individually or group)
• 30% Group Final Project (Event-based backtesting)

This module I would say is not bad if you want to get a better understanding of the various indicators used in investing. For the backtesting, do note that you need to do it in class format like CS2030, but the OOP principal doesn't matter as long as it can run. The content I would say is quite surface level in terms of knowing the indicators and metrics but ultimately you learn more in-depth while doing the project and coming out with strategies and rules.

For the group project, is quite hard to split the workload, most people would have dedicated 1 or 2 coder for the strategies and the rest do the report writing. The only problem I have with this course is that the project tends to release quite late. Mid-term had like 1 to 2 weeks to prepare and Final Term had like 3 to 4 weeks to prepare, so can get a bit rushed.

For this semester, only Mid Sem Project has Presentation due to the high student intake (The presentation took 2 Lectures Slot). By right should have both Mid Sem and Final Sem Presentation (each taking 1 lecture slot). So lucky me that there is no final presentation. For lectures, there is actually very minimal irl lectures, considering 2 slots for presentation, 1 slot for consultation and 1 e-learning lecture and 1 make-up quiz (week 13). Hence, I would say this is quite low to mid workload. Mid workload when things get last minute. Would recommend if you are interested in the investment indicators and how backtesting works.

Expected Grade: A/A- (Most people should have roughly the same marks, all boils down to the project marks with some influence from quiz marks)

author: DarkAxel
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Semester 2
Lecturer: Prof Lim Jie, Freddy

Content:
• Introduction to Financial Markets
• Risk & Return, CAPM
• Interest Rate Risk (Bond Price, Macaulay Duration, Convexity)
• Value at Risk / Expected Shortfall
• Derivatives Risk (Arbitrage, Black Scholes)
• Credit Risk (Credit Default Spread, Merton Model)

Assessment Components:
• 5% Participation: Do Surveys
• 30% Assignment - Do 6 out of 9 assignments/tutorials (effort based)
• 35% Group Project - Silicon Valley Bank
• 30% Final Exam (open-book)

Taking the course under Prof Freddy was amazing, very knowledgeable and helpful prof and good at teaching. The CAs are very chill as it is mainly effort based and the lectures are always updated with latest news like the Trumps tariffs. However, the final exam is extremely tough where the time really short to finish 16 questions with 1 to 3 parts each. Really test your math as well with proving questions such as proving the derivative of the put-call parity, utility equation, finding the formula given the information. Very good course for finance spec in BZA if you want to learn more finance stuff. Highly recommend.

Expected Grade: B+ (hopefullly as my Finals I think I just pass only)

author: DarkAxel
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Semester 2
Lecturer: Prof Rudy Setiono

Content:
• Continuous Optimization (Gradient Descent)
• Regression (Linear/Logistic)
• Support Vector Machines
• Information based learning (Decision Trees)
• Similarity Based Learning (KNN)
• Neural Networks (Back Propagation)
• Bayesian Learning (Naive Bayes)
• Bayesian Network (Naive Bayes)
• Genetic Algorithms 
Did not manage to do Gaussian Mixture Models Topic

Assessment Components:
• 15% Midterm 1 (open-book) - Week 5 (LQ: 13.25, Median: 20.5, UQ: 24) out of 40
> Topic Tested: Continuous Optimisation & Regression
• 15% Midterm 2 (open-book) - Week 8 (LQ: 11, Median: 18, UQ: 23) out of 40
> Topic Tested: Support Vector Machines and Decision Tree
• 30% Group Project with Presentation (Standard ML project)
• 40% Final Exam (open-book)
> Topic Tested: Continuous Optimisation, Similarity Based Learning, Neural Network, Naive Bayes

This is a full math ML mod with a sprinkle of explanation. Statquest will be your best friend as the understanding of Math behind ML is brutal. The topics are meant to be taught are stated above but did not finish on time, hence, missing 1 topic. The topic for the exam depends on how much content prof can cover. The good thing is the exam is not cumulative except finals where some of the topics in midterm1/2 may come out subjected to Prof announcement.

Note that after midterm 1, most people that do poorly will drop the course, increasing the statistics for midterm 1. Our class started with 75 ish people to 43 people. Prof say even those who do very bad will still be able to pass.

PS: The 1st few chapters will be quite painful (getting used to the math rigor) but after which the topics get slightly easier (still difficult) so don't give up hope after midterm 1.

Expected Grade: B (hopefullly)

author: DarkAxel
message: ### REVAMPED BT4221 Taken in AY24/25 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof Yeo Wee Kiang Class: 2hr lecture + 1hr tutorial Content: • Introduction to Big Data • Batch Data Ingestion (AWS Glue, Crawler, Data Catalog, S3) • Hadoop • Building and automating data pipelines (Apache Airflow) • Data Lakes (AWS S3) • Data Warehouses (Snowflake) • Pyspark Resillient Distributed Dataset (Apache Spark, Lineage Graph) • Pyspark Dataframes (Apache Spark) • Machine Learning in Big Data (Databricks & Spark MLlib library) • Recommendation Systems using Alternating Least Squares • Real-Time Data Processing (Spark Structured Streaming, Apache Kafka) • Deep Learning: CNN (Pytorch) • Deep Learning: RNN (Pytorch) Assessment Components: • 10% Individual Assignment 1 (AWS Glue) • 10% Individual Assignment 2 (ML using MLlib library) • 50% Group Project (Find a dataset that is minimally 500mb and can only use Pyspark. Sklearn is banned - Standard ML Project) • 30% Final Exam (closed book 1 page cheatsheet) The content of the module is decent if you want to learn more about Big Data and how AWS and Hadoop are used (Snowflake might be removed as it feels separated). The prof is very good and very chill. However, as a Year 2 (with ML knowledge prior from poly) taking this mod, it is definitely on the hard side of things (if you don't know any ML) and the Prof always mentioned that this mod is meant for the senior as the content can be quite advanced. The prof consistently provided a lot of additional resources such as comic book, good youtube video for the Y2s to learn better. The exam tested some definition but more on the application of the stuff we learn such as how to apply the use of AWS tools and Snowflake given a scenario. The prof also mentions that the pre-requisite for this module would likely to changed so that it will be harder for Y2s to take. Highly Recommend for People who are taking the Machine Learning Spec. Probably one of the more useful to know course in BZA ML specialization. (Note that there is no programming of Java, that is for CS4225 the preclusion mod. BT4221 is more focused on the application in Big Data/ML and analytics) Expected Grade: B+

author: DarkAxel
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Sem 2
Professor Ng Teck Khim

Content:
- Pandas, Seaborn
- Machine Learning Pipeline (e.g. backpropagation algorithm, performance metrics)
- Dimensionality Reduction (PCA)
- Graph (Laplacian, Spectral Clustering, Convolution)
- Audio (Fourier Transformation, Spectrogram)

Assessment Criteria:
- Tutorials (20%) --> 8 weekly basic tutorials (most should get full mark or close to full mark if careless) - 19.875/20
- Individual Project (30%)  --> 2 page report, 2min recorded presentation, code (prof say most should get about the same marks)
- Final Exam (50%) --> Open Book

Workload and content wise quite light as the Final Exam only cover machine learning pipeline onwards. However, I would say although the module covers very less things and for the most part can learn in other modules, the final exam is really no joke.

The final exam had 4 questions 25 marks each and 2 parts for each question usually. The prof expects u to write like very short like a few lines will do to get the full 10+marks per part. Basically the exam is all about thinking than writing. You need to understand concept fully for the most part (no code tested), with the open book being mainly useful for the formulas to calculate performance metrics etc. Like looking at the pyp in the library (22/23), it was way easier than the final exam this year.

PS: Good to have a good foundation in eigenvectors and eigenvalues and its purpose (got tested quite a bit in final exam). The prof is a big advocate that people should learn SVD during their summer break. But for the most part if you weak in linear algebra, still can take the course as there won't be MA1522 level of calculation but more about understanding the concept behind eigenvectors and eigenvalues.

Definitely one of the least intense ML course but also least useful (other than pca, audio and graph portion) as feature engineering is getting taken over by AI as prof mentioned.

Expected Grade: B+

Taken in AY23/24 Semester 2
Lecturer: Prof Ong Ee Cheng (Micro), Prof Chan Kok Hoe (Macro)
TA: Caleb Lee

Content:
Micro
• Chapter 1: Opportunity Cost, Five Core Principles, Production Possibilities Frontier, Gains from Trade
• Chapter 2: Supply and Demand
• Chapter 3: Elasticity, Welfare Economics
• Chapter 4: Government Intervention in Markets
• Chapter 5: Market Failure, Market Structure (Perfect Competition)
• Chapter 6: Market Structure, Game Theory
Macro
• Chapter 1: Measurements in Macroeconomics
• Chapter 2: Long Run Macroeconomics
• Chapter 3: Fluctuations, the Keynesian Model and Fiscal Policy
• Chapter 4: Money and Banking
• Chapter 5: Crisis, Recession and Monetary Policy
• Chapter 6: Exchange Rates and Macroeconomic Policy

Assessment Components:
• 10% Canvas Quizzes (Lower Quartile Lost a few marks here and there - 10 quiz if I remember)
• 5% Problem Set Submissions - Effort Based (Assume Full Marks)
• 10% Tutorials – Participation and Preparedness (Assume Full Marks)
• 25% Midterm Exam (Slightly Above Lower Quartile I think) 70/120
• 50% Final Exam (Prob Lower Quartile)

This course is content heavy and with a decent amount of workload to be completed for tutorials. Micro was definitely easier to learn compared to Macro and I was more familiar with it. Good Prof and TA. Don't be complacent with Midterms even if u get Upper Quartile. I have a JC friend that get B even with above Upper Quartile Midterms. JC may have advantage as compared to me a Poly student but people can still choke on the 2nd half, the harder content.

Expected Grade: B-
Actual Grade: B

author: DarkAxel
message: Taken in AY23/24 Sem 1 under Prof Oh Lih Bin & Prof Isabel Topics SOC - Leadership Roles, Business Continuity Plan, etc. CELC - Self-Awareness, Pathos Logos Ethos, SCARF, Heroes Journey etc. Assessment: Soc Assessment (60%) - Lectures AKA Workshop - Workshop In-class participation and quizzes (18%) Best 9 of 10 quiz. This quiz will only be opened after finishing the lecture slides, hence do need to attend the lectures. - Case Presentation and Discussion (10%) Group Presentation (1 Question) + 5 Case Study Quiz (Only available during lecture too, after going thru the case study) - Simulation Game (7%) Harvard Business Publishing. Is legit a simulation game where you talk to people to get information. The more information you get by choosing the right options = more marks. - Team DT Proposal Project Report (25%) Surface Level Research is needed (5000 word limit) CELC Assessment (40%) - Recitation AKA Praxis - Professional Impact (10%) Basically Participation Mark during Recitation + Reflection Every Session - Individual Oral Presentation (10%) Individual Presentation as a CCA Leader (3min limit) - Final Oral Presentation (5% Group + 15% Individual) Individual Presentation as a Department Head based on your Group Project (3min limit + Q&A) Group is mainly graded base on the flow of the presentation and how each member individual presentation are linked to each other. Conclusion: I would say is considered a fluff module but for someone like me who is not as sociable, the 3hr recitation can really drain your energy within the first hour XD. The Lectures is only 3hrs for 5 lectures with the case studies. Other than that is 2hrs. This modules do end early, by week 10 lecture ends, week 11 recitation end, and week 12 is just your project deliverables. There is only 7 recitation lessons, 2 of which is your Graded Presentation and 1 is Consultation. Grade (70% Shown - Grades are Normalized with the Cohort): Simulation Game: 5/7 Workshop Quizzes: 17/18 Report: 19/25 FOP: 13.5/20 (RIP) Expected Grade: B+ (hopefully)

author: DarkAxel
message: Taken in AY22/23 Sem 2 + ST 1 (iBLOC) under Prof Tan Tiow Seng NOTE: Prof Tan will not be teaching this module in AY23/24 Assessment: 2% Participation (2/2) - I think as long you attend lesson should be free marks and do all the surveys. 33% Coursemology (33/33) - Reach level 36 to get the full marks. - The gamification of Coursemology is nice but very stressful when you look at the leaderboard and seeing all your other peers being so high up while you are so far behind) - Moreover, the deadlines for all your missions are quite early. For Midterms, the tested topics was up to Lecture 8 but the coursemology deadline for lecture 9 to 11 I think are before Midterms. Hence, the stressfulness of coursemology before Mid Terms can be quite high. Luckily after Mid-Term, it is more relaxed since u left a few things to complete till Practical Exam. 16% Mid-terms (me 25/32 mean 21/32 upper quartile 27/32) - Around End March - 2 hour-long written paper - Pretty alright. Made tons of careless mistakes. 16% Practical Exam (me 7.5/32 mean 10/32 upper quartile 14/32) - Early June - 2 hour-long paper done on personal computer - Insanely Hard. My 2-hour long was basically me reading the question and trying to understand what the questions wants me to do and still can't do it. 33% Finals (Undisclosed) - Mid June - 2 hour-long written paper mean 35.5/48 upper quartile 40/48. - I don't think I scored too well for Finals. I am pretty sure I am around 30/48, below average. Conclusion: Even with Python background in Poly, this module content for the most part are very new to me and hard for me. Especially the theory of Time and Space Complexity. In general, I like Prof Tan teaching methods as he prefers to test us on computational thinking than just us regurgitating codes out for exams. I took this module while still in NS so it was pretty tiring for me at times. Hence, I would recommend to join if you already ORDed and have more time to study or those haven't ORDed but are very good in Programming and have significant understanding on Time and Space Complexity. I think to get an A- to A in CS1010X, you would generally need to be above average for all your assessment or do very well for Finals. If you are average to below average, your final grade is highly likely B to B+. Anything below B is quite hard to get, you must really flunk almost everything. Expected Grade: B+ Actual Grade: B+

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Taken in 25/26 semester 1

Lecturers: Tan Nguyen Minh, Liu Yang

Grading breakdown: 
10% Homework (2 sets)
15% Midterm
25% quiz (take home multiple choice)
50% final exam

Topic coverage: 
1) Supervised Learning
- Linear Basis Regressions & Classifications
- Ridge & Kernel Ridge Regressions
- Support Vector Machines
- Decision Trees
-Boosting (ada boost)
- Fully-Connected Neural Networks

2) Unsupervised Learning
- Dimensionality Reduction (Principal Component Analysis)
- Clustering (K-means & Gaussian Mixture Model)

3) Reinforcement Learning
- Markov Decision Processes
-Q learning
-Q networks 

4) Graph Neural network (not tested in finals but covered in take home assessment)

5) Transformer (not tested in finals but covered in take home assessment)

6) Page rank algorithm (covered in exams) 

Content: 
This is a graduate level module taught by the mathematics department. As such, you are expected to possess a solid foundation in introductory mathematical topics (at minimum a proof based calculus, linear algebra (MA2001) and statistics courses (ST2131)) and good maturity in understanding and writing proofs. If you are from NUS, the unstated prerequisites are MA2001, MA2002, ST2132 and CS2040 (because data structure based algorithms are covered like dynamic programming and page rank). 

As such, if you are rusty in the modules/topics described above, I would recommend having a quick review. 

Having said that, this is not a purely proof based course and I would think the proofs that you are required to produce in the exams are probably at a level of a typical 2000 MA module. (I did not come from a math background, but had experience in statistics at the undergraduate level and could mostly follow the proofs covered in lectures/tutorials). 

Coming to the actual  content, this module covers traditional ML algorithms in a rigorous manner (much more focus on math compared to undergrad classes like CS3244/ST3248). However, as this is still introductory, most of the material is covered at a surface level. (Can refer to Probablistic Machine Learning by Bishop for a rough gauge of the rigour of this class, I would say the rigour is about 80% of the material in the book)

While no ML knowledge is required, having some intuitive sense of the algorithms covered can help to better appreciate/follow the mathematics during class. 

Assessments/workload: 
The 2 homework assessments take about 3-5 hours each to complete (can be faster/slower depending on your grasp of the material). The assessments are similar style to exam questions, true/false and a mixture of computation and proof questions. 

The midterm was a relatively easier exam, with most of the questions being a rehash of tutorial/homework questions with the exception of one or two parts which tested some obscure slide in the lecture notes. 

The 2 quizzes were take home to complete within a week (no time limit once started). Some questions are tricky so it is easy to make careless mistakes. But they are not too difficult/time consuming. 

The final exam is the most challenging assessment portion. There were approximately 30% worth of proof questions in the paper, testing relatively new concepts and requiring a good understanding of the material and mathematical maturity to produce. The computation parts were of similar difficulty to the homework sets and as such, one needed speed and accuracy to do well for all the questions. 

Lecturers: 

I really enjoyed Prof Tan's lectures as he would write some proofs from scratch during class which helped to understand the concepts better. 

Prof Liu had a slightly different style where he did not do this but still, he was enthusiastic and helpful. 

My biggest complaint about this module is the lack of practice problems (only 2 problem sets and 2 homeworks for the entire semester) which led to many of us being underprepared for the rigour of the final exam.

Overall, you can expect to spend about 6-7 hours a week on this module (study + lecture + assessments) which is the typical workload of a math module in NUS.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Semester: 23/24 semester 1
Professor: Dr Li Cheng

Syllabus: 
1. Inversion/rejection sampling
2. simple/Importance sampling/Antithetic estimator/Blackwell Rao
3. EM algorithm
4. Markov chains
5. MCMC algorithms
6. Bootstrapping (not examinable)

Contents: This module's ideas are not difficult to understand but the difficulty lies in being able to apply the ideas in the real world context/problems described in tutorials/exams. A degree of comfort with statistics and calculus is assumed (st2132 knowledge should be sufficient). Hence, one should dilligently attempt all tutorial problems and homework questions and understand all the concepts behind solving them. Prof Li Cheng typically sets exam questions in a similar style to the tutorials and homeworks and examples covered in lectures.

Professor: Prof Li Cheng is an excellent professor who is very patient in explaining concepts and is able to make abstract ideas easy to understand. He is also very understanding and usually accomodates requests of students well (he even gave us 5 minutes extra during the midterm exams). He also focuses on understanding of concepts so arithmetic mistakes are not penalised heavily.

Takeaways: I gained an understanding of important statistical ideas such as estimation using the EM algorithm (frequently used in Machine learning), and posterior estimation using MCMC. Would recommend for anyone interested in statistics and data science.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Semester: 23/24 sem 1
Module Coordinators: Dr Lek Hsiang Hui and Dr Shalinda.

For our semester, there was a severe shortage of projects, with only 8 listed projects for about 24 groups and like the other reviewer mentioned projects are allocated purely on a first come first serve basis. Only groups who submitted their bid within the first 30 seconds got projects (not necessarily their first choice). 

Some of the projects included things like portfolio optimization, classifcation of eye cancer images or using gen AI to create analysis reports.

The other groups had to self source for a company or come up with their own problem statement and dataset (the latter would render your group at a huge disadvantage).

Our group managed to find a company with a suitable project but since details such as NDAs etc were not finalized, we only got to access the data and start the project at the end of recess week.

I would recommend that if you intend to take capstone in a subsequent semester, it might be wise to spend your vacation to source for a suitable project to ensure a smooth sailing semester even if you do not manage to secure a project from the listed ones.

Typically the project should ideally involve real world data and aim to solve a significant business problem or aid some processes in the company.

Assessments: 
6% weekly journal submissions
2% peer review submission (simply submitting earns you the 2%, your actual grade can be heavily impacted by what your peers write so be sure to contribute adequately)
35% Final submission
10% project proposal
15% interim presentation
32% individual continuous progress (This probably refers to how much you visibly contributed to the project)
Workload: Typically, in a week you would have consultations with the client company, and group meetings and have to solve tasks. One can expect to spent around 15 to 20 hours a week on this module during typical weeks and much more during weeks where there are major submissions/presentations). I would say workload for our group was equivalent to around 1.5 BT 4k modules but could be lower or higher depending on the actual project and company/company supervisor.

Word of advice: Do not blindly follow what the company supervisor tells you to do. Always cross check with your academic supervisor because ultimately it will be the academic supervisor that is grading you (at least for my semester, feedback from the client was not factored in). Also, pick a project that best aligns to your group's strengths and start early (although this depends on your groups ability to have a project and relevant data early which is why I would recommend finding a project before the semester starts).

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Semester: 23/24 sem 1
Professor: Dr Zhang Jin Ting

Assessment: 15% tutorial submission and attendance, 25% midterm, 60% final.

Reference textbook: "Linear Models in Statistics" by Alvin C. Rencher and G. Bruce Schaalje.

This module is a revisit of concepts seen in ST3131. However, this module is a huge step up from ST3131 because one needs to understand the mathematical and statistical proofs and intuitions behind those concepts. (Eg, why does a SSE follow a chi sq distribution with a given degrees of freedom) At the end of the module, one would be expected to be able to derive proofs and statistical tests relating to different types of linear models.

Topics: 
1. Linear algebra review/concepts
2. Multivariate expectations and results, Eg, E(Ay) where y is a random vector and A is a constant matrix.
3. Multivariate normal distributions.
4. Quadratic forms, (eg y'Ay where y is a random vector and A is a constant matrix).
5. Simple linear regression
6. Multiple linear regression.
7. Hypothesis testing
8. Non full rank models

Difficulty: Throughout this module Linear algebra is used heavily and as such, one needs to be comfortable with MA1101R/MA2001. Proving techniques are also used extensively so one needs to be comfortable/pick up proof techniques on the fly (induction, contradiction, necessary and sufficient conditions etc). Also, a degree of comfort in concepts seen in ST2131 and ST2132 is assumed, eg MGFs, probability distributions, hypothesis tests, MLEs etc. Overall, content is extensive and difficult and one can expect to spent much time absorbing the concepts discussed in lectures. This module felt more like an MA module with theorems and proofs being the bulk of the lecture content rather than typical ST coded modules.

I would say that this is one of the most challenging but rewarding ST modules I have taken with exposure to concepts not seen in other classes such as multivariate distributions, expectations involving matrices and random vectors (rather than the typical univariate expectations), vector and matrix calculus and optimization etc. Mastery of these concepts would enable you to link Linear Algebra and statistics and enable you to better appreciate these ideas and the usefulness of linear algebra.

Professor: I have no complaints about Dr Zhang besides the fact that his accent can be quite hard to understand at times. He also tends to assume proficiency in proofs and linear algebra so he moves quite fast in class. The weekly tutorials submissions are graded and can be quite time consuming so do start early.

Prerequisites: I would say the listed prereqs are misleading as one does not really need to know the content from ST3131 for this module as all the concepts are revisited from scratch. The true prerequisites are in my opinion MA2001/1101r and ST2132 and some knowledge of calculus (eg MA2104).

Tips: To do well in this module, get comfortable with being able to derive most if not all the proofs in the lecture notes by yourself and attempt and understand all the tutorial problems given.

TLDR: This module is very challenging and should be avoided in a crowded semester. However it is very rewarding in my opinion and is rather important to learn statistics well.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
AY 22/23 sem 2, Prof Micheal Choi.

Topics:
1. Probability review
2. Maximum likelihood estimation
3. Confidence intervals
4. Simple linear regression
5. Hypothesis testing (1 sample, 2 sample proportion)
6. Order statistics
7. Non parametric tests.
8. Chi Sq tests
9. Power of a statistical test
10. Neyman pearson lemma

Syllabus:
30% Midterm
20% Assignments (6 biweekly assignments)
50% final

Lectures: Micheal Choi is an excellent lecturer, probably the best I have encountered in the statistics department. His notes are clear and his annotations during lectures are extremely helpful to understand challenging concepts. In addition, he tends to end lectures early, (5.15 instead of the usual 5.30) and is also very approachable if you have questions after class.

Difficulty: In my opinion, the most challenging chapters are order statistics and Maximum likelihood estimation, as these chapters can contain rather abstract questions. The other chapters tend to have questions that are rather mechanical in nature, although one has to have a good understanding of the derivation and purpose of each statistical test to do well in the module. However, these tests have real life applications in the data science and data analytics space and I would receommend anyone interested  to pursue industry jobs in these fields to take this module. The assignments are not very time consuming and overall this module is low-medium workload.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Sem 22/23, Prof Gan Fah Fett 

Content:
1. Simple linear regression
2. Multiple linear regression.
3. Residual analysis
4. Outlier and influential points
5. Polynomial regression and splines
6. Indicator variable
7. Multicollinearity
8. Variable selection

Book: Introduction to Linear regression analysis by Montgomery.

Lectures: 
I would say Gan Fah Fett is a decent lecturer who can illustrate content clearly. However, his notes are very poor (screenshots instead of typed, meaning ctrl F is not possible). In addition, his notes contain a whole bunch of formulas and it is uncertain which of these formulas are examinable and which are not. Many of these results are unproven (because proof requires strong understanding of linear algebra and statistics beyond the scope of this course). Therefore, revising this module can be a stressful experience when staring at all these formulas. 

Assessment:
Midterms (30%): This was a giveaway assessment which most being simple questions in R and easy to answer if one attended lectures. Hence the median was around 17/20.

Finals (70%): More challenging. However, one question was directly lifted off tutorial. Overall, most of the complex formulas dicussed in the lectures and notes were not tested and if one did tutorials and read the textbook (instead of the lecture notes) dilligently and copied the key formulas discussed onto the cheatsheet, finals is not too difficult. However, failure to copy/remember the right formulas would result in relatively poorer performance. Hence, do squeeze in as many formulas as you can, especially, those that repeatedly appear (such as eigenvalues and SSR, Unit length scaling etc).

Overall, the content of this module was interesting and a great introduction to linear regression.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Taken in 22/23 semester 2, under Prof Qiao Dandan.

Syllabus:
1. Text analytics methods (syntactic parsing etc). 
2. Linear/Logistic regression
3. SVM
4. Bagging (Random Forest)
5. Boosting
6. Neural Network models like ANN, CNN, RNN, LSTM etc

Assessment:
3 10% take home assignments
15 % in class quiz
5% class participation (attendance)
50% final project

This module is an applied machine learning module, with the focus being on application rather than theory. Having said that, the theory can be difficult to grasp as well especially for someone with no mathematical/statistical background. However, the theory covered in class is quite surface level and the mathematics learnt in modules like ST2334, MA2001, MA1521 should be sufficient to understand the concepts covered here. Proficiency in python is assumed and you are expected to self learn the PyData ecosystem (Pandas, Numpy, sklearn etc) with some usage examples provided.

Prof Qiao is an ok lecturer, but sometimes difficult to catch because she tends to cover easier material at a slower pace (for example, a she spent 30 minutes on a simple calculation slide but simply glossed over the concept of Bootstrapping despite most of the class never hearing of it before) and more difficult material at a faster pace. Recordings are also not present so do be prepared to do self study if you cannot catch what is covered during lectures. She sometimes tended to explain difficult concepts in a manner than makes them even more difficult. Having said that, she includes examples (for Naive Bayes and LSTM models) that do make it easier to visualize how the models learn with data.

As for the class participation component, Prof Qiao uses a "stochastic attendance system", which simply means that on 3 random lecture days, you have to scan a QR code to register your attendance.

The worst part of the module this semester is the timing. 6.30 to 9.30 pm lectures on a Wednesday are a no go. In addition, Prof Qiao usually ended around 9.20 so early releases were rare.

Overall, the most important portion of the module is the final project, so do decide your project early and ensure that the business problem that your project solves is significant.

Ps: If you have taken BT4012 before, this module is almost a carbon copy in terms of content and assessments but in my opinion, BT4012 was much better conducted and had more interesting content like model explainibility and adversarial attacks). If you are choosing between the two, definitely choose BT4012.
thread_title: BT4222 Mining Web Data for Business Insights

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Taken in AY22/23, Sem 2 under Zhou Wang.

Syllabus is as follows:
1. Probability review.
2. Markov Chains (A very long chapter spanning most of the semester, includes topics like first step analysis, irreducibility, stationary distributions etc)
3. Branching processes.

The lectures closely follow the relevant chapters in the book An Introduction to Stochastic modelling.

Assessment:
30% assignment
70% Final

From the first lecture in the module, it is easy to see that this module is more of a mathematics module than a statistics module, with plenty of proofs and rigorous definitions. Though the requirement is only ST2131, some level of comfort with reading and writing proofs and sequences and series is expected. 

The lectures are easily the most difficult part of the module, containing plenty of proofs, and rather abstract concepts. I usually required a second or third viewing to understand concepts discussed. The lecturer is decent although he does gloss over some non trivial steps and students are left to fill in the gaps.

However, the tutorials, assignment and final exam were rather straightforward compared to the difficulty of the lectures leading to an extremely steep bellcurve (the median assignment score was 96/100). The difficulty of the final exam was significantly lower than past years and had questions similar to those in the tutorial. Hence, overall, under Prof Zhou for this semester, the workload was rather low, with tutorials only requiring around 1 hour per week and the assignment could be solved within 3 hours.

To  truly understand and do well for this module, I would recommend reading the textbook and attempting the problems in the text book to reinforce important concepts. Keep pace with the lectures as well as all the topics are connected.

Overall, this module under Prof Zhou appears to be an easier iteration of the module but the trade off is the steep bellcurve.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Year taken: 21/22 Semester 2.
Taught by: Prof Vivek Sundar Magesh.

Syllabus:
1. Value for customers and customer lifetime value
2. Forecasting methods (Includes scenario analysis and bass forecasting)
3. Market segmentation (using tools like K means or hierarchical clustering)
4. Positioning (Using perceptual maps)
5. Conjoint analysis
6. Modern marketing (using techniques like NLP, social media marketing and digital marketing)

Assessments:
10% class participation
5% coursera course (you get the full marks as long as you complete the course and submit a 1 page report on the material. The course is not long and takes around 5 hours in total to complete)
5% presentation
15% case studies (Pick 3 out of 5)
30% final project
35% final quiz

The case studies were all on a website called enginius and one of the case studies would be assigned to you to present (for the 5% presentation) and you could pick 3 out of the remaining 5 cases to submit to be graded on (5% each). These case studies can be quite time consuming (easily >6 hours a week) because the questions are quite open ended and leave much to interpretation. Nevertheless, Prof Vivek grades quite leniently here as long as your answer is logical and conceptually correct. The final project requires you to find a dataset and perform some analysis on it to answer a marketing related business problem (you can do any analysis you want even techniques not taught in class). Here, a good data set is key and I suggest finding a highly informative and thorough dataset before proceeding as it would make the analysis much easier to perform.  Both the case studies and the project are done in the same group. For this semester, we were allowed to pick our group mates.

The final quiz was open book and administered on luminus. However, most of the questions were lifted directly from the lecture slides and therefore it is likely most people got full marks. 

Workload:
The first half of the module had almost no workload and we just had to listen to the lectures. However, the second half of the semester is a different story because the case studies and the group projects come in and you can expect to spend more than 12 hours a week on the module (could be worse if your group mates are not contributing).

The material covered is not difficult and you would likely not have to spend a lot of time trying to understand the content. However, I did find it much more interesting than MKT1705X because we learnt what tools were applied in order to apply traditional marketing concepts in the real world. Having said that, I do wish that more time was dedicated to the topic on modern marketing, instead of cramming all three major topics into a single lecture.

Teaching and administration:
Prof Vivek is a very good lecturer who explains the concepts well. When watching his lectures, it was difficult to tell that it was his first time conducting a module. In addition, he is also highly receptive to feedback and is willing to adjust the schedule and syllabus on the fly based on student's feedback. The first few lectures were standard lectures but after that, half the lecture was dedicated to teaching and the other half was dedicated to presentations. 

Overall, this module is for you if you enjoyed MKT1705X and even otherwise, if you interested in the tools and methodologies people use to perform marketing analysis, this module is for you.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Taken in Sem 2 AY 21/22
Taught by: Shashank Shekar Tripathi

Syllabus:
1. Css, HTML
2. Javascript
3. Vue.js
4. SCRUM framework
5. Testing and deployment

Assessment:
15% - Class participation
30% in lecture quizzes
25% Midterm report
30% final submission

For the class participation component, we were never required to speak up in class. I think as long as you attend all the lectures and submit the weekly assignments (as demonstrated in lectures) you should be getting full marks. The in lecture quiz component is broken down into 3 10% quizzes. The first quiz was very easy and I did not put in as much effort for the subsequent 2. This was a mistake because they were far more difficult. 

The midterm report is a requirements analysis of any application that we would like to develop as the final project. We were to write down all the requirements and acceptance criteria. Our group was very unfamiliar with requirement analysis and we struggled to come up with a proper template. During lectures, this was not explicitly covered and we were told to be 'creative' when coming up with our report. I thought that this would have been much easier if we had a template to work off of. 

For the final submission, we were to submit a detailed documentation in SCRUM format and a working implementation of our project. Again, we did not have much exposure to SCRUM templates during the lecture itself and our group was quite lost until the final week or so and my advice would be to google for a template early and use it thoughout the weeks of working on the final project and avoid doing the whole thing on the last week.

As for the development, hope that you have good team mates and be sure to divide the workload well every week. The lecture itself does not teach Vue in depth so you are expected to pick it up by reading the documentation or watching online tutorials.

Overall, I think alot of self learning is required for this module (avoid taking in a crowded semester). The first half of the module is rather low work load but when the final project comes in, I would say the workload is extremely demanding. Also, try to find good groupmates as they can make or break you whole experience with this module.

author: disqus_nJFMKjAtd8
message:
Taken in AY21/22 SEM2 
Taught by: Aditya Karanam

For assessments and syllabus, refer to the post made by Buwoo. I would just be sharing my view of the module.

Difficulty:
In my opinion, this is probably one of the more difficult BT modules and will come as a culture shock especially after going through the non-math intensive BT1101 and BT2101. In addition, I think that the listed prerequisites are not representative of the module (why 2030 is a prerequisite remains a mystery). In my view, you should only take this module after completing CS2040 and ST2334 as they teach the fundamentals (such as graphs and statistics) which are required for this module but not explicitly taught.

Content:
This module covers a more niche area of machine learning (Probabilistic graphical models) and I think that it would be highly beneficial to have taken a machine learning module prior to this or have some machine learning experience in order to better appreciate this mod. I think that the BT department should have an introductory machine learning module which should be a pre requisite to this. If you enjoy machine learning and diving into the mathematics behind algorithms, the content will be very interesting and enjoyable for you. If you are a future BZA student (22/23) onwards wondering if you should take this module, a good gauge would be to see if you enjoyed 2040 (you would be learning some interesting algorithms here) and ST2334 (since most of the module deals with probability).

Homework:
I think giving these a 30% weightage (5% each) is too much and it makes every careless error extremely costly since most people get full marks. However, the difficulty is manageable and you can expect to spend around 2-3 hours per homework (1 every 2 weeks) which a very manageable workload for a 3k module. Again, this is under Prof Aditya and mileage may vary under different professors. My advice would be to discuss with your friends and ensure you do not make silly mistakes.

Exams:
The midterms were conducted very poorly and most of us were very confused during the whole conduct. Different rooms received different instructions regarding the mistake in the paper and I think that this was rather unfair. There were also weird phrasing in some of the questions which made me second guess my answers. Poor phrasing notwithstanding, the difficulty of the paper was slightly higher than that of the homeworks and the mean score was around 32/40 (indicating a rather steep curve).

Finals were conducted much better and proceeded much more smoothly. Having said that, the proctor did not check our cheatsheets over zoom and no one would have known if you brought in more than one sheet. The difficulty of the finals was significantly higher due to the topics covered in the second half being more abstract and difficult and time is of the essence.

Teaching:
Lectures: The first few lectures of prof Aditya were rather fast and difficult to understand. He assumed that we possessed some knowledge on machine learning and were already familiar with concepts like the sigmoid function and maximum likelihood estimation. Therefore, sometimes he straightaway went very mathematical making it difficult to understand at times. However, I believe that due to feedback, the lectures in the second half of the semester were slightly better with Prof Aditya spending more time to explain. Nevertheless, I think the lectures still left out too many details and much time was spent trying to understand how certain concepts worked (for example gibbs sampling and Forward backward algorithm). I think the AIMA textbook is extremely useful and helped me to really understand the concepts when the lectures were not so clear. My advice would be to not allow material to pile up because subsequent lectures often made use of material covered previously. Also, there are excellent videos on youtube that cover the material in the lecture (particularly by Prof 
Pieter Abbeel). Nonetheless, one of the good things about Prof Aditya lectures is the annotated slides and the fact that he stops often during class in order to take questions from students.
(I stopped attending tutorials after the first few sessions because I felt that my tutor was not the best)

One thing to note would be that this was Prof Aditya's first time teaching. As such, it is very likely that he would improve in subsequent semesters both in terms of administrative matters and teaching.

author: dogshit_cs_student
message:
Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 + ST 1 (iBLOC) under Prof Tan Tiow Seng

Some dank runes/designs made by students in the contests: https://ibb.co/album/y6TNMN

TLDR: Recommended to take if you have the time to commit for it. Taking this also gives you the option of taking cs2040 in special term before matriculating, which allows you to take modules that have cs1010/2040 as a pre-req once you matriculate if you are interested in speedrunning your course.

Review:
Course is well taught and smoothly executed. Little to no hiccups. Everyone is helpful and nice. 100% recommend.

Workload:
Course is conducted over 6 months. If you have little to minimal programming experience, I would recommend this to be your full time commitment so you get your fundamentals nailed down. Course was conducted smoothly. My TA was extremely helpful and asnwered my spam of questions.

To do well I would recommend doing all the work consistently and definitely max out the 33% weightage for coursemology. You can always clarify doubts from your groupmates, TAs, prof. Also good to make friends to hang out with when in person classes start. Doing the past year papers for midterms/PE/finals definitely helps.

Course had around 80++ students if Im not wrong. There are people who will complete everything in the first month or so and sit on top of the leaderboard for the rest of the module but no need to feel pressured and just do at your own pace.

Do refer to kok's review for the course content/topics/etc as Im very lazy.

Expected grade: A
Final grade:

author: elfinmimic
message:
AY24/25 Semester 2
Lecturer: Brett McInnes
Textbook: The lecture notes with font size 48 (sorry I copied you)

Content is already covered in some other comments

In my opinion, this course is a really "bashing" course, because heavy machinery is brought only to teach basic things
1. Tensor product was used (I don't know what it is really), but perhaps were specialized and brought down (for what I don't know). To study how tensor products come in place, I believe modules should be studied beforehand.
2. The analysis of determinant was a disgusting abuse of differential forms, specializing functions to just linear functions. However, you only studied it after you have done at least Linear Algebra II and Analysis II. Moreover, this was taught after the JCF, where you need the determinant to calculate the characteristic polynomial, just to embrace those differential forms. Ugh...
3. Things that works and seems to be cool does not necessarily mean that it should be brought into use.

The material was ... uh ... unreadable
1. The professor has the talent of making 20 pages of easy-to-read latex into like 50-100 of size 48, without any bold, making it extremely hard to read. 
2. Instead of the traditional Theorem Proof, that allows students to prove the results on their own, the notes is a mish-mash of (Theorem + Proof) and (Proof + Theorem).
3. Some definition are unclear, for example, what do you mean by a change of basis matrix acting on a row vector ? Definition guessing occurs a lot in this course, because they are not always given clearly.

Miscellaneous
1. On quiz 1, prof does not accept that {1} is a basis of R, when prof mentions a linear transformation that takes R as the codomain (really ?)
2. Yes, the bell curve is steep (even though he complains about it), a few percents is the difference between A- and A+ (maybe I miscalculated it with my friends, or he marks a wrong solution right, I don't know, take it with a grain of salt)
3. There is a proof of the JCF that uses most of the idea from Cayley Hamilton, which is to decompose the vector space into generalized eigenspaces (subspaces that are (A - \lambda I) - nilpotent, or send every vector to 0 if applied enough times), and then decompose each generalized eigenspaces into cyclic space (like the way you decompose a finite abelian group into cyclic subspace). However, prof applied JCF to prove Cayley - Hamilton. Fine, but I hate that approach 
4. "Eigentriangularization", "Principle of Canonical Sloppiness" are just jokes, there are rarely any mathematician that names things in such a reckless way.

Message to Prospective Students
1. You may like the course, and it may be just me who just takes this course to fulfill the prerequisites and then study another material to fill up the gap. He may be cool and amazing to a lot of people.
2. I have to clean my brain after the final so that I do not abuse notation and higher theories like this course (in other words, forget anything taught in this course).

author: elfinmimic
message:
Taken in AY24/25 Sem 2
Lecturer: Eldon CHUNG

Content is covered by other reviews
Message to prospective students:

- In exam, if you have two algorithms, one is sure correct but inefficient and the other you are not sure by is faster, write both. I did so for the UFDS O(n^2logn) question and got full marks for it. Since the assessment this semester changed (more towards open ended questions then MCQs), it is reasonable to let one save another rather than let one minor details take away a lot of marks. They also said that go for the sure right first in a technical interview, so that's something you can try in case you are not so sure abt something in the test

- Eldon's speaking is quite fast in my opinion, but he is quite good at attracting attention and make the content interesting

- Remember to state what you are using, because I forgot to declare how to store the graph and got 4 marks taken away from finals

author: elfinmimic
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Ng Kah Loon (later changed to Dr Timothy Wertz because Prof Ng was busy)

Content:
1. Systems of linear equations, Gaussian elimination.
2. Matrices, matrix operations, invertible matrices, determinants.
3. Euclidean n-space, linear spans, subspaces, linear independence, bases.
4. Vector space associated with matrices.
5. Orthogonality, orthonormal bases, orthogonal projection.
6. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, diagonalization.
7. Linear transformations.

Assessment:
 https://uploads.disquscdn.com/images/a7e5c30610f10ba57e4bf67e432da7cd84175bb4ca10a1b41df3ffb53c4eebba.png 
Some notes:
- Textbook: Linear Algebra: Concepts and Techniques on Euclidean Spaces (Second Edition) by S.L. Ma, K.L. Ng and V. Tan, McGrawHill,
2016.

- The most important stuff for this mod (I believe) is the imagination of vector space as 3d and 2d objects (line and surface) makes most of the important stuff for this mods clearer and more intuitive (and easier to remember also). For example, Ax (A being a n*n matrix, A = (c1 c2 ... cn), you can think Ax as sum(x_ic_i), which is like the linear combination of the column vectors, allowing a different perspective on matrix multiplication and helps in doing proving stuff)

- 6.4 and 7.3 were not tested (I think they only introduce stuff without digging in and make the use other than applying the materials introduced before)

- The concept of diagonalization is not quite intuitive (perhaps because I did not attend the lecture, yes I slacked off) and the book does not explain this well either (it helps a lot in calculating powers of matrix, but besides that and why do we need to do so, I do not find the answer in the textbook).

- Calculation is still quite central, perfecting (speed and accuracy) it would allow an A for this mod I believe. If you want to try out with proving stuff, thinking stuff geometrically will help (as most of the things about math), and you can also see that there are some "expression" and "pattern" that keeps appearing, taking note of it will also help. (Like AA^T for example and its properties which you can group them up and remember). Finally, practice proving (maybe ask for help on checking the proof).

Miscellaneous:
- Prof asked if he can join the group chat, but this is a lecture with like 200 students, and most of us were shy (to reply I think) and there was not too much communication (unlike higher math mods I believe). :(

- The quiz was much nicer than that of its counterparts for SoC students I believe, since we only had to do it twice to get full marks for the quiz (I believe some of us did like 10% marks twice and take the full marks). Its counterparts take the average of attempts, which is a horror to me when I know that.

author: elfinmimic
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecturers: Wang Fei and Bao Huanchen

Content:
- Another review by Flippy (same everything)

Some notes:
- For the volume calculating stuff, I find it easier to remember how the shape is divided into disjoint chunks and change the limit to an integral

- The only new things compared to high school calculus (I believe) is epsilon-delta, with which you struggle, consultation is a must if you plan to take further analysis or calculus. I think the main objective of the mod is this thing, since note that you cannot prove IVL, EVL, or if the riemann sum converges regardless of the choice of points, and integral calculation will only reappear in MA2104 I believe ?? (From CS1010S: "We (the teachers and tutors) don't bite")

- The rest is just memorization, regurgitation, and repetition of formulae.

- There are some proving stuff at the end of the paper. You may want to leave them and make sure every calculation question is correct. I have no tips for this (I am so sorry). There is even a know-how question at the end, if you know you know, it is not even doable if you do not know about the properties of a convex/ concave function. You should be quite at the top of the curve if you perfect the calculating stuff I believe (it is quite lengthy already)

Miscellaneous:
- Why does Math department don't allow APC tests later, not every international student can take it because it is so early ?
- The final does not allow cheatsheet (for what ?)
- Although the quizzes are only for a small part, but it really forces everyone to be at the class (either only for the quiz or at 8am for all the class), because without it, you will be shredded by the curve, as everyone get that 20%. This now becomes an all-final mod.
- I believe the mod was executed well, because the study time was maximized by the blended learning, leaving time for the students to absorb epsilon-delta, which I have to say again, is like 50% in understanding of the mod. (Not in test because it does not take up much anyway)

author: imwaihon
message:
I took this module in AY2014-2015 Sem 1.

A core module for Year 2 Business Analytics students. Bit of a breadth module, and aims to cover a little bit of everything - linear/logistic regression, Support Vector Machines, decision trees, text/web mining etc. A huge step up from BT1101 in my opinion, but still a very manageable module nonetheless as the math is somewhat stripped out again. However, this does not mean it is an easy module as you will need to fight the bell curve on this one.

Breakdown of marks:

50% Finals
15% Individual Assignment
30% Group Project
5% Participation

REAL learning can only be done if you read beyond the syllabus. Try to read up more as the module can only cover so much content. A good eye-opener to what you will somewhat be doing in analytics.

Finals was kind of easy and doable (read: open book) , but I made a fatal error in one of my calculations and tried to be too clever in the straightforward MCQs, which resulted in a decent but disappointing grade. The group project was decent though, and one of my members scored worse and another scored better than me, so it definitely was the finals that caused the mess. So focus on your finals guys.

Expected Grade: B+/A-

Actual Grade: B+

author: imwaihon
message:
Taken in AY2014-2015 Semester 1

A very useful module. People who have taken this module in Semester 1 under Dr Lou-Jiann Hua will know what I mean.

Being pretty useless at Calculus, in which I barely survived with a lucky B+, this module turned out to be not the run of the mill statistics you encounter during your super easy A Levels. (if you are thinking that way)

The first 6 weeks of the course is still bearable, as the material still resembles the probability you have already learned, albeit the questions are phrased in a more abstract way.

Mid-terms was pretty doable, and I managed to do decently well (35/40) for it with some luck in the end, despite being not very good at math in general. Quite a few people got full marks for the midterm, although the median was only 24/40. Standard deviation was over the roof though, I think it was like 12 marks. Those with statistics background can go infer themselves.

Second half of the semester comes the difficult part. A lot of calculus, and some of them cannot be integrated by the 'normal' way - you have to use certain results to get to it. You will also need to spend a lot of time struggling with the problems in the tutorial on your own in order to truly grasp the material, unless you are a true math whiz kid scholar - then by all means, continue to party hard. Mere mortals like me will have to spend 5 hours on a difficult question which is considered to be 'a piece of cake' by the whiz kid standard.

Dr Lou can be oddly humorous at times, with the random out of place Chinese proverb and pointing out very useful formulas such as 7.7.

Focus on the calculus part if you are taking this module under him, as it is more 'nutritious' knowledge.

Having completely bombed the finals (like getting less than 50% of the marks), I was fearing the worst. Thankfully and somewhat miraculously, I somehow salvaged an A-.

Good luck if you are taking this module in a hectic semester. Unless you are good at math. Then party on and let the curve nudge you to a glorious A+.

Expected Grade: B-/C+

Actual Grade: A-

author: imwaihon
message:
I took this mod in Semester 2, AY13/14.

Covers limits, differentiation, sequences/series and integration. Concepts are really not that hard, it is the application that requires 1) careful but quick arithmetic skills and 2) ability to recognize what the question is trying to test. If you are trying to get a decent grade, I would say tutorials would be enough. Otherwise, try to practice past year papers and get used to the "style" of questions. One thing to note about this module is that once you see the answers, you will want to facepalm yourself for flunking the easy questions.

The lecturer for my semester, Dr Wang Fei in my opinion is quite good. Despite his accent, I feel that he delivers content quite well. His analogies on how to study Math need some improving though, because they were really cheesy.

message:
I took this mod in Semester 2, AY13/14.

The Jumpluff(fluff) is strong here. Basically just very superficial knowledge about IT and IS systems. Nothing technical, although a technical background might help.

As with exam formats, for semester 1 the final exam is almost completely open ended, while in semester 2 the exam focuses more on MCQ. This might change, however.

author: imwaihon
message:
I took this mod in Semester 2, AY13/14.

Having taken CS1010S, the content taught in this module is extremely manageable. The problem here is that instead of Python, the module now is taught in Java and the effort to switch from the familiarity with one programming language to another might not be easy. If you did not clear your misconceptions earlier in the introductory programming course you took earlier, it might be difficult to follow the pace of the lectures.

What you will learn will be basically the title of this module really. Data structures and algorithms. Some basic data structures include stack(LIFO) and queue(FIFO).

Try to do well for the lab component and you should be fine.

author: imwaihon
message:
I took this mod in Semester 1, AY13/14.

The content is quite interesting, and applicable to everyday life. Your perspective of advertising, packaging and sales will change.

There is subject pool, in which you participate in some social experiments/surveys by grad students for thier research and you get 10% of your final grade. You might even earn money - I earned $20 in one of the experiements haha.

Another 10% is class participation, and 15% each for both the individual assignment and group assignment. Finals is 50%. Finals is the make or break here.

All the best! :)
