author: imwaihon
message:
I took this mod in Semester 1, AY13/14.

Firstly, a disclaimer. This is almost JC economics. Most of the microeconomics concepts are pretty much the same. Demand and Supply, Elasticity, Types of Markets, Government Intervention - If these concepts sounds familiar, this module will be a breeze.

UNTIL the second half comes. Macroeconomics. The concepts they teach is somewhat different from JC. You will have to pay attention and study more. Some chapters were completely alien to me - perhaps because I was away from school during National Service. I spent a lot of time trying to understand these concepts.

Secondly, lectures to be honest are not that useful. I personally attended almost every lecture, but most people skipped it. It is very possible to not study a lot for this module and get an A because the exams are all MCQ. Conversely, you may study a lot and still not do as well due to the steep bell curve. Luck also play a role. Most people are already quite good at economics due to JC background and the differentiating questions will almost always be certain vague questions. Here is where luck plays a big role.

Thirdly, there is no physical tutorials for this module. Just E-tutorials. We were made to use MyEconLab which endured a not-so-great start where the access keys got delayed and we only had access to the system on week 4 or 5. Basically you are required to do graded assignments which takes up a ton of time. I think 2-3 hours per week. Try to score well, it makes up 10% of your grade.

author: imwaihon
message:
I took this mod in Semester 1, AY13/14.

First module to start off for students in the Business Analytics stream in School of Computing.

This module is quite easy as almost ALL the math is stripped out. The program used is MS Excel, and the 3 types of analytics are discussed - Descriptive, Predictive and Prescriptive. You will learn how to make and interpret charts and graphs, use the Linear Regression tool, ANOVA(Analysis of Variance), Hypothesis Testing and Linear Optimization. Very basic but fundamental concepts.

A decent intro mod nonetheless.

author: Justin Tan
message: Lectures were recorded tutorials are not.

author: Justin Tan
message: AY22/23 Sem 1 Prof Zhang Lei Assessment Homework: 3 assignments across the span of the semester (Week 5, Week 9, Week 12) each bearing 8%, 8%, 9% respectively. Questions are manageable and expected to get close to full marks. Median was around 24/25 for all iirc. Midterms: 25%. Midterms was manageable as well. Median was about 42/50 iirc. Finals: 50%. Prof Lei already mentioned he was going to make finals hard as the median for midterms was higher than expected. Finals was way harder compared to midterms, tutorials and homework. The disappointing thing was that we were only give ONE practice paper for finals. Tutorials and attendance are not graded. Only attended 3 tutorials. Review Syllabus-wise, content was manageable and easily absorbable especially after doing practice. Pace picked up quickly after midterms. The module content was interesting and would recommend to those considering taking QF as a 2nd major or just want to learn more about QF. Do note that the bell curve for this module is expected to be quite high and hence there is little room for mistakes. One complaint I have is that the number of errata in the module lecture notes, tutorials, homework answers etc is uncountable... You would need to download a new set of lecture notes, tutorials etc every week because they are constantly updating the content. I once was stumped 1hr + on a question just to realise they released a updated answer key. Nevertheless, interesting and fun module, recommend to do with friends to discuss homework and tutorial answers.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY19/20 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Yu Haifeng

Assessment: 40% Midterm, 60% Finals

The mod has zero programming, and is very "mathematical" in nature. "Mathematical" in the sense that it is rather proof-based and is taught like a typical mathematics module where one first define the objects in the playground, then use these first principles to proof certain lemmas before getting to the main theorems.
 
Prof Yu expects proofs to be rigorous, however, if you are generally exposed to modules in mathematics that requires proving you will do fine, and in fact you will realise is rather manageable. The concepts are more or less intuitive, just stated in a very formal manner. Some of the proofs in the module can get very long and convoluted. Be reminded that memorising proofs is not the point of module but rather, understanding a certain principle and applying it will help anybody taking the module in the long run.

The workload is generally light because there are no assignments, just some questions set by Prof himself or from the textbook that he goes through every week. The assessment is generally not too difficult if you master the concepts and know the basic definitions. The last question of the paper is usually the most difficult question that has a lower weightage, and requires some out of the box thinking.

Finally, I like to add say that I enjoyed this module thoroughly because it exposed me to some new Computer Science, and I would think that if you are very used to the style of mathematics modules, this could be a suitable module for you to take.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taught in AY20/21 Sem 1

Lecturer: Prof Gan Wee Teck

Weightage:
50% Homework (5 sets, biweekly)
50% Presentation 

The module is divided into two parts namely, Lie Algebras and their representations as well as a brief introduction to Lie Groups. 

The content is generally a difficult one since firstly I guess Lie Algebra is generally non-associative and one needs to think somewhat differently compared to usual associative algebra representations. (but nonetheless, the theory is still a fascinating one) Secondly for the section on Lie Groups, most of us were like fish out of water due to our lack of familiarity with the zoo of differential geometry and their gadgets, so we weren't able to reap the benefits of the lecture as much as we would like to. Perhaps it might be wise to come back and recap some of the material after one learns more about differential geometry.

Prof Gan is a good lecturer as he can fully explain the intuition behind a certain idea/proof despite the sheer technicality. However, the pace of lecture is generally on the fast side since he does not have to write down the proof on a board and he just goes through his notes which he prepared before hand while lecturing.

Overall a refreshing module to take, but I think one would truly reap the full benefit of this module if one has some understanding in root systems, weyl groups and also some background in differential geometry.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY20/21 Sem 1

Lecturer: Prof Han Fei

MA5209 studies algebraic invariants in topological spaces. One utility of this theory is that we are able to better decide whether two topological spaces are different; in general, such a negative question is much harder to answer since one has to show that such a homeomorphism cannot exists but fortunately, Algebraic Topology gives us means to answer these questions and much more. 

Prof Han Fei follows Allen Hatcher's text closely. His teaching style is less pedantic and rigorous than what one usually would experience in NUS. While he does make up for the handwaving with imparting more intuition, I still think students reap more benefits by translating this intuition into a more rigorous discussion, especially so for a first reading in Algebraic Topology. 

Despite dropping a lot of details, Prof Han's pace can be considered a little on the slow side, as he follows Hatcher's text (almost) to the dot. Once he repeated 14 examples on Hatcher's text which is frankly very unnecessary. 

Finally, if one already has some exposure to some (basic) Algebraic Topology in MA4266, I am not entirely sure how much one can get out of MA5209, since there are only a few new topics to be learnt namely, covering spaces and some basic homology theory with almost little to no applications. YMMV, depending on the instructor.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY 19/20 Sem 2
Lecturer: Assistant Prof Bao Huanchen

Assessment:
70% Weekly Homework
30% Finals (Take Home)

Topics Covered:
Representation Theory of Finite Groups, Symmetric Groups and Finite Dimensional Associated Algebras. Homological Algebra

Lecture pace is on the quick side since he skips some of the details. Be sure to brush up on your linear algebra (and maybe some ring theory). That being said, Prof Bao is patient and often pauses the lecture to answer your queries during and even after class. There is a lot of content to go through for 13 weeks but a substantial portion of it boils down to terminology and definitions. Personally, I think that the iteration under Prof Bao is well suited for individuals trying to learn representation theory for the first time. 

Assessment wise, the weekly homework are fairly straight forward and most took less than a day to think and write out. As for the take home exam, most of the questions were a repeat of things that were covered in class/previous homeworks. However, there was one question in particular that required some thought and maybe further reading up.

author: monikernemo
message:
Review for 19/20 Sem 1 
Lecturer: Prof Chan Heng Huat

Assessment breakdown:
20% Weekly Homework (pick any 4 questions to do)
20% Midterm Test (2 Midterm Tests, one in week 6 the other in week 11)
60% Finals

Galois Theory is the study of fields, field extensions and field automorphisms. The highlight of the course is the famed Abel-Ruffini theorem, which says that in general, there is no solution in radicals for polynomials having degree 5 or larger. Another central tool to Galois Theory revolves around groups, or more specifically Galois Groups. The correspondence between intermediate extensions and Galois Groups makes issues less abstract and more tractable.

The main difficulty of the course is the sheer number of terminology. So be prepared to be bombarded by them. Try to revise consistently and have a good mental picture of them.

author: monikernemo
message:
AY 18/19 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Chan Heng Huat

Analytic Number Theory, as the name suggests, uses tools in real analysis (sequences, series, integrals, etc.) and complex analysis to study facts about primes and functions related to primes. These facts are established thru various computations. There isn't a whole lot of "theory" floating around - more like nifty tricks and techniques one would utilise to infer useful facts about primes and their properties. If you are interested in number theory, you should definitely check out analytic number theory.

While the prerequisite states that group theory and complex analysis are prerequisites for this course, it uses facts from them only sparingly. Basic number theory is not required to have a head start in this module. Also, the course follows Hildebrant's Analytic Number Theory course in UIUC closely. Another book that was helpful to me in furnishing some understanding in the earlier chapters is Apostol's "Introduction to Analytic Number Theory".

Proofs revolving these topics are dense! So be warned! However, the result are much more important than the proofs.

Assessment-wise 40% CA (Midterm and Homework), 60% finals. Weekly homework where you do 4 out of 6 questions and two midterms - one before recess week and the other one in week 10.
thread_title: MA4263 Introduction to Analytic Number Theory

author: monikernemo
message:
Sem 2 AY18/19

Lecturer: Prof Seth Gilbert

Content:
Focuses on different tools to analyse probability bounds and runtime of of Randomised Algorithms. The module also introduces other randomised algorithms to contextualise the usefulness of these tools.

You do need a solid understanding of basic probability. While the later topics requires more advanced machinery like Markov Chain, Martingales, we are only interested in using the results as a black box for our algorithm. 

Assessment consists of:
5 - 6 assignments, one of which is an experimental assignment where you need to answer some questions based off the results of your experiment. 

A Midterm test in week 7/8

And a final project where you attempt to simplify and explain idea behind a newly published paper on randomised algorithms. You also need to extend the result of the paper in a final report that you will submit. Try to understand the paper fully before diving in! I picked a topic on Stochastic Gradient Descent and I spent alot of time understanding what the paper was talking about. Didnt think I do too well for this component.

Another thing to note is that Prof Seth doesn't really give feedback on our submitted work (save for the final report and explanatory report). But if you have any doubts, he is more than willing to clarify it  during class / on the Slack channel he set up for the course.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY18/19 Sem 2 

Lecturer: Dr Daisy Pham

Content: This module aims to justify the methods behind statistical tests and also introduces new concepts and statistical tests beyond what one would have usually learnt in high school (the normal, t test, etc.). There is heavier focus on computation but the theoretical explanation gives us better intuition and justification to the statistical tests one would employ. Content is not difficult 

Compared to the last semester, Dr Daisy tries to incorporate some practical tools such as R. Or at least, in one of the assignments. There might be more emphasis on similar tools in future iteration of the modules if they are taught by Dr Daisy again.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY18/19 S2 
Lecturer: Prof Zhang De Qi

Overview:
Algebra 2 focuses on the study of rings and other related structures such as ideals and modules. The most enlightening aspect of Algebra 2 would be the structure theorems surrounding finitely generated modules over PID. 

Content is not difficult. Fairly straightforward. I feel that Algebra 2 is alot easier than Algebra 1. Or at least before the section on modules. Don't let the difficulty of Algebra 1 deter you from doing Algebra 2. 

LA2 knowledge is not necessary in the discussion of Ring Theory but perhaps under the discussion of Module Theory, having some LA2 background is nice. Prof Zhang tends to omit the proofs in the lecture notes but he discusses it informally during lecture. Might have to copy them down.

Questions from Midterms and finals mainly focus on definition as well as lecture note content. Might be due to the fact that this is Prof Zhang's first time teaching this module.

author: monikernemo
message:
18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Yang Yue
20% Homework
20% Midterm
60% Finals

This course is an introduction to some abstract measure theory and Lebesgue Integration. Integration is an integral (pun intended) part of mathematics. There are defects in the theory of Riemann integration which in this course we see that Lebesgue Integrals bypasses many of the technical difficulties and yet preserving many of the key results often with much simpler proof (Monotone convergence theorem and Fubini's theorem, just to name a few). If you are interested in the theory of Mathematical Analysis, Measure and Integration is surely a course that you must take.    

Content-wise, for a level 4 mod, most of the proofs and ideas involved are quite intuitive. Weekly tutorial problems are on the easy-manageable side but it is best to do them to consolidate your learning.

author: monikernemo
message:
18/19 Sem 1 
Lecturer: Mr Ravi Suppiah

While content of the module have remained the same over the years, sequence in which it has been taught is reversed (or at least in this iteration). Module first reviews C programming language before diving into more low level stuff such as Machine code (MIPS), CPU design then boolean algebra and combinatorial and sequential circuits. The module closes with very simple variants of cache design and pipelining. Content of the module is rather intuitive so if you are looking to fulfill a CS minor, CS2100 is definitely a good choice; its one of those modules that you can keep up with without consistent work. 

There are weekly labs for the modules which you are supposed to prepare before hand. That being said, the lab assignments are not difficult. They are rather quick and easy to do. Workload-wise it can get quite heavy because some of the tutorial problems are rather tedious and mechanical. I gave up literally after the 3rd week. Finals problems are similar to homework problems so you might still want to do it after all to solve finals problem more efficiently.

author: monikernemo
message:
18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: A/P Kan Min Yen/Chris Boesch
5% Class Participation
5% Google Collabs Notebook Submission
20% Midterms 
30% Project (Kaggle Competition / Study)
40% Finals
Flipped Classroom

The method of instruction in this iteration of ML is radically different from past iterations. I believe that Prof Kan wanted to focus more on the hands-on, implementation/coding side of things as opposed to keeping it mainly theoretical (since any monkey can just implement packages). 

While it was a modest attempt in transforming the module, I felt like I was struggling throughout the entire module because I was very unfamiliar with Python and its libraries and the Mathematics presented was quite watered-down and as a result, the concepts wasn't as intuitive as it should have been. I needed plenty of revision to understand concepts thoroughly and of course, the problem were compounded by the Flipped classroom. Anyways, enough with the rant. I believe that the module, under Prof Kan, would improve in future iterations. 

The module gives a very rough, macroscopic and general understanding of Machine Learning methods and some of its pitfalls and also how we can overcome these pitfalls. There is certainly some elementary (multivariable) calculus, linear algebra and probability required to understand certain concepts but do not let these deter you because the concepts involved are rather straightforward and concrete. It is still best that you refresh your linear algebra (what is Inner Product and its interpretation) , calculus (Chain Rule) and probability (especially Bayes Rule) before taking the module because these will pop up rather frequently. I feel that multivariable calculus modules will somewhat help in understanding some of the problems faced in ML.

author: monikernemo
message:
18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Lawrence Kelvin
Assessment:
20% Midterms (Open Book)
15% Term Paper Draft
15% Term Paper
20% Online Journal/Class Participation
30% Finals (Open Book)
Not Webcasted.

As the title of the module suggests, this module primarily focuses on the story of "Singapore" from 1300s till today. Personally I feel that the module widen my preconceived notions of certain ideas, for instance, National Identity, Colonialism and Imperialism and many more.

If you like going to museums (?), rejoice as some of the tutorials and lectures would take place in museums. Your term paper would also require a museum visit as the task requires us to make suggestions to improve a museum we have visited.

Workload for the module is rather light. Despite being a history module with many assigned readings with the lecture following the readings rather closely, it is possible to survive the module without going through any of the readings. The content is not important. Being a GE module, the lecturer is more interested in imparting critical thinking to students as opposed to cold hard history knowledge (which clearly, the former is the more useful choice). This is also reflected in the type of question he sets in midterm and finals paper; the lecturer rarely test actual content of the syllabus. So if you're looking for a chill GES mod to slack off this semester, GES1011 is not a bad choice.
thread_title: GES1011 The Evolution of a Global City-State

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY17/18 Special Term 2
Lecturer: Dr Henry Chia

20-25% Lab
25% Practical Exam
50% Finals

CS2030 focuses more on code design and desirable principles one should adhere to. The entire course will be focused on Java - in particular the OOP paradigm and for the last 2 weeks of the course branching off into some functional programming and parallel computing - which I find the later topics to be quite weird since it has no clear synergy with the front parts and functional is not really a thing in java hmm
 
This being a course in special term, it feels like dissemination of new information can be a little quick because there are some concepts that are difficult to understand/appreciate at first glance. And before the information settles, you're being thrown more information. However, I found first part of the course on OO being "easier" because of my background in 2040. For people in special term, I guess it would help more if you had prior exposure to Java/2040 (and not to mention, some data structures in 2040 can really help with some of the lab problems). 

Lab workload is generally manageable with 2 lab assignments per week. Tutorial are not essential (?) Mainly on Java interactions. That being said, 2030 is not really a traditional lecture-tutorial module. Students can only really reap its benefit by writing more code.

author: monikernemo
message:
AY17/18 Sem 2
Lecturer: Assoc Prof Chin Chee Whye

Assessment:
40% Homework
20% Midterm
40% Finals

Content:
Topology is concerned with the study of topological spaces continuous maps between topological spaces. Module can get quite content heavy: we covered topological spaces (standard topologies and various notions like compactness, connectedness), topological groups, compactifications, Tychonoff theorem, Urysohn Lemma and Tietze Extension and also nets (generalization of sequences), Fundamental group (and very fun results like there is continuous map from unit ball to circle that is a retraction, Borsuk-Ulam) and also briefly touched on the topology of compact convergence. Prof Chin does follow discussion in Munkres closely.  

Homeworks:
Starts off quite chill but can get quite tough when you need to use some very very involved and non-trivial tricks. Just keep in mind some heuristics like showing a set is open you just need to show that you can write it as a union of open sets or even connectedness; if you want to show that a property holds for an entire set that is connected, you just have to show that a subset with such a property is non-empty and simultaneously closed and open.

Midterm/Exams:
Midterm was quite tough because of time constraint (4 questions in 1.5 hours) and questions are far from trivial (like the Lebesgue Number Lemma??) ; there were no "free-marks". Finals was much easier and merciful but I could have done better than expected.    

Ultimately the mod is rewarding because of the slew of applications topology has in different branches of mathematics.

author: monikernemo
message:
AY 17/18 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Dinh Tien Cuong

20% Homework (5 sets to be completed every fortnight)
20% Midterm
60% Finals

Content:
Module split into two halves: first half focuses on the elephant in the room, Complex analysis, which is the study of holomorphic functions (which a whole lot nicer than real differentiable functions) and the second half is an introduction into Riemann surfaces. This course under Prof Dinh is really difficult for 2 main reasons: the entire complex analysis part of the module is squeezed into the first 6 weeks, which can be horrendous given that it is our first time studying complex analysis and also we spent first 2-3 weeks reviewing topology. Secondly, Riemann surfaces are not exactly the easiest things to study as an undergrad; to have a good grasp of it requires many areas of Mathematics: Complex Analysis (duh), Manifolds, Geometry, Topology, Group Theory. Its not easy trying to put everything together. So be warned! Lecture pace is really quick so you need to do a lot of revision so as to not fall behind (I fell behind quite often and I totally gave up for Riemann surfaces). 

Prof Dinh is a lecturer that focuses more on the big picture of the proof rather than the nitty gritty technicalities so I guess in some sense it is good because it helps to develop some intuition as to why certain results are true. 

Tutorial:
Tutorials are not easy. But they contain many useful tricks and results essential to the study of complex analysis and Riemann surfaces respectively. The questions are not important but the way of thinking and proving are more essential than what is presented.

Tests/Exams:
Thankfully, Prof Dinh is quite merciful when it comes to exam/tests. Computational question usually comes out for exams and usually only 1/2 questions will be really difficult. Prof Dinh is also very lenient with his allocation for marks (even homework)

I think its one of the few modules that you have to work hard consistently because it is very fast paced and the content is difficult. I think the end results can be rewarding but then again if you have no interest in learning Riemann surfaces, perhaps the iteration of S-mod under Prof Dinh is not suitable for you.

author: monikernemo
message:
AY17/18 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Tan Ban Pin

25% Midterm
75% Finals
Webcasted 

PnC is one of those few things that I liked fervently in JC but some how when I take this module, I really loathed it. Essentially first half of module covers basic combinatorics; actually more towards A level H3 stuff if you are pre 2014 batch. Pretty much on the easier end of things save for some additional identities Dr Tan would test like the Vandemonde; which I found burdensome to remember. I think the most interesting to learn in first half of the class would be the generating functions; it simplifies calculation by a whole load. I thought solving recurrence relation is quite interesting too but too bad theres no theory being covered at all. 

2nd half of module covers Graph theory. Being a fairly new topic, expect it to be bombarded with definitions. Getting definitions is half the battle won. The topic is not difficult per se, but getting through all the definitions is quite tedious. The next most difficult thing (and alot more interesting) would be the proofs of the graph questions. It is crazy (but also remotely interesting) how a traversing several nodes on the graph guarantees certain properties. Really do try and think out of the box for this one. 

Dr Tan is a good lecturer but just that he is overly repetitive. If you prefer a slower pace of teaching with plentiful of worked examples, Dr Tan would certainly float your boat. 

On tutorial; combi mainly focuses on calculation questions. If you are fairly steady with your calculation there is no need to put too much effort on them. Graph questions there's a mix of proving and calculations. Being a fairly new topic, it is advisable to practice some calculation questions. MTT is general standard fare; nothing out of a blue. Finals would recommend people to do the combi part before the graph part because certain graph questions can be a time sink.  

For Math Students: I don't really recommend doing this module unless you want to venture further into graph theory? Personally found Dr Tan's style to be on the mundane side. so...

For Computing Student, I would not recommend doing this module for your science requirement. The graph algo taught in this module is very basic (and by that I mean really basic). Covers only Kruskal, Graph Colouring Algo, Depth First Search and Dijkstra. I think Graph and Combi 2 would be a great follow up to this.

author: monikernemo
message:
2017/18 Sem 1 
Lecturer: Prof Yang Yue

(+-10% accuracy)
20% Homework
20% Midterms
60% Finals

Not as difficult as one may think for a module that is decidedly "pure math" in nature. Do expect the course load for first half is very light; basically iteration of whatever you've done in MA1100 under Prof Chin, (basic axioms, sets, ordering relation, equivalence, construction of N/Z/Q/R, Cantor-Bernstein) and perhaps some stuff from 'real analysis' (like facts about R and its various set-theoretic properties). Only difference may be definitions being more specific and other subtle differences etc.

I think it is only in the second half where you learn new things like ordinal and cardinals numbers, well-ordering, and an more in-depth discussion on the axiom of choice which in MA1100 (at least in my memory), where there wasn't enough time for proper treatment/discussion.

Homeworks are to be submitted weekly; but it will at most be 2 questions and questions are not very difficult. Tutorials; I don't recommend doing them because questions are really trivial. But that being said, tutorial is a good way to formalize your ideas. Tests and Finals are not super difficult per se but requires clever use of certain construction. I was quite tilted by the finals and effectively lost 20-30 marks from it? And also, be very careful with True and False questions even though it is like really really simple. 

I think discussion in Prof Yang's class is pretty much 2 ways. Content can get really dry sometimes because the proofs are quite technical? Also, Prof Yang focuses more on the big picture than rigor and axioms. Which I personally think it is not a bad thing; without ideas, creativity and motivation, mathematical ideas would are just mere exercises in logic. but funnily enough, he still requests for proofs to be rigorous (LOL) 

Ever since my time as a freshman (cheh only 1 academic year ago), I was intrigued by set theory and despite how naked and simple it is, it manages to hold much of mathematics together. When I attend Prof Yang's class, it still never fails to amaze me and I legitimately look forward to every other lesson. Do take this module if you want to know more about set theory that is not covered in MA1100.

author: monikernemo
message:
17/18 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Chin Chee Whye

40% Homework
20% Midterm
40% Finals

This mod is essentially Analysis III in disguise. Instead of doing single variable calculus, Prof Chin SUPS up the discussion into the analysis of multivariable calculus. Depending on the lecturer that is teaching the course, linear alg 2 may or may not be useful here. In multivariable context, it is certainly useful (I was a blind bat in the discussion of differentiation because I had no LA2 knowledge whatsoever).  

Instead of the "traditional" route of differentiation, integration followed by sequences of function, Prof Chin (with very good reasons) reverses the discussion starting with spaces of functions, integration (yes, without differentiation) and ending off with differentiation. Content is more abstract than normal module counterpart but abstraction is good not for its own sake (we can have these results in a more general context). Some content for lecture comes from rudin but he usually diverges discussion from Rudin quite some bit.

This rendition under Prof Chin, as his typical policy,  homework weightage is high; though difficulty varies it is largely manageable given the amount of time we have to complete each homework (1 week).  There are no tutorials per se; he goes through lecture content during supposed tutorial sessions). Midterm was the hardest part of the assessment whereas finals is manageable as he recycles some content from homework. 

If you are undetered by MA1S, MA2S is a necessary follow up for all the basic machinery developed in MA1. But depending on your lecturer, your experience and what you learn may vary.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY17/18 Sem 1
Lecturers: Mr Aaron Tan (first half)/ Prof Gary Tan (second half)
Webcasted

(+-5% margin of error)
5% Attendance
10% Take-home lab
10% PE1
15% PE2
15% Midterm
40% Finals
(Not bellcurved? At least according to Aaron/Gary)

CS1010 introduces programming using C as a medium. Learning how to program is useful but this, being an introductory module, only introduces the very fundamental, almost barebone,  concepts in coding/programming. For an individual without computing background, I personally found this mod to be on the one of the "easier" modules I have taken in the semester (the others being proof intensive math mods). (PS this morning is not the last word on programming; alot more algos and stuff to learn to make good use of them)

The content covered are as follows: fundamental data types, conditionals, loops, arrays, sorting, strings, file processing and recursion. Though it may sound like there's loads to cover, pace of the course is on the slower side; but maybe because the lecturers tend to overrun their lecture 10-15 min after the bureaucratic 1.5 hrs set by NUS. Both Mr Aaron and Prof Gary are very competent lecturers. 

Workload is moderate; take home labs are not very difficult but not exactly trivial (in sense that it is very easy to pass all test cases). Do read carefully and think carefully about the algorithm you want to implement. Tutorials are useful for understanding "idiosyncrasies" of C coding; behaviours and such. Otherwise my tutor find most of the question a waste of time and skips most of them anyway. I do recommend doing additional lab practices for the sake of getting familiar with C and problem formulation and solving which is essential for PE. 

PEs (practical exams) are the real killers; while I did relatively well against the cohort I felt that I could have done better if time was not the limiting factor. The problems can be a little timesink so hopefully, all the time you spent doing those additional practice would come into play in helping to solve the PE problems.

Midterm and finals: not very challenging except for 1 or 2 problems? Some problems are quite standard (tracing, syntax etc) and there will always be a few questions on recursion and file processing so be sure to practice well for those.

author: monikernemo
message:
17/18 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Zhang Lei
Not Webcasted

10% Assignment (once every two weeks)
30% Midterms
60% Finals

Algebra I mainly focuses on the study of a type of algebraic structure known as groups. Personally I found this module to be rather eye-opening because there are many interesting things that we can infer about (finite) groups given basic group axioms and some other property known to us.

First half of this course (the S version) parallels its normal module counterpart (more so, because Dr Zhang was also teaching the normal module in the same semester). First half introduces group axioms, basic definition and important structures and notions that we are interested to study in group theory. Pace is generally slow before recess because there is absolutely interesting one could do with just mere definitions. However by the midway mark, we hit the isomorphism theorems and that's where the more exciting parts starts to come in and the course picks up pace (also when things start to go downhill too). After the isomorphism theorems, the course somewhat diverges from its normal module counterpart - we went into group action, Sylow's theorem, then group classification before ending off with a little representation theory.

Dr Zhang is a good lecturer; he explains clearly and concisely every step of his proof. However, he does not really recap some of the concepts we left off in the previous lecture so I think it is best to do your own revision otot. Dr Zhang's tutorials are on the challenging side - also because he does not upload the tutorial questions until the day before tutorial starts. I think his tutorial are good practices for you to formalize your ideas in proofs. 

On assessment - Assignments award very little for the amount of work you have to do (and more not every question is graded) and midterms was very easy (average was 90/100). Finals can be on the challenging side but in fact the paper was rather manageable after we found out that a large proportion of his questions come from his lecture notes or even the normal module's tutorials. Unfortunately I messed up big time so I was hoping (my peers did as badly as I did jk) the CA can make it up?

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in 16/17 Sem 2

Lecturer: Vik Gopal, Tutor: Cai Zhibo
Webcasted. (in fact due to shitty lecture slots, I survived entirely on webcast)
Cross-listed as ST2131

10% Assignment, 10% Quiz, 30% Midterm, 50% Finals

If you're a Pure/Applied Math major, do take module under ST2131 in CORS because you can satisfy faculty requirement without using up other UEs. Also do take module with MA1104 or MA2104 (multi-variable calculus) to have better knowledge and practice of multivariable integration, change of variables (thoroughly used and abused) through out this module.

While this module serves as an introduction to university level probability, it is not a walk in the park as one may expect. First half of module is flipped classroom (a la GER1000) revises some JC level Permutation and Combinatorics or Counting Problems before going into Probability axioms and discrete probability distribution functions for discrete cases (your binomial, poisson distributions). There are plans to turn first half of the module flipped for subsequent batches. Second half introduces continuous probability density functions (and of course integration), joint probability density functions (multivariate cases) and how to deal with them, moment generating functions, properties of expectations and last but not least, the central limit theorem. Dr Vik is less concerned about the theoretical cornerstones of probability theory and focuses more on intuition, thinking, problem-solving and computation. 

Personally felt that module is quite difficult even though it is taught during Sem 2 (where the lecturer comes from the department of Stats and Applied Probability). Difficulty does not stem from concepts/understanding; they are fairly straightforward and rather concrete. However, the kind of thinking processes involved, the applications of certain principles to solve problems makes this module difficult. For instance, the use of conditional probability on indicator functions to calculate expected value - under test conditions, it is difficult to think about it. 

Like I mentioned earlier, there was much focus on application, thinking processes and use of 'statistical'/'probabilistic' intuition in Dr Vik's iteration of the module. Hence for those more 'mechanically' inclined with manipulation, integration and differentiation, I would advice doing the module in Sem 1 because types of questions offered seem to be fairly standard - if you can solve the harder tutorial questions with ease, the exams would not be much different and I don't see how the module offered by Maths department would/could make it less computational.

Dr Vik is one of the best lecturers one can get. He is patient in his explanations and goes thru each and every concept very thoroughly. Dont think anyone should really complain (apart from difficulty of tutorials, Midterm tests and final examinations).

author: monikernemo
message:
Took in 16/17 Sem 2

Lecturer: Loy Hui Chieh
Tutor: Wilson Lee
Webcasted

This module is a good exposure module for the subject of philosophy. Good balance between depth and breadth of the topics discussed. If you're interested with the issues philosophers dabble in such as ethics, or even if ethics is legit, eating factory farmed animals, should you overthrow the government and how you know if you're living in a 'pseudo-matrix' then you'll definitely find the module interesting. There is also a good attempt to incorporate various 'logic' concepts such as, necessary and sufficient conditions (if, then), conjunction (and), disjunction (or), contradiction/inconsistency, which people may (or may not) find it useful in helping them with their logical thinking.  

Though there are assigned readings and readings are typically mandatory, I think it is fine to not do any before hand. You'll be able to understand the lecture perfectly though weekly quizzes test some of the 'trivia' in the reading.

Assessment wise, quizzes take up to 36% of the total score, Project nearing end of the term (to create any media that discusses/conveys topics covered in class) takes up to 12%, Tutorial participation another 12% and Final Examination (opened book mcq) takes up about 40% of the total score. Bell curve can be quite steep, 75th percentile was about 24? And median was around 21/30. Quizzes were rather interesting but can get super mind boggling at times; do take this module with a group of friends so you can have a better time handling the quizzes.
thread_title: GET1029 Life, the Universe, and Everything

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in 16/17 Sem 2

Lecturer: Vik Gopal, Tutor: Cai Zhibo
Webcasted. (in fact due to shitty lecture slots, I survived entirely on webcast)
Cross-listed as ST2131

10% Assignment, 10% Quiz, 30% Midterm, 50% Finals

If you're a MA or AM major, do take module under ST2131 in CORS because you can satisfy faculty requirement without using up other UEs. Also do take module with MA1104 or MA2104 (multi-variable calculus) to have better knowledge and practice of multivariable integration, change of variables (thoroughly used and abused) through out this module.

While this module serves as an introduction to university level probability, it is not a walk in the park as one may expect. First half of module is flipped classroom (a la GER1000) revises some JC level Permutation and Combinatorics or Counting Problems before going into Probability axioms and discrete probability distribution functions for discrete cases (your binomial, poisson distributions). There are plans to turn first half of the module flipped for subsequent batches. Second half introduces continuous probability density functions (and of course integration), joint probability density functions (multivariate cases) and how to deal with them, moment generating functions, properties of expectations and last but not least, the central limit theorem.

Personally felt that module is quite difficult, be it in Sem 1 (taught by Math Deparment), or Sem 2 (taught by department of Stats and Applied Probability). Difficulty does not stem from concepts; concepts are fairly straightforward and not horribly abstract. However, the kind of thinking processes involved, the applications of principles statistics to solve problems makes this module difficult. There was much focus on application and thinking processes in Dr Vik's iteration of the module.  Hence for those more 'mechanically' inclined with manipulation, integration and differentiation, I would advice doing the module in Sem 1 because types of questions are fairly standard - if you can solve the harder tutorial questions with ease, the exams would not be much different and I don't see how the module offered by Maths department would make it less computational. 

Dr Vik is one of the best lecturers one can get . Dont think anyone should really complain (apart from difficulty of tutorials, Midterm tests and final examinations).

author: monikernemo
message:
Lecturer: A/P Chin Chee Whye

Taken in 16/17 Sem 2
Weightage:
Assignment 48%
Midterm 16%
Finals 36%

Took this module to try-out SPM. Typically in blocks of 3 hours lecture, what you see is what you get on the timetable. No 'tutorials' per se because tutorials are integrated into lectures and he only spend times discussing material from text. There are no 'tutorial questions' allocated, only homework and style of lesson is roughly like MA1100 if you've done the module under him. Generally class size is small (my year was around 14) and can vary, so I suspect the grades for module is most likely not moderated.

Course is divided roughly to 6 parts:
1. (Ordered) Fields and other preliminaries
2. Sequences 
3. Construction of the Real and Properties
4. Series and various convergence tests
5. Continuous functions and properties
6. Introduction to Topology (Not really examinable but you can use concepts from topology in exam)

Some parts of the course material can be dry. I often lose attention and focus. But otherwise most mathematical proofs are not entirely foreign and not difficult follow. It's good to review and revise the proofs that he produced (on the spot, hand written, A for affort) during discussion after lectures.

I believe the whole point of the module (or rather of Prof Chin) is to let us learn about the whole business of analysis and more importantly, various concepts fundamental to analysis, minus the limited scope/lenses of analysis. What are the behaviours endowed to fields of with different properties? How do we extend meaning of continuity to a general context? How do we extend the meaning of continuity to other functions that are necessarily real valued? There is also an emphasis on utility for why certain concepts are developed as opposed to others.

He does not go through homework questions during lecture unless requested because I think he trusts that we know what went wrong and fix it? That being said, homework questions are not impossible, just need to think carefully sometimes.

Midterm questions are tough. There are no 'standard calculation questions' (i.e. showing convergence for a particular type of sequence etc.) and I think at least one or two question requires uses result (not entirely trivial) from your homework. Heuristics is important but I believe he also wants us to present the solutions rigorously. So be warned! Did quite badly for Midterms despite given 8 questions in 2.5 hours.

On other hand finals is rather easy; 6 questions in 2 hours. I think only 1 question is regurgitation (reproduce the proof that the alternating harmonic series is convergent), otherwise other questions can be challenging but still considered 'humane' by his standards.

Past year papers generally vary in difficulty and types of questions. But in general, I think depending on how your lecturer wants to teach the module and the pedagogical goals he wants to achieve, the types of question would be different. So doing PYP might get you super demoralised or happy, but it will not be a good gauge for your performance in your finals.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in AY 16/17 Sem 2
Lecturer: Dr Zhang Yang
Tutor: Lou Jing
Webcasted

Assessment:
Online Quiz
Tutorial Participation
2 Assignments
Midterm
Finals

Simply put, this module attempts to justify on a fundamental level, the use of the demand and supply model that is essential for any form of economic analysis. It is insightful not only for economic majors (rather than regarding the demand or supply curve as arbitrarily given but also looking at the necessary assumptions that creates such a useful, applicable model) also individuals who wish to find out more regarding backbone of microeconomics. 

Workload is relatively light, only one lecture and tutorial per week in FASS and for content for first half (consumers and choice of consumption) is conceptually similar to the second half (firms and choice of production).

Mathematics used in modules can be a a teeny weeny bit foreign to the unitiated (mainly lagrangian multiplier for optimisation) but it is nothing deep or complicated and Dr Zhang would guide you through easily. 

Speaking about Dr Zhang, she is a fantastic lecturer and she explains concepts rather well and effectively. Concepts were easily digestable and tutorial questions were on the easy side which serves as an affirmation of your mastery over the typical calculation type questions. (read 'bread and butter') however when it comes to mid term tests or final examinations, questions could get a little challenging because the question aims to test your understanding as opposed to your mechanical abilities to do tutorial questions hence most of the student population were thrown off quite a fair bit (bell curve easy game) as the methods to do the questions could be a little different. Tutor Lou Jing (I believe) is a PhD student with the department she is patient and revises the concepts well and effectively.

Anyhow, I would recommend this module for people interested to take this module as an UE but only if you are interested. Otherwise do some other mods that you can SU instead.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in 16/17 Sem 2

Lecturer: Prof Tan Ser Peow
Tutor:Wang Liu Quan
NOT webcasted (but strongly suspect only for this sem)

Focusing more on computation than theory, this module aims to extend some fundamental concepts in calculus to 3D space and also introduce vector calculus. Personally, I feel that while the questions can feel less concrete to visualise (seriously how do you even draw a parabolic hyperboloid properly??), this also implies that questions that come out aren't typically out of the norm and usually quite run off the mill (as opposed to MA1102R under Dr Wang Fei). Quite an enjoyable and useful module to take. Prof Tan is rather an amiable, friendly and approachable Professor and so is Mr Wang Liu Quan, our tutor. 

Module can be split into 3 parts:
Differentiation -which explores partial derivative, vector differentiation and some optimisation problems. Also introduces and revisits some theorems in higher dimensional case like extreme value theorem, ivt etc.,

Integration - introduces integrals of higher dimensions, double/triple integral, exploring different parametrization, change of variables (substitution) in higher dimensional case, and 
Last but not least, Vector Integration - line integrals, surface integrals and their vector field counterparts as well as the big 3 theorems from Green, Gauss and Stoke. 

Midterms generally not difficult since involves only differentiation (and we know how we all like to do differentiation than integration) which also means a steep curve; median was 38/50 for my year. Finals typically not difficult but time management is a problem if one spends too much time on the easier questions in front and have no time left for the harder questions at the back. 

There are three ways to do well for this module: practice practice and practice! Like all calculus examination, the only way one can do well is really to get exposure and practice since time management can be of concern in finals. So do strive for accuracy and speed in practice which will certainly give you some edge over your peers. 

Would recommend people to take this as an (only if you have fufiled prerequisite, otherwise don't bother) UE since it is really just purely computational. For any majors that require you to do MA2216/ST2132, I think it's good that you do this module with that mod since the double/triple integral part can come in handy.

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in 16/17 Sem 1

Lecturer: Kelvin Seah (Micro) / Georgios Georgiou (Macro) 

Tutor: DIng Gong Yi 

Having no prior background in Economics, I thought it would be helpful to write a module review based on my experience. Chose to do this mod in my first semester because I am doing a double major in Mathematics and Economics and I can advance to other modules in the following semester. (since there is only 1 level 1000 econs mod) 

The module is split into two parts, Microeconomics and Macroeconomics. In Micro you will learn about the theory of Supply and Demand (this is every important and will be the foundation of every other topic in this module) and different types of market structure and the rationale behind decision making in these market structures. In Macro, it is the study of productivity at the national level and also how various 'natural mechanisms' and government policy plays a role in regulating the economy. 

Personally I feel that micro is way way easier to understand than macro because it is a lot more intuitive albeit for some parts, for instance, why a firms makes the most profit when the firm's Marginal Cost (MC) Curve intersects with the Average Variable Cost (AVC) Curve, are not very well-explained and feels quite arbitrary. For Macro I felt that there were many leaps of logic when we are moving from concept to concept and takes more time for concepts to sit in (for instance, purchase of goods improves the economy and central bank purchase bonds creates more money for the country) But after learning about Macro, you'll be able to better appreciate the issues surrounding issues of global economics. 

I feel that one should try all the pre-lecture material as well as attend all lectures because there are participation marks given out for both. 

On tutorials:
In general, the type of questions that comes out for tutorial will not come out for tests and finals. Aside from calculation questions, the other open-ended, non-calculation tutorial questions is more 'application-based' - in the sense that they'll give you an atypical scenario and expect you to apply the concepts you learnt in this scenario. 

I feel that my tutor, Gong Yi has done a fantastic job as he usually recapitulates the key concepts in lecture before each tutorial and also reinforces his explanation of tutorial questions with more advanced knowledge if necessary (to cover for the leap of logic in macro content because he mentioned to us that its really watered down as opposed to the previous years). Last but not least, he is also always willing to help and also sends us a summary of what we've learnt in the previous week on the weekends. YMMV depending on tutor.

Mid-Term Tests and Finals:
As others have mentioned, bell-curve is rather steep (but the definition of steepness depends where you're coming from). The median was 31 for mid-term; was quite satisfied with the 38 I received for MTT. Content-wise,  Mid-term would generally cover all of Micro and finals will cover some micro (generally the last one and a half chapters of micro) and all of Macro. In general try to read every question properly and do every question meticulously. There are some clever play of words and terms in the MCQ questions which may make you lose some marks. 

To prepare for MTT and even finals one should also forgo your intuition and try to stick with definitions (although in case of Econs, a lot less rigorous than mathematics) - especially so when there are so many counter-intuitive examples in economics. Personally, I feel that to achieve success in this mod, it is mandatory to have a good understanding of the underlying mechanism of various market structure and Macroeconomics Model. 

MTT feels more time-tied compared to the final examination - I had about 5-10 mins to spare after finishing the MTT paper and one round of checking whereas for finals I had around half an hour left after finishing and checking.  

Final note: 
Dont be deterred by the 3 hours lecture slot! Lecture usually lasts an hour and a half or at very best (or worst), two hours. 

For students trying to clear UE: Since it is not too difficult for a non-H2 econs student with no Econs background so I think those who enjoyed Econs at JC level would certainly do well for this mod. 

Expected grade - A
Actual grade - A+

author: monikernemo
message:
Taken in 16/17 Sem 1

Lecturer: Chin Chee Whye

The main purpose of the module is to first introduce the cornerstone topics of modern mathematics (mainly the language of logic and set theory before introducing more familiar 'mathematical objects' like natural/integers/real numbers, and concepts like counting), As an introductory module, it is also fitting for this module to set the expectations for university level mathematics (instead of focusing on the 'how's of mathematics which we are familiar in our 12 years of education, there is a larger emphasis on the 'why's and 'what's) and future mathematics module. 

The module follows the text 'Fundamentals of Mathematics: An Introduction to Proofs, Logic, Sets, and Numbers' by Bernd. S. W. Schroder quite closely. I recommend getting a physical copy of the book as there are some definitions that Prof Chin abides(whereas for definitions that he does not agree with, Prof Chin will point out and explain some flaws the author made in definition and then revise the definition made by the author). Prof Chin uses a PDF version of the text to teach us during the lecture (reminds you of the pastiche OHS days where your teacher will go thru lines and lines of comprehension). There are also a few exercises that you can try in the book to check your understanding or for practice before a test. The other recommended text written by Polya, 'How to Solve it', is not mandatory and its content will not be tested during the test nor the finals.

While Prof Chin does not follows the schedule (uploaded onto IVLE) super religiously (depending on how the previous lecture went and how much progress he was able to make), it is still very helpful to do readings (as written on schedule on IVLE) because I guarantee you without doing some prior reading you will be quite lost during the lecture. It is not helpful that the author is somewhat convoluted in his explanation and often confuses his readers. But with Prof's Chin's explanation (and with your initial 'blur' impression of reading), it clears things up quite significantly.

Personally, I find that question on some of the topics, especially set-theory, divisibility, and cardinality, difficult to do because for set-theory and cardinality they are rather abstract while for divisibility it can get quite tedious with working down inductions on inductions. For homework, make sure you attempt every question and do take your time to think through carefully. At no point should you rely only on 'intuition'; definitely use definitions to guide you through. Try to get close to full marks for all your homework assignment. (discuss with your kakis if possible! they can point our your mistake quite easily)

Glimpse of topics discussed in the module:
Logic
Naive Set Theory (as a brief precursor) and ZFC (Zermelo-Frankel) Set Theory Axioms
Equivalence Relations
Total Order Relations
Construction of Natural Numbers, Integers, Rationals, Reals and Complex
Proofs of various theorems in different set of numbers (for instance, for Natural Numbers: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, Unique Remainder arising from Division Algorithm, Well-Ordering Principle etc.)
Mathematical Induction 
Cardinality
Continuum Hypothesis
Well-Ordering Theorem

Mid-Term Test and Examination:
There will be 2 Mid-Term Test and one Final examination. The 2 mid-term test will constitute 26% of your final grade, while the final examination constitutes 50% of your final grade. There will be a few proving question, single answer question, and many many true-false questions. There will be negative marking for the true/false questions in all both tests and the final examination. (Possibly to de-incentivise people from guessing which will yield you around half the marks of the section if negative marking not applied). Duration of both tests are quite tight - slightly more than an hour to an hour and a half. For finals, time management is alot more bearable - two hours to complete one proving and two less single answer-type question than the mid term tests. 

My advice for the test and exam? Start with the proving questions and try the ones you can do first (usually the mathematical induction proofs are quite easy). Skip the ones you cannot do (i.e. after thinking for 5 minutes, nothing comes out). Then move on to single answer type questions and skip if nothing comes out of your head before doing a set of 10 true-false questions (the 10 true-false questions are related to the same topic). Dont leave all the true-false questions to the end as you will tend to make some careless mistake so do alternate them and the single-answer type questions.

If aiming for an A try to score above 90 and 110 (out of 120 and 140) for MT1 and MT2 respectively. As for finals I left around 2 true-false blank (each 1 mark) and was not able to do half of a proving question. 

Final note:
For students looking to clear faculty requirement/UE, please do not do this module. As mentioned previously, it is not the usual mathematics that we all know and love (or hate). I suggest you do something more computational based like MA1101R - Linear Algebra 1. While MA1101R does have some emphasis on proofs, larger proportion of the marks go into computation. 

Would recommend MA/AM majors to do this in y1s1 because first year got free S/U? And I feel Prof Chin will make this module an enjoyable experience for anybody who has a passion for Mathematics. 

Expected Grade - A- / A
Final Grade - A+

message:
CS1010X Module feedback by Yan Ting
Semester taken: AY15/16 Sem 2

I find CS1010X really interesting although I would say that the concepts can be challenging to understand at first. This module really trains your problem-solving thinking skills and you would find that you become smarter after taking this module.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Sean Tay
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

CS1010FC serves as a good stepping stone for (just about) anyone who is going to enroll in NUS soon, especially for those whose brains have collected dust and dirt after their numerous field camps in NS. Trust me, at first your thought processes may feel slow and cumbersome, but after awhile, they'll get faster.

The online course was spread out over a duration of 6 months for me, so workload was easily managed. It utilizes an online system called Coursemology for homework distribution/submission, lecture slides, past year exam questions, a forum for Q&A, contests, and other paraphernalia. Video lectures are used, as well as WebEx tutorials and recitations, so mostly everything can be done from the comfort of home, except for exams (which allow for 1-2 cheat sheets anyways) and the class-based Recitations/Tutorials. A part of the final grade will be based on how much participation was done in Coursemology - so do everything.

Professor Ben is a very good and experienced teacher. Not only does he keep the in-class sessions interesting, he imparts life lessons too. His flexible Teaching Assistants are dedicated to our development in this course, and extra help is definitely given when needed. Homework is graded quickly, and occasional comments are given for self-improvement.

The programming language used in this class is Python, which is very syntactically light when compared to others. This is to allow for easier problem solving, avoiding a lot (A LOT) of unnecessary trouble searching for that missing semicolon at the end of code statement, hence keeping our focus towards formulating a solution. Later on there will be some C, but the problem solving methods are usually more important than the syntax (unless stated otherwise).

The whole idea of this class is to manage complexity. Not just the complexity of the problem itself, but to sort through an infinite number of solutions to find the most efficient one. Problem solving skills and methods that are taught in this class can also be implemented in other applications besides computing.

To summarize, this module is a must do, especially if this is one of your core modules. The basic material is important, and the long duration of this class will help to strengthen your understanding of them better than a regular-length module would.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Vinny
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

I took this module without any prior programming experience and I still managed to do quite well. So for anyone who didn't code before, rest assured that this module is more than accessible as long as you diligently do the assignments.

The course centers around a rpg game concept, where students gain levels and earn achievements by doing the assignments/participate in contest on an online platform called Coursemology. It turns out to be very fun and the level serves as a performance indicator, letting us know if we need to catch up. There are many interesting optional contests for students to take part in, which will take quite some time but are very rewarding. Coursemology also comes with a very active forum where the students, professor and tutors engage in discussions.

For those who wants to read up before joining, the programming language we used is Python and some C near the end of the course. For extra practice, you can try honing your skills on projecteuler.net (which is a math/programming challenge site) though there should be more than enough practice available on Coursemology.

The content is interesting, the supporting platform is great and the workload just right. By the end of the course, you will be able to do things that are unimaginable (creating an AI that plays a mobile game) and learn concepts that will be invaluable in future computer science modules.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Mak Qi En
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

Summary: For NSmen, this is a great way to "de-rust" your brain after 2 years in NS. Strongly recommended!

[Info]

Took the module from January to June 2014, lecturer was Prof Ben Leong and TAs were Mr Brian Koh and Ms Michelle Teo.

[Content]

For those who has not done programming before, it can be quite a steep learning curve. Then again, since CS1010 is a compulsory module for all Computing and Engineering students, I'd recommend you to take CS1010FC since this module spans 5-6 months instead of the usual 18 weeks for CS1010/CS1010E, which means you will have more time to learn (and of course pester the lecturer/TAs for consultations).

This module is taught in Python, nonetheless the skills taught in this module can be easily applied in other programming languages. As the name of the module suggests, this module teaches you the different methods of programming, and does not focus on Python-specific functions. Having done programming in C before, I find that Python is easier to use as it is closer to the human language in terms of syntax and key words. At the end of the module, basic C programming was covered and it was mainly about coding some simple programs that were already covered but in C this time.

[Delivery]

Video lectures were uploaded weekly, and the lectures were uploaded in parts based on sub-topics, usually 5 videos of approximately 10 minutes each. This was very beneficial during revision as you need not have to scroll through an entire hour of video just to replay the lecture on a specific topic. On certain weeks when students had difficulty understanding concepts covered in the previous week, the lecturer took time to recap those concepts before moving on with the lecture proper. In addition, the lecturer and TAs were very helpful and were always more than willing to stay behind after the class has ended to clarify any doubts.

Recitations/tutorials were conducted online for the first three months, and physically for the next three months. Online recitations/tutorials were conducted "live" using WebEx, and if you have a microphone can speak to your lecturer/TAs (instead of having to type your queries). The lecturer and tutors were very engaging and it will be difficult to doze off during their lessons! While the online recitations/tutorials were recorded, it is best to attend them instead of watching the webcast as you can clarify your doubts with the lecturer/TAs "on-the-spot".

Assignments are given and graded through a portal called Coursemology, a "Gamified Online Education Platform". In fact, everything from lecture slides to announcements and administrative information are uploaded onto Coursemology. When you complete assignments, tutorials and (optional) practices, you are given EXP points. Earn EXP points to reach the next level, and your level at the end of your course determines your CA grade. The assignments are designed to be like games, there will be a background storyline (for your own reading pleasure) and a number of tasks to complete. The few assignments which I remember the most are Facebook stalking (count the number of likes, comments, etc. on a FB post), 2048 (to create a 2048 game which you can play on your computer) and Hungry Games (a variant of the Hunger Games). Tutorials are meant to test your understanding and should be done before the assignments (which test your knowledge application). Grading for tutorials were instant and gradings for assignments were usually by 24 hours, if not 2 days! Helpful comments were also provided by the graders, who are our seniors, when they grade our assignments.

Besides assignments and tutorials, there are also many optional practices for you to reinforce your knowledge. By many I do mean a lot, only 2 people in the entire class completed all the assignments, tutorials and practices! There are also achievements and levels for the more competitive ones to compare with your fellow course mates.

[Workload]

Practise, practise, practise. Consistency is the key to doing well for this module, you will find yourself falling behind once you miss a week or two of lessons. Help is always available, from the lecturer, TAs and fellow course mates, and there is a Forum on Coursemology for you to ask questions. Consultations (both online and offline) were also readily conducted for students who require them.

[Examinations]

One mid-terms, one practical test and one finals. The tests can be tricky and the lecturer tries to make the students learn something new during the exams. My cohort was small (49 in total), so there was no bell curve.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Hover
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

Took this iBLOC from Jan to Jun 2014 under Prof. Ben Leong, You can read more about iBLOC here: http://community.nus.edu.sg/ib...

This is essentially an introductory course on programming. Computing and engineering students have to take this since it’s a compulsory mod. NS guys who have ORDed or going to ORD between Jan to Jun can take this mod before matriculating in August.

If you’re an NSF/NSman reading this, YOU SHOULD TAKE CS1010FC INSTEAD OF CS1010 (which is equivalent but starts in semester 1).

WHY CS1010FC IS BETTER:

1) iBLOC is essentially a combination of e-learning and face-to-face classes. The first half of this course (Jan to mid Apr) has NO real life classes. All lessons are uploaded online as videos or conducted real-time on an online interactive platform. In the 2nd half you have real-life recitation classes where the prof reinforces what was taught in the lecture videos, and also tutorial classes where your helpful tutors would answer all your doubts.

2) If you’re completely new to programming, you get 5 WHOLE MONTHS to dedicate yourself fully to building a good foundation in programming. Since I was also new to programming at that time, I can tell you in the beginning you will STRUGGLE and spend HOURS of your weekends doing the assignments (called ‘missions’) because you will TRY and FAIL again and again until you get it. Having more time to learn and familiarize yourself with coding really helps especially for beginners. Also you are better off doing something productive in the few months, rather than just sitting around waiting for school to start.

3) If you take CS1010 in sem 1 instead, you have to learn and do the same things in a short period of only 3 months, along with 4 to 5 other mods plus your CCAs and hall activities. I am thankful that my sem 1 is free from CS1010 and you would, too X)

4) This mod teaches everything in Python, which in my opinion is a beginner-friendly language that most people can understand and pick up easily. It’s also A LOT more forgiving on syntax errors compared to other languages.

5) All assignments, quizzes, tutorials etc in this course can be 100% done and submitted online. For most practice questions and tutorials (MCQs and short coding questions), your answers are instantly marked and given feedback by the system. Missions are submitted online and graded within a few days by your tutors.

6) This mod uses a platform called coursemology (which is great) and also tries to integrate some RPG elements into its features which worked surprisingly well. Instead of simply receiving marks for your online assignments, you gain a certain amount of EXPs (depending on the difficulty) which let you level up and climb the leaderboard. By completing certain tasks you even get badges! Because of this most students were quite motivated to do as many tasks/assignments as possible.

7) Prof. Ben Leong is awesome and passionate about teaching. What I like most about him is the amount of effort he spent on designing our assignments and exams. Even though programming is tedious and frustrating in nature, the settings/backstories that he set for the tasks are interesting and lots of fun. My favourites are the ones involving Inception and X-men. XD 

ASSESSMENT:

35% Coursemology assignments (easy to score)
5% tutorial participation (easy to score)
15% Midterm (WRITTEN)
15% Practical exam (IN COMPUTER LAB)
30% Final Exam (WRITTEN)

IMO the exams are can be quite hard but reasonably so. The written ones are particularly challenging since you cannot test out your codes on a computer. Really tests your concepts and thinking. Cheatsheet is allowed but from my experience, you won’t need one if you already know your stuffs.

TL;DR – THIS MOD IS SPECIALLY CREATED FOR NSMEN WHO HAVE ORD-ED OR GOING TO ORD. IF YOU ARE ONE, TAKE THIS MOD.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Aparna
Semester Taken: AY14/15 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Daryl Seah and Michelle Teo

This is a brilliant course. I encourage everyone who has the chance to take it.

And I'm not just saying this. I am the last person anyone would expect to like programming (because I sucked majorly at it in school). But after this course, I can truthfully say I LOVE programming :D The prof is VERY good at imparting the concepts in your head - he explains every single thing, so those with no programming knowledge whatsoever can easily understand. And he is very nice and entertaining - you will never be bored in class. Plus, assignments are done on Coursemology, which is a really effective platform to make you do work :D Submitting assignments allows you to get EXP (points) and you can level up - as though you're playing a game! For me, doing the assignments alone was quite enough to make me learn things - I didn't even have to study much outside of class. Again, definitely would recommend people to take this - I have never had any other class in which studying was so easy and effortless. That being said, I wouldn't say that you shouldn't study at all. I think the most important thing is to focus, do the practices they give you, and ask questions when in doubt. In fact, posting questions on the Coursemology Forum will get you more EXP i.e. more CA marks. Which other course gives you marks for just asking questions?? Exactly.

Special shoutout to GIRLS: DON'T be afraid of programming. A lot of girls I know think programming is only for guys who game a lot or whatever but it's not. I too used to think that. But after this course I know it's not true. I assure you, it can be done by ANYONE. I did it and I'm a girl! So yeah :) Girl power woohoo!

Anyway, so the structure of the course is as follows:

1st half - online video lectures (posted on Coursera/IVLE) , along with assignments (that will be submitted on Coursemology) , + tutorials and online recitations on WebEx each week(Saturday or Monday)

After 1st half - Midterm Exam (15%)

2nd half - basically the same as first half, except recitations and tutorials are at COM2 at NUS.

After/During the end of 2nd half - Practical Exam (15%) and 2 weeks later Final Exam (30%)

The remaining 40% of your grade will be split into Coursemology (35%) and Class Participation (5%). Honestly, you should be able to get full marks for Coursemology as long as you do your homework and submit on time. As for class participation...I would say if you want to get 5% for sure, then do every single thing on Coursemology (it is not easy) and make sure you get the "OCD" badge.

As for exams, I would say, study consistently and think clearly. It's not that bad - the questions are not as twisted as you would expect. Although admittedly the final exam was tough. But the midterm was easy and the practical was doable. So make sure you score decently on those.

All in all, definitely take this course!! :D

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Poké Yoke Kai Jie
Semester Taken: AY14/15 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Daryl Seah and Michelle Teo

CS1010FC is strongly recommended for those interested to start learning programming and/or requiring to clear a CS1010 module.

Being targeted at returning full-time NSmen, this six-month-long course provides sufficient time and plenty of study materials for participants to pick up the Python programming language. Information and lesson slides are available via the online platforms, Coursemology and Coursera, where students can learn and go through the lessons at their own pace. The first half of the module would be conducted on these online platforms (as well as WebEx, a platform for online tutorial discussions), whereas the second part (April onwards) would have students attend the weekly face-to-face sessions (while the online Coursemology and Coursera platforms still remain).

The Coursemology platform creates a conducive and friendly study environment, where students are encouraged to explore and complete their training assignments and actual assignments (interestingly 'played' out as 'missions', with an overall, simple and lighthearted storyline to follow). These assignments would be auto-graded or graded by tutors, providing quality feedback to improve your learning experience. Students can also raise doubts and questions they have on the forums in Coursemology, where their peers, tutors or the professor would attend to it within a short period of time.

The professor of the module, Prof. Ben, makes learning Python very fun and interesting, and incorporates entertaining storylines or popular culture into his work and test questions, dispelling the dreariness commonly associated with school. He and his tutors also take good care of the students, accommodating any reasonable requests and makes the appropriate arrangements for his students.

The learning curve for this module is not very steep. Having come in with zero programming knowledge, I am glad I left this module being satisfied and entertained, picking up Python programming skills that I would have never thought I would pick up.
So if you need/are considering to take this module, think no further! CS1010FC is the way to go to learn programming! :D

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by TK
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

Programming is not as simple as it looks but this class taught by Prof Ben really gives you a good foundation in the basics of programming. A lot of time and effort has to be spent in practising it and the online portal Coursemology is a very efficient tool for learning and practising. It even includes a forum for fellow students to discuss questions and solutions.

The class is really interactive and is a very positive environment for learning. I admit that at times you will be lost in the lectures but you can easily contact the prof or even the tutors and they would gladly spend time with you just to ensure that you understand the topic.
