I would really recommend this module to all those seeking to learn programming in a really unique and efficient way. The classes are never boring and help is always readily available.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Shane Leung
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

Introduction:
CS1010FC (CS1010 Flipped Classroom) is part of NUS’ internal
Massive Online Open Course series, or iMOOC for short. It is intended to cover programming
methodology and is targeted at Returning National Servicemen (RNSmen) who have
served their compulsory two years of National Service. CS1010FC is intended to
cover all the basics to computing, by using Python; an easy to understand high
level language that is written using C. You will cover a wide variety of topics
and understand what it means to use a computer that to translate a “Human
Readable Essay” into reality, or as it is termed; functional abstraction to
create something that emulates reality.

Lecturer: Professor Ben Leong, School of Computing,
National University of Singapore.

Professor Ben Leong taught this module for AY2013/2014 iMOOC
Semester, and on an interesting note for those who are interested, he is a Masters
in Electrical Engineering and a PhD in Computer Science from the Massachusetts
Institute of Technology, and on a side note is the person in NUS who sends Computing
Science majors to Google. Aside from that he is also employed as the Director
of Experimental Systems and Technologies Laboratory as of July 2014.

Professor Ben Leong began this initiative of establishing
CS1010FC as a means to allow fresh incoming students exposure to actual
computing over a comfortable period of time intended to accommodate to a
variety of students, and actually does this in his free time because he
believes strongly in teaching and teaching to the masses; he is also continually
looking for ways to improve the iMOOC system on CS1010FC’s end, and will always
be friendly to students and help them enthusiastically.

On a personal note, Professor Ben is a very intriguing
individual. He has shown great enthusiasm as well as initiative in taking time
off his schedule to come and give consultation slots, along with his Teaching Assistants.
If you get him in your normal semesters, you are very blessed.

What content is covered, what are the delivery methods,
and how is it graded?

The content covered will be exactly as that of a normal
semester CS1010X (X can be any code) module; except it is held over January to June.
Content delivery is via a series of Online Lectures which you will have to
download (fret not about where to go – they will direct you) and attend online
tutorials, discussions, recitations, and complete missions. Missions being
assignments which you have to complete online. The content covered is short and
sweet, and viewable anytime due to the cyberspace medium, which is so because
the intended objective is understanding in a short time anywhere!

During my iMOOC, the iMOOC started using face-to-face (FTF) sessions to cover the best of both worlds: Online and FTF, to cater to a variety of students.

Furthermore towards the end of the course, CS1010FC will cover two
things not covered in CS1010X during normal semesters: Object Oriented
Programming, and Basic Introduction to C. For those in Engineering who have to
take CS1020E, CS1010FC covers literally (in Python language) CS1010E (C) and
CS1020E (C++). How cool is that?

For the grading portion we have a midterm exam, a practical
exam, online assignment portion and a final exam, all contributing to your
grade. The weightage may be subject to change, so I will not publish that
information here lest it conflicts with the NUS SoC’s arrangement.

How can I be prepared for this iMOOC CS1010FC?

The content covered is very thorough and you need not go to
take additional classes to try and be ahead. Even if you have had prior
experience in computing in Junior College, Polytechnic or external course, few
would usually come close to the content that is being covered, or the depth,
and so people are more or less on equal footing when they begin. Bluntly put –
computing at the university level is not easy to learn. So come with an open
mind and learn.

But if you want to be prepared, the only thing you can do is
purchase a computer and download Python. But even so, please wait until
Professor Ben announces which version of Python you have to do. Or for all you
know he may be using a very different language!

What if I am still serving NS during the period of iMOOC?

Don’t worry – the intention of iMOOC itself is to specifically
cater to RNSmen, especially those who are still serving. As long as you have
your interest, the teaching staff can accommodate, and their online sessions
are usually held such that you will be able to attend; be it at night or on
weekends when you are free. Furthermore, the staff are very helpful and will do
their best to answer any queries or assist you in any problem that comes up.
After all that is the purpose of iMOOC. (Or at least, rest assured Professor
Ben Leong will make them helpful; but generally they are very helpful.)

Recommendation is STRONG for Four reasons:

1 – If you clear this iMOOC early you need not take it in
the subsequent semester as an Advanced Placement Credit with Grade (APG), which
is 4 Modular Credits.

2 – If you are not planning to use it as an APG you can
still pick it up so you can learn a new language in the future.

3 – The content covered is very thorough and extensive: it
will definitely help you in shaping your perspective before entering NUS, and
also Professor Ben will cover on higher level concepts such as thinking about
Life. (No, I am serious, he will cover that as well. You’d be surprised at what
life should be like and where one is now.)

4 – Period of time is over 6 months. That allows people to
slowly get back into the habits they need.

How do I apply for this iMOOC?

Look out for a letter from NUS for incoming undergraduate
students to participate in iMOOC. If you do get it (all RNSmen should get it)
you can follow the instructions to enrol and request for the modules, including
CS1010FC as well. The letter should come a few months short of the calendar
year which you enrol in. (You are matriculating in year 2015, so the letter
should come in year 2014.)

What if I intend to drop it halfway, let’s say I lose my
interest as I do not want to continue it any further?

I cannot say if this will apply to all iMOOC years, but for
my experience iMOOC allows us to take the module until the halfway mark period
and then we have to start paying fees; this allows us to gauge whether we do
want to take it – a mercy compared to semester module dropping.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Khairul Rizqi
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

This module is a good way to take a step into programming world. While programming is not an easy thing to learn, never fear because there are abundance support that can help for those behind.

Quoting from the wise prof of the course: "no man gets left behind". He really meant it when he said that because there's support online through forums where friends help each other, web-ex where it is online consultation in real time with the prof and TAs and of course through email or facebook chat with your Profs, TAs and undergraduate tutors. There's also support offline where the prof and the TAs will gladly spend their time to coach you face-to-face ensuring you understand the concepts being taught and to help you progress in the module.

Lectures were short and sweet. They get to the point and with the prof's voice and style of lecture, you won't fall asleep.

The module itself is like a game which will definitely gets you to do the workload that is given. It may prove time-consuming for those who are slower. But it is definitely worth it because the concepts you learn are important fundamentals that will go on with you for the rest of your computing modules. And of course the choice of language use, 'Python", is a wise one because it is a relatively easier to learn which then helps to sparks your interest in programming as well as allowing you to have more time to learn the various important concepts being learn.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Shi Bohan
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

The module is very useful for those who has just ended their NS.
The professor is extremely helpful and caring to the students.

The workload given is just right however constant hardwork is needed to score well.

On the whole you will definitely learn a lot of programming knowledge, not just mere programming but fundamental concepts that will be essential in pursuing deeper understanding.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Ryan Lou
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

CS1010FC:

Lectures: The video lectures fulfil their purpose - and then some. Prof Ben delivers the concepts in a way that will engage you. The lectures allow you to learn and absorb the material at your own pace, at your own time. Prof Ben's questions for discussion sets you thinking about the concepts just mentioned as well as further expansions that you can find out on your own.

WebEx Sessions: The boon of not needing to come down to school during our time still in NS outweighs any disadvantages the online lessons may have. The sessions may be jittery sometimes, but there is nothing like learning in the comfort of your own home.

Coursemology: An awesome platform for hosting the assignments for a module like CS1010FC. The gamification concept built into Coursemology makes the learning experience much more fun and fulfilling as any MMO gamer will know when gaining a level. You can get near-instant feedback on your assignments from your tutors and the forum is the go-to place to have any question of yours answered in mere hours. The achievements for doing well in your assignments are just icing on the cake after that.

Overall, the module was conducted extremely well and while I came in with some programming background, I left with much, much more.

author: NUSMods
message:
CS1010FC Module review by Thng Kai Yuan
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Taught by: Prof Ben Leong, Brian Koh and Michelle Teo

I STRONGLY RECOMMEND this module to anyone who wishes to hone their thinking process in a structured and fun manner.

Contrary to what most people think, you do not have to have a background in programming or Computing to benefit and do well in this course. Personally, I had no formal computing background and was still able to do very well in this course thanks to the A-team of teaching staff! And even if you do not intend to study computing as your course of study, you will still learn very useful thought processes which can be applied in most situations. Who knows, you might even discover your passion for computing here and decide to change your course of study to Computing! (I did :P)

Prof Ben Leong, who is teaching this module, delivers lectures and assignments in a fun and engaging manner. The lectures are bite-sized video clips of 3-5 minutes which you can upload onto your mobile devices to learn anywhere, anytime, in whatever manner that will fit your hectic schedule. If you are a fast learner, you can even fast-forward through all the lectures on the Coursemology platform. The assignments are structured into very fun quests, missions and contests which you complete to earn you EXP points to level up (though I know, you will not believe how engaging and fun they are until you try them for yourself -- I was also skeptical at the start). Best of all, you get near-instant feedback on your assignments either from the auto-grading system or from the pool of dedicated tutors. This way, you can know how well you did and quickly learn from any mistakes committed.

Also, do not worry even if you are still in NS or will not ORD until late June. Prof Ben Leong will do his best to accommodate you and help you in whatever ways he can. Personally, I did not ORD until after the course ended and I still found the workload to be manageable.

Best of all, at the end of the course, you have a choice of dropping or keeping your grade for this course. Most people will choose to keep their grades because almost everyone will do well in this course with enough effort. To put it simply, why not join this course to hone your thinking process with the most awesome Prof and at the same time, earn a 'A' or 'A+' grade to pull up your CAP?

So, if you are waiting for a sign to join this course, wait no more! Just do it! You've got nothing to lose and all to gain! =)

author: NUSMods
message: Guys, please be kind with your words. Thank you.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Sarah Tan: http://sarriest.wordpress.com/2014/01/10/module-review-ay1314-sem-1/

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

Overview/Workload: Ethics mod, very light especially for a year 3.

Teaching style/lecturer: 3h sectional teaching group every week. However, aside from the first couple of weeks, most of the other sectionals are split into 2 groups of 1h 30min, and you only need to go for yours. The sectionals are seminar style, where learning takes place through discussion. The 2 profs, Irene Woon and Anand Ramchand, were both really really nice! Just that sometimes prof Anand forgets to upload stuff (not that I download them anyway OOPS).

Notes/textbook: 1 page handouts are given out during most of the sectionals. However, they are pretty bare, and almost all the content comes from the class discussion. I didn’t really take a lot of notes though, since most of it was common sense/application. The textbook used was Ethics For the Information Age by Michael J. Quinn. I strongly recommend not buying the book (or at least get 2nd hand); just download the softcopy. It’s good to read I guess, though I gave up after getting to the part on the different ethical theories lol.

Grading:

Final exam (open book) – 40%

2h (too much time!), consists of MCQ and open ended questions. I found the final exam really easy, and some of the MCQ questions/options were hilarious! “What is the Great Firewall of China? a) A tourist attraction” LOL. Also, even though it’s open book, I didn’t refer to any of my notes. For the open ended application questions there’s no right or wrong answer as long as you can argue using any ethical theory. I only used Kantianism, Act and Rule Utilitarianism for the entire paper.

Group assignment – 30%

Form a group of 4-5 and write a report based on a particular theme (my group did cyber warfare). Each person does a ~5 page case study on an incident which falls into your chosen theme (I did Stuxnet), and as a group analyse the ethical dilemas/similarities and differences of each case. My group did mostly individual work though, only the intro was discussed as a group.

Class discussion – 10%

This is important! As mentioned previously, this is where the bulk of the learning takes place. It’s also free marks cause all you need to do is 1. give your opinion 2. discuss as a group and *try* to come to a consensus. I found this the most fun part of the module haha, in part due to how much my group contributed to the chaos vibrancy of the class :P

Individual writing – 10%

There were 2 identical in class writing assignments, 1 given at the start of the sem when most people (except those who took KI/philo before) knew nuts about ethical theories, and 1 at the end of the sem to see how our ethical reasoning changed. We were given about 15min for each of them, so most of the freshies wrote 1 entire page (CRAZY), while I spent 5min writing 1 short para lol.

Individual Presentation – 10%

This was fun! Haha we had to do it Ignite style (5min with 20 slides, each slide auto advances after 15s), with a maximum of 7 words per slide. Each person gets a different topic and I was the first to present in the entire class, on the second week before the sectional was split into 2 groups. The profs were nice enough to ask us (the seniors) if we wanted to go first (and set a high bar :P) before our other mods got heavy.

My verdict: easy, fun, and pretty slack. I really enjoyed this mod! Sorry to the year 1 who grouped with us though, he did quite a lot of stuff cause us seniors were headache-ing over other crazier mods >< Also, bell curve for this mod is very steep. My friend forgot to submit a 5% assignment and his marks dropped by 3 grades (he verified this with the prof)!

My grade: A+ yay!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/04/nus-module-review-semester-2-ay-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2
By Dr Ben Blumson

What is it about?

Reasoning with logic. That's really the most apt description I can give you. Well, you learn how to tell if an argument form is valid via truth tables, trees, etc.

What is the workload?

1 mini textbook which is like damn tiny and little.
Fortnightly online quizzes - all MCQ. Not as easy as it sounds, but you can discuss with friends la.
Mid term exam + final exam.
1 weekly 2h lecture and 1 fortnightly 2h tutorial.
Lectures and relatively important though I find he can be done usually in 1h+ and he talks alot of crap as fillers. Tutorials are very important because you'll truly learn and understand the techniques and you can ask questions if you don't know.

How difficult is it?

The key is to understand the steps and the, erm, logic behind everything you're done. You will learn a few proofs here and there which you must try to internalize so that you can apply in the same way for the exams.
There is relatively little to read and it's more chim than it is heavy.  Once you get it, you should be all good.

Miscallenous Tips

Do the practice questions in the textbook! They're probably the only way you can practice trees and predicate logic and etcetc.
Try your best to understand and take down whatever he teaches during tutorials, they're always important and useful. You really need to UNDERSTAND the reasoning behind certain stuff, because the exam tests your ability to apply the deductive reasoning process.

Should you take it?

If you have a need for cognition and want something relatively light in terms of workload/readings, this is for you. It seems to be a popular GEM too, so be prepared to pay abit more for it if you're bidding as a GEM.How did I do for it?
Mid terms was decent, I prepared well for it and got a decent score (I think). For the online quizzes.. they're difficult as hell lol. Even with discussion with friends, I got 1/10 for one and 3/10 for another. So.. all the best for the quizzes :p
Finals was ok.. didn't seem THAT difficult. But I think my bad quizzes will pull me down.Predicted Grade: B+

Grabe obtained: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2012/05/nus-module-review-semester-2-ay-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By Dr Eddie Tong

What's it about?

Many factors/issues related to Emotions, with regards to the social-cognitive component. E.g. The role of appraisals in emotions, emotional expression, emotion and cognition, etc.

How's the workload?

One class presentation (2-3 man group), class participation, one mid term exam (1 essay qn), one finals (mcq + short answer)

Textbook is quite useless, talks about many things that seem totally irrelevant. Study his lecture notes well, and use the textbook as a supplement.

One 2 hr lecture every week, one 1 hour tutorial every week.

How difficult is it?

Content is relatively easy to understand, the class presentation/project may require some creativity (you're supposed to come up with a novel research question/hypothesis).

Finals were relatively easy MCQs, with some application kind open ended questions. The MCQs dont require much memorizing of stuff, just good understanding of concepts

Any miscellaneous tips?

Go for lectures - they are probably the crux of this module. I didn't find the textbook very useful (if even at all) for the finals, he tested almost exclusively from his notes.

One thing to note about the finals is that it's very application-based : You need to have a good understanding of all the concepts he covers in lectures and be able to recognize and apply the various concepts in different scenarios. Thus, study his lecture notes + any required journal article readings, those may be helpful. Read the textbook sparingly, if you have the time, but for things not covered in his lecture notes and is in the textbook, you probably don't have to touch.

Should you take it?

Eddie Tong is a very nice professor and is humorous as well. His lectures should be quite enjoyable, though he does make frequent pronunciation errors which may make grammar nazis cringe.

I like his style as he doesn't emphasize rote learning but encourages us to understand the material instead of memorizing it - for example he cites many studies to show certain points in his lectures but his description of the study is always very brief and succinct - he doesn't require you to remember the name of scientists, exact methods, etc, but just the variables and the results and what it shows - the gist of it.

One of the more enjoyable Psych modules out there, don't miss out on it :)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2013/05/nus-ay-1213-semester-2-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
By Prof Dr Michelle See

What is it about?

Many aspects of social psychology - social cognition, persuasion, aggression, etc.

How's the workload?

One lecture a week ( 3hours). Two thought papers (800 word essays), one group presentation, one open book final exam (two questions, first question compulsory, second question 2C1). Oh, there are class participation points.

How difficult is it?

Social psychology is probably the easiest branch of psychology in terms of understanding the concepts. Thus you shouldn't have too much difficulty in this. Also, Prof See picks pretty short and simple papers most of the time, so that's good :)

Miscellaneous Tips

Speak up in class. She is quite blatant about her marking of class part, literally puts a tick next to your name when you speak up

Should you take it?

The lessons feel really slack - I'm not sure how much Prof See actually prepares for class, because it feels like she's winging it most of the time. She isn't that bad at explaining things, but sometimes she can be very confusing.
Most lessons are mostly occupied by group presentations, so she does very little 'teaching'. You get to watch one movie (12 angry man) and documentary (bowling in columbine) during class. Pretty good shows.

If you are looking for maximum learning experience, you should probably take it in Sem 1 when Dr Tong takes it. If you want a slack Level 4000, this could be it.

Predicted Grade: B+/A-
We seemed to do well for our group presentation, and my thought papers have to be one of the better ones unless she has some retarded marking scheme (All thought papers are uploaded so you can have a look at others' work) (sorry if this sounds damn arrogant or what, but imho, it's true. I acknowledge when I'm bad, and I also acknowledge when I'm better)
Final exam was unexpected. Based on past papers (not by See, by Tong), the questions were very broad and requires good organization and integration of information from the entire semester. However, her questions were extremely specific. For example, the compulsory question focused on one particular week's readings and just asked you for strengths/limitations of both studies.
Not sure how well I did, I didn't felt like I spent enough time on my second question, so abit concerned about that. Oh well.

Grade obtained: A-
thread_title: PL4207 Social Psychology: Theories and Methods

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2013/05/nus-ay-1213-semester-2-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
By Prof Sivasothi and Prof Hwang Wei Song (each takes roughly half, siva does more)

What is it about?

Many aspects of animal behavior, such as foraging, mating, territory, living in groups, human-animal conflict. Very interesting stuff even for people who aren't super passionate animal lovers (e.g. me)

How's the workload?

One lecture a week, and a fortnightly 'lab'. The schedule for lab is something like every alternate week for first 3 weeks, then 2 more times according to the symposium schedule.
One group project where you have to go observe any animal and present for 10 minutes. Write an animal blog (Full list of blogs here. My blog post here.). One final exam (closed book), everything from lecture notes.

How difficult is it?

Nothing is difficult to understand, since it's just animal behavior! It's kind of like primary school biology. The lecturers are pretty good too, especially the main man Siva.. he's funny and talks in a casual, relaxed tone that makes you just want to listen. He has funny anecdotes to share during class and he makes lectures interesting. Wei Song is pretty clear in his lectures too, just not as funny.

Miscellaneous Tips

Go for lectures. This was the only lecture this semester which I faithfully went for, both because it was important and because they were interesting.

For your group project, try to be as detailed as possible and consult your TAs/prof as much as you can to clarify things early.

Should you take it?

One of the most interesting modules I've taken in NUS, I highly recommend it because it is so interesting and funny thanks to Siva. You learn something interesting every week, such as... do you know that a certain male spider mates with a female, then offers his belly to her so she eats him up, while he ejaculates into her a second time?

Predicted Grade: B-
Once again have no idea how I did, assessment grades were never revealed.  Thought we bombed our group presentation though, hence the poor grade.
Thought I wrote fairly well for the open ended portions, but I already got a few mistakes for the MCQ. I think my predicted grade still stands.

Grade obtained: B+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2013/05/nus-ay-1213-semester-2-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
By Prof Wong Yean Ling

What is it about?

You learn about several basic areas of statistics/research methods like experimental vs observational studies, confounding variables... hypothesis testing, normal distribution.. et and how the media can misrepresent information. Not much calculation involved (actually for the exams she said no calculations involved.. not sure how true that is).

How's the workload?

Two lectures a week, with no tutorials. Two group essays (which are due on the same date) (which is basically apply some concepts to any newspaper article), one closed book final exam. There is a textbook but it's unnecessary because everything is in the notes. The notes are just the slightly summarized form of the textbook.

How difficult is it?

If you have any basic research methods knowledge (e.g. from PL2131, 2132, or other stats/research methods knowledge), most of the stuff should be familiar. There are some new things here and there, and a lot of it is quite logical so it's not THAT difficult to understand. 

Miscellaneous Tips

Ermmmm. I didn't go for lectures because I knew a lot of the concepts and felt it was a waste of time. The lecturer isn't that bad but isn't that good either. If you have no experience with research methods then do go for lectures I suppose.

Should you take it?

Fairly light in workload, everything you need is in the lecture notes. The essay assignment isn't that difficult either. Then again, I haven't got any grades so I have no idea.

Predicted Grade: B
I'm being quite pessimistic but it's mainly because I have no idea. I don't know how I did for my assignments, don't know what the exams will be like. So. Maybe I'll update this after my exams.
Updated: Will keep the predicted grade. Bell curve is steep as everyone got A for group project. So B or B+ perhaps.

Grade obtained: B+
thread_title: GEM2901 Reporting Statistics in the Media

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2013/05/nus-ay-1213-semester-2-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
By Prof Belal E Baaquie

What is it about?

You learn about Special Relativity, Particle Physics, Quantum Mechanics, and Cosmology and Superstring theory. Those are the broad topics.

How's the workload?

Two lectures a week, but they may  be webcasted. He only started the webcasts halfway through the sem. One fortnightly 1hr tutorial, which comes along with a compulsory assignment which you have to complete before tutorial. Your CA consists of tutorial attendance, assignment submission (and I guess accuracy), and 3 tests (20, 20, 40%) which are all open book.

Just study from lecture notes and tutorials. Pretty light in terms of absolute content, but some topics may be quite difficult to understand.

How difficult is it?

The topics are quite foreign to most people, and the prof is pretty darn terrible at explaining anything and his lecture notes seem quite random. I only did well in the first test because much of the content was also covered in a previous mod I have taken, Einstein's Universe and Quantum Weirdness (PC1323).

I found tutorials a lot more helpful than lectures, as the tutor is actually better at making things clear than the boring lecturer. The lecturer drones and drones and has some issues with any whispering or chattering in class and will annoyingly stop to ask people to keep quiet. Content is not easy to understand, so you either need to have a physics brain or hope you understand his lectures.

Miscellaneous Tips

Go for tutorials for sure - you learn much more. Make a cheat sheet so it's easier to refer during your tests or know where things are. Some answers can be directly lifted from the notes.

Should you take it?

No. If you are interested in the topics I mentioned above, go take Einstein's Universe, the lecturer is 10219083 times better (both in explaining concepts and in humor). This one is just boring. Plain boring. And the questions are much harder, more calculations involved. I see I warded off half of you with the word 'calculations'. Good.

Grade obtained: B
thread_title: PC1323 Great Ideas in Contemporary Physics

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2013/12/nus-ay-1314-semester-1-module-review.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1
By Dr Marlene Lee

What is it about?
You will learn to answer the question ' what is an addiction?' (not as simple as you think), the two main groups of addictions - substance and behavioral addictions, their etiology, treatment, prevention, and some ongoing controversies and issues in the addictions field. 

How’s the workload?
One group paper (about 12 pages), one group presentation based on your paper (20-30 minutes), finals (open book; 2 essays, 1st qn compulsory, 2nd qn is 2C1)
Readings are about 2-4 each week, about standard Honors level reading workload

How difficult is it?
The group paper is entirely up to your group to decide the topic (about addictions of course, and after prof's approval), and you need to be creative in presentation delivery (not standard PPT slides). The final exam questions are quite manageable if you prepared well for her 'style' of questions. 

I wasn't really enjoying the content much as I'm not a clinical psych kind of person, but as I was preparing for the exam I realized what we were required to answer wasn't really what the readings were saying, and some of her lectures were useless for finals, e.g. one entire lecture where she talked about the properties of many drugs, their effects, etc.  And since it's an open book exam, there's no need to memorize anything much. :)

Any miscellaneous tips/How was the exam?
Be creative, and keep within the time limit for your presentation. Do note that she does follow the 5 criteria she set out in IVLE (e.g. Creativity, Time, etc) when giving you your marks.
My group made a video for this:

Part 1 - http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVczsz5ki3M
Part 2 - http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VQ02nt4IsB8
Music Video - http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZM2g8cW4aU8&feature=youtu.be
(I'd recommend only watching Part 1 and Music Video, as Part 2 is abit technical)

For the exam, the compulsory question asks something along the lines of the different conceptualizations of an addiction, and asks you to explain the controversies and existing issues and the implications. (Same for two semesters that I've seen the questions)
The 2C1 questions can include: Can ABC be an addiction? ; Something about how to help individuals to change (MI, Transtheoretical model stuff) ; Some application kind question, like Imagine you are planning a nationwide prevention programme for internet addiction, what would you have in place, what would you consider.

Should you take it?
If you plan on doing clinical next time, I guess this is a natural module to want to take, since you learn about the current status of the field. I'm kind of ambivalent about it - I felt lectures were generally boring as I had little interest, and the prof has a droning voice, but I did quite well for the CA components. 

Grade obtained: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2014-09-05T19:00:00-07:00&max-results=3&reverse-paginate=true

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
By Dr  Jia Lile

What is it about?
You will learn about how our unconscious may influence our preferences, perceptions, decision making, etc. 

How’s the workload?
About 1-2 readings a week (quite light for a level 4000), which you don't really need to read because Dr Jia goes through the readings as part of his lecture. Two tests, one final 15 page writeup on a research idea (intro + method + predicted results + discussion). Class part worth 10% which can be obtained by participating in forum or speaking in class.

Something I found strange was that the second test was on Week 9 - this means everything after Week 9 was not tested. You can think about what that means heh.

How difficult is it?
I may be biased because I've always liked social psychology, but I found the subject matter fairly straightforward and easy to understand. Dr Jia also structures his lectures well and explains concepts quite thoroughly and slowly with multiple studies, so it's not too difficult.

Any miscellaneous tips/How was the exam?
If you can understand the lectures, there really is no need to read the readings. I didn't read any readings past week 2 when I realized he covered everything in the readings in lecture. Just understand the main points of each study presented (Dr Jia summarizes them at the end of each lecture) and be able to think critically about them, you shouldn't have much problems with the tests

Should you take it?
Yes. Dr Jia is a great person who is very friendly and nice, replies emails very quickly too. He structures his lectures simply and clearly, and the assessment is rather forgiving ... except for the final paper perhaps, which some may struggle with (you are supposed to come up with an original research idea for the writeup. It's kinda like doing a thesis but without actually running any experiments haha)

Grade obtained: A
thread_title: PL4880I Social Psychology of the Unconscious

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2014-09-05T19:00:00-07:00&max-results=3&reverse-paginate=true

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
By Prof Nina Powell

What is it about?
You learn about particular theories about what drives moral judgments. Is it intuition or reasoning? Do we judge whether something is good or bad based on a gut feeling or based on logical reasoning? 

How’s the workload?
One 3-h seminar style lecture a week.15% class participation, 30% for 3 essays, 15% for 2 'moral fieldwork' assignments, and 40% finals.
There are also about 120398 readings each week. Ok fine, I exaggerate. But there are way too many to be motivated to read them at all, so I began just reading abstracts after the first few weeks. It's about 5-7 each week.
UPDATE: I stopped reading readings after Week 4. 
Essays are short 500 word essays.

Fieldwork was really weird, just record instances of moral vice/virtue, and another one was present your friend with a moral situation and see how they react. She didn't define the assignment till like Week 11. May change again for future sems so no point elaborating.

How difficult is it?
It's probably easier if you are good at expressing your opinions on reasoning processes and articulating why you think something is good/bad, to do well in class participation. The prof doesn't strictly track the class part because she can't remember everyone's names so not sure how exactly she grades this. 
Update: Ok, apparently you just have to talk regularly so that at least she notices you. Then for class part, she gave out a piece of paper and told us to write the grade we thought we deserved. Whatever we wrote would be the grade we got - and if she finds out someone who didn't talk much and gave themselves a high grade she would give a 0 (I think no one got this).
This just demonstrates how lazy she was to even remember who said what. I bet she didn't even check it and just followed it. She can't pronounce Chinese names so gave up learning names after the first class.

The content is fairly understandable, but it's not as philosophical as you may think. Rather, it looks at WHY and HOW people make certain moral judgments, not which moral judgment is more accurate (as philosophy may do it).

The essays are weird, I've no idea what she wants even after completing two essays and receiving feedback. Her feedback makes no sense sometimes and seems slipshod. Don't bother staying back for the 'feedback sessions she gives after she returns your essays, she gives generic writing comments which don't help at all as it's not specific to what you wrote.

Miscellaneous Tips / Exam
The best tip I can give you is to not take this module.

Should you take it?
No. Unless a better professor takes it. Nina seems unprepared, unmotivated, and is not a good educator. She has very low EQ. For instance, there was one class where she said something like : " All of you wrote bad essays. They were ALL terrible, with bad writing, shallow arguments, and lacked depth, and were all terrible". 

Way to motivate students! Woohoo!

She doesn't do much besides read off her slides, and when even so her slides aren't structured well such that sometimes she describes an entire experiment which only had a one line description in the slide, and you are frantically scribbling down the main points of the study.

She doesn't seem to prepare well for each lecture, sometimes she is reading off her slide and hits a point which she didn't know how to explain, and, I quote her: "ok nevermind skip that." ???
She did this multiple times. in one lecture.

She doesn't upload slides on time half the time, so often I was left with no lecture slides for lecture and had to copy notes on a blank piece of paper. This is after me personally emailing her to remind her to upload before class (because she promised she would, just that she sometimes forgot).

As mentioned, her comments for our essays made little sense, and the advice she gave was generic and not tailored to our individual essays. It feels like she has a list of 'Random bad feedback to give essays', and she rolls a dice to pick a random one and inserts it into our essays. Sometimes her comments directly contradict each other (e.g. Your intro was well written and concise. -one sentence later - need to elaborate more in your intro)

Her 'writing tips' were just strange writing stylistic matters she insisted we follow. Some examples:
- Don't start a sentence with 'However,', no matter what. 'It undermines your argument'. (?? That IS the point gosh.)
- Don't use flowery language, such as 'upon' (I swear, she said this). Use 'on' instead.
- Each paragraph must have at least 3 sentences

I haven't even begun to talk about how unstructured her entire module felt, how poorly defined the assignments were, how sloppily the syllabus was dealt with. These points can be mitigated by the fact that it's her first time I guess. But it's her poor attitude and inability to teach and weird writing idiosyncracies that were unpardonable and I had to list above.

All in all a bad experience and I was extremely unmotivated to even try to do well in this module. Worst psych module ever.

Grade obtained: B

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
By Dr. John van Wyhe

Very interesting module on the history of Darwin's theory of evolution. The lecturer is the world's leading expert on the history of Darwin's theories and related theories. There are many misconception about Darwin's theory which is explored in this module. After taking this module and understanding more about Darwinism, you might begin to wonder why the debate between evolution and creationism exist in the first place.

Workload is relatively light. I only turned up for the first lecture, mid terms and finals. Lectures are webcasted. Notes are pictures and most of the content that the tests are based on is lectured by the prof. There is quite a bit of copying to do, but you can do it in your free time and in comfort over webcast.

Mid terms and finals are 50 MCQs, 50% each. Midterms are open book, so the scores are quite close. Finals are close book. Finals was relatively easy, but I did not expect him to have questions asking dates and time periods. The prof took the high ground of history is not about testing dates and I believed him. It is a trap. The prof probably did it to differentiate the scores a bit more and sadly I fell victim.

There are many dates in the notes, the focus was on the post darwinian theories such as genetics, the modern synthesis etc. One lecture is dedicated to these theories so take note of when the theories gain prominence.

Overall an enjoyable module and good general knowledge. I was contemplating whether to s/u this module, but ultimately decided against it.

For those who are interested in taking this module, here is a short video of Dr. John talking about evolution.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=18YwBwIK_no

Grade obtained: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Jonathan Low / Xapheron: http://elitistjerk.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2013-06-30T11:16:00%2B08:00&max-results=3&start=9&by-date=false

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2

The best SS module in the whole of NUS. Take this if its the last thing you do. Open book MCQ midterms and finals, midterm paper assignment; slight load for the paper, practically zilch the rest of the term. That's all, 2 2hour lectures. I skipped about 30% of lectures. It was fairly interesting and opened my eyes to the biodiversity and environmental stuff about Singapore. The MCQ exams were pretty hard (or rather I severely underestimated MCQ exams) but they were open book so it was all good. Midterm paper was pretty easy, straightforward GP style essay.

A very easy to pass module but hard to score well given the huge number of Science faculty students. Steep bidding points as well (Cost 675 G this semester). It really really sucked to S/U this module but I did it anyway to ensure I got Dean's List this semester (which I did, surprise surprise). This module is recommended for people who love animals, the environment and those who don't want to do essay exams. This SS module is the only one which does not have essay exams so take note of that.

Grade obtained: B+ (S/U-ed)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Jonathan Low / Xapheron: http://elitistjerk.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2014-07-12T22:18:00%2B08:00&max-results=3

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2

Open machine (no joke, no internet though) mid-terms, cheatsheet finals (lol). Pretty funny module, never went for lectures except for mid-terms and went for 1 tutorial by accident as I thought mid-terms were to be given out in that session. No attendance, yeeeeeeeeeeah. Didn't really do that well for mid-terms but my finals pulled me through (finals is an f-ing 80%!).

This module is not to be underestimated though. Even though the preclusions are quite in the favour of those who are good in math and are able to take this module, it isn't that easy. Trust me. I went into this thinking FREE A but no, my grade needed effort. It's fairly interesting if you like probability and applications like game theory; practically zilch on statistics which is why I got this score instead of the score for the module below. Learning curve can get steep sometime in the middle or so and then smooth out again thereafter. My experience will be particularly skewed for this GEM because I like probability and game theory.

I watched webcasts to fill in notes for the open machine/paper mid-terms and to get a feel of the professor. Be warned though that he often goes on parabolic explanations about extremely unrelated stuff. Practice the mid-terms, finals  (all downloadable, no answers though).

Grade obtained: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2013-12-04T07:28:00-08:00&max-results=7&start=3&by-date=false

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
By Dr Chua Tin Chiu

This module is more qualitative than quantitative. Dr Chua Tin Chiu who is also our Vice Dean, spent most of the time sharing with us his experience as a statistics/survey consultant. Although his stories are very interesting, it gets tiring after awhile. The technical aspect are often lacking in his explanation and notations are confusing as 2 textbooks with different notations are used. Not a very interesting module, but workload is light and it is one of the few modules which fulfill the Business and Finance specialization requirement.

Mid-Term mean-median: 17.5-17 (/25)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung:
http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2014-06-11T08:21:00-07:00&max-results=7

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2

Extremely popular module as it is one of the few SS mod that have an open book MCQ midterm and finals. Bell curve is extremely steep with half the cohort scoring above 25 out of 30 for the midterm. If you want to do well in this module, you need to be extremely familiar with the textbook, otherwise, you might find yourself flipping through the textbook in frustration wondering where the hell do I find those answers. There are a few guest lectures which, in my opinion, is totally useless for the exams, so there is no harm skipping them. The online discussions/tutorials and field trips are also optional.

Midterm mean-median: 24.26-25(/30)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung:
http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2014-06-11T08:21:00-07:00&max-results=7

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
By Prof Xia Yingcun

Data-mining, Big Data, Data analytics are the buzz-word in recent years. Everybody wants a slice of it, even in NUS. The CS and stats are already offering it, the biz has recently started offering it, and last I heard, even the math department want to join the party. Although Big Data Analytics is raging hot in the United States. The market is still at its infancy stage in Asia, but nonetheless, it is steadily picking up its pace (I just read on newspaper today that LTA is tabbing on the power of big data to improve our transport system) and I believe there is huge potential in this field. The demand for data scientist (NOT data analyst) will be strong, but unfortunately, most data scientist roles need at least a master or PhD.

The content is rather heavy for this module as Prof Xia Yingcun tried to cramp in a lot of data-mining analytics techniques into the module and the math behind these techniques are also not easy to understand. However, I think the module placed too much emphasis on the theory part and lack hands-on component for us to practice analyzing data using these data-mining techniques. Workload is light with only midterm and finals. Both exams are manageable.

Midterm mean: ~80 /100

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/search?updated-max=2014-06-11T08:21:00-07:00&max-results=7

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
By Vik

Simulation is a new module taught by a new lecturer. Vik is extremely dedicated and you can easily tell the large amount of effort he puts in to prepare his lectures and tutorials. Clear explanation of concepts and very well defined learning outcome. The only thing I can complain about is that he spent a little bit too much time dwelling on the basics during the start of the semester.

Content includes generating random variables from various distributions, Monte Carlo methods, and discrete event simulation such as queuing models. While the module may requires a lot of R, especially for discrete event simulation, they will not be tested in the midterms or finals. Emphasis was placed on understanding the algorithms, and perhaps a little bit of memorization was needed. This module will also lay foundation for ST4231.

The workload of this module is not heavy. 5 graded assignments, midterm and finals. The midterm is easy, with questions taken directly from tutorials, assignments and sample paper. Finals is slightly more challenging but manageable.

 Midterm mean-median: 76.3-82.5(/100)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By A/P M.S. Uddin

CN3124 is a core module taught by A/P M.S. Uddin. Prof Uddin's lecture delivery style is a bit languid and mundane so grab that cuppa to stay awake. Seniors told me that the course was easy to score in - truth be told, i did well. However, the content covered was far from easy. I found this to be the most conceptually-challenging course in Sem 2, 2011/12. Of all the 7 modules, i took the longest time to grapple with the concepts covered by CN3124.

There is 1 mid-term, 1 group project and 1 final exam. The mid-term was easy but the marking terribly inconsistent. The same mistake took 12 marks from me while causing my friend to lose just 2. Of course, a trip to Prof Uddin's office rectified the mistake but my situation is the norm rather than the rule. Just ask any Chem E 3rd years if they recall the epic queue in LT6 to change scores. The project is more interesting and requires a fair bit of coding. Groups of 5 worked on the same problem statement albeit with different parameter values. It was fun :)

There are 2 text-books: Coulson and Richardon's Particle Technology and Separation Processes and Martin Rhode's Introduction to Particle Technology. As the quiz and examination are both closed-book based, i didn't purchase the texts. Moreover, the notes had enough mind-boggling concepts to keep you occupied. I had no time to flip through any of the books.

Topics covered:
1) Particle Charaterisation
2) Motion of Single Particle in a Fluid
3) Flow through Packed Bed
4) FIltration
5) Sedimentation
6) Centrifugal Filtration
7) Cyclone Separator
8) Centrifugal Sedimentation
9) Particle Size Reduction
10) Mixing of Solids

Prof Uddin has taught this course for many years and his passion for it seemed to have waned quite a fair bit. He no longer explains concepts in vivid detail, leaving too much to our own imagination. Much effort is needed for this course.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By Dr Jerome Whittington

The course is taken on an S/U basis and open only to seniors from Tembusu College, NUS. It's basically a multifaceted stab at climate change (with emphasis on anthropology). Course was taught by Dr Jerome Whittington.

There are weekly readings (30 pages on average) that span policy, sociology, climate science, etc. We even had a closed-door session with Prof Tommy Koh, Singapore's Ambassador-at-Large where he shared his experiences in international diplomacy (specifically his charing of the Earth Summit in Rio - 1992).

There are ups and downs to this course. At times the readings were way too technical and we just drift off the discussion. Dr Jerome could also have done better at moderating the views - sometimes we were pulled from climate change to Kant to Pandas to ???. However, the S/U basis of the course led many of us to scale our efforts accordingly so it all balanced out in the end.

There are 4 assignments:
1) What is GEM2902?
2) Describing Climate Advocacy Groups
3) Evaluating Climate Science Claims
4) Carbon Pricing and its Critics

author: NUSMods
message:
Module Review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By A/P Hsu Wen Tai

EC3381 is taught by A/P Hsu Wen Tai. It was his teaching course in NUS as he is a visiting fellow. He specialises in urban economics so i found his classes enjoyable as they imparted cutting edge economic thinking (for urban spaces, sprawl, etc.).

There is 1 mid-term and 1 final examination. The papers are very very very easy. This is especially the case for an engineering student who will find the differential and integral calculas very simplistic yet utterly confounds their pure FASS counterparts.

There are 1 text-book: Lectures on Urban Economics by Jan K. Brueckner. Content is easy to follow but you have to get the book since the tutorials are taken from the book.

Topics covered:
1) Why there are Cities
2) Internal Structure of Cities
3) Housing
4) Local Public Goods
5) Traffic Congestion

Very enjoyable course content and an approachable lecturer though his delivery can be better at times. I scored and A+.

Grade Obtained: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module Review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/06/flirting-with-academic-suicidenot.html

Taken in Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2
By Dr Xue

Another compulsory module for Chemical Engineers. The module is taught by Dr Xue and assisted by tutors from many faculties. The tutors can range from junior TA to Associate Professors. MLE is, unfortunately, as much about memory work as it is about application. The chapters can be really boring at times but an adequate understanding of the processes should see you do fairly well. There is a compulsory textbook (Smith's Foundation of Materials Science and Engineering) which i didn't purchase. This book is rather necessary so i relied on weekly visits to the library.

Exam Format:
The mid-term examination was designed to make sure no one failed this module. There were 12 MCQ questions that took me 15 minutes to complete. I ended up spending the last 45 minutes revising for my economics paper with the question script. This segment makes up 20% of the overall grade.
The final examination, which makes up 80%, is entirely open-ended. The questions are really varied and some are rather tough.

Comments:
Dr Xue is one of the most committed and responsible lecturers so far. He answers every query and replies every email. His lecture delivery may be boring and laced with intricate details because of the subject matter so beware. I guarantee that if you pay full attention in his class, you will do fine. 
My tutor was Dr Mansoor.
thread_title: MLE1101 Introductory Materials Science And Engineering

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/06/semester-in-review-
semester-2-year-1.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

CM1502 is a core module for all Chemical Engineers in NUS. You will typically be allocated this module in semester 2 of the first academic year. There are no pre-requisites for this module other than A-level chemistry, which all of you would have satisfied to qualify for this degree anyway. As the module title suggests, CM1502 is all about physical chemistry. You will be brought through the entire spectrum from acid/bases to electrochemistry to nuclear magnetic resonance. About 70% of the content have been covered in your pre-university education, assuming of course, that you remember them. The remaining 30% can be pretty tricky. The module is also split up into 2 portions, each covered by a different lecturer. This is also your first brush with lab sessions.

Part A - Dr Emelyn Tan
1) Gas laws 
2) First law of thermodynamics 
3) Second law of thermodynamics
4) Gibb’s free energy
5) Chemical equilibra
6) Acid-base equilibra
7) Electrochemistry
8) Spectroscopy

Part B - Dr Prabha
9) Chemical kinetics (2 lectures on this)
10) Quantum mechanics (4 lectures on this)
11) Theories of covalent bonding (2 lectures on this)

Exam format
15% Mid-term 1 (Dr Emelyn’s parts)
15% Mid-term 2 (Dr Prabha’s parts)
20% Labs (6.67% for each of the 3 lab sessions)50% Finals

Teachers
Good:
1) Dr Emelyn and Dr Prabha are both tutors for this module as well so there is a high chance you could get your queries answered fairly efficiently. 
2) Dr Emelyn replies emails extremely quickly.
3) Dr Leonard Perreira, who is in charge of your lab sessions, is very nice so you can approach him if you find your lab scripts unprofessionally graded.

Bad:
1) Dr Prabha does not really entertain emails.
2) Depending on your luck, you could get a lazy lab tutor that just ticks your script and randomly assigns you a grade. 
3) Both lecturers are new so they might confuse you on some concepts. Dr Prabha messed up VSEPR a bit and Dr Emelyn lost me in spectroscopy. However, further clarifications with them solved the problem.

Workload
Weekly tutorials are pretty manageable but the lab sessions add a new dimension to the entire module. With 11 topics to cover in 13 weeks, lectures are extremely rushed. There are no webcasts so my advice is to not dream during class. Overall, workload is bordering on heavy so this is potentially one of the modules in which more time has to be invested. 

Tips
Labs are extremely time consuming so it is best to free up the weekend after each lab session. The lab reports (which typically span 6 pages to 16 pages) are due the following week. It is important to do some reading up on the topics used for lab sessions. You are randomly allocated the order of the labs so you could (as i did) end up doing your kinetics lab 4 weeks before kinetics is taught. 

The 3 lab topics are: chemical kinetics, acid-base equilibra (titration) and chemical equilibra (precipitation of salt).

Also, don’t be fooled by the ease of the tutorial questions and 2 mid-terms! The finals is meant to segregate the good from the average so it is EXTREMELY TOUGH. There are 4 questions, each worth 25 marks with many, many parts. I did not do 4 of the parts and still ended with an A so do not be alarmed! Work hard and you should be fine.

Grade obtained: A
thread_title: CM1502 General and Physical Chemistry for Engineers

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/05/year-2-sem-2-ay201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

Human Factors Engineering (HFE) is about Ergonomics, Workplace design and task-design. It puts the human operator as the reference to design everything around.

I missed all but 3 lectures for this course and regretted it. The professor is really engaging and you can feel his passion for what he teaches. I did not really enjoy the module at the beginning (when I didn't really study and did not attend lectures), but started to get into it as the term progressed. The course covered Human Information Processing, Anthropometry, Visual Signals, Auditory signals and workplace design.

Assessment consisted of a final exam (40%), a mid-term exam (25%), a project (20%) and assignments (20%). The project involves analyzing any machine and improving it by applying ergonomic principles, while the mid-term and final exams are essay based. The mid-term (primarily) involves regurgitation of concepts, while the final exam involves design-based application questions.

Grade Obtained: A-
Comments: Was a good, fun course. I regret not going for lessons for this.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/05/year-2-sem-2-ay201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By Prof Ang Beng Hwa

Quality Engineering outlines the methodologies and tools used to measure variability in quality attributes in the industry. It is very industry specific, and heavily emphasizes context. I loved the professor (Prof Ang Beng Hwa). This lectures were structured and systematic, and they were delivered largely in a case-study format. Assessment consisted of a final exam (70%), a mid-term exam (25%) and a homework assignment (5%).

The mid-term exam was largely numerical in nature while the final exam was essay-based. Both the exams were open book.

Grade Obtained: A
Comments: This was the only course for which I consistently went for lectures. Was totally worth it!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/05/year-2-sem-2-ay201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

IE2100 is about using probability models to gain insights that can be applied to real-life problems. It has a heavy emphasis on Discrete- and Continuous-Time Markov Chains and Queueing Theory, and is very important for anyone who wants to go into operations or logistics management. Assessment consists of a final exam (50%), mid-terms (30%) and assignments (20%).

This is a challenging module with difficult examinations. The mid-terms exam had a mean score of 29 out of 50, which is really low for an ISE module. The challenge does push people though, and I had a good time studying for it.

Lectures and tutorials are structured well and are conducted at a good pace. They are, however, a bit dry, although it's difficult to see how they could be made more engaging given the content matter.

Grade Obtained: B+
Comments: Really screwed up the mid-term (scored 22/45), and gave up a good grade the moment I did that. (Sort of) redeemed myself in the finals though. Could've done a lot better for this, but I'm satisfied with the effort I put in after the mid-terms.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay-20132014-semester-2.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
Lecturer: Dr Tsai Fen Fang

If you see that this prof is teaching this module, avoid at all costs. 

In general this is a good module if you are proficient in statistics. Good to score for economics/stats major. Things covered are very basic if you are taken some higher level statistics module. After taking EC3303 and GEM2901, 3/4 of the things taught in this module was already covered somewhere.

The workload of the module differs with semesters and professors, and I have heard that this module can be very easy under others. But note that this module requires memorising (definitions for statistical terms). A simple t-test that would otherwise been complete in 3 lines requires you to define single thing to the tune of a full page length. If you are mathematically trained in statistics in the first place you will find this awful and useless.

For my sem, we were required to get the non scientific calculators (yes the ones you see in pasar malams) for the mid terms. As to why it is beyond my comprehension. We also had to write a 4 page full APA-format paper and do some data processing. This amounted to some 2000 word count length.

Enough of my rant, as long as you avoid this prof, you should be fine. Also note that the functions of scientific calculator (the normal sharp one) is very useful. Unknown to many, you can use it to calculate mean, standard deviation and even run simple 2 variable regressions. This can be very helpful.

Bell curve can be steep as statistics module normally consists of 2 kind, people who what is going on (can score) and people who are clueless. Because it is a basic stats mod, if you are good with the concepts, a good grade is almost guaranteed.

Overall, it is a good module to score, but not enjoyable.

Grade: A+
thread_title: PL2131 Research and Statistical Methods I

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014_08_01_archive.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Lim Tee Tai; Prof Low Hong Tong, Prof Yeo Khoon Seng

This module is divided into 3 parts with focus in fluid mechanics concepts. Though it sounds difficult especially to physicsphobic people like me, the difficulty of content is similar to what you see on HowStuffWorks. First part is thought by Prof Lim, focusing on weather systems and how tornados/hurricanes/rain etc are formed. It was almost like a geography module. 

Second part was by Prof Low, who taught on fluids in the human body(blood) and the various issues with blood viscosity etc. I found this part interesting as it shows how clots etc actually happen, and the innovative ways we have to solve them and prolong out lifespan. Many people found this part boring though.

Last part was by Prof Yeo, who teaches fluid mechanics in flight. Examples are how birds make use of air(fluid) mechanics to fly, save energy etc. 

The assessment follows the end of each part, so the timing of tests will be off your mid term/finals schedule, which may or may not be good. Do practice how to draw the various diagrams as speed will be key in the tests. 1st test is open ended like geog paper, 2nd is MCQ and the 3rd is also semi-open ended.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014_08_01_archive.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr. Phil Chan

This is by far the most interesting and eye opening module I have taken in NUS. Phil is insanely passionate about physics and the philosophy of sciences. He got his degree in philosophy of science and then went on to do his p.h.d in theoretical physics. This guy is no joke.

The module focuses abit on the history/philosophy of science and makes very difficult concepts palatable to normal human beings like me. I have never seen science and mathematics in the "correct" way until I took this module.

Assessment is 2 MCQs test(open book), participation (class and star gazing), and a group story writing project. For the project, the prof looks for creative ideas. You have to write a fictional story using the concepts involved. This is relatively simple if you watch scifi movies/tv. I wrote a story based on tv series Fringe and I believe I did well.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014_08_01_archive.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

I do not think the lecturers matter. A very easy module with low workload compared with other FASS 1101E subjects. Good to take for fass students to clear the social science basket requirement. Assessment consists of mid terms (memorise the readings) + some assignments on user design + finals (memorise again). Personally I do not like memorising type of modules, but we all have to clear the basket requirements. Finals consist of multiple choice which can be done in 1 hour. Because of the easy content and assessment, this module can be very difficult to score.

Grade: B+
thread_title: NM1101E Communications, New Media and Society

author: NUSMods
message:
Module Review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014_08_01_archive.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By Assoc Prof Chua Tin Chiu

I think he is still teaching this module. He has taken this module for many years. This module is about understanding statistics in the media. Examples include how surveys are done and the various flaws, how flawed reports of research are in news in general. Most concepts are intuitive if you have some training in statistics. If not, they are also easy to master. The prof is patient and will clarify doubts if you have any.

This module has 2 short projects (around 2 page each or something) as part of 40% of the grade. 60% of the grade is finals. There is no mid terms, which makes this a relatively low workload module. The only downside is the two 8am lectures, which can really drain you.

The finals are very important in my opinion. Most people do very well for the 2 projects (there is a template to follow). Good english is important in case the prof misunderstands you, which happened to my group. I did not do well for the projects but managed to score for the module through the finals.

Grade: A+
thread_title: GEM2901 Reporting Statistics in the Media

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay-20132014-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
Instructor:  Dr. Rajasekhar BALA

Good module on global issues such as pollution, global warming and all issues related to environment. One lecture per week and one tutorial. Lecture and tutorial are combined. A class presentation on a simple paper is needed. Groups are randomly assigned by the prof. Content is relatively easy. Midterms and finals consists of MCQs and short response questions. This is a typical "easy to score" gek as long as you put in the effort.

Global warming has been in the spotlight recently, with President Obama passing a new bill on energy related emissions. A video lecture by Nobel Laureate Thomas Friedman was shown in class during one of the lectures (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JXZK3J00oz4). I think this lecture puts into perspective some of the the environmental-economic-political issues we face today.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay-20132014-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2.

Prof Chia is probably one of the best profs in the department. Teaches very well and looks out for her students. She even brings home cooked food to classes for us during the very long lessons.

Workload is relatively light. This module has no exams are is 100% continual assessment. 10% Class participation, 2 x 20% Response Papers and a final project (paper+presentation) 50%. Since there are no exams, there is no usual shit of having to read textbook and grind questions.

The response papers are only 2 pages long, with presentation in class. Do not be fooled by the length of the papers. You have to come up with your own topic for the first paper (prof may assist in helping you see if topic is suitable). Mean time to completion based on my observation is around 2-3 days for lesser mortals (like me). It can be done in one day, if you are really good. The second paper response is to the Gov budget 2014, where you can choose a specific area to respond to. Again, the choice of topic is yours.

General strategy for choosing topics includes choosing a topic/area that is doable - able to find statistics to validate your argument, easy to apply economic theory etc. The prof likes to good application of theory in real world scenarios and some amount of quantitative work (if not, you are just talking cock). Different people have different strengths so choose accordingly to yours. I like to avoid areas that are difficult to do/time consuming, like macroeconomics. However, other people have also attempted such topics with considerable success so to each his own.

The assessment for the papers is quite rigorous. During presentation be prepared to be scrutinized by the prof/class. Any obvious mistake in theory will surely be pointed out on the spot (so everyone can learn). There is no room for throwing smoke. You have to meet some minimum system requirements to join the class, which means that there are no noobs. For the response papers, marking is also quite stringent (well prof puts in a lot of effort). The prof goes through every line we write and all the mathematical simulations we do. 

The last part is the project work, done in groups of 4. A 20+ page paper and presentation on some topic of our choice. Originally this was supposed to be individual work so do take note. I dare say this is my best group project experience in NUS, where everyone knows their stuff and are on the ball. Things get done when needed. No usual bullshit of "i need more time/i got other project/i do not know how to do/ etc". Again, prof is more than willing to help on the project, so be sure to consult. All in all, the project was a very enjoyable experience thanks to very good team mates.

On top of the normal curriculum, the prof also managed to invite a senior vice-president from United Overseas Bank and a partner from a consultancy firm to talk to us. I have learnt a lot from the talks but the content is too much to be included in this post.

This is a very good module to take if you are interested in economics and policy making in the Singapore context. Policy making in my view is extremely difficult in Singapore. In light of current political trends, it will probably get worse.

Grade: A
thread_title: EC3352 Seminar in Singapore's Economic Policy

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Grace Ng: http://theappleearthen.blogspot.sg/2014/08/module-review-nus-year-1-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2

The second part of a year-long module which significantly improves on the first part, which was just a series of lectures that absolutely no one listened to. This time, you have to draft and complete a mock research proposal according to NUS guidelines. Over the course of the semester, you submit a few drafts to an assigned supervisor who you meet twice for consultations, followed by the final deadline in Week 12.

This was a lot more useful and a lot more enjoyable than the previous semester. Mainly because it forces you to actually do something, rather than just sit there and fall asleep continuously. It also won't take up too much time; my supervisor (Dr Leung) actually reminded me outright that the module was pass/fail, advising me not to attempt anything too ambitious. One huge plus point about this project is that you can use it to think about what you want to do for your Independent Study Modules (i.e. research projects) and honours thesis. Even if you don't, it's still really valuable to familiarise yourself with the proper protocol.

Difficulty: Not too bad. Depends on how good you are at writing essays. Also, it's pass/fail, so you shouldn't have to worry too much about it.

Workload: I spent about 2-3 hours on each draft, tops, and I only had three submissions. You will have to do quite a bit of background research, since all academic research builds on previous literature (I was reminded of this multiple times), but Google Scholar is always your friend. Thinking of a viable topic might take some time, but again, it's pass/fail so it doesn't matter too much.

Assessment: Your supervisor marks your final draft on a pass/fail basis, which is great because s/he will spend up to 40 minutes with you beforehand going through exactly what they want. It shouldn't be too hard to pass.

Interest Value: Very relevant to USP students especially, since they'll be doing a lot of these proposals in the future.

Teaching Quality: Dr Leung, my supervisor, gives very targeted advice and isn't long-winded in the slightest; my last consultation took all of five minutes. She's also very understanding, and often reminded me that I only needed to aim for a pass, hehe.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Grace Ng: http://theappleearthen.blogspot.sg/2014/08/module-review-nus-year-1-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2

I like communications research. A lot. I like how in some contexts it's basically close reading applied to everyday media, and I've always been fond of squeezing water from apparent rocks. I also really like qualitative research, which focuses on rich, individual responses and description rather than statistical aggregation and analysis. Qualitative communications research involves things like interviews, focus groups, and even observation of and/or immersion into communities and cultural scenes. It reminds me a lot of journalism (which I did before university) and creative nonfiction (which I did after taking this module, in Yale), but tempered with academic validity and accountability. If I went into academia, I'd do research like this.

Hence this module succeeded in terms of getting me interested in the subject matter, but unfortunately I was almost as frustrated with how this module worked as I was with Introduction to Psychology. Lectures were dry and theoretical, and a lot of it was reading off slides. Tutorials were always more interesting, partially due to my engaging and enthusiastic tutor (Ms Shobha Vadrevu) but largely due to the real-life examples and practice that went on during the class. I ended up skipping quite a few lectures and reviewing the textbook and lecture slides later; as it turned out, I didn't miss much.

My main frustration, however, was the group project. The group project is a killer. Qualitative research was not meant to be done in a group, and the requirements feel contrived to ensure equal participation from everyone. For instance, you only can choose one research method (interviews OR focus groups OR participant observation) because each group member has to meet a minimum quota of hours for each. Choosing a topic that engages every member equally is also very hard; I was the relative expert on my group's chosen subject area, and therefore ended up with most of the project on my shoulders. Time management is absolutely crucial, and something my group failed at. After weeks of research and participant observation, my group ended up rushing the whole 6000-word research paper in a single night.  Do not do this. It sucks.

This is a pre-requisite module for all NM majors, so I can't exactly recommend or not recommend this module. I'm also pretty sure that the group project will change next semester, since we were the pioneering batch for it. Just... all the best, I guess.

Difficulty: The content isn't difficult to master, and mostly comprises theoretical frameworks for investigation and analysis. A lot of jargon, so you have to study, but you don't have to study that much. Cover the textbook readings at least once, then study the lecture slides.

Workload: One midterm exam, one final exam, and one 6000-word group research project. The last one may or may not slay you. The other two are okay.

Assessment: The exams are mostly MCQ with some short answer questions that focus on big concepts, so they should be fine. The group project has a peer evaluation form requirement, so if you end up shouldering a lot more work than you should, there's a way to reflect that in your grade.

Interest Value: I ended up loving communications research, but others I talked to found it quite dry and uninteresting. I guess it depends on personal inclination.

Teaching Quality (Lecture): bluh

Teaching Quality (Tutorial): A lot more interesting than the lectures for sure. Ms Shobha really goes the extra mile for you if you need it, and always hangs around after class for group consultations. One time she went through my field notes in Google Drive and added comments everywhere even though I didn't know if I was even going to use it in the final project. She's great.
Special Note: Like all group projects, some groups are better than others. Just pray you get a good one, because you can't choose.

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Grace Ng: http://theappleearthen.blogspot.sg/2014/08/module-review-nus-year-1-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
