Got an A-. Very dry and easy module, which is why I did not put in enough effort and didn’t get an A. Bell curve was terribly skewed as most people scored really high for it. Those with H2 math will be at an advantage.

Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2014/07/module-review-for-year-1-mods.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1
By Prof Phil Chan

General/special relativity. Quantum Mechanics. Particle Physics. No difficult math involved at all as it is only an exposure module. Abstract ideas that you can either accept it or you can’t.  Which is probably the reason why no matter how badly u do, the lecturer won’t fail you so that you can S/U it. Highly recommended for those that like to think a lot and has an interest in modern science and abstract ideas. 

Teaching staff:

Prof Phil Chan is a passionate lecturer. However, he like to say 'please take note of it' at basically everything. 
Tutors are very clear in clarifying doubts and explain concepts in another way to complement what was being taught in the lecture.

Assessment and workload

- 30% - Group Project (in groups of 2-3) Write a few thousand words report (either a story you made up or a book review). Choose your teammates carefully, avoid those who are intending to S/U if possible 
- 20% x 2 - 2 tests one held during midterms (40 mcqs,1hr) and the other (45mcqs,1hr) before reading week. Both open book. Even though it is open book, you still need to be extremely familiar with the material as you will not have the luxury to flip through the lecture notes slowly to find your answers.
- 5% - star gazing (just go for 2 sessions. It's on certain Fri 7-10pm) Just need to go and sign attendance if you are not interested.
- 5% - forum discussions
- 10% - E-learning. Does not always apply. Not every semester will have e-learning.
- 5% - In class assignment. Free frag, TA essentially give you the answers.
- 5% - Tutorial Attendance

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2014/07/module-review-for-year-1-mods.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1
By Dr Cornie

Almost similar to H2 Econs but more in depth and some new stuff. Not recommended for those without Economics background as the content is really heavy.

Teaching staff:
Dr Cornie and another male lecturer. Dr Cornie's lecture just rips off everything from the textbook. Sometimes she seems a bit confuse over the slides. The male lecturer was much better, teaching stuffs that are beyond the textbook. 

Assessment and workload
- Online tutorial (10%) - some technical screw up of the platform at the initial stage but was quickly rectified. 
- Mid-Term (30%) - MCQ. Easy. Everyone scored very high. If you are aiming for A+, u need to score full mark for this.
- Finals (60%) - MCQ. Slightly harder but still quite simple. You still need to score close to full mark to get A+

Personal experience
Bell curve is extremely steep, majority of the students come in with econs background. Careless mistakes is not tolerated if you are aiming for A or A+.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2014/07/module-review-for-year-1-mods.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1
Lecturer: Wang Fei

More in depth compared to A level calculus. Some proofing questions that JC students might be unfamiliar with, and will take some effort to get the hand of it.

Passionate and particular in teaching the right concepts.  He also like to spend some times during the lecture to share how students should adopt the right attitude when doing math. Some might think that he is wasting time talking about it, but I personally find them quite useful. Pace is quite fast, hence it is advisable to do pre- and post-lecture reading up on your own.  

Assessment and workload
- Homework - 15%
- Lab assignment - 5%
- Midterm - 20%
- Finals - 60%

Personal experience
I made a comeback for this module and got an A. Basically I screwed up my mid-term due to some personal reason, scoring slightly below the mean score. And partly because I don’t know many people in the course, I am among the few that could not get full marks for all the homework and lab assignment as I have to do them on my own while most of the others just copy or discuss with each other. However, I put in a lot of effort after I got back my mid-term results and breezed through the finals while many others complain that the paper was ‘imba’. So with a lot of hard work, you can score for this module.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2014/07/module-review-for-year-1-mods.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1
By Dr Ng Kah Loon

A lot of theorems which can be quite intimidating at first, so it is necessary to be familiar with all of them in order to use them during the time-packed examination.

Dr Ng Kah Loon is the best lecturer I have encountered so far and I voted him as the best lecturer for this semester. Great in delivering concepts. Pace is consistent and manageable if you are prepared. Helps to keep the lecture quiet so that it is more conducive for those that are keen to learn. And a sense of humour that keep the lecture engaging.

Assessment and workload

20% midterm. 10% lab quiz. 70% final. Lab quiz is for you to score so do prepare for it. His Mid-term is challenging, expect the mean to be around 22-24 out of 40. Finals on the other hand is much easier.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2012/12/academic-update-ay201213-sem-01.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

Worse module I have ever taken. Both of the lecturers cannot really teach. Always try to cramp more contents than possible within the lecture hrs and rush through it. People with geography background should have some advantage as we are required to look at images and identify some landscapes and their features most of the time. And if you are color blind (like me) DON'T EVER TAKE IT. There was a project which carries quite a lot of weight-age as well, and it was extremely time consuming.

Grade: A-
thread_title: GEK2503 Remote Sensing for Earth Observation

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2012/12/academic-update-ay201213-sem-01.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

Overall an interesting module which provide foundational knowledge of genes and introduces some of the current research in the field and the controversies arising from it. Personally, I have never taken a single biology class before, so the learning curve can be quite steep especially with the number of jargon thrown at you. However, once you are past that stage (which require considerable amount of effort), everything will be easily comprehensible.

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2012/12/academic-update-ay201213-sem-01.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
By Dr Zhang Yang

Taught by Dr Zhang Yang, a new lecturer. Content is very light, and second half of the module is some-what an analog of the first half, so if you understand the first half of the module, it means you also understand most of the concepts from the second half. Mid term was extremely easy, in fact, I think it was easier than the homework she set. So, try to score full marks for it. There were 36 students who scored full mark. Finals was much more challenging, and really test on your understanding of concepts.

Mid-Term mean-median: 87.17-90 (/100)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2012/12/academic-update-ay201213-sem-01.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
By Dr David Chew

This module will introduce a lot of concepts from all over the place. But do not be worried if you are new to these concepts as only basic understanding is required. The main focus is using the softwares to compute statistics. 3 programs will be taught (R,SPSS & SAS) and might be a little confusing initially due to the different syntax of the 3 programs. Mid term was extremely easy and Dr David Chew is really lenient in his marking. Final is much more challenging, but if you had taken CS1010 before, you will have a distinct advantage as the grade differentiater requires u to be able to code something that you will never expect and wouldn't probably prepared yourself for. Dr David Chew will provide one sample paper for both mid-term and finals. Do practice them as the format of the actual test closely resemble the sample paper.

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2012/12/academic-update-ay201213-sem-01.html

By Lim Chingway

Although a lot of seniors had warned me about the difficulty of  this module, it is in fact not as difficult as I thought it would be. This is probably because the lecturer Dr Lim Chingway had watered the module down. His Mid terms was easy (Very similar to tutorial questions) and the Finals was very do-able. Be diligent in doing his tutorials plus some additional practices from the textbook and you will be fine.

Mid-Term mean-median: 27.17-29 (/40)

By Luo Xiao

Very interesting module. However it can be quite abstract some times and I have to re-read the textbook several times in order to understand it. It does not help that the lecturer, Luo Xiao, has a very thick accent and poor command of English. Although his mid terms (and possibly finials) is challenging, he is very lenient in his marking. Even so, the midterm median is not very high. The module is taught by Chan Yiu Man. Although many say that he is not a good lecturer, I actually find him decent in delivering concepts. His illustrations make it much easier to understand. author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay201314-sem-02.html

Instructor: Dr. Eric Fesselmeyer

 An extremely important and useful module for all economics majors. Workload is extremely light with only midterm, finals and 1 tutorial every fortnight. author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay201314-sem-02.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
By Sng Tuan Hwee

Extension to EC2101. Lay the foundation for many other lvl3000 modules. But as someone who has taken many other lvl3000 EC mod before this, most of the materials taught were actually a toned down version of what I have learned before. Despite that, I still did not do well for my midterm due to my carelessness. Luckily, I managed to save myself during the finals. Both midterms and finals has its fair share of tricky questions.

SNG Tuan Hwee is a decent lecturer and is able to explain concepts clearly. But for some reason, I get really confused by the way he phrased his some of his tutorial/homework questions.

Midterm mean-median: 54.8-55.5/75

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2013/12/academic-update-ay201314-sem-01.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

One of the worst module I have taken. Lots and lots of readings. I was caught off guard during the mid terms as I was not expecting it to test so much on readings, and got a less than desirable score. Having learnt my lesson, I spent considerable amount of time and effort going through all the boring  readings to prepare for my finals, BUT NOT A SINGLE QUESTIONS ON READINGS CAME OUT. Although Alberto SALVO is an Engineer during his undergrad years, his module is highly qualitative. Will not recommend this module to those who prefer modules which are more quantitative.   

Mid-Term median: 78/100

Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2013/12/academic-update-ay201314-sem-01.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

By Peter James McGee

Taught by Peter James McGee. Most of the concepts in the first half of the semester is similar to EC2101. The mid term is straight forward while finals have some challenging questions. Also, McGee like to set funny questions which often involve some extra-terrestrial beings. 

Mid-Term median: 72/100

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2013/12/academic-update-ay201314-sem-01.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1
By Dr Chua Tin Chiu

This module is more qualitative than quantitative. Dr Chua Tin Chiu who is also our Vice Dean, spent most of the time sharing with us his experience as a statistics/survey consultant. Although his stories are very interesting, it gets tiring after awhile. Workload is light, no tutorial, no mid term, only 2 very simple 800-1200 words essay.
thread_title: GEM2901 Reporting Statistics in the Media

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2013/12/academic-update-ay201314-sem-01.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1
By A/P Lim Tiong Wee

For some reason this module is extremely popular. I spent over 1000 bid points for the module as I'm really determined to get it because it will help me prepare for my SOA exam in February. I'm not sure why others are so keen in it tho. Perhaps, because it is only offered once every 2 years, or because of the word 'Actuarial' in it (Taking this mod, however will NOT help you break into the actuarial career if you are not intending to take the professional actuary exams). 

The module is taught by A/P Lim Tiong Wee. A good lecturer and extremely patient when going through his tutorials. However the pace for tutorial can be quite slow sometimes. The workload is extremely light for this module with an average of 3/4 questions per tutorial and a total of 6 assignment questions which are a give away. The finals is, however, a completely different story. While his assignments focuses on calculations and application, his final is purely theoretical. I believe many, including me, are caught off guard by it. It also does not help that the paper is extremely difficult and I can barely do half of it.

Grade: A
thread_title: ST3246 Statistical Models for Actuarial Science

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lam Yan Tung: http://investwizar.blogspot.sg/2013/12/academic-update-ay201314-sem-01.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1
By Dr Alkema Leontine

The module is taught by Dr. Alkema Leontine. She is really passionate in her teaching and very approachable for consultation. Module is not difficult, but it may take some time getting used to the various types of time series. Workload is light with one take-home mid term and a project. The only complaint I have is that she released the details for the project very late into the semester despite covering all the necessary content a few weeks prior to that. Finals was very do-able, the only challenge is probably to compete all the questions within the time limit as I overheard a few person saying that they did not have time to complete it.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by: http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay-20132014-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2

Instructor: Dr. Zhang Jie/ Dr. Zhu Shenghao

This module is reputed to be the most difficult content-wise, and it is true. The module is split between the 2 professors. This module is highly mathematical in nature. If your foundation in mathematics is not strong, you are going to have a bad time. The module involves differentiation, integration, multi-variable taylor expansion and copious amount of algebraic manipulation. All economic models at level 4000 are denoted in mathematics - there is no escape.  

Fortunately, mid terms and finals were very easy. Mid term median was 45/50, 75th percentile 47/50. The scores imply that (1) the paper was easy (2) despite the difficulty, half the class scored 90% and above. The honours student cohort is way stronger than the usual cohorts in level 4000 and lower modules so expect stiff competition. Finals was also similarly easy. To illustrate, people started to leave at the 1 hour mark, and by the exam end only less than half the cohort was left. This does not happen for core economics modules.

For this module, the aim is to be able to derive all models the basic parameters from memory. Consider that each model when written in comfortable spacing takes up 3-4 pages on average. It takes a bit of practice. Derive the models a few times over and you will be comfortable in solving them. The papers also have substantial marks for qualitative answers to test you on your intuition and understanding. The papers are set close to the lecture notes, so as long as you are well prepared, should be ok.

From what I hear, many As were given out and the module is likely not curved (with the tight spread of scores, I do not see how they can reasonably curve it). Furthermore, Zhang Jie is a full professor and I suppose he can do what he wants. For a easier experience, take in semester 2. EC4102 in sem 1 is taken by prof Aamir, and exams under him is reputed to be very difficult.

Grade: A

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by http://beatingthesyst3m.blogspot.sg/2014/06/academic-update-ay-20132014-semester-2.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
Instructor: Dr. Eric Fesselmeyer 

This module is known to be the 2nd most difficult, perhaps only behind 4101/4102. First half of the module builds on the multiple regression model taught in 3303. Given a good foundation in EC3303, this part is manageable in my opinion. The intuition behind the models are largely similar. It may be confusing at first, but after a bit of thinking you should be fine.The 2nd half introduces time series models, which is completely new. This is the harder part, and some things can be really confusing. There is a steep learning curve that needs to be overcome, but once you do so, everything will fall into place nicely. Class participation 15%, Midterm 35%, Finals 50%.

Eric is a good teacher, though at times he may go through content a little bit too fast for comfort. He has slowed down considerably, comparing to EC3322 which he also taught him 1 year ago. Back then he already commented that he was working on slowing down. In this semester, the last lecture was cancelled, which I believe should have been used to cover time series more in depth. Comparing to past semesters where time series was covered by Prof Tilak, we seemed to have covered less ground.

Mid terms and finals are of reasonable difficulty, consistent with Eric's style (this is based on my own experiences). He also releases past year papers that he has set, and he generally does not repeat questions. It is still good practice to do so, variants of past questions do appear in his new exams. In the finals, he repeated a past year mid term question.

Midterm for this semester was a little bit different than the past semesters. (15+25)/100 was allocated to 2 questions that was application in nature. A research hypothesis was given, and you have to suggest how to test the hypothesis using the models we have learnt. Based on past trends, it was unprecedented and everyone was caught off guard. The obvious lesson is to not spot his questions. The questions require a good understanding of the models, if not you will have considerable difficulty. The 25 mark question was similar to an empirical question set in tutorial. Normally, students tend to ignore the empirical questions in tutorial. This is something to note.

For finals, the prof will say that the test is focused on content after mid terms. Do not believe him, it is a trap. I decided to be lazy and not revise his past year mid terms, and sure enough a 15 mark variant question came out and I got it wrong. Around 40% of the paper was on content before the midterms. This was also the case in the previous semester where Prof Tilak covered the time series portion. Again, emphasis for finals was on understanding and application of the theory learnt.

Econometrics II is not a easy module, considerable time is needed to fully understand the concepts.

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This course was taught by A/P Denis Tkachenko. He was also new to NUS at that point in time though his delivery of lectures would have fooled you into thinking he is a veteran of the course. The course textbook is mandatory and would be used for both EC3303 and EC3304.

The class had a fortnightly tutorial that imparted knowledge of the statistical software, STATA. My tutorial was conducted by Ms Mun Lai Yoke, a PhD student with work experience. She used to work in the banking industry and is immensely bright. She was a great tutor and made concepts easy to grasp.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This was taught by A/P Satoru Takahashi. He was new to NUS when he delivered this class. Fortunately, his lectures were succinct and clear. My background in math meant that I had little problems with the class.

The class covered all 4 types of games - static games of complete information, dynamic games of complete information, static games of incomplete information, and dynamic games of incomplete information.

I managed an A+
thread_title: EC3312 Game Theory & Applications to Economics

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This was taught by A/P Ko Chiu Yu and A/P Bag Parumal. My section was exclusively taught by A/P Ko. He is relatively new to NUS and got us a little confused sometimes. However, things were eventually sorted out and I did enjoy the smaller class size as it gave us greater opportunity to engage the professor.

The math was intended to be relatively involved but A/P Ko and A/P Bag decided to water it down during the examinations. I feel that the workload did not merit 5.0 MCs.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This course was taught entirely by Professor Reginald Tan. He is very eminent in the field of safety studies. Industrialists would have heard of him. He can be snide at times but his classes are largely enjoyable. The numerical models covered in this class are tougher than you would imagine. Make sure you understand the differences of the various puff and plume models.

This class gives you the mental fortitude to be a real engineer. No more equation crunching - a real engineer merely needs to know the section to refer to when called upon. This is exactly what you need to do. Make notes in the textbook so that you can draw out the right equations and information when needed. The textbook is very much a necessity. Exams are open book.
thread_title: CN3135 Process Safety, Health and Environment

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This is the only lab module in Chemical Engineering that is worth a full 4.0 modular credits. And there are very good reasons why this is so. There are 6 labs in total. Each group of 4 to 5 is expected to submit 5 lab reports and do 1 presentation. Each group is given 2 weeks to complete every lab session though they typically can be finished in a single 4-hour session. Reports are due 2 weeks after the first of the two lab sessions. It takes an inordinate amount of effort to work on the lab reports, each of which end up about 40 pages long.

I found it tough and tiring. Beware!
thread_title: CN3108 Chemical Engineering Process Laboratory II

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

CN3121 was taught by A/P Lee Dong-Yup (who is now, fortuitously, my thesis supervisor) and A/P Chiu Min-Sen. The class is heavily based on Laplace Transformation (from MA1506). If you learnt it well back then, this will be an easy class for you. Your classmates will tend to do really well as well so grades may fall a bit short of expectations.

I managed an A.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2013/07/a-year-in-review-20122013.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This course was taught by Professor Farooq Shamsuzzaman and A/P Kus Hidajat. Prof Farooq covers the first half of the course while A/P Kus covers the latter half. Both professors skimp on details and want students to learn by doing and thinking. Some students enjoy this form of academic freedom while others are upset that the professors are seemingly "teaching less". There is a project during the course and it took up a large chunk of my time. The project counts for very little but is a good academic exercise to practise the concepts from the class.

I found the course to be immensely challenging but I emerged relatively unscathed. Prof Farooq tends to intimidate during the project presentation so hold your cool and you will do fine.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

This is a lab module - there are no lecturers, no textbooks. Just TAs guiding you through labs every fortnight. I found labs to be terribly time consuming though they offered a good chance to work with peers in Chem E. Each lab group had 4 to 5 members and every group had to submit a lab report (Results, Discussion, Analysis - around 10 pages?) a week after the lab session.

The lab sessions varied from year to year but centred mainly around thermodynamic, fluid mechanics and biomolecular concepts.

I dislike labs and lab reports but i did fairly well.
thread_title: CN2108 Chemical Engineering Process Laboratory I

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

CN3124 is a core module taught by A/P M.S. Uddin. Prof Uddin's lecture delivery style is a bit languid and mundane so grab that cuppa to stay awake. Seniors told me that the course was easy to score in - truth be told, i did well. However, the content covered was far from easy. I found this to be the most conceptually-challenging course in Sem 2, 2011/12. Of all the 7 modules, i took the longest time to grapple with the concepts covered by CN3124.

There is 1 mid-term, 1 group project and 1 final exam. The mid-term was easy but the marking terribly inconsistent. The same mistake took 12 marks from me while causing my friend to lose just 2. Of course, a trip to Prof Uddin's office rectified the mistake but my situation is the norm rather than the rule. Just ask any Chem E 3rd years if they recall the epic queue in LT6 to change scores. The project is more interesting and requires a fair bit of coding. Groups of 5 worked on the same problem statement albeit with different parameter values. It was fun :)

There are 2 text-books: Coulson and Richardon's Particle Technology and Separation Processes and Martin Rhode's Introduction to Particle Technology. As the quiz and examination are both closed-book based, i didn't purchase the texts. Moreover, the notes had enough mind-boggling concepts to keep you occupied. I had no time to flip through any of the books.

Topics covered:
1) Particle Charaterisation
2) Motion of Single Particle in a Fluid
3) Flow through Packed Bed
4) FIltration
5) Sedimentation
6) Centrifugal Filtration
7) Cyclone Separator
8) Centrifugal Sedimentation
9) Particle Size Reduction
10) Mixing of Solids

Prof Uddin has taught this course for many years and his passion for it seemed to have waned quite a fair bit. He no longer explains concepts in vivid detail, leaving too much to our own imagination. Much effort is needed for this course.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

CN2116 is a core module taught by Prof Lee Jim Yang (Head of Chem Eng Dept) and Dr Xie Jianping. A caveat - be prepared to throw everything you know (well almost) about reaction ratesout of the window. This module takes an empirical stab at reaction kinetics while all we have learnt in pre-university deals with theoretical rates.

There are 2 mid-terms, weekly graded assignments and 1 final exam. The mid-terms are very challenging - typical of Prof Lee's style. The final examination was also tremendously challenging so be prepared to study hard for this module. Prof Lee likes to ask questions that are non-conventional so mugging past year papers are not as effective.

There are 2 text-books: Chemical Reaction Engineering by O. Levenspiel and Elements of Chemical Reaction Engineering by H. Fogler. I got both of them and used them rather sparingly - i believe most of the stuff necessary is in Prof Lee's and Dr Xie's notes. If you want to purchase just 1, i suggest the latter.

Topics covered (Prof Lee):
1) Representing Chemical Reactions, Types of Reactions
2) Definition of Reaction Rates, Rate Laws, Types of Reactors
3) Mole and Mass Balances, Design Equations
4) Analysis of Kinetic Data
5) Design of Single Ideal Reactor, Concept of Partially Converted Feed
6) Series and Parallel Arrangements of Reactors
7) Recycle Reactor
8) Instantaneous and Overall Yield Functions
9) Product Distributions
10) Energy Balance Equation
11) Design of Adiabatic Reactors

Topics covered (Dr Xie):
1) Residence Time Distribution and its Moments
2) Models to Predict non-Ideal Reactor Performances
3) Elements of Heterogenous Catalysis
4) Catalyst Deactivation
5) External Mass Transfer Resistance
6) Diffusion and Reaction (Internal Mass Transfer Resistance).

Prof Lee is very witty and sharp - expect humorous barbs now and then. This makes the lectures, which tend to have very challenging material, more palatable. Dr Xie is a very nice lecturer who goes out of the way to help students understand the course material - write to him and he responds rapidly. Effort has to be put in to get fairly decent grades for this course.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2012/07/another-semester-in-review.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

CN2125 is a core module for Chemical Engineers taught by Prof Wang Chi Hwa and Dr Praveen Linga. There are 2 mid-terms, 1 group project, 3 assignments and 1 final exam. The mid-terms are unbelievably easy 80% of the students scored within 2 marks of perfect score. The group project is basically to interest you in the subject further and has little bearing on the overall grade. You have full liberty to choose any topic (from heat and mass transfer) to investigate on and write a 2 page report. The final exams is very tough and will segregate the cohort according to ability. I did very well for mid-terms and assignments so i believe it was the finals that undid my good work.

Textbook is: Fundamentals of Mass, Momentum and Heat Transfer by WWWR. It is compulsory and i recommend getting it since the papers are open-book based.

Topics covered (Prof Wang):
1) Fundamentals of Heat Transfer
2) Steady State Conduction
3) Unsteady State Conduction
4) Convective Heat Transfer
5) Convective Heat Transfer Correlations
6) Boiling and Condensation
7) Boiling and Condensation Correlations
8) Molecular Mass Transfer

Topics covered (Dr Linga Praveen):
1) Molecular Diffusion
2) Convective Mass Transfer
3) Convective Mass Transfer Correlations
4) Radiation Heat Transfer

Prof Wang, who teaches 70% of the course (the entire heat transfer component), is quite possibly the clearest Professor you will ever get in NUS. He goes through his slides extremely methodically, logically linked one portion to the next. Really easy for students to understand if you pay attention to what he has to say. Dr Praveen is pretty new at this module and he teaches the mass transfer components. He pretty much teaches from the book so if you are a fast learner, you can skip his lectures.

Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/12/module-reviews-sem-1-year-2-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

Another foundation economics module that i found to be too easy. As a level 3000 module, it is in fact easier than EC2102 taught by Dr Serene last semester. This course is conducted by Dr Zhu ShengHao and is at times simply insulting our intelligence. The content is too minimal, only covering 3 themes (IS-LM, Growth Theory and Macro foundations). 

The recommended text is Macroeconomics by Greg Mankiew. Easy text.

Exam Format:

40% Mid-term comprising of open-ended questions. Easy like mad. Average score was over 80 and 90th percentile was 96/100. I got 96.
10% for class participation. Make sure you attend once every fortnight at least. Unbelievably easy to get this 10%.
10% for assignments.
40% Finals comprising of open-ended questions. Another rather simple paper. A step up from mid-terms but still way too simplistic.

Comments:

Dr Zhu is barely audible in lecture and his microphone always seems faulty. His accent doesn't help but he does try hard to make class more interesting with real-world scenarios. Lecture attendance is usually dismal and this says a lot. The biggest issue i have with this class is that its actually simpler than EC2102. There is also some miscommunication between lecturers and some assumed knowledge was never imparted. My tutor was pHd student, Wan Jing. She's not bad but not perfect either. Made a hash of regressive taxation even though her thesis is based on fiscal policy.

Overall, the module is way too easy - this is not a good thing. I had friends who are obviously brilliant in economics and ended up without an A. The bell curve is pure lunacy.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/12/module-reviews-sem-1-year-2-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

This is a foundation microeconomics module that requires EC1301/EC1101E and EC2101. The content covered is similar to EC1301/EC1101E and EC2102 albeit more mathematical in nature. I find it such a small step-up from the 2 pre-requisite courses that it almost insults our intelligence. The lecturer for my year was Dr Peter McGee. He is a visiting fellow from Ohio State University. The text book recommended is Microeconomics by Hal Varian. A very readable book that is easy on the mind.

Exam Format:

30% Mid-term comprising of MCQs and open-ended questions. Very doable paper.
15% for Graded Assignments.
10% for Class Participation.
45% Finals comprising of 10 open-ended questions. Challenging but doable.

Comments:

Dr McGee was my lecturer and tutor. He is a nice professor though some of his notes are error-strewn and he tried to cover too much content in a single semester. 

The content is simple but his test questions are challenging. Quite a nice module to attempt. Easy, possible to score well. I got A+.

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/12/module-reviews-sem-1-year-2-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

CN2122 was taught by Dr Ti Hwei Chen and Prof Chen Shing-Bor. There are weekly tutorials, in addition to electronic tutorials at Imparo. The module covered the following topics:

Dr Ti's part:

1) Intro

2) Dimensional Analysis

3) Fluid Statics

4) Fluid in Motion

5) CV Approach, Mass Conservation

6) CV Approach, Newton's 2nd Law

7) CV Approach, Energy Conservation

8) Shear Stress in Laminar Flow

9) Analysis of Differential Fluid Element in Laminar Flow

10) Closed Conduit Flow

6) Thermodynamic Analysis

Prof Chen's part:

1) Differential Equations of Fluid Flow

2) Inviscid Flow

3) Viscous Flow

4) Turbulent Flow

5) Fluid Machinery

The compulsory textbook is : Introduction to Fluid Mechanics by Fox, McDonald, Pritchard. Recommended to get it although the notes are enough. Exam is open notes, not open book.

Exam Format:

The mid-term had 2 MCQ questions and made up 10% of the course.

There are weekly electronic assignments worth 5% in total.

3 "On-the-spot" quizzes account for 15% in total.

The finals (6 questions - mix of MCQ and open-ended) accounted for 70% of the module.

Comments:

Dr Ti is pretty bent on messing with your head. He sets MCQs that are efficient for the marker but deadly for the student. You will know what i mean when you get to it. Prof Chen is much nicer in class. I have to add that both lecturers reply to emails and are very cordial regardless of the questions you ask. This course needs quite a bit of math (especially stuff in MA1506) so if you enjoy math, you should do fine. Study hard, good luck. Only CN2121 is tougher.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/12/module-reviews-sem-1-year-2-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

CN2121 was taught by Dr Jiang Jianwen and Dr Lanry Yung Lin Yue, 2 committed professors. There are weekly tutorials. The module covered the following topics.

Dr Jiang's part:

1) Basic Concepts

2) First Law

3) PVT

4) Second Law

5) Thermodynamic Properties of Fluids

6) Thermodynamic Analysis

Dr Lanry's part:

1) Thermodynamic Properties of Systems

2) Multi Component Systems

3) Phase Equilibrium

4) Chemical Equilibrium

The compulsory textbook is : Introduction to Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics by Smith, Van Ness and Abbott.Highly recommended to get textbook - that's assuming you actually aim to pass this module since its open book.

Exam Format:

The mid-term had 2 open-ended questions and made up 30% of the course. (Open book)

There are tutorial presentations that count for 10%. Your group basically does the assigned question and presents it as a power point.

The finals (5 open-ended questions) accounted for 60% of the module. (Open book)

Comments:

The lecturers are pretty good but the module is so conceptually draining that you will get lost here and there (and pretty much everywhere). However, if you study, the mids and finals are quite manageable. The lecturers also double as the tutors during their respective portions. Tutorials are CRAZY. Takes a full day to complete each tutorial (many needing matlab and all). Study hard, can pass.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/12/module-reviews-sem-1-year-2-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

Another compulsory module for Chemical Engineers. The module is taught by Dr Kim Chu Young and Dr Lam Siew Hong. There are no tutorials but there are lab sessions. Coverage includes basic biochemistry and some nucleic acid content. There is a compulsory textbook: Biochemistry (7e) by Campbell and Farrell. I recommend getting it, you will definitely need it when you reach Dr Lam's part. 

Exam Format:

There is no mid-term examination, just weekly assignments up to week 8. Weekly assignments are crazily simple - all you have to do is read the article that Dr Kim uploads and come up with 2 questions on the article. These will go to the question bank that will become 10 of your final exam questions. The amazing thing is this contributes 30% to your grades. Lab sessions contribute another 30%

The final examination, which makes up 40%, is entirely MCQ. It is open book but maddeningly hard. 

Comments:

You will not find 2 more contrasting lecturers working together for a single module. Dr Kim is extremely slack but for good measure. He sees the module as one to pique our interest in Biochemistry. He explicitly told us that only what is written in his notes is tested, not what is said or mentioned in the textbook. He frequently lets us leave class on time or early. Dr Lam, on the other hand, is overly passionate about the content. He is very animated in class so you will never fall asleep but don't even think about leaving on time. Class ends at 9.35am but he will lecture until 9.55am. Dr Lam uses stuff in the book so you should get it. The labs are pretty interesting but the TAs are lazy and don't prepare. Mine started to read the lab manual when i asked her questions. Very hard to do well. Students who took Bio may do better. Mug hard. The weird thing is i actually got an A+ for this class. o.0

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/12/module-reviews-sem-1-year-2-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

CM1501 is a foundation module for Chemical Engineers taught by Dr Zeng Huaqiang. There is a lab component and there was a pretty major cock-up by the administrators as they confused week 7 (as defined by NUS to be the week after the break) with the recess week. Some students missed their labs and had to go for make-ups due to this. There is a compulsory textbook: Organic Chemistry by Janice Gorzynski Smith. Not really required since the notes are extremely detailed.

The first half of the semester was extremely easy as it covered the following topics:
1) Structure and Bonding
2) Acid-Base
3) Intro to Organic Reactions
4) Alkanes
5) Stereochemistry
6) Organic Reactions
7) Alkyl Halides (up to SN2)

Mid terms were pretty straight forward. Almost a cinch. But the real 'fun' starts after Week 7.

The 2nd half covered these pretty content heavy topics:
1) Alkyl Halides (SN1, E1, E2)
2) Alcohol
3) Alkene
4) Alkyne
5) Conjugation
6) Aromatic Compounds
7) Acid Derivatives
8) Carboxylic Acid
9) Aldehyde
10) Amine

Finals were crazily hard. Period.

Exam Format:
The mid-term was a 67-question, MCQ paper. Very simple paper. Highest score was 99, i got 94.

Labs were an unknown quantity. I did terribly for my labs, especially the 2nd and 3rd. My yield (which affects your score) was a meagre 50% due to some accidents. Be careful with your labs. Some TAs know their stuff...some.
Final exam was a 77-question, MCQ paper. Very hard. Very.

I can't remember about the exact % of each component but i think the finals counts for quite a bit more than the others.

Comments:
Dr Zeng responds to emails really quickly and is generally a nice professor. He does have a tendency to rush like mad (really really mad) in his lectures. Perhaps its a Chemistry department thing. As an organic chemistry module, you know that memory work will be required. Tutorials are handled by various TAs. My TA is Ong Wei Qiang who is amazingly thorough yet concise in his classes - definitely one of the better TAs in NUS for sure. He does his own slides to augment and reinforce the lecture content. There is also a lab component for this module and my Lab was managed by Dr Ship Chee Peng who is okay. Can do reasonably well if you study.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/06/flirting-with-academic-suicidenot.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2.

This is a foundation macroeconomics module that requires EC1301/EC1101E. Many people recommend not doing EC2101 with EC2102. Also, each semester is taught by a different lecturer using different text-books and covering different content. I was taught by Dr Serene Tan who uses (Macroeconomics by Williamson). She focuses on macroeconomic models and macroeconomic optimisation. Math is almost everything about this module. If you hate math, take the semester taught by Wong Wei Kang. He's is more qualitative. Topics centered around economic models with the Monetary Inter-temporal, Real Business Cycle, Keynesian IS/LM models most frequently used.

Exam Format:

25% Mid-term comprising of open-ended questions. Tough paper meant to kill anyone who doesn't like math. I suppose Dr Serene wants you to work hard for your grades. Average was 75/150.

10% for class participation. This depends on the number of times you present your tutorial solutions.

65% Finals comprising of open-ended questions. Tough paper meant to differentiate the great from the good.

Comments:

Dr Serene exudes confidence and delivers her lectures rather brilliantly. She does not respond to emails generally (she will tell you that) but holds office hours to entertain queries. I found her to be a really good lecturer.

My tutor was Dr Li Bei who speaks English with a slight British accent despite being a Chinese national. She is concise in her classes and overall a good tutor.
Overall, EC2102 is one of the tougher Economics modules but if you love math (As i do), you fill find it a cinch. For the mid-term examinations, despite the harrowing average of 75/150, i topped the cohort with a 147. This is a testament to the importance of Math in this module.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/06/flirting-with-academic-suicidenot.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

This is a foundation microeconomics module that requires EC1301/EC1101E. The content covered is similar to EC1301/EC1101E albeit more mathematical in nature. Microeconomic optimisation (utility maximisation and cost minimisation) formed a large portion of the module, implying the frequent use of differentiation. There is a compulsory text (Microeconomics by Bernheim). I recommend getting this book as it makes attending lectures redundant.

Exam Format:

35% Mid-term comprising of MCQs and open-ended questions. Very doable paper.

10% for 2 Graded Assignments. Discussing with your peers should see you get full marks here.

10% for class participation. 

45% Finals comprising of open-ended questions.

Comments:

The lecturer for my year was Dr Hou Zhaoyang. However, he is a visiting fellow and will not be teaching this module from now on. He's English is concise but he's delivery can be monotonous at times. He also takes a while to understand questions although he almost always comes up with good responses.

My tutor was Ms Nona May who was a PhD student. She has since graduated and left NUS to teach elsewhere. She was a good tutor with deep knowledge of economics.

Overall, the module is pretty easy and you can learn it without attending lectures since Dr Hou merely teachings from the book.

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/06/flirting-with-academic-suicidenot.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

This module was touted to be the toughest of all CN modules. However, at least for the past semester, it turned out rather farcical. The 2 professors managed to miss out 2 important chapters and ended up apologising to the cohort for the mistake. Each is pointing the finger at the other but i am sure it is a departmental oversight. One of the professors is new and might not have been briefed on the content that he is supposed to cover. I am of the opinion that this error will have troubling consequences as we advance through the years in NUS. As such, i will not be able to provide a good review for CN1111. In the end, the module covered some numerical basics, process variable, mass balance, energy balance and transient balances. Single and multi phase equilibra were left out by mistake. The compulsory text (Elementary Principles of Chemical Engineering) is extremely important so i recommend getting a copy.

Exam Format:

The mid-term was rather straight forward and made up 35% of the overall grade.

The finals, set at 65%, was no tougher. I suspect this was "compensation" for the terrible administrative oversight mentioned above.

Comments:

There are 2 lecturers, Prof George Zhao and Prof Zeng. Prof Zhao taught the first part and was supposed to cover the 2 missing topics base on accounts from seniors. Prof Zeng covered all the balances. Prof Zhao is new and struggles at delivering lectures. Prof Zeng has been teaching this module for 5 years or so and is experienced. Both Profs respond to emails rather quickly.

The tutorial system is also rather curious. Every week, we get a new tutor. They could be TAs (PhD students) or the 3rd Prof (Dr Photinon). Tutors are generally competent although tutorials are unbelievably time-consuming and difficult at times.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2011/06/flirting-with-academic-suicidenot.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

MA1506 is the 2nd part of engineering mathematics that is a foundation for all engineers. It builds on the knowledge taught during MA1505 although surface, double and triple integrals are entirely omitted. An overwhelmingly large portion of this module was spent identifying and solving differential equations. Another huge chunk was spent on mathematical modelling, which (yes!) is also about differential equations. Although modelling cannot possibly be testing in a 2 hour examination, i urge all juniors to pay close attention to this part. As an engineer, i cannot stress the importance of process modelling and simplification. Other topics covered include Laplace Transforms, Matrices, Partial Differential Equations, Linear Transformation. Rishabh found the mid-term tests to be rather straight forward. I agree with him on this one but the difficulty really varies from year to year. There is also a mini lab component that tends to favour all Chemical Engineers because it is predominantly based on Matlab which you have taken in Semester 1. Engineers from other departments have no such luxury. There is an optional textbook (Pearson's Differential Equations and Linear Algebra) which i didn't bother getting.

Exam Format:
The mid-term was a 10-question, MCQ paper that was all about solving differential equations. Speed was of the essence. This segment made up 20% of the overall grade.
The lab component was a meagre 5% with optional lab sessions to teach you Matlab. Chem Engineers, of course, gave the labs a miss.
Final exam was a 8-question, open-ended paper. Each question had 2 parts. This segment made up 75% of your grade.

Comments:
I belonged to lecture group B that was taught by Prof Chew Tuan Seng who i found to be funny and engaging. I attended his classes despite the luxury of webcast lectures. My tutor (whose name escapes me) is pretty committed and knowledgeable in the module. Despite his relative youth and nationality (PRC), he spoke adequate English and answered my queries very well. I find that he helped my revision quite a bit with his summaries for each topic.
Overall, this is a do-able module if you are good in math since competition is not that stiff with 1600 classmates around. If you hate math, more practise always helps a bit.

Grade: A+.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2010/12/and-so-dance-begins.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

All engineers are required to take a Human Resource module as their degree requirement. As DDP students, we are given yet another discount. This introductory Sociology course double counts as an Engineering HR module and as an Arts exposure module. The module is fun and i have gleaned important teachings from it. It has definitely changed the way i viewed society. The lecturers (Prof Tan Ern Ser and Dr Kelvin Low) are jovial, making the module interesting. There is actually little synchronisation across tutorial groups - my tutor, Dr Enrique, leads us into entertaining and interesting tutorials that require minimal preparatory work (no homework!) while some other tutors may not take this approach (summary for each read :X).

Exam Format: 10% Term Paper, 20% 1 Essay Mid-Term, 10% Participation and 60% 3 Essay Finals.

Comments: It's an essay heavy module which i would have preferred not to take despite the fun i had. You really need to study hard for this module as there is plenty to learn, recall and use in your essays. I wrote some controversial things in the finals and still managed to do well. Individuality is key, don't parrot the text and readings.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2010/12/and-so-dance-begins.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

This is a "discounted" module for double degree students. Arts majors are required to take the "supposedly" more rigourous EC1101E equivalent and this module is closed to Arts majors (less DDP students). Content is basically just 'A' level H2 Economics. I even found that this module covered less than what was taught in JC. The first half was lectured by Dr Debasis Mondal while the second by Dr Seevaratnum. Dr Debasis tends to speak softly and has a strong accent, making his lectures a tad difficult to comprehend at times. Dr Seeva is the module-coordinator and lectures very well.

Exam Format: 30% All MCQs Mid-Terms, 10% Participation and the rest, all MCQs Finals.

Comments: Not as easy as imagined as almost everyone has H2 Economics knowledge so study for this module! I actually regretted taking this module in place of EC1101E. If your math is extremely good, take EC1101 and avoid the potential careless mistakes. Moreover, you probably can do well amongst the "math-shy" Arts majors.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2010/12/and-so-dance-begins.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

This module is a core module for Chemical Engineers. This means that the cohort is small (~240) and it is not easy to score well though content and knowledge is very manageable. The first half of the semester is lectured by Dr Steven Halim and the second half by Dr Saif Khan. Both of them are very good lecturers and you will not find their lectures boring. Through the semester, you will learn basic coding with matlab and some matlab default functions (ode45, fminsearch, fmincon, et. cetera.). Module is generally manageable and fun if you have a taste for logical thinking.

Exam Format: 30% goes to weekly labs, 30% goes to mid-terms and 40% goes to the finals. Mid-terms and finals contain MCQs, short-answer questions and essays.

Comments: The mid-terms can be quite daunting as it is entirely set by Dr Steven who likes to challenge his students. Moreover, the MCQ portion carries a disproportionate weightage (10 MCQs account for 50% of the marks) in the paper and will cause potential slip-ups. If you are a below-average student, focus on MCQs and get them right - you will rejoice. If you are above-average, any carelessness will kill you. I lost 15 marks by getting 3 MCQs wrong due to carelessness. Labs are fun and pretty easy, listen to your TAs and start early.
thread_title: IT1005 Introduction to Programming with Matlab

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Denis Tan: http://chbeandecons.blogspot.sg/2010/12/and-so-dance-begins.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

This module is a compulsory module for all students enrolled in an engineering course. The course contents is basic engineering math, including but not limited to functions, calculus, sequences and series. The first half of the semester is basically a recap of 'A' level H2 Math knowledge. You will find the lecture contents pretty familiar unless you have forgotten everything you picked up in JC (Then your in trouble). The second half of the semester is much, much harder. Topics covered include double and triple integrals, surface integrals, line integrals, et cetera. I will echo Rishabh's sentiment on this one: Make sure you get the hang of these as soon as they are taught in lecture. If you can visualise 3 dimensional surfaces and objects well, the topics covered should be manageable with a good amount of practise. If you can't...then this is going to be a long and hard module. I recommend the text book (Thomas' Calculus) only to the latter group.

Exam Format: The mid-terms (20%)  is all MCQ (10 questions) while the finals (80%) is all open-ended questions (8 questions each with parts (a) and (b)).

Comments: Lecture groups are apparently extremely important. My lecturer was Prof Koh Khee Meng who is universally liked and even has a facebook page in his honour. He explains concepts extremely well and uses home-made visual aids to awesome effect while some other lecturers are known to be "not as awesome". If history serves, Chem Eng students tend to get him as their lecturer. A note on the mid-terms: the math department loves using the option (E) None of the above to trip students. I only managed 5/10 for the mid-terms because self-doubt crept in with option (E). Since the module is taken by 1600 students, A is well within reach if your math is good.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2014/01/module-review.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

Prof: Dr Esther Goh

Reflection Paper for Agency Visits (4 pages): 15%
Portfolio of News Articles & Reflections (7 pages): 25%
Tutorial and IVLE participation: 20%
Finals: 40%

In all frankness, I took this module expecting an A because (1) It's a Level-1000 module and I've aced Level-2000 ones and managed Level-3000 ones; (2) It sounds easy; and (3) I'll be up against lost university students in their first year - right? Wrong. This module made me realise that there is no direct/ straightforward correlation between time spent in university and the effort that one devotes to particular modules, because everyone in this module was so hardworking............. Ugh. Maybe this module just wasn't for me. But I found the textbook an ultimate bore and lectures weren't too much better. It had more readings than my Level-2000 modules, and while the text was probably more reader-friendly than archaic sociology ones, they were s o o o o b o r i n g :(

The module was quite insightful though, opens your eyes to the less fortunate and marginalised members of society and the role of social work. The visits were quite revealing as well; case studies stand right before you and you realise that not all parts of Singapore are pretty and shimmering with glitter.
thread_title: SW1101E Social Work: A Heart-Head-Hand Connection

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2014/01/module-review.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

Prof: Dr Zhang Weiyu

Mid-Terms: 35%
Class Participation (Weekly tutorials & IVLE posts): 25%
Finals: 40%

A friend warned me beforehand about how dry and boring this module was, but I headed straight towards it anyway like a stubborn bull (because it fit perfectly into my timetable ha ha ha) and got away with an A, unscathed. I really enjoyed this module quite a lot, although I tended to fall asleep (a lot) during the lectures. I blame the U-Town lecture theatre and its dim lighting lol. In all seriousness though, as long as you're willing to read and understand the textbook, this module isn't hard at all. Exams merely comprised of MCQs and Short Questions which you will be able to answer as long as you understand your stuff. I suggest making notes out of both powerpoint slides AND the textbook; they are equally important.

There's also quite a bit of irrelevant content in the textbook, but Prof only tests what she covers in her lecture so you don't have to remember the other stuff. This module is not made up of pure memorisation, there is actually quite a good bit of understanding as well so just try your best to digest the concepts. The textbook is quite good, but in case you are still confused, Google and Wikipedia are your friends.

NM Majors, don't look upon this module as a compulsory one that you dreaaaadddddd so much. Coming from a sociology major, it's quite fun lol.
thread_title: NM2101 Theories of Communications and New Media

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2014/01/module-review.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

Prof: A/P Ho Kong Chong 

Mid-Term Test (Open-book) OR Fieldwork Project: 40%
Tutorial Participation & My Life in Pink Assignment: 10%
Finals (Open-book): 50%

SC2213 Lectures, despite being scheduled at the inauspicious and unfortunate timing of 4-6 PM on FRIDAYS, were fine because Prof Ho makes them as engaging as it gets. I thought Prof Ho made for a very nice father-ly figure, he has a nice loud voice haha. Lectures are important because Prof goes through all the essential readings and tells you what to look out for so you're not lost in the sea of readings - the readings for this mod really are not thaaaaaat easy at all :( SC2205 readings were so much easier lol #oops

Attractive points of SC2213: Mid-terms and finals are OPEN-BOOK. That means you don't have to memorise statistics till your head bursts; that means you can look to your readings for reference during the paper itself; that means you can bring all your files into the examination hall for comfort and security. That said, the thing about open-book examinations is that you really need to stop flipping and start writing ASAP. You can have all the relevant material at hand, but as long as they are not on paper you'll not get the grade you feel you deserve. So preparation is still needed, however nice open-book sounds.

If you opt-out of the midterm, you have to write a fieldwork-based paper as replacement. I opted out of it because I was swamped with assignments due that very week, and I thought the paper was going to be fine, but HOOOOLYYYYY it was not (and the mid-term questions was easy -_-). I spent so so so so so so so so so so much time on the paper and was so uncertain about it that I was checking CORS for NUS' policy on module-dropping. I didn't drop it in the end because I was already into my 8th week and dropping it would result in a FAIL grade which I wasn't/ will never be ready to accept. So I continued to plough through and ended up with a B+ for the paper. It was returned in the middle of my finals wtf. Mr TA placed the paper under my table as I was furiously flipping through my files for the relevant readings and I was like ($@*$(859*(%£*$%$@ and contemplating so hard whether or not to flip it over. Didn't think too much about the grade but wasn't particularly disappointed either.

Anyway, do make consistent effort; as bad as I make it sounds I still managed a B+ after screwing up both the paper and the finals up so lost sheep are in good company and at least the lectures are enjoyable and the professor is nice.

Grade: B+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2014/01/module-review.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

Profs: Dr Rose Liang & A/P Paulin Straughan

Assignment 1 (Outline of Term Paper): 10%
Assignment 2 (Term Paper): 30%
Tutorial Participation: 10%
Finals (2 essays): 50%

Prof Straughan's lectures were extremely engaging and enjoyable; she always livens up the whole place at 10am in the morning (where I'm usually half-awake) and her energy is sustained throughout the lecture. (& OMG I JUST REALISED THE CANDY CANE SHE GAVE US DURING THE LAST LECTURE IS STILL IN MY PENCIL CASE. AFTER 2 MONTHS.) The readings for this module aren't at all heavy and were rather enjoyable at that, so yes I do recommend this module. Dr Liang is a lot more soft-spoken than Prof Straughan, but she does her job well too so it all works out pretty nicely I guess.

There is only a single assignment (term paper) for this module, and it takes up 40% of your final grade. So it is important to do well for the paper, I think it pretty much determines your grade. I'm not sure how the bell curve works, but it seems as if CA performance is more definitive of your grade than finals, no matter what they say. Either that or there are just really few people who do well for finals, so that CA becomes the differentiating component. Anyway, pick a good term paper topic for yourself (you devise your own question), and do a good job of it.

Finals: I really didn't expect the questions to be so readings-based -_- I thought they would be more general ones, but they were in fact rooted in particular readings - so apparently it IS important to finish your readings. Start early in the term, don't let readings pile up because the amount of sociology readings are scaaaarrrrrrryyyyyyy. Consistency is key!!!!!!!!!!!!! Gogogogogogogogogogogogogogogogogogoggoo!!!!!!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2014/01/module-review.html

Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1

Prof: Dr Feng Qiushi

Assignment 1 (Review of Literature Review): 10%
Assignment 2 (Qualitative Interview): 20%
Assignment 3 (SPSS): 20%
Finals (MCQs, T/F, Short & Long Answers): 50%

Dr Feng is the whole package. He delivers lectures really well, he's nice, humorous and approachable, he has nice and informative PPT slides (!!!!!!!!!***) and he also makes everything (e.g. assignment guidelines) crystal clear. I really dislike ambiguity so I'm deeply appreciative that he tells you exactly what to include in the assignments and what you'll have to study and know for the exams. Lectures are important because he gives tips and also because the text-book is only secondary to his lectures; in fact, if you are able to digest the lecture content, the textbook is not necessary. I suppose the textbook makes lecture content clearer, but exams do not go beyond his lecture slides. Make sure you understand every single slide.

Three (individual) assignments: (1) Review of a literature review (1 page); (2) Qualitative Interview (4 pages); (3) SPSS Assignment. None of the assignments were particularly tough, and they weren't time-consuming either. SPSS might be confusing at first, but Dr Feng uploads step-by-step video guides so you've no excuse to not know how to work SPSS. He's also a really approachable and encouraging prof, so have no fear~ haha.

You'll not have enough time for your SC2101 Finals. So do not spend too much time on the MCQs and T/F questions, get the short questions over and done with quickly, and focus on the long questions because there are many parts to it. You don't want to have a perfect score for the front sections but not have time to finish the rest of the paper; you won't be doing yourself justice. So manage your time properly, don't fret, and write your answers clearly.

Past year papers are available HERE (http://libportal.nus.edu.sg/frontend/exam-papers).

Got a B+ for PL2131, but got an A+ for this. 雪恥 lol

*** TIPS for PPT slides:
- Calibri is the perfect font for PPT slides (Arial is ugly)
- Keep the font size consistent, do not alternate between font sizes within sentences and between lines.
- Do NOT add a drop-shadow for text other than the title
- Capitalise the first word on your bullet points
- Tab (above caps lock) is your best friend for sub-points
- Do not number your points after the bullet. Use the numbered list instead of bullets?
- Spare just five minutes to spell your words out properly. DO NOT txt speak me because AUTOCORRECT EXISTS YOU HAVE NO EXCUSE.
- Before you add pretty backgrounds and fancy slide templates (that, more often than not, only result in titles taking up a disproportionate amount of space and leaves the main content cramped with a line spacing of -0.4255), do save a plain version to upload onto IVLE so that students do not waste ink while printing gray-scaled backgrounds
- Actually don't bother decorating the slides if it takes too much time; as long as the content is clear enough design is unimportant.
- DO NOT include your name on every single slide - what are the chances of people stealing your slides???
- Do not text speak
- Do not text speak
- Do not text speak

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2012/12/ay20122013-semester-1-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

Class participation: 10%
Weekly summaries: 50%
Finals: 40%

Okaaaaay so I only attended 2 lectures of the 12. And I haven't watched a single webcast. I mean come on it's the only lecture I have on Friday and because it's Friday... therefore. Nevertheless I made my own notes from the lecture notes (which I assume are comprehensive enough) and slides (which might contain information not in the notes) and read them through more than once before the exams. Also for the weekly summaries I got them done at least two days before the due date. Maybe if I attended all the lectures and stuff I'd have gotten an A+ hahahahha.

FYI the assignment is usually announced over the weekend and due on the following Thursday 1pm. None of the readings are too long so you definitely have more than enough time. Also, the readings are fun. Philosophy papers are not written like dull academic readings, you'll have fun with them ^^

This is not saying all of them are easy. Look at this one: Summarize Frank Jackson’s reply to the charge that epiphenomenal qualia are “an excrescence.” Like...... waht am I reading? Nevertheless don't be intimidated by these words, just dictionary.com them and all should be good. The summaries are worth 50% so make sure you score for them!!!!! The finals are all MCQs and make up 40% of your final grade... and the average is 10/20. I'll leave you to make sense of that statistic.

Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2012/12/ay20122013-semester-1-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

Class participation: 10%
CA (Four Assignments): 25% 
Midterms: 35%
Finals: 30%

Aiyah love-hate relationship with this module -.- I know I've posted a post earlier saying there is no place for theory in new media, but I think what I failed to clarify is that I sort of love theories -_- Because I enjoy studying the random thoughts of people and I secretly hope one day there'll be a theory  named after me. Like LZA's theorem or something. Whatever this theory will contain, I'm not sure yet - you will know in due course. Hahaha.

Basically you need to recognise that you will have to complete readings & attend lectures (no webcast) to do well in this module. Also, put in consistent work for the four assignments, some are quite easy and fun so no worries there. I got Ms Wendy for my tutorial and she was effective in clearing up concepts and stuff, she'll also advise you on what to look out for and what's coming up in the lectures so do not be late for the tutorials!

Anyway read the readings and you should be fine. So many questions on human-computer interaction in the finals are confusing though; the MCQs are definitely not straight forward. I still don't know if my answers are right or wrong @@ And Professor Mc Kevin has a penchant for answering questions on the forum with even more questions directed to YOU so I think you'd better be as clear as possible in your question and show that you've used your brain already.

Grade: A
thread_title: NM1101E Communications, New Media and Society

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2012/12/ay20122013-semester-1-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
By Prof Kevin Low

Class participation: 10%
Term paper: 10%
Midterms: 20%
Finals: 60%

I absolutely love love love love love the lectures. In fact, I'd pay to attend the lectures. Professor Kevin Low's lectures are so interesting and ever captivating with his lovely sense of humour which pokes fun, occasionally, without being crude or insensitive. I also love that sociology lectures always leave you with some food for thought... it really is awesome. Copy notes during lectures! Tutorials are so fun and relaxing too, even more food for thought. Once again, remember what everyone says.

I would say get the textbook. It provides more insight and information into different theories and stuff, also makes stuff clearer because lectures just cannot cover everything in detail. Some of the readings are quite long and *coughboringcough* though but I don't think they are thaaaaat essential, though definitely beneficial. Obviously. But if you don't have time, make sure you go through the lecture notes (which should be covered with your notes after the lectures!!) and look for more examples (from the readings, Internet, anywhere) from different contexts and time periods. Also it helps to make your own notes :)

Take this module it is good :D

Grade: A+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Lim Zi Ai: http://www.ohmytian.com/2012/12/ay20122013-semester-1-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

Midterms (100 MCQs): 20%
Term paper (2 page paper): 30%
Finals (100 MCQs, 5 short answer questions): 50%

I had intended to major in psychology so I basically placed a lot of emphasis on this module. Prior to taking it I've read reviews on module-review.com (which btw is a fantastically awesome site, you should use it too) saying a lot of memory-work is needed and every bit of random information from the text book should be memorised because it might just be tested.

And memorise I did. I got 92/100 for the mid-terms (100 MCQs), and while I guess it sounds !!!! I think it's definitely achievable as long as you put in enough effort. I didn't even read every chapter twice and I attempted no practice questions, but the test really was a breeze. I have to say... Boy do I miss the feeling of feeling so confident after a test. Pay attention to the text-book when studying!!!!! Making your own notes help too I guess. Even if you just copy information directly from the textbook, the action of copying helps.

For the finals... Another 100 MCQs and five short-essay questions. The 100 MCQs only cover the remaining 3 chapters (not previously tested) but the essay questions cover every chapter, a question each. So basically you still have to study everything! You cannot forget about the brain and nervous system after the mid-terms. I was so stressed before the finals because I was afraid they would test random specifics, but the questions turned out to be quite general. The first question was something like 'Define split-brain and discuss two functions of the right and left brain respectively'. Last question on abnormal psychology asked you to list the ten symptoms of major depressive disorder and... risk factors of suicide I think. This is the serial position effect: the tendency to remember information presented first & last in a list. I don't remember the rest of the questions. Hahaha. Okay la I do with some effort, another question asked 'Why do people forget'?

Psychology is cool and interesting. But really a lot of memory work is needed and I'm not sure if I'm ready to memorise so much information for four years :(

Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/12/year-3-sem-1-ay-201213.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
By Dr Ng Kien Meng

Was really intrigued by the concepts taught in this module, though I didn't find the classes engaging and ended up skipping most of them. We covered Integer Programming, Advanced Linear Programming, Game Theory and Heuristics. Dr. Ng Kien Meng was a very organized lecturer and covered all topics thoroughly. But I just couldn't focus in his classes for some reason.

Assessment consisted of a mid-term (20%), assignments (10%), a group project (10%) and a final exam (60%). Did not buy a textbook for this module.

Predicted Grade: A-
Grade Obtained: B

My first grade below B+ in NUS :/ I probably made a careless error in my finals somewhere.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/12/year-3-sem-1-ay-201213.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1

This course was taught manufacturing planning and management. We covered Capacity Planning, Inventory Management, Manufacturing Resource Planning and Line Management. The lecturers were Dr. Huang Boray and Dr. Adam. I did not attend lectures for the first half of the module, but attended most lectures in the second half.

Assessment consisted of a mid-term (15%), project (25%) and final (60%). Almost everyone scored full marks for the mid-term. The project involved giving a presentation on the application of a ManuLog concept in real life. The textbook was pretty much useless for this module (regretted buying it).

Predicted Grade: A-
Grade Obtained: A-
Comments: Did not manage to finish my final exam paper, so guess it was a fair result. Like all ISE modules, this probably had a crazy bell curve.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/12/year-3-sem-1-ay-201213.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
By Prof Lee

Loved this course and Prof Lee. His enthusiasm for the subject was contagious, and he focused on the real life problems that people face in using statistical tools rather than focusing solely on the tools themselves. There were loads of case studies, loads of assignments and a very heavy project (for which I had an awesome team). The project involved applying simulation to a real-life scenario and giving a presentation about your methodology and results.

Assessment consisted of a mid-term (10%), assignments (10%), lab-test (10%), project (25%) and a final exam (45%). You don't really need the textbook for this module.

Predicted Grade: B+
Grade Obtained: A
Comments: I have absolutely no idea how I got an A for this module. Was in the bottom 10% for the mid-term, and I think I made a couple of mistakes in my lab-test as well. This was the only course for which I consistently attended lectures (mainly for the prof), and I guess it payed off. Working on the assignments and contributing heavily in the project really helps in internalizing the concepts taught in class.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/12/year-3-sem-1-ay-201213.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 1
By Prof Goh Thong Ngee

This course covered the theoretical aspects of statistics in engineering applications. It mainly covered hypothesis testing, ANOVA and Design of Experiments. I loved the professor, Prof Goh Thong Ngee, but a lot of people did not really like his lecture style. Both lectures were at 8AM though, which invariably meant that I skipped most of them.

Assessment consisted of a mid-term (40%) and an open-book final (60%). Both of them were rather straightforward, but because of the insane bell curve in ISE, this wasn't a good thing. Get the book, it's really important.

Predicted Grade: A-
Grade Obtained: B+

Comments: ATTEND THE LECTURES! I attended about 10% of them, and regretted not going for more. Prof Goh has awesome wit! :D Awesome entertainment value.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/05/year-2-sem-2-ay201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2

IE2101 is (mainly) about the Systems Engineering Management Plan and its applications to manufacturing situations. The module syllabus was revamped this semester, and most of the students (myself included) were not too happy with the new syllabus as it doesn't include any form of systems modelling and instead focuses on following a sequential template. Assessment consists of a project (60%), attendance and a quiz (20%) and a couple of assignments (20%). The project assessment consists of an interim report, a final report and a presentation.

The project was about making a flight-capable toy for young users. It was a fun experience, but applying the SEMP template to the project seemed like a forced fit.

Grade Obtained: A-
Comments: Expected an A+ for this, but not complaining too much about the final grade. Fine, I lied. I'm grumbling like an idiot about it.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2012/05/year-2-sem-2-ay201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 2
By Prof Bimlesh Wadhwa

This course was about the methodologies behind managing large-scale software projects. It had a heavy emphasis on software management methodologies and documentation, but didn't really provide the context in which they could be powerful. Assessment consisted of a final exam (50%), project (35%), a couple of assignments (10%) and tutorial participation (5%).
