The project was about making an event planner for NUS students. I had an insanely talented person in my group who spearheaded it, and that made my life a lot easier. Expect loads of effort put into the project if you want to secure a good grade. If you really want to learn, and not just get the grade, put even more effort into coding and less in documentation. The project can be coded in C++, C# or Java.

Lectures and tutorials were okay, but not too great. More case studies would have been welcome. The content is rather dry. The final exam tested some modeling concepts (drawing use case diagrams, domain models and sequence diagrams), along with a lot of memory-intensive stuff (like definition of concepts :/). It also had some application-based stuff, but was largely content heavy.

Grade Obtained: A-
Comments: Study consistently for this and be aware of your limitations in doing the project. Documentation is extremely important for the module (and in the working world), do it well :)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/12/year-2-sem-1-ay-201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

Most challenging module I have taken in NUS so far. I had a love/hate relationship with this module. Did not attend any lectures or tutorials, which was a stupid decision considering how difficult this was. Had to study extra hard as a result, but this was the first module so far that really stretched most of my friends. Some people took it in the previous semester (when it is supposed to be easier), but I think those of us who took it this semester got a lot out of it. This module included multi-variate calculus, uni-variate and bi-variate probability distributions and loads of integration! Really important if you want to go into financial engineering, financial research and (to a lesser extent) risk management.

Assessment consisted of 40% Mid-Terms and 60% Finals. Mean score for the mid-terms was 70%, with a standard deviation of 9 marks out of 40! The finals were relatively straightforward, and it wasn't that hard to solve the questions if you didn't make any careless errors (like I did -.-).

This is a compulsory module for ISE Students. My opinions about this module varied depending on how many tutorial questions I could do xD, but on the whole, it is really important if you want to go into hardcore mathematical modeling of any situation for which the variables are not known with certainty.

Expected Grade: A-
Actual Grade: B+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/12/year-2-sem-1-ay-201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

Extremely useful module if you are into computational logistics or want to go into optimization later. Also a really good module if you want to set up a business selling B2B optimization software. Involved Linear Program formulation and solving, transportation models, network models and basic non-linear programming. This was the only module for which I attended lectures! The concepts are pretty straightforward once you get a hang of them, but they are very much applicable to real life.

Assessment consisted of 15% Mid-Term, 15% Term Paper, 10% Homework Assignments and 60% finals. The mid-term was really easy (most people scored close to full marks) and the term paper was really fun. It involved analyzing any real-life problem and finding an efficient solution to it using the methods we learnt in the module. I had a really good group that created a computer program to find the quickest route from one point to the other for a disabled person in the Faculty of Engineering, and had a great time constructing it.

The finals were not too hard, but they were not the cakewalk that the mid-terms were. Not sure what grade I'll get, thanks to the crazy ISE bell curve, but I hope for the best. Had a great time studying this module.

Expected Grade: B/B+/A-
Actual Grade: B+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/12/year-2-sem-1-ay-201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

Only business module that I enjoyed this semester. The content, especially at the beginning, overlapped a but with IE2140. But this has a lot of new topics, including Bond Valuation, Stock Valuation, Options and Futures and Capital Budgeting. This is a pre-requisite for almost all other FIN modules (except Financial Statement Analysis), so you better study hard for this if you wanna specialize in Finance. Again, I did not attend lectures and did not really do my tutorials, but I had the advantage of having some knowledge about finance before I took this module. If you are completely new to finance, you better study hard for this :)

Assessment consisted of 5% Class Participation, 10% Quizzes (best 2 out of 3), 25% Mid-Term and 60% Finals. The mid-term consisted only of MCQs while the finals has both MCQs and Structured Questions. Mean score for the mid-terms as 80%, and the finals were (refreshingly, for a business module) challenging. I expect a 'normal' bell curve for this.

Expected Grade: A-/A
Actual Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/12/year-2-sem-1-ay-201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

One of the more enjoyable (and challenging!) modules this semester. Had a great time with this, and although my grades are not likely to be too hight for this, I learnt a lot from it. The module gave an introduction to creating customized data structures and classes, and emphasized heavily on the efficient usage of pre-existing data types in the Standard Template Library of C++.

All lectures are webcasted, though I skipped them and decided to learn on my own through wikipedia and C++ forums on the web. Came in pretty useful. You absolutely MUST take this module (and CS2103), if you intend to start your own software business at some point in the future. Even though the coding required is pretty basic, it'll give you a good idea of how object-oriented programs are written.

Assessment consisted of 5% Tutorial Participation, 20% Lab (2% per weekly lab), 25% Mid-Term and 50% Finals. The average score for the mid-terms was really low, and most people didn't fare too well for the labs either. Expecting a 'normal' bell curve for this one.

This is a compulsory module for all ISE people, and it'll come in pretty handy in the future. So do make sure you study hard for it :)

Expected Grade: B+
Actual Grade: B+
thread_title: CS1020E Data Structures and Algorithms I

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/12/year-2-sem-1-ay-201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

Another module with which I had an attitude problem :/ Didn't attend any lectures for this one. Fortunately, all lectures were webcasted, and unfortunately, I didn't go through them. Concepts were pretty straightforward if you have studied Econs in JC though, so studying for this wasn't all that hard. I'm not qualified to comment about the lecturer, but the tutor was really good! Going for lectures/watching webcasts is highly recommended though, since the notes are pretty skimpy. I did not buy any textbook for this module as I thought it wasn't very helpful.

Assessment originally consisted of 15% Homework Assignments, 15% Presentation, 30% Mid-Term and 40% Finals. Our mid-term got cancelled due to a horrendous error by the school though, and it was re-calibrated to 25% Homework Assignments, 15% Presentation and 60% Finals. The assignments and presentation are done in a group of 2 or 3. The Presentation is really fun and involves analyzing a case given by the lecturer. Make sure you find a good partner for this one! I had a really good partner and the assignments and presentation were a breeze.

The finals were pretty straightforward, which made this yet another module in which we don't really learn much and are given easy-as-hell exams which screw up the bell curve and don't really test a student's competence. Not challenging at all for the vast majority of the student population. NUS Business School really does need to take another look at its examination structure.

Expected Grade: B+/A-
Actual Grade: S (S/U-ing this)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/12/year-2-sem-1-ay-201112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

No disrespect intended to the business school and the lecturers for the module, but this was one of the worst modules that I have taken in NUS. It seemed that the only requirement was plain common sense, and it was mainly an exercise in planning the expected production levels and budget of a company given information about a company's expected sales and production processes. I did not attend any lecture for this module, and did not prepare for my tutorials except when I had to present. As such, I don't think I'm qualified to comment on the quality of the lecturers or tutors. The lecture slides were really good though, and they rendered the textbook useless (except as the source of tutorial questions).

Assessment consisted of 30% Mid-term, 50% Finals and 20% Tutorial Presentations (there were two of these). The tutorial presentations simply required students to present answers to the tutorial questions for a given week. It was a drag for most groups, and acted as a slumber session for most of the class. If you want to do well for this category, make your presentation interesting by engaging the audience and putting in relevant videos.

The Mid-Term was a really easy paper (the mean score was 90%), which required us to know how to prepare a budget using Activity Based Costing and Job-Order Based Costing. It also required a basic overview of analyzing a firm's variable and fixed costs. It had a few MCQs and two structured questions. It was important to concentrate and be careful while doing the paper, although it wasn't really necessary to study much. I score 92% after studied for a majestic total of 2 hours for the mid-term, and I guess you can easily score if you're good with numbers (and most engineers are), so don't fret too much about it.

The Mid-Term really put me off, and subsequently, I did not study for this module until two days before the finals, thinking that I would simply S/U it. Surprisingly, however, it was not that hard to get. And I thought I did pretty well in the final paper, leading me to postulate that Parkinson's Law works perfectly well in reverse too.

Overall Comments: I hated this module, and downloaded most of my notes for the first time a couple of days before the finals. Having said that, I think I'll still end up with an A-. This really made me reconsider whether studying Business was adding any value to my education at all, and I'm not entirely sure if it is.

Expected Grade: A-/A
Actual Grade: S (S/U-ing this)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/05/year-1-sem-2-ay-201011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

This module is a slightly more advanced rehash of the H2 Math statistics taught in JCs. The tests are rather straightforward and many people score close to full marks. However, the final exam had some tricky questions that caught a lot of people off guard. The lecturer was clear and concise but my tutor wasn't all that good. I didn't really enjoy this module, and missed or slept through a number of lectures and tutorials. The content is pretty easy, but slacking off like I did is a very bad idea, especially with the crazy bell curve.

Assessment consisted of 30% mid-term, 60% final exam and 10% of other small assignments. I did not buy the textbook and found the lecture notes sufficient. I did not do well in the final exam though, because my concepts were not as clear as they should have been.

Grade Obtained: B+

Comments: I don't really have much to add for this module so do add in you views in the comments section if you have taken it!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/05/year-1-sem-2-ay-201011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

A rather unique module. This deals with issues that affects managers and organizations like teamwork, culture, leadership, motivation etc. Lectures and tutorials are interactive, and students are not required to memorize much. My lecturer was Dr. Sandy Lim, really engaging and entertaining. My tutor was Ms. Riwa Akiyama and kept discussions lively and fun.

Assessment consisted of a group project (35%), final exam (35%), tutorial participation (20%) and subject pool (10%). The Group Project was about making a positive difference in someone's (person or organization) life and making a video and report on it. We made a system for the sustainable donation of excess/old furniture from furniture companies to needy families. Had awesome group mates for this project, and even though the video wasn't that professional, it turned out pretty great. The exam was a rather fun test of reasoning ability and critical thinking. 60% of it touched on questions that were explored in lectures and tutorials, while 40% of it was about creative problem solving while applying lessons learnt in class in a real life scenario. Tutorial Participation consisted of performance in class as well as three small 'one-pager' assignments that were given to us.

I found the textbook interesting but not very useful for exam purposes. You might wanna skip buying it if you're not all that interested in Organizational Behaviour.

Grade Obtained: A+
Comments: Have fun while taking this module. Try to have cordial relations with your group mates, and make sure you don't 'smoke' your way through by throwing big words or talking about grandiose ideas. A sensible, common sense approach that is backed up by some theory about Organizational Behavior will be well rewarded.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/05/year-1-sem-2-ay-201011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

The most useful module I have taken so far in NUS. The lecturer, Prof. Lau, was a bit dry but he sure knew his stuff extremely well. I did not attend any lectures except for the first and last one since all lectures were webcast, but his insights and the real-life examples that he brought into the frame were extremely useful and relevant. The module reshaped my perceptions of marketing as I realized that it is not just about reaching out to customers, but rather is about analyzing the various components of a business and developing the synergies between them. My tutor, Ms. Wong Pei Wen, was a really cool teacher who had tons of corporate and personal stories to share about marketing. She helped bring zest and corporate experience to the rather dry concepts. Textbook is compulsory for this module since tutorial questions and assignments come directly from the textbook. Also, the final exam is open book so it's good to have it handy.

Assessment consisted of 15% Individual Assignment, 15% Group Assignment, 10% Tutorial Participation, 10% Subject Pool and 50% Final Exam. The Individual assignment involved analyzing a case study from the textbook and answering several questions based on your analysis. The group project involved selecting a business headquartered in Asia and analyzing its marketing strategy and giving recommendations to improve the strategy in a 20 page report. This is a time consuming but fulfilling project. The final exam is rather easy since it is open book, and simply requires you to manage your time well.

Grade Obtained: A+
Comments: I did not really see the webcasts until two weeks before the final exam, and did not read the textbook until a day before the exam. I'm not mentioning this to showboat, but rather to emphasize that it's important to have a healthy dose of common sense and not overcomplicate things by thinking too much for this module. For the individual assignment and group reports, it helps to make the reports and visually attractive as possible. Also, it is important to put in the hours doing research for your assignment and ensuring that is of a high quality. Lastly, try not to focus too much on theory while writing your reports and rather try to ensure that what you right is relevant and makes logical sense.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/05/year-1-sem-2-ay-201011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2
By Toh Pee Choon

I loved this module! MA1506 deals with mathematical modeling in various scenarios through matrices and differential equations, and also introduces the Laplace transform. It also introduces basic programming with Matlab/Scilab, but no one took the programming part seriously since it was a minute component of the overall grade. Assessment consists of 20% mid-term, 5% lab assignment and 75% final exam. The mid-term questions are in an MCQ format and mostly test students on solving differential equations. The questions are rather straightforward. Lab assignment consists of a set of questions that you must solve using Matlab or Scilab and then submit to the maths department. Most people scored close to full marks for this assignment. The final exam consists of questions that test students on their problem solving skills and mathematical modeling (as opposed to simply solving equations). Students are allowed to bring a double-sided handwritten A4 helpsheet in which they can write down important formulae. Most people did not buy a textbook for this module.

Did not attend any of the lectures for this module since they were all webcast and did not attend most tutorials since the answers were uploaded in IVLE.

Grade Obtained: A+
Comments: Similar to IE2140, know how to think laterally and ensure that you understand the underlying concepts well. The module is all about applying mathematical models to real-life scenarios :)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/05/year-1-sem-2-ay-201011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

I took this module when it was still called EG1413 Critical Thinking and Writing.

This module involves writing critiques of articles and a position-paper that outlines a recommended course of action for a given issue. If you have a flair for expository writing, this is going to be a breeze for you. Assessment involves writing a critique (an individual assignment worth 21%), position paper (a group project worth 21%), oral defence (9%), class participation (9%) and final exam (40%). My teacher was James Stephen, a really funny and understanding guy who made lessons relevant and engaging. There is no official textbook for the module.

If you are good at writing, try not to follow the algorithmic process that is outlined in the lectures, you'll probably do better if you don't. But if writing is not your thing, be attentive in class and follow the process that the teacher outlines for critiques. Also, try not be too stiff during your oral defence. It's a fun module, have a good time while at it!

Grade Obtained: A
Comments: Writing and thinking critically is an incredibly important skill to have. Make sure you don't treat this module as something you have to 'clear' and try to pick up the relevant skills if you don't already have them.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2011/05/year-1-sem-2-ay-201011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

This module involves interpreting and making financial statements to reflect the operating realities of a business. It is a compulsory module for all biz students, and a pre-requisite for all finance modules, and is a useful and relevant introduction to accounting. Assessment consists of 20% mid-term, 25% group project. 15% class participation and 40% final exam (which was open book). The mid-term was relatively straightforward, but the group project had really convoluted questions and the final exam was a mess thanks because of the time contraints. Textbook is pretty much compulsory since most tutorial questions are from the textbook.
My lecturer was Winston Kwok. Funny guy who tried to make things as interesting as he could, but he said that he will not teach ACC1002 after this semester. My tutor was Ms Bernadette Chen, she is very good at clarifying problems and concepts but I found the tutorials rather dry.

Grade Obtained: A-
Comments: Practice regularly for this module and you won't go wrong. And don't fall behind your work! Regular revision will take you a long way and will help you save loads of time during the mugging period. Try to solve as many questions as you can from the textbook and don't get complacent. It might cost you a good grade.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2010/12/year-1-sem-1-ay-2010-2011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

If you like Physics, you'll really enjoy this module. But if, like me, you don't quite like physics and are just concerned about getting a good grade, you can do well as long as you are consistent with your work. The lecturer is really engaging and explains concepts really well, so do not miss lectures (I really regret missing a vast majority of them). The module consists of Newtonian physics + Thermodynamics and might seem like simply a more challenging version of JC stuff at the beginning of the year. However, it picks up fast in the second half and you better be consistent if you want to get rotational dynamics in your head. Thermodynamics is not too difficult. I did not read through the lecture notes for the topic and rather relied on the (awesome) explanation done in tutorials. Assessment consists of 20% mid-term, 10% labs (there are two of these), 10% weekly online assignments (almost everyone scores close to full marks for these, but be sure to do the additional practice questions) and 60% final exam. The mid-term is in MCQ format, and most people did not find them too difficult. The finals were killer, however, and most people (including yours truly) were not able to complete the paper.
I managed to score well without using a textbook, but a lot of people found it very helpful. Give it 3-4 weeks before deciding if you want to buy one.

Grade obtained: A+ (5.0)
Comments: I was expecting an A- for this and was extremely surprised when I got my A+! I have no idea how I managed to get this grade, because I was not as consistent in this module as I should have been. But I guess practicing lots of past year papers helped.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2010/12/year-1-sem-1-ay-2010-2011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

The first part of this module is deceptively easy and consists only of JC stuff (differentiation and integration). However, things begin to get progressively more challenging as fourier's series, partial derivatives, line integrals and surface integrals enter the picture. Make sure you get the hang of these as soon as they are taught in lecture. Else, you'll be playing catchup like I did.
Assessment consists of a 20% mid-term and 80% final exam. Speed is the key, so make sure you time yourself while doing past year papers. I did not buy a textbook for this module, and regretted it. So do consider buying one for yourself!

Grade obtained: B+ (4.0)
Comments: I was hoping for an A- for this, but had a horrendous paper for reasons unknown. It wasn't particularly difficult, so I guess sleep is a must before taking the exam for this.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2010/12/year-1-sem-1-ay-2010-2011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

I hated this module at the beginning of the semester, but I ended up really liking it by the time it ended! This is about understanding the basic concepts of electrical engineering like DC-Circuits, energy storing components, AC-circuits, logic gates, transformers and rectifiers and DC-motors. Assessment consists of 65% final exam, 20% midterms (there were two of these) and 15% labs (these are a headache, but you can ask friends you have taken a lab before you for tips). Contrary to popular perceptions, you do not need physics to do well in this module. It entails problem solving skills and loads and loads of practice. Work hard at the beginning of the sem for this module. You will find yourself lagging behind if you don't. Also, a lot of people get full marks for the tests and labs so you better make sure your marks are up there if you want a good grade!
You don't really need a textbook for this module.

Grade obtained: A+ (5.0)
Comments: Practice, and remember to time yourself when you do! This module isn't all that difficult, you can score as long as you keep you cool :)

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2010/12/year-1-sem-1-ay-2010-2011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1
By Dr Henry Chia

I loved this module! As someone who programmed for the very first time, it was a very very interesting introduction. This module is about basic C programming and involves really interesting lectures and engaging tutorials. Assessment consists of 32% sit-in labs, 8% tutorial participation, 20% mid-term exam and 40% final exam. The sit-in labs are very algorithm based while the mid-terms and final exams test both your algorithm skills and knowledge about the nuances of C-programming.
To do well in the module, the most important thing is to practice! Put in loads of effort for it, and you'll reap the rewards. Also, discuss things on the IVLE forum and with your friends. You will find that things are a lot more fun that way.
Textbook is not required for this module.

Grade obtained: A- (4.50)
Comments: I was expecting an A for this module and was disappointed when I got an A- instead. But I really enjoyed exploring programming. So no regrets about how I approached this module :D

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Rishabh Srivastava: http://iseandbiz.blogspot.sg/2010/12/year-1-sem-1-ay-2010-2011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

This module is about corporate law and legal challenges that a business might face. Parts of it are extremely useful module if you are thinking about starting your own business. But I didn't fancy the module as a whole. Assessment consists of 20% class participation (activeness in tutorial+discussion on IVLE), 20% group assignment and 60% final exam, which is an essay type exam in which you must recommend the legal course of action for fictitious business scenarios. Write as much as you can for the final exam (which, btw, is open book). At the same time, make sure you pick the right group mates. I had to pretty much solo my group assignment because I didn't. Also, be active in tutorials and attend lectures! I did not attend a single lecture for this module and regretted it later. Textbook is a must for this module and is written by the lecturer, Prof. Ravi.

Grade obtained: B+ (4.0)
Comments: It's not hard to score well for this module, but between B/A-/A, the differentiating factor is usually participation, group project and speed of writing for the final exam.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Toh Weiqing.

I took the class under Dr Mabel. The class was interesting in general, we gone through a few cases in the textbook, plus some math (not too much for Biz students). I would say it is a little more in depth than Ops Management with some real life cases - definitely a fun module to take!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Cynthia Ho Sin Tian.

This module will be interesting for those who want to understand how to read financial statements. This module requires a lot of consistency ,especially for the newbies because it will get very confusing when you meet similar accounting terms. Try to understand the concepts and learn to make the links.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Cynthia Ho Sin Tian.

Imagine 2 years worth of JC economics plus some new stuffs crammed into one semester. That will be a lot to cover. The most important thing is to understand the concepts because the finals will comprised of 80 MCQS.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Cynthia Ho Sin Tian.

This module is for those who want to explore the study of language with the use of Natural Semantic Metalanguage. it is very interesting because you will get to read articles written by scholars in the field of linguistics. I love how the professor engages the class.
thread_title: IEM1201Q English, Singlish and intercultural communication

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Teo Xi Shi.

4 Homework assignments, midterms + finals.

Since this is a continuation of MA2108, helps to have a strong basis of it. Mainly about differentiation, integration, sequences and series; the last chapter on power series requires students to link the chapters up. Expect about 85% proving questions. Tutorials are important so remember to go for them!

Prof Lee was clear in explaining the concepts, and his lectures are well-paced. He's friendly too, so don't be afraid to book consultations with him.

Overall, interesting module and good lecturer!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Teo Xi Shi.

CA - 30%, Practical - 10%, Online Quiz - 5%, Essay - 5%, Finals - 60%

The bid points are insane but definitely an interesting course to take! A lot of topics would be covered and questions asked are rather specific, so do spend some time on the lecture notes. Some hints on the finals were given during the last few lectures. There is a continuation to this module but admission is based on the grade you get for this module.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Isabel Liew.

Introductory modules can be quite dry because it covers an array of topics. SW1101E is no exception. How fun the module is REALLY depends on the lecturer. So, check who is the lecturer before deciding if you wanna take this module that semester. (:

The best part about this module is that we have fieldtrips to two social organisations. It is a first-come-first-serve basis. They only reveal the timing when you choose your fieldtrips, sadly. Reflections are about 10%. Quite worth it, I think.
thread_title: SW1101E Social Work: A Heart-Head-Hand Connection

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Isabel Liew.

There are seriously toooooo much rave about this module. Many people say it is easy to score, hence many non-Arts students take this module as their elective/GEM but really, it depends on the individual. It can be quite a lot of memory work and it depends if you are a skeptic. If you are, good for you. Just be angry about the topic and write with the theoretical knowledge.

Readings comes in stacks. For those who read slow, this module is definitely a no-no.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Isabel Liew.

Let me start by saying that this is my FAVOURITE module. TS was like a 'no choice, have to take one from the humanities basket' but it turned out to be one of the best modules (not talking about grades alone).

Make sure that you get ROBIN LOON for as many things you can (Tutorial, lecture, practical). He is awesome but don't make him blackmark you (I'm serious.)

Don't take this module if you can spare the time. If you are already overloading or taking 4 heavy core mods, I advise you to take this module another sem. This module is TIME-CONSUMING. A lot of outside-class hours needed. Additionally, choose your acting cast team well. They will make or break you and your grades. I still hang out with my team ever since so lucky me. (:
thread_title: TS1101E Introduction to Theatre and Performance

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Isabel Liew.

This module's bidding points killed me. So, if you're a freshie, TAKE IT or don't ever take it ever unless you have a lot of points to spare.

Like all other introductory module, the topics differ from year to year. It can be quite dry. The lectures are oh-gosh-eeeek. But the good thing is that it is web-casted, so you can watch it in the comfort of your room.

The 30% Mid-term paper is actually quite fun. You get to go for the Malay Cultural fest (OTOT) and write on it.
thread_title: MS1102E Malays - Tradition, Conflict and Change

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Suheti Lee.

Roughly within the syllabus covered in JC, with maybe a little bit more. According to my experience, if physics hasn't been your headache subject, you will do fine (by fine I mean around B+ or more).

But if you are a yr3/4 student, these concepts from JC time may have already grown rough. Workload in terms of concepts covered is definitely heavy in this case.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Justin Yeo.
Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1.

Easier than MA1521 Computing for calculus I feel... No multivariable calculus, no series, just a bit more proving which is not very difficult - inequality, delta-episilon, rolle's thm, intermediate value thm, extreme value thm, mean value thm. For BZA students, I think this would be better choice given that there is some multivariable calculus proving in ST2131 Probability.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by NDPC.
Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1.

I found it constructive to write my own reflections on each topic covered in class, dig up old diary entries, look up excerpts and articles from leisure reading, compile them and then bring them to the exam hall, as it is an open-book exam at the end.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Gary Goh.
Taken in AY13/14 Sem 1.

This module is all about the construction industry in Singapore, which might not be that intuitive from the title of the module. It covers material from construction demand, supply, a little bit of Singapore's construction history and government policies in national development in the context of construction.

I enjoyed this module, as the lectures are easy to follow and understand. I also learnt a bit on the basics of how the construction industry operates and the government policies to manage them, especially HDB which applies very closely to our daily lives when we reach the day to need to buy a HDB flat unit.

There is a project group component, which is to present on a tutorial question with a group of 2-4 members, and it is only required for 1 tutorial in the entire semester. Tutorials are conducted fortnightly, makes the workload much easier and lighter.

Exams are open-book, and critical as it forms a high percentage of this module's grade. Due to it's relative ease of content, the bell curve is steep for this one, and might be hard to score if insufficient effort is put in. However, to pass should not be a problem for everyone.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2013/05/nus-ay-1213-semester-2-module-review.html

Taken in AY12/13 Sem 2

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Prof: Wong Yean Ling

What is it about?

You learn about several basic areas of statistics/research methods like experimental vs observational studies, confounding variables... hypothesis testing, normal distribution.. et and how the media can misrepresent information. Not much calculation involved (actually for the exams she said no calculations involved.. not sure how true that is).

How's the workload?

Two lectures a week, with no tutorials. Two group essays (which are due on the same date) (which is basically apply some concepts to any newspaper article), one closed book final exam. There is a textbook but it's unnecessary because everything is in the notes. The notes are just the slightly summarized form of the textbook.

How difficult is it?

If you have any basic research methods knowledge (e.g. from PL2131, 2132, or other stats/research methods knowledge), most of the stuff should be familiar. There are some new things here and there, and a lot of it is quite logical so it's not THAT difficult to understand. 

Miscellaneous Tips

Ermmmm. I didn't go for lectures because I knew a lot of the concepts and felt it was a waste of time. The lecturer isn't that bad but isn't that good either. If you have no experience with research methods then do go for lectures I suppose.

Should you take it?

Fairly light in workload, everything you need is in the lecture notes. The essay assignment isn't that difficult either. Then again, I haven't got any grades so I have no idea.

I'm being quite pessimistic but it's mainly because I have no idea. I don't know how I did for my assignments, don't know what the exams will be like. So. Maybe I'll update this after my exams.
Bell curve is steep as everyone got A for group project. So B or B+ perhaps.

Predicted Grade: B

Actual Grade: B+
thread_title: GEM2901 Reporting Statistics in the Media

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2010/11/nus-module-review-sem-1-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

What is it about?
Basic french, such as introducing yourself, basic grammar and vocab, colours, numbers, time, etc.

What is the workload?
TWO 2 hour lectures every week with one 1 hour tutorial.
2 sets of grammar + listening + oral tests, one close to mid terms, one close to finals. No finals.

How difficult is it?
I heard there are 1-2 bad lecturers who are very confusing. Mine (Mr Yannick) was very good and nice so it was relatively easy enough to understand. Not EASY though, I would say it is fairly challenging for newbies like me. I had the benefit of having a friend who knew French to be my 'second lecturer' during lectures so it was easier for me.

Miscallenous Tips
Requires constant revision of work, as with all language modules I guess.
Highly recommended to take with friends, so that you all can practise speaking French together. It really really helps to just keep talking in (your limited) French as it helps you remember the grammar and vocab better.

Should you take it?
It is a competitive module, since there are many people who know abit of French and still take the module and may unbalance the bell curve. Take it if you have several friends who are interested as well!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2010/11/nus-module-review-sem-1-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

What is it about?
A wide range of philosophical debates - is killing the same as letting die? Does every event have a prior cause? Is pleasure all that matters? Why is there something rather than nothing?

What is the workload?
One philo article per week which you have to do a summary writeup on. The summary is not necessarily of the entire article. Usually it is about a portion of the text, and it depends on the summary question the prof sets. In a sense, this 'forces' you to do your readings every week. They generally aren't very long.

No mid terms, just a final exam which was 10 'MCQ's and 2 essays (choose 2 out of 3)
I put 'MCQs' in inverted commas because it's not exactly a 'pick a b c d e ' thing all the way. For instance, one question required you to match a particular statement to the philosopher who agrees with it. (5 statements, 7 philosophers) There were about 2-3 of these sort of 'short answer' questions.

How difficult is it?
I found all the readings really interesting, so it is not really a chore to do the readings. Even if you can't understand the reading, because the articles are usually very popular articles, you can google for a 'summary' or an explanation of the main arguments of the author :) This really helps for your summary assignments.

Miscallenous Tips
It is important to understand what each philosopher argues for. I find the main idea to be to know how to argue for each side, and the lectures and notes cover this really well. Study Prof Pelchzar's notes for your exams and you will do well :)

Should you take it?
I think Philo is as such - you either like it or you hate it. I know of plenty of people who really dread it and dislike it to bits, though I personally enjoy it and love the readings.
If you are a naturally inquisitive person or can construct arguments and understand both sides of the picture very well, this is the module for you.

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2010/11/nus-module-review-sem-1-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

What is it about?
A wide variety of topics from online communities, video games, copyright, development of the internet, etc. It is so broad that it is hard to describe it aptly in a paragraph.

How's the workload?
2 group projects, 1 individual project. 1 mid terms, 1 finals.
Readings are usually some sort of (outdated) journal articles, the length and how interesting it is varies greatly.
 1 2hr lecture per week, 1 1hr tutorial every week.
Mid terms consists of MCQ and short answer and essay. Finals are all MCQ.

How difficult is it?
The projects are generally fun and interesting to do. For instance, my individual project was about blogging! So fun haha.
Readings can be VERY VERY dry and complicated, and what I disliked most about them was that some were waaay oudated. (e.g. 10 years ago?) It was quite ridiculous to read about 'what the Internet is going to be' when it has already surpassed what was predicted.
Of course, there are several more interesting ones. But the readings are generally tough, I wouldd say.

Mid terms was REALLY easy, the MCQs were a total joke. I think a student who did not take the module would easily get half the questions correct as the way the options were written made the correct answer really obvious
E.g.
Which of these is a fruit?
a) chicken, b) apple, c) red, d) pajamas

The short answers and essay are slightly tougher but still manageable.
I think many people did well, so the bell curve naturally shifted higher.

Finals were the killer for my sem - the 60 odd MCQs were REALLY challenging. All the  options were very close and the 'application MCQs' (4 marks each mcq!) were quite difficult.

Miscallenous Tips
Read the LECTURE SLIDES. Seriously, it is a definite B at least if you read the lecture slides and study them religiously. To get an A of course you need to do well in your projects and do read the readings abit as some questions do test from readings.
However, I would say lecture slides are waaay important. They nicely capture all the important concepts you need to know so make sure you study them.

Should you take it?
Yes! Pretty interesting topics and generally slack module. The readings are not too heavy and not alot of studying is required (since revising lecture slides seems sufficient)
thread_title: NM1101E Communications, New Media and Society

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2010/11/nus-module-review-sem-1-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

*It is important to note that I heard this module varies GREATLY for Sem1 and Sem2 due to different lectureres. So, take these comments with a pinch of salt.*
What is it about?
Hypothesis testing, z tests, t tests, chisquare tests, correlation, regression, effect size, power.
I think I have just summed up all you need to study for your exams.
This module is an introduction to several tests used in psychology and how to obtain your results.

How's the workload?
Two textbooks, though you won't cover every chapter. Readings aren't very long, and are filled with examples to help you understand the concepts.
2 assignments (1 week deadline to complete for each), 1 closed book mid term mcq test, 1 open book finals (short answer questions)
one 2h lecture weekly, one 2hr tutorial fortnightly

How difficult is it?
This module is very concepts-based. Memorization is NOT the focus for this module - it is much more important to understand the concepts and when to use which formula.
Mid terms may require a little memory since it is closed book, but it's not very content heavy, just several key concepts which once you understand they won't be difficult to remember.
Finals are open book so it's all about knowing your content well and applying your concepts well.

Contrary to popular belief, good mathematics knowledge is NOT required. As long as you know how to multiply, add, subtract and square root (and square) on a calculator, you will be fine. It's also important to be able to recognize which formula to use for which situations.

Assignments are generally ok, though one of them may require an external program so will be a small hassle to download it and run it.

The most difficult thing of this module may be understanding the HK lecturer's accent HAHA. :( 

Miscalleneous Tips
I will repeat this - understanding the concepts are very very important! It's not about memorizing, but about applying the appropriate formula to the question. You should make a summary formula sheet for your finals for easy reference. 

Should you take it?
Yes, if it's Prof Mike teaching it. He sets really easy papers apparently (for my sem, the finals paper was almost identical to the paper 2 years ago -_- )
Not difficult at all, though the competition may be high as it has limited slots and it's a prerequisite for psych majors.
thread_title: PL2131 Research and Statistical Methods I

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2010/11/nus-module-review-sem-1-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

What is it about?
The title says it all - the module goes through a large variety of topics such as memory, learning, intelligence, developmental psych, abnormal psych, etcetc. If you have interest in how the brain works and what human behaviour is like, this is a module for you.

How's the workload?
The textbook is a monster - it was 800++ pages in total. (excluding references + appendix) Readings every week are usually one chapter, though you may have to read more in some weeks. Each chapter is about 40-60 pages. Do note that the textbook may change every sem(or year?) so it may be different.

There is one mid term paper (which you know the topic from about week 3 or 4, and is due about week 8?), a 100 MCQ mid term test, and a 100 MCQ + 5 short essay finals. (both closed book)

One 2h lecture per week (should be 6-8pm on monday as always) and one 2h tutorial fortnightly

How difficult is it?
The readings sound like alot but they are really not that bad. Firstly, the information is generally interesting to read and are not dry readings like journal articles. Next, there are many experiments and examples to aid your understanding, so it is not as long as it seems. The textbook is pretty easy to understand so there shouldnt be any problems here.

The exams are almost entirely memory-based - if you are able to memorize the textbook from the first to last page, you will very likely get a close-to-perfect mark. That said, understanding the concepts is quite important (versus just memorizing definitions) as the MCQs like to rephrase certain terms and see if you can pick the correct definition. Do try to read every thing from the textbook and remember the gist of everything, because they will test everything.

Miscalleneous Tips
This is one module in which you cannot fall behind in your readings - everything snowballs really quickly. Also, it's all about remembering everything, so ideally try to read your textbook at least twice over. Making a summary sheet for every chapter REALLY helps in revision.

Also, get started on your mid term essay early since they release the topic so early. The topic was fairly straightforward for this semester, not sure about future sems.

Lectures are an absolute waste of time, I stopped going for lectures in the second half of the semester. All they do is regurgitate what is stated in the textbook, and do a rushed job (because realistically all the content cannot be covered within a short 2hr lecture)
Tutorials are boring as well, but I guess it depends on which TA you get. No tutorial participation, so go ahead and switch off during tutorials :p

Should you take it?
A content heavy mod and should be balanced accordingly with whatever else you are taking. Not difficult at all to score if you have the time to keep up with readings. Known to be one of the most interesting exposure mods, and psychology is always interesting to read about. Go ahead and try it!

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/04/nus-module-review-semester-2-ay-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Prof: Dr Cha Yeow Siah 

What is it about?

Psychology with regards to social relationships and social situations. A lot of common sense theories and some theories that defy common sense. To give you a tease: brainstorming has been shown to result in a LOWER quality and quantity of ideas!

What is the workload?
1 standard textbook with about 1 chapter per week, 1 mid terms, 1 finals, 1 essay. Pretty standard and manageable.
Mid terms is 60 MCQ and 15 Fill-in-the-blanks.
Finals is 60 MCQ, 15 FOTB, 3 choose 2 short essay questions.

1 2h weekly lecture, 1 2 hr fortnightly tutorial. Both are a waste of time, tutorials are worse though. Prof Cha tries to make the lectures interesting with videos and stuff and he is mildly humorous, so it's not that bad, even though the lecture is more or less useless and you can just study the textbook.
Tutorials seem to be an afterthought - It's like because they can't NOT have tutorials, so they have them. Absolutely useless.

How difficult is it?

How well can you memorize? It's another memory module, though it helps if you understand the theories and concepts because you will have to apply them. For example, you learn what 'social facilitation theory' is, and an MCQ may then describe 4 scenarios and they ask you which is social facilitation theory.
You need to memorize the terms though, for the fill-in-the-blanks. Not just the definitions, but the terms.

Miscallenous Tips

You need to study lecture notes as well, because he tests stuff from there which he randomly puts in and is not in the textbook. Just the occasional terms.. So you need a friend to help you copy notes if you plan on ponning lectures.And remember to learn the TERMS not just the definition. So if I provide you with the desciption: "the tendency to not put in your full effort when working in a group" you should be able to respond "social loafing!"
This is exactly what the fill in the blanks tests. If you can recall terms by reading the definition/scenario.

Should you take it?

It is fairly interesting, learning about all the social phenomena and learning some stuff which may contradict conventional thinking. I liked it, just that the textbook that Prof Cha chose SUCKS BALLS. It's the worst textbook in the world - authors Baumeister and Bushman
Very disorganized, very random examples, random pictures, etc.How did I do for it?
For midterms, I neglected to memorize the terms and  thus i couldn't recall what the definitions. Hence my memory couldn't recall what the terms were when shown the definitions. So don't make this mistake!
Did well for the essay, average for the mid terms.. hope I do well for the finals :S

Predicted Grade: B+
Actual Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/04/nus-module-review-semester-2-ay-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Lecturer - Hanoi Lok

What is it about?

A very theory-based module on the key aspects of employee management - such as training and development, performance appraisal/management, etc.
Don't expect to learn anything factual such as how Singapore companies do this this this etc, that's not what this mod is about.
It is very general and theoretical which I thought was quite dumb, since alot of it is common sense. For example, 'what is an employee like today as compared to in the past?'. You can probably smoke out something like 'oh more demanding, want flexi-hours, want work-life balance, etc'.

How's the workload?

1 group project which involves conducting an interview with an organization, a presentation, and a report.
No midterms, 1 open book final exam, which is basically GP style. 3 choose 2 essays.There is one textbook which is virtually useless.. I didn't touch it after the first 2 weeks because its contents are quite useless. Lectures are very important - the module only has one weekly 3hour lecture which is a small-group seminar style. There is class participation, so try to speak up.The project is manageable, provided your group doesn't suck. There is virtually no readings to be done, just ... listen in class and think.

How difficult is it?

What I really dislike about the module is that what you learn in lectures is not really assessed in the finals! Or rather, the format of the final exams doesn't adequately and accurately assess what you were taught.
The issues we were asked to think about during class are not really helpful to writing the essays in the finals.
The essays are really GP style, and you need to have alot of examples so that you can support it well. Of course, knowing the basic theory is necessary, so read your lecture notes.The lectures were quite interesting because Hanoi is a funny lecturer and shares alot of her personal experiences. However, as I said, it doesn't map onto the final exams, so I found this module more or less useless.

Miscallenous Tips

I don't know. Read newspapers and cut out articles? Learn how to write a good essay? Keeping up with current affairs regarding employee management will definitely help you write a better essay.

Should you take it?

No. Take another SS. Unless you're good at essay writing, then go for it.How did I do for it?
My group more or less screwed up the presentation, and more or less got a sub-par report, and I more or less smoked my way through the finals. I'm PRAYING for a C so I can S/U this mod. plsplspls.

Predicted Grade: C

Actual Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/04/nus-module-review-semester-2-ay-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Prof: Travellia Tjokro

What is it about?

The main part of it is all about the brain - how different functions such as memory and emotions link to the brain. If you like learning biological stuff like how suprachiasmatic nuclei controls the circadian rhythms of the body, this is for you.

How's the workload?

One Biological Psych textbook, which to me is the same as two normal textbooks. Haha ok maybe i'm biased, I don't like bio much. But it's basically pure memory work.

1 mid terms, 1 finals, 2 presentations to do on 2 different journal articles (pair work).

one 2h lecture weekly, one 2hr tutorial fortnightly.
Tutorials are basically useless besides for you to do your presentations. You do nothing in weeks that you don't present besides sit there and listen to the other groups.
Lectures are.. more or less useless too. She just teaches stuff from the textbook so, and if i'm not wrong there's nothing from the notes that isn't in the textbook, apart from the occasional term here and there. You may want to have a look at her notes just to be safe. Totally ponnable imo.

How difficult is it?

I don't know, how good is your  memory?
I didn't feel like it tested my application much, just memorize and memorize. I mean, it's bio!

Miscalleneous Tips

Do read your textbook thoroughly - the finals' essay questions were EACH more or less asking you regurgitate 3 studies outlined in the textbook. So if you memorized the textbook you'll ace it.

Should you take it?

It's a core mod for Psych majors/minors so you have no choice. If you like Biology, why not. There's not alot on genes though, so if you want that, go try Genes and Society (Some GEK)How did I do for it?
Did only average for mid terms, I didn't memorize enough. I do selective memorizing so ... you need to memorize everything. :S And don't think my presentations were good. :( Finals was so-so. I could remember the 3 'empirical evidence' for the essay portion though so I guess it's good.

Predicted Grade: B

Actual Grade: B- :(

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/04/nus-module-review-semester-2-ay-1011.html

Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Prof: Sim Teck Ngee

What is it about?

A continuation from PL2131, this teaches you that everything you have learn in PL2131 is useless and you should use what you learn in PL2132.

Ok fine, it teaches you more advanced statistical techniques besides the t-tests that you've been learning in PL2131.

How's the workload?

There is a whopping THREE textbooks for this module - however, I daresay you can clear this module without even touching any of them, IF you are taking it under Prof Sim.

In fact, this module was probably the one with one of the lowest workloads this semester, apart from PH2110. Read the next section for an elaboration.

There is one mid term exam (short answer), 2 small quizzes (MCQ), 1 final exam, and 1 experimental study (plan and design a simple experiment as a group, carry it out, and write an individual report).

It's not as much as it sounds, because the mid terms and finals are both open book, and the quizzes are short.

One 2h lecture per week and one 2h lab every week

How difficult is it?

After scaring you with the fact that there are 3 textbooks, I have to clarify something.

Firstly, one of the textbooks is written in a very readable, casual, humorous way (but is also the thickest) (andy fields), one of them has information that you should already know from PL2131 (black book), and the last one is probably the most important and small one, but also the most complicated one. (ANOVA)

Textbooks aside, the way Prof Sim conducts this module is that he focuses on UNDERSTANDING why we you are doing what you're doing, rather than becoming proficient at using all the complicated formula in ANOVA (there are alot which he didn't even touch).

Also, for him, the textbooks are supplementary, meaning he just tests whatever he teaches during lectures (and is in the lecture notes). Thus lectures are VERY important.

It also helps that he is a VERY good lecturer, in that he is very clear and deliberate in his explanations. If you can't understand when he lectures, you won't understand when anyone else does.

He also seems to pick out only what he deems important from the textbook and teaches them, so you don't have to read the entire textbook(s). When I compare with other years' exam papers, his are definitely the easiest ones. (which may or may not be a good thing)

As for the experiment, it's just designing a simple experiment that you will carry out on your own as a group. Of course, your design needs to be free of confounds, etc. The report is an individual assignment though - and I suggest you work on it properly.

Miscalleneous Tips

Study the lecture notes. Go for lectures. Study the lecture notes again. He only tests from his notes! (you need to understand the concepts well to answer the questions, so PERHAPS reading the textbook MAY help. -shrugs-.)

Should you take it?

If you have taken PL2131, I suppose you will have to do this module too. If you haven't, you can't do this module. So ... it's either you end up taking or you don't. It's not a choice. Haha. But yea it's pretty easy overall, assuming you're under Prof Sim. I don't know about other lecturers.

How did I do for it?

I screwed up the report for the experiment, probably because I didn't invest as much time in it as I should. I started on it early and ended early and never made continual improvements. Only got a C+ for it which was devastating, given my performance in the other areas have been stellar - I'm pretty sure I got A/A+ level marks for my quizzes + mid terms. (way above median score)

Finals was relatively easy.

Predicted grade: B+

Actual grade: A-
thread_title: PL2132 Research and Statistical Methods II

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/11/nus-module-review-semester-1-ay-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Prof: Dr Cha Yeow Siah
What is it about?

The study of intergroup issues and processes, such as cohesion, conflict and structure.

What is the workload?

1 mid terms, 1 finals, in classic Cha Yeow Siah style - mid terms was MCQ + fill in the blanks, finals was MCQ+fillintheblanks+casestudy+short answer questions.
1 standard textbook.. quite readable and good textbook IMO.One 2 hour lecture a week, one 2 hour tutorial every fortnight.One group project - This is really fun, you basically can choose to do ANY group activity. (E.g. go play frisbee, jam, cook, skate, play LAN, etc) and after that you write a report on the group activity, applying your group dynamics concepts. You also have to present your activity, and try to do it in a creative way, because alot of other groups will do so and it makes a difference I think. My group did this:

(Our activity was cooking)

How difficult is it?

The theory is pretty much logical and very easy to understand as it's things you already observe in everyday life, just attaching a term to it.
The difficult part could be that most people are accustomed to recalling the definitions of terms (e.g. Define Photosynthesis) instead of the actual terms itself. The Fill-in-the-blanks recalls you to recall the TERM as they provide a description/definition so if you didn't encode your information well.. good bye marks.
Weightage of the FOTB questions are quite high too, like MCQ is one question 0.5 marks, but FOTB can be 1-1.5 marks each.The group project is probably one of the most fun projects in NUS, as long as your group decides to make it fun :)

Miscallenous Tips

If you want to be in the same group as your friends, for the first tutorial when he does this 'network analysis' thing, say you don't know your friend. Because he will deliberately split you guys up ;)Have fun with your group project, and do a good presentation. I really think this matters. And of course do your report properly.Remember to memorize the terms and be able to link a term to a concept, because you have to do this for the case study question in the finals too.

Should you take it?

Yes, a very fun mod overall, I enjoyed it. Relatively simple compared to chim things like cognitive psych, so you should take it.

How did I do for it?
I didn't do well for mid terms because I didn't encode the terms properly as I said - just couldn't recall the terms with the description. Scored slightly below median.
My group's project got an A-, which was (supposedly) because both our report and presentation (i.e. the video above) was good.
I'm hoping my finals pull my mid terms up!

Predicted Grade: B+
Actual Grade: B+

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/11/nus-module-review-semester-1-ay-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Prof: Dr Chua Foo Kee

What is it about?

Another core mod for Psych majors. You'll learn about the mind and how it operates with regards to perception, memory, object recognition, etc.

What is the workload?
1 textbook. But this textbook REALLY sucked. Quinlan and Dyson. Terrible. Alot of random useless material and it's not very well structured. I get a headache everytime I do my Cog Psych readings, dunno if it's because the subject is difficult or the TB sucks.
Oh, some weeks have multiple chapters readings.
1 2 hour lecture per week, with fortnightly 2 hour tutorials.
2 class tests (both short answer, 1 open 1 closed book), 1 finals (2 questions, essay)

How difficult is it?

I don't know if Cognitive Psychology is just difficult or it was the textbook's problem. I think it was a bit of both. It doesn't help that the prof sets pretty tough papers too.
His finals paper was considerably easier than previous sems' though, but I think previous sems' one just looked hard because we weren't taught in the same way they were.

Miscallenous Tips

These tips are probably relevant for CFK (the prof) only - remember the experiments+results that support the different concepts and theories (even if they're not the best/correct one), he may ask you to describe one.
Try to recognize what's the useless information and which is important. I was quite selective in my memorizing because alot of stuff was waaay over my head and felt useless to learn. He didn't test those things.
(one example is there was this 2 page+ section detailing Bayes Theorem, a mathematical formula. WTF? )

Should you take it?

Textbook was horrible, Dr Chua is really eccentric so... if you like that kinda thing go for it.
My tutor was HORRIBLE though, just some random Masters student (who was specializing in Clinical Psych) who couldn't answer a single question we had.
You really don't have a choice with this mod (core), so try to take it under a good prof.How did I do for it?
I'm not sure if my cohort was slack or something, because I somehow scored pretty well for my tests in relative terms. For instance, 70/100 doesn't seem very good but the mean was 52 ><
I got way above mean for both my tests, but I didn't think the questions were super hard or anything was quite straightforward? I mean, I know if it's a hard test or not, and while it's hard because Cognitive psych is hard, I don't think the tests were really hard.
Not sure how I'll do for finals, but I'm hoping for an A.

Predicted Grade: A-
Actual Grade: A-

author: NUSMods
message:
Module review by Alan Teo: http://crazerk.blogspot.sg/2011/11/nus-module-review-semester-1-ay-1112.html

Taken in AY11/12 Sem 1

If you have any questions, please send it to my email atqhteo@gmail.com. Don't worry, there are no stupid questions! I've received many emails asking me all sorts of questions. I reply all of them :)

Lecturer - Gregor Lange

What is it about?

One of the 5 core modules for Psychology majors. It is about.. psychological disorders. That's pretty much it. Every chapter involves the Symptoms/Main Characteristics, Causes, Statistics, Treatment for each disorder.

How's the workload?

1 standard psych textbook, about 1 chapter every week. Manageable load. For this sem he even removed one entire chapter because of public holiday that week. woohoo!
1 casebook which I didn't even touch apart from the chapter that I had to present on. Not necessary to read it.
1 mid terms (60 MCQ), 1 class presentation, 1 finals (60 MCQ + 4 short answer). All closed book.
One 2 hour lecture every week and fortnightly 2hour tutorials.
(Tutorials are mega waste of time because you basically just listen to the other groups presenting)Lectures are pretty much pointless since everything is from the textbook, but the Dr Gregor is fairly interesting and humorous so it makes it worth going for.

How difficult is it?

Quite memory-based subject, just need to memorize the textbook.
Exams do test your application of the knowledge though, such as identifying what disorder it is from a description of a patient.

Miscallenous Tips

Memorize the textbook. Organize your notes according to the same structure - Characteristics, Causes, Stats, Treatment. Helps you to remember better (Some chapters swap it up so it's quite stupid)

Should you take it?

If you are a Psych major, you dont' have a choice. If you're not... you can't take it unless you take the pre-reqs which will mean you're minoring or something. It's not that hard a module, just quite memory heavy.

How did I do for it?
Didn't do very well for mid terms.. not sure why. I did study , guess I didn't get those ambiguous questions right. No idea how the presentation went, the tutor didn't give feedback at all.
Finals felt alright, but after talking to a few friends they thought it was easy so I guess bell curve will be steep.

Predicted Grade : B-
Predicted Grade : B+
