I feel that in physics, the problems that we tackle have very clear constraints and underlying phenomena, and we don’t really need some abstract algorithm to solve them for us. It’ll be faster to resolve the underlying mechanics, or otherwise, brute-force searches/iterative/gradient methods are generally sufficient. I would instead strongly recommend CS3244, as a class that teaches more practical and ubiquitous algorithms.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: GEH1056 Cultural Diversity in the Contemporary World Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1 (22 MCs) For more reviews, check out https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer Dr. Jennifer Estes Teaching assistant Norazim Bin Azami Grade Distribution Participation: 20% Reading Responses: 30% Quizzes: 30% Book Review: 20% Contents Cultural Anthropology Culture Migration Political Organisation Political Economy Class Gender and Sexuality Ethnicity and Race Kinship Religion Lecture (One 2 hr Lecture / Week) The few lectures I attended were pretty interesting, with Estes bringing in many real-world examples and case studies to help illustrate her points. She engages the class using quizzes during the lecture time (which counts towards participation), which also lets students throw in their ideas of what is relevant to the lecture. Overall, a fairly fun and engaging class. Tutorial (One 2 hr Session / Fortnight) Tutorials were mostly discussions about the reading material provided as course texts. We split up into groups and think about the questions provided in class, and how they relate to our other course material. Azim goes around making sure that all groups are on task, and often highlights out interesting discussion points being brought up. Reading Responses Due every two weeks, we had to do a quick analysis on the required readings over the past fortnight. It was annoying because I’m not used to how flowery social sciences texts can be, and it was difficult to grasp the ideas behind the readings from a quick skim. Could have probably done better for these if I bothered to read the material more thoroughly. Quizzes There are two quizzes, which effectively serves as a “midterms” and “finals” for this module. The format was closed-book MCQ, and was reasonable, given that you revised the content. Book Review We were given a few choices of books to read through, and provide a book review. The idea behind the review was fairly straightforward, if not a little annoying. The idea behind the review is very, very different from what we usually see in STEM. What I would call a good review in STEM/communications would be considered a poor review in this module, and vice-versa. That took a little while to digest. To be fair, I had similar disorientations with how political science (GES) writes their essays as well, but it’s standard in their field I assume. The books themselves were extremely annoying to obtain, with many dead links and fake sites, on both the library and Google. Honestly, didn’t bother to read the book thoroughly, so I did as poorly as expected. A minor peeve I had after receiving the review feedback is that being below the word limit was apparently a demerit point to be marked down. Silly rubrics. Expected Grade: B Final Grade: tbc Thoughts Overall an interesting module, which introduces me to many new ideas and concepts that I would normally never encounter in my field of studies. I took the class with full intention of SU-ing it, so my experience would likely be very different from what a more engaged student would have.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC4230 Quantum Mechanics III Disclaimer: Am working on a quantum FYP, and so my perspective is from a student who is relatively very well-versed in a large portion of the module content For more reviews, check out Physics Life at NUS Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1 (22 MCs) Lecturer Prof. Gong Jiangbin Grade Distribution Problem Sets (4): 40% Small Project: 30% Finals: 30% Contents Perturbation Theory (Recap) Time-Independent Degenerate Time-Dependent Quantum Dynamics Computational Approaches Discrete Variable Representation Discrete Fourier Transform Split-Operator Method Adiabatic Approximation Triple-Well Matter Transport Stimulated Raman Adiabatic Passage Berry Phase Shortcut-to-Adiabaticity Hamiltonian Engineering Open Quantum Systems Density Matrices Decoherence Models Dynamical Decoupling Born-Markov Master Equation Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) Gong is a good lecturer, taking the time to dive deep into the fundamentals before moving forward with his planned lecture content. He takes the time to carefully derive every step of the material, ensuring that each step is clear and well justified. He also takes the time to pause for breaks, to answer any questions that any students might have along the way. In terms of module content, I think that if you intend to go deeper into quantum engineering, everything covered here is highly relevant and up-to-date with the state-of-the-art experimental techniques. A lot of what was covered in class, I have seen in some form or another in my final year project He began slow, going over the fundamentals of perturbation theory (a recap of PC3130), while simultaneously diving deeper into the derivations. As we moved on into the new content, the pace remained the same, which was good for those of us who are treading into unfamiliar territory. There were some points were his class time was delegated for matlab simulations. I thought this was quite good, to ensure that the students had some hands-on experience. The main code was provided, and we just had to make some small modifications to investigate some approximations and key ideas in class. Lectures aren’t recorded, and derivations aren’t on his lecture slides. If you choose to skip class, you will definitely be missing out on content that will be important for the graded components of the module. My only pet peeve was the choice of location for the lecture. He opted to use a small classroom as the teaching location, presumably for the whiteboard. I thought it was small and quite warm, but his choice I guess. Problem Sets There were 4 problem sets given over the semester. Most of them were fairly chill, and can be completed quite quickly (within a day). They generally covered a minor extension of the lecture material, applied to a unique Hamiltonian, or under various constraints. The ones I spent more time on are the matlab-derived sets, more due to the language barrier than anything. He also returns the submissions with feedback, so that was great for clearing up misunderstandings Project A matlab project, to apply some of the concepts and principles of class to a model Hamiltonian. It is basically identical to the problem set, but is slightly more open-ended, and requires a report submission. Honestly, the matlab part was a huge struggle, but he was okay with students using python as well. That was a huge relief and time-saver. Finals Felt dependent on how well you made your cheat sheet. The questions were fairly okay, although brushing up on quick ways to diagonalize matrices would be good (or include common diagonalized Hamiltonians on your cheat sheet). Overall an average paper, nothing too tricky. Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: tbc Thoughts Workload was fairly light for a physics module, and time was quite flexible. Having this was very helpful for a final year student working on their FYP at odd hours. I like how relevant the module is to my own FYP. Reading up on state-of-the-art quantum protocols, then finding the basis for the physics behind them was really helpful for learning. Some examples would be Hamiltonian engineering for optimal control pulses, quantum error correction protocols, various decoherence models, and adiabatic control of quantum systems. I think that if you intend to continue working in practical quantum implementations, especially especially in experimental quantum computing, the concepts covered in the class will be very helpful.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: For those interested in theoretical physics in any form, you should consider taking this module. It covers plenty of what you need to equip yourself with. I'm only disappointed that it doesn't seem to touch upon quantisation of classical systems and classical field theory. The Gap in NUS Physics Signed, A busybody physics senior who has been waiting for a module like this to come out since forever

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC3233 Atomic & Molecular Physics I Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 (24 MCs) For the updated review and more, check out https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer Prof. Kai Dieckmann Teaching assistant Yang Anbang Grade Distribution Groupwork and Class Participation: 40% Student Presentation: 20% Finals: 40% Contents Basic Atomic Structure Quantum Angular Momentum The Hydrogen Atom Angular Momentum II The Hydrogen Atom II Atoms in External Magnetic Fields Multielectronic Atoms Diatomic Molecules Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) This year, Kai opted for a flipped classroom format, where we have to watch pre-recorded lectures before coming to class. Since the content was fairly similar to PC3130, I didn’t bother with it. Since attendance was graded, people showed up most of the time. Towards the end of the semester I got fairly busy, and so missed a couple of classes. Class time instead became a random discussion time where we were forced to do assignments in groups. I personally felt that this was quite silly, since most of us didn’t actually spend class time doing the assignments. The assignments were not particularly… discussion-worthy, and far too tedious to complete during class. We instead spent a while discussing approaches to the problem, then moving on the miscellaneous chitchat. The assignment answers were gone through in class the week after we submitted them. The latter half of the semester also saw some in-class quizzes to ensure that we were on task in terms of lecture content. Was fairly guessable even without watching lectures. There were also student presentations during class time. I thought that this was good – incorporating presentation skills as part of the course was quite helpful. Student Presentation Kai selected some papers for us, from which we were to choose a one to present a summary on. Alternatively, you could propose your own topic of presentation, as long as it was related to AMO physics. The presentation was done during lecture hours, with 4 students per lecture slot. Some Q&A was done after each presentation. I think it was quite interesting to listen to the various branches of AMO physics presented by our peers. This offers us the opportunity to learn more about the various esoteric corners of this field without digging through them ourselves. At the same time, we had to self-learn our own presentation topics, which is an important skill if you intend to pursuit further studies. If you choose a topic that you are quite interested in, I believe you’ll find that preparing the presentation is quite an enjoyable process. Finals Was held live, closed-book, with a single A4 cheatsheet. The questions were not too challenging, and there was enough time to complete the paper. I felt that a thorough cheatsheet was more important than a thorough understanding of the course material for the paper. Overall a little trickier than most physics papers I’ve taken. Some time was spent wondering what the paper wanted from us. There was a strange question that asked us to draw the particles that constitute a Magnesium atom. You can imagine how there may be some confusion over this. Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: To be Updated Thoughts I think that the overlap of this module with its pre-requisite is quite extensive. The overall structure – laying out the pathway from the hydrogen atom, to higher order corrections, to multi-electronic atoms, and molecules – was reasonable. However, it is quite clear that the latter half is covered in every introductory quantum mechanics course, and so perhaps less time could be spent there. The actual new content was passed over fairly quickly, with few exercises for practice. Some of it was algorithmic, and so was handwaved through fairly quickly, with the implicit understanding that we don’t really need to know them. The most annoying thing is, in terms of time, this module is the heaviest. Exercises are fairly tedious, without actually delivering much in terms of learning. Graded attendance, pre-recorded lectures, assignments, and a presentation – all these together constitute a large chunk of time. Even going in knowing the content, you will have to be prepared to spend some time here. If you are in it for the learning, this module doesn’t do itself justice. If you want low-brainpower grades, this module can technically be grinded, given its grade distribution. Overall, not much was learnt in terms of physics content given the time-investment. No doubt the course is structured to give students a thorough understanding of the foundations. However, given that the foundations are already covered in introductory quantum courses, perhaps it should be a preclusion choice with PC3130. I would daresay that for those with an interest in AMO physics, perhaps you could skip this one and just hop straight into its successor module (if it still exists).

author: Rain and Coffee
message:
PC4245 Particle Physics For the updated review and more, check out https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 (24 MCs) Lecturer Prof. Oh Choo Hiap Grade Distribution Project (Report): 5% Class Participation (Presentation Summary): 5% Midterms: 30% Finals: 60% Contents Introduction Relativistic Kinematics Symmetries Bound States Decays and Scattering QED QCD (Not really covered) Introduction to Gauge Theories (Not covered) Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week)
You don’t need to understand the physics
~ Prof. Oh, 2022 The classes are held live. Interestingly enough, CH prints out his lecture notes, then uses the overhead projector to flash his printed out copy on the screen. He also has handwritten workings as additional lecture notes, which he will scan and place on LumiNUS. The lectures aren’t recorded. I would recommend not going for classes. His notes are very obscure and workings are hard to decipher. It should be sufficient just reading from Griffiths (Introduction to Elementary Particles). The lecturer does add plenty of additional content that isn’t covered in the textbook. However, the way he covers them was undecipherable, and so there’s no point listening to it. At the start of the semester, he has a tendency to spend the first 30 – 60 minutes of class ranting about how we need to ask him questions, about how we need to take charge of our own education, etc. He then proceeds to get upset when no one has any questions to ask. When he finally begins with lecture content, he then spends the longest time on trivial things that are covered in the prerequisites for the module, up till his timer rings. This timer rings at the T-15 minute mark of class. It also demarcates the time when he begins rushing through his “extra content”, sometimes overrunning his time for the class. Occasionally, students would ask him questions in class about his extra content. He answers them with facts that are utterly irrelevant to the question. If provoked further, he takes the special effort to shut the student down, deeming their question as irrelevant. Towards week 3 onwards, he decided the lecture engagement can be improved by adding an additional component. Students are to spend the first 15 mins or so doing a recap of the previous class. This was definitely an improvement over his ranting over how we choose not to learn. At least, we can all learn by having one of us sacrificing time to deciphering his hieroglyphic scribbles. He also loves democracy. He was kind enough to allow us to vote on the distribution between the (a) project report, (b) midterms, (c) presentation summary, splitting up the last 40% of our grades. We all voted, then he comes back the following lecture with his own preferred grade distribution. We were told to self-read Bound States in Griffiths, and that only the last part is important for the following chapter (Decays and Scattering). He says that he will not go over them in class. He then proceeds to spend 2 weeks covering this exact chapter. It comes out for finals in the end His only redeeming feature is that he does take time to provide tutorial questions, which he then goes through in class. The questions are taken from Griffiths, and the answers are google-able, so no worries if you decide not to attend class. Minor peeve – his notation is different from most other sources, so you tend to muddle up the distinction between similar objects, e.g., four-vectors and vectors, metric tensor signature, etc. I’m sure his knowledge far exceeds what I know, and there is much to learn, but his inability to answer questions or explain concepts makes the point moot. Project To be very honest, I don’t know what he wants for this. We were asked to write a report on this article detailing the overthrow of parity. I’m not sure if he wants a summary, my thoughts, a critical review, or a literature review. At this point, I couldn’t be bothered to ask him any more questions, and since he made it 5%, I just winged it. In the end, he gave everyone full marks as long as you submitted something coherent. Presentation Summary To even book the presentation, you need to do a lot of back-and-forth engagements with the lecturer. He INSISTS that you choose a date for your session and email him. Only then, he will tell you whether that day has been taken by another student. If you ask for the available dates, he will ask you to send him a date. I spent several frustrating emails going back-and-forth for a date and got absolutely nowhere. I ended up waiting until the end of the semester to book a timeslot with him. This in itself had its own demerits since now I had to present the more challenging rear topics. I got absolutely destroyed covering the introduction of QED. Midterms A physical closed book assessment. The questions are more-or-less related to the Griffiths content, and doing the tutorial questions are sufficient practice. There was sufficient time to complete the questions. There are some questions that I felt were slightly out of syllabus, but whatever. Finals A physical closed book assessment. The test covers the remaining content from the course (bound states, decay and scattering, QED, QED of quarks). It was a choose-3-from-4 format, so you can pick whichever chapter you are more familiar with. There was sufficient time to do the paper, and think about the questions that you cannot do. The past-year-papers are a good indicator of what will likely come out. However, you will find that the questions can be ridiculously difficult. It was fortunate our year had one of the easier papers IMO. Expected Grade: B+ Final Grade: tbc Thoughts There is no point taking this module. You could learn as much just reading Griffiths. The point of taking a module is in hopes of learning more from the lecturer, for them to explain concepts you cannot understand on your own. This lecturer cannot provide either of those services. If you still insist on taking the module, I would recommend skipping classes and read Griffiths on your own. A minor mercy is that you don’t really need PC3232 to take this module, so waiver it if you are just interested in this module. An upside is that the lecturer only tests content from Griffiths, and so this is easily one of my lowest workload modules. I just spend an hour or two reading a chapter, and I would have covered 2 weeks worth of content. Mileage may vary depending on passion and familiarity with quantum mechanics. You could consider taking this module, just to clear level 4000 requirements. A further disappointment of mine is that he spent exceedingly long on CPT symmetries, which are frankly, the easiest part of this module. We could be going on to QED or QCD, but no, we spend weeks deciding on the parity of particles. To his credit, he does realise the general interest in these two topics, and makes the effort to cover them in the final class. We covered up to section 8.1: QED of quarks. Additionally, I was hoping to pry more into the deepest cracks at reality. If you see my review on PC3232, I was still hopeful for a theoretical breakdown on particles. A fact I had to come to terms with is that, particle physics is a phenomenological field. Top-notch research in this field is simply staring at the particle collisions, and then doing data analysis. The physics in this field is completely observational or empirical in nature, and theories are utterly irrelevant. Perhaps I haven’t learnt enough (and I won’t from NUS), but it seems like even at the highest level, this field is not a field where much thinking is involved. Even flipping CERN’s notes, you will find that great importance is places in interpreting collision data rather than fitting to theoretical models and predictions. If you are looking for a theoretical treatment of particle physics, you could perhaps look to Qing Hai’s PC5206 Selected Topics in QFT. For a slightly better module on phenomenological particle physics, you can consider University of Geneva’s course on Coursera. It’s free for NUS students for now (2022).

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC3274 Mathematical Methods in Physics II Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 (24 MCs) For the updated review and more, check out: https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer Dr. Edward Teo Grade Distribution Quizzes: 10% Term Tests: 40% Finals: 50% Contents Complex Variables Complex Integration Calculus of Variables Group Theory Representation Theory Tensors Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) The lectures are very thorough and Dr. Edward explains the concepts step-by-step such that it is very understandable. Most of what he talks about are already present in his notes, but he occasionally throws in tidbits of information that clarifies certain ideas. He also allocates some lecture time to going through the tutorial questions. Overall, a good lecturer that has a clear presentation of ideas and concepts. Quizzes The quizzes are a more-or-less weekly affair, which acts as a application/re-cap of the content that was taught across the week. It’s usually not too challenging, and it really helps to clear up how much of the content you understand. Term Tests and Finals They are all essentially in the same format, open-book with 2 to 4 questions to answer. We had two term tests, covering two topics each, followed by a final exam which covered the whole module content. Similar to PC2134 (Mathematical Methods in Physics I), the questions are quite computationally heavy, testing not only your understanding of the topics, but also your patience and carefulness. Expected Grade: B/B+ Final Grade: To be updated Thoughts The module is similar to it’s precursor module, going into the mathematics that you may encounter in your other physics modules, but were not properly explained. It was quite interesting to delve deeper into how and why we do things in certain ways. The module content, compared to almost all the other physics modules, is quite tame. Having only six chapters rather than twenty (looking at you, PC3232) is a breath of fresh air. It is really different when you can actually take the time to study in detail a few ideas at a comfortable stress-free pace, rather than die-cram-vomit content that NUS likes to present. If only more modules can take this approach… On the other hand, sometimes I wonder if there could be more application-questions to be encountered in the module. Perhaps it was designed to complement the other modules you are taking. Regardless, this module presents more theoretical math questions, rather than esoteric physics questions that we see in PC2134. A minor detail that I am a little disappointed in.
thread_title: PC3274 Mathematical Methods in Physics II

author: Rain and Coffee
message: CS3244 Machine Learning Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 (24 MCs) Full module review and more here: https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer Bryan Low and Daren Ler Grade Distribution Graded Tutorial, Tutorial Attendance, LumiNUS Quiz: 5% Term Project: 25% Midterm Assessment: 30% Final Assessment: 40% Contents Concept Learning Decision Trees Neural Networks Clustering (Not Tested) Hypothesis Evaluation (Not Tested) Bayesian Inference Computational Learning Theory (Not Tested) Lecture (1 + 2 hr Lectures / Week) The lectures are held over zoom. Bryan took the first half of the semester, while Daren took the latter half. Both lecturers are okay. Daren also serves as a teaching assistant (not sure about Bryan but I had Daren), and is rather caring about the well-being and understanding of his students. The lecture notes were quite clear. I think the challenge comes in distilling out the useful information from the decorations. I would dare say you could go through the module by just reading off the lecture slides and skipping classes. I would still recommend that you attend classes, or at least watch the recordings. The lecturers occasionally drop tidbits of information that make understandings about the concepts clearer. Plenty of the other reviewers seem to have a poor opinion of Bryan, but I found that he was quite clear and concise in his explanations. Certainly not horrendous enough to recommend people switch semesters just to avoid him. The contents are rather theoretical, and less geared towards applications, but this was not beyond what was expected of a CS module. I struggled with Bayesian inference, but it wasn’t too bad. The lecturers do occasionally wander slightly beyond the module syllabus. They sometimes expand on explanations with ideas from beyond the notes, or bring in further applications and practical engineering of algorithms. It’s great that they show their passion for the topics, but no harm will come to you if you choose to tune them out. There are diagnostic quizzes that are graded, due on the weekend. These are placed to make sure students stay on task. They are generally also not to hard if you attended class. Regardless, they are like, less than 1% of your grade, so feel free to just YOLO them. Tutorial As mentioned, I had Daren as my tutor. He’s a thorough explainer, making sure to illustrate ideas and concepts with diagrams. He also takes the time to answer questions that students may have. The tutorials are fairly easy. They also go through the tutorial answers before asking for submissions, so you should just go grab that 5%. Having to travel to computing was a pain though. I guess we’re all used to asynchronous classes by this point. Project Done in groups of 2 + 3. I felt that… the lecturers are kind of out of touch with what is possible for students to do. They go on endlessly about the different possible interesting ways you can apply machine learning, without considering the practicalities of it. They stress that a good project is one that is catered to a specific need/problem. We should ideally start by choosing a problem, before proposing our project. They even go to the extent to create a theme (“Singapore!”) that we must all adhere to. But let’s be very honest. We all know that the hardest part of embarking on any machine learning project is procuring your data. There’s a reason why companies love to sell your data. Where on earth are broke students supposed to get organized data suitable for practical machine learning? So of course we start by looking for data before proposing a problem. My main gripe with this is that the lecturers are clearly looking out for innovative projects. But I think this is not appropriate for an introductory machine learning module. Of course good projects should be rewarded, but not at the expense of people who are learning ML for the first time. The project should take on a more hands-on case study direction, where students try their hand at applying the concepts we learnt into code. The lecturer’s misplaced enthusiasms aside, given that all you had to submit was a report, there isn’t too much onus to doing it perfect rather than just finishing something. Midterms Covered the first three topics. There were 5 questions – one of which is the proving question that you should leave to the end. I think you can safely leave that question for method marks to decide your fate. If you take the time to complete the tutorials, you should be sufficiently prepared for the midterms. If you take the time to chew through the sample midterms, you can definitely clear the paper. If you take the time to do more past-year papers… maybe you can do the proving question. In short, the paper was honestly easier than expected. Finals Covers the same content as midterms, with the addition of Bayesian inference. Again, wasn’t too hard. The tutors prepared some tutorial questions that turned out to be fairly similar to what came out, so if you went over those, you’re in luck. I’ve come to realise that the proving questions focuses a lot of the FIND-S/G algorithms, so as long as you are fairly familiar with them, writing a guess down shouldn’t be too challenging. Working out neural network weights by hand was a pain. I’m sure there’s an intelligent way to go about it, but with too little sleep I resorted to brute forcing values to get the desired target function. I spent to most amount of time on this question. Overall, again, easier than expected. Expected Grade: A-/B+ Final Grade: to be updated Thoughts Despite not being a CS major, this is best module of my semester. The contents are understandable within a single sitting, and the lecturers are clear and succinct. Homework is a 30 mins affair, don’t know what the other reviewers are complaining about. Project aside, this is the lowest workload module on my plate this semester. Don’t need to go out of your way to avoid Bryan teaching the module. He’s not even close to how the other reviewers describe him. Or perhaps he has changed his pedagogy since the last round. For Daren, it is his first time teaching the module, and it feels strange that the chapters that are covered by him were not tested at all. Overall, both lecturers were equally good. As a physics major, I found the Bayesian chapter the most interesting. Some of the ideas and algorithms that are presented are highly reminiscent of the models that we use in statistical / quantum mechanics. It isn’t too strange in hindsight, given their common origin in probability theory. All the same, it was a surprisingly pleasant encounter with these ideas again. Overall, an interesting module. It presents some basic ideas of ML algorithms and ways to evaluate them. Workload was quite manageable, and content wasn’t too tough. Would recommend to take if you want some knowledge in being able to write and evaluate your own ML projects.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC4228 Device Physics for Quantum Technology Taken in AY21/22 Sem 2 (24 MCs) For more information and other reviews, check out: https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer Prof. Alexander Ling Grade Distribution Tests: 50% Experimental: 25% Peer Review 5% Seminar Report & Interview: 25% Contents - Basic Optics - Gaussian Beams - Polarisation - Polarization Elements - Polarimetry - Polarization Devices - Transmission Methods - Lasers and Detectors - Single Photon Detectors - Quantum Random Number Generator (QRNG) - Spin-Parametric Down Converter (SPDC) - Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) - Quantum Computing and Sensing Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) The content itself covers a wide range of topics, that are commonly encountered in quantum laboratories. This leads to a lot of content that are covered in a short time, in insufficient depth. It can’t be helped, given the many different branches of physics and engineering used in quantum research. His slides are pretty bare, and he doesn’t follow any regular textbooks. It’s more likely than not that you’ll have to watch the lectures to keep up. The assignments dished out in class are pretty handy for testing your understanding of the material. Alex likes to engage his students in his class by asking questions and keeping his students thinking. He also enforces consult timings for the class, to clarify with him any doubts you have. Assignment Alex gives assignments as part of his teaching curriculum, which are ungraded. They are typically questions that have some application on the content covered in class. They provide some basis to what he’ll test in term tests Experiments The experiments are pretty much a quantum-focused PC2191. Just follow the lab manual , record everything, and you’ll be fine. We worked in groups of 4-5, and report was due for each experiment. The experiments themselves were quite interesting, and provided some solidification of the material covered in class. For our year, there were three experiments, Polarimetry Building a Passive Quench Circuit for a Single Photon Detector Quantum Key Distribution Term Tests The term tests essentially covers the lecture content. Nothing far-out. A good reckoning of the test material can be gleaned from the class assignments. Seminar This was done in the same teams as the experiments. The seminar report involves us taking a paper of interest, and writing a report on it. A presentation of the report had to be given to the class at the end of the semester. Additionally, Alex would interview the members to ensure everyone contributed to the report. There was also a mini-interview session midway through the semester. A paper was given to us to read, and Alex would host a group interview to gauge our understanding of the material. Expected Grade: B+/A- Final Grade: To be updated Thoughts A fairly chill module, designed to provide a general introduction to a quantum laboratory.
thread_title: PC4228 Device Physics for Quantum Technology

author: Rain and Coffee
message: Quick Review A full review will be given when the semester ends. There is no point taking this module. You could learn as much just reading Griffiths. The point of taking a module is in hopes of learning more from the lecturer, for them to explain concepts you cannot understand on your own. This lecturer cannot provide either of those services.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC3130 Quantum Mechanics II Taken in AY21/22 (20 MCs) For the updated review, and more, visit: https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer Prof. Quek Su Ying Grade Distribution 15% Class Participation 20% Problem Sets 10% Project Work 55% Final Exam Contents Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Hydrogen Atom Linear Algebra General Angular Momentum Spin Composite Systems Additional of Angular Momentum Identical Particles Approximation Methods Time-Independent Perturbation Theory Applications of Stationary Perturbation Theory Quantum Dynamics (Rabi) Time-Dependent Approximations Light-Matter Interations Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) Lectures are held over zoom. QSY doesn’t provide any lecture notes to follow. Instead, she goes over the content for lectures, writing everything she wants you to know down. In turn, she expects that we write down everything she writes too. Her written lecture notes are uploaded after class, along with slides that she may use. However, they often end up corrupted and incomplete so you’re better off watching lectures or the recordings. I found her lectures structured in an uncomfortable way. As someone who already writes down their own lecture notes in class, it is exasperating when she dives into an example before explaining what the example is for. I generally prefer to be pointed in the direction we’re going before heading into that direction. But I guess it works if you like to be guided into the outcome step-by-step with that little bit of surprise at the end. She begins lectures with a recap of the contents covered in the previous lecture. This was quite helpful as oftentimes I don’t quite understand everything in the previous lecture. Occasionally, new bits of information appears during this segment, so do pay attention to them. She holds polls and quizzes during lectures which are graded components. You don’t have to get them correct, but you do have to answer them. The poll questions are as usual for these kind – they just help introduce tricky concepts and serve as a gauge for QSY to figure out how dumb we are LOL. Do make sure you attend class for these as they can appear ANYTIME. Quizzes She has quizzes, which are typically more challenging incarnations of the concepts or examples covered in class. At first, she made us do them in lectures, devoting half an hour for us to do them and submit by the end of class. Towards the end of the semester, we had no time for it in class, and so it became take-home assignments. The lowest two quiz scores were dropped. Problem Sets These are extensions of what was covered in class. In similar vein to the quizzes, these are often harder versions or derivations of concepts covered in class. Some new concepts are also covered in this segment as well, similar to Kenneth’s modules. If anything, the assignments were surprisingly easy compared to the ones given in other modules Extra Problem Sets These are a series of extra challenging problems that QSY gives as practice. Some of them are taken from the standard QM textbooks, usually covering a little more content than the main lecture material, e.g., scattering cross-sections. Although they are ungraded, there is the expectation that we would be able to do them as part of the learning outcomes of the module. They were quite helpful as practice when looking towards the finals (although admittedly I didn’t do any of them). Project The fun segment of the module. You are to research on your own topic of interest that is related or an extension to the content covered in the module, and write up a report on it. Had a lot of fun here, going down the Wikipedia rabbit hole and learning up your own stuff. We were given half the semester to complete this. Finals Given the difficulty of the problem sets and quizzes, I honestly expected a more intensive paper. It was of similar difficulty to Valerio’s midterms (PC2130). Expected Grade: B+/A- Final Grade: tbc Thoughts As you might have noticed, there are many, many components to this module. It can be quite difficult finding the material you need for revision, or the exact location of something you suddenly remembered. Relevant content for the course are covered in all components, so it can be detrimental to skip any parts. It was quite confusing when doing revision. QSY goes more in depth and covers a lot more than the previous lecturers for the module. Looking over the previous lecturer’s material won’t help much. If you get lost, do your best to find alternate material to refer to. I used a smattering of textbooks and lecture notes from other universities to digest through this module. QSY is a dedicated lecturer, doing her best to ensure we have plenty of material to refer to in this course. She also takes the time to make sure we understand and keep up to task. The feedback for the various components of the module are personalised and come back very quickly, which was really appreciated. Overall a confounding module, as expected for a quantum mechanics course. One of the last compulsory modules for a physics major, it really takes QM to another level beyond what was covered in PC2130. For those of us who are not going down the quantum path, it is one last hurdle to cross. All the best.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC3232 Nuclear & Particle Physics Taken in AY21/22 Semester 1 (20 MCs) For the complete review and more, visit: https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer A/P Chung Keng Yeow Grade Distribution 20% Take Home Assignments 20% Midterms 60% Finals Contents 1. Size of Nucleus 2. Mass of Nucleus 3. Spin, Parity and Electromagnetic Moments 4. Shell Model 5. Collective Model 7. Radioactivity 8. Alpha Decay 9. Beta Decay 10. Gamma Decay 11. Nuclear Reactions 12. Nuclear Fission 13. Nuclear Fission Reactors 14. Nuclear Fusion (and Reactors) 15. Radiation-Matter Interactions 16. Detectors 17. Accelerators 18. Particle Zoo 19. Quarks and Leptons 20. Feynman Diagrams and Gauge Bosons Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) Chung’s lectures are held twice a week, live. Knowing my own procrastination energy, I made myself attend them as much as possible. This didn’t last as other events clashed with the lecture timings, and a Covid case in the physics department prompted a transition to a completely asynchronous mode of teaching. The amount of content covered in this module is ridiculous. There are twenty chapters, and Chung covers a new chapter more or less every lecture. To be fair, the actual tested material isn’t that much, so you just got to figure out what is important and what is not. Chung’s lectures are… somewhat dry. The course is essentially an infodump covering history and methods of analysis in nuclear physics, which makes for really dull classes. After the switch to asynchronous teaching, I realised I could really just make do with reading the notes, then consulting the lectures on any unclear points. To be fair, this really is more of a fault of the nature of the module, rather than the lecturer. Chung tries to cover the interesting bits of nuclear physics, and a glimpse at the math behind it. And his notes are fairly comprehensive. If you choose not to attend class, be prepared to lag behind everyone. Uploading of the lectures is rather irregular and so you might have to wait a little before you can catch up with the class. That said, each lecture/chapter is pretty much a entirely new module on its own so you can just hop into class any time anyway… Chung does take the time to go over the questions from past-year papers, under the guise of tutorials. It helped us set some expectations on what is expected from us, given the massive mountain of material that the module covers. Assignments One assignment was given near the start of the semester. The other assignment appeared over week 13. I suspect we all (including Chung) forgot that assignments were supposed to be a thing… The first assignment was not too challenging. Chung made a point that reading papers was an important skill for physicists, and so added an extra component where we had to summarise a paper from a selection that he prepared. I just went with the paper that was related to an experiment I was concurrently doing in PC3193. The final assignment was a primer for the finals. It was basically set in a similar style that we should expect for finals, and so was a good warm up. Midterms Was held live. Not too challenging. The nature of the module meant that this was pretty much a test of memory rather than mathematical prowess. A good conceptual understanding of the course material is definitely help, as you can reason out anything you can’t remember and you won’t be penalised too harshly for it. Finals The finals was different compared to the midterms. It was held (live) as a 5-pick-4 questions format, with questions 4 and 5 as 4-pick-2 questions. These last two questions focuses more on explanations of concepts, while the front questions were slightly more computational. I like this format as it allows me to focus on the topics that I feel more comfortable in. Any point that I felt unsure about, I could always skip and do something else instead. That aside, there was a LOT of writing to do. Two hours was barely enough for me to complete the paper. I suppose this can’t be helped, given the similarly large amount of content covered in the module. Given that it was mostly regurgitation, thinking time wasn’t really needed. The exam covers all content, from lecture 1 all the way to lecture 21. The questions that came out spanned pretty much all of it, including repeats from midterms. Don’t bother trying to spot questions using the midterms because Chung has planned for you trying to be smart. Overall was fairly okay. Had a terrifying time leading up to it, given its weightage. Doing it was a pain, but when it’s over, it’s over. Expected Grade: B+/A- Final Grade: tbc Thoughts Took this module just because I was interested in the last three chapters. I can’t believe I slogged through 18 chapters of nuclear physics just to reach them – only to find that it covers just a little more than PC1144. At least there’s PC4245 to look forward to… Overall, the course was surprisingly organised, especially given that the lecturer of the course has changed hands many times right prior to Chung. Overall, you would just have to accept that nuclear physics, at the undergraduate level at least, is a fairly practical and empirical field.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: GES1034 We the Citizens – Understanding Singapore’s Politics Taken in AY21/22 Semester 1 (20 MCs) For the updated review and more, visit https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Lecturer A/Prof Bilveer Singh Teaching Assistant Kacey Alvin Grade Distribution 20%: Tutorials (Class participation) 30%: Essay Assignment 50%: Finals Contents Citizenship in Singapore British Immigration Policies Political Administration of Singapore Evolution of Key Political Institutes Independence of Singapore, and the development of the Executive, Legislature and Judiciary Role of Key Political Office Holders: Prime Minister and President Changing Role of MPs Political Citizenry Challenges Facing Singapore Citizens Government’s Response to Political Citizenry Redefining Singapore Citizenship Lecture (One 2 hr Lectures / Week) Barely attended them. Was held over zoom. Bilveer conducts his class like a conversation, where he goes on and talks casually about the topic at hand. He’s quite a fun and engaging lecturer, and makes attempts to interact with the students attending his class. He has a tendency to move a lot when talking in class, which is not really an issue, except that his microphone has a poor judgement in deciding what is background noise, and what is actual speech. It leads to long periods where he speaks at normal volume, then suddenly falls very quiet, only to blast your eardrums off when the microphone realises its silly mistake. His slides are very pictorial in form, and doesn’t say much. To get the actual content of the class, you’ll be better off reading his published books on Singapore politics. It covers the main points of the class, albeit not quite up to date on recent events. Didn’t really keep up with classes as the content was fairly abstract. Only right before finals did I start delving deep into the main points that are covered in class. A lot of it is rather historical, which kind of disappointed me as I was hoping for a more psychological take on the political events (How and Why, rather than just What and When). He regularly invites politic figures in Singapore to come and give lectures and discussions for his other political science classes, which we are invited to attend. Unfortunately, the physics and political science departments love each other so much that they regularly schedule clashes in these lecture timings. Tutorial (One 2 hr / Fortnight) Tutorials were pretty tiring. Made the mistake of choosing an 8 am class. At least it was online… In class, we are expected to prepare a presentation to give regarding one of three topics. We take turns presenting, with people from each session answering their own take on a certain topic. This was then wrapped up by Kacey, who then goes over briefly what was covered in lecture that week. Preparing the presentation took longer than I expected, given the amount of self-research we had to do. I went with the topic that was closest to the mid-semestral essay that we had to write, to reduce the amount of work I had to do. Essay Assignment Given that we had all the time in the world to do it, I took my time to write it out. Spent about three days in essay planning and writing. Got a B for my efforts. Shame. Finals Was held as a do-at-home exam, is open-book and non-proctored. The paper consists of three questions, of which we picked two to answer. The topics of the questions were made known to us beforehand, which allowed us to selectively study for the topics. Was pretty tough given that essay-exams have so much writing and thinking under time-pressure. At least it was open book else I’d be really dead. Expected Grade: B Final Grade: tbc Thoughts I chose this GES in hopes that I would take a more vested interest in Singapore politics. and be more politically aware. I’m glad to say that I learnt a lot from this module, both about recent events and the political history of Singapore. There’s a lot more that I could have learnt if I attended class, but well, it is what it is. Kacey is a very passionate TA, and really puts in the effort in this module. She’s pretty much in charge of all the emails we receive, and is our go-to for the module. She puts in a lot of time in making sure we have all the details we need for class, emailing us regularly on updates. Overall, a module that lives up to expectations. A nice throwback to JC days, writing essays in GP and Economics. Don’t take this module if you’re not prepared for writing essays.
thread_title: GES1034 We the Citizens - Understanding Singapore’s Politics

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC3247 Modern Optics For more reviews, visit https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Taken in AY21/22 Semester 1 (20 MCs) Lecturer: Prof. Ji Wei Grade Distribution Tutorials (30%) Midterms (30%) Final Assignment (40%) Contents Electromagnetic Phenomena Plane Waves and Complex Refractive Index Polarisation of Light Double-Parallel Interfaces Coherence and Interference Diffraction Light as Rays (Ray Transfer Matrices) Laser Beam Optics (Gaussian Beams ) Fourier Optics Optical Imaging and Resolution Lecture (Two 2hr Lectures / Week) Lectures were held at 8am, the start of a 6hr lecture block that I have twice a week. As such, after the first week I never bothered to attend class. Too early… Ji Wei makes absolutely God-Tier notes that are very comprehensive and easy to follow. Granted, he basically lifts word-for-word from Peatross, but sticking to the textbook really helps in understanding the lecture content. I just took note of the tutorial deadlines, and went over the lecture notes right before so that I could complete them. Tutorials Ji Wei gives a tutorial for every chapter he covers. Honestly, they can be rather tricky, but doable, given time to think about it. Typically, I spend a day or two on catching up with lecture content, and then doing the assignments. Midterms There were 5 questions, each from a different topic covered in class. Most of the questions were relatively straightforward – with the exception of one that I found trickier. Is slightly harder than the tutorials given in class Final Assignment This assignment was given over the finals week. It essentially covers some problems from the last two chapters (which had no tutorials). Was thereabout the same difficulty as his regular tutorials. I also just read the necessary chapters right before doing the assignment. Thank goodness for his crystal-clear notes. Expected Grade: A-/B+ Final Grade: tbc Thoughts Ji Wei prefers NOT to use LumiNUS for, well, everything. All of his zoom links, announcements, lecture material, assignments, submissions, and marked scripts are done via email. This can be a little challenging to find the necessary material for the module, and very easy to accidentally miss something. You’d better get over that habit of not reading emails and letting it pile up. Ji Wei makes the effort to make the physics very understandable to the lay-student. His slides are very involved and has many diagrams and demonstrations. We also got our hands dirty with excel (or python) and Mathematica for some chapters. Overall, was a pretty interesting module. The math can be absolutely horrendous at times, but working through them is not that big of a hurdle (unlike some 2k modules). If you have any interest in optical phenomena or intend to pursue experimental quantum physics, I would recommend this module. The topics covered are quite relevant to what is often found in quantum laboratories. The time-investment is also relatively light for a 3k module, so you can take this as a breather module.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC3193 Experimental Physics II For more module reviews (and the complete review) visit https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Taken in AY21/22 Sem 1 (20 MCs) Lecturers A/Prof. Tok Eng Soon Dr. Ren Minqin Dr. Vajandar Saumitra A/Prof. Thomas Oscipowiz Teaching Assistants Arif Er Chow Lin Er Tan Chuan Jie Zhang WenHan Lab Staff Dicky Seah Lee Soo Mien Grade Distribution Start of Semester 90%: Lab work (Report/Summary + Viva) 3 Short Experiments 2 Long Experiments 10%: Oral Presentation End of Semester 100%: Lab work (Report/Summary + Viva) 2 Short Experiments 2 Long Experiments We don’t actually know the grade distribution for the lab work. Covid struck one of the students taking the module and one experiment was dropped. Later on, because of the rising number of cases, the oral/presentation was dropped as well. List of Experiments Experiments done are underlined Short Experiments Channelled Spectrum Comparators Diffraction & Polarization of Microwaves Eddy Current Ferroelectric Measurement Ferromagnetic Resonance Gyroscope Laue Diffraction + Duane-Hunt Relation & Planck’s Constant * Laue Diffraction + Moseley’s Law & Rydberg Constant * Magnetostriction with the Michelson Interferometer Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Peltier Heat Pump (Attempted, gave up) Plasma Physics Principles of Lasing X-Ray Crystallography X-Ray Fluorescence Analysis Zeeman Effect Long Experiments Atomic Force Microscopy Compton Scattering Digital Video Microscopy Faraday Effect Fourier Transform Infra-red Spectroscopy Kerr Effect Michelson Interferometer Mossbauer Spectroscopy Nuclear Lifetimes One-electron & Two-electron Spectra Optical Pumping of Rubidium Particles-Matter Interaction Raman Spectroscopy Silicon Nuclear Detector Laboratory Sessions Just like the PC2193, this module requires you to go down to the lab to do the experiment yourself. The main difference is that now, you work individually rather than as a pair. The experiments themselves are more in-depth than in the level 2000 module – you actually have to do quite a bit of self studying to understand the concepts behind the experiments. I was told by the laboratory staff that the experiments are designed to supplement your learning in the level 3000 modules, so you might want to pick the experiments that are directly related to the modules you’re taken (or have taken). Like before, you will have to fulfil a certain number of hours by some arbitrary deadline during the semester. Rest assured no one actually checks that – it just serves as a good pacing to follow. You will also have to fill up your lab notebook, but again, no one actually checks that. Traditionally, you should be able to go down to the lab whenever you feel like it (From 12 to 5). But because of Covid, lab hours are now restricted so that everyone can only come down twice a week during the allocated slots. They don’t really enforce this, so there are weeks I don’t bother going down at all, while there are weeks I pop in four times. The staff are quite understanding so don’t be afraid to asks them. Because the experiments are more involved, there are required pre-experiment quizzes you have to complete before you can start collecting data. Be sure to read up on the experiments first as you will definitely be a little lost when you first start. Additionally, because of the restriction on when you can go down to lab, pre-reading is important so that you know what you’re doing. If you make a mistake in collecting your data, you might have to come down again on your assigned day and that’s half the week wasted (Out of the 12 weeks you have) The first experiment is assigned to you. The others are free and easy pick-what-you-want, as long as it isn’t taken by someone else already. You can start on the next experiment while finishing up the data processing and report writing for the previous, so that you don’t waste the limited time you have. Reports/Summary Same as PC2193, just that you might need to delve a little deeper into the background information section. Viva Similar to PC2193, although in my experience, the focus on data analysis and methods is more lacking than that of understanding the concept behind the experiment themselves. (Although to be fair the questions they have are rather dependent on the report you submitted so it may differ with individuals) Experiments In chronological order Particle-Matter Interaction (Long) Was assigned to me as the first experiment. Is completely computational, so if possible, one should do this one over recess week (Lab isn’t open). The program was a pain to set up on my computer, but worth it as you can collect data anytime. Even if you cannot set it up, no worries, as there are computers in the lab that have the program installed. Note that some of the simulations are very computationally intensive and may take hours to complete. Theory-wise, I felt that it wasn’t that extraordinary. The basics of scattering (covered at the start of PC3232) and a little reading up will cover you just fine. Optical Pumping of Rubidium (Long) I chose this one because of my interest in light-matter interactions. Out of all the experiments I’ve tried, this one is definitely the hardest. The theory behind it is surprisingly involved, and the equipment is a pain to learn, with many one-of-a-kind quirks. If you have experience with AMO (atomic, molecular and optical physics) and worked with oscilloscopes before, it’ll definitely help you. I don’t regret picking this experiment, but if you do, be prepared to spend quite some time here. Principles of Lasing (Short) Has absolutely nothing to do with principles of lasing. The first part of the experiment has been removed, due to how long students tend to take to align the equipment. The second half can be done in a matter of hours, depending on how thorough you want to be in collecting data. The theory itself is not too bad, especially if you already understand the basics of lasers (from JC or otherwise). The main bulk of the required theory is covered in the latter half of PC3247, so you might want to consider doing this experiment last, while taking it alongside PC3247. A trivial experiment. To be very honest, I picked this one because of how long I spent on the previous experiment and I wanted to catch up on time. Peltier Heat Pump (Short) Initially, this was going to be my last experiment. But after playing around with the set-up and trying it out for myself, I realised that it would take too long. Minimally, you need two full days to collect ONE set of data for each segment. That is equivalent to 2 weeks of time (since lab is only open half-days twice a week), for the bare minimum if you make no mistakes. It wasn’t worth the time. Theory-wise, it is relatively simple. However, there appears to be a mistake in the theory suggested for the first part of the experiment and how the results for power and efficiency are computed. This further dissuaded me from continuing with this experiment. Zeeman Effect (Short) So I went with this experiment instead. It was really quite easy compared to the previous one. Theory-wise, not too bad, although reading up about the weird interferometer took some time. The experiment itself took two sessions to complete. Expected Grade: A- Final Grade: visit https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Thoughts Overall, a pretty fun module. It’s one of the few physical modules where you can interact with people, definitely making it enjoyable in the current climate.
