author: Rain and Coffee
message:
ST2334 Probability and Statistics

Taken in AY20/21 Semester 2

Physics Major (24 MCs)

For more reviews, go to https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/

Lecturer: Dr Chan Yiu Man

TA: Chen Jianan

Grade Distribution:

Quizzes 30% Finals 70%

Contents:

Basic Probability Random Variables Two-Dimensional Random Variables and Conditional Probability Distributions Special Probability Distributions Sampling and Sampling Distributions Estimation based on Normal Distribution

Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week)

Lectures, or at least those I've attended, are rote readings of the lecture notes. In the end, I decided to progress on my own by self-reading the lecture notes. The quizzes came every 3 weeks or so, so I'd only bother to catch up on content a few days before the quiz.

By right, we were supposed to watch the pre-lecture videos before class. Frankly, I don't think anybody in the right frame of mind would want to go over 4 hours of pre-recorded lectures a week before attending another two sessions of 2 hour lectures.

Tutorial (One 1 hr Tutorial / Week)

Didn't bother to attend as attendance isn't graded. The tutorials also released answers to the questions after class, so even less reason to attend. Jianan however, was kind enough to reply emails regarding questions and help whenever I got stuck, even though I never appeared for class.

Quizzes

The quizzes was pretty challenging to get through in the time given. I ended up barely passing most of them. To be fair, I only caught up on content a few days or hours before taking the quizzes, so I didn't expect too much from them. Looking over the answers right before finals, I can see where I went wrong, so I'll say it isn't too difficult, provided you put a little more time into learning the content well.

Exams

The finals were also in a timed quiz format. It was not too bad. Most questions were thereabouts on the same level as the quizzes. Having caught up on content at this point, I found it to be quite reasonable. There were a few questions that were pretty tough, which I expect was set to distinguish the geniuses from the rest of us plebians.

Expected Grade: B+

Final Grade: A-

Thoughts:

An easy module. Like the other reviewers, I agree that this is basically A'level probability and statistics with a little more added. The content is quite slow paced. By recess week, the only new thing I learnt was 2D Random Variables. And I didn't need to touch content until week 8. I'll recommend taking this module over the other probability and statistic modules if you want a lighter load. Of all my modules, I put the least effort into this one.

The quizzes and all aren't that difficult. It only remains to be seen how kind is the bell-curve for this module.

author: Rain and Coffee
message:
PC2230 Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics For more reviews, visit https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/

Taken in AY20/21 Semester 2

Lecturer: Dr Loh Huan Qian

Grade Distribution:

Tutorial 15% Quizzes and Polls 10% CA Test 1 25% CA Test 2 25% CA Test 3 25%

Contents:

Zeroth and First Law of Thermodynamics Microcanonical Ensemble and the Second Law Maxwell Relations Heat Engines and the Third Law Canonical Ensemble Grand Canonical Ensemble Identical Particles

Lectures (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week):

Huan Qian first goes through all the hot things that you've seen before in PC1142: The usual laws of thermodynamics, heat capacity and so on. After a couple of weeks, things get whackier. We started delving into statistical mechanics - using probabilities to determine the properties of a system. Things only get weirder and weirder from there on. The microcanonical ensemble begins with dealing with an isolated system, considering all possible states of a system and finding out the most probable outcome. Here, ideas were frankly not too far out. We then moved on to the canonical ensemble, where we consider energy being free to exchange with the surroundings. At this point, things started getting stranger, although it was all still grounded in the same ideas. Finally, we reach the grand canonical ensemble (Sounds like a FGO final move), where not even the particles of the system are constant. Along the way, we also made a few pitstops, looking into Maxwell relations and heat engines, before wrapping up with Bose-Einstein condensates.

Huan Qian is a meticulous lecturer. She thoroughly dives into the concepts of TSM, explaining every step of the way so that we do not end up too lost. Her lessons involve working through examples and derivations, which are very helpful when doing her tutorials. Nothing that she teaches ends up going into waste. She also engages the class with PollEV quizzes. Don't miss out on lectures, even if only for the quizzes.

Tutorials (Assignments):

HQ's tutorials are usually very similar to the content covered in class. The questions are usually direct applications of concepts, or a reapplication of an example she went through. Not too difficult, although there may be some that require a little out-of-the-box thinking.

The questions are usually quite interesting, about some funky phenomena you might not have heard of before, or some application of some problem in her lab, or just neutron stars. It makes the questions less of a chore to do, and more fun to apply what you've learnt.

She also includes some questions that require the use of programming to solve. Do take some time to brush up on your python skills if you can. What was especially tricky was graph plotting. This wasn't explicitly taught in CS1010. You would have to look up how to do it and implement in yourself.

Exams

The CAs were alright. I was a little pressed for time, but honestly, not challenges that can't be done, or a complete bolt from the blue.

Expected Grade: B+

Final Grade: A

Thoughts

One of the more interesting modules I've taken so far. At some point in my education I've just come to believe that physics is either equations that describe exactly what's going on, or approximations that break down at the extremes. This module covers the in-between, where we use probabilities of states in order to describe systems far too complex to consider the exact solutions of. Using probabilistic methods, we can arrive at answers that manage to be very close to what we see in the real world, even closer to the extremes.

The idea that what is likely to happen, rather than what will happen, is not something we see used thus far in our physics education. Coupled with quantum mechanics this semester, these two modules opened my eyes to another way of seeing the world.

I really appreciate the broad range of questions hurled at us. The use of programming in our tutorial questions especially. Programming would definitely be a necessary and useful tool when applying physics. Having an early exposure to using programming to visualise and problem-solve, and having to learn how to self-source for ways to implement is, in my opinion, a very meaningful lesson to have.

This is the last of the 2000 physics module reviews I am writing, and so I'll conclude with a ranking of my favourites from the ones I've taken so far:

PC2134 > PC2131 = PC2230 > PC2193 = PC2130 > PC2132

author: Rain and Coffee
message:
PC2130 Quantum Mechanics I For more reviews, take a look at https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/

Taken in AY20/21 Semester 2

Lecturer: Valerio Scarani

Grade Distribution:

Quiz 1 (6 Quantum Pieces): 30% Quiz 2 (Operators and Two Level Systems): 40% Quiz 3 (Viva): 40%

Contents:

Basic Quantum Phenomena Linear Algebra and Operators Two Level Systems The Material Point The Material Point in 3D (Including Spin)

Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week)

Lectures was conducted on zoom. Valerio prefers not to use his given notes, unless there's a tedious chunk to go thru. Instead, he prefers working things through with the class, going step-by-step to make sure everyone can keep up.

His lecture notes are filled with gaps here and there, and so I found it difficult to get through by reading them alone. Oftentimes, I would have to watch the lecture, pause it, consult the notes, work it through myself, in order to understand what's going on. Quantum mechanics is a strange topic, and the way Valerio teaches it diverges quite a bit from the well-known textbooks (Hello again Griffiths!).

I'll recommend Landau, if you are willing to read through a textbook to learn.

Exams

The first quiz was a three day assignment. Not much going on.

The second quiz was an exercise in math, making sure you understand the core concepts and how to obtain the quantities of a quantum system.

The viva is a presentation cum interrogation by Valerio and one of his tutors. Twelve topics are given, from which one is chosen. The topic is randomly selected, and you have to do your best to explain it.

I got lucky and pulled an easy topic, which was a breeze, compared to some of the other ones. It wasn't too bad. Just a slightly more intense form of PC2193's viva.

Expected Grade: A

Final Grade: A

Thoughts

Valerio covers a bit of Chinese in his class, so do brush up on your mother tongue (Or pick up Chinese), if you want to fully appreciate his class :D

I struggled a lot in understanding the ideas rather than the math. If anything, going over the math helped me understand a lot better. This module ties up first place with PC1142 in terms of time I needed to digest.

A rather different take on QM compared to PC1144, so you might be in for a wild ride (Esp. if your linear algebra is weak). Overall, a pretty enlightening module, that really goes into the guts of basic quantum mechanics.

It's just quantum.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: CS1231 Discrete Structures For more reviews go to https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ Taken in AY20/21 Semester 2 Lecturer: Prof. Aaron Tan and Prof. Lawrence Tin TA: Li Yunzhe Grade Distribution: Tutorial Attendance 5% Assignments (2) 20% Midterms 25% Finals 50% Contents: Mathematical Symbolisms Compound Statements Quantified Statements Proving Sets Equivalence Relations Modular Arithmetic and Partial Orders Induction and Recursion Functions Cardinality Counting (Probability) Graphs Trees Lecture (3 hrs total / Week) Lectures are held over zoom. Attended only a few lectures, realised it’s essentially the prof reading aloud the notes. Decided to skip them all and just do reading on my own. Don’t skip the examples given in the notes like I did. You’ll realise they are actually pretty important in understanding the content. I didn’t bother going over the content until the assignments rolled around. Only then did I go over all the content just to finish the assignment. Tutorial (One 2 hr Tutorial / Week) Tutorials are held over zoom. Attendance is graded so I went for classes. After attending a few classes, I also realised that it was pointless for me because I usually don’t bother catching up on content, and I don’t actually do the tutorial worksheets. I ended up just leaving my computer in the zoom call while doing something else. Assignments I felt that given the amount of time we had to complete and submit them, it was quite reasonable in terms of doability. Exams The questions can be broadly categorised into 3 types – Multiple choice, Multiple response and Open-ended. I understand that this module requires open-ended questions to testing things like proving and so on. MCQs were a bonus for stragglers like me. But MRQs… I really hate them. They take up a lot of time as you have to go over all the options, and you can’t get any marks unless you hit all the right ones. Disgusting. I really didn’t like the time pressure to do the questions. It was very pressing to go through the paper, knowing that the questions were doable, if I had more time. It became more of a game of choosing which questions to sacrifice. Didn’t finish the paper, not very surprising. Expected Grade: B Final Grade: B+ Thoughts: I can kind of see why this module is a prerequisite to everything else in Computing. The ideas taught here are quite important in logically rationalising how you approach problems. At the same time, I can see why people wouldn’t like this module. It’s quite difficult to follow and grasp the broad range of ideas that is covered. Overall, I’m ready to SU this one

author: Rain and Coffee
message: For more module reviews, take a look at blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com PC1141 Introduction to Classical Mechanics Taken in AY19/20 Sem 1 Lecturer: Wang Qing Hai TA: Ho CanYang Asaph Contents: Kinematics Vectors Circular Motion Newton’s Laws Friction Work and Energy Potential Energy Linear Momentum and Collisions Centre of Mass & System of Particles Rotation and Moment of Inertia Rotational Dynamics and Rolling Motions Angular Momentum Static Equilibrium & Elasticity Fluid Mechanics Gravitation Oscillations Wave Motion Sound Waves Superposition of Waves Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week): This was the first ever lecture I attended in NUS. Looking back, I didn’t appreciate how good Qing Hai’s lectures were. He takes the time to go over problems, which helps to succinctly convey the ideas and concepts taught. I also find that he very nicely bridges the gap between H2 physics, and university physics. Yes, the module content is very easy at the beginning, covering concepts covered in H2 Physics. However, as the module progressed, you start to get a handle on the rigor and challenges of university level physics. Integration over volumes and surfaces becomes a staple in every other problem. The actual tough content came about when rotations were involved. It is the first breath of university physics, and the math that comes along with it. Graded Components Learning Catalytics Qing Hai engages the class using quizzes. You have to answer the quiz (Doesn’t matter on your answer) in order to get participation points. Usually the problems are quite interesting and thought-provoking, and really tests your understanding on the concepts MasteringPhysics Really really annoying. The questions are not too difficult, save for that one gigantic bombastic one at the end. It is graded and worth quite a large portion of your grades so be sure to do them. There is no time limit on the assignments so take your time to check over each question in order to score well here. Laboratory (Once every Two Weeks): Like every level 1000 physics lab, it was an absolute waste of time. You go in, follow the manual, fill up your post lab worksheet, and submit at the end of the lab session. One good thing is that there is no take-home deliverables. Just make sure you prep’d your excel sheet, and quickly finish the lab. Exams: Unfortunately, I was ill and missed the final examinations so I don’t know much about it Expected Grade: B Final Grade: C Without the final exams I still managed to pass with a C so I accepted it and SU’d the module. Big regrets. Honestly, this is one of the most meaningful level 1000 physics module I’ve taken. I cannot emphasize this enough, that this module is truly the foundation for all the other physics modules to come. If you are a physics major and you found this module distasteful, I would recommend rethinking your choice of major. Every other physics module is a more rigorous and fast-paced compared to this one. But if you enjoyed this module like I did, please do continue to take more physics modules! HAHAHA enjoy your physics journey!

author: Rain and Coffee
message: For more reviews, take a look at blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com GEQ1000 Taken in AY20/21 Sem 1 (Post Circuit Breaker Semester) Lecturer: Several TA: Kuah Ting Ting Grade Distribution: Tutorial Participation Biweekly quizzes Forum posts (6) Final report Contents: Philosophy, Physics, Computational Thinking, Engineering, Economics and Design. From these 6 areas, we are thought different methods in inquire about ourselves, others and the world around us. As a pass/fail mod, you don’t have to put a lot of effort into any component of the work. Lecture: Self-watch the uploaded videos. Tutorial (One 2 hr Tutorials / 2 Weeks): Just a chill session. It was conducted on zoom. Quizzes MCQ. Not very challenging. Forum Posts: It puts to the test your ability to smoke out topics. Report: Just summarise the module and each of it’s separate areas and you have a completed report. Expected Grade: S Final Grade: S Thoughts A fluff module. Don't spend too much time on it since it's a pass/fail module.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: PC2193 Experimental Physics I For the full review and more, visit https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com Taken in AY20/21 (Post Circuit Breaker Semester) Physics Major (24 MCs) Module Coordinator: Dr Wang Xuesen Lecturers: Dr Wang Xuesen Dr Eda Goki Dr Nikolai Yakovlev Dr Ariando Ariando TA: Various (Unimportant, they do nothing at all) Grade Distribution: 2 Presentations 1 Report + Viva 3 Summaries + Viva Experiments: • Basic Diode Electronics • Ultrasonics • Magnetic Moment in Magnetic Field • X-Ray Diffraction of Crystals • UV-Vis Absorption Spectroscopy • Propagation of Laser Light • Pohl’s Pendulum • Absorption of Gamma and Beta Rays • Electron Spin Resonance • Franck Hertz • Hall Effect in P/N Type Semiconductors • Band Gap of Germanium • Brewster’s Angle • Diffraction of Circular Aperture Laboratory: The whole module is own-time-own-target, with deliverables every two weeks or so. You may choose to do any six experiments, and use them accordingly for presentation, report or summary. In total, we have to complete 2 presentations, 1 report and 3 summaries. Both the report and summary require a viva to be done as well. There are several constraints on which experiments you can use for the deliverables, which will be briefed on the first day of lab. The module is usually done in pair-work, although you can choose to do it alone. Viva: The profs will ask you on different aspects of the experiments you conduct and your report/summary. These questions can range from how you calculate errors, how you account for them or even conceptual questions about the experiment themselves. Not too challenging or scary, it is conducted as more like a personal conversation with the prof who is in charge of that particular experiment. Just have knowledge of the experiment that you conducted, the working physics behind it and the reasoning for the data analysis that you performed on your results, and you’ll pull through easily. Presentation: The presentation is held in the lab, with all the professors in attendance as graders. Each student is given 10 minutes to present, and 5 minutes for QnA. All students who are taking PC2193 are allowed to come in and spectate the presentation. The presentation is likely the first of its kind of content-grading for physics majors. It is a good opportunity to test the presentation skills of the students. I think what’s important for presentation is knowing how much to include in your slides. A brief overview of the experiment and the concepts behind it, the aim of the experiment, results and conclusion, and potential future work. You should have a good sense of the kind of data analysis needed in each experiment, and justification for every step you take in processing the results. The presentation QnA is trickier than the viva, simply because there are now more graders to bounce their questions off you to really test your understanding of the experiment. Don’t be too rushed to answer the questions, clarify and think through first before giving your answer. For my presentations, I did X-Ray Diffraction and UV-Vis Spectroscopy. Summary: Nothing much. A quick write up of the experiment, concepts, methods and results. Not too difficult. Make the font size larger to help reduce the strain on the professors’ eyes. For the summary, I did Brewster’s Angle, Magnetic Moments and Absorption of Gamma & Beta Radiation Report: An extended version of the summary. Usually for the report, more experiments would have to be done, or in further/greater detail. Here is where you can put in a lot more effort into researching the phenomena involved, and thoroughly investigate every little detail of your experiment. (If you haven’t already been doing so) For the report, I did Hall Effect in Semiconductors. Expected Grade: A-/B+ Final Grade: A- Thoughts: The most enjoyable physics module I have taken. Perhaps it is because it is the online semester, having a module that requires me to go school helped to freshen up my days and provide me an outlet to interact with people. The experiments themselves were pretty fun to do, but can be extremely frustrating at the same time. Part of the challenge of this module is troubleshooting. Sometimes, the data you obtain from the experiments can be extremely different or have very large error bars, and you would have to either find an unresolvable justification for the results or return back to the lab to attempt to get better results. This problem is especially prominent in light-based experiments, which are very sensitive and twiddly in general. Troubleshooting is a very tiring process. Fixing the experimental set-up/procedures can take hours or days, depending on how fast you arrive at the solution. Examples that I’ve encountered include swapping of samples with the lab coordinator, interchanging parts between the machines set up for our use, and redoing the experiment in more controlled settings or running them in reverse. Despite all this, I assure you the feeling of finally figuring out what went wrong and getting good results is extremely fulfilling. Personally, my favourite experiment was Hall Effect, simply because of the richness of the experiment. With many different parameters to investigate, you find that there are many different concepts and phenomena interplaying with each other that you can exploit in your report for completeness. This was the first experiment done by me and my partner, but ended up being the last deliverable to hand up. The different professors each have their own quirks and priority for learning outcomes for the module. Nikolai focuses more on experimental methods (and troubleshooting), and data analysis. Ariando usually tests concepts related to the experiment, and understanding of the student. Xuesen and Goki asks hypothetical scenarios which tests the students understanding of concepts and ability to extrapolate to different cases. I feel that this shouldn’t affect how you choose the experiments you want to do. Either way, you will have to go through all of them and the quirks of the prof-in-charge doesn’t really matter. I think choosing experiments based off your understanding would be for the best. Experiments such as Hall Effect require some knowledge of semiconductors and the different energy bands, while ESR requires knowledge on electron spin and how it interacts with external applied fields. If you lack the required background knowledge, or are a little rusty, you might want to pass up on that experiment. Understanding of the physics behind each experiment would aid you in troubleshooting, data processing, and viva/QnA later. Don’t be afraid to ask people for help. Although the TAs can be seen doing nothing and lazing about in the lab, they can be (slightly) helpful if you approach them. The lab coordinator and the supervising prof in the lab can also be approached for more directed and meaningful assistance. Teamwork is key here. Do talk to the other people doing experiments in the lab. There are some experiments that are straight up a waste of time, or simply ridiculously tedious to finish. Some experiments have tips and tricks that you need in order to get good results from. Problems that you may be facing in your experiment may have already been solved by someone else. Talking to other people and helping each other will make this module a more enjoyable one. Even for presentation, going to each other’s presentation for support (Or just to banter, the profs won’t mind too much) would make everything a lot more fun and less stressful. Get a lab partner that you are comfortable talking with. It really helps to have someone working alongside you that you can bounce ideas off and think through problems with.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: Full review and more at blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com Taken in 19/20 Summer vacation (Circuit breaker) Physics Major Module Coordinator: Zhao Jin Grade Distribution: Milestones Evaluation by peers and advisor Contents: The module itself is held over summer vacation, although it officially appears on LumiNUS in the following semester. There is no consequence of dropping this module. Students are required to propose and build their own software from scratch. They work in teams of two, with teams subdivided into different levels (Vostok, Gemini, Apollo and Artemis) which have different levels of difficulty and complexity of project. Learning is very self-driven, and dependent on the type of project undertaken by the team. Some workshops are conducted, and there will be an advisor assigned to a group of students to oversee their progress. The module itself is pass/fail. Work is graded by each milestone, with the advisor and other teams grading your work. Thoughts: Because the module is very self-driven, I believe it is important to find a partner that you can work well with. My team made a game as our project, although the project is not limited to just games. There are teams that make websites to track shows, to match your modules, and even dating apps. There is no limit to the kind of software you want to create. Most of the work is up to each team, with a meeting with your advisor over zoom every 6 weeks or so to check in on milestones and progress. The milestones are very relaxed. Complete a working front-end. Make a poster. Make a video. Fill in worked hours. Not much. The module is only as hard as you want it to be. I believe that if you are to take this module, do make the most out of it and make something you can be proud of. Learn something new out of the experience. Or if you’re ambitious, make something that can go on your future portfolio. My personal experience was really fun; we spent a lot of time play-testing our game and working through all the bugs. Expected Grade: CS Final Grade: CS Project: Temporal Game Video: Video Showcase Walkthrough: Video Walkthrough

author: Rain and Coffee
message: Full review and more at blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com Taken in AY20/21 (Post Circuit Breaker Semester) Physics Major (24 MCs) Lecturer: Dr Hong Chong Ming Kenneth TA: Dr Hong Chong Ming Kenneth Grade Distribution: Lecture Worksheets Assignments CA 1 (Midterms) CA 2 (Finals) Contents: • Infinite Series • Vectors • Complex Variables • Partial Differentiation • Multiple Integrals • Second Order Linear Differential Equations (2ODE) • Partial Differential Equations • Fourier Series • Gradient, Divergence, and Curl • Line, Surface, and Volume Integrals • Integral Theorems • Curvilinear Coordinates • Fourier Transform • Dirac Delta Distribution • Laplace Transform • Sturm Liouville Theorem (Untested) Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week): This semester, lectures are held over zoom. Kenneth is a great lecturer, taking time to cover each important point for each and every topic. He works out problems with us in class, which is very helpful for learning. Despite the overwhelming content for this module, I feel that the things I’ve learnt are very meaningful and not entirely incomprehensible compared to other modules I have taken. Fortunately, in this semester lectures are recorded, which greatly facilitates learning. Previous (and perhaps future) iterations of PC2134 are not recorded. Although Kenneth’s notes are very well annotated, I would still recommend attending lectures. It is worth waking up early for. Kenneth explanations really aid in understanding the vast module content. Tutorial (One 2 hr Tutorials / Week): Kenneth’s tutorials are questions crafted from previous modules he taught. You may notice that statistical mechanics make up the hardest questions in his tutorials. He takes the time to go over the tips and trick for each of the challenging questions. Although you may not have attempted or completed the tutorials, I would recommend attending. He often has funky tricks which he uses to solve problems, which may come in handy in future problems. The many different (and unusual) methods you can approach a problem are quite interesting and you can learn a lot from just attending his classes. Through the roof spiciness levels. Lecture Worksheets: He goes through the lecture worksheets in class, so you just need to follow along and copy them down. He also uploads a copy of the annotated lecture questions after each chapter. The deadline for us was the end of the semester, so you can take your time in submitting the lecture worksheets. Although I would recommend doing them on the spot, which would really help in learning, and because the large amount of module content can make last minute work extremely painful. The questions themselves are usually an example of a concept that was covered in the chapter or a derivation of a theorem. Not particularly challenging. Mildly spicy. Assignments: Spicy. Very spicy. Kenneth’s assignments are not as difficult as his tutorials, but are very challenging nonetheless. Every assignment usually has one or two simpler questions, then a killer question that can take days to solve. I must say that the questions are NOT IMPOSSIBLE to solve, although they can feel very overwhelming at times. Do them continuously. This is not work that you can pull off any last-minute miracles on. Even if you can’t solve the problem, write something and Kenneth will be able to give you some method marks. Like the lecture worksheets, Kenneth allows us to hand up all assignments by the end of the semester, which really helps to ease our workload and make learning more enjoyable and effective. Exams: In most years, Kenneth’s exams are abysmally difficult, leaving many students floundering at every question. This year, he has kindly opted to make the exams doable at least. The questions are not the kind that would leave you completely stunned when you look at them, leaving the time pressure as the only constraint. The questions are definitely doable, given time and an open textbook. The exams are open-book, but only for hard-copy materials. CA 1 tests content up to 2ODE, and CA 2 tests content after 2ODE. Kenneth is quite a generous grader, giving marks for students who are on the right train of thought. As such, you should write down all your trains of thought during the exam in order to score these method marks. Expected Grade: A+/A/A- Final Grade: A Thoughts: One of the most meaningful modules I have taken. Everything I’ve learnt from this module has applications elsewhere in my other physics modules. It very nicely bridges the gap between theoretical understanding of more challenging math, and direct applications where we can use them. Although the content covered is extremely heavy, I feel that the module itself is not very difficult. I had more problems with PC1142 than this module. 10/10 would take again.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: For more reviews, go to blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com PC2132 Classical Mechanics Taken in AY20/21 (Post Circuit Breaker Semester) Physics Major (24 MCs) Lecturer: Dr Yeo Ye TA: Nil Grade Distribution: Example Sheets Lecture Participation (Attendence) Midterms Finals Contents: • Dynamics of a Single Particle • Conservative Forces 1 • System of Particles 1 • Central-Force Motion 1 • Conservative Forces 2 • System of Particles 2 • Lagrangian Mechanics • Small Amplitude Oscillations Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week): This semester lectures were conducted in E-learning format. Originally, Dr Yeo was reluctant to engage students in the chat. After the first few lectures, he opened up and was very willing to talk to students and answer their questions. This was good, as students could directly clear up any misconceptions that the students may have. But at the same time, there were many students who took advantage of his kindness and asked pointless questions, wasting lecture time. He conducts his lecture not by referring to his prepared lecture slides, but by writing out his derivations and calculations on Microsoft word, or using a whiteboard he has in his office. This was helpful for understanding, as his lecture notes are full of derivations without annotations, which make them extremely challenging to understand. However, he rarely prefaces his derivations and workings with explanations on what he is doing. He prefers to instead work things out, and hope that the students grasps what he is doing along the way. His lectures are rather challenging to follow. He would move from his whiteboard, to the shared screen, then he’d stop sharing screen to read the chat. This is especially annoying as we would have to keep changing the screen we focus on and sometimes, he would stop sharing screen before the students have caught up with him. This makes his convoluted lectures even more confusing. The textbook is not much better. Englert’s is just as confusing, requiring more mathematical background to appreciate fully. Nonetheless, if you cannot understand what Dr Yeo is covering in class, Englert is another option you can consider. My experience with this module was not the best. I found myself not listening to class at all, and then running the recordings at twice or three times the speed to get over all the pointless questions thrown at Dr Yeo over the course of his class. Having the textbook on hand really helps as it allowed me to move at my own pace, and learn from another perspective. Example Sheets: The example sheets are worksheets supplied on LumiNUS, with solutions already given. The answers themselves are quite sparse in step-wise working, which requires you to actually think through the question in order to follow the steps. Exams: The exams were concepts covered in class, but more algebraically tedious. Dr Yeo’s exams follows the 3-choose-2 format, and students can choose whichever two questions they found easiest. The exams for our year was open book. Expected Grade: B+/B- Final Grade: A Thoughts: I did not learn much from this module. The workload is very, very little compared to the other physics modules I’ve taken. As expected, the ideas covered were not particularly out-of-this-world, which makes understanding them not too challenging. Overall, an easy module, but I wish more was covered and in more detail. A fluff module. I feel that you don’t actually need PC1141 (Introduction to Classical Mechanics) to understand this module, although MA1101R (Linear Algebra) is definitely needed.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: For more reviews, go to blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com CS1010E Programming Methodology I Taken in AY19/20 ST 2 Physics Major Lecturer: Don’t know, never watched the videos TA: Forgot, didn’t pay attention Grade Distribution: Tutorial attendance, coursemology, midterms and finals Contents: Basic data types Loops Functions Recursion Search/sort Sequences Multi-dimensional arrays Object-oriented programming Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week): Didn’t attend Tutorial (Two 2 hr Tutorials / Week): Slept through most of it Coursemology: With experience in python, I found most of it quite easy. I think the most important part is that this module is a lot lighter in workload during special term, compared to during a normal semester, and the coursemology assignments aren’t as challenging. Exams: Here’s the interesting bit. The exams were mostly code tracing rather than us writing our own code. The midterms were placed on week 5 of the term and finals on week 6. Expected Grade: A Final Grade: A Thoughts: I think that the workload for this module is very light. The main vexing point was, the lectures are in the morning, and 3 hours later, the tutorials for that particular lecture would start, giving students very little time to actually do the tutorial and consolidate their learning. To be fair, the tutors do spend time going through each question and example, explaining his thinking behind each question. But this scheduling of learning content seems a little unreasonable to me. As someone who already knows python, I found this module a breeze. I would recommend taking this module during special term if you want to clear it quickly without hampering your other modules. For the layperson who is less familiar with Python, I would recommend taking the module during the normal term, where you can receive more practice, guidance and time to learn the actual content.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: Complete review and more at https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/2021/01/02/pc2131-electricity-and-magnetism-i/ Taken in AY20/21 Semester 1 (Post Circuit Breaker Semester) Physics major taking 24 MCs this semester Lecturer: Dr Murray Barrett Douglas TA: Nil Grade Distribution: • Assignments (5): 40% • Finals: 60% Contents: • Introduction to Vector Calculus • Electric Fields • Special Methods for Finding Potential • Electric Fields in Matter • Magnetic Fields • Magnetic Fields in Matter • Maxwell’s Equations Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week): Murray’s style of teaching is very different from the other physics modules that you may or may not have taken. Bear in mind that this is first time teaching this module so he’s still working the kinks out. There will probably be some changes to his pedagogy in future iterations of conducting this module. Also, do note that this semester is the online semester, where everything is in E-learning format. In Murray’s opinion (And I concur), Electromagnetism at the undergraduate level is more than sufficiently covered by the various textbooks. Griffith’s (The main textbook used in this course), in particular, is quite detailed and well written, such that you can learn the ideas in mind by simply reading the textbook. If you want a deeper understanding )or more rigorous math), you can consult Jackson’s, which is more in depth and heavier in math, but much less detailed and is infamous for being needlessly complicated and obscure. Due to this, Murray favours having the students read the textbook at their own pace, and then come to lecture as a tutorial, where we can ask questions about the course material or clarify some things that we are unsure about. However, this ends up in the scenario where very few people actually attend lectures, as they are not graded and we are allowed (encouraged) to learn at our own pace. Initially, Murray’s lectures where spent on going over difficult problems or interesting mathematical concepts, which are not in the syllabus and untested (which further reduced the number of students attending lecture). The things covered in lecture were fascinating, but often too far above our heads for understanding. Additionally, the ideas covered in vector calculus were skimmed over, under the assumption that we’d be taught before (We hadn’t), which further distanced the class. Fortunately, Murray changed his style slightly, and instead prefaced his lectures by covering content in the textbook, with example problems to give some idea on how to approach and solve them. He also brought in graphs and drawings to help illustrate his points better, which really helped in elucidating the challenging concepts and problem-solving methods covered. Murray really encourages students to explore and think own their own, and approach him if stuck or confused. I feel that towards the end, his alternative lecture style was quite clear and concise, and adds on to the learning. Unfortunately, the students driven away at the start of the semester never returned… Tutorial: Nil Assignments: Murray’s assignments are quite challenging. You need a good grasp of the concepts covered in class in order to solve the problems. Some of the questions require problem solving methods covered in lecture, which requires you to watch them (Unless you’re already very familiar with the concepts). I didn’t find the questions undoable, but I strongly recommend doing them with friends in order to test your understanding against each other. Murray is also amenable to students asking him how to tackle to questions in class, and he will provide some guidelines or hints to solving the problems. Laboratory: Nil Exams: His final exam was surprisingly simple, as compared to his assignments. He promised an exam that would focus more on understanding rather than tedious algebraic manipulation. The questions were taken directly Griffiths and was open-book. Not too challenging, but might be easy to have careless mistakes. Expected Grade: B+/A- Final Grade: A Thoughts: One of the most interesting physics modules I have taken (Out of PC2134, PC2132, and this module). The different methods used to calculate potential was the most fascinating section of the module to me. Seeing how electromagnetism can be taken one step further beyond what was covered in Kenneth’s PC1143 is really eye-opening. For extra reading material, I recommend reading Pollack’s, or Jackson’s, if you’re up for the challenge.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: More reviews at https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/2020/06/27/pc1144-introduction-to-modern-physics/ PC1144 Introduction to Modern Physics Taken in AY19/20 Sem 2 (Peak of Covid-19) Physics Major (24 MCs) Lecturer: Dr Dzmitry Matsukevich TA: Tseng Ko-Wei Grade Distribution: • Before Covid-19: o Tutorial Homework Assignment (5): 10% o Laboratory: 20% o Mid-terms: 15% o Finals: 55% • After Covid-19: o Tutorial Homework Assignment (6): 20% o Laboratory: 25% o Mid-terms: 20% o Take home Final Test: 35% Contents: • Einstein’s Theory of Special Relativity • Quantum Physics • Introduction to Quantum Mechanics • Atomic Physics • Nuclear Physics • Particle Physics Lectures: (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) “We learn how to build a nuclear bomb” – Dzmitry PC1144 takes you through all the fancy things you see on the news about physics – relativity, nuclear, particle physics. Being a module that is taken by H3 students, the content is rather … mild. It is closer to the JC style of teaching – memory work and blind application of formulas, rather than derivations like other year 1 physics modules. Being an introductory module that has to cater to JC students, the content is limited in the depth. Many of the topics I felt were touch and go, formulas dropped-from-the-sky, and accept-it-and go kind of style. Relativity in summary, is just a few formulas to apply, derivations not really needed (Although you’ll understand it better if you try deriving the formulas), and a little admittedly a little counter-intuitive. Concepts such as length contraction, time dilation and space-time intervals between events are covered. The tricky part comes when wrapping your head around different reference frames and doing funky stuff like differentiating time with respect to time. A portion of the content covered under quantum physics are stuff that H2 Physics students may recognise – De Broglie wavelength, photoelectric effect, and so on. Introduction to quantum mechanics is basically Schrodinger’s equation applied to several different constraints. In atomic physics we cover everything you learn in H2 chemistry, but a little deeper. Oh, and also the Zeeman effect. Understanding the four quantum numbers of electrons around an atom is a little tricky here, although the math isn’t too hard. Nuclear physics we go back to H2 physics – nothing special here. Calculation-wise, we go over binding energies and radioactive decay – nothing too funky here. And finally, particle physics. We cover the basics of the standard model (Up to strange quarks), and a few conservation laws thrown in for good measure. Dzmitry is an okay lecturer – he goes through every concept thoroughly and diligently. His pace is very constant, no matter which topic he’s at. As mentioned above, there are some topics which are quite familiar to H2 physics/chemistry students, while others can be utterly bombastic. I found that many of his lectures can be a little slow, for the topics I’ve seen before (Although quite understandable, given the spectrum of students taking his module). I don’t think you’ll be missing out much by just reading his lecture notes and not attending lecture. Dzmitry is also willing to help out students who are having some trouble keeping up. You can ask him for a consult, ask him questions on the Luminus forum or email him. He might not go through very in depth in lectures due to reasons mentioned above, but if you feel that knowing more would help facilitate understanding, he is always willing to explain more outside of class. Right before finals, he designated a 1-hour slot every day for two weeks as consultation on zoom for those who are still lost. Tutorials: (One 1 hr Session / 2 Weeks) Tutorials are a once fortnightly event, not required to attend. Most of the questions in the tutorial aren’t particularly tough – just be careful of careless mistakes and you’ll get through the homework just fine. The tutorial itself was an uneventful affair, the TA just went over all the steps in the questions without delving too much into how or why. I gave up attending unless I happened to be in school around that time. Didn’t learn much there. Laboratory: (One 3+ hr Session / 2 Weeks) Like every other year 1 physics lab, I found this a waste of time. We would go in, follow exactly as the lab manual says and leave. Of course, it helps to read the lab manual beforehand and prepare the excel sheets beforehand. If you’re prepared, you can easily finish the labs in an hour or so. The labs require pair work, and before each lab there will be a demonstrator to give a brief overview of the experiment. Before lab, you will have to complete a pre-lab worksheet, and before leaving you will have to submit a post-lab worksheet as well as a print out of the excel sheet used for calculations. This format is good in that we don’t have to write any lab reports – everything to do in lab stays in lab. I recommend not spending too much time in lab. Some experiments are very tedious or have very troublesome components in the post-lab which may drag your lab hours. Don’t dawdle and lab will be a somewhat pleasant experience, or at least not a bad one. Exams: Midterms was a 10 MCQ test, a terrifying affair when you remember each question is 1+% of your final grade. Overall, not too difficult, a little tricky. Finals was changed to an open-book format that was 3 hours long. Dzmitry was kind enough to prepare several different questions papers for us to do. I felt that most of the questions were not particularly difficult – everything you need are in his lecture notes. I made the mistake of spending a little too long at the start and didn’t finish the last two questions. There was one tricky question that required some out-of-the-box thinking, as well as knowledge from PC1143 (A bit unfair, I know). Overall, not very difficult. Expected Grade: B± Final Grade: B+ Thoughts: PC1144 ≥ PC1143/PC1141 > PC1142 Of the four primary year 1 physics modules, this was the one I enjoyed the most (Even though I didn’t do particularly well in it). Learning about the edge of physics that you heard of YouTube science channels or random Wikipedia surfing is pretty cool – especially with math than is not as rigorous as the other modules.

author: Rain and Coffee
message: Full review at https://blackforestcheesecakeandcoffee.wordpress.com/ PC1143 Introduction to Electromagnetism Taken in AY19/20 Sem 2 (Peak of Covid-19) Physics Major taking 24 MCs Lecturer: Dr Kenneth Hong Chong Ming TA: Kenneth Hong Chong Ming Grade Distribution: • Before Covid-19: o Midterms: 10% o In-lecture Worksheets: 10% o Laboratory: 15% o Assignments: 15% o Exam: 50% • After Covid-19 o Midterms: 20% o In-lecture Worksheets: 15% o Laboratory: 30% o Assignment: 30% Contents: • Electric Force and Field • Electric Potential Energy and Potential • Capacitance • Direct Current Circuits • Magnetic Force and Field • Electromagnetic Induction • Inductance • Alternating Current Circuits • Unification of Electricity and Magnetism • Electromagnetic Wave Lectures (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) If you’ve been through Wang Qing Hai’s PC1141 previously, you would not be in for such a huge shock. PC1143 covers the basics of electrostatics and magnetism, and more importantly the computational thinking required to solve problems. Kenneth (As he prefers to be called) has a special affinity for deriving everything from first principles. He emphasizes a lot on understanding, rather than rote memorisation. He spends time going through the thinking behind each problem, rather than just blindly applying formulas. Kenneth is always willing to give his time to students who are struggling or need extra help in understanding the content. Don’t be afraid to ask for help if you need it. The lectures themselves are moderately paced, if not a little slow. Kenneth usually starts the lecture with a small recap on the previous one, and spends some time to go through the worksheets mentioned earlier. At the end of the semester, we only covered up till inductance – in part due to the covid-19 situation. However, he covers some of the beginning of PC2131 Electricity and Magnetism I as well (so I heard), so you wouldn’t go wrong in paying extra attention to the more challenging portions. A good grasp on calculus would be very helpful in processing the problems he regularly gives out. The content itself is very similar to what is covered in PC1432 Physics IIE (My friend was taking this module in the same semester as me so I had a look at the content as well). There are several differences, mostly in the scope of what is expected from the student to be able to solve (vector calculations and Taylor expansions). The topics themselves wasn’t particularly mind-blowing; the difficulty usually arises from its application to problems that require a good understanding of the content and a little out-of-the-box thinking. The in-lecture worksheets aren’t particularly challenging. Rather, each one is designed to emphasize on a particular thinking or content that Kenneth wants students to take away from each topic. The worksheets themselves are usually given at the start of the lecture, when he is about to cover a new topic. If you skip/miss lecture, no worries – Kenneth uploads his annotated slides with the questions on the worksheets and he will accept submissions written on any paper. I found these worksheets extremely helpful, as he goes through the thinking process needed to solve problems. It allows you to see his thinking when approaching problems, as well as shortcuts and tricks which would simplify calculations. I recommend that you do not miss Kenneth’s lectures. It is not webcasted normally (due to covid-19 it was for this semester). His slides are sufficiently well-annotated and comprehensive that you can definitely learn on your own by reading his notes. However, I find that his lectures are worth waking up early for as his teachings are very helpful. Also, it would be rather tedious to go over the notes on your own to find the worksheet questions to fill up. Tutorial: (One 1 to 2 hr Session / Week) Attendance is not compulsory as no component of tutorial is graded. However, it is strongly recommended that you attend his tutorials. If you take the time to do his tutorials, you’ll find that many of the questions may leave you flabbergasted and lost, or tired when you realise the amount of calculations you have to process to solve the question. However, even if you haven’t done his tutorial, do show up for his class as you’ll definitely learn something worthwhile during your time there. The tutorials were originally in two consecutive 1 hr timeslots. However, we persuaded him to combine the slots into a single 2 hr slot as many people were joining the earlier slot in order to have a longer break time. This gave him more time to go through the questions during the class itself, which really helped in understanding as he did not have to rush through the explanations. In the end, most tutorials didn’t use up the full 2 hrs so we usually left earlier. Assignments: Assignments are done on mastering physics. As usual for mastering physics, it is very tedious to do. There is supposedly an assignment every week, but with Kenneth’s more relaxed pace of teaching, more often than not the deadline for the assignment gets pushed back a week or so. This gives us ample time to do them. Unlike PC1141 and PC1142, assignments for PC1143 are tougher – every other question requires calculus to solve, rather than just one tricky question at the end. Laboratory: (One 3+ hr Session / 2 Weeks) Like every other year 1 physics lab, I found this a waste of time. We would go in, follow exactly as the lab manual says and leave. Of course, it helps to read the lab manual beforehand and prepare the excel sheets beforehand. If you’re prepared, you can easily finish the labs in an hour or so. The labs require pair work, and before each lab there will be a demonstrator to give a brief overview of the experiment. Before lab, you will have to complete a pre-lab worksheet, and before leaving you will have to submit a post-lab worksheet as well as a print out of the excel sheet used for calculations. This format is good in that we don’t have to write any lab reports – everything to do in lab stays in lab. I recommend not spending too much time in lab. Some experiments are very tedious or have very troublesome components in the post-lab which may drag your lab hours. Don’t dawdle and lab will be a somewhat pleasant experience, or at least not a bad one. Exams: In previous years, all of his midterms are MCQs. However, due to the volatile conditions of our time, the midterms were changed to 5 open-ended questions. Doing past year MCQs was a very painful experience. You’ll feel very lost at some questions, followed by relief when you find something doable, only to be hit with despair when you see the solutions. I definitely preferred the open-ended format as it allows you to get method marks, so you won’t score too badly. In the end, the mean was ~40 of 100. Don’t be too disheartened as Kenneth intentionally sets rather challenging papers. Going through it myself, I feel that the questions were not undoable if you have been regularly keeping up with the content – rather, there was insufficient time to do all the questions for the average person. Of course, there’s always those geniuses who end up doing extraordinarily well, but most people clustered between 30-50. Finals was cancelled due to Covid-19. Quite a shocker as this came about only after midterms, meaning that most of our grades pretty much fixed already. Kenneth was talking about how he was always looking for a reason to do away with finals, so beware: If any trying circumstance falls upon NUS and your PC1143 midterms becomes open-ended, don’t be too surprised to have finals cancelled. Of course, the plus side is that there is no need to go over the course materials again, at the expense of the rigidity of your grades at this juncture. Expected Grade: A-/B+ Final Grade: A- Thoughts: PC1144 ≥ PC1143/PC1141 > PC1142 Of the four primary 1k physics modules I enjoyed the most, I would rate this tied in second with PC1141. Some of the content was new when compared to A levels, but more importantly I learnt plenty about the math and computational thinking required to solve problems. After hearing so much about the legendary Kenneth I felt a little underwhelmed. However, it cannot be denied that he is an excellent teacher and his lessons were very fruitful. For computing students looking to take this module for your science requirement, I’ll just describe the key points I feel are important. Content-wise, not boring enough to sleep over, but not as interesting/bombastic as PC1144. Math-wise, yes, a little bit more challenging than A levels, but if you remember your basics (integration and differentiation), you won’t struggle too much. Grade wise, you won’t do badly if you put in some effort. For physics students, math-wise, this module is PC1141 revamped. You may have heard of Kenneth’s legendary super-difficult papers, but don’t fear, everyone else will suffer with you. Enjoy your time with him.

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
ES1103
Taken in AY23/24 Semester 1

Assessments:
Attendance: 10%
Text Analysis: 10%
Synthesis: 20%
Intro + Problem (Draft 1): 10%
Intro + Problem (Draft 2): 20%
Solution + Conclusion: 20%
Complete Essay: 10%

Overview:
To prospective students who want to avoid NUS just because of the possibility having to take this course, I just wanna say that it is not bad at all. Classes were once a week this semester and it was extremely chill. Not sure about everyone’s tutor but mine is very chill. I would always use this time to do my other stuff. Even though it might appear useless, I did learn some useful things like how to cite properly, how to avoid being redundant and stuff like that. If you take this course, make sure to deliberately make use of these concepts in your essays.

Synthesis:
They will give you sources and you will have to use those to form up your arguments. Now that I realise it’s basically QET but not timed. Word count around 500.

Intro + Problem: 
Pick a problem of your interest that target a society and write an introduction and problems with that. 500 words also. For draft one, some of your classmates will give you comments instead of the tutor. Then you will use the comments to make a better version (draft 2). The tutor would only give comments for draft 2.

Solution + Conclusion:
Even though this is submitted separately, the solution should be the solution of the problem mentioned in our draft that we submitted earlier. The solution has to be at least 1 current solution and 1 of our own solution as well as evaluation. Around 500 words as well

Complete essay:
Use comments from the tutor to combine intro + problem and solution + conclusion together to make a whole essay.

Text analysis:
Choose a text, make a deck of slides with voice over to analyse the text. This is quite easy because they give model answers for us. However, try not to last minute this.

My grades:
Synthesis: 77/100
Intro + Problem Draft 1: 76/100
Intro + Problem Draft 2: 78/100
Solution + Conclusion: 75/100
Text Analysis: ???
Complete Essay: ???

Expected Grade: B+/A-
Final Grade: A-

The crux of this course I feel. Basically there will be like many dataset for everyone to choose from. Then in a group 4, we have to make a report, code and present our finding. 

Exams:
Mostly MCQ, the questions are quite doable. I wouldn’t say it’s easy but there are very little questions given the amount of time that they gave us so a lot of us left early. However, they are closed book so one still need to put in effort to study in order to do well.

Participation, Assignments: Full

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
Taken in AY22/23 S2

Lecturer: Prof Bernard Tan
Assistant: Lydia Lam

Assessment: 
20% Music Composition
20% Essay
30% Midterm
30% Finals

Opinion:
This mod is basically Math, Physics and Music, in that order. The math comes from calculating difference, ratio and rate. Basically like sec 2 math at best. Physics is basically just the frequency and wavelength all that. Music got like the intervals, pitch, tuning. Having music background does help a lot but it doesn’t really carry one through this whole mod. Don’t be scammed by the music part if you are a music person, your math foundation gotta be strong too (physics don’t really need cuz they will teach and exams are open-book).

Content: 
There’s a range of content. For what was tested, I would categorise the content into 3 groups (MIDI, Waves and Random Stuff). For MIDI, we learnt how music is being transmitted digitally. For waves, we learn about how string, open pipes and closed pipes can produce waves and the harmonic.  Random stuff would include basic music theory, loudness, intervals and blah blah. As a music person, I only get advantage for like 1 or 2 questions in the exam and even so I have to familiar myself with the way they ask the question.

Essay:
Write an essay about a concert that you attended. Earliest is 3 months b4 the mod starts. 200-800 words. Write about the good and the bad of the concert and impacts of technology on the concert. Personally did this essay before the mod started cuz I actually went to a concert during Christmas, 2 weeks before the sem started. It was due in Week 10, quite a busy period also.

Music Composition:
This one might be a bit difficult without music theory but they don’t have high expectation on the piece of music. As long as it sounds like a decent music, it should be fine. The actual writing of music took me like a few hours but before that, I trial and error quite a lot of times cuz I wanted to create something more complicated LOL. For this, I wanted to start before the sem also but I had no time so I actually only finished this on the day of submission (Week 13)

Exams:
Open book, 25 x MCQ, 1 hour for both exams. Open book means can use physical notes or digital notes but cannot use internet (even though they didn’t explicit mention). Don’t waste time making your own notes, just search online. There’s a very comprehensive note online. For the exam, just practice pyp with the notes and you should be fine. The exam papers are almost 1 to 1 copy so yea you know what to do (like technically pyps are also notes or smth). On top of that, do try to do tutorials as well. They help to solidify your knowledge gradually.

Results:
Midterm: 25/25 
Finals: 25/25
Mean and Median of both exams are also like 20+.
Essay + Music Compo: Not released

Expected Result: A/A+?
Actual Result: A (Bruh I thought I was gonna get A+ sosicjwkskaj but I guess I didn’t do well enough for essay and compo)

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
Taken in AY22/23 S2
Tutor: Nathaniel 

Assessments:
30%: TBWs (5 x 6%)
30%: ILAs (5 x 6%)
15%: Interim DTJ 
25%: Final DTJ

Opinion:
A lot of people said this mod is useless but I kinda disagree. Out of the 5 standard chs mods (HSS, HSA, HSI, HSH and DTK), I find DTK the most relevant. It does not just teach you how to design in the sense of like designing a product but it can be linked to design different aspects of your life. Once you can see the link, it’s actually quite meaningful. But I do agree from everyone that the workload is honestly not it (for me) which I will elaborate on later. 

Nat is a fun and engaging TA. What I really appreciate is he gives tips on DTJ and ILAs for those who wanna do well but at the same time he makes TBW fun for those who just wanna S/U this mod.

Individual Learning Assignment (ILA):
To be submitted on week 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 Friday 2359. A set of instruction for the ILA in the form of videos or miro board were uploaded on Monday of the week before the submission week. You will have to submit a set of slides with a given template. I would say that it should one too much time if there are just aiming to pass but if you want to do well, you will have to spend a lot of time on it to refine your ILA. It’s better to start very early because some of it isn’t just filling in the blanks but to also conduct interviews or making prototype. 

Team-based Workshop (TBW)
It’s basically tutorials. I believe that every TA has different ways to grade the TBW. Even though not explicitly mentioned, I’m pretty sure that my TA grade us by out pre-TBW reflection and post-TBW reflection as well as our class participation. TBWs are fun in nature but having said that, if you wanna do well, you have to show that you understand what was taught like the theory and articulate those through reflection or class participation.

Design Thinking Journal (DTJ)
Format is like ILA but much longer. The questions are also more of reflective instead of about the process. This has a highest weightage so make sure to do it well and do it throughout the sem. Don’t be like me and start doing on the week of submission. This will be submitted once in Week 6 (yes, the same as one of the ILAs) and Week 13 (together with millions of things) so yea. I think each TA has different expectation on DTJ but treat it like your diary. Make it unique to you.

Results:
Interim (2TBW, 3ILA, DTJ): EE (≥ 32.5% / 45%)
The other 55% weren’t disclosed (at least officially)

Expected Result: A-/A
Actual Result: A

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
AY22/23 S2
Tutor: Benjamin

Assessment:
30% Individual Assignment
15% Presentation
20% Final Essay
15% Poster
20% Class Participation 

Opinion: 
Content taught is quite interesting and if you take A level GP before, you would be familiar with some of the topics like race and ethnicity or social inequality. This mod is paired with HSS and I think it’s quite fitting given the workload of this mod as compared to HSA. My tutor is chill and fun too. I enjoyed his tutorials because he made everyone sit in a circle when he’s leading the class discussion. My tip is to start planning things early as well as knowing the requirement clearly. Consult your TA if possible.

Individual Assignment (IA):
1000 (± 10%) words essay about your history and how it’s linked to the content taught. You are also required to “interview” someone to learn more about your history and include that in your submissions. I made a mistake of not linking to tutorials content but only lecture content so I did not get upper quartile. 

Presentation:
There are 5 tutorials and 4 groups. So from the 2nd tutorial onwards, there will be 1 group presenting for each tutorial. The group will present on what was taught in lecture for that period and the assigned reading. After they present, they will lead a class discussion which you can be creative on this. I was in the first group to present and I think we are kinda disadvantaged cuz we haven’t seen anyone presented yet. However, I think the bar is lower if you present earlier.

Poster:
Your group will have to conduct a field trip and take photo. The field trip can be anywhere except like a place which they will specify. Your group will also have to do additional research then make an A2 poster of it. Each tutorial slot will vote 1 to be the best then that one will “represent” your tutorial group and there will be another voting based on the location of the sites in the posters.

Field Based Essay:
This is the extension of the poster but individual. It will be 750 (±10%) words essay about a specific site within the location that your group went. Link this to the content taught. For me, I linked to 3 main themes but apparently it was quite diluted and fragmented.

Results:
Individual Assignment: 75/100 (Stats: 69/73/77)
Field based Essay: 75/100 (Not disclosed but tele poll shows most in 70-74 followed by 75-80 range.
Expected Result: B+/A-
Actual Result: A-

message:

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
TakenAY22/23 Sem 1

15% (2 x 7.5% Reflective Essays)
25% (best 5 out of 6 x 5% Quizzes)
10% Final Quiz
5% Meme Competition
25% (5 x 5% Group Summary)
5% Survey + Peer Review
15% Tutorial Participation

Course Structure:
The idea of this mods is learning concepts like metaphor, importance of history, immorality, etc through the given texts. There were a few texts ranging from 4 pages to 100+ pages. Lecture is usually about the concepts more than the texts themselves. There are 5 tutorials in total. Tutorial is dependent on your tutor. My tutor were Melissa and we usually apply the concepts taught rather than dwelling into the texts. 

Meme competition:
Just have fun with it and create a meme about stuff taught in this mod. As long as we submit something, we get 4.4/5. Remember that the target audience is a bunch of boomers (the staff) so don’t use gen Z humour ☠️

Tutorial participation:
My tutor was pretty chill about this, it’s not like the more you talk the more class part you have but more like if you do the poll then that’s 1 point. So basically for my class it’s free marks unless we are not engaged at all.

Group summary:
There are 5 of them. Each group is supposed to come up with a 400 words write up answering one of the two questions given in each tutorial. Deadline is 1 week after the tutorial. This one is dependent on the group. Personally I got okayish group mates. We are all nice to each other but we usually don’t have much direction when it comes to meetings. Also, we didn’t have any carry because our majors are not related to this mod and we are also not necessarily good at English to express what we think well. My tutor gives comment after every summary but will not review the marks. I think my group did not do very well for these summaries based on the comments 🥲

Quizzes:
Each quiz has 10 MCQs and some of them have multiple correct answers. They also didn’t mention how many correct answers are there. Honestly I didn’t really care and only actually tried for my 1st and 2nd quiz. The rest I just use telegram. But please don’t be like me if you can because using Tele purely will not yield exceptional result but only around the median. The first and second quiz took the latest attempt but the 3rd onwards took the best out 3 attempts. The content of quiz is doable honestly but I was just really lazy to analyse the questions and the answers. It’s best to do quiz after your tutorial.

Final quiz was the same thing but double the weight for each question. What I found annoying is that there were multiple answers questions but only one correct answer. For me, 3 of the questions I chose 2 answers but because there were only 1 correct answer, I got 0 for all 3 questions. So at the end I got 6 point something out 10 only 🥲

Reflective Essays
There were 4 questions and we had to choose 2 to write about. Each essay max 400 words iirc and we have 1 week. The questions might be different so I won’t say much but for these essay, I only wrote about the first text (the 4 pages long one) so I’ll see how it goes 🤡

Opinion:
Content was quite interesting and it will make you look at things in a different way. It makes you question what set humans apart from says animal or god. In movies, what set humans apart from demons, devils, aliens and stuff like that. The texts are classics so if you read it, it just means you read a classic text which is not a bad thing at all. I do like the content but I did not put much effort into learning them because I also no time for this 🤧

Expected Grade: B-/B
Actual Grade: B+

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
Taken in AY22/23 Sem 1
Lecture: A/P Dlip Raghavan

15% (12 x 0.8% Weekly Quizzes)
20% (4 x 5% Homework)
5% Lab Assignment
60% Final

Course Structure:
There’s a lecture every week and a 1 hour optional tutorial. We bid for tutorial slots but in reality we can just go to any preferred slot. There is a set if questions to be done every week and the solution will be discussed during tutorial.

Weekly Quizzes:
About the current lecture, quite doable but only 2 attempts so need to really be careful. It wasn’t timed but there’s a deadline every week. When in doubt, always consult telegram.

Homework: 
50 marks each. These questions are quite similar to tutorial questions and the topics tested are taught around the time the homework questions are released. We have 1 week to complete each homework. Personally I collaborated with my friends for homework as we compare answers and help each other to unstuck and point out each other’s mistakes. I think it also depends on the tutor but mine did help me with one of the questions in the homework. It’s also quite normal to get full mark for homework but I only got one full mark 🥲, my marks were 49, 46, 49, 50. 

Finals:
6 questions which consisted of 4 computational questions and 2 proving questions. 100 marks in 2 hours. I think the finals for this semester was very doable and I even manage to do some proving questions which usually I can’t do. There are also a list of skills to have for finals so just ensure that you have those skills then you will get full marks for 4 computational questions. 

Opinion:
As someone who is pretty good with H2 Math, it didn’t help at all. If anything, my H3 knowledge was more useful in this mod due to the proving questions. However, the computational questions are quite similar to H2 Math in the sense that it can be trained and there’s always a fix way to do it. I can see this mod will be useful and I hope they could have showcased it more instead of just having a lot of rigorous and proving to things that we can’t even imagine. 

Expected Grade: A
Final Grade: A

author: Russell Saerang
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Lecturer: Ashok Charan (and the CFG team) Assessment: 10% In-class Participation 40% Open-book MCQ Test 50% Group Project Overview The module teaches you how to run a business, make decisions, and observe how the market works over time. It started off with marketing introductions, financial management, moving on to advertising, team development and conflict management as well as business strategies, continued with business presentations, product development, negotiations, and many more related, finally ending with price and promotion. Module Difficulty As someone who is not an expertise in this particular field, the lecture contents were understandable in most aspects because they reflect the way things work in real life, but some required more time to digest because they were rather technical. That being said, it was still in the easy side of the difficulty spectrum. The workload below is measured weekly. Lectures - 3h While the timetable says 3 hours of lecture, it usually took at most only a couple. They were mainly brought by Ashok as the main lecturer, while the remaining were covered by the CFG team. As you were to be assigned into groups in the project, the in-lecture groupwork, if any, will be done mainly within that same project team. I could deal with the lecture contents, though some were easier to skim through yourself instead of listening to it due to the lecture pace. The Project The course mainly relies on the marketing analytics simulation website developed by Ashok himself (I think). This is where the group project went on for almost the entire semester, observing the changes in volume, price, profit, and many more metrics as each group makes a decision on any business transactions such as negotiating with other groups, adjusting the price of certain products, and many more. That being said, I personally had little to no idea of what I should've done in the website, so my group just ensured that any decision that we made was compatible and that would be it. Near the end of the semester, each group will present their business review, how their business and products fare with the others, and what measures can be taken next. You will be given some past presentations as a benchmark or a sample run, so this part wasn't that worrying for me. Personal Opinion Is it an easy module? Probably not among the easiest ones. Is it light enough for you to go with the module flow? Certainly. I did learn a thing or two, though I probably could've taken away more tricks up the sleeve. What a way to end my journey in NUS with this module. Expected grade: CS
thread_title: CFG2100 Introduction to Decision-Making in Business

author: Russell Saerang
message: AY23/24 Sem 2 Assessment: 2x10%+20% Self-reflections 3x20% MCQ Quizzes Overview The module teaches you the basics of investing and what motivates one to do so. You learn about the investing horizons, the different types of investing, the pros of cons of doing each one of them, and how to achieve a balanced investing portfolio and lifestyle. Module Difficulty Very easy. Simply take some time off to complete the e-learning modules on your own time and that would be it. The self-reflection activity didn't take long either because they are rather simple reflections. Personal Opinion Just take it, the earlier the better (requires you to take CFG1003 as well which is also very light). Absolutely no harm doing so. In fact, you get to learn a thing or two about investing! Expected grade: CS
thread_title: CFG1004 Financial Wellbeing - Art and Science of Investing

author: Russell Saerang
message: AY23/24 Sem 1 Assessment: 10% Fortnightly Logs 40% Performance Assessment by supervisor 20% Internship Presentation 30% Internship Report Overview This module was my final year internship (FYI) module, which was one of my requirements for graduation (otherwise would be DSA4199 which is FYP). TLDR, you simply do an internship during the semester, get allocated a staff assessor/advisor to keep track of your internship progress, and then present your progress in the form of one presentation and one final report. Module Difficulty Depends on your internship, how you can describe the process of how things work there to a certain extent. All deliverables other than the Fortnightly logs are due at the end of the semester. There will be a staff visit within the semester but they're mainly just chill one-or-two-hour sessions. Personal Opinion Two tips that I would like to share are to have a good communication with both your company supervisor and your staff assessors/advisors, and to try relate your internship as best as you can with the modules you have taken in your entire DSA course, especially when writing it on the internship report and presentation. One irk, probably major, is the lack of grade feedback that's given throughout the course. You will probably get some verbal feedbacks from your staff assessor, but that's about it. Since almost all professors in the department were staff assessors of this course, I didn't really know how standardised the gradings are, let alone the bell curve since there's no partial grades. Regardless, if you're not doing FYP and you're in my batch I guess you had no choice. The next batches have a different set of final year graudation requirement. Expected grade: B
thread_title: DSA4299 Applied Project in Data Science and Analytics

author: Russell Saerang
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1 Lecturer: Young Lee Assessment: 10% Homework/Exercise 30% Midterm 20% Project Part I 40% Project Part II Overview The module is basically some concepts you might have known being recalled such as regression techniques, classification techniques, Bayesian methods, neural networks, language models, and VAEs; and then along the way comes the project that feels just like DSA3101.
Module Difficulty The module itself feels like DSA3101 practically and DSA4212 theoretically, so it's just somewhere around the middle of the difficulty line.
The workload below is measured weekly. Lectures - 4h While the timetable says 4 hours of lecture, Prof Young usually just goes through the lecture in an hour or two and then dismisses the class. The lectures were however not recorded but the slides were given anyways. There were a week of two where the lectures were a guest lecture, so that's pretty refreshing considering we're learning from the ones directly into the industry.
The Project The project consists of two parts: creating a neural network to predict some given data (which its result would be compared amongst other project teams, allegedly), and exploring AI/ML problems in healthcare/medicine (create a proposal, make a technical report, and present it).
