message: (repost since the old one is uneditable) AY20/21 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof. Wang Fei Assessment: 15% Homework Assignments, 10 each weighs 1.5% 40% Tests, 2 each weighs 20% 5% Lab Quiz 40% Final Exam Overview This module covers about functions, intuitive and precise definitions of limit, continuity, differentiation, integration and its techniques and applications, inverse and transcendental functions, and finally ODE (ordinary differential equation). In addition, the lab class uses SageMath as the helping language (quite similar to Python) which covers about basic syntaxes, how to compute limits, how to differentiate, how to integrate, and how to solve ODEs. Module Difficulty In a nutshell, this is a further recap of what you have learned in H2 Mathematics, it's not much different in terms of concepts. The module is OTOT when I took it. This means there is no fixed timing for lectures and tutorials every week but they will upload the videos on a certain schedule. There will be a homework assignment every week to be completed until there are ten of them, each covering a single lecture topic, a single-topic tutorial questions every week and a SageMath note every two weeks until there are five. The first tests covers the first half of the whole module content while the second test tcovers almost all the whole module content with the same amount of question, so just recap the concepts of the first half of the module and you're good for the second test! Personal Opinion The pace is really really on your control because it's an OTOT module. I do not know for the following semester but for me it's manageable. I like the accent of the lecturer (Prof. Wang Fei) but aside from that I found the lecture's content very understandable without me repeating it. There may be some point where it doesn't even exist in H2 but trust me it's related, so it's not impossible to understand it, such as the well-known precise definition of limit and the fundamental theorem of calculus, which are new to me but fun to know. The tutorial sheets, as I mentioned previously, are given for you to solve beforehand and the solutions will be provided after that. Although I found the tests to be a challenging time trial, the results were pretty decent and much around what I expected. The lab quiz is quite chilling for me, for it's quite similar to Python so I had a quick understanding on the notes. The final exam is doable, except for the last question. The rest is very similar to the past papers and regardless of the allocated time there should be some sparked ideas on the questions. Aside from that, I was very satisfied I finished the rest :-) My own suggestion for the final exam, explore all the past year papers as quick as possible so that it may be brought up during the consultation sessions provided, if you wish to. They are very similar time to time, year by year. Final Comments (or maybe the TLDR part) Despite the module being a bit different this sem (OTOT unlike usual), it's still a module that you can go through and finish. Again, it's a further recap of H2 calculus, hence you might understand some parts already. I believe you can have fun learning about the very basics of calculus. Expected grade : A Final grade : *inhales*

author: Siong Hwee
message: AY 22/23 On a contrary to the module prerequisite, Prof Halim does not require cs2030 (s) to take this module. Competitiveness: on 28/11/2022, registration was open to everyone. on 11/12/2022, registration was closed (39 ppl have passed >500 kattis pts/ have past cp/mo achievements) There were ~50 ppl in total competing for the 39 slots. By recess week, only 29 people remained (not me, skill issue) Benefits: Free dinners, free merch and quant firm connections, see the ICPC doner list. These firms will literally throw money at you if you take this module. If you have been coasting about in NUS, you will learn about humility in this module. Cons: pressure (public scoreboard , weekly contests) massive time sink low skill floor and high skill ceiling. Personal opinion: I learned more from this module about what I wanted in university than CP. You will learn about your FYP in nus, see the might of the giants around you and properly deal with the sleep - study - socialize trilemma. For the mere mortals that decide to tread this path, I wish you all the best. Expected Grade: B+ Actual Grade : W

author: Siong Hwee
message: AY 22/23 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof Yap Von Bing Assessment: 20% in-lecture quizzes (best 20 out of 21) 10 % Assignment 10% tutorial attendance ( 8/10) 60% finals Module difficulty: The lectures are over the place with the the Prof having the habit of going off tangent and also revising previous lecture notes in current lecture. TBH, the content of the module is trivial and can be self-studied. I would not have went for lectures but the quizzes were in lecture and had to be completed in 5 mins. The Prof has unannotated lecture notes, you need to annotate them. Tutorial covers extension of lecture material. The prof likes to link tutorials together so not doing tutorials might lead to a snowball effect in knowledge acquisition. I was a little confused after tutorial 8 and had to bring in the tutorial to do during my cs2040 PE. Assignment: R code for monte carlo approximation, doable in <30mins. Pseudo code is given too Exam: All the exam questions were taken from the tutorial, boring and much easier than the practice paper he created. Personal Opinion: You will most likely not use the content covered in this module. Unless you want to work as a quant trader, do reinforcement learning/simulation based methods or do more stats mods, you have to go out of your way to use the module content. Overall, the Prof has some problems in the execution of the module, but it might be better in future sems as this is his first iteration in a while. Workload 4/10 (I redid all the tutorials + read lecture notes in reading week) Enjoyable 5/10 Usefulness 5/10 Expected grade: A

author: Siong Hwee
message: AY22/23 Sem 1 Lecturer: Prof. Vik Gopal Tutorial: Russell Saerang + Carel C Assessment: 10% DataCamp assignments 3x 10% Assignments 20% Midterm 40% Finals Module Difficulty: Tidyverse go brrrrrrrr. Jokes aside, the module is basically pandas + mat plot in R, with some functional programming. Tutorials: Covers the previous week's content, it is very important to do. I skipped doing tutorials after week 10 and I paid the price in the form of an horrendous plot for my finals. I attended Russell's tutorials up to week 8/9 before I went to one of Carel's classes as it was more convenient. Both of them are 10/10. Exams: Midterm was a 4 hour open-internet take home exam The fivenum sum was 0 18 24 32 47. I personally had to search on stack overflow for panda commands and their corresponding tidyverse commands as I did not practice at all for midterm. The midterm was not doable for most of the cohort. Advise: Get good at tidyverse or u going to get rekt. Finals was made easier, with majority of the code being from relevant tutorials. However, I struggled with coming up with a creative plot. Personal opinion: Vik makes this module interesting because of his teaching attitude. However after taking this module, I realized I have subpar data visualization skills and would probably not work in a consulting related field. Workload 1/10 Enjoyability 7/10 Usefulness 7/10 Expected grade: A

author: small monkey
message: Module Overview Semester Taken ---> AY25/26 Sem 1 Instructor ---> Imran Bin Tajudeen Reason for Taking the Module ---> Clear C&E CHS pillar Expected grade ---> A- Final grade ---> A My background ---> 1. Y4 DSA + CS minor + stats spec Workload & Time Commitment Commitment ---> Weekly Assignment Frequency ---> 1x group essay + 1x personal reflection essay Overall Workload Rating ---> heavy workload Nature of Assessments ---> individual + group components Percentage Breakdown Sorry i forgot. However, there was 1. Tutorial and workshop attendance 2. two to three compulsory visits to interviewees houses 3. Final group project presentation 4. Final group project essay 5. Personal reflection General Review During this rendition of the course, there were three big tutorial groups we could choose from and each tutorial group would be graded VERY differently so we had to choose wisely from the first two weeks. I chose my tutorial slot because it seemed like the least commitment. For lectures, all three tutorial groups were learning the same thing (mostly about singapore's historical malay studies). I went for the first lecture and skipped thereafter since lectures were recorded and not graded. Given that I was doing a full-time internship along with this course, I deprioritized this course naturally. However, for bare minimum, I still had to attend classes/workshops weekly. Important: DO NOT BE FOOLED BY what you see on nusmods. There are additional exposure sessions that may be required from your tutorial slot. Under prof imran, we had a visit to URA, and 2x training sessions at NUS museum, between 2~3 visits to our interviewees house. Including the biweekly tutorial, it is actually a weekly commitment to the course, with some weeks where you have to commit 2 days including travelling a distance to interviewee houses if you stay further. However, since my batch was a brand new course, prof imran was very kind and fair. When I had questions regarding certain tutorial lessons regarding the lost history of Jalan Eunos (if you take this course you may be exposed to the missing histories of various kampung), he answered fervently and succintly. It's refreshing to listen to prof imran's answers as it is driven by his own research. Because of the constant back and forth with prof, I was able to score A. For instance, the instructions for the final project was slightly ambiguous and I clarified with prof Imran, and the result of that was our group doing excellent in the final presentation. My recommendation to future students is to ALWAYS clarify with prof Imran if there is any dissonance between given instructions, this would help you obtain a good grade as you work towards what prof really wants. In terms of pre-req knowledge, i'd say none is required. In fact, I believe that you'd score even better if you build good rapport with your prof AND be prepared and highly sensible during the interviews. As a bonus, I got to try out authentic Javanese food cooked by my interviewees and hear some very interesting histories of singapore pre-1965. Some of these stories are saliently missing from Singapore's narratives but through the interviews, I got to gain some unique insights that cannot be googled. Good luck to future batches taking this course! If you simply cannot commit weekly, i'd recommend not taking this course. However, if you have the time, I'd recommend this course strictly for exposure + clearing CHS in a single semester.
thread_title: GEN2009 Voices from the ground: stories & community narratives

author: small monkey
message: Module Overview Semester Taken ---> AY25/26 Sem 1 Instructor ---> ZHOU Wang Intake size ---> ~27 (no bellcurve) Reason for Taking the Module ---> Expand knowledge in statistics Expected grade ---> B Final grade ---> A- My background ---> 1. Y4 DSA (major done); 2. CS (minor done); 3. Only Wisharts Distribution learnt from ST3131 in University of Toronto exchange; 4. Did a data engineering internship during this semester involving a lot of pyspark and product management; Workload & Time Commitment Commitment ---> A+/A -> Consistency every week A- and below -> Low commitment Assignment Frequency ---> Two big assignments Overall Workload Rating ---> light workload Nature of Assessments ---> individual only Percentage Breakdown Assignment - 40% (obtained 40%) Final paper - 60% (unreleased) General Review I went for the first lecture and skipped thereafter since lectures were recorded and not graded. In general, statistics concepts are very vague to me so I usually just do the tutorials to see how the concepts learnt are applied in questions. Given that I was doing a full-time internship along with this course (**not registered under UPIP), I couldn't give my 100% for this course. However, I spent a good 2 to 3 days prior to each big assignment and finals preparing. For the big assignments I scored an average of nearly full marks. For finals, the results weren't released by I scored decently I guess. Given that you've understood how to use the concepts taught in lectures in tutorial questions and assignments, if you are able to give yourself 1 week cumulative preparation time for finals, you would easily score full marks for this course. It's funny because prof copied 2.5 questions (out of 6) from tutorials and the assignment, so if you included it in your cheatsheet, you would've gotten an easy A. I redid the entire tutorial and assignment up to 2-3 days before so even though some of the examples did not end up in my cheatsheet, I was able to recall the answering techniques. In terms of past courses that were helpful, definitely any linear algebra course would help with the first three chapters. ST3131 from my exchange introduced Hotelling's T2 and Wishart distributions so it was basically redoing the same thing again. The other topics get more confusing in my opinion, but definitely doable as it is quite algorithmic such as canonical correlation and factor analysis. Good luck to future batches taking this course! Would highly recommend so that you can break out of the uni-variate calculus and start joining the big leagues.

author: small monkey
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Lecturer: Associate Professor Leung Man Chun

Structure:
1x Midterms - 20%
1x Finals - 80%

Workload: 1/10
Easiest math module. This module is astronomically simpler than calculus MA2002. Lesser proofs and more formulas. A friend of mine who attends lectures regularly said Prof Leung explains concepts as well as a Ted Talk. Personally, I didnt bother going for any lectures and used the textbook and tutorials to mug for finals. Heres a tip from me: I highly suggest referring to the Thomas Edison textbook if you do not understand any concepts. I learnt tests for convergence/divergence really quickly compared to the lecture notes given.

Results:
Midterms - 19.0%/20.0% 
*Finals - ?/80.0%
*component not released

Final rating 10/10
This is the module you can definitely score well in. That can be bad too because it means that the score distribution could be severely left skewed. However, I screwed up my finals. I only realised careless mistakes that could've cost me the 5 marks straight into the drain. Also, if they tell you not to do qn 1b, do not waste your time doing qn1b HAHAHA.

message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Tutor: Frau Chen Ing Ru

Structure:
3x Vocabulary quiz (30%) - once every month
1x Semester Test 1 Midterms (10%)
1x Semester Test 2 Finals (10%)

Workload: 8/10
Expect the workload of LAG1201. If you enjoyed LAG1201 or want a language certificate eqeuivalent to A1 standard, take this module. Just as 1k german , I spent a great deal of my time memorising German vocabulary for the quizzes. Although I prepared for the semester tests, I did great and average for semester test 1 and 2 respectively. Despite my review on LAG1201, we were introduced heavily on dative and accusative word forms and SVO and OVS sentence structure. Some verbs naturally flow better with OVS (eg. gefallen, schmecken, other dative words). We finally also learnt basics real world concepts such as colours, clothings, shopping, visiting the doctor, travelling overseas, our dream house. Frau Chen is a really good german teacher. While one might wish to have a native german teacher, Frau Chen has extensive knowledge and experience in the culture and landscape of germany. Her versatility in german, english and chinese proves that she is excellent at delivering the materials required for learning.

author: small monkey
message:
AY23/24 Sem 1
Co-lecturer: Dr. Wang Fei, Dr. Wertz, Timothy Michael

Structure:
4x Quiz (20%) - once every 2 weeks 
4x Homework Assignment (20%) - alternates with quiz weeks
1x Finals (60%)

Workload: 5/10
Previous semesters had both mid-term and finals while this semester only has finals. Discipline is the key for proper understanding - something that I lacked due to the absence of mid-terms. As an average NUS student, I take 4 other modules that have continual assessments. I decided to focus on this module only during critical periods: 2 days before quiz and homework assignment submission. This was my greatest downfall as I would often refer to answers and not fully understand what I wrote. I had time during reading week to complete all 11 tutorials properly but I often forgo the conceptual understanding of the concepts and brute force an algorithm to solve questions (not ideal).

Results:
Quiz - 19.5%/20.0% (minimally discriminating)
Homework assignments - 17.5%/20.0% did not notice the first assignment due - possibly costing a grade (minimally discriminating)
*Finals - ?/60.0% (assuming slightly right skewed score distribution)
*components not released

Final rating 5/10
Overall I enjoyed the concepts taught in this module and would recommend for future batches to ensure that they are highly consistent from the first tutorial. During your reading week, REVISE everything from tutorial 1. Most students (including myself) focus on concepts taught for the second half of the semester, only for our profs to include 60% of concepts fromm tutorial 1-4 for the finals exam. Only gave it a low rating because I did not study hard enough for this course and it's not SU-able. Take this review with a pinch of salt, if our lecturers see this they might change what is examinable.

author: small monkey
message: Taken in AY 22/23 Sem 2 TA: Gwendaline Ng Course Components Tutorial / Microsoft Teams Participation - 20% Video Presentation - 40% Reflections - 40% Sentiments As a DSA student, this module seemed hella fluffed to me. I came in with the expectation to SU. Every lecture was surrounding freedom and broken up to 3 components... 1. The Good 2. The Bad 3. The Uncertain Before tutorials began, the lectures seemed confusing as there was no "expected" answer to the concepts taught. After tutorials began, my tutor made this module a lot simpler to understand. TL;DR a student is expected to argue from multiple perspectives and present evidence and logical reasoning to substantiate their points. I was able to be a class part slut and freely express my thoughts every week during tutorials and when my tutor opens a new prompt on Microsoft Teams. Although it may have been troll at the beginning, I decided to follow the course requirements. For the final group video presentation (which I believe to be the most discriminating component for scoring well), my group mates were not the most proactive bunch. I had to consult my tutor alone because my group mates couldn't find the time. My group mates also seemed very unsure about the content, which meant that I had to proof check the work they did, and correct many errors along the way. Expected Grade: A- Final Grade: A+ Final Words This is one of the modules that can boost your CAP. I recommend putting in the effort, the workload is little so it is possible to get your As here even if you have no interest in a FASS-related mod

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message: Taken in AY25/26 SEM 1 with Prof Ren Weiqing Project Topic: PS2510-03 Textbook: Monte Carlo Simulation with Applications to Finance by Hui Wang The Project Topics List: PS2510-01: Hypergraphs with Applications in Data Science W Prof Han Fei (MA2101, MA2108) PS2510-02: Hilbert’s 10th Problem W Prof Dilip Raghavan (MA2202, MA2108) PS2510-03: Monte Carlo Methods and their Applications to Finance W Prof Ren Weiqing (Coding skills in any preferred programming language) PS2510-04: Annotated Translation of and Introduction to a Classic Mathematical Paper in a Foreign Language W Prof Charmaine Sia (MA2104, MA2108, MA3211) PS2510-05: Foundations of Large Language Models and their Ethical Implications W Prof Soh Yong Sheng (HS1501) PS2510-06: Markov Chains and Mixing Time W Prof Sun Rongfeng (MA3238/ST3236) PS2510-07: On Magic Squares/Cubes/Circles/Triangles/Stars/Pentagrams/Mexagrams, Vertex-Magic Graphs and Edge-Magic Graphs W Prof Tan Ban Pin (MA2214 and MA3233) PS2510-08: Lie Algebras W Prof Tan Kai Meng (MA2101/MA2101S, MA3201) PS2510-09: Infinitesimal Calculus W Prof Wong Tin Lok (MA3210) There was a great variety of topics specialising in different domains. You have to apply for this module on the MA4198 website through an application form where you rank the topics that you want. You have to apply for this module early so do check the deadline for application. For example, I had to apply by 4 August 2025 0900. I personally wanted a manageable topic so I chose option 3 then option 5. Note that you might not always get your first choice. I think that every professor conducts this module differently. Prof Ren Weiqing is quite a chill and nice professor. He is quite knowledgeable and willing to answer any questions. It is preferable you prepare something to show him and bring questions to him. If not, the session kind of becomes ineffective for you. His teaching style is really relaxed and easy going. For our paper writing, he gave us a lot of freedom and leeway, as long as it is about the topic and Monte Carlo Simulations. If you are slower, he is also willing to adapt to your pace and not scold you. I really appreciated that he was my professor for this module. Highly recommend if his name pops up anywhere in the future. For the timetable, we met biweekly on Wednesdays starting from week 1. First meeting was just professor introducing himself and the topic. Subsequent meetings was the group presenting what we have done and any questions/problems we come across. Presentation was done a bit earlier for our group on Friday Week 11. Note that each student will submit their own report. Prof Akiko Ishii is quite a gentle professor. She teaches quite ok but can be often too soft.

The reflection essay was to attend a tea ceremony and write a 600 word reflection about it. The group presentation was a 12 minute presentation on a research question about Japanese food. It can be either based on Portrayal of Food in a Text or Japanese Food in Singapore. My group had 4 members and we did Japanese food in My Neighbour Totoro & Spirited Away. The In-class test was in week 13 with 3 short-answer questions and 1 medium-length essay about the course materials. The test covered rice, hospitality, umami and gender roles.

Personally, this module was quite interesting and informative. I will say the module is also quite light and chill. Highly recommended if you will like to know more about Japanese food and its significance from past to present. All in all, it is quite a straightforward module.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY24/25 SEM 1 with Prof Yan Yingwei

The Module covers the following topics
1. Introduction to GIS
2. GIS Data Model I: Vector Data Model
3. GIS Data Model II: Raster Data Model
4. GIS Data Visualization and Spatial Representation
5. Spatial Reference and Coordinate Systems
6. Geometric Transformation
7. Spatial Data Quality and Spatial Data Editing
8. Spatial Database and Attribute Data Management
9. Spatial Analysis - Vector Data Analysis
10. Spatial Analysis - Raster Data Analysis
11. Spatial Statistical Analysis
12. Conclusion

Module Structure :
Lab assignments : 15% * 4
Finals : 30%
Participation : 10%

The module teaches about geographical mapping and the works. It teaches you how maps are crafted, created and analysed. Laws of mapping and methods of analysing are introduced. The software ArcGis is also used for laboratory sessions. 

Module Intro from Prof:
Welcome to the fascinating world of Geographic Information Systems (GIS). This course focuses on the important concepts and the practical use of GIS in problem-solving in both the social and physical sciences. Topics to be covered include what a GIS is, how things in the real world can be represented on a map, and how we can use GIS to conduct spatial analysis. This course is designed as learning through practicing, so practical laboratory exercises utilizing GIS software will be major classroom activities. This course is mounted for undergraduate students throughout NUS with interests in GIS applications in sciences, social sciences, engineering, and business analysis.

Prof Yan Yingwei is quite chill and portrays himself as a fun guy.

Laboratory sessions were biweekly and we needed to submit an assignment after each session based on ArcGis. Participation marking was a little sketchy and Pollev was used to track participation during lectures. Finals had 10 MCQs and 4 short-answer questions. Closed book with no cheat sheet allowed.

Personally, this module is alright in workload. I learnt quite a bit on mapping and its analysis. The lab assignment marking was quite strict and particular. However, the lab handout provided is step by step and quite good in guiding. The finals being closed book was kind of unfortunate as the finals test the whole module and I could not remember every single minor details. 

Overall, it is an alright module. Good for students with an interest in learning about maps and ArcGIS.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY24/25 SEM 1 with Dr Zeehan Jaafar and Prof Henry Mok

The Module covers the following topics
1. Introduction
2. Chemistry of life
3. Cell structure and function
4. Energy of life
5. DNA and hereditary
6. Gene expression
7. Biotechnology
8. Evolution
9. Biodiversity
10. Plant form function
11. Animal form function

Module Structure :
Lab assignments : 9% * 4
Tutorial assignments : 4% * 2
Museum assignment : 6%
In-class quizzes : 5% * 2
E-exams : 20% * 2

The module teaches about basic biology knowledge across plants and animals. From the start of life to DNA to organs.

Module Intro from Prof:
This is an introductory module that explores what a living thing is, the basics of life, and the science behind it. It is structured in two parts, looking in detail about the cell and molecular biology of living organisms  (Weeks 1-6), and also the big picture of biological organization and diversity (Weeks 7-12).

The course will introduce the chemistry of life and the unit of life. We will tackle the question of how traits are inherited and even delve into the field of biotechnology, including its applications and the ethical issues involved. We will explore the diversity of life on earth and discuss on how life on earth possibly came about. We will reconstruct relationships between organisms and how biologists try to classify and make sense of the biodiversity on earth.  We will learn about both plant and animal form and function and try to understand the ecological interactions that affect ecosystems. By the end of the course, students should be able to identify the unifying concepts in biology and how they play a role in everyday life.

This course will be based 100% on continual assessment which will include tutorials, laboratory practicals as well as two e-exams (in week 6 and week 12).

Both professors were alright. Dr Zeehan Jaafar is quite passionate and Prof Henry Mok is very chill.

The first 6 weeks were done by Prof Henry Mok and the next 6 weeks were done by Dr Zeehan Jaafar. Lecture attendance was somewhat compulsory as the In-class quizzes can appear randomly during the lecture slot. There are 4 lab sessions and it is not every week. My Sem was on week 4, 5, 10 and 11. Tutorial and Museum assignments were own time own target assignments. The museum assignment required us to visit the NUS museum to seek for answers for the assignment. E-exam 1 had 40 MCQs with each having 5 options. E-exam 2 had 40 MCQs and MRQs. Both E-exam are open book without internet.

Personally, the module is quite informative. My biology knowledge stopped at the Secondary 2 level and I almost know nothing at a higher level. Hence, this module was quite heavy for me with a steep learning curve.

The quizzes and exams were quite brutal for me haha. Quiz 1 was done directly after the lecture and I blanked out. E-exam 1 was difficult for me and I tikam almost everything. The questions were difficult and I swear some of them were not taught in lecture. E-exam 2 was less brutal. Laboratory sessions and the museum assignments were quite fun though. However, their marking scheme for assignments including tutorials are very strict and particular.

All in all, this module was great at feeding me basic biology knowledge that I could not learn in Secondary School and JC. It is great for people that did not get a chance to learn biology. However, this module is not so kind and expect a difficult climb.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY24/25 SEM 1 with Prof Christian Von Der Weth

The Module covers the following topics
1. Creating and populating tables
2. Simple queries
3. Aggregate and Algebraic queries
4. Entity relationship model
5. Nested queries
6. Relational calculus and algebra
7. Programming with SQL
8. Triggers
9. Anomalies and functional dependencies
10. Boyce-codd normal form
11. Third normal form

Module Structure :
Group project 1 : 3%
Group project 2 : 6%
Group project 3 : 6%
Assignment 1 : 5%
Assignment 2 : 5%
Midterms : 25%
Finals: 50%

The module teaches about database logic and coding using pgAdmin 4.

Module Intro from Prof:
This module aims to introduce the fundamental concepts and techniques necessary for the understanding and practice of the design and implementation of database applications and of the management of data with relational database management systems.  The module covers practical and theoretical aspects of design with the entity-relationship model, theory of functional dependencies, and normalisation by decomposition in third and Boyce-Codd normal forms.  The module covers practical and theoretical aspects of programming with SQL data definition and manipulation sub-languages as well as relational algebra.

Prof Christian Von Der Weth was alright but boring at times.

Assignment 1 was a coding assignment based on a dataset and some questions. (Like CS1010s missions) Assignment 2 had 10 MRQs on database logic. Group project had us in groups of 4 to do a project together. Project 1 was creating and populating tables. Project 2 was drawing up an entity-relationship (ER) diagram. Project 3 was coding out triggers. Midterms had 8 MCQ/MRQ on ER and 4 coding questions on pgAdmin 4. It was open book with no internet. Finals had 26? MCQ/MRQ questions on database logic, triggers and ER. (Mainly database logic and I cannot remember the number of questions.) Only one paper cheat sheet was allowed. 

Personally, this module was great for me in terms of learning database coding. However, I was shocked to see how a large part of this module contains logic. I personally dislike logic and am deficient at it. I took this module thinking coding will be a larger part of this module. I was super super wrong XD.

Lecture 1, 2, 3, 5 were good for me. Then the rest were just "I don't understand what the hell is going on." This module focuses a lot on database computational logic. I honestly was totally lost despite trying hard. It is not like your typical mathematical proving questions but good logical thinking is needed. A lot of computational logic symbols are used too. Midterms had coding so I was still alright. Finals... I just tikam everything and left in 1 hour.

Overall, this module have quite a lot of computational logic. If you hate logic, this module might not be for you.

Group project 1 : 1.5/3
Group project 2 : 4/6
Group project 3 : Not released
Assignment 1 : 5/5
Assignment 2 : 1/10
Midterms : 15/25
Finals: Not released

Expected Grade : I might fail ngl. Logic is too difficult for me ><

This module gives an introduction to the design of sample surveys and estimation procedures, with emphasis on practical applications in survey sampling.  Topics include planning of surveys questionnaire construction, methods of data collection, sources of errors, basic ideas of sampling, simple random sampling, stratified, cluster and systematic sampling, sample size determination and cost.  This module is targeted at students who are interested in Statistics and are able to meet the prerequisites.

Prof Chua Tin Chiu is like an uncle professor. He is quite chill and like your typical Asian Grandfather. Perhaps Prof Gan and Prof Chua are twins lmao. His slides are alright and not too bad.

Every student is assigned 2 tutorials to do as homework assignment. With a 70% finals, there is nothing much to say. Any hardcopy material is
allowed for the finals.

Finals and homework were manageable and not too difficult. Homework typically have around 1 to 2 questions.

Personally, this module is quite standard and chill. It mainly revolves around expectations and variances of population mean and total through the understanding of survey and sampling methods. Some survey understanding and logic is needed for calculations such as population proportions and total cost of surveys.

All in all, it is an informative and manageable module.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY24/25 SEM 1 with Prof McMorran
TA: Jordan Tan

The Module covers the following topics
1. Political Ecology - Theoretical tools for understanding who controls nature
2. Defining Nature - Nature in religion, art, design
3. Anime Animals - Human/animal relations over time and in media
4. Forest History - Cycles of deforestation and reforestation
5. Sustainable Japan - SDGs, degrowth, sustainable development, and the limits of recycling
6. Feeling Pain - Modernization, industrialization, and the pain that proves humans cannot escape nature
7. National Parks - Conservation or consumption?
8. Designing for Risk
9. Depopulation - Disaster, or sustainable future?
10. Disasters - Exploring the granularity of the 2011 triple disasters and their ongoing impacts
11. Disasters Part 2 - Fast or slow? natural or man-made?
12. Conclusions: Apocalypse, Godzilla, and wrapping up the module

Module Structure :
Participation : 15%
Short Tutorial Assignments : 10% * 4
Class Leader Project : 20%
Final Paper : 25%

The module teaches about Japan's environment and sustainability. The thinking that "Japan is green." will be challenged. These issues will be discussed through the perspectives of many stakeholders and their needs. Japan's history of nature interactions will be introduced together with prominent historical events.

Module Intro from Prof:
Japan has a heavily forested landscape, a long, beautiful coastline, and a rich history of celebrating nature in religion and the arts. Japan also has a disastrous history of resource extraction, deforestation, and industrial pollution. With such contradictions, is Japan “green”? What representations and understandings of nature, uses of natural resources, consumer behaviors, waste disposal practices, and forms of energy production can be considered “green”? What can we learn from Japan’s successes and failures with respect to its uses--and abuses--of nature? This module explores these questions from a variety of perspectives, including popular culture, tourism, political ecology, geography, history, and everyday life. This module should interest anyone curious about nature, Japan, or both. You will leave the module with knowledge about Japan, and hopefully, greater understanding of the myriad challenges we face on our finite planet.

Prof McMorran is an excellent prof. He is very responsive and interactive. He teaches well and gives many insights. His lectures are very lively and enjoyable. Passionate, knowledgeable and experienced. His style is extremely inviting. I highly recommend this professor. He is probably one of the best professors I have encountered in NUS yet. My review on Prof McMorran still remains the same and positive from JS1101E.

Every lecture, there will be a 1 hour slide presentation by Prof McMorran. The last 30 mins will be led by a group of 3/4 students. The session is called "Class Leaders". The whole class is split into groups of 3/4 students and assigned a week. The group will then do an activity/discussion based on that week's topic. The tutorials were mainly discussions. The final paper was to analyze a cultural work depicting nature-society relations in Japan with a word limit of 1500. A reading and short reflection is also needed to be done before each lecture.

Lectures were quite fun and interactive. You will be "forced" to interact and discuss with other students. I was assigned Anime Animals for class leaders. My group did a class discussion regarding the political ecology of 2 movies, Princess Mononoke and Totoro. Each tutorial was unique and it includes sharings, discussions and "stakeholder debates". My final paper topic was on the relevance of political ecology to Pokémon which was quite fun to write.

Personally, this module challenged my thinking that Japan is a green nation. I gained a lot of knowledge about Japan's nature situation and how nature is sometimes abused. The problems and issues faced by Japan and Japan may not be as perfect as what one assumes to be. I personally found the module manageable. It is not too light, not too heavy. However, it might not be good to enter the lecture on a sleepy mood as you need to be attentive! I found the disasters and nuclear aspects to be interesting. It really helped me to understand Japan's perspective on nature better and that Japan might not be as green as one thinks.

All in all, it was fun taking JS2234. Highly recommended as an informative and fun module. Taking JS1101E before/with JS2234 might be beneficial but not needed as JS1101E helps to set the context for JS2234.

Class Leader Project : Anime Animals
Final Paper Topic : The relevance of Pokémon to political ecology

Participation : Not released
Tutorial 2 : 8/10
Tutorial 3 : 6/10
Tutorial 4 : 9/10
Tutorial 5 : Not released
Class Leader Project : 17/20
Final Paper : Not released

I did not do that well for the final paper. My overall argument was good, but not great. Nonetheless, it was still quite fun to craft and write the paper :)

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
AY24/25 NUSWS
Conductor: Mr Francis Tan
Percussion Tutor: Mr Joachim Theodore Lim
Teacher In Charge: Ms Sing Moh Li

This series of CFA modules started back in AY23/24. Originally, you can be a normal band member, take the CFA module and do ordinary band member work. However, for AY24/25, it is completely different. Here is my rough summary.

For CFA1101A, I think it is still the same with ordinary band member responsibilities. 
For CFA2101A and CFA3101A, it is different.
CFA2101A expects you to do exco work and take on extra responsibilities. 
CFA3101A expects you to lead and do the above too. Students also need to write a reflection paper on performance review and leadership.

The attendance policy has become stricter too.
"As this is an experiential module, student who missed classes must produce a medical certificate (MC) or other official document to explain their absence. Students who missed 2 classes without MC or official document will bear a grade penalty. In this case, the final grade for the course will be capped at a Pass (i.e. D grade, 40%) if the work produced has met the requirements of a Pass grade. Extenuating circumstances or health reasons: Students who missed 6 or more classes with MC or official documents (due to illness or extenuating circumstances) will be advised to take one of the following steps:  * Apply for Special Consideration (a long extension) to complete the work over the vacation period * Discuss with Tutor or Course Leader for an alternative assignment ( if applicable) * Withdraw (Drop) the course as they have missed too much to catch up."

There are 4 departments where the CFA2k and CFA3k students will be allocated to. Production and Logistics, Marketing and Publicity, Curatorial and Community Engagement. Each department have their own responsibilities which last over the whole semester.

NOTE: In order to be in NUSWS, you HAVE to take the CFA module. It is a hard no to be in NUSWS and not take the module.

To be honest, I am not sure why the module structure became like this. I think it is to ensure that everyone is contributing in some way and make sure that everyone treat the module seriously?

In my opinion, I feel that the introduction and restructure of this series of CFA modules is very unbecoming. It drove away people who want to just play in the band for enjoyment and passion. It drove away my whole percussion section and most of my friends. It ultimately drove me away and I dropped the module. As the module becomes "academicized", it becomes highly stressful and we are suddenly at the mercy of the bell curve again. A grade is assigned to us and once again, we are fighting each other for that A grade and to see who is more "wayang". I am someone who has an interest in music making and support Mr Francis Tan the whole way. However, as someone who has a chronic medical condition, 7 modules and a part time internship, I had to think really hard whether I should take on the CFA module.

Ngl, I feel that this CFA module is highly unnecessary and gatekeeping. For people that love to be always on their toes, this module perhaps suit your style. However, for students that just want to relax and make music, the module kind of deters them from taking it. I feel that it is unfair to the students of NUS that are already stressed from the academic workload. This module just gatekeeps us from NUSWS. We do not want to take on additional high workload which causes us to not be able to join our favourite CCAs and enjoy our hobbies.

To CFA, if you see this review, please reconsider your decisions. I am willing to come and discuss on this matter. It is not fair and denies us from experiencing and enjoying the NUS student life. As someone who has chronic medical issues and yet an interest in music making as a percussionists, I feel very upset that I am denied the opportunity to continue playing in NUSWS. I am unsure if there are other students out there that share the same sentiments as me. However, this is my opinions and what I think about the series of CFA modules. You are trying to promote performing arts to the young but yet driving away the current performing arts members. Are we really that desperate to grow the performing arts scene in Singapore? If any of my comments above are wrong and inaccurate, please correct me.

I will not blame Mr Francis Tan at all. I feel that he is just following instructions and trying his best. However, I hope that CFA in NUS can be more understanding. I remember when the band was huge and strong. However, with the introduction of this module, I felt the spirit perishing. I understand that students in the NUSWS are somewhat dispensable and we always can be replaced by year 1/2 students. However, please consider it from our perspective.

To the incoming batch of NUSWS, my advice is to fully understand what you are getting into for NUSWS and CFA modules. You are not guaranteed to get an A. Not everyone in the module will get an A. Even if you did your best and think you deserve an A, you might end up with a B. You will be fighting with your other band members to be at the top. You have to be always on your toes and be "wayang".

Is this what CFA and NUSWS has become? A competitive stressful environment just like our academic modules? Is this really the right and best way moving forward? Are we really promoting the Arts or are we killing the enjoyment of the Arts? Do we want to become performing arts robots?

Please take this module review with a pinch of salt. However, I really hope that CFA can better/rethink it's current plan for the performing arts groups in NUS. Additionally, Mr Francis Tan has been very active, participative and passionate. However, I feel that Ms Mohli can do more and be more participative. It feels like only Mr Tan is trustable and he is solo handling everything. I also feel that Mr Joachim can be less strict and more kinder as I am here as a NUSWS member, not a percussion major that needs to be extremely serious. Mr Joachim also gets distracted easily and I feel he needs to lead more too.

   https://uploads.disquscdn.com/images/01924011e907dea4adf726f06ba28589665f515366d1ea1d4528726fb0ce3408.jpg

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM 1 and 2 with NUSWS
Conductor: Mr Francis Tan
Percussion Tutor: Mr Joachim Theodore Lim
Teacher In Charge: Ms Sing Moh Li

Module Structure :
Attendance
Performance
Tutor Review
Peer Review
Event Participation

This module is for those in NUSWS (and certain other performing art CCAs). For students in NUSWS, it is compulsory. It is a year long 4MC module and assigns a grade based on your performance and attendance in the CCA. We are grouped according to sections of the band. (I think it is to prevent bell curving.) I think the aim of this module is to promote and improve the performing arts scene in Singapore. This is also the first iteration of this module.

I feel that making our CCAs become a module and assigning a grade to it made NUSWS less fun and more stressful. Almost everything you do in NUSWS have to be positive and contributing. Moreover, it was also compulsory so you are unable to be part of NUSWS without the module. I was marked down for poor performance during tutorship and felt that it was unfair given my poor medical condition. I also felt that as compared to the other sections, the percussion section was graded harsher with more requirements. One example is that our parts were allocated by our tutor instead of self allocation. The module structure, requirements and expectations were not made very clear to us too. My section swapped parts and got scolded for it is one example. Another one is I did not know that we had to be "wayang" during our tutorship too. The module also had a devastating impact on our percussion strength. 

AY22/23 Sem 1: 9
AY22/23 Sem 2: LOA
Implementation of Module
AY23/24 Sem 1: 4
AY23/24 Sem 2: 1

Yes, I was the only one left in the section. It was lonely and somewhat frustrating. I had the option to leave, but I chose to stay for personal reasons. Whether I am going to continue is another story. I am not going to promote or encourage people to join NUSWS. However, I want people to know what they are getting into when they join NUSWS and pick up this module. 

Sem 1:
Role: Member of the percussion section
P6 Outreach Performance: A-
Interactive Fringe Event for P6 Students: B+

Sem 2:
Role: Section Leader of the percussion section
Intempo 2024: Triumphs of the Human Spirit

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM 2 with Prof Zhang Junyan

Disclaimer: This mod review is filled with frustration and sadness, and done by a B/C grade student. Please take this mod review with a pinch of salt. 

The Module covers the following topics
1. Transport equation, 1D wave, Multi-D wave, Causality and energy
2. Fourier transformation, Heat equation, Fourier method, Maximum principle of heat equation
3. Separation of variables, Pointwise convergence of Fourier series, Examples of 1D wave in a finite interval, Fourier series L2 theory, Eigenvalue problems
4. Calculus of variation
5. Examples of harmonic functions, Properties of harmonic functions, Poisson equation and Green's function
6. Topics related to further studies

Module Structure :
5 Homework
1 Midterm
1 Finals
Grade = max { 25% homework + 25% midterm + 50% final, 25% midterm + 75% final }

This module teaches about partial differential equation with the speciality in vector calculus. 

Prof Zhang is the professor of this module and he specializes in Analysis of nonlinear PDEs. To be honest, from the start of this module, I tried to believe that he is a nice professor who can teach well, just that the content of the module is extremely difficult. This is why I did not drop the module and persisted on. However, throughout the module, I lost faith in that belief and started to become extremely unmotivated. The professor's lecture notes and slides are not very kind to the not so smart. He occasionally skips steps and expect students to know the skipped parts. There are also around 11 announcements regarding errors in his materials. "There are some prerequisites for PDE course, basically including mathematical analysis, Fourier series and ODE". I still can understand if MA2108 and MA3220 is assumed to be known. However, MA4229 (Fourier Analysis and Approximation) ??? I was also hoping that the professor covers what he expect us to know from MA2108 and MA3220 as I did not see MA3220 and MA2108 used much. A little bit of MA2104 appeared too. This made me miss Prof Yao Yao's teachings from MA3220 which are step by step, detailed and easy to follow. I do not mind learning more stuff. However, please help the poor and not so smart souls like me. 

There are 5 homework. Each had 5 to 7 questions. They were extremely difficult. I resorted to copying the professor's lecture notes, consulting the internet and anyhow whacking it without fully understanding the question. Each homework gave me quite a headache. Prof was nice enough to give me a time extension which helped me a lot. MA2001/MA2101 appeared in homework 5 also unexpectedly. The midterms was of moderate difficulty with 5 questions. The finals was insanely difficult. I could not confidently answer any of the 7 questions in the paper and my brain just decided to shut down halfway. Convergence of summations (MA2108) appeared in question 1 and unfortunately, I forgot the test and hence wrote some nonsense. An A4 cheat sheet was allowed for the Finals and Mids. 

I wanted to achieve a dream in NUS. It was to learn as much calculus NUS had to offer as possible even though I was not good at it. Hence, my goal was MA3220 and MA4221. I was, hence, greatly disappointed with the module under Prof Zhang. The module throughout the semester gave me a lot of pain, frustration and trauma. I understand that there are different kind of PDEs. However, I had trouble linking the topics together and felt that they were just randomly inserted here and there. Basics are taught, then Fourier analysis appear, then version 2 of Fourier analysis appear, then green's theorem appears. It was a mess and I was confused. Some principles regarding the PDE's are taught like the Maximum principle. Perhaps the aim of the principle was stated somewhere, but most of the time, I do not know why I am learning about such a principle and how is it relevant. I also suppose Prof wanted to introduce vector calculus. I did learn something. However, I also felt I learnt nothing. 

All in all, I had a bad (and traumatising) module experience. I understand that other students in this module might feel differently. The previous module reviews also reviewed that Prof Zhang was great which made me take the leap of faith to take the module under him. If you are extremely well versed in MA4229, MA3220, MA3210, MA2108, MA2104, MA2101, MA2001 and idk what else, I recommend MA4221 under Prof Zhang. Otherwise, you might face challenges and hardships. As much as I tried and tried to believe, I hope I do not go through such an experience again. 

Homework: 22 + 24.5 + 25 + 20 + 22/25
Midterms: 70/100
Finals: I gave up halfway and almost wanted to walk out of the exam hall early. 

Expected Grade : I have no idea. D i guess?
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : 27

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM 2 with Prof Brett Mcinnes

The Module covers the following topics
1. Motivation and Revision from MA2001 
- Linear Transformations, Determinants, Inverses, Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues, Diagonalisation, Change of Basis
2. Vector Spaces
- Vectors, Vector spaces, Subspaces, Vector space isomorphisms, Spanning, Basis, Linear independence
3. Linear Mappings
- Linear mappings, Everything as a vector, Kernel and range, Duality, Tensor products, The matrix, Transformation multiplications, Matrix of a product, Change of basis
4. Classifying Linear Transformations
- Eigenspace, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, Fundamental theorem of algebra, Upper triangular matrices, Jordan canonical form
5. Linear Algebra and Geometry
- The dot product, Bilinear forms, Inner products, Lengths, angles and geometry, Orthonormal bases, Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization, Riesz representation, Complex cases, Schur triangularization theorem, Spectral theorem
6. Volume
- Multilinear forms, Linear algebra in 1D, Determinants and significance, Volume

Module Structure :
Quiz : 2 * 25%
Finals : 50%

Module Intro from Prof:
This module is an intensification of Linear Algebra I . If you are wondering whether it is any different from Linear Algebra I: it is indeed. Many of the topics are the same but the viewpoint is very different. You will learn a more abstract approach to linear algebra. It isn't what you think. There will be a great emphasis on motivation and on really *understanding* what linear algebra is all about. Major topics: Revision of basic linear algebra but with more emphasis on motivation and on geometric understanding. Definition of vector space. Linear transformations. Vector space isomorphisms. Finite-dimensionality. A basis is REALLY just a linear transformation. Yes, you read that right. Linear transformations are vectors. Yes, you read that right. Since the set of linear maps on a vector space is itself a vector space, it must have  bases, right? We put a lot of effort into understanding what those bases are. Matrices are just the components of a linear transformation with respect to a basis like that. Change of basis. Eigenvectors and eigenvalues. Jordan canonical form, as a basis for the classification of all linear transformations. Inner product spaces. Symmetric and Hermitian transformations.  Unitary and Special Orthogonal groups. Grand Finale: WHAT A DETERMINANT REALLY IS! No, it isn't that weird formula, it is something much deeper and in a way simpler than that thing.

Prof Brett is a really cool, fun and chill professor. He teaches really well and is awesome. His lecture notes are in a story format which was quite fun to read. It made MA2101 so much more bearable and enticing. His explanations were also very clear, well supported and detailed. 

The 2 quizzes had 4 MCQ questions each and lasted 1 hour. The MCQ questions also needed us to provide a justification for our answers. The finals was in the same format but with 5 MCQ questions. One A4 paper help sheet was allowed for all of them. 

Personally, this module was quite fun. I enjoyed this module and the professor's teachings. It made MA2101, an abstract module, pretty enjoyable. Knowledge from MA2213, MA2001 and MA3220 has helped me a lot with the understanding of this module. Chapter 1 to 5 was pretty manageable for me. Chapter 6 made me stumbled hard and I could not understand it in time for finals. The quiz and Finals questions were of moderate difficulty too. The phrasing of some questions were a little questionable. I understood that some students did not like the testing format and the way the lecture materials are presented in a story format. (They would rather see a list of theorems.)

All in all, I learnt quite a lot from this module. I highly recommend this module under prof Brett. It made learning MA2101 unexpectedly interesting and fun. 

Quiz 1: 19/25
Quiz 2: 13/25

message:

The Module covers the following topics
1. Module Introduction
2. Pharmacology & other sciences
3. To cure and alleviate: Medicinal drugs
4. To enlighten and transcend: religious and cultural drugs
5. To socialise and belong: recreational drugs
6. To Conquer and Destroy: Violent Drugs
7. To manage and prohibit drugs
8. To prevent and reduce harm from drugs
9. To control & consume: greedy drugs
10. To control & consume: greedy drugs (Part II)
11. Beyond the physical realm of drugs
12. Review

Module Structure :
Participation (tutorials etc.) : 20%
CA1 Picture-caption/Concept map : 25%
CA2 Mid-semester test : 25%
CA3 Group work (strategy report): 10%
CA4 Group work (debate): 20%

The module teaches about drugs in general. Firstly, the chemistry and biology behind the different kind of drugs and its effects on the human body. Secondly, the social aspects of drugs in the past, present and future. 

I interacted with Prof Linda more and did not really interact with Prof George. Prof Linda is extremely attentive and kind. She is very approachable and enthusiastic. It was quite enjoyable taking this module under her and experiencing her excitedness in teaching the module. 

The module has 5 tutorials with one of them being allocated for the group debate. The tutorials were fun and light as we discussed as a group and class about the module topics and some articles introduced. CA1 was to create a concept map and picture about your interpretation of an aspect of drugs and society. CA2 was an exam with a mixture of MCQ, MRQ and structured questions. Self prepared hard copy notes was allowed and a digital copy of the lecture notes was provided via the Examplify platform. CA3 was a group project to write a report on how to effectively manage and/or reduce the problem regarding a particular drug at a certain point in time in a certain region. CA4 was a group debate on a topic that we were suppose to bid for from a list of topics. 

Personally, this module was enjoyable and fun. I was also blessed with hardworking, initiative and great group mates. As someone who did not touch biology before at all, I learnt a lot from this module. I did stumble really hard originally with the biology parts especially with lecture 2 and 3 slides having many empty spaces. I understand the rationale for the empty spaces but it hindered me quite hard. However, from lecture 4 onwards, the empty spaces disappeared thankfully. CA2 tested quite a number of biological questions and hence, I kind of botched it :*) .Tutorials were great and chill.

All in all, this module was conducted pretty well especially for it's first run. I highly recommend this module for a fun and interesting experience. My only other complain was probably the microphone that we were required to use for the group debate haha. Really hated that thing. One hand holding the microphone and one hand holding the script. Can't really do hand actions and I preferred to use my normal voice. Overall, this module has taught me a lot and that drugs always have its good and evil sides, not only the evil side.

The module teaches about the Earth but in scientific terms across Biology, Physics and Chemistry. Hence, it consists of geography concepts but they are all "scientified". Concepts such as electromagnetic spectrum and waves are commonly seen and used.

Both professors were like robots at teaching this module and did not seem interested in teaching this module. Not much comments for them.

The module has 5 tutorials. However, only 4 of the tutorials were graded by submitting a completed tutorial worksheet as a group. There were 2 quizzes of 15 questions and 15 minutes each. Almost all questions were scientific by nature. The finals had 50 MCQ questions and 4 open ended questions, some geographic and some scientific by nature.

Personally, this module feels very empty and lifeless for me. It is basically the "scientified" version of HS2907 but taking away the fun and fun of geography. The tutorials were standard short answer questions on geographical concepts and data. The quiz was annoying as it sometimes tested on concepts that are easily missed out or minor. The finals format was announced as "MCQ’s and Open ended questions" so when I saw 50 MCQs and 4 short answer questions, I was kind of shocked because I expected more essay type questions to appear.

All in all, this module just feels empty and unenjoyable. If you are looking to learn about Geography and have fun, I highly recommend you head over to HS2907. HS2907 and GE2103 are almost the same. Just one talks about space and one talks about science and sustainability. This module is recommended for those interested in seeing Geography and Science going hand in hand. I only took this module to fulfil the requirements for geosciences minor. Some of the components' grades are not released for unknown reasons.

In short, HS2907 is the fun and cool module. GE2103 is the hardcore academic like module.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM 2 with Prof Chin Chee Whye

Textbook: Dummit and Foote

The Module covers the following topics
1. Groups
◦ definition and examples
◦ subgroups, normal subgroups
◦ cyclic subgroups
◦ subgroup generated by a subset
◦ product of groups

2. Homomorphisms
◦ homomorphisms
◦ isomorphisms
◦ kernel, image, preimage

3. Quotient groups
◦ definition and examples
◦ cosets
◦ Lagrange's theorem
◦ isomorphism theorems
◦ composition series

4. Symmetric and alternating groups
◦ permutations of a set
◦ transpositions
◦ sign of a permutation

5. Abelian groups
◦ free abelian groups, rank
◦ torsion subgroup
◦ classification of finitely generated abelian groups

6. Group Actions
◦ G-sets, G-equivariant maps
◦ orbits, stabilizers
◦ conjugation action, conjugacy classes
◦ orbit-counting formula (class equation)

7. Sylow's thorems
◦ p-groups, p-Sylow subgroups
◦ Sylow's theorems
◦ simplicity of alternating groups

8. Other topics
◦ free groups
◦ linear groups, classical groups
◦ Banach-Tarski paradox  (if time permits)

Module Structure :
Homework : 40%
Midterm : 20%
Finals : 40%

Module Intro from Prof:
There are two main objectives of this course.  The first is to introduce the basics of Group Theory as a central organizing principle of modern mathematics.  The other equally important objective is for students to learn to read and write mathematical proofs.

The concept of a group originated as the mathematical manifestation of transformation and symmetry.  The various transformations of a mathematical object preserving its properties form a group --- the group of symmetries (automorphisms) of the object.  In Group Theory, the axioms of a group are formulated in the abstract and studied in their own right, detached from the original notion of symmetry.  In return, one discovers many instances of groups in different areas of mathematics, and the abstract theory can be systematically exploited to simplify and unify general concepts.  Group theoretic notions are pervasive in contemporary mathematics and physics, making group theory an essential tool for understanding advanced topics in these subjects.

Prof CCW is a professional professor. He teaches very well and has excellent and detailed lecture materials and notes. However, for homework matters, he is extremely strict. No late homework submissions and homework extensions allowed even if you have a valid medical memo. In his reply email to my homework extension request, he called my NUH medical memo vague and gave tons of reasons. Essentially, the email is just saying "hell no!"

There are 5 homework with 6 questions each from the textbook. Only the best 4 homework marks were used. Midterms and Finals were kind of hellish with 5 and 10 proving questions respectively. Some mentioned that the MA2202 Finals was much harder than the MA2202s Finals. No cheat sheet allowed.

Personally, this module kind of made me lost interest in algebra. I only understood and remembered like 10/24 lectures worth of content. Prof notes were quite detailed. However, I could not really see what I was learning and the point of it. It was quite abstract and difficult for me to visualize the groups. I got extremely hard stuck at normalizers and centralizers. Strong understanding of MA1100 is essential for this module too as many proving techniques and some number theory concepts are used. 

All in all, this module was not fun for me. I don't know what I was doing at all and the relevance of this module to my studies. It is an abstract module after all. I took this module out of interest but lost all instead. All I remember was Isomorphism and Homomorphism. Take this module if you are interested in Algebra groups and group actions, and strong in MA1100 and number theory. (Under CCW if you love drowning in abstract art.)

Homework : 60 + 57 + 60 + 52(void) + 60/60
Midterm : 11/20 (25th percentile)
Finals : Only answered one question confidently out of 10. :*)

Expected Grade : B-
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : It was a fully bided module and not displayed.

The 25-th, 50-th and 75-th percentile test scores are:  11, 14 and 17 respectively --- out of 20 for Mids.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM 2 with Prof Zhang Tengren

Textbook: "Topology" by Munkres and "Principles of Mathematical Analysis" by Rudin

Course Description: This course revisits ideas in analysis from a more abstract point of view, in the settings of metric spaces and topological spaces. These abstract notions form the basis for much of modern mathematics, especially in geometry and analysis.

Short Syllabus: 
Metric spaces, convergence, completeness, contraction mappings, topological spaces, compactness, compact spaces of functions and locally compact spaces.

Module Structure :
Homework : 10%
Midterm : 30%
Finals : 60%

The module teaches about metric and topological spaces. It introduces the basics of topology and metric before delving into deeper concepts such as continuity, compactness, types of topology spaces and metrizability. 

Prof Zhang is a very nice and knowledgeable professor. His lecture notes are all digitally handwritten. His lectures are also all handwritten on whiteboard. His lecture notes sometimes can be illegible and require some inferring/googling which was a little frustrating for me. However, the lecture notes are very detailed and rigorous. 

There are 2 homework with 10 and 8 questions respectively. It was quite difficult and took me some time to complete both of them. There are some standard/textbook questions. Midterms and Finals had one T/F question and 4 proving questions each. One 2-sided cheat sheet paper was allowed for both exams.

Personally, I did this module not out of interest. I only did this module to meet the requirements for MA4273. Hence, it was kind of a gamble but MA2108 did pique my interest a little in metric and topology. This module was quite understandable and manageable at the start with the topic introductions. However, as the module progressed towards the end, I got incredibly lost and clueless. The concepts became extremely abstract and it became hard for me to visualize and understand what I was doing. All I remember from the module was up to the different kind of compactness and continuity. Afterwards, I didn't understand and remember anything. I also saw the tutorial labelled MA4266 and thought to myself. "Guess I will just die? XD" For this course, the professor expects that you are familiar with the material in MA2108 but to be honest, I think prof still did a good job doing the explanation and introduction of the basics. 

I lack enough understanding to explain the contents of the module. Hence, if you want a glimpse, you can probably email me or something.

All in all, this module was interesting. But was it fun? For me, it was not. I probably understood MA3210 more than MA3209. I also probably found MA3210 more fun and interesting. I recommend this module for those interested in abstraction and visualization of topology and metric spaces rigorously. (And also if you like to draw circles and circles) I don't recommend taking this module just for the laughs. I would rather you go to some other MA3k module like MA3220. I also probably will not be continuing to MA4273.

Homework : 48 + 43/50
Midterm : 23/50
Finals : I totally understood what I was writing.

Expected Grade : C
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : 48

Note: This module is somehow overbidded. However, many people also dropped the module also I guess.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM1 with Prof Yao Yao

The Module covers the following topics
1. 1st Order Ordinary differential equation
2. 2nd Order Ordinary differential equation
3. Power Series
4. Ordinary differential equation with more than 1 unknown function
5. Boundary Value Theorem
(For more details, refer to Acekhoon review)

Module Structure :
Homework : 20%
Midterm : 30%
Finals : 50%

The module teaches about ordinary differential equations and the ways to solve its many different forms. It introduces theorems, some proofs such as Wronskian and Abel's theorem and phase portraits. I am also not sure why MA2104 is a prerequisite for this module as I did not really see much of MA2104 though I might be wrong.

Prof Yao Yao is a professional and nice professor. She teaches very well and has excellent lecture materials and notes. Her examples and explanations made understanding and learning easier. I felt her materials were also quite concise and to the point.

There are 4 homework with 6 to 8 questions which are quite manageable. Midterms and Finals were the standard 7 to 8 questions paper. The first question is a True/False question. The rest are calculation and proving questions with around 70-30 ratio respectively. One 2-sided cheat sheet paper was allowed for both exams.

Personally, this module is one of the easier MA modules but still of average difficulty. I would not say it is too hard or too easy. It has a slower pace than usual MA modules which was great for me. I had fun learning in the module. As I have interest in calculus, it is indeed interesting to see the different solving methods and different forms of ODEs. Some basic MA2001, MA2002 and MA2108 knowledge is beneficial when attempting this module. The idea is to identify correctly the form of the ODE and then apply the correct theorems and solving techniques. It has a relatively higher proportion of calculations which was nice. It is cool to know that you can solve 2nd order ODE y'' without going to y' then y. You are able to find y just from y'' directly. You will also be able to learn how to solve ODEs with more than 1 unknown function in one go. For example, x' = Ax where A is a 2x2 matrix. Hence it will somewhat look like
(x') = (1 1)(x) and you find (x)
(y') = (4 1)(y) and you find (y) directly. Note it is in (x',y')T form.
I just do not know how to present this in a nicer way.
There are more concepts taught but ill let you experience them when you bid and take MA3220.

All in all, this module was fun, interesting and informative. I might not have done well for this module but I find it great. The module has somewhat high bell curve distribution for my semester. Highly recommended if your interest is in calculus or you just want an easier MA3k module.

Homework : 18.5 + 20 + 19 + 20/20
Midterm : 52/80
Finals : I tried my best. I did mess up some stuff but I hope it will be alright.

Expected Grade : B
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : 66

Note: I had trouble bidding for this module due to its high demand. I only had year 2 priority and was fighting year 3/4 priority.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM1 with Prof Bernard Tan and Lydia Lam

The Module covers the following topics
1. Music
Elements of Music, Musical Notations, Scales, Intervals, Notes, Time Signature, Music Scores
2. Physics
Waves, Vibrations, Frequency, Frequency Intervals and Ratio, Harmonics, Closed and Open pipes, Synthesis of Waveforms, Transmission and Storage of digital signals
3. Instruments
Different types of instruments, Physics and Musical properties, Piano Structure and Inner Workings, MIDI and it's functions, Disks, MP3

Module Structure :
Essay : 20%
MIDI : 20%
Mid-Term Test : 30%
End-of-Term Test : 30%

The module teaches about the interactions between Physics and Music. The literal science behind music, musical instruments, musical software and musical instruments.

Lectures were all on YouTube and online. I skipped them and read the lecture slides instead.

Personally, this module is quite informative and interesting. It is somewhat a refresh on JC/Secondary Physics and basic Musical theory. The new concepts will be the interactions and links between Music and Physics. Even though you might have seen most concepts before, you might still be able to learn some concepts you have never seen before. I did learn some new concepts and understood more about the physics and inner workings behind Music.

The test consists of 25 MCQ questions and is 1 hour. If you practice past year papers that you somehow managed to obtain, you should be alright. It is all about accuracy and confidence in your answer. The option "None of the above" kind of screws your mentality. It is open book but there is really no time to refer, might be better to just create a cheat sheet. The essay requires a reflection on a recently attended concert. It also needs you to adapt some Physics and Musical concepts you learn into the reflection. The MIDI requires you to create a 20s-60s sound track in MIDI format. This assignment was not really the best as I am tone deaf and lack in composing skills. Moreover, the sound quality of MIDI files are not the best so checks are needed to make sure your work sounds good in MIDI format, not MP3 format.

All in all, the module essentially is Music, Physics and Math formulas. Quick and accurate calculations is necessary for the tests. It is an interesting and informative module. Recommended if you want a not so heavy module and wish to see the Science behind Music. There is also no history taught in the module and is unrelated to HSI1000.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM1 with Prof Gan Fah Fatt

The Module covers the following topics
Introduction to Linear Regression Analysis. Fifth Edition. Douglas C. Montgomery, Elizabeth A. Peck and G. Geoffrey Vining.
1. Simple Linear Regression
2. Multiple Linear Regression
3. Model Adequacy Checking
- About, Methods for scaling residuals, Residuals and outliers, Lack of fit and pure error
4. Transformation and Weighting
- Variance stabilising transformation, Linearizable functions, Analytical methods (Generalised and weighted least squares, Box cox method)
5. Leverage and Influence
- Diagonal elements of Hat matrix, Measures of influence (Cooks, DFBETAS, DFFITS, Estimate variance)
6. Polynomial Regression Model
- One variable, Two variables, Piecewise polynomial splitting splines (Cubic spline, Linear spline), Non parametric regression model, Locally weighted regression LOESS
7. Indicator Variables
- Interaction, Allocated codes, One way factorial design and regression analysis
8. Multicollinearity
- Effects, Sources, Diagnostics (Examination of correlation matrix, Variance inflation factors, Eigensystem analysis of X'X), Methods to deal with multicollinearity (Model specification, Ridge regression, Principal component regression)
9. Variable Selection and Model Building
Full and subset models, Subset evaluation, Technique for variable selection (Forward, Backward, Stepwise)

Module Structure :
In-class participation quiz : 20%
Midterm : 30%
Finals : 50%

The module teaches about linear regression in mainly the simple and multiple form. It also teaches about the other forms of linear regression. Model checking techniques are introduced such as detecting multicollinearity, checking appropriateness of the model and indicating influential outliers. Matrices are used extremely heavily in the module so some general knowledge of MA2001 is essential. ST2131 is not really a good prerequisite indicator to this module. ST1131/DSA1101 is enough for ST3131.

Prof Gan is a nice professor and is like your typical Asian Grandfather. The only thing is his notes are literal screenshots of the textbook he used. Whether it is a bad or good thing depends on one's learning style. I find it alright. It just that prof squeeze everything into the lecture slides, it is hard to tell what's important to know and what's not important. As it is somewhat a direct copy of the textbook, some parts lack explanations or details that would better one's understanding, especially in the MA2001 aspect. He is often called "useless", probably because of this. Other than that, he is actually alright, kind and approachable.

Quizzes were quite easy as it is meant to encourage students to attend the lectures. Midterms consisted of 20 MCQ questions on data interpretation and proving logic. Finals contained 5 open ended questions on the same too. It is 60 percent data interpretation and 40 percent proving. One 2-sided cheat sheet paper was allowed for both exams.

Personally, this module is very informative on linear regression. It covers quite a lot of aspects. From the many forms to model checking, it introduces how the best data models are built and chosen. It also does data interpretation and analysis which mainly contributes to considerations of which data model to use. Under Prof GFF, MA2001 and matrices is heavily used in introducing and proving the models. Unfortunately, prof doesn't cover matrices basics and the important theorems that is used in the module, and yet it is tested in exams as proving questions. Even if he did, it is not enough. I feel this is a flaw of the module and those that did not do MA2001 might lose out a little. Many topics are covered with topic 1 and 2 having many subsections. My cheat sheet essentially was filled with tiny words to the brim. For topic 2, it had section A B C...… Alpha, Beta. It is quite a lot of information to take in and understand, and the topics are somewhat linked to each other too.

All in all, the module does quite a lot. Expect to see proving logic on top of data interpretation. It is an interesting module ultimately and not as "simple" as you think about simple linear regression. ST3131 is for those interested in in-depth linear regressions and model building.

In-class participation quiz : 15/16
Midterm : 15/20 (Median)
Finals : In bell curve we trust!

Expected Grade : B/B+
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : Around 200+

Not sure who got 0. But hope it is because the person went for LOA or something. Lets pray for this person.
