author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM1 with Prof Hui Yu

The Module covers the following topics
Principles of Mathematical Analysis (Third Edition) by Rudin
Chap 5: Differentiation
Chap 6: The Riemann-Stieltjes Integral
Chap 7: Sequences and Series of Functions
Chap 9: Functions of Several Variables
Chap 11: The Lebesgue Theory

Module Structure :
3 Homework : 10%
Participation : 10%
Midterm : 30%
Final : 50%.

First off, the module description is kind of invalidated under professor Yu Hui. Secondly, this review is brought to you by the 0th percentile of MA3210. Thirdly, prof assumed that everyone had done MA2108s in one way or another. Hence, chapter 5 and 6 were sped run and assumed to be already learnt. The module structure and lack of lecture materials resulted in many students dropping the module. I also took MA2108, not MA2108s.

This module teaches about integration and differentiation at a higher level under prof Yu Hui specifically. It also teaches about uniform convergence, matrixes and sets. It is a pure proving module with almost 0 calculations. It is a mix of MA1100, MA2001, MA2002, MA2104, MA2108 and a little MA3220.

Prof Yu Hui is a nice and approachable prof. He is very experienced and knowledgeable. His structure of this particular module and teaching style is debatable. It is extremely beneficial for the elites as higher level of knowledge and skills are taught. However, to the not so elite, without strong basics, it is quite... overwhelming and confusing.

Lectures and tutorials honestly were very boring and confusing. I relied on YouTube and the textbook instead. There are no lecture slides, lecture notes or lecture recordings. The experience varies from person to person depending on your learning style and background.

Each homework consisted of 3-4 questions from Rudin's textbook exercises. Midterms was 4 questions and Finals was 3 questions. It is not really your standard MA2104/MA2108 paper with 7-8 questions. No cheat sheet allowed.

Personally, this module was extremely difficult for me. It was difficult to learn the proofs and get use to the language of proofs. Chapters 5 and 6 were kind of reviewed quickly so my foundations were probably not there already. I only learnt and remembered the more important concepts such as uniform convergence and Lebesgue definitions. I was utterly lost and confused at some of the theorems. Homework was of average difficulty but I often referenced to online solutions and information in order to check my answers or get started on the questions. Midterms utterly annihilated me. Question 1 was a True/False question. I was lucky enough to get the True/False correct but could not provide a counterexample and proof. The counterexample was quite specific and I probably would have never found it anyways. Question 2 was essentially a MA2104 proofing question which I screwed up because I did not realise it was just a MA2104 concept. Question 3 and 4, I was confused and wrote nonsense. Finals also whacked me real good. Question 1 was True/False question. Question 2 and 3 were proving questions. Question 2, I had somewhat a little idea. Question 3, I did not get it and wrote nonsense anyways. Overall, finals and midterms screwed me over real good. Me becoming the 0th percentile, I am not surprised.

All in all, it was "fun" taking MA3210. I did learn new things ultimately anyways. Take this module under prof Yu Hui at your own risk. If you want a challenge and learn higher level pure Mathematics, this module under prof Yu Hui is definitely for you. Perhaps to an elite, it is "Trivial". It is MA3210s essentially. I just was stubborn and did not want to drop this module. I see it as a challenge and wanted to see this module till the end.

Homework: 10/10 * 3
Midterms: 10/100
Midterms Adjusted: 40/100
Finals: Dieded and Dieded

Expected Grade : F
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : 37

Note: The module experience varies from student to student. Apparently half of the class are SPM students. There are also probably some that reverse engineered from higher level 4k 5k mods to MA3210. This resulted in the statistics for midterms to be very extreme. (Probably because of me also) There are people that did well and then there is me. :*)

message:

4. Climate What-ifs (Group Work)

Module Structure :
Group presentation : 10%
Group report : 20%
Tutorial 1,2,3,4 : 7.5% * 4
Finals : 40%

The module teaches about the interactions between physics and geography of planets. It also introduces space exploration and it's intricacies. It is like a human geography module with some physics concept and a little biology.

Both profs have passion for the module and subject. I feel that they are alright.

Personally, I enjoyed the content of the module and tutorials 1 and 4. I took H1 geography in A levels so some of the content are familiar, with the addition of the element, Space. It has prompted some interesting concepts for discussion and exploration during tutorials 1 and 4. We then need to submit a report at the end of the tutorial based on some questions. I enjoyed both of them and had fun. I particularly disliked tutorial 2 and 3. Rocks and minerals are not my cup of tea and there is a quiz at the end of the tutorial. We were made to create a one paper cheat sheet for the quiz which can be quite stressful. I ultimately learnt nothing except copy pasting the tutorial slides to my A4 paper, and then getting stressed by the quiz. The project work for me was done on the topic, Planet Geology: Mars. We needed to create a report on our topic and present it to the class during tutorial 5. Finals was essentially a full on geography paper with MCQs, short essays and long essays. The finals was notable for asking about magnetic fields of planets which was kind of a nasty trick. There was no cheat sheet too so... kind of difficult to memorize everything especially if you have exams on 2 consecutive days. This paper brings me back to my A levels H1 Geography days, write essays until hand pain.

All in all, the module does quite a lot of human geography and some physics. Expect to see geography and geography and geography. It is an interesting module ultimately. Highly recommended if you like geography, planets and space.

Group presentation : Not released
Group report : Not released
Tutorial 1 : 6.5/7.5
Tutorial 2 : 7.13/7.5
Tutorial 3 : 3.75/7.5
Tutorial 4 : 7.5/7.5
Finals : Not released

Expected Grade : B/B+
Final Grade : TBC

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM1 with Prof McMorran and Prof Yuen
TA: Natalie Lie

The Module covers the following topics
1. Introduction and diversity in Japan
2. History in Gender, Arts and Culture
- Jōmon, Heian, Kamakura, Edo periods etc.
3. Modernization, Industrialization and Westernization: Meiji
- Meiji restoration
4. Empire, War and Peace
- Japan in WW2
5. Subcultures
6. Popular cultures
7. Japan's Foreign and Ethnic "Others"
8. Class and status
9. Numbers & Economic Miracles
10. Protest
11. Population Problems

Module Structure :
Lecture Participation : 5%
Tutorial Participation : 15%
CA1 Individual Creative Essay : 20%
CA2 Group Project : 25%
CA3 Group Presentation : 5%
CA4 Essay ("Finals") : 30%

The module teaches about the history, economics and socials of Japan. It is an excellent module to take your first step into learning about Japan. The module covers many areas about Japan with each lecture somewhat linking to each other, which is quite cool. It teaches about the many norms in Japan and debunks some of the popular beliefs such as all Japanese are hardworking and that Japan has only one race, one language, one ethnicity.

Prof McMorran is an excellent prof. He is very responsive and interactive. He teaches well and gives many insights. His lectures are very lively and enjoyable. Passionate, knowledgeable and experienced. Even if I was sleepy or "dead" in lecture, I can somehow remember some parts of his lecture. His style is extremely inviting. Even the "dead" listens and acknowledges. I highly recommend this professor. He is probably one of the best professors I have encountered in NUS yet.

Prof Yuen is also a good professor. She is more on the informative and professional side. Her lectures based on Japan's history can be a little painful and heavy. However, the rest of her lectures and mini lectures were very informative and eye opening.

Natalie Lie is excellent TA. Probably one of the best TAs I have met. She is very kind, caring and responsive. She is unlike most NUS TAs who are like robotic and/or monotone. She is knowledgeable to hold her own in tutorials and is very approachable. It would be nice to have her as my TA for future JS mods

Lectures were quite fun. I was "dead" most of the time but I admit they were good quality lectures. Tutorial really helped me a lot to cover what I missed in lecture and discuss about the topics more in-depth. The discussions expanded and went deep allowing for good exchanges of information and learning.

Personally, this module helped me learn quite a lot about Japan. I gained a lot of knowledge about Japan and how it became what it is today. The problems and issues faced by Japan and Japan may not be as perfect as what one assumes to be. Not only that, I was also kind of forced to learn how to write papers (Structure, References, Academic style) which I find was quite useful. I personally found the module manageable. It is not too light, not too heavy.

All in all, it was fun taking JS1101E. No complains. Perhaps a touch of politics can be added into the lecture :) Take JS1101E under Prof McMorran. Highly recommended. 

It is a little saddening and unfortunate that I am below average for the components but it is what it is. 

Group Project Topic : Isekai and Escapism
Individual Creative Essay Topic : Hikikomori
https://js1101e.shorthandstories.com/hikikomori-is-it-the-beginning-of-the-end/index.html

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY23/24 SEM1 with Prof Yap Von Bing

The Module covers the following topics
1. Probability Review I
2. Probability Review II
3. Point Estimation
4. SE, Bias, MSE
5. Interval Estimation
6. Method of Moments
7. Maximum Likelihood Estimation
8. Large-sample Distribution of MLEs
9. Testing Hypotheses
10. Goodness-of-fit
11. Conclusion

Module Structure :
In Lecture Quiz : 20%
Tutorial Attendance : 10%
Coding Assignment : 20%
Finals : 50%
(For more details, refer to Joel Lo review)

The module teaches about proving statistical methods and distribution. It is like taking ST2131 and ST1131, and then showing us the proves of their content. The module is a full on proving module with some calculus concepts and tests on your statistics proving skills. 

Prof Yap Von Bing is a chill professor and is like your typical chill Asian dad. His lectures are quite chill too. His quizzes unfortunately are not. He also tries to use real world examples to show us the motivations behind some of the concepts which was nice. However, his explanations at times can be puzzling and a little too much to handle at point of listening. 

The coding assignment needed us to code out a simulation of Monte Carlo in R based on some data to estimate the parameters. At the end of almost all lectures, there is a quiz. It tests mostly on recently taught content. 4 mins for 2 questions. Finals was 12 MCQ questions and 3 open ended proving questions. No cheat sheet allowed.

Personally, this module was quite brutal for me in structure and content. The coding assignment felt too easy. It is either I screwed up or it is what it is. I could not do much for the quizzes as the content was just taught and had a short timeframe. 4 minutes is just too short for me. It was stressful and ended up with a lot of 0 and 1. I was also lost for finals. The lack of cheat sheet was quite detrimental for me. I only remembered topics 1 to 5. Topics 6 to 11 was just me smoking in the exam due to the lack of brain memory space. The question asked for loglikelihood. I just gave up, drew a log and wrote "How likely is this a log?" To be honest, I was already not very motivated for this module and did not do much. I kind of abandoned this module halfway in favour of my other modules. It is partially due to the quizzes and module structure. I really really tried still but I was confused and lost. Perhaps this module would be interesting if I was able to keep up with it and hence see the inner workings behind statistical methods in distributions and the likes. I was only able to remember some distribution concepts (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Multivariate etc.) and basic probability concepts (Expectation, Variance, Covariance, Conditional and Joint distributions etc.), also kind of because of MA2116. The partially incomplete lecture slides were also a pain and annoying. 

All in all, I did not learn much from this module. It is unfortunate but it is what it is. The structure of this module requires consistent work and keeping up with lectures really closely or even preparing in advance. This did not work in my favour as I needed time to process and think about the concepts. Do expect to have consistency and hard work required from you for this module if it is conducted similarly in the future. My lack of motivation and inability to keep up was really detrimental. Ironically, it helped me quite a bit for ST3131 when I took this module together with ST3131.

None of the grades were released. I also did not have the motivation and energy to keep track of the scores for each of my poorly done quizzes.

Expected Grade : C+ and below
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : 263

Note: I was extremely unmotivated for this module. The module experience differs from student to student. As I did not learn much from this module, I am unable to share the beauty of ST2132 and relevant details.

From what I know, this module teaches topics close to A levels Mathematics such as Vectors, Differentiation, Integration, Basic Sequences and Series. I will say this course is good for modules such as MA2002 and MA2104 if you did not take A levels Mathematics. It is somewhat helpful in courses like MA2001. author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY21/22 SEM2 with Prof Pham Thi Kim Cuc (Prof Daisy)

The Module covers the following topics
1 Introduction to R programming
2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
3 Collecting Data
4 Probability
5 Random Variables (Discrete and Continuous Random Variables)
6 Sampling Distribution
7 Statistical Estimation
8 Hypothesis Testing
9 Linear Regression
10 Some Limitations of Linear Regression

Module Structure :
Two individual assignments
LumiNUS quiz
Final examination

This module teaches data manipulation and basic statistical command for R. There is data extraction and manipulating it such as changing variable names. Basic stats commands include outputting graphs/charts. It also teaches binomial and normal distribution, including how to use R to generate the probability values. There are many others too such as sampling methods and its analysis.

There were two individual assignments. One was a R datacamp course and the other was a Linear Regression and model creation assignment. The Luminus quiz has unlimited attempts. It will only tell you how many marks you achieved without telling you where you went wrong. There was only one exam, the Finals with R coding.

Personally, the assignment was not too bad. Just that linear regression and model was new to me at that time so I struggled quite a little. The luminus quiz was just plain annoying. I remember my highest attempt being 44 times just because I made a careless mistake in one of the 20 questions (one question can have multiple parts) If your basics fail you, the quiz is a nightmare. Finals was not too bad in difficulty.

Overall, its a manageable mod.

Expected grade : A-
Final grade : B+
Yes, I was a little disappointed.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY21/22 SEM 2 
Tutor : Derek Wong

Module Structure :
Book Chapter Reflection: 5%
Science News Article 1: 20%
Science News Article 2: 30%
Reflective Commentary: 5%
Individual Oral Presentation: 25%
Interaction and Engagement: 15%

This module teaches how to write scientific new articles that "sensationalizes" scientific papers/findings. Methods such as "using non technical terms" and "analogies" are used to make reporting the scientific findings easy for the public to read and understand. 

My tutor was very professional. He conducted the tutorials quite well and clearly. I appreciated that he was my tutor as he is very dedicated and nice.

Personally, I feel that Math majors in this module are handicapped really heavily. Math articles and papers tend to be relatively difficult to read and understand. Moreover, it is even more challenging to attract the audience's attention with mathematical concepts. Honestly, who is going to be interested in "The theory of matrices in numerical analysis" in the general public's eyes. Most will avoid and probably even run away from it. Majors such as Life Sciences probably have an easier time. Isn't "Dogs may hold key to treating cancer in humans" more exciting? However, if you are able to choose the correct articles and present it well, perhaps it will still be viable. 

The scientific articles were fun to write and explore. I had the time of my life writing them and presenting them. Perhaps it is because I take joy in writing ( does not mean I do well in writing :*) ) I also found it interesting to read articles written by other students and experiencing their style of writing.

The peer review I felt was a let down. I wrote a comprehensive peer review for all my peers. However, most of the peer review I received back was either almost empty or poorly written. It felt like it was not worth writing and my time was wasted. The peer review is not graded and monitored so... yea. 

Expected Grade : B+/A-
Final Grade : B

I was so so disappointed in my grades. No idea what happened. I thought I did kind of well. I suspect that for my Individual Oral Presentation, I probably made mistakes or something. 

Overall, I found the module fun and informative. Just that my efforts and time spent were not enough and worthy for that A.

However, the one observing... kind of avoided questions and work. When we asked her how to implement certain experiments, she was lost and gave us wrong instructions. This resulted in my group being lost and uncertain. Moreover, our session was not the first few so I kind of expected some form of familiarity...? The final experiment separated us into the solar panel one or the windmill one. It felt a little unfair as the solar panel experiment was easier to implement and get results. The windmill one was more difficult and confusing at times. Overall, the lab sessions were kind of a let down. It was suppose to be fun but... yea.

Overall, it was fun and informative. I still did learn some interesting information for my personal knowledge.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 SEM 1 with Prof Cai Zhenning

The Module covers the following topics
1. Numbers and Algorithms in Computers
   - Numbers in Computers, Computer arithmetic, Algorithms and pseudocodes
2. MA2001 in Computational Sense
   - Matrix multiplication, Multiplication of special matrices, Time complexity and numerical stability, Gaussian elimination, Gaussian elimination with
row labels, Partial pivoting, Scaled partial pivoting, LU factorization

   - Lagrange interpolation, Divided differences, Interpolation with
Chebyshev nodes, Error of Lagrange Interpolation, Cubic Spline Interpolation
4. Least squares approximation
5. MA2002 in Computational Sense
   - Numerical integration: Trapezoidal and Simpson’s rules, Proof of error terms & Newton-Cotes formulas, Composite integration rules

Module Structure :
Final exam: 40% 
Mid-term exam: 20%
Quiz: 10%
Lab test: 10%
Homework 1: 10%
Homework 2: 10%

This module teaches MA2001 and MA2002 operations but in a computational way of thinking. It is a very interesting module to do math in a computer way of thinking. The module consists of algorithm creations for mathematical operations and tedious calculations. It introduces quite a number of new concepts and basic knowledge of MA2001 and MA2002 is needed and will be helpful.

Lab test was to create a MATLAB code for calculating divided differences. There were 2 assignments consisting of 7-8 questions each. Mid term was a 50 mark paper and Finals was a 80 mark paper. The quiz was 33 marks and held in week 5 and was kind of like a mini midterm.

Prof Cai Zhenning is a very passionate prof. His lecture slides are very detailed and step by step. 

Personal Comments :
I was totally lost and scored badly for many of the components. I also missed out on important concepts and did not learn some in time. Many of the concepts were new and sometimes abstract. The lecture slides for each lecture sometimes had over 100 slides and filled with details. This made me lost often although I will not blame the prof lecture slides structure. The MA2001 concepts were super painful to pick up and learn. The MA2002 concepts were kind of lost in the woods for me. I only appreciated the calculation part of the module. Mid terms was a total disaster and Finals was an exam of uncertainty for me. 

I feel that in this module, a clear direction is needed for the 20 lectures worth of content. I often don't know the aim of the lecture resulting in learning unnecessary parts and getting lost. 

Regardless, it is actually an interesting and cool module. Just that the start of the module can be a little painful with the MA2001 concepts and algorithm creation. 

Final exam: ?
Mid-term exam: 17/50
Quiz: 17/33
Lab test: 4/10

Homework 2: 76/80

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY21/22 SEM2 with Prof Tan Ban Pin

The Module covers the following topics
0. Sets and Functions
1. Limits
2. Continuous Functions

Module Structure :
3 Assignments
Midterm 
Final examination 

The module features calculus and a more deeper insight into it. Definitions of limits and continuity will be introduced. More techniques of integration and differentiation is taught. 

Prof Tan has quite good lecture slides for this mod. There were no live lectures and no tutorials. Tutorial files and pre-recorded lecture videos were released. It was like own time own target. The videos were of horrendous quality though with the videos having terrible sound output. I relied on the slides more for this mod.

Personally, it is quite a manageable mod if you still have your JC skills in calculus. Just add on a few more theorems and formulas here and there. The hard part for me was integration by sub as the substitution term is no longer provided and you need to infer it.

3 Assignments (50m) : Full Marks
Midterm : 33
Final examination : ?
Expected Grade : B+/A-
Final Grade : B

I flunked the finals quite badly so I am not surprised. Could not even do volume calculation properly during finals.

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY21/22 SEM2 with Prof Wang Fei

The Module covers the following topics
1. Linear Systems & Gaussian Elimination
2. Matrices
  - Determinant, Matrix Operations, Elementary Matrices etc.
3. Matrices
  - Linear Combinations and Linear Spans, Linear Independence, Bases etc.
4. Vector spaces associated with matrices
5. Orthogonality
6. Diagonalization
7. Linear Transformation

Module Structure :
Four assignments : 20%
Midterm
Final examination 

This module teaches Matrices which also includes equations and vectors. You will learn the different kind of matrices, formation of the matrices and the many different kinds of interactions and properties matrices can have. It can be abstract and interesting.

Prof Wang Fei taught the module quite well and had many interesting questions. 

The assignments were of slightly difficult difficulty. Midterms and finals were surprisingly manageable and not "destroyed". There were a good mix and easy and difficult questions. 

Personally, it is quite an interesting module. However, there are times when I wonder what I am even doing and is there even a point in doing so. It is important to have a clear direction in this mod where you can visualize the matrices, vector spaces and vectors. It was still quite challenging for me as it is a new concept.

Four assignments (50m each) : 50 + 50 + 0 + 48
Midterm : 33/50
Final examination : ?
Expected Grade : B+/A-
Final Grade : B+
No of students : 600+

Note : I got 0 for one of my assignments because I submitted it late and it was rejected as a late submission

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY22/23 SEM1 with Prof Wang Fei

The Module covers the following topics
1. Combinatorial Analysis
  - Permutations, Combinations, Number of Integer Solutions etc.
2. Axioms of Probability
  - Probability etc.
3. Conditional Probability
  - Bayes’s Formula, Independent Events etc.
4. Random Variables
  - Bernoulli and Binomial Random Variables, Poisson Random Variables etc.
5. Continuous Random Variables
  - Normal Random Variables, Exponential Random Variables etc.
6. Jointly Distributed Random Variables
  - Independent Random Variables, Sums of Independent Random Variables etc.
7. Properties of Expectation
  - Covariance and Correlations, Conditional Expectation etc.
8. Limit Theorems
  - Chebyshev’s Inequality, Central Limit Theorem, Strong Law of Large Numbers

Module Structure :
Five online quizzes : 25%
Midterm : 25%
Final examination : 50%

The module teaches probability and much much much more. It is interesting to see the random variables interact with each other. Instead of the conventional X ~N(0,1) , you will get to see X/Y , 2X, X^2 , and many many more. Calculation of expectation and variance of these interactions becomes common as their properties are being analysed. And no, there are more alphabets and algebra than you think. 

Prof Wang Fei is an excellent prof. He is very responsive and interactive. He teaches well and gives many insights. This is also true to his insanely difficult tutorial, legendary online quizzes and mythical exams.

Tutorial had out of the world questions with out of the world solutions. Quiz was rigorous and digs into your basics. Exams were just pure...mythical. 

Personally, this module made me rethink what probability was. Its not just numbers anymore. Its a big world out there. The concepts were actually not too bad, just I was too slow to pick them up and understand fully. Your basics have to be strong in order to attempt the questions by the prof. I just wished I had more time to play around the formulas and concepts. Warning : This is extremely different from your JC probability stuff especially in the later chapters. Be open and absorb the knowledge.

All in all, it was fun taking MA2116 under Prof Wang Fei. Just RIP my marks and grades :*)

Quiz : 10 + 10 + 9 + 9 + 9.5 (I think)
Mids : 36/60
Finals : All Hail the Bellcurve!
Expected Grade : B
Final Grade : TBC
No of students : 243

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY22/23 SEM1 with Prof Pham Thi Kim Cuc (Prof Daisy)

The Module covers the following topics
1. Statistical software (R, Python, SAS)
2. Describing numerical data
3. Robust estimators for location and scale
4. Analysis of categorical data
5. Inferences on one-sample and two-sample data
6. One-way ANOVA
7. Regression Analysis
8. Simulation studies
9. Resampling methods: Bootstrap

Module Structure :

Two individual assignments : 20% 
Midterm : 30%
Final examination : 50% 

This module teaches how to do data manipulation and statistical commands in 3 languages. This module was not easy for me as there were many codes to be tested and they are all used differently in the 3 languages. I highly recommend keeping your ST1131(R) and CS1010(Python) notes for this module. I took this module thinking I will have a manageable time in it. Boy I was wrong and in for a ride. If possible, learn all 3 languages before hand. All the time spent suffering under CS1010 will definitely be worth your time for this module. (Example : for loops and for in for loops)

Assignment 1 had us learning python and r basic coding on DataCamp
Assignment 2 had us code out ANOVA functions in r (Like a CS1010 mission) 

Midterm:
R coding : Function creation in CS1010 style
Python : Contingency tables

Finals:
R coding : Simulation (30m)
Python : Data manipulation and statistical commands (30m)
SAS : Data manipulation and statistical commands (40m)

Finals and Midterm are open book. (If it was closed I think I would have gone crazy) 

Personal Comments :
I did not do that well for the midterms and was utterly destroyed in Finals. For the midterms, I was unable to think logically a way to code out the second function in R and another function for python. For the finals, I only managed to answer 1.1 questions out of the 4 questions. I coded out my answers too slowly and spent way too much time looking for codes in my notes. I will admit that I lacked practice and only learnt SAS 2 days before the finals. This module really requires you to practice using the codes in all 3 languages. I was unhappy that the lecture slides only sometimes displayed partial codes and sometimes do not display the code at all. Most of the tested and relevant codes were all in the tutorial instead. I was also not too happy that the codes were dumped on us like waterfall. There was not really much on how to think computationally but more of learning and memorizing the code. Ultimately, I still blame myself more for the lack of time and effort.

All in all, this module is definitely not light in workload. It is heavy and can be almost treated like a coding module. If you are weak in coding like me, then be prepared. It can be very time consuming and brain wrecking. 

Assignment 1 : Full marks 
Assignment 2 : 100/100
Midterm : 28/50
Finals : ???
Expected grade : B- (Hopefully)
Final grade : TBC
No of students : 67

author: Wayne Wong Hoi Yin
message:
Taken in AY 22/23 SEM 1 with Prof An Xinliang.

The Module covers the following topics
1 Preliminaries;
   - Includes sets, mathematical induction and functions (MA1100) etc.
2 The Real Numbers;
   - Includes density theorem, supremum/Infimum and bounded/unbounded etc.
3 Sequences and Series;
   - Includes proving convergence/divergence and finding limits of series/sequences etc.
   - Example : limits definition, limits theorem, squeeze theorem, Monotone convergence theorem and Cauchy criterion (sequences)
   - Example : n-th term test, Cauchy criterion test, Partial sum bounded test for series with nonnegative terms, Comparison test, Limit comparison test (series)
4 Limits;
   - Includes Cluster points and (E, δ)-definition etc.
5 Continuous Functions;
   - Includes Continuity, IVT and Uniform Continuity etc.
6 Infinite Series (Part);
   - Includes Absolutely Convergent and Tests etc.
   - Example : Limit Comparison Test II, Root Test and Ratio Test
7 A Glimpse into Topology
   - Includes Open and Closed Sets and Metric spaces etc.

This module feels like a continuation of MA2002 and MA1100 in mathematical language. 

Module Structure :
20% homework ( 5 sets x 30m )
30% midterm
50% final exam

Homework was quite manageable with 2 to 3 questions each and one week to submit. Finals and Mids were closed book with no cheat sheet allowed. Professor is quite nice and gave hints in the homework.

Personally, I am not very good with mathematical language like MA1100(B grade). However, MA1100 and MA2002 knowledge really helped me quite a lot in this module making it manageable. As there were no cheat sheet allowed, it took me some time to memorize and understand the theorems. Ultimately, I did not manage to understand and memorize everything. Hence, I focused on the core concepts and gave the module everything I got for finals. Finals was not too bad in terms of difficulty.

For my Mids, I kind of yolo it as it was my 3rd Midterm for that day and I was quite dead. Quite surprised I still managed to get 56/100. The Mids to me was not easy.

Homework : 30 + 30 + 28 + 27 + 30
Midterm : 56/100
Finals : ???
Expected grade : C
Final grade : TBC
No of students : 93

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY23/24 under prof Alex Ling

Lectures are quite fast because this is not one of those "derive every equation" modules. Equations are flashed and explained but sometimes it takes a while to understand what is going on. It helps if students have a strong background in optics. However Prof mentioned he will cover more background in the next rendition. 

The course structure necessitates this pace though, because almost the entire second half is experiments and seminars.

Tests:
2 tests 25% each. very manageable, as long as you follow the lecture and remember a few key formulas.

Experiments:
Conducted in groups of 5, unfortunately it's very squeezy but the department has not been able to provide a bigger venue. Also as you can imagine it's very difficult to have 10 hands on the table, so it seems more like each group has 1 or 2 people doing and the rest are watching. It was an unpleasant experience, due to real world concerns (because ideally if there were more space and more setups it would be 2 to a setup or something). Each of the 3 experiments requires a corresponding group report, which means the workload is not very light

Seminars:
In the same group, every group reads a paper and presents the paper. Usually I love these sessions but the makeup of the class meant that many of the masters students were reciting robotically which was really painful to sit through. There might be adjustments to this component of the module next year

Interview:
Everyone gets interviewed 1 on 1 by the prof regarding their group's read paper. I enjoyed it a lot because it was stimulating, although some people may find it nerve-wrecking. Part of the reason for this mode of assessment is to check if individuals pulled their weight for the presentation of the paper (if they really understand the paper)

Overall it s a lot more physics than I expected, because initially I thought it's going to be very experimental. However Prof Alex does go into the physics behind the devices. Generally a great guy to talk to, he's very happy to have a chat with students

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY23/24 under Prof Kuldip

Almost entirely agree with physicskid's reviews. 

This is a theoretical minimum for theorists. It is quite abstract and takes a while to digest but Prof Kuldip is a really good explainer, and very patient too. 

For our batch, test 1 was very routine with a large component of definition questions. I guess the average was too high (60% fo the class scored better than 50/60) so test 2 was quite tedious, and most people could not finish. Finals was very routine, but I guess it's reasonable because to even absorb the entire content takes quite a bit of effort.

Ditto for the lecture vs lecture notes point, the problem is that Prof's harddrive for the notes spoiled so he cannot edit the notes anymore. So best to turn up for lectures, although sometimes they may be a bit slow because Prof Kuldip likes to remind people of certain points(which serves the general cohort well) as well as entertain questions in class which may take up a fair bit of time

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Rain and coffee is pretty on point. Would just like to add a few comments for AY23/24

For lectures: yes the venue is v ulu and not particularly comfortable. Pace is quite slow but likely is because a lot of masters students take this class and they are not familiar with english yet. 

For assignments: It's true, sometimes the assignment is quite vague in exactly what it is asking for. Phrases such as "Design... a value" are used and it was unclear what exactly we were required to do. When in doubt, do what you think is morally right; that is usually what is expected. For example, instead of guessing a value, you are supposed to prove the range of values it can take mathematically, then plot one of the values to show the prescribed behaviour.

For project: It's nice to be able to choose your own partner, given how few phys mods have pairwork. The optional question wasn't really optional, almost everyone lost most of our marks there even though we attempted something. Or it could just be that the curve killed us because there was one person who went above and beyond

For test: I love how there was 1 whole question that consisted of many parts, each to explain a concept taught in class in your own words. Otherwise, one of the calculations was a little more tedious and then the extension is something very few people got, kind of a heuristic argument which is a "whether you see it kind of thing", and whether you're morally dubious enough to try something like that

For the lecturer: Prof Gong is very kind and almost always stayed back to answer our doubts. He is also very generous with his time in opening consult sessions, as well as entertaining requests for discussions beyond the stipulated time.

Overall it's largely computationally/theoretical-techniques focused mod which is possibly not relevant to all (as compared to AQM for example), but whether you're a mathematical physicist or an experimentalist you should acquire a minimal understanding of computational QM and so it's a useful mod for all

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
String theory 101 taken in AY22/23 sem 2 under Prof Tan Meng-Chwan. 

Although I'm not sure if this module will be offered in the near future, I'm writing this review to give credit to the very enjoyable lectures by Prof Tan.

It's really just lectures, a 2hr+ lecture every week. There are no tutorials but it does take some effort on your part to review the content to remember enough to keep up with the next lecture, since every lecture builds on the previous and the content is quite different from anything I have ever seen before.

String theory is known to be possibly the hardest theoretical physics field, and so Prof Tan really did an immense job in covering the main ideas in ways the layman like us can understand, frequently using analogies and stories to engage us. Being a physics module, there's bound to be math, but here Prof Tan has really stripped it to the barebones to make it accessible to us. That being said you still need some basic knowledge in physics(or some self study) to really appreciate what's going on, some examples include commutators, creation and annihilation operators, operators etc

Since the lecture was focused on ideas, it was really my cup of tea. But I have to admit it's possible not everyone loves it, because some people are more interested in details and formal derivations. However, Prof Tan was happy to point the class to references for the full gory details, very consistently.

Coming into this module, like many, I have had heard things about string theory and I do think that when you actually learn it, you gain a different perspective and a better appreciation of what it is all about. Bottom line is, as with anything, it's better to form your judgment only after taking the effort to learn about it, rather than from rumours.

On top of the enjoyable lessons, the small class size made it such that we were a lot closer than most other modules, everyone knew everyone and Prof made an effort to go for lunch with us after class many of the weeks, and we would just chat about everything. His willingness to share and the camaraderie of the class is perhaps just as important a factor in the module being enjoyable as the lessons. 

Overall, I really liked this module, in fact it's probably my favourite over this sem. Would recommend to anyone interested in theoretical physics, just to have an exposure and make friends with people with similar interests. It's not all rosy though, I have to admit at the start it is easy to feel overwhelmed by notation you've never seen before, the abstractness of the content, and there's some pressure when a lot of the classmates seem to already know a lot of stuff.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY22/23 under Prof Wandrille Gaultier, Naomi Hoe, Chen Kan

This was billed as an introductory module but holy it's very intense. I have actually read up quite a lot(or so I thought) on investments and financial instruments etc before the module, and the first lesson covered maybe 70% of all I knew. Pretty sure a lot of people dropped the module immediately after the first lecture.

Lectures(seminars) are 3 hours, most of the lecturers are sufficiently engaging but it really is very draining to pay attention for 3h, even for the lectures where I already knew portions of the content. There's only a 10min break in between, plus lessons sometimes stretch the entire 3h. It's a lot of content, around 40-60 slides every week generally. If you are passionate about learning more this is the mod for you but generally that's not the case because those people would have taken some proper finance mods alr presumably. If you are a complete newbie be prepared to put in a lot of work, running through the slides quickly before class starts help to keep you on track and maybe get a coffee to push through the 3h.

Tests are painful because most of it are ridiculusly easy, it's quite difficult to get it wrong when you have the relevant reference materials. The median for test 3 was 19/20 lol. The difficulty comes in printing the humongous number of pages of notes(please make it softcopy) for the hardcopy-open-book tests, and squeezing in as many things as possible for the finals cheatsheet. As mentioned, if you have the reference material, you're going to get it, so it's really mainly about taming the alarming amount of content via cheatsheet.

The general vibe of the class is very quiet, there's about only 4 out of the cohort of 40 that speak up in class, which is great in the sense if you do, your question is bound to be answered during class.

There's also a group project, groups are assigned(very well I might add, there's a mix of people in terms of background and ability) and you choose a finance idea to present on for about 12min or sth, basically 2mins per person. Write a report as a group on the same topic. 

Overall, as someone who would keep up due partly to prior knowledge, I did enjoy the module(although I didn't like travelling to a cold seminar room since lectures are not always recorded, and it's easy to fall asleep due to the cold). Did not like the test structure of generally striaghtforward MCQs(sometimes there's trick questions but it's really just tricks, it's hardly anything conceptually difficult) but thank god finals was hard apparently(based on the performance of the cohort). I'd say, maybe quite banally, that it's the people that made the experience; be it new friends or the adjunct professors who were very willing to chat with us.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in 22/23 Sem 2 with Prof Yeo Ye(lecturer,tutorials) and Yunchao(TA)

Truly a difficult module.

It is prof's first time teaching this module so he will definitely make adjustments after talking to us, but this rendition has a lot of information, esp heavy maths, and not enough of highlighting big ideas which made many of us quite lost in the details. There's a lot of details presented at various points of the lecture and these sidebars do not really help the first-time learner of the content to focus on what's the key ideas, esp those introduced at the beginning of each chapter. Furthermore, it's a remix of the textbook which is slightly confusing, and the textbook itself is not very good at all. 

Also, it did not help that we did not receive sufficient training in Thermo and Classical Mech(Phase space integrations). It certainly was difficult trying to absorb the necessary background info and learn new SM things at the same time.

Tutorials were time consuming and quite difficult, but it definitely did help sharpen your understanding. It's the kind that's difficult and tedious even if you already understood what's going on, because often it required thinking out of the box a little, so much much more if you did not already know what's going on.

Assignments were quite frustrating sometimes because it was time consuming to work out things and small algebraic mistakes really mess up your entire life, you would have to redo the entire huge calculation.

Tests were very manageable though, prof tests a lot of bookwork which basically means reproducing parts of the lectures. If that sounds overly easy, trust me it's not, because there's so much math that hardly anyone can memorise anything. You really have to understand and then use the guidance of the questions to proceed, but very doable for the most part if you knew what's going on.

For finals, he gave a lot of hints on what will come out, so please please please familiarise yourself with all the tips and tricks in the relevant chapters(there are, again, quite a few of them). They are almost impossible to think of on the spot if you did not already have an idea of them, so do focus your attention only on the relevant chapters and save your overall revision for after finals.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Physicskid is absolutely right, the lectures are really just for introducing ideas, a lot of details are glossed over and takes a lot of time to work through and understand. 

However the presentation is hugely enjoyable as you get to hear from everyone on different topics, I really loved that about this mod and understandably the lectures have to be rushed a little to make time for so many presentations.

Short review because I heard this module is not offered next year. If it is, comment down below and I will give a more detailed review.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY22/23 under Prof Brett Mciness (Lecturer) and Luca(Tutor)

Two things I disliked about the module: 8am unrecorded lectures and the insanely high bell curve tests. Everything else was excellent.

Lectures were a joy due to prof being extremely entertaining and riveting. He has his own style for a lot of things, from content to notation and notes, so I have to admit that it's probably not everyone's cup of tea but I loved it. It's delivered in an almost casual way, and the notes are written in the same way as well, which is immensely enjoyable like standup comedy due to how humorous the prof is(for most of us, the math kids apparently hate how it's not in a formal Theorem-lemma-proof style). Notation-wise, he does have some which are quite unique and einstein summation is something that physics students might have seen before but probably takes a lot of getting used to for other majors.

Tutorials were reasonably straightforward if you understand the content completely, AND get his hints. The latter is not easy because a lot of the hints are very unconventional and some are frankly sketchy if you do not understand the reasoning behind it. When I fail to get the hint usually it's either a lot of extra work or straight up being unable to prove a result. The length was also pretty good to get familiar w the concepts and there's some questions here and there which force you to think a little bit.

note: Next year's format of tests will be different!
Exams: Exams were painful though, both tests were 4 MCQs in 1h or sth and finals was 5MCQs in 2h. Despite the necessity of justification, there's still way too much time for literally 4/5 questions and the questions were usually not particularly difficult either, leading to a ridiculously high bellcurve. You mess up one question that's about it, game over for you. Just for reference test 2's upper quartile was full marks, mean was 20/25. The median was 21/25, which means more than half the cohort was scoring above 84%! Not to mention prof is pretty kind when marking so a lot of undeserved people get full marks just from choosing the right option and writing something for the justification.

Overall I benefitted greatly from and enjoyed the module a lot, particularly as a senior physics student who has seen some of the more advanced structures/results before but never had the opportunity to really train my abstract thinking or my geometrical intuition for LA before. I have to admit though, it probably is very fast paced for someone seeing the second half for the first time, because it's very abstract and so it takes time to process. Prof took my feedback though and said he will try to work through more examples to get people familiarised with the abstractions for the next iteration of the mod. 

Recommended for anyone who will use/ want to learn LA in depth! (Yes, even if it's probably 8am unrecorded lectures again)
