author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY22/23 Lecturer: Jiwei

Content is actl somewhat useful and interesting, these basic optics are things that every physicist must know imo(however the math machinery isnt taught in detail in Y1)

Tests were all open universe, it's a matter of speed and accuracy I guess

However the lecturer really made the journey painful. It's a self study mod for me and for many of the physics majors taking the class. The lectures are insanely slow paced and hardly insightful, but I wouldn't know because I only went for 1.

 The rest of the review will be dedicated to my least favourite prof in my entire NUS career so far:

there are too many problems so I would just pick some of his biggest ones
This guy is simultaneously incompetent, disinterested, and caustic 

Incompetent: he barely knows the syllabus, doesn't have deep understanding, and is uncomfortable once pushed slightly out of the syllabus. He returns tutorials more than 2 weeks late, by then we have been studying 2 topics ahead and the timeliness of the feedback is severely lacking. Doesnt know how to use luminus, much less zoom recording. So emails are the only way to contact him for anything and everything, including submission of tutorials. But guess what? This guy doesnt check his emails. Apart from tutorials, the only email he replied was after 2 weeks. There's no way to schedule a consult with him officially because he doesnt check his email. He doesnt even know 21/10 was wellbeing day cos he doesnt check his email.
Disinterested: Any question that is not directly taught by him(even if it is found in the textbook he's heavily referencing) is not entertained. Tells me to not fuss over details, and to just use the results, going against the spirit of inquiry I feel all physicists should have. This is because "he is an experimentalist". What a joke, first of all he's teaching a theory mod so he better damn well know his stuff, secondly there are amazing experimental profs teaching theory mods amazingly. When it comes to his marking for tutorials, he is no better than a dumb machine. He checks the final answer literally and that's it. If your answer is exactly the same but not simplified, you get 0?(not exactly sure how the marks are distributed but I know for a fact the penalty is massive). That's how ridiculous it is, thank god it's more reasonable for tests.
Caustic: I once asked him for a question that i couldnt solve, he not only told me it's irrelevant to the syllabus(it's literally a question that's in the textbook, that is exactly the part that he taught), he also insinuated that my math is lacking and that I should have studied harder in secondary school. But guess what he did after insulting me? He didnt even bother helping me after I prompted him to. He told me he wasnt paid enough to do this, when it's literally part of his job scope

Seriously why is he even hired? I doubt many people can do a worse job if they tried

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY22/23

Prof Loh HQ(first half)/ Quek Su Ying (second half)

Prof Loh starts off with a recap of QM1, great for many who didn't know what's going on in QM1. Her lectures are ridiculously lucid, it's very well motivated and the flow of the lecture is incredibly smooth. One of the best lecturers I have ever had, her explanations are clear and she checks w the class their understanding, and makes adjustments on the fly to address things that most people haven't understood.

Tuts workload isn't very heavy, her test was great in that it always requires a little jump from reading the question to figuring out what you actually apply what you learnt, yet it is super clear as usual what she's asking for. If you know your stuff, her test would be completely doable. On the flipside if you dont have a good understanding of the syllabus you might struggle a bit. Thankfully she remarked a few rounds so that everyone had decent grades at the end

Prof QSY is a very nice prof, however I really disliked her lectures. Things arent motivated, the flow is unnatural, a lot of times something just drop from the sky and we don't know why or where it comes from. For the interaction picture lecture I did not even know that's the focus of the lecture until I was more than 30mins in. Furthermore there are random breaks in the middle of the lecture for us to copy down what she writes down. It's quite ridiculous how secondary school this is, I've never seen this in uni in my entire 3 years. It makes absolutely no sense because she eventually uploads her handwritten slides, so those who are slow can simply copy from there, there's no need to spend precious lesson time waiting for a select group of slow note-takers, when most people don't even take notes. Not to mention that it really disrupts the flow of the lecture, almost like NBA fouls. 

Another hugely annoying thing is that there isn't a set of reference material containing all the information. I mean it's understandable that she doesnt provide a full set of notes(even though physics major take it for granted generally that this is the case), but there isnt just a single reference textbook, she uploads ppt slides which are half empty, which is supplemented by her handwritten slides which are independent from the ppt, and finally there's this set of notes which is more like a summary + further reading(most of us didnt even know about this until halfway). So when you study you're looking at 10000 places to hunt for the information you need, just feels very disorganised. Compared to what other modules have: a reference textbook, a complete set of notes, or at least a complete set of slides with all the info needed. 

Her tutorials are not very difficult if you know what to do, the workload is very light actually(thank god, because second half of sem is always harder). However, many a times, partly because of her command of the language, partly because she doesn't give the form of what she's looking for, many of us do not really understand what she's looking for and what she needs us to do. The problem is she thinks there's one obvious way to answer the question but she doesn't realise that actually it's not very obvious to us what we need to do. Well for the future batches, somehow someway there's always someone who understands her perfectly, so get that guy to explain to the rest what's going on.

The problem is more for tests, 3 separate people clarified with her what she's asking for, to the point where for finals she had to stop everyone to explain what she's looking for. No one knows at first and a lot of us were completely stunned by what form we were supposed to leave the answer in.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY 22/23, taught by Prof Lim Hock

Godly Module

This module is the epitome of high effort high reward. In fact it's more insane effort insane reward. 

Lecture
The content is super heavy, each lecture was a full 1h45mins, super dense the prof is just writing on the board the entire time. However everything is derived properly and if you have been doing your work consistently, each new concept builds on the previous concepts meaningfully and it is really satisfying to see how you can derive the basics of an entire field "from scratch".  There are a lot of new concepts and techniques and you really walk away from each lecture with insight. I think within 2 lectures, I have learnt more things than during the entire sem of some modules. 

The downside is that it really is quite draining to keep up with all of the taught content. Since every lecture builds on the previous lectures, it pays to familiarise yourself with the concepts covered before(sounds obvious but it's really hard to do it when you're faced w the 30 page lecture note every week). I do go through the notes once fully and in detail, after the lecture (something I never do for any module till now), and still once in a while I struggle to recall a certain concept, esp those smaller ones, until I check w someone else. No cap this single module takes up 50% of all my studying time.

Also this module uses a lot of techniques and almost everything you have ever learnt in your first 2 years, especially vector calculus. I feel that most people's vector calculus is actually quite weak because the syllabus so far never drilled us on it; how many people have taken divergence more than 10 times in their physics career by the end of year 2? So this was really very good practise to sharpen your mathematical skills which everyone has learnt but most aren't proficient at all. To give a list of techniques/ideas used: CM, EM, PDEs, TSM, complex analysis, fourier analysis, tensors amongst others

Prof:
Prof Lim Hock is a legend, I think there were a couple of lectures where he did not refer to the notes a single time. Imagine writing for 1h45mins and not referring to anything, it's absolutely unbelievable. Very nice too, he once stayed back 30 mins after class with me to answer my questions, and he answers emails very promptly, in great detail too. He is always happy to entertain our doubts, and discuss things with us. I can tell he is very glad that we grew as physicists during this module too.

Tutorials:
The tutorials are similarly long, given that all of the concepts need to be given as questions. Some of the math can get abit heavy, and overall it takes up quite an ungodly amount of time too. If you have time, do try every question though, even if you don't get it, because every question is a learning point. Many a times I skipped something thinking it's similar to a previous question, but often times for these questions Im bitten because they actually are very easy and simply build on the result of the previous question. Prof never gives 2 questions that are similar. During tutorial sessions, he waits for students to request questions that we want him to go through. This is because the solutions are too long it's impossible to even cover half of them during the session, so it's only those that people struggle the most that are gone through. Otherwise he will go through the ideas for each question quickly, if no one has questions in particular(or more often than not, haven't attempted). As far as I know, no one has completed a single tutorial before? Usually there's always some question that's insanely long that's pretty much unsolvable and you just try to learn from the tutorial solutions the main idea

Tests:
Both open book, basically copy from textbook and tutorials. Questions are almost exactly based on that, and everything that isn't has also been mentioned in class, so 0 surprises. Prof said most people got near full marks so there's no point returning scripts. There's a joke that the gaussian curve is centred at 98

Finals:
Closed book, but surprisingly less tough than expected. 3 out of 5 questions were recycled, so it really pays off to do PYPs. In particular there's one question that's a mainstay(it comes out every year) that's basically for you to pass if you memorise that solution. I have made a excel table for question spotting that should be circulating. The hardest part is probably making the cheatsheet though, generally cheatsheets are quite useless here because there's so much content you can barely fit all the equations in, yet his questions mostly are derivations and he gives you the result alr, so unless you have a derivation on your cheatsheet it doesnt help much. Watch your time though, because if you cant recall a derivation, it takes a lot of time to think it up on the spot

Overall, if you have 20 hours to spend a week, I highly recommend this module. I've never learnt this much in a module. There's talk that fluid dynamics is a dead field where people just do simulations all day, but trust me the basics are surprisingly interesting and insightful.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Lecturer: Daryl Ooi, Tutor:Shibukawa Youhei

It's been touted as a interesting module but I guess I overhyped it internally. Not bad, unlike some of the other GEs, you actually were forced to think a little bit. It did prompt some reflection on my part regarding aspects of Singapore society that most, including myself, would normally not question as we are too used to it already.

Quizzes:
MCQ, but requires close reading. If you understand what the reading is talking about, all you have to do is then to figure out what they're asking for. It's sometimes quite vague and sometimes they're asking for things that are not explicitly mentioned in the text so you kind of have to guess the author's intentions and use your common sense as much as possible to sniff out what they want as an answer. It takes a bit of getting used to, I got stumped multiple times because I always thought "well technically the author didn't say this". But this is very important because finals is exactly like this,except 4 times longer, but without the power of friendship.

Project:
It's really difficult imo to come up with a topic that's simultaneously relevant to the question in that it presents a "moral dilemma", as well as interesting to us, plus having to cut across 2 or more topics. Once you have that the moral dialogue part is also pretty trite cos you have to present the dialogue instead of the content which seems quite weird to me but thankfully I had fun teammates so it was overall an enjoyable experience.

Lecture:
I personally didnt like daryl's lectures because as a physics major I'm very used to info dense lectures and I get bored when I listen to lectures that aren't very info dense. But I think that's a me problem more than anything, daryl's pretty nice

Tuts:
2hour slot but they always end at 1h30mins so that's nice. But it felt a little rushed sometimes cos we didnt really have time to discuss things in depth with the group or the class, which is also understandable because I guess most people aren't really super into debating about these things.

Overall it's not too bad at all as a mod, the readings can get abit heavy though so please make sure to read consistently, best if you read daily

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY22/23 w paul lim

Content: everything from griffiths after EM1, except a couple of topics that were skipped e.g. waveguides

He speaks quite slowly and tends to emphasise the really simple stuff as well as go through algebra step by step, and makes mistakes along the way so it's really draggy. I just read the textbook after the first lecture, though I heard it's got slightly better afterwards(once he realised he's not going to complete the syllabus on time if he continued the initial pace)

Tests were basically lifted for griffiths and contained the easiest questions from griffith's too, so the bell curve is ridiculously steep. My test 2 I scored 75 and that's 25th percentile. lol 

Tuts were ungraded, about 5 questions every 2 weeks and he spends 1 lecture going thru them. This is where he makes mistakes, when he's holding the answer script, so there's really no point going. He's just working thru everything himself in real time, there isnt too much additional insight 

To balance out the curve, finals killed us. Not a single person completed the paper 
I believe(unless there's a hidden god). The last question was a coupled DE which is near impossible to solve in the timeframe. So much much harder than tests, come prepared. One of the questions were repeated though so it's worth looking thru the PYP.
thread_title: PC3274 Mathematical Methods in Physics II

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in 21/22, sem 2 under prof edward teo

The most enjoyable mod of the semester. Prof Edward is amazing at explaining things and it always make a lot of sense when he explains something. There's great flow of logic as well, each point is well motivated

To be honest this is one of the lighter physics mods unlike what people might guess, but it is mainly due to how clearly things are expounded upon and the super readable notes prof edward has taken great pains to make(I took a look at the textbook, he really elucidated things greatly). Furthermore, because you are introduced to all these different topics, the workload actually allows you to slow down and fully grasp them, building great fundamentals for future applications rather than rushing through without understanding as content heavy mods tend to force you to. 

As physicskid mentioned, bell curve is ridiculously steep. Test 1 average was 60-sth and someone scored full marks. Test 2 average was a ridiculous 78 with the 75th percentile at 90, so we got demolished for finals to balance things out. 

Breakdown is extremely reasonable, 10% from quiz and 90% from 2 tests+finals, so no free marks thank god. The tests were also exceptionally well designed, usually having some trick you need to see and some part that test your understanding conceptually, but also parts that are easy enough for most people so that everyone has some fun doing the paper.

Overall a great mod and would highly recommend anyone interested to take it if you're brave enough for the curve
thread_title: PC3288 Advanced UROPS in Physics I

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
You contact a prof during or before hols to ask to do a UROPS because the application deadline is very early, I seem to remember it's even before modreg is done but anyway there's no time to do this only when modreg starts because you need time to talk to profs and explore options etc

The mod is very lacking in planning and communication, there's no email about anything until week 10 or something when suddenly we are informed about the due date in week 14. 

Complete lack of information dissemination so here's what I know: 
-to choose as level 3k UROPS your supervisor has to select it as such before he posts the project on the website. It's a hassle to change it afterwards.
-There must be a presentation with your prof and someone else, that someone else should be post grad(I am not very sure about this) so it is possible to do it intra-group if you have a post-doc in the group, there's no need to find someone outside of the group for this
-Report must be submitted to your prof and a moderator who must be a prof outside of your group, they decide your grade after discussing about it.

Overall your mileage may vary hugely based on your prof and the type of project you get, expect to spend a lot of time on this possibly, sometimes even devoting whole weeks to this when you need to get results. Do not treat this as merely 1 single mod and only plan to devote 6-10hours a week. 

Also generally do not expect that this is pitched at a "year 3 mod" level, you could be expected to do some grad level reading for your project possibly. Again this really depends on your prof and your project but I am glad I learnt a lot because the project was challenging.

Highly recommending doing year-long UROPS because you have so much more than just 1 more sem compared to 1-sem urops, you have the entire summer as well which is pretty much the duration of another sem. If you are planning to complete a project in 1 sem from scratch you can expect to take 2 weeks to write a report fully. This means in my case you should have completed your readings and theory, starting on your code in as early as february. Essentially meaning you learn one mod's worth of stuff or more within 1/3 of the sem. So highly recommend the year long UROPS otherwise be prepared to do a lot of readings before the sem starts

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY21/22 Sem 1 (20 MCs) (staff and assessments same as rain and coffee)

Teaching Assistants
The TAs are really great, nice people and very knowledgeable too. Unlike PC2192 they are actually able to help with your experiment and it was very heartening to see them tell each other about the problems they resolved(Arif and Chuan Jie, I approached them mainly) so both know how to resolve the problem in the future.

Laboratory Sessions
There is A LOT of unnecessary red tape, much more than PC2193. Luckily not everything is so strictly regulated, and the lab staff are actually very nice people. 
Timing wise you are only supposed to go in for 2 3-hour blocks a week, so as you can imagine your experiment time is extremely limited and so it's very important that you read up very well and know exactly what you are doing.

Reports
Instead of 3 summaries and 1 report in PC2193, there's 5(4 in this sem) reports. You can imagine the exponential increase in workload, it's basically 1 fully latexed report every 2 weeks, and each one takes 1-2 days. Be very prepared for the workload.

Viva
Everyone has a different style so there's no one description that encapsulates viva, but I've heard Prof Thomas doesnt need viva if your report is stellar enough. Dr Saumitra is pretty chill and asks you questions about the theory which are reasonable. Dr Ren is extremely nice and will even sometimes fill you up on the theory, while sharing real life applications. Look forward to an indepth discussion with Prof Tok, and he will prompt you regarding real life applications too.

Experiments
Let's be honest, not all experiments are equal. Some are way easier and takes much less time, while others might have faulty setups. Do your research and share info regarding faulty setups.

Overall thoughts:
My only offline physics module so I was really happy to finally see fellow physics students. I thought that the theory here is less intense than EP1, as I could generally have a good grasp of what's going on and did reasonably well for my viva, whereas in EP1 I did band gap and hall effect which have theory that can be significantly more complicated if you were to delve in. Of course this depends on your choice of experiments, and I spent a lot of time reading up this year. All in all I feel like this is less an experimental mod than theory mod, because honestly who can't collect data(unless your setup is faulty)? Rather it is the theory understanding that is more self-paced and up to the student to research.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
PC2132 Classical Mechanics
Taken in AY21/22
Lecturer: Dr Kenneth Hong
TA: Nil

Grade Distribution:
Worksheets
Assignments
Test 1
Test 2

Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week) + Tutorials 1hr/week :
The Tutorials are also lectures but will usually cover mainly problems. Lectures are on the longer side, usually around 1h45mins. Kenneth goes into great depth and will explain and emphasise, resulting in the length of the lecture being as such, however I have to say because he goes into such detail, this is maybe the only mod this sem for me where I really felt I understood everything deeply. 

Worksheets:
Exactly what has been gone through in class, you can do along but better to do yourself to make sure you understood lectures, and only refer if you're stuck.

Assignments:
All the worksheets and assignments are due in reading week. In the best case scenario yes everyone will do in their own pace but generally the effect is much worse than expected, most people will leave it to the end. The assignments are not easy and the algebra is usually time consuming, so in the end a lot of people just cross referenced. Not to mention we have no idea how we scored for this mod until the tests came back during reading week/exam week. I really didn't like this arrangement, it's better to have weekly submissions so that people keep up with the lectures, plus there's some form of feedback at least for your proficiency in this course.

Tests:
The tests were pretty hard, the first one was so bad he made us redo the test as an assignment. It's open book but hard copy only which is really a mystery because we are forced to basically print out the slides. I am pretty sure a lot of other mods allow soft copy materials and no internet so that seems to be more reasonable. 

Thoughts:
Overall as with any kenneth module, the workload was pretty heavy but you learn quite a bit. I would say the experience of rushing assignments during reading week was extremely frustrating(day in day out that's all you are doing) but I also had a lot of insights so that was an interesting experience but a stressful one.
thread_title: SP1541 Exploring Science Communication through Popular Science

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in 21/22 Semester 1
Instructor : Clare Chong
Scoring:
- 15% Interaction & Engagement
- 5% Book Chapter Reflection
- 20% News Article 1
- 30% News Article 2
- 25% Oral Presentation
- 5% Reflective Commentary

Seminars:
You are expected to do some prereading before coming to class. It's mainly going through some new technique for writing science communication articles and then discussing in small groups. If you have heard that you do not learn much here I'd say that's somewhat reasonable, it's sort of almost teaching a standard formula to follow for writing these articles(which is quite useful honestly). Thankfully my seminar group had very interesting people though so the company made it better. 

Assignments:
This was quite time consuming honestly and the only non-core mod that I've taken that depends almost core mod time and effort. Selecting a good reserach paper is truly half the battle won and so it pays off scouring the internet for suitable one. As  a physics major the first writing(pre-course writing) they made us do was disproportionately difficult compared to the other majors' and finding an easier one for the news article 1 made the writing almost effortless. 

Extremely demoralising when we received the grades(which comes out very late, almost one month after the first graded writing was submitted, by then it's near the end of the semester already), most of us got around 60 and it is something nobody expects I guess. Honestly it's supposed to be based on in-class discussion, which you can expect is not super deep, so we are not sure how to score better as well, especially when feedback given is general and vague too. It is annoying because the first assignment was only 5% and it was not easy deciding if you should just SU this mod since it takes up so much time but you have no idea how you're doing compared to the rest of the batch. 

However I heard that in other sems people who consistently get 70-80 dont even get an A-, seems like the grading is very inconsistent (unless somehow everyone in my seminar group just sucked? unlikely though). I decided to proceed with an SU mentality for the second half of the deliverables and it think it would have sucked a lot more if I got 70-80, put in even more effort, and came out with a B+. 

Overall the general consensus for the module is definitely well warranted, but there are some things to take away(incl friendship) so don't make up your mind before even trying. Though try to work fast and do things ahead(as soon as they are announced) so you dont waste too much time on the deliverables.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY21/22 Semester 1 (Post Circuit Breaker Semester)
Lecturer: A/Prof Murray Barrett Douglas
TA: Nil

Grade Distribution:
• Assignments (5): 40%
• Finals: 60%

Contents:
• Introduction to Vector Calculus
• Electric Fields
• Special Methods for Finding Potential
• Electric Fields in Matter
• Magnetic Fields
• Magnetic Fields in Matter
• Maxwell’s Equations and electrodynamics

Lecture (Two 2 hr Lectures / Week):
Prof Murray's lectures generally go through derivations and a couple of harder examples. He really goes at a breakneck speed because he skips the algebra which means that you really need to be very familiar with the content(after reading Griffiths yourself as he specified) to be able to keep up. The majority were unable to keep up and thus many resorted to studying themselves instead and not attend lectures. He encourages you to interrupt him with questions but it is generally the case that many are so lost we do not know where to start asking questions, especially when you confused for several consecutive steps.

After a round of feedback collection halfway he tended to cover a little bit more content before going through the examples. 

Tutorial: There are no tutorials, so this is where you better try griffiths problems yourself. I advise the best way to learn from this module is fully understand griffiths first, then do a few problems so that you are already familiar with the solution techniques before coming to lectures. You will take away a lot more if you understand the solution techniques well enough to follow his train of thought in class.

Assignments:
Generally quite diffcult and there's occasionally an extra step not covered in lecture that you have to realise. Luckily usually the lecture one day before the assignments are due, he will give hints, run through the rough idea, as well as entertain clarifications about the assignment questions. Generally there are a lot of discussion for assignments, so everyone, deservingly or not, do extremely well. 
So it really hurts to lose a few marks here and there.

Exams:
Honestly the exam is really very doable, much easier than assignment questions. 

Thoughts:
Ideally you treat this like prof peter ho's PC1142, and really come to class prepared, so that you gain insights. Unfortunately I was crushed by SP1541 and was unable to do so, because in hindsight once you understand what's going on generally, it's not as ridiculously hard as it seemed.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message: love the review!
thread_title: GET1014 The Art of Science, the Science of Art

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
Taken in AY2020/2021 S2
Prof:Johan van der maarel
Quite a chill mod, prof is super chill. One lecture he literally said im feeling abit tired today, and we're ahead on schedule, so let's stop here HAHA. Really charismatic and lovable guy, but do appear for the quizzes which form quite a big component for the assessment. They're timed and it closes like 1hour after lecture ends so if you're ponning lecture please remember to do them

Main complaint is how we're not given grades at all. You have no idea what you got for quizzes the entire sem, answers arent released at all(the reason given was people who are sick or sth need to do them first. that's abit funny because how many people are sick for 12-13 weeks, such that we cant even get the very first quiz' answer?) You dont get a grade for the essay as well. So literally no way to know if you should be aiming to SU this mod halfway thru the sem.

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY2021/2021 S2

Lecturer/tutor: Prof Mankei Tsang

Workload
Quite some stuff are removed from past years' syllabus such that now this is a more manageable module, there's also no insanely hard weekly assignments. It's 2 assignments of 8 questions that are quite manageable if you pay attention in class. 

Lecture
Prof Mankei is a transcendental lecturer. His notes are impeccable and he just flashes his notes during class. However most of the lecture is just him writing on his notebook, which was really elegant as he starts from nothing and you can see the ideas slowly develop, rather than just a bunch of equations on slides. He explains things extremely well, it's unbelievable how quickly we understand the concepts he's talking about. Moreover there are small in class quizzes to check your understanding and he goes through them to make sure you understand everything. Stellar stuff

Tests are also manageable. Both the midterms and finals are doable if you understood the material, and there were people leaving the exam venues early lol. I believe that's the way to go for tests, they should test learning and not IQ. What's the point of ridiculously difficult tests that requiring ingenuity to solve, which doesnt test the amount of understanding the students have? I personally enjoy a good challenge but I feel that that isnt the purpose of an exam. Prof Mankei's tests are well structured, where if u have insight u can use little tricks to expedite the process, otherwise you can still solve them by the traditional methods if you studied the material. I was suitably entertained, and it's certainly not one of those exams where the median is 10/100

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
AY 2020/2021 S2

Only half the cohort are physics majors but the bell curve is still steep. For reference midterms were upon 120 and the median was 80, 75th percentile was something like 85. If you dont know how to do the "routine" questions you're gone

Didn't really like the lectures, felt that many things were not well explained and some introductions were rushed when they need a lot more time for the ideas to set in.Found it easy for attention to drift during his lectures as well, even on the recorded lectures I had to replay a few times. Reading the textbook and googling helps immensely

Tutor was Ko-Wei Tseng. Finished the first tutorial and could barely understand what the tutor was talking about, didnt go for any other tutorials since you can do them yourself generally anyway. Jaren is a very good teacher, please go for his tutorials 

Lab was useless but lab TA alvin leow was good and he tried to teach us a few things

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
PC2193 Experimental Physics I

Taken in AY20/21S2
Physics Major (24 MCs)

Module Coordinator: Dr Wang Xuesen
Lecturers:
Dr Wang Xuesen
Dr Andrivo Rusydi
Dr Nikolai Yakovlev
Dr Ariando 

TA: Various (Unimportant, they do nothing at all)

Grade Distribution:
2 Presentations
1 Report + Viva
3 Summaries + Viva

Experiments done(varies year to year):
• X-Ray Diffraction of Crystals
• UV-Vis Absorption Spectroscopy
• Absorption of Gamma and Beta Rays
• Hall Effect in P/N Type Semiconductors
• Band Gap of Germanium
-Rutherford scattering

Laboratory:
It's OTOT but please rush the labs. Your deliverables take time and they can only be done after your experiments are complete. You would rather get bottlenecked
by deliverables then experiments(which take time to complete before potentially getting bottlenecked again by deliverables)

The module is usually done in pair-work, although you can choose to do it alone.

Viva:
Viva experiences really depend on you and your profs. Nikolai is very particular about the details and he typically asks questions about expeirmental procedures, interpretation of data etc instead of theory. Meanwhile Ariando is very theory based and my experience with andrivo was very guided theory. Ariando was very patient for my first viva and guided me but for the second, maybe he's tired but he just asked if i knew how to explain a graph. When I didnt he explained the entire thing to me, so presumably I got 0 for that part lol.

Presentation:
Practise and make sure it's around 10 mins. If you exceed you might be cut off, not sure(first presentation was delayed significantly as QnA took a lot of time, then second presentation was very well scheduled, presumably the Lab techs reflected about the length of QnA to the profs after the first).

The first presentation's questions were fire lol. most of us were absolutely stunned and I got asked something that the other prof admitted he didn't know why also. Second one was a lot more manageable. Whatever the case presentation viva is only 5 mins so u dont get grilled too hard. Even if you have no idea what you're talking about, try to clarify and interact w the profs instead of just stoning cos else how are they going to grade you?

Second presentation didn't have script allowed. Which was very very annoying and you really have to practise hard

Thoughts:
You enter the mod thinking this is going to be unlike y1 labs which are useless apart from 1142, slowly you realise it's all just PHYWE playsets and so it's actually the same thing as y1 labs essentially. Nonetheless there are takeaways, including data analysis, interpretation/accounting of data/trends/anomalies, and troubleshooting. 

For troubleshooting, please read ahead on the lab manual and do further reading on the underlying theory as well before entering the lab. You have a much better idea of the components of your experiments and can thus anticipate the problems easier and go through each component. Please try yourself before you ask the lab techs(generally the TAs are as lost as you are) because this is actually an essential skill if you're going to do experiments in the future, and things not working initially is the norm, not the exception

Choose your partner carefully, hopefully one of you has a flexible schedule else it seriously sucks. Having someone else to bounce ideas off and cover your mistakes really helps. If you're going in yourself it can be really sian

Our batch got this week 10 surprise where X-ray and Absorption both suddenly had additional tasks. Ridiculous if you ask me, esp since i did both of them. Really tough luck but luckily I finished X-ray before they announced. Nonetheless I had to head back to my lab to do the additional task which took a few more days. Writing this in a paggro way so that future batches dont have to randomly have a few of their experiments get extra tasks so late into the mod, esp when timings are tight then and a lot of things are due. 

Avoid absorption of beta gamma, variance is really high and so you need huge amounts of data to get a trend, which means A LOT of time in the lab. For reference one reading takes 1hour for one sample and one material. You have been warned. 
ESR was really hard conceptually as well, and I have heard things about magnetic moments too. The rest of the experiments I did were all ok, I did my research. Please plan out your experiments because the experiments' difficulty and time commitment vary hugely and there's a lot of conditions(you cant repeat experiments under profs for summaries, you need to do one experiment mininum under each prof etc etc)

I would say there isn't any experiment where you completely grasp the theory already, so dont choose the ones that you're familiar with because you learn less. Rather, wtv you choose, just read and read and read. Easier said than done ofc because you have time constraints and it's a rabbit hole where everything eventually goes into quantum lol but just try, it's rewarding when you finally get a rough picture of the physics involved.

A lot of setups have problems in the beginning. Please have a group for the batch so that everyone can share which setups are problematic and which computers are infected with viruses etc

Overall, just like army, the experience may not be the most enjoyable but you would take away more if you put in more effort, especially if you stay very curious. A good habit is to write down any questions you might have about anything, then see if it can be answered with reasonable amount of effort in research/discussion with others

author: Wuu Yyiizzhhoouu
message:
CS1231
Taken in AY20/21 Sem 1
Difficulty: Moderate-Difficult
Workload: Moderate
Lecturer(s): Prof Colin Tan and Prof Li Wei
Tutor: Li Yunzhe

Additional remarks: 
Lecture Webcasted: Yes
Tutorial Compulsory: Yes
Help sheet: Open universe
Calculator: Open universe

Thoughts: Had some sort of Math Olympiad experience so I took this module thinking it's gonna be an easier version of MA1100 and gg to be easy A but oh how wrong I was. There's a lot of proving and the content gets harder towards the end, though it's still manageable if you spend time to sit down and go through everything. Relations got to be the hardest topic in the whole mod(as my tutor concurs), and it's the only topic that I couldn't understand fully even after going through the lecture notes a few times(had to consult my tutor). Otherwise for a core mod it's alright if you put in consistent work. Lectures can be watched at double speed, they upload the lectures in very convenient forms in multimedia and very up to date. Can even just read the lecture notes, prof liwei's are quite complete and only go back to watch the lecture if you need an explanation for something. Saves a lot of time, took me about 20mins to crunch through a 2hr lecture for topics I've done before

Lecturer: Prof Colin is very very humorous and good on time. Not gonna lie I took this mod this sem(instead of CS1231S) because I saw the reviews about prof colin. Very personable too. Prof Liwei's lectures seem a bit more rushed and sometimes she struggles with finishing on time, even though it got better towards the end as she adjusted after feedback. Both of them were very dedicated, making compilations of formulas and answering questions on the group chat, sometimes at ridiculous hours like 4am. Probably that's not recommended for their health but as students, I definitely recommend doing your research(SoC has  a page on who is teaching what next sem) and taking the mod under these two lecturers

Assignments: Quite a bit harder than tutorials, but apparently a lot of people checking answer with each other so the bellcurve is steep. Be very careful, check your work and check the lecture notes, make sure that every step is there because one seemingly trivial step can cost you a whole mark which is 2.5% of the 40 mark assignment.

Tutorials: About 10 questions every tutorial, thankfully they're quite manageable if you've been paying attention during lecture and understand everything. Tutorials are 2 hours (usually yunzhe takes 1.5hours) and it's kinda duh because if you did your work you are just coming to check answers, once in a while u get something wrong or u learn something new when the tutor deviates from the given question. If u didnt do your work you wouldnt understand anything. Abit inefficient the system, plus video doesnt have to be on and attendance is marked just based on names(no participation marks or wtv). Many ppl just check in and go to sleep. HOWEVER, yunzhe is an excellent tutor and he explains things v clearly and pateintly. He is also extremely willing to take questions, and goes the extra mile in answering them(even emailing us about it after the tutorial session). Despite this tutorial system, I must say i enjoyed the tutorials because of the tutor and he really makes the last afternoon lesson on friday bearable

Mid terms and Finals: Reviewing tgt as they're of similar difficulty. The content might seem manageable but the finals and mid terms requires you to grind in order to have everything at the back of your hand, time is very tight. Finals has a lot more questions that are non MCQ/MRQ(multi-response question), means u cant just guess them. Obviously there's a few proving questions which require you to write quite a bit, but in terms of overall percentage it's not a lot you can probably get away with a good grade without spending too much time on that.

Final comments: If you put in consistent effort, and practise the PYP, there's no reason you don't do well. There's a lot of consult sessions for you to ask questions outside of class, both for ppl who cant catch up and for others with extracurricular questions or PYPs. This really rewards the people who did PYPs as the solutions are not available, if you have been doing PYP early you get a lot more of your doubts clarified. Though consults are all recorded and uploaded so you can just review them. all in all a meaningful mod, the discrete math covered are gg to be useful

author: Xiaobbb
message:
The most BS mod i took in my first sem. Taught by prof chang. I think they just changed the group project scope to some smart nation solution and tbh, as a 1000 level course it seemed way too demanding on the technical side with no background info provided. Basically we had to come up with a new system that can help solving the country's existing problems e.g. traffic, health issue etc. Like, hello? If this was my honours proj then i would probably understand but as a first year? Its simply not feasible and just a waste of time. 
Dr chang was although very vague and contradicting in giving instructions ( -so dont get fooled)
Overall a time consuming mod and do not overlook the bonus qns at the end of the sem! It determines your grade hahaha surprisingly.
Glad they are getting rid of the mod soon, but for those who are taking it in 16/17 sem 2, good luck.

author: Yangshun
message:
Sorry for digging up this super old post... I happened to see it and felt very inclined to reply.

I think you have failed to understand the point of programming. You are aware of "managing complexity" but I'm not sure if you truly understand that takeaway. 

> "Why do you need to program Python to find the transpose of a 3x3 matrix when you can easily do it by hand?"

Sure, you can definitely do a transpose of a small matrix by hand. It's definitely faster to do it by hand than to write a program to do it for you.

But... the point here is to write a reusable and extensible program. Let's say you wrote a transpose() function, that helps you calculate the transpose of an n x m matrix. Fair enough, doing a transpose of a 3 x 3 matrix is faster by hand. But consider these points
- Do you think it will be faster to find the transpose of a 1000 x 1000 matrix by hand or by your program?
- Let's say you had 1000 3 x 3 matrices to find the transposes of. Would it still be faster to do by hand?
- Your friend wants to find the transpose of a 3x3 matrix but doesn't know how to do it. He asks you for help. Would you be willing to help him every time he asks for it, or would it be faster to pass him your transpose function and let him DIY?

Computers are stupid, yes you have to tell them exactly what needs to be done, but they are incredibly good at doing repetitive stuff and doing it correctly without mistakes.

I don't deny that CS1010S takes up a lot of your time and it's not for everyone. Nevertheless, I hope you have learnt important lessons from the course. Blaming your own stupidity and incompetence is not the best way to handle it. I'm very glad you worked hard for the class, and am sorry it didn't work out for you in the end. In life, it's not only important to work hard, you also need to work smart. Did you evaluate your studying methods in the middle of the course to see if there were ways to improve your approach? 

Wish you all the best for your future.

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in: AY14/15 Semester 2

Module Lecturers: Irene Woon, Anand Ramchand

Ever since AY14/15, IS1103 has been rebranded as IS1103FC (and now as IS1103X), where an FC (flipped classroom) approach has been taken. Flipped classroom just means that some of the lessons will be conducted digitally, via the Coursera platform, and students can attend them at their own pace and flexibility. Whether this change is for better or for worse, I'll leave it up to you to decide after reading this review.

The module covers ethical issues in the context of technology and relevant interesting scenarios are given to students for them to analyse and discuss. Examples of such issues: If a monkey uses a photographer's camera to take a selfie, does the IP of the photos belong to the monkey or the photographer? Should drones be allowed in urban areas as they are double-edged swords? (They can provide value through delivery services, but at the same time, be misused for illegal purposes). Sorry I'm unable to provide too much an overview of the class as I can't remember what was taught after Week 4 (if anything at all)... on to the next part.

Firstly, there are only around 6 physical lessons every other week. The lecturers are supposed to upload lesson video content every week so that students can view them at home. Also, there are supposed to be weekly quizzes to allow students to test their understanding of the lecture materials for each topic. However, for some reason, after Week 4, the lecturer stopped updating both lecture videos and quizzes. There was nothing left to do in between the physical lessons. The module syllabus wasn't even updated lol. I kinda slacked off after Week 4, not touching the module much. This is good if you are a slacker like me, bad if you are a good student and you are keen to learn about the ethical issues in technology.

Secondly, there is the Term Paper, where you work in teams of 4/5 to explore an emerging topic related to the course. Note how vague the aim of the paper is? It's really as open as you can imagine, and you are free to research on whatever topic you like. Example of topics include internet addiction, gaming addiction, social media depression, phantom vibration syndrome, etc. My team picked Facebook Depression (FD) and we researched on the causes of FD and how they can be prevented/cured. There were a few milestones in between, supposedly to check on the progress of the team, but the module instructors did not give very useful feedback anyways. We managed to cough up 6,000 words within a week and submitted a paper, crossing our fingers and hoping the instructors have a similar tolerance for nonsense as us.

Another major CA component was oral participation. There is a Coursera forums but it's not widely used. In an attempt to gain some CA marks to make up for my predictably poor Term Paper and Exam scores, I tried to gain more CA marks through being active on the forums. In class, try to speak up more also, as it's really beneficial for oneself to contribute your opinions and share them with the class. Your just might gain new insights from the lecturers and classmates! Class discussion is perhaps the most fun part of the module.

Lastly, the exams. Since it was open book, I didn't prepare for the module at all, and merely printed out the lecture notes and brought in the textbook. However, in the last lecture, the module instructor did advise us on how to approach the exam questions. It's really very structured in the sense that you bring up some ethical theory or codes from "Software Engineering Code of Ethics and Professional Practice" and apply them to the exam questions. However, you have to make a stand on whether a particular decision is ethical or not. Examples of ethical scenarios given in the exam: Your friend is in a financially tight situation and you have purchased a software that he/she can benefit from, do you make a copy of the software for your friend? You have to use some of the ethical theories (consequence-based, utilitarian-based, etc) to evaluate the scenario and come up with the decision. It's my final semester and I was in the YOLO-mode, hence I just suibian answer all the questions and left the exam hall early.

In retrospect, the module instructors weren't very responsible in ensuring that the students were imparted the relevant knowledge throughout the duration of the module. As lazy students who just want to get the module over and done with, my friends and I did not really chase them about it, so that everyone will be happy (:

Final Grade: A (LOL.)

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Marcelo Ang

This is the most chill and relaxed module one can ever ask for. CS graded, no exams, almost no homework and a project which I completed in onr hour. The aim of the module is to come up with an innovation and students are given $250 to build it.

Every week, we attend a two-hour seminar which is mostly spent on class presentation of our project ideas. My partner and I spent 3/4 of the semester getting suan-ed by professors about how lousy/stupid/naive our ideas were. By the end of the semester, I was panicking because I couldn't think of a decent idea and was afraid that I would fail the module. I spent a few days, really thinking about what/how to make, and I finished my product in a day. In the end, there was a poster session (somewhat like a FYP presentation), and we won two awards: Best Poster Design Award and Merit Award. We won the merit award because we had a real working prototype of a collapsable hanger that makes taking out and inserting clothes easy.

Grade: CS (Everyone in class passes this module).

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in AY10/11 Sem 2

Lecturer: Dr Ang Chuan Heng

My class only had 22 people, hence we were not graded using a bell curve. Initially I thought it was not a good thing for me, because the people who took this advanced programming class would surely be good in programming/they liked the previous programming module CS1010E. In the end, it didn't really matter I guess, because I managed the module pretty well, mainly because I spent a lot of time learning stuff outside the syllabus and attempting competitive programming questions. It's really interesting to solve programming questions!

When I thought I screwed up the mid-terms for that module, it turns out I did quite well and scored 86/100! Perhaps because I designed the code using the conventional approach taught in the lecture notes, and that the few lines of code I missed out, albeit important, didn't cost me a lot of marks. Finals were easy. I breezed my way through. At the end of it I couldn't be bothered to do the MCQs and I filled in the answers randomly.

Grade: A+

I served as a Lab Tutor in the subsequent semester and created this resource website for the CS1020E students. Have a look: https://sites.google.com/site/cs1020e/
thread_title: CS1020E Data Structures and Algorithms I

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in AY14/15 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Zhao Shengdong

Admittedly, I did not really know what the module was about before taking the module. I was keen to take level 4000 CS modules to take to fulfill a focus area so that I can graduate by the end of the academic year. I took CS4249 with CS3240 together in the same semester, although CS3240 is a pre-requisitie for CS4249. I would say it's not a bad idea to take them together as there are some overlapping content and I had to only study those parts once for both modules.

Module content:
While CS3240 focuses on the Design Thinking process, CS4249 focuses on HCI theories behind the experimental stage of Design Thinking. In AY14/15, Prof Zhao reversed the order of the syllabus and spent the first half of the syllabus teaching experimental design and statistical analysis. For this part, he goes very in-depth and the materials are pretty manageable to grasp. There are also many examples to guide you in the whole process from experiment design to analysis.

The second half of the module covers the various theories, laws and models that govern HCI, such as Fitts' Law, GOMS, Power Law of Practice, etc. This half of the module feels lighter, as there is no math (in the statistics sense) involved. Most of these laws involve simple mathematical formulas. We also watched many videos (TED talks) on Cognition, Decison Making and Psychology of Habits. The TED talks were highly interesting and I thoroughly enjoyed them.

Students can also participate in Prof Zhao's PhD students' lab experiments for 0.5% bonus CA each. The value in participating in these experiments is not only to get the bonus 0.5%, but also to learn the process of conducting experiments, which will be valuable in the experimental design assignment. One of the experiments was about the effectiveness of notifications while using Google Glass when driving, so I got to play with Google Glass :D

Lectures:
Part of the CA participation marks is for attending the lectures, so I had to wake up early every Tuesday morning and drag myself to school. Prof Zhao takes note of latecomers, so better be early/punctual for lessons. Typically, he also starts each lecture with a review quiz. I don't have the habit of revising my content regularly, so most of the time I can't answer the review questions. He said the review performance will be counted as part of the attendance.

Quizzes:
Twice a semester, there's a 5% CA quiz, which has 5 questions (mix of MCQ and short answer questions). I flunked them super badly, scoring 3.5/5 and 1.5/5 for both quizzes respectively. The average for the quizzes isn't very high though, because the questions were really tricky! Somehow, there were crazy people scoring full marks for both the quizzes.

Assignments:
There were two assignments, one for each half of the course. The first assignment was on Experimental Design and Analysis. We had to conduct experiments to compare two different copy and paste techniques and evaluate their effectiveness. The skeleton code we were given for the autocomplete widget and experiment interface was horrible and it was hard to set up. Hence I used the given code for the front end aspects, and re-implemented the rest from scratch. There were many parts to this assignment: Creating the experiment, conducting the experiment and analyzing the experiment. Each part will take up quite an amount of time but in the process you'll revise the lecture content and be familiar with it. Statistical analysis will be done in R and there's a basic R workshop for those who aren't familiar with it. Sadly, the first assignment is individual work.

Luckily, the second assignment is pair work and it involves using CogTools to compare the time taken for completing certain tasks between two interfaces using the GOMS model. We compared two IPPT calculator interfaces, and design our own improved IPPT calculator interface that meets the task of showing the different combinations that a user can achieve to reach a goal (gold/silver/pass). Thereafter, combining with the design from your partner, come up with a combined IPPT calculator interface and justify it. Although only mockups were needed, but my partner and I created a working prototype of an improved IPPT calculator with goal features while most of the class did interactive non-workable prototypes. This gave us some bonus points I guess (:

Exams:
Exams were pretty manageable, as the format and style was similar to past year exams. Prof Zhao gave us answers for sample past year papers, and whoever that tried it would benefit from it greatly. Exam consisted of 40% MCQ (20 questions worth 2 marks each) and 60% open-ended with many short answer questions. ANOVA kept appearing in many questions of the exam paper, and I wasn't familiar with it, so I had to make smart guesses. I also read the question on Fitts Law wrongly and missed out one whole part. There were also some tricky questions, and they involved more than just mere substitution of values into formulas.

Grade: A. 

I guess I did well for my assignments (around 8% above average). My quizzes were slightly below average and I'm not aware of my participation marks. I'm actually unsure if I did well in the exams, but besides a few careless mistakes that I made, the rest should be correct.
thread_title: CS4249 Phenomena and Theories of Human-Computer Interaction

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in AY14/15 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Ng Teck Khim

Module content:
This module gives you an introduction to a few Computer Vision-related topics: Image Processing (change detection, corner detection, histogram equalization), Geometry (camera projection models, camera calibration, epipolar geometry and structure from motion), Pattern Recognition (Principal Component Analysis).

This module has a lot a lot of linear algebra. Be sure to be familiar with matrices as almost every topic depends on it. It's okay to have forgotten what you have learnt in your level 1000 MA class (was 4 years since I last did it) but be sure to revise it throughout the course as you will need it often. Many of the geometry topics are also interlinked, hence you cannot study them in isolation as your exam question can involve a combination of a few topics.

Lecture:
For certain topics, the lecture slides are full of matrices and equations, and understanding them within the lecture duration is almost impossible. An adequate amount of time will have to be spent at home revising the concepts. There are also no webcasts. I missed many lectures due to the lecture slot clashing with CS3216 (I was a CS3216 tutor) and had to self-study many topics. Fortunately, Prof Ng is a very nice and patient professor that doesn't mind taking out time to give consultations to needy students. Do approach him if you need help understanding the content.

Labs:
From what I know, the labs have been made easier as compared to when the previous CV prof (Prof Leow Wee Kheng) was teaching it. In the past, students were given the steps to be done, and they had to code out in OpenCV (Python). Now, students are provided with the final code and they simply have to cut and paste into the editor and run it. Questions were asked to test students' understanding of the given code. But I would have preferred the previous approach of letting students code it out themselves, will definitely learn more. At least we got to learn basic OpenCV functions, which are really useful for doing simple image processing.

Assignments:
Assignments are a new thing that were added this semester. I thoroughly enjoyed doing the assignments as we were asked to code it out ourselves. We had to implement the algorithms behind Histogram Equalization, Corner Detection and Camera Projection Models-related tasks, without the help of OpenCV except to read/write images. It was through doing these assignments that I got to understand the said topics well. I wish there were more assignments for us to do and learn from.

Project:
The project topic was to create a 12 seconds flythrough video out of a 2D image. Almost no guidance was given to us and many teams (including us) had no clue where to start. The recommended approach by Prof Ng was to build an interface to give each point in the image a 3d coordinate, and then proceed to slice out portions of the image and lastly map them to the surfaces of the 3D model. The flythrough video can then be generated from the model. This made me wonder if we were studying a CV class if we had to do these CG stuff rather than using CV techniques. In the end, due to time constraints (read: procrastination), we simplified the scene into a cuboid and sliced up the given image into 5 planes, corresponding to the 5 faces within a cuboid (without back face of course!). 

Dealing with UI in Python is a pain from my experience with Tkinter, hence we decided to use a web interface instead. We chose Flask as the web app framework because it was lightweight and also implemented in Python, which meant easy integration with OpenCV. I created a web interface for users to draw the camera path while my awesome teammate Le Minh Tu wrote the code to map the sliced image textures on to the plane. The initial version of the video was very jerky because the path was not smooth, hence I changed the interface to allow the user to draw control points for a bezier curve instead. After completing the above tasks, we realized that we could generalize our project to work for any photo/image with one vanishing point. Hence we added another interface for the user to specify the vanishing point and the size of the front face of the cuboid. When we presented to Prof Ng, even though we did not follow his suggested approach, he was very glad that we used the concept of vanishing points in our project, which stood out among other teams that adopted this cuboid approach, and that gave us decent grades for the project! View out final generated flythrough videos here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p45J37Ha3uQ and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TCJ2wBEXRpc.

Exams:
I went to find Prof Ng for consultations for two days straight and finally got to understand the lecture content (I would say at a surface level). Initially, when Prof Ng went through the past year exam paper I was very demoralized as I wasn't able to do most of the questions. The PYP's last question was worth like 25 marks and required 3 lines of answers; either you know and you score full marks, or you don't know and you get 0. My exam was relatively kinder, as almost every question was broken up into smaller parts, with each worth about 5/10/15 marks.  However, many questions required super short answers (such as one number, or circling the right dot), and they were almost impossible to guess; you'll only get them right if you have a good understanding of your the lecture materials. I got the first two questions correct but answered parts of the third and fourth questions wrongly and scored 77/100 for the paper. I went to look for Prof Ng to view my script and find out where I went wrong, but after his explanation, I still did not understand, probably because I never had a solid understanding of the lecture materials in the first place.

Grade: A+

Admittedly, I don't think I deserve an A+ for this module because even after the exams, I still don't understand a good part of the lecture content. I guess the Bell Curve God was in my favour and many students fumbled in the early questions which were heavy on calculations and the project component. Everybody scores full marks for the labs and assignments, so the only way to differentiate yourself would be to excel in the project and exams.
thread_title: CS4243 Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in AY14/15 Sem 1
Lecturer: Dr Bimlesh Wadhwa

Module content:
This is one of the unconventional CS modules where the focus is not on hardcore CS concepts. Instead, the whole module revolves around a problem-solving methodology called Design Thinking. Design Thinking is one of the hottest approaches in problem solving these days. SAP charges over a thousand for a Design Thinking workshop that lasts a few days. The Faculty of Engineering has a programme called Design-Centric Programme, which also emphasizes on Design Thinking through the curriculum.

Students in CS3240 will be taught and brought through the various stages of Design Thinking: Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test.

Lectures and Tutorials:
The lectures are highly interactive and almost every lecture involves hands-on exercises to reinforce the concepts taught in each phase of Design Thinking. The lecture content is light as there are no tough and technical CS theories to be learnt. Most theories and concepts are quite straightforward and actually makes a lot of sense. Hence they were easy to understand in my opinion.

Note that the tutorials and lectures are combined together into one massive 3-hour lesson, so it might get tiring halfway. There's also no distinguishing between lectures and tutorials. But somehow time passes pretty fast during the lessons. Also, Dr Bimlesh she brings snacks (Oreo, sweets, etc) and puts them at the back of the class and they are available for any hungry/student to eat. I visit the snacks corner often (:

In the last few weeks, us students took over the lectures instead. In our project groups, we were each assigned an Interaction Design-related topic and gave presentations to the class. However, the quality of the presentations is subjected to the groups effort in preparing the content. In my opinion, some groups' presentations were off-topic and did not have much valuable content.

Labs:
There were four lab sessions in total covering the following topics: HTML/CSS, Front-end development with Bootstrap and jQuery, HTML5 Canvas, and Chocolate Chip UI. Personally I did not attend any of the lab sessions as I had prior experience with front end development so I am unable to comment on the lab sessions. From the materials covered, the content covered is pretty basic, and students would have to really spend their own time delving into them to be proficient enough in them.

Lab Assignments:
There were two lab assignments for the course: Bootstrap and Chocolate Chip UI. The topics were very open and you could make any app that you wanted, as long as they made use of the various Bootstrap/ChUI elements. For the Bootstrap lab, I made a portfolio site template (https://github.com/yangshun/portfolio-site-template) and for the ChUI lab, I made a Hacker News and Designer News aggregator (https://github.com/yangshun/builder-news). The final outcome can be a mockup, as long as interactions were allowed. The labs are only worth 3% each, and each small mistake will cost you 0.3%, hence really negligible impact. I spent quite a lot of time (one-two days each) on the assignments though, because I liked doing front end web development.

Project:
The project group is formed early in the semester and there are three deliverables spread across the semester so that progress can be seen over the semester. The project topic was to solve a problem with a technical solution by applying the Design Thinking techniques and process that we learnt in the class. For the first deliverable, we had to identify our target users, analyze the problem and draft out the requirements for the solution. For the second deliverable, we had to define the primary user persona, define key tasks that our solution will support, build a paper prototype and also get people to test and give feedback. For the third and final deliverable, adjust our prototype according to the feedback, build a final interactive prototype, and evaluate with users. It is up to the technical standard of the team for the final interactive prototype. While it is perfectly fine to build static web mockups, building real working prototypes will definitely score you more points. 

For my project, we built NUSMods Collab, extending upon NUSMods and adding group collaboration features, allowing group members to combine their timetables to find common timeslots to plan for future group meetings. Our project got 2nd place in the STePS (SoC Term Project Showcase)! Watch out video produced by talented videographer Darren Chua and starring my super 上镜 members Khor Poo Siang and Joan Tan: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYGOF_FzwFc

Exam:
Dr Bimlesh was very kind in giving us adequate practice for the exam, and her exam questions were an altered version of the exam practice that we were given. Doing the past year papers will definitely help in managing expectations. However, there were many many exam questions, and most require a lot of writing. Know Bootstrap well, because we were asked to draw out the appearance of a webpage based on some Bootstrap-styled HTML code. Many exam questions were on the design thinking process applied to different real-life scenarios (you'll be given a sample problem/issue/scenario), so it will help if you did the project seriously. I started the exam paper from the back because the questions at the back had a higher marks/time spent ratio. In the end, I skipped questions that I knew I had to spend time thinking or didn't know how to do, and left about 8 marks blank.

Overall, a light module that I enjoyed. If you are the creative kind that likes hands-on and front end web development, I would recommend it.

Grade: A+

message: 太厉害了！Please be my CS3230 tutor next sem orz.
thread_title: CS3230 Design and Analysis of Algorithms

author: Yangshun
message:
Taken in AY13/14 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Ng Teck Khim

The module covers a few topics: Android development, Basic signal processing, Audio feature extraction, Image processing. Stuff you will learn include: Fourier Transform, Convolution, Histogram Equalization, Sampling, MFCC.

It was the first time the class was running, and the class only had a size of 8. Lectures were understandable and content was relatively light. A lot of time was spent on understanding fourier transform, and you will know it at the back of your hand by the end of the module.

The tutorial assignments were about making Android apps that processed an audio signal and plotted it out on the canvas. The difficulty of the labs is progressive, and most of the labs took me around a day to complete.

Tutorials sessions were an extension of the lecture, and Prof Ng spent a lot of time revising lecture concepts. You won't even need to spend much extra time on your own revising for this module because Prof Ng does a very good job revising it with you during the tutorial sessions and lectures.

There's also a project component but it was a bit vague. We were free to make any app that made use of signal processing (audio/image) within it. I'm totally uncomfortable with Android, hence together with my partner, Xiangxin, we made an iOS app for children to learn new words. We made use of one of the popular speech recognition libraries to handle our speech recognition parts. Prof Ng was okay with that, because we have implemented parts of speech recognition functionality in our tutorial assignments before. Other projects from the class included a voice-controlled flappy bird and voice-activated Tap Tap Revenge.

The final exam was extremely easy (Prof Ng made it that way). The class size was small, hence there was no bell curve anyways. The first question was a little tricky as it tested on Android concepts. I didn't study much of the Android concepts but I dumped the lecture notes into my cheatsheet and happily referred to it during the exam. I left at around the hour mark after completing it and checking through.  In the end, Prof Ng let us view our exam scripts and I scored almost full marks for the exam.

CS3218 aims to cover more depth than breadth, and is a good foundation module to take before venturing into hardcore signal processing modules like CS4347 Sound and Music Computing and CS5241 Speech Recognition. 

Grade: A+
thread_title: CS3218 Multimodal Processing in Mobile Platforms

author: Yangshun
message:
Semester Taken: AY13/14 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Juzar Motiwalla, Prof Wong Weng Fai, Prof Pete Kellock

The course consists of 5 assignments constituting 30%, 20% class participation and 50% final project. The assignments were fun to do and didn't take up too much time. We had to review TechCrunch Disrupt finalists' ideas, generate our breakthrough ideas using the Big-W framework (a very useful idea generation framework), do a patent review exercise and a simple exercise on storytelling. For the final project, together with my friends from CS3217, we built Pomoc, a customer feedback service that mobile app vendors can integrate into their existing mobile applications. We would get useful feedback regarding our ideas which helped us polish it up.

Next, I will go through the various aspects of the class.

People - There were only 11 students in the class and the students were well selected and all were competent, which the insightful comments brought up made every class interesting and engaging. However, I wonder if the course can actually be scaled to accept more students when evidently, we were running short on time for many of the classes even though we only had 6 teams. It is a useful class for students interested in entrepreneurship and the trend is that an increasing number of students are paying more attention to startups and wanting to found their own startup. Hence I would encourage my peers and juniors to join this class.

The course instructors were also very helpful throughout the course, with each of them having expertise in related fields. Prof Juzar and Pete were experts in startups and the experience they shared with the class was extremely insightful. Personally I enjoyed attending the two review sessions as I received helpful comments from the instructors and I took effort in implementing them so as to improve the product. 

Content - I found the BIG-W particularly useful as I am always interested in new ideas. In fact, I used the BIG-W approach to come up with the idea for Pomoc, to integrate live chat customer support into mobile apps. Also, after I learnt about BIG-W, I observed that that was how many successful startups came about. Hence, BIG-W is definitely an approach worth learning and adopting. I would try to adopt BIG-W in the generation of ideas for my future projects.

Variety - It was good that product validation was allowed to be done via many approaches (prototyping, mockups, surveys, interviews, etc) to accommodate the lack of technical competency in the groups. In our final project presentations, we saw a variety of validation methods and it was an eye-opener for me as all of these would be useful for validating real products if we were to startup in the future.

The storytelling session was also fun to attend because we saw many different approaches that the teams took to tell their story: Comics, videos, animations. I liked the variety and dynamic content that the course and its students offered.

Homework - The amount of work given to us was reasonable and each deliverable’s deadlines were also mentioned to us in the first lecture so that we could manage our schedule well. I appreciate that very much.

Structure - The flow of the course was very clear, from idea generation, to patents, then storytelling and validation. These are the steps that a real startup would have to go through. Hence the structure was realistic and reflective of real-life situations.

In the end, Prof Juzar recommended me to pitch my project for VasCo grant. We didn't pitch in the end because not all of us had the time to pursue the project after the module. It was a great experience nonetheless.

Grade: A+

message:
I took this module in AY2011/12 Sem 2.

This is a really horrible module. The assignments take up time and the tutors are strict with the grading. I got a horrible grade despite putting in a lot of effort into my assignment. Also, the notes do not have text and are mostly pictures. I ended up studying off wikipedia.
