Grade: D+ :(

author: Yangshun
message:
I took this module in AY10/11 Sem 2.

This is the one of the PRC GEM combo pairs (the other is GEM2900) because it is a super easy module that any decent JC student can manage. Beware of the number of PRCs and foreigners in this module, they are good at math and are very likely to own. Like what Yet Sin said, the content is highly similar to CS1231, but much easier. Question patterns don't change much from year to year. Just buy the textbook and do the questions inside you will be fine.

Lecturer is very boring and classes were at night. Hence I didn't go for a single lecture. I didn't go for any tutorials either. Never saw my tutor before hehe.

I also did all the past year papers and checked with friends. Questions are similar in each semester.

As this is an open book exam, I brought in all the past year papers that I have completed, and can refer to them in case I get stuck during the exam. I also printed out all the dates and days from 1st Jan 1970 to 2030. That way, I would be able to verify my answers for the date calculation questions.

I walked out of the exam hall very confidently, thinking I would get above 95 for the paper. However, after I checked answers with my friend, I found out that I misread some questions and made some careless mistakes here and there. Ultimately, I got an A- for the module with an estimated of ~87 for final exam and 85/100 for midterm. This shows how steep the bell curve is.

Grade: A-

author: Yangshun
message:
Took this module in AY10/11 Sem 2.

This module totally feels like a 4MC module, with 3 labs and tutorials every week. It starts of with a refreshment of JC electrical circuit knowledge. A few new laws will be introduced, such as KVL, Thevenin's Law, Norton's Law etc. They may seem confusing as first, but practice makes perfect. Phasors will be introduced after DC circuit concepts are covered. This is basically introducing complex numbers into AC circuits, hence solid knowledge of complex numbers learnt during JC will come in handy at this point of time. The first part of the module is relatively simple once the concepts are grasped. The second part of the module is quite simple too, it can actually be viewed as four separate topics, logic gates, transformers, diodes and motors. Logic gates are quite simple, dealing with alot of boolean algebra. Hence those who have taken CS1231 or GEK1505 will be at an advantage. The content covered in transformers, diodes and motors are quite surface level; hence memorisation of formulas is sufficient.

Didnt attend most lectures and attended only a few tutorials. My tutor was very good. Before she starts on a tutorial, she will go through the topic and highlight the key important points and she explained the concepts really well. Each question is also given adequate coverage. Very useful.

Midterms are quite easy. The first midterm consists of 4 questions worth 10 marks each. For the first midterm, due to the extensive amount of practice that I did, I had time to do every question twice, using a different approach to make sure the answers are the same regardless of approach. For the second midterm, it was 15 MCQs. Most of the questions are easy, with some involving one step workings. However, do not underestimate the paper as some questions, beneath its innocent exterior, hides a cruel intent. Read questions carefully. The mean was 22/30 (11 out of 15 questions) correct, which in my opinion, is quite high.

Labs are long and draggy. Try to understand the experiment before going for the lab. Each lab is given a grade based on the quality of the lab report. Not difficult to get an A, just need to be meticulous and detailed. Average for labs is A,A,B for the 3 labs.

The standard for the final exams differs for each semester. Some very complicated circuit diagrams are actually very simple if the correct rule/law is applied. I would advise students to do past year paper questions to get a feel of the kind of circuit questions that may appear during exams. They are usually not difficult, merely requires the candidate to see through the trick to doing the question.

Phasor calculations are pretty straightforward. I would advise ALL EG1108 candidates to get a CASIO fx991ES calculator, which can do phasor/complex number calculations. This is they key to saving time during examinations, rather than doing phasor calculations manually. Most questions are similar to tutorial standard. Hence it is imperative to do your tutorials dutifully. 

Lastly, the motors question is A MUST DO. It's simple and has a high mark weightage. Generally, I would advise candidates against doing both the circuit questions because they are quite time-consuming to analyse and calculate. 
Circuit questions: 2-3 lines of working -> 1 mark
Motor/Diodes/Transformer questions: 1 line of working -> 2-3 marks

Hence, its much wiser to spend time doing questions from part 2 of the syllabus and the remaining time checking the paper. 
I would not say this module is difficult, as long as one puts in time and effort to study the notes and attempt tutorials and past year questions, a decent grade is attainable. 

My CA is above average, but my finals pulled me up quite abit. The only thing I’m unhappy about this module is that its only 3MCs, when the workload made it seem like a 4MC one.

Grade: A+

author: Yangshun
message:
This is an extremely selective module where the professor in-charge (It has been Prof Ben Leong for the past two years) chooses around 5-6 students to be part of Facebook Open Academy, a program where students join open-source projects for around 10 weeks. I joined the Mozilla Marketplace (Galaxy) project and was in-charge of doing front end development. The Github project can be found here: https://github.com/mozilla/galaxy. 

Participants got to travel to Facebook HQ in Menlo Park for a few days (all expenses covered) and attended the Facebook Open Academy Kickoff where we met and interacted with our open-source project teammates and mentors.

Back at NUS, the 6 of us in the class would meet Prof Ben every week and update everyone on the project's progress.

This is an extremely good module to pick up open source development. My mentor, Chris Van, is a very experienced full stack Mozilla engineer. I learnt many useful software engineering skills from him: git-fu, maintaining a good JavaScript and CSS coding style, writing modular JavaScript, etc. A full reflection of the module can be found here: http://yangshun.im/#/facebook-open-academy-reflections

Grades for this module are not bell-curved and is determined by the mentor. Thankfully, my mentor ranked me as 2nd out of the 9 students in the project and awarded me an A grade. I was top contributor to the project and completed most of the front end pages. I guessed that helped.

Grade: A

author: Yangshun
message:
Took this module in AY2010/11 Semester 2.

This module touches on 1st and 2nd order differential equations, mathematical modelling, matrices and some linear algebra. Partial Differential Equations are newly included this semester (brought over from MA1505). 

I didn't attend a single lecture nor tutorial. Watched all webcasts but didn't do tutorials towards the end. Webcasted lecturer was Toh Pee Choon. He explains concepts rather clearly, but often he will pause the lecture to scold misbehaving students, which can be quite funny. I have never seen my tutor before. No comments about him. No need to do the 3 preparatory lab tutorials, just do the final lab assessment.

20% Mid-terms 10 questions of MCQ. Similar format as MA1505 mid-terms. 5% Lab assignment 75% Final Exam One piece two-sided handwritten cheatsheet allowed Same format as MA1505, 16 questions, 5 marks each. 

My semester’s midterms was quite easy, the mean was quite okay, at around 6 to 7 out of 10. 
The lab assignment is a must score. Most people will score full marks for this section. You can attempt to do the lab questions by yourself, it will be similar to the questions given in the lab tutorials, but remember to compare answers with your friends. Most people will do that, hence you will be on the losing end if you do not do it too.

I was lagging behind in MA1506 after the midterms. Only managed to finish the syllabus during reading week. Spent a few days spamming past year exam questions. It's very important to do past year exam questions for math modules as most of the questions have similar formats. Doing past year papers will definitely prepare you well for the final examination.

I could do most questions in the final exam. Some of the questions will take you a lot of time, while some questions are really no-brainers, involving one-step workings etc (you just need to spot the trick/method to do the question). Just be careful while doing. Didn’t study the topic on oscillations, hence could not really do the oscillations question (luckily only one question on it).

With 9/10 for the midterms, close to full marks for the 5% lab assignment and one/two questions wrong in the final exam, I was quite Iucky to get an A+. It is not bad considering that I only started studying one week before reading week.

Gade: A+

author: Yangshun
message:
Hi Cheryl, thanks for posting a review for JS1101E. We have moved Corspedia to be part of NUSMods, would you post your review there again?

Here's the link for your convenience:
http://nusmods.com/modules/JS1101E/reviews

and your review:

JS1101E is a must-take module if you are interested in Japan. It has a broad focus, covering Japanese history, geography and anthropology amongst others, but has enough depth within each field to leave you with a deeper understanding of the country. Prof Chris McMorran is awesome and I highly recommend taking the module under him. There is a large group project component though, which requires research and a wise choice of topic. But it's totally worth it la, I feel. This is the kind of module which you would remember and feel grateful about taking four semesters down the road.
thread_title: CS2010 Data Structures and Algorithm II

author: Yangshun
message:
This module is easier in Sem 2 when Dr Ang Chuan Heng is teaching it. I took it in AY2011/12 Sem 2 under Dr Ang. Dr Ang introduced a lot on the different types of data structures (AVL Trees, Red Black Trees, B-Tree, 2-3-4, Skip Lists) and assesses students on how well they understand the behaviours of the various types of data structures. It is not difficult as long as a student understands the lecture content well.

On the contrary, in semester 1, Prof Steven Halim adopts a more algorithm-centric approach, and it is much tougher in my opinion. Prof Halim's exams have been known to be a killer :P

Grade: A

thread_title: NUSMods · NUS CORS Module Finder & Timetable Builder (Unofficial)

author: Yangshun
message:
I took this module in AY2013/14 Semester 1.

I really hate this module with a passion. Firstly, this module has 3 hours of lectures and 2 hours of tutorials a week. It is sometimes tough to understand Prof Stephan Bressan because he speaks with an accent. The lecture content is understandable but it is super DRY. The slides are tediously long and the design is repetitive, without any pictures or illustrations, which makes it really hard to sit through one lecture without falling asleep. Tutorials are 2 hours long but honestly there isn't a need for it to be so long. Each tutorial only has ~5 questions and there's no reason it takes 2 hours to complete. During my tutorial, there were a lot of time given to use to carry out discussion but it's pretty pointless IMO.

The worst part of the module was the project. Prof Bressan tries to be innovative and makes everyone form a group and create a project to explain a mathematical theorem. In the past years, students had to make videos and presentations. For my semester, we had to draw a comic. Yes, you read right, a freaking 10-page comic on explaining Carmichael numbers. It was a tedious process to draw the comics page by page in pencil. I tried a Japanese manga style and hence spent quite long, but I was pretty satisfied with the outcome, as the comic looked decent in the end.

However, despite me and my friend's efforts (the other two girls in the team were slacking away), we only got average grades for it. I should have just drew XKCD style comics and spent the extra time somewhere else. The project was LAME and USELESS in enhancing our knowledge in CS1231, except causing people distress and pain. Prof Bressan's attempt at making his dry module more creative sucked, and has also sucked the souls out of many poor SoC freshmen. I would have been happier doing another midterm or something.

In Semester 2, this module is been taught by the Maths department, which has less emphasis on proving, and more on calculating. There's only midterm and exams; no stupid projects to waste your time. Although learning the skill of proving is important to SoC students for future modules in algorithm analysis, it is still worth it to take in Semester 2 to just avoid Bressan and his extremely lame project requirement. The choice is yours.

author: Yangshun
message:
I took CS2105 in AY2013/14 Semester 2 and it was taught by Prof Ooi Wei Tsang.

His lecture notes are super minimalistic: one-word per slide. Most of the content comes from his lectures which you can view online at http://vimeo.com/user4464553. The content is relatively light and the textbook is used as supporting material. We are expected to read the textbook because his lecture notes don't have much words. We had 3 assignments, two of which was coding in Java. The last one was a practical assignment involving WireShark. The median for the assignments are close to full marks, so it's important to grab every mark for such assignments (that applies to all modules in SoC).

My CS2105 midterms was a killer, the MCQs were really tough and I was guessing answers for all 6 MCQs. In the end the average was 16/30. From the midterms, I realised that the trick to scoring in a Prof Ooi exam is to understand the concepts taught in his tutorials. I didn't study much for CS2105 but studied his tutorials thoroughly, making sure I understood and could do every tutorial question. It helped me in my final exam and I could do almost every question. In the end, with scoring around 80% for the midterm and exam, I managed an A grade for this module.

In Semester 1, CS2105 is taught by Chan Mun Choon, who has a different focus. For Prof Ooi, he covered more breadth than depth. I believe Prof Chan covers more depth than breadth. I think networks is harder under Prof Chan. Talk to seniors who have taken CS2105 in Semester 1 for an accurate description of the module under Prof Chan.

Talk to seniors who have taken CS2105 in Semester 1 for an accurate description of the module under Prof Chan. Grade: A
thread_title: CS3216 Software Development on Evolving Platforms

author: Yangshun
message:
I took this module in AY2013/14 Semester 1.

I really hate this module with a passion. Firstly, this module has 3 hours of lectures and 2 hours of tutorials a week. It is sometimes tough to understand Prof Stephan Bressan because he speaks with an accent. The lecture content is understandable but it is super DRY. The slides are tediously long and the design is repetitive, without any pictures or illustrations, which makes it really hard to sit through one lecture without falling asleep. Tutorials are 2 hours long but honestly there isn't a need for it to be so long. Each tutorial only has ~5 questions and there's no reason it takes 2 hours to complete. During my tutorial, there were a lot of time given to use to carry out discussion but it's pretty pointless IMO.

The worst part of the module was the project. Prof Bressan tries to be innovative and makes everyone form a group and create a project to explain a mathematical theorem. In the past years, students had to make videos and presentations. For my semester, we had to draw a comic. Yes, you read right, a freaking 10-page comic on explaining Carmichael numbers. It was a tedious process to draw the comics page by page in pencil. I tried a Japanese manga style and hence spent quite long, but I was pretty satisfied with the outcome, as the comic looked decent in the end.

However, despite me and my friend's efforts (the other two girls in the team were slacking away), we only got average grades for it. I should have just drew XKCD style comics and spent the extra time somewhere else. The project was LAME and USELESS in enhancing our knowledge in CS1231, except causing people distress and pain. Prof Bressan's attempt at making his dry module more creative sucked, and has also sucked the souls out of many poor SoC freshmen. I would have been happier doing another midterm or something.

In Semester 2, this module is been taught by the Maths department, which has less emphasis on proving, and more on calculating. There's only midterm and exams; no stupid projects to waste your time. Although learning the skill of proving is important to SoC students for future modules in algorithm analysis, it is still worth it to take in Semester 2 to just avoid Bressan and his extremely lame project requirement. The choice is yours.

author: Yangshun
message:
I took CS2105 in AY2013/14 Semester 2 and it was taught by Prof Ooi Wei Tsang. 

His lecture notes are super minimalistic: one-word per slide. Most of the content comes from his lectures which you can view online at http://vimeo.com/user4464553. The content is relatively light and the textbook is used as supporting material. We are expected to read the textbook because his lecture notes don’t have much words. We had 3 assignments, two of which was coding in Java. The last one was a practical assignment involving WireShark. The median for the assignments are close to full marks, so it’s important to grab every mark for such assignments (that applies to all modules in SoC).

My CS2105 midterms was a killer, the MCQs were really tough and I was guessing answers for all 6 MCQs. In the end the average was 16/30. From the midterms, I realised that the trick to scoring in a Prof Ooi exam is to understand the concepts taught in his tutorials. I didn’t study much for CS2105 but studied his tutorials thoroughly, making sure I understood and could do every tutorial question. It helped me in my final exam and I could do almost every question. In the end, with scoring around 80% for the midterm and exam, I managed an A grade for this module.

In Semester 1, CS2105 is taught by Chan Mun Choon, who has a different focus. For Prof Ooi, he covered more breadth than depth. I believe Prof Chan covers more depth than breadth. I think networks is harder under Prof Chan. Talk to seniors who have taken CS2105 in Semester 1 for an accurate description of the module under Prof Chan.

author: Yangshun
message:
This module is easier in Sem 2 when Dr Ang Chuan Heng is teaching it. I took it in AY2011/12 Sem 2 under Dr Ang. Dr Ang introduced a lot on the different types of data structures (AVL Trees, Red Black Trees, B-Tree, 2-3-4, Skip Lists) and assesses students on how well they understand the behaviours of the various types of data structures. It is not difficult as long as a student understands the lecture content well. 

On the contrary, in semester 1, Prof Steven Halim adopts a more algorithm-centric approach, and it is much tougher in my opinion. Prof Halim's exams have been known to be a killer :P
